> Two questions. Answers please <G>

1) A TROLL is generally thought of as in the context of
Billy Goats Gruff (maybe I've forgotten the actual title).
The person tends to stir up trouble in a news group --
tending to start arguments, name calling, etc.

2) You report them to their ISP. If their ISP agrees that
they are posting off topic, then they may well get their
email account yanked and the ISP may even cause their posts
to be cancelled (effectively withdrawn).

Robert Wagner wrote:
>

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

<snip>

Since you brought up the subject of CENSORSHIP, yes, I have
the right to discuss this and within this news group.

my ISP telling them that I have been making off-topic posts.
You also have the right to put my posts into a kill file

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: To "killfile" or not to "killfile"

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-01T08:23:24+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3od2f$v5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<b3j8vq$j46$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5dabd5.31132429@news.optonline.net> <dcedb8d0.0302270928.59f4f343@posting.google.com> <3e5f4f5c.138547903@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> jzitzelb@tsys.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote:

> I'd like to believe that. I really would. But when I see threads with
> subject 'Robert Wagner screws up again', it sure looks like flame city.
> How often do you see a poster's name in the subject line? Almost never,
> unless it's 'personal'.

He has explained why he does this.  It is so that others can be forwarned 
that this may be boring, taking you to task for spreading complete 
misinformation, and they can then easily avoid it and move on to more 
interesting stuff.

He could, of course, just ignore you spreading mythology and misleading 
obsolete and just wrong opinions, but then some novices who haven't yet 
developed a critical and selective mode may think that what you say is true.
 
As for 'flame war'.  No.  You need to be able to distinguish between 
criticism of 'what you say' and criticism of 'who you are'.  It is usually 
a feature of teenagers that they take criticism as a persdnal afront.

>>You ought to reread some of his comments and take them into
>>consideration rather than taking umbrage.
> 
> Some are indeed based on fact, and include quotations from the standard.
> But others are pure emotion.

Some are based on fact ?  Only some ?  Which ones are 'pure emotion' ?  
Please quote relevant passages.
 
> Richard is the same. Some of his replies are reasonable while others are
> pure insult.
> 
> Do we have a Jekyll-Hyde dynamic going here?

No, it is called escalation.  When you make a statement that is simply 
wrong in fact then this is pointed out with references to the correct 
information.  If you persist in making the same error then a firmer reply 
is used to indicate that you should be taking notice.

After that you get told you are a fuckwit.

Or, putting it another way, after criticising what you say with no 
noticable change then it probably _is_ a problem with who you are.

Recently, however, I notice a change where you are actually reading up on 
this stuff and learning something.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: To "killfile" or not to "killfile"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-01T19:23:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e60fc4b.248371328@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b3j8vq$j46$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5dabd5.31132429@news.optonline.net> <dcedb8d0.0302270928.59f4f343@posting.google.com> <3e5f4f5c.138547903@news.optonline.net> <b3od2f$v5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>As for 'flame war'.  No.  You need to be able to distinguish between 
>criticism of 'what you say' and criticism of 'who you are'.  It is usually 
>a feature of teenagers that they take criticism as a personal afront.

I had a girlfriend who had an English degree from a very prestigious university
and worked as a writer/copy-editor for a well-known science magazine. One day
she used the phrase "In the lexicon of political science ..". I calmly pointed
out that 'lexicon' means 'vocabulary'; it doesn't mean 'style'. She should have
used 'jargon' or 'patois'.  My seemingly innocent correction launched World War
III. She spent a month looking up cites to support her use of the word. She
couldn't let go of it until I apologized. Why? Because I was stomping on her
turf. She, not I, was the English expert. 

That painful experience taught me that _some_ authority figures become
emotionally upset when contradicted in their field of expertise. They handle
correction in other topics with equinimity, but go ballistic when challenged on
their home turf. Their reaction is strongest when they suspect the critic might
be right. The trait isn't limited to teens.

When someone catches me making a COBOL error, I thank him .. if he's _correct_.
For instance, the thread about return code 35 on file not found. If he's
incorrect, such as the thread on Micro Focus indexed files, I defend my
position.

Robert
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3r6vr$np1$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b3j8vq$j46$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5dabd5.31132429@news.optonline.net> <dcedb8d0.0302270928.59f4f343@posting.google.com> <3e5f4f5c.138547903@news.optonline.net> <b3od2f$v5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e60fc4b.248371328@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e60fc4b.248371328@news.optonline.net...
> Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
 <snip>
>
> When someone catches me making a COBOL error, I thank him .. if he's
_correct_.
> For instance, the thread about return code 35 on file not found. If he's
> incorrect, such as the thread on Micro Focus indexed files, I defend my
> position.
>
> Robert

Have you yet accepted that NO currently supported IBM compiler requires an
EXIT PROGRAM statement to be in a paragraph by itself?  Have you verified
and confirmed that *NO* other ANSI/ISO '85 Standard conforming compiler has
this requirement?  (Have you tried this with any compiler - with or without
ANSI conformance checking turned on?)

If you are still "researching it" - please keep us informed about your
progress toward your "thanking those who 'caught you in a COBOL error'".

    ***

It has been pointed out to you that (at least one - probably more) other
person tries to do what I do

  - start out just pointing out the error (hopefully as succinctly as
possible)
 - when you "refute it" - I give "chapter and verse" - hopefully online -
to show you your error
 - when you CONTINUE to claim you are correct (when you aren't - and have
"zero evidence" to support your position) I get more vocal and personal
about it

If you were simply to start "checking your facts" between step 1 and step 2,
you would never see step 3 replies.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Accepting your errors? (was: To "killfile" or not to "killfile"

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWS0202@comcast.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2_udneHjisvXVvyjXTWcqw@comcast.com>`
- **References:** `<b3j8vq$j46$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5dabd5.31132429@news.optonline.net> <dcedb8d0.0302270928.59f4f343@posting.google.com> <3e5f4f5c.138547903@news.optonline.net> <b3od2f$v5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e60fc4b.248371328@news.optonline.net> <b3r6vr$np1$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:b3r6vr$np1$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net...
>
> "Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> and confirmed that *NO* other ANSI/ISO '85 Standard conforming compiler
has
> this requirement?  (Have you tried this with any compiler - with or
without
> ANSI conformance checking turned on?)
>

    I don't see where it is that big of a deal.

    If you want something that can be used anywhere, main program or
subprogram, IBM or not, CICS or not, try this:

REAL-PARA-NAME.                 *> Possibly used in program
    OPTIONAL END OF PROGRAM CODE.
JIJDCKDIVIKD9OFVU.
     EXIT PROGRAM.
OOINDSLKOWESUIV.
    STOP RUN.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: To "killfile" or not to "killfile"

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-03-01T14:17:00-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0303011417.178a32ed@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b3j8vq$j46$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5dabd5.31132429@news.optonline.net> <dcedb8d0.0302270928.59f4f343@posting.google.com> <3e5f4f5c.138547903@news.optonline.net> <b3od2f$v5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e60fc4b.248371328@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> If he's
> incorrect, such as the thread on Micro Focus indexed files, I defend my
> position.

You may well defend your position, but it is indefensible.  While you
may be correct about what you have seen in your own playpen, you have
made statements about the generality which are just not true, and made
'corrections' about what happens in specific environments that you
steadfastly remain ignorant of.

Your advice about 'reading as flat or line sequential' was made in the
complete absense of any information about his environment, which over
several messages, has been made quite clear may be completely
different to yours.

Many may have learned quite a lot about the various file options and
structures as we attempted to teach you something you otherwise not
know unless you started to read.  However you seemed to have
obstinately and deliberately learned nothing (except possibly that
deleted records are a problem) while you closed your eyes and ears and
repeated your mantra "I am always right, what I see is all there is,
reading from books is evil".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: To "killfile" or not to "killfile"

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-02T00:24:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e614cc3.8177130@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b3j8vq$j46$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5dabd5.31132429@news.optonline.net> <dcedb8d0.0302270928.59f4f343@posting.google.com> <3e5f4f5c.138547903@news.optonline.net> <b3od2f$v5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e60fc4b.248371328@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303011417.178a32ed@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

>Your advice about 'reading as flat or line sequential' was made in the
>complete absense of any information about his environment, which over
>several messages, has been made quite clear may be completely
>different to yours.

Had it been explained dispassionately, preferably with cites, I would have given
your arguments more consideration. When presented with ad hominem, I, like most
humans, react by by tuning it out. 

In the instant case, one sentence would have settled the issue. 'The default
format on Unix is C-ISAM (type 2) whereas the default format on Win/NT is type
3.' It should not have all this flammage to settle a simple question of fact.  

>Many may have learned quite a lot about the various file options and
>structures as we attempted to teach you something you otherwise not
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>reading from books is evil".

Reading from books exposes one to the vagaries of language and ambiguous
documentation. I'm a hands-on experimenter rather than a theoretician.
Real-world evidence on my screen carries more weight than someone's vision of
how it _should_ work. 

I conceded in this case. You were correct and I was incorrect. Next time, my
approach will hopefully reveal Truth more efficiently than books. 

Robert
```

riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote
> 
>> I had a girlfriend who ..
>
>I notice the past tense.

At the time, 5 years ago, she was 24 and I was 55. She said I looked "too old'.
Will somebody please shoot me? 

>> she used the phrase "In the lexicon of political science ..". 
>
>Interestingly a google search for the phrase shows it in common use.

Popular use doesn't make it right. Argumentum ad populum. I could overlook the
gaffe from most people, but not from a copy editor at Scientific American. 

>> I calmly pointed
>> out that 'lexicon' means 'vocabulary'; it doesn't mean 'style'. She should
>> have used 'jargon' or 'patois'. 
>
>'Jargon' is also 'words', just a part of the lexicon.

No, 'jargon' and 'pataois' mean industry-specific _meanings_ of words which are
in everyone's vocabulary. 

>> She couldn't let go of it until I apologized. 
>> Why? Because I was stomping on her turf. She, not I, was the English expert. 
>
>No. It was probably because you were _wrong_.

That's what she kept insisting. The OED definition of lexicon wasn't
authoritative enough. 

>But then the problem is that you continuously insist that you are
>right, regardless of all evidence and then when they tell you to piss
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>And you really don't notice the flaw in this ?

Ok, emend the above. Perhaps I should shoot _myself_ to cleanse the gene pool or
out of simple guilt for being stupid. 

Problem is, if stupid people dispatched themselves, we'd have no trash
collectors nor systems administrators ... not to mention managers. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: One of my "pet peeves" (was: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-01T14:36:35-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3r5kq$1i9$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b3dm0b$1n16$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <dcedb8d0.0302250837.77cdd07a@posting.google.com> <b3g86g$es9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0302260639.71e35bc8@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302261226.5b9d4d37@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302270850.24c3ff03@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302281101.7bbf255@posting.google.com> <b3ocpc$9fs$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3oekr$2pv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3BF689C1128DA9D7.C4253D0340213011.84486FA34A45DA4A@lp.airnews.net>`

```
Correct that it is NOT true

   GoBack is valid on all IBM platforms (well, actually - I haven't checked
OS/400 recently but I *think* it is valid there)

  Stop Run is *also* valid in all platforms (and must be as it is part of
the ANSI/ISO Standards)

"traditionally" GoBack was strongly suggested (if not required) in both CICS
(all platforms) and IMS/DC (MVS-ish platforms)  because you wanted to leave
what looked to the programmer as a "main program" while keeping the
transaction "driver" running.

The use of GoBack in all programs in all environments "simplified" having to
think about what your target was - because it ALWAYS worked - while using
STOP RUN sometimes, might cause problems.
```

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-02T11:53:29-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<C4300DD8A99CDCC0.270F8B3D8BB053D6.AD44975637EA6C95@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<b3g86g$es9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0302260639.71e35bc8@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302261226.5b9d4d37@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302270850.24c3ff03@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302281101.7bbf255@posting.google.com> <b3ocpc$9fs$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3oekr$2pv$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3BF689C1128DA9D7.C4253D0340213011.84486FA34A45DA4A@lp.airnews.net> <b3r5kq$1i9$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Sat, 1 Mar 2003 14:36:35 -0600, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> enlightened us:

>Correct that it is NOT true
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>STOP RUN sometimes, might cause problems.

Thanks.  I don't remember using Goback in batch VSE programs, always
STOP RUN, however I do know/remember you use GOBACK in the main
program of a CICS transaction.  I just never recalled doing STOP RUN
in MVS batch programs but then I work more in online environments than
batch.

Regards,

```
SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote:

>On Sat, 1 Mar 2003 14:36:35 -0600, "William M. Klein"
><wmklein@ix.netcom.com> enlightened us:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks.  I don't remember using Goback in batch VSE programs, 

In other words, you never wrote a CALLed program. This style is the reason
'monolithic' came to be associated with COBOL.

>however I do know/remember you use GOBACK in the main
>program of a CICS transaction. 

In the Good Old Days of VSE, issuing STOP RUN from a CICS transaction terminated
the whole CICS partition. Then a big, ugly systems programmer showed up at your
desk and whacked your hands with a ruler.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: One of my "pet peeves" (was: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-01T00:41:28-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3pkmt$vl2$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <YUT5a.48433$495.2618807@news1.telusplanet.net> <b3dhte$6ah$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3dm0b$1n16$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <dcedb8d0.0302250837.77cdd07a@posting.google.com> <b3g86g$es9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0302260639.71e35bc8@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302261226.5b9d4d37@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302270850.24c3ff03@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302281101.7bbf255@posting.google.com> <b3ocpc$9fs$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5ff60c.181226308@news.optonline.net> <b3otkp$iv5$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3e601306.188644662@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert,
  This certainly hasn't been true of any IBM mainframe COBOL compiler in the
last 20 years.  For the most recent product compare:

  http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IGY3LR10/6.2.14

"6.2.14 EXIT statement"
    ...
with a format diagram (limiting this to a single sentence of a paragraph by
itself) of:

"paragraph-name.__EXIT.__________________________________________>< "

with

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IGY3LR10/6.2.16

"6.2.16 EXIT PROGRAM statement"

that says,

"The EXIT PROGRAM statement should be the last statement in a sequence of
imperative statements. When it is not, statements following the EXIT PROGRAM
will not be executed if a CALL statement is active."

   ...

The same information (showing that EXIT PROGRAM doesn't need to be in a
paragraph by itself) is in the VS COBOL II manual at:

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IGYL1101/3.15

I'd give you the OS/VS COBOL documentation for this too - but it isn't
online.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: One of my "pet peeves" (was: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-01T17:16:45+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3pcaj$e8h$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3ocpc$9fs$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5ff60c.181226308@news.optonline.net> <b3otkp$iv5$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

>> The problem with EXIT PROGRAM is that it must be alone in a paragraph.
> Why?

> Robert,
>   Any chance that you are confusing the "EXIT PROGRAM" statement and the
> "EXIT" statement?  There is certainly no rule in the '85 Standard (or any
> Standard conforming compiler) that "EXIT PROGRAM" needs to be in a
> paragraph by itself.  

In older MF Cobol/2 manuals (eg 2.5) the EXIT PROGRAM has the rule "The 
EXIT PROGRAM sentence must be the only sentence in the paragraph" marked as 
"not enforced" as an extension for MF and VSC2.  In later MF manuals (eg 
3.2) the "not enforced" has ANS85 added to it.

It seems that '74 required EXIT PROGRAM to be in its own paragraph as for 
EXIT, and that MF failed to note the change in '85 in its manuals until 
later, or it may be that it was 'clarified' by the 89 addendum.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: One of my "pet peeves" (was: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-01T11:41:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e6078b3.214678546@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3dm0b$1n16$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <dcedb8d0.0302250837.77cdd07a@posting.google.com> <b3g86g$es9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0302260639.71e35bc8@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302261226.5b9d4d37@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302270850.24c3ff03@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302281101.7bbf255@posting.google.com> <b3ocpc$9fs$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5ff60c.181226308@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302281838.6a34a04b@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

>> It's easy for Micro Focus because _all_ programs are called .. by 'the
run-time
>> guy': cobrun. 
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>provide the required entry points.

Perhaps you could help me here. I worked at 'Shop A' where MF COBOL was compiled
to Unix executables of reasonable size. Now I'm at 'Shop B', also Unix, where
they used the default compilation to .int (interpreted p-code). The first thing
I did was change the default option to executable (-X) and was dismayed to see
every program sized >1M. Obviously MF was linking the full run-time into each
executable. 

So I retreated to .gnt format, which works fine but uses cobrun. I'd be happier
to have clean executables using a shared run-time. Do you know which compiler
options 'Shop A' used?

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: One of my "pet peeves" (was: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-01T03:32:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E6027D7.498DB309@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3dhte$6ah$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3dm0b$1n16$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <dcedb8d0.0302250837.77cdd07a@posting.google.com> <b3g86g$es9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0302260639.71e35bc8@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302261226.5b9d4d37@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302270850.24c3ff03@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302281101.7bbf255@posting.google.com> <b3ocpc$9fs$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <217e491a.0302281705.1a462cc1@posting.google.com>`

```


Richard wrote:

> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote
>
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> interesting (to me).

Richard,

I recall you mentioning your 'standard block' in previous messages. Your latest
triggered the thought in my mind. On reflection I realized I was doing very
similar on the archaic Radio Shack using RM/COBOL '74. Jeez ! Perhaps I'm not so
dumb after all <G>. I did it with a fixed copyfile passed to linkage of receiving
programs - and as you point out - change the standard block and you are up against
recompiles and tests - groannnn - that was like 30 minutes a compile per program
on the Shack machine.

Here's an alternative approach to get at those parameters, which client, plant
whether input will be Imperial/Metric etc. Specifically OO, but food for thought.
By default a Default File (one Relative record) holds last client/facility/Input
format etc. That stays until user selects another combination from Master Menu.

Part of my initial housekeeping is to check for existence of files/DB tables.
Historically that goes back to Shack days checking I hadn't got any corrupted
ISAMs on those dangerous and error prone platters. I still do the same now -
initializing objects for all (a) generic files/tables and (b) specific plant
files/tables.

I wanted to keep my Menu selection as simple as possible - so all it does is
invoke a Business program(class), 'Edit a File' or 'Print a File' etc and passes
Desktop = os-Parent (the Master Menu dialog) and self (the Application) as
objects. In the receiving class the sent 'self', (via linkage) is referenced as
os-App (object reference)

The invoked Business module *knows* which files/tables it wants - so using a
static copy file I have a standard method "getHandles" :-

 set DefaultFile to true
 invoke os-App "returnHandle" using FileFlag returning os-DefaultFile

 set DescripsFile/Table to true
 invoke os-App "returnHandle" using FileFlag returning os-DescripsFile/Table

set AnotherFile to true
invoke os-App 'returnHandle" etc..........

Now I did use the word 'static' with reference to the copyfile. In the event I did
add something else that I wanted to reference, then of course I would have to
change the copyfile, and under Net Express a compile would automatically trigger
recompilation of all classes affected by the copyfile change. Doesn't mean I have
to test every one, just the new class/program which will use the additional info -
and given the speed of computers now, even a recompile on some 30- 60
programs(classes which tend to be 'small' compared to non-OO programs) is still
only minutes, (probably less than 5).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

I do that at least once a week. Recompiling a system consisting of 10-50

two. I can have the system changed, recompiled, verified and in production in
less than five minutes. 

Distribution is ultra-simple: 'cp *.gnt ~'

Robert

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>

Joe Zitzelberger wrote:

>> <Shrug>, my system doesn't work like yours.  It _requires_ a STOP RUN
>> to exit the Cobol run-unit.  A GOBACK in the initial program simply
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> GOBACK is usable in sub programs, 

No.  I use EXIT PROGRAM in all other programs.  These programs will not 
work when started directly because they rely on data set up in the core 
program - sort of like global data but passed as parameter.

> why would you ever choose to use a STOP RUN?  

Because STOP RUN and EXIT PROGRAM are standard and GOBACK is an IBM thing 
that some other compilers have as an extention and IBM have finally managed 
to get into the 2002 standard.  IBM people don't seem to know this.

> Doesn't such a choice require you to give up the useful
> abstraction of not knowing which type of program you are working with?

I _always_ know what 'type of program' I work with.  There are 2 or 3 
variations of the 'core' program: ADIS, SP/2, Windows CGI, other CGI, .. 
and there are called programs which are passed parameters and can rely on 
certain settings within these.

"Not knowing" is not a _useful_ abstraction.  Relying on certain 'global' 
data is useful.

In fact I do have some code that is used both in my Cobol CGI system(s) and 
in interactive systems, it only requires that the same interfacing is used 
and that the CGI core program arranges for it to be set correctly.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-03-02T20:19:42-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<16e2f010.0303022019.3f34d699@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <dcedb8d0.0302270850.24c3ff03@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302281101.7bbf255@posting.google.com> <b3oepm$1hd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:<b3oepm$1hd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>...
> > why would you ever choose to use a STOP RUN?  
> 
> Because STOP RUN and EXIT PROGRAM are standard and GOBACK is an IBM thing 
> that some other compilers have as an extention and IBM have finally managed 
> to get into the 2002 standard.  IBM people don't seem to know this.

I have no interest in playing
my-compiler-is-better-than-your-compiler.  The original start of this
thread was discussing the BEHAVIOR of STOP RUN as opposed to the
functionally identical BEHAVIOR of EXIT PROGRAM (non-mainframe) /
GOBACK (mainframe).  There is no need to get pedantic about it.  I'll
try and remember to use "EXIT PROGRAM" when I mean "GOBACK" just to
make things clearer.

The behavior of STOP RUN, as I understand it, is to terminate the
currently running process.

The behavior of EXIT PROGRAM, as I understand it, is to return control
to the program that originally invoked the EXITing program.

The difference is subtle, but important.  There is a certain symmetry
to most programming -- files are opened and closed; memory is
allocated and freed;  processes are created and terminated.

When I say process, please include the other possible terms like RUN
UNIT, TASK or THREAD, I'm trying to be OS non-specific.  Process
creation takes many forms -- command shells can create a process in
response to user input; existing processes can 'fork' or 'attach'
another process to perform some work in an asynchronous fashion.  All
operating systems offer some facility to create a new instance of a
process, for which I will use the generic term "CreateProcess".  In
all cases, it just means taking the necessary steps to build an
environment, load the program text, allocate resources and begin
execution of said program.

When thinking of the "TerminateProcess" event there are several things
we know need to happen.  Buffers need to be flushed; database and
network connections dropped; files closed; memory released; resources
unlocked; etc.

In most cases, Cobol programs will never perform the "CreateProcess"
actions for themselves.  That is usually performed by the invoker --
operating system or command shell.  As such, allowing that invoker to
perform the "TerminateProcess" event is nicely symmetrical.  There is
no other language and/or environment that performs the
"TerminateProcess" as a normal case -- it is usually the exceptional
case (C's abort(), S/390's ABEND service, etc)

To make this the normal case violates the principal of a single
entry/single exit.  If program A calls program B which calls program C
which performs a STOP RUN, you have just introduced, at runtime, a new
exit for programs A and B.  It is possible, as you note, to make sure
that the STOP RUN always occurs in program A, and thus does not
violate this principle, but it is an unnecessary juggling act.

But lets get back to my original statement -- Cobol dates back to the
days when the 'operating system' was a small deck that served as a
loader, computers were single task batch systems.  Such was life in
the early 60's (or so I'm told -- I'm just a wet behind the ears PFCSK
that started this gig in the late 70s).  An operator would place an
object deck in the hopper of a device called a card reader, and press
the "read" button -- this is analogous to the "CreateProcess" event
described above.  Thus, it fits that early Cobol would end its
execution with a "TerminateProcess" style action to  return the
machine to the "I'm waiting for input, Dave" state.

>"Not knowing" is not a _useful_ abstraction.  Relying on certain
'global' data is useful.

The useful abstraction is not knowing if you are going drive a stake
through the virtual heart of your process, or if you are just
gracefully handing control back to program that invoked you.  That is
the cool of "EXIT PROGRAM".

I'm not sure what global data serves in discussing the behaviors of
these two constructs.  You can certainly simulate STOP RUNS with API
calls if flag-x is set, or simulate EXIT PROGRAM if flag-y is set, but
the entire thing is unnecessary, the EXIT PROGRAM/GOBACK is smart
enough to keep track of things for itself, so you don't have to.  And
isn't that really the point of a high level language?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-03-03T11:10:12-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0303031110.581ec12d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <dcedb8d0.0302270850.24c3ff03@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302281101.7bbf255@posting.google.com> <b3oepm$1hd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <16e2f010.0303022019.3f34d699@posting.google.com>`

```
psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote

> > > why would you ever choose to use a STOP RUN?  
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> violate this principle, but it is an unnecessary juggling act.

I have no idea why you have this strange idea that any of my programs
would ever do a STOP RUN after being CALLed.  The few small programs
that do a STOP RUN are _NEVER_, _EVER_ CALLed.  There is no point in
CALLing them, it just wouldn't work. There is never any requirement to
want to CALL them.

The programs that are CALLed cannot usefully be started from a command
line, they would be missing all the interfacing data that is required
before they do useful work.  I can, however, arrange that they be
executed by using a command line that activates one of the few core
programs specifying the program for it to CALL after the data has been
set up.

My systems are not like your systems, my environment is not like your
environment.  Making up spurious and ficticious arguments about how
you think my systems might operate just doesn't work.

> The useful abstraction is not knowing if you are going drive a stake
> through the virtual heart of your process, or if you are just
> gracefully handing control back to program that invoked you.  That is
> the cool of "EXIT PROGRAM".

Doing an EXIT PROGRAM instead of a STOP RUN just won't work (read the
manual, an EXIT PROGRAM in a program that was started from the command
line is a null operation), using GOBACK is an IBM mainframe thing and
my systems are not IBM.

In the places that I use STOP RUN a GOBACK would simply do exactly
what a STOP RUN does.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-03T13:21:52-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b40a0i$d75$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <dcedb8d0.0302270850.24c3ff03@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302281101.7bbf255@posting.google.com> <b3oepm$1hd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <16e2f010.0303022019.3f34d699@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0303031110.581ec12d@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard,
  *NOT* arguing with how your systems are desined  (and in fact, I think
this is a GOOD design approach), I will say that I have occassionaly written
programs that are (or might be) CALLed - but do a STOP RUN at the time of a
"particularly BAD exception condition".

What I have done in such cases is create an "error report" (this may be as
simple as a DISPLAY or as complex as writing out a "printed report") and
then issue a STOP run to "kill off" any programs that CALLed this module (up
to the "O/S" level).

Personally, I think a BETTER design is to "pass up the chain" some error
indicator and have the "main" program do all appropriate clean-up (including
issuing the errors) and then "close" at the top level.  HOWEVER, I have
worked in environments where I could neither "modify" nor even "see" what
happens at the top level program and that I simply "had to" kill off the
entire run-unit when my "sub-program" found some particularly unrecoverable
problem.  (Often a missing or corrupted file - sometimes a "lost" connection
to some other required resource)

As I do *normally* use GoBack in all my programs (mainframe and PC - with
IBM, Fujitsu, Realia, or Micro Focus - all of which have supported GoBack
for years), the "alternative" code for the Stop Run is usually in a "well
documented" paragraph that has a name similar to
"Kill-the-RunUnit-Because-the-world-has-ended"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-03T12:05:15-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b40chs$23hs$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <dcedb8d0.0302270850.24c3ff03@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302281101.7bbf255@posting.google.com> <b3oepm$1hd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <16e2f010.0303022019.3f34d699@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0303031110.581ec12d@posting.google.com> <b40a0i$d75$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:b40a0i$d75$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net...
> Richard,

> What I have done in such cases is create an "error report" (this may be as
> simple as a DISPLAY or as complex as writing out a "printed report") and
> then issue a STOP run to "kill off" any programs that CALLed this module
(up
> to the "O/S" level).

In my view that is an excellent illustration of a valid use of STOP RUN in a
CALLed program.  The STOP RUN specifically and explicitly takes everything
in the run unit (both above and below in the "CALL hierarchy) down, as
distinct from simply leaving the current program.  Whether you find the
feature useful is largely a matter of style and operating environment, I
think, but the feature does have a purpose in standard COBOL.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

Along the same lines, given COBOL File/DB Table errors or missing data etc., I
want my classes to go back (No - not GOBACK) to the Menu and still let users
pursue other Menu choices which will work - given that they don't find a
complete failure in the database. Then a 'Quit' selection from the Menu does the
closedown using STOP RUN.

Now the following is more in the vein of Goofy (moi), slipped up. I created a
small class with the syntax to create collections and dictionaries, passing
parameters. First time I did something slightly different - weirdo errors, "114
Beyond Limit" or "Invalid Object". So now I'm left puzzled. Found the error in
that small class. Why continue ? I don't even bother with a fancy Windows
message box - simple ANSI (DOS) display and STOP RUN. Agreed this is a
programmer/test error rather than an 'operational error' - but why bother trying
to get back to the menu to do the STOP RUN.

> Personally, I think a BETTER design is to "pass up the chain" some error"
> indicator and have the "main" program do all appropriate clean-up (including
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

"Pass up the chain some error indicator....". I PROMISE - you give me a neat way
of doing that with OO and I'll put your picture in a frame on the mantelpiece
and burn candles in front of it <G>.

On that one, just have to see what the implications of the COBOL 2002 additional
Declarative, Exception Conditions etc., actually allow us to do - when there's
an appropriate compiler available..

Jimmy, Calgary AB

until it hits one of my breaks, where the user's display is replaced by the source for
#3.  Then I can step through the code, line by line, interrogating every field that is
applicable including objects. Does F/J Debugger function in a similar manner ?

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T01:03:27+00:00

- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <dcedb8d0.0302270850.24c3ff03@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302281101.7bbf255@posting.google.com> <b3oepm$1hd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <16e2f010.0303022019.3f34d699@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0303031110.581ec12d@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>want to CALL them.

I cannot tell you how many times I've seen in a deeply nested program: 

open filename
if  status-code not equal to zero
    display 'open error ' status-code ' on foo'
    stop run
end-if

That's like a file clerk declaring the company bankrupt because of a jammed
handle on a file drawer. 

Joe is arguing for critical decisions to be made at a high 'executive' level
rather than by low-level workers.

>The programs that are CALLed cannot usefully be started from a command
>line, they would be missing all the interfacing data that is required
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>set up.

There is an example of how a 'command line' program could be called. 

Most of my systems have a 'qa suite' of transactions which can be processed in
batch mode. Before installing my newest and greatest in production, I run the
'qa suite' and compare its output to previously validated results. Now, suppose
I have ten such systems with cross-coupling, meaning the same functions are
called by several. I decide to automate the qa test and run it every night. If I
did it with a make 'script', no problem. Let's say make doesn't provide the
decision-making I need, so I write it myself in COBOL. Now the formerly 'main'
programs are being called. 

>My systems are not like your systems, my environment is not like your
>environment.  Making up spurious and ficticious arguments about how
>you think my systems might operate just doesn't work.

Perhaps it's just 'a question of style'. Bad programmers use the phrase
routinely do defend their mediocrity .. with your ascent. It seems fair to be as
non-judgmental when we use it to defend good style. Of course, this isn't about
fairness, it's about defending the Old Guard. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> arguments.

Now I could take that as being said literally (I, me = RW), but I don't 
think that you would have noticed these failing in yourself.

I could quite believe that you intended it as being sarcastic mimicry.

Of course you have used GOBACK for decades, and now you cannot imagine how 
anyone could write a system that wasn't exactly like yours.  

You have seen a bad programmer do a STOP RUN in the error routine of a 
CALLed program.

Therefore if _I_ use STOP RUN then I _must_ be doing it in CALLed programs 
and _must_ be like that bad programmer.

If only I could believe High Priest Roger Wagner's 'revealed truth' and 
pray at the alter of GOBACK then I will be clensed of my sins.

Your conclusions about how I use STOP RUN and EXIT PROGRAM are entirely 
flawed.  You arguments about using GOBACK are not 'cogent' but are purely 
an emotional response to your attachment to GOBACK.

If you do actually find someone who uses STOP RUN in the way you example 
then go an preach to _them_.
```

standards. Later, in the '90s, I learned that Micro Focus and Fujitsu had
GOBACK.

Robert

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T16:30:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b42kbf$hd8$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <dcedb8d0.0302270850.24c3ff03@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302281101.7bbf255@posting.google.com> <b3oepm$1hd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <16e2f010.0303022019.3f34d699@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0303031110.581ec12d@posting.google.com> <3e63f9e2.92613822@news.optonline.net>`

```

On  3-Mar-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> I cannot tell you how many times I've seen in a deeply nested program:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> end-if

What's worse is a program that is now obsolete at our shop that was written by
Pinnacle and purchased as part of a package by us.  This program was used to
handle all KSDS VSAM access and had:

IF READ-TYPE = 'U'
    OPEN INPUT RND-FILE
  ELSE
    OPEN I-O RND-FILE.
MOVE 'Y' TO OPEN-SW.
IF FILE-STATUS NOT = '00'
    MOVE '7' TO RTN-CODE.
GO TO EXIT-THIS-PGM.

When the status code was '97' (which is a successful open), the operators had no
way of knowing that they simply needed to restart.  If they had let it simply
abort the way yours did, (well, make sure it gives them an abort return code),
they could have checked the instructions for this job and restarted.   (The
called program doesn't know what to do with a '7', so it did an abort - without
telling anybody what was wrong with the open - because it didn't know).

Because this program was used by lots of calling programs, it would require
tremendous amount of work to get a simple change tested and signed off from the
users, so we just lived with it.

This is one reason why mainframers are not sold on re-usability.  We have tough
standards for user acceptance testing of everything a component touches.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T00:02:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e653ea6.175766225@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <dcedb8d0.0302270850.24c3ff03@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302281101.7bbf255@posting.google.com> <b3oepm$1hd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <16e2f010.0303022019.3f34d699@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0303031110.581ec12d@posting.google.com> <3e63f9e2.92613822@news.optonline.net> <b42kbf$hd8$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>Because this program was used by lots of calling programs, it would require
>tremendous amount of work to get a simple change tested and signed off from the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>standards for user acceptance testing of everything a component touches.

You have inappropriate and counterproductive, not "tough" standards. I've
encountered this problem at many mainframe shops (but never at Unix shops
handling just as much volume). Management wants you to test every application
that touches the utility. This discourages reusable code, universally considered
a _good_ thing, by punishing you for using it. I can think of two responses:
one correct and the other diplomatic. 

The correct answer is have them read an elementary Statistics text. Where it
talks about sample size required to achieve the desired confidence interval, pay
particular attention to 'beta', the probability of a false negative. Parallel or
'regression' tests are called 'negative-finding' because there is no difference
between the before and after, assuming everything works. Beta is a function of
population size, typically millions of transactions, _and_ standard deviation of
the thing being tested. Most parallel tests do require a large number of
samples, much larger than positive-finding tests, but function calls are an
exception because their standard deviation is typically low. A test harness and
one or two application is all that's required. I could go into detail about how
to compute standard deviation for software components and beta, but nobody would
believe a HS dropout posting on the internet. 

Of course, asking them to do their job in a professional manner would be
regarded as an insult. The diplomatic approach would point out that all this
butt-covering testing is intended to produce more reliable systems. Systems that
don't embarrass the manager by crashing in the night. Discouraging reusable code
has the opposite effect. It makes systems _more_ vulnerable to crashes,
inconsistancies and problems. Play on the fear factor. Then mention that
reusable code would save the company x dollars. Every manager likes to take
credit for saving money. 

One question. I believe you said you were toying with OO COBOL. Why? The
advantage of OO is reusability. If management says you cannot use classes in
other applications, if you must reinvent the wheel, what's the point?

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T15:26:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b454uk$luq$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <dcedb8d0.0302270850.24c3ff03@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302281101.7bbf255@posting.google.com> <b3oepm$1hd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <16e2f010.0303022019.3f34d699@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0303031110.581ec12d@posting.google.com> <3e63f9e2.92613822@news.optonline.net> <b42kbf$hd8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e653ea6.175766225@news.optonline.net>`

```

On  4-Mar-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> Of course, asking them to do their job in a professional manner would be
> regarded as an insult. The diplomatic approach would point out that all this
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> credit for saving money.

The other way is to not give them any choice.  This sometimes happens when
people change platforms and languages.

> One question. I believe you said you were toying with OO COBOL. Why? The
> advantage of OO is reusability. If management says you cannot use classes in
> other applications, if you must reinvent the wheel, what's the point?

There are other good reasons for OO.   OO makes it easy to interact with Java,
for instance.   There are business shops that use OO, but are very careful and
limiting about what they allow to be reused.

Of course, in my case, other reasons for toying with OO include because I enjoy
it, because it might be useful in my career, and because I want to continue to
be the person in my shop who knows CoBOL the best.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-03-03T18:10:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<16e2f010.0303031810.3147861e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <dcedb8d0.0302270850.24c3ff03@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302281101.7bbf255@posting.google.com> <b3oepm$1hd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <16e2f010.0303022019.3f34d699@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0303031110.581ec12d@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote in message news:<217e491a.0303031110.581ec12d@posting.google.com>...

> I have no idea why you have this strange idea that any of my programs
> would ever do a STOP RUN after being CALLed.  The few small programs
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
><snip>

I don't have any idea about your systems.  I only started this with
generalities about "STOP RUN" being like a full-stop for an entire
program.  The whole program-a, program-b, program-c thing was intended
to be a general example.

 
> My systems are not like your systems, my environment is not like your
> environment.  Making up spurious and ficticious arguments about how
> you think my systems might operate just doesn't work.

I'm not talking about your systems.  I'm talking about general design
rules for well behaved programs.  As a normal case I prefer to
terminate programs with the kinder gentler method of a
return-to-caller instead of a absolute tear-down-envorinment method. 
Call it BR 14 -vs- SVC 3 if you want to take the personal component
out of it, but thats all I've been saying.

When I make a statement like "I don't know why you would ever want to
use STOP RUN" you shouldn't read that as "I think you personally write
problem systems."  I am using "YOU" in the general sense, Cobol
programmers at large, if you will.  This is a newsgroup, not an email
discussion, it is read by many and posted to by many.  I'm not
targerting you specifically, such a statement should be read as "Why
would you, Joe CobolProgrammer, use a STOP RUN, a statement with such
problems when other alternatives are available"


> > The useful abstraction is not knowing if you are going drive a stake
> > through the virtual heart of your process, or if you are just
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> what a STOP RUN does.

That may well be the case -- as I have said, I am mainframe focused --
if I found myself in such an environment, I think I would need to
simulate GOBACK with the line:

    EXIT PROGRAM STOP RUN.

(or maybe "EXIT PROGRAM. STOP RUN.")

as the normal exit.

STOP RUN is very much a 'full-stop' for an entire run unit -- that is
my problem with it.  That behavior should be reserved for an
exceptional condition, it should not be a normal case.  This is one
place where IBM got it right.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T17:16:54+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b419fp$vav$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <16e2f010.0303022019.3f34d699@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0303031110.581ec12d@posting.google.com> <16e2f010.0303031810.3147861e@posting.google.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger wrote:

> I'm not talking about your systems.  I'm talking about general design
> rules for well behaved programs.  

General design rules may be quite different for different environments and 
different systems.

> As a normal case I prefer to
> terminate programs with the kinder gentler method of a
> return-to-caller instead of a absolute tear-down-envorinment method.

eg if the program is a CGI then the Web Server (Apache for example) will 
set the environment variables, start the process and will collect from 
stdout until the process terminates.  This is done by the program issuing a 
STOP RUN.  There isn't any mechanism for the initial Cobol program doing a 
'return to caller', nor should there be.

(well OK there are ways for the program to effectively return to the server 
and await another message without stopping the run-unit, but it isn't EXIT 
PROGRAM _or_ GOBACK).

> Call it BR 14 -vs- SVC 3 if you want to take the personal component
> out of it, but thats all I've been saying.

I have no idea what those are, they certainly aren't anything to do with my 
systems.
 
> "Why
> would you, Joe CobolProgrammer, use a STOP RUN, a statement with such
> problems when other alternatives are available"

There are no 'alternatives' available in my environments.  While that IBM 
thingie 'GOBACK' may be an alternate way of writing 'STOP RUN', it does 
exactly the same thing as I write STOP RUN to do on my environments.

STOP RUN may be 'such problems' where you are, but it does _exactly_ what I 
want at the points that I use it, and no problems at all.
 
Now GOBACK may also be an alternate way of writing EXIT PROGRAM, but when I 
write a either a STOP RUN or EXIT PROGRAM it would never be the other.

The vast majority of my programs do an EXIT PROGRAM and would never be 
anything other than CALLed from another program.

A few small program do STOP RUN and that is exactly what is required.
 
> STOP RUN is very much a 'full-stop' for an entire run unit -- that is
> my problem with it.  That behavior should be reserved for an
> exceptional condition, it should not be a normal case.  This is one
> place where IBM got it right.

IBM 'got it right' for IBM environments.  Other environments are not IBM 
environments.  If you write for IBM environments then please do use GOBACK 
for every program because that is 'right' for your environment.

If I write for 'other' environments then I will use whatever is most 
suitable for those.  In all cases I find that I use STOP RUN when 
appropriate, and EXIT PROGRAM when appropriate, they aren't interchangable.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

Apparently someone had managed to bring down his httpd through some
careless code.

Still -- I know where you are coming from.  The environment does
indeed dictate many things.

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message news:<b42kfm$hdm$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...

> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> more obvious manner.  Sometimes "kinder gentler" isn't appropriate.

I whole-heartedly agree  --  if you need to abort it ought to be loud.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-03-03T11:35:36-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0303031135.1dae071a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <dcedb8d0.0302270850.24c3ff03@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302281101.7bbf255@posting.google.com> <b3oepm$1hd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <16e2f010.0303022019.3f34d699@posting.google.com>`

```
psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote

> The original start of this
> thread was discussing the BEHAVIOR of STOP RUN as opposed to the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> make things clearer.

Actually, it is not an issue about 'being pedantic'.

STOP RUN and EXIT PROGRAM are _NOT_ functionally identical and not
interchangable.  You are confused because GOBACK can be used instead
of both, but GOBACK will do the completely different work of either
through testing which to do:

        GOBACK-paragraph.
           
            IF ( program-called )
                EXIT PROGRAM
            ELSE
                STOP RUN
            END-IF
            .

> The difference is subtle, but important.  

The difference is _not_ subtle at all.  You have built a mental image
of GOBACK as doing one thing, or the same thing wherever it is used
and think that there are only 'subtle differences'.  I know that
GOBACK does two completely different things that are quite unrelated
to each other and chooses between these based on how the program was
started.

A STOP RUN will close all remaining open file, will CANCEL all
remaining CALLed programs, and even all programs in the run-unit, and
will finally stop the run time.

EXIT PROGRAM does none of that.  If the program has open files then
they stay open, W-S data is left in last used state (unless INITIAL)
and the program may be CALLed again to continue.

It would require the CALLing program to CANCEL the program to make it
similar to a 'local STOP RUN'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods - just my personal opinion

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-03T13:55:49-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b40c07$jp6$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b363dg$tj2$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <dcedb8d0.0302270850.24c3ff03@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0302272203.7c4d5399@posting.google.com> <dcedb8d0.0302281101.7bbf255@posting.google.com> <b3oepm$1hd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <16e2f010.0303022019.3f34d699@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0303031135.1dae071a@posting.google.com>`

```
"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:217e491a.0303031135.1dae071a@posting.google.com...
> psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote
>
 <snip>
>
> The difference is _not_ subtle at all.  You have built a mental image
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> similar to a 'local STOP RUN'.

Furthermore, the behavior of EXIT PROGRAM (but *not* STOP RUN) is totally
dependent upon the "nature" of the program as it is executed (not as it is
compiled).

An EXIT PROGRAM in a "main" program is defined (in the '85 Standard) as
doing EXACTLY the same thing as a "CONTINUE" - i.e. "nothing" but pass
control on to the next (logical) statement in the SAME program.  On the
other hand, a STOP RUN in a CALLed program *still* closes down the entire
run-unit.  If this is NOT what is intended, then you are "in trouble".
Similarly, if one issues a GoBack (in those environments where it is
supported) in a CALLed program and one really WANTS to do a "Stop Run" -
then one is also "in trouble".
```

- **References:** `<b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <b3ghes$j3f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5c8aca.5244651@news.optonline.net> <b3j3b2$93n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5daafb.30914451@news.optonline.net> <b3kuke$cad$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5ea9fa.96202831@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302281506.2549b980@posting.google.com> <3e601550.189230888@news.optonline.net>`

```
On Sat, 01 Mar 2003 02:12:17 GMT, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
enlightened us:

>riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>good Shakespeare quote about "hung from his own petard."

"Hung from his own petard"??????  Shakespeare must be rolling over in
his grave at your use of the word hung with petard, a particularly
impossible action.  Much like your "Best Practices".  Here, for your
enlightenment:

The French used pï¿½tard, "a loud discharge of intestinal gas," for a
kind of infernal engine for blasting through the gates of a city. "To
be hoist by one's own petard," a now proverbial phrase apparently
originating with Shakespeare's Hamlet (around 1604) not long after the
word entered English (around 1598), means "to blow oneself up with
one's own bomb, be undone by one's own devices." The French noun pet,
"fart," developed regularly from the Latin noun pditum, from the
Indo-European root *pezd-, "fart."

And before you attempt to squirm your way out of this obvious gaffe,
hung and hoist in this context are not the same thing.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

"Going to war without France is like going deer hunting 
without your accordian."  - Donald Rumsfeld / US Secretary of Defense
 ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve

<snip>

Interesting this, because it is missing one other item. I 
recollect a petard as being a tool used with artillery, 
specifically a canon. After firing a canon, one runs a wet mop 
down the muzzle to the firing chamber. After this one puts the 
charge into the mouth of the canon and then rams it home with a 
"petard." If the wet mop wasn't really wet, and there was still 
something hot enough to ignite the charge...

Another meaning of "hoist by their own petard".

Later,
Steve.T

Not quite.  In terms of an artillery piece like one from the English
Civil war era, the petard is an explosive, bell-shaped device which
had to be fixed manually against a gate or even a wall to blow a hole
through it; the petardier, even if he survived he attentions of the
garrison, risked being killed by the explosion, or 'hoist with his own
petard'. Before the petardier George Cranage blew down the door of
Oswestry Castle with his petard, he had to be 'well lined sacke'
before he would attempt it.

canons by the 1860s could be considered similar to the petard of

do so would have it behave as a petard in the English Civil war case.

minds of such a thing...

My Oxford Shorter English Dictionary gives the following two
definitions for petard.

1 A small bomb made of a metal or wooden box filled with powder, used
to blow in a door etc. or to make a hole in a wall.

2 A kind of firework that explodes with a sharp report, a cracker.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standard names (was: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-01T01:47:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e6010b3.188049784@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <b3ghes$j3f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5c8aca.5244651@news.optonline.net> <b3j3b2$93n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5daafb.30914451@news.optonline.net> <b3ljas$109r$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jtqcnThLHobySMOjXTWcpw@comcast.com> <3E5EE6CD.E0917083@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

>Question - how important is portable code, particularly as the majority here
are
>probably mainframers ?

>- Contractors - like DD and I think Skippy - do they deliberately write their
>code as 'portable' so that they can take it with them to another installation.
>Again legally the contractor owns the code not the contractee.

Putting aside the question of legal ownership, think about your portable brain.
I move to a new compiler once a year (the IRS mandates it, under per diem). I
want to have one mental model of how COBOL works everywhere. If a feature works
differently between compilers, I discard it from my repertiore. If it simply
doesn't work on one platform, I stop using it on all under the logical principle
"if one dog has spots, all dogs have spots."

I've learned that the functions which work on all platforms are all you really
need anyway. They are if, evaluate, call, move, perform, compute, search and
goback. 

>- COBOL Libraries - we just don't have them, (bearing in mind the utitlities
>available in Java, C++ etc., are one of their strengths - avoids re-inventing
>the wheel).

If you have their library on your machine, call their functions. Think outside
the COBOL box. 

In theory, you can do MFC or COM as easily as they can. In practice, you cannot
because they are passing objects for which you don't know the internal
structure. Given enough time, you could figure it out by reverse-engineering.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<01c2dfa3$b4d1b220$6395f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <b3ghes$j3f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5c8aca.5244651@news.optonline.net> <b3j3b2$93n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5daafb.30914451@news.optonline.net> <b3ljas$109r$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jtqcnThLHobySMOjXTWcpw@comcast.com> <3E5EE6CD.E0917083@shaw.ca> <3e6010b3.188049784@news.optonline.net>`

```
If this blurb from the author of Microsoft .NET for COBOL programmers is
correct then we can have access to some powerful libraries right now.

From the Author
Microsoft's .NET Environment represents the single most substantial impact
upon COBOL since the advent of IBM's ISPF in the 1970's. Not only does it
provide an ultra-modern development environment in Visual Studio .NET,
which COBOL now plugs into, but it provides in excess of 5,000 callable
classes and API's that COBOL developers have direct, unfiltered,
non-wrappered access to. COBOL is now on a level playing field with C++, C#
and Visual Basic, as COBOL developers use the identical screen painters and
development environment for developing GUI Windows applications as well as
ASPX Web Form applications, and may even develop Web Services in COBOL.
Legacy programs can be brought into this exciting new environment, allowing
COBOL to fully participate on the PC, on the Network and on the Web. This
book includes approximately 23 hours of video based training on 2 CD's. It
is not fluff - it is real programming, using these new technologies and
includes numerous sample COBOL programs to help get programmers started.
But more importantly, is explains the concepts from the ground up, so that
COBOL programmers who are unfamiliar with this new environment may learn
how to use it quickly and efficiently. I truly hope that COBOL programmers
everywhere will find this extremely useful in programming for the future. 
-Howard E. Hinman

Well that is my fault as this is just some text I copied from
www.amazon.com.  I didn't copy it all because I did not want  to be accused
of pushing a commercial product, especially one that locks you in to M$.

I just decided to order it but have not received it. I think it is more CD
than book.  The book part is only 62 pages and the CD part is "23 hours of
video".  The price is $49.95.  It is published by Fujitsu. IMO the price of
the compiler is too high for someone who is just looking to learn and keep
up.  I personally don't see how I could recoup the cost.  Perhaps after I
retire I may become a consultant and will be able to justify it then.

PS. be careful when searching amazon for ".net for cobol" as you will get 2
hits.  The reviews for the other product are not as good as for this one
and people who bought both said only buy this one.  That doesn't mean it is
true or that they are correct, just that you should look into it and find
out as much as you can before deciding.

```


Robert Wagner wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> I move to a new compiler once a year (the IRS mandates it, under per diem).

Probably by no means the exception, but I would suggest not the norm for the
*majority* of COBOL programmers.

> I
> want to have one mental model of how COBOL works everywhere. If a feature works
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

So totally ignore any coding in OO - because it only got the imprimatur in December
2002 ? But there again, mine wont work on IBM. Unisys, Acu, Realia etc., etc.... So
just don't use it ?.

> >- COBOL Libraries - we just don't have them, (bearing in mind the utitlities
> >available in Java, C++ etc., are one of their strengths - avoids re-inventing
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> structure. Given enough time, you could figure it out by reverse-engineering.

Ahhhh. As one learned gentlemen wrote a while back, (from memory)  :-

FACT - you can do it in Java !
FACT - you can do it in COBOL !
FACT - you can do it with COBOL and Java !

1. COBOL is good
2. Java is good
3. Java is sometimes better than COBOL
4. COBOL is sometimes better than Java

Trouble was he didn't support 3 or 4 above with examples - so as they stand they are
meaningless !!!!

If and when I need to get into the Web - which I *seriously* doubt, (the application
being numeric data intensive input ( 10 x 24 table) - go figure doing that with a
Web page/form), then yes, I can invoke Java support classes from Net Express.

Meanwhile - that 10 x 24 table above - initial suggestion from M/F at Newbury - use
Activex. As a beginner I was desperately trying to figure objects and GUIs; I really
didn't need another problem at that stage comprehending what was involved using
Activex. Solution - it has been created from N/E GUIs doing dynamic creation of the
entryfields in the table - the whole shebang in COBOL.

If I did switch - and he will 'love' me for saying it - I'd switch to Bob's SP2 <G>

I'm not ignoring Java - I'll get around to it if I have a need - i.e., if I get
stumped and it just can't be done in COBOL. N/E does a very nice job, thank you, in
providing support classes. The point I was making in my original message was a lack
of "common" (J4) standard classes as yet in *any* OO COBOL compiler - weakens a
perception of what can be done in COBOL by the whiz kids using the language du jour.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standard names (was: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-01T05:07:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E603DF8.9D03CCAF@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <b3ghes$j3f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5c8aca.5244651@news.optonline.net> <b3j3b2$93n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5daafb.30914451@news.optonline.net> <b3ljas$109r$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jtqcnThLHobySMOjXTWcpw@comcast.com> <3E5EE6CD.E0917083@shaw.ca> <3e6010b3.188049784@news.optonline.net> <01c2dfab$9f1d62a0$9dccf943@chottel>`

```


Charles Hottel wrote:

> Regarding reverse engineering, Dr. Young S. Kim is working on the Win32API
> for COBOL. See his web site at: www.kimsoft.com/ and also he has a series
> of articles at www.aboutlegacycodind.com/

Charles,

I hope I succeed, but I have to try and be as diplomatic as possible on this
one.(And I think you already have a copy of Will Price's book on OO).

I have conversed briefly with Young, who is an extremely likeable and
knowledgeable man. If you refer to cobolreport.com and legacy.com - they both
contain his articles.

In the cobolreport.com articles he demonstrates using Net Express GUIs. As a
result of a comment he made, I asked why the switch to APIs, (which I might add
have been successfully used for years by Ken Mullins). Ken  uses the N/E Dialog
Editor that Calin, Young, I and others have used. Young's  basic logic - it
gets back to APIs - so why not use them rather than N/E GUIs.

Our friend Robert used the phrase to me, 'Think outside the box....". Sorry,
but Young didn't. His cobolreport.com articles show a 'cut and paste technique'
for GUIs based on the minimal demo examples provided by M/F. Having switched a
legacy application, he found the cutting and pasting bloody tedious - too damn
right - it is.

But that's not what OO is about, GUIs or otherwise. How do you play the violin
to get the sweetest tune - and REUSABILITY. I took that same Dialog Editor and
created a separate sub-class containing parameters for a specific screen - no
cutting and pasting - I use instances of ONE COMMON class that handles all my
GUIs.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standard names (was: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-03-01T16:00:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c2e00b$8dd4f0c0$95c2f943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <b3ghes$j3f$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5c8aca.5244651@news.optonline.net> <b3j3b2$93n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5daafb.30914451@news.optonline.net> <b3ljas$109r$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <jtqcnThLHobySMOjXTWcpw@comcast.com> <3E5EE6CD.E0917083@shaw.ca> <3e6010b3.188049784@news.optonline.net> <01c2dfab$9f1d62a0$9dccf943@chottel> <3E603DF8.9D03CCAF@shaw.ca>`

```
You were diplomatic and you did do well.  I appreciated it as the level of
attacks on this newsgroup have dramatically increased lately. IMO we all
come here with a common interest in COBOL and there are too many divisive
statements lately that attempt to divide us into groups and set us against
each other i.e. pc vs mainframe.

I will defer to your greater experience on this one.  I am just a mainframe
COBOL programmer trying to climb the learning curve on PCs and OO COBOL etc
and trying in general to keep up.  This is done by trying out things when I
can afford to purchase the products.  I personally have not be able to
justify purchasing an up-to date COBOL compiler so most if not all of my
learning in that area has been via reading. As we all know there is a great
difference between reading and thinking you know something and actually
doing it. Perhaps I get too excited whenever I learn that we COBOLers can
access any of the newer technologies.  Actually I need to upgrade to a
newer PC as I don't have the disk space to
install even the demo versions that come with most of the books.

I appreciate your posts on OO COBOL and on this and I think you are right
as far as thinking outside the box.  His web site indictes he is following
the book Microsoft Windows 2000 API Super Bible and translating from Visual
Basic to COBOL.  I think it is nice to have the APIs in your tool box, but
I am sure you are right about the other approaches which you have direct
experience with. Thanks.


James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in article
<3E603DF8.9D03CCAF@shaw.ca>...
> 
> 
> Charles Hottel wrote:
> 
> > Regarding reverse engineering, Dr. Young S. Kim is working on the
Win32API
> > for COBOL. See his web site at: www.kimsoft.com/ and also he has a
series
> > of articles at www.aboutlegacycodind.com/
> 
> Charles,
> 
> I hope I succeed, but I have to try and be as diplomatic as possible on
this
> one.(And I think you already have a copy of Will Price's book on OO).
> 
> I have conversed briefly with Young, who is an extremely likeable and
> knowledgeable man. If you refer to cobolreport.com and legacy.com - they
both
> contain his articles.
> 
> In the cobolreport.com articles he demonstrates using Net Express GUIs.
As a
> result of a comment he made, I asked why the switch to APIs, (which I
might add
> have been successfully used for years by Ken Mullins). Ken  uses the N/E
Dialog
> Editor that Calin, Young, I and others have used. Young's  basic logic -
it
> gets back to APIs - so why not use them rather than N/E GUIs.
> 
> Our friend Robert used the phrase to me, 'Think outside the box....".
Sorry,
> but Young didn't. His cobolreport.com articles show a 'cut and paste
technique'
> for GUIs based on the minimal demo examples provided by M/F. Having
switched a
> legacy application, he found the cutting and pasting bloody tedious - too
damn
> right - it is.
> 
> But that's not what OO is about, GUIs or otherwise. How do you play the
violin
> to get the sweetest tune - and REUSABILITY. I took that same Dialog
Editor and
> created a separate sub-class containing parameters for a specific screen
- no
> cutting and pasting - I use instances of ONE COMMON class that handles
all my
> GUIs.
> 
> Jimmy, Calgary AB
> 
>
```

```


Charles Hottel wrote:

> You were diplomatic and you did do well.  I appreciated it as the level of
> attacks on this newsgroup have dramatically increased lately. IMO we all
> come here with a common interest in COBOL and there are too many divisive
> statements lately that attempt to divide us into groups and set us against
> each other i.e. pc vs mainframe.

<snip>

One point I failed to make in my previous. There is nothing wrong with APIs, as
indicated by Ken's usage. But as Ken also pointed out to somebody else who made
the mistake, APIs are NOT "OO" - you don't use 'messages' to send/receive
results from APIs. And Young's original intent was to illustrate OO.

Check out his OO articles in Legacy - some mainframers were less than kind in
their comments.

Jimmy
```

```
I guess I would like to mention that when I think of the "Windows API" I am
NOT just thinking of GUI.  I notice on this newsgroup that sometimes the
recommended solution to a problem is a CALL to the API. From that I assumed
that knowledge of the API, how to define the variables properly in COBOL
and how to make the CALL, and set up callbacks might be things that are
useful to know.

I saw in Thane Hubbells book how to use SP2 and that certainly beats using
the API for GUI.

On 28 Feb 2003 22:12:49 -0500, docdwarf@panix.com enlightened us:

>In article <3E5EE6CD.E0917083@shaw.ca>, James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
