Point 1.  I don't know if I mentioned this to James before or not, but
some ISPs and news services (like mine--Airnews) block anything that
comes from shaw.ca because they are a haven for spammers and do little
to curb abuse on their system.  The point being, I can't see James'
posts unless someone answers him and includes it.  

Point 2.  I'm not a contractor.  I am an employee who is more or less
a hired gun that goes anywhere in the world my company thinks I'm
needed to work on projects requiring my application knowledge and
assembler and COBOL skills.  I used to go do 1 and 2 year projects
just about anywhere.  Anymore, I've become more of a homebody
preferring to work from home and reach out and touch my clients.  Not
as much fun as traveling and living in other countries but my family
likes the stability.

Point 3.  I do have my own private library of programs and routines
that I have written over the years to solve various problems or
perform interfaces to other applications and/or systems.  Not wanting
to reinvent the wheel, I'll look at my collection if a particular
project/problem sounds familiar.  I'll use not the exact same code
because it rarely does everything it needs to do at that particular
moment, but the concept and logic of the code and change as necessary.
It saves time and headaches.

>Actually... I try to write code as close to my client's standards as 
>possible; if I'm doing something that I can't find an example for then I 
…[83 more quoted lines elided]…
>DD

Would not an EVALUATE be clearer or is that a sin to use in your shop?

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

"Going to war without France is like going deer hunting 
without your accordian."  - Donald Rumsfeld / US Secretary of Defense
 ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Steve

>>01  WS-MISC.

>Would not an EVALUATE be clearer or is that a sin to use in your shop?

'Clarity' is in the mind of the beholder, of course... were you thinking
of a page-'o-code EVALUATE like (using the same variable-names for
convenience here)

EVALUATE INPUT-PROV-CD(1:1)
    WHEN 'A'
        MOVE 01 TO WS-SUB1
    WHEN 'B'
        MOVE 02 TO WS-SUB1
...
    WHEN 'Z'
        MOVE 26 TO WS-SUB1
END-EVALUATE.
STRING WS-SUB1, INPUT-PROV-CD(2:4)
 DELIMITED BY SIZE INTO WS-PROV-CD
END-STRING.

... etc. ... or did you have something else in mind?

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-01T01:47:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e5fff3f.183580935@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <3e5c78bd.623393@news.optonline.net> <b3ip48$23v4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5d3cff.2754448@news.optonline.net> <b3jkv2$2mte$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5e9600.91088633@news.optonline.net> <qDy7a.3708$Uy4.295135@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3e5f4dfc.138195421@news.optonline.net> <b3nrn7$d2v$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>Two specific examples of where USAGE INDEX items *could* fulfill a purpose
>that INDEXED BY items would not.  (BOTH of these *could* be met by
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>the "proper" value, it does retain its value in the CALL.
supported and indeed unsupportable.

Both are valid examples of index data names serving a function not provided by
indexes, but they are horribly Bad Practices. 

Storing an index in a file means other installations using other languages or
COBOL implementations will be unable to process your file. In the financial
services industry, where I work, we routinely exchange files with other
installations daily. Most of them are into C or Java cultures. 

Storing an index in a structure passed to another program is similarly flawed.
That program might be written in SmallTalk or C++ or whatever. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL indexes

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-01T02:30:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nXU7a.5414$Uy4.427077@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<2558420.1045840915@dbforums.com> <V7-dnUC7QI9aIsujXTWcjg@giganews.com> <3e5a4b8a.526004@news.optonline.net> <b3ducv$gmj$1@nntp2-cm.news.eni.net> <3e5ac95f.19953401@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302251104.30d85097@posting.google.com> <3e5c78bd.623393@news.optonline.net> <b3ip48$23v4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5d3cff.2754448@news.optonline.net> <b3jkv2$2mte$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e5e9600.91088633@news.optonline.net> <qDy7a.3708$Uy4.295135@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3e5f4dfc.138195421@news.optonline.net> <b3nrn7$d2v$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3e5fff3f.183580935@news.optonline.net>`

```

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message news:3e5fff3f.183580935@news.optonline.net...
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
> 
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> indexes, but they are horribly Bad Practices.

Your opinion is based on irrelevant factors.

> Storing an index in a file means other installations using other languages or
> COBOL implementations will be unable to process your file. 

That would be an issue if the system design specifies external file interfaces,
but would be negated by storing a subscript value instead of an Index Data Item value.

That is not an issue if the system design does not include external interfaces.
None of which is relevant to the inclusion of Index Data Item functionality 
within COBOL per se, which is what you questioned the need for. 

>In the financial
> services industry, where I work, we routinely exchange files with other
> installations daily. Most of them are into C or Java cultures.

This is totally irrelevant to the context of the question you posed. 
> 
> Storing an index in a structure passed to another program is similarly flawed.
> That program might be written in SmallTalk or C++ or whatever.

This is also totally irrelevant to the context of the question you posed.
```

bills itself as portable.

Yes, because the value stored in an index data name is "implementor defined". My
PC and your mainframe may use different representations for the same value. We
have seen mention here of one platform that stored ordinals (subscripts) inside
index variables. 

>I could just as easily state that such usage is most common in in-house batch
systems
>developed by companies who have no intention of ever sharing those files
externally.

Until the company gets bought out, and programmers in the new parent try to read
its data.

"Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote:

>If you had the hands-on experience in coding a complex system or module
>which made use of Index Data Items, then you would be familiar and comfortable
>with the functionality and need for Index Data Items, and would not be
>wondering why they were introduced.

I've developed systems I _thought_ were complex. Evidently they weren't as
complex as yours. 

>If you possessed the deductive reasoning skills to figure out a use by yourself
>for Index Data Items, then you would be familiar and comfortable
>with the functionality and need for Index Data Items, and would not be
>wondering why they were introduced.

Deductive reasoning derives specific conclusions from general principles,
whereas inductive reasoning derives the general from specifics. Given that we
start with a functional description of index data names and try to figure out
what they are used for, shouldn't you have said inductive reasoning, Mr. Logic?
```

## Thread: Reading MF Files from Powercobol7 — 18 this month / 27 total

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reading MF Files from Powercobol7

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-01T01:47:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e600256.184372586@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2573793.1046215399@dbforums.com> <3e5cbe97.18507712@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302261142.3a10eded@posting.google.com> <3e5ead7e.97103717@news.optonline.net> <3E5ED99F.F42D96B0@shaw.ca> <b3mofi$3a$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>"In addition to the standard COBOL organizations, the following file types
>are also supported:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>- C-ISAM (UNIX and 32-bit OS/2)"

IDXFORMAT"4" is what I meant to say, rather than EXTFH. 

Whether a file is accessed via EXTFH vs. run-time code doesn't change its
format. 

Vanilla, default Micro Focus indexed files are formatted like ASCII text. They
can be read by utilities such as sort and text editors. Only files designated
IDXFORMAT"4" are in an ISAM-like format.

The other formats referenced above are foreign to MF. If a shop has 'MF format
files', they are either vanilla or IDXFORMAT"4".  In a typical Micro Focus shop,
all or a vast majority of indexed files will be the former, which means you
don't need conversion software, you can process them as flat or 'line
sequential' files. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reading MF Files from Powercobol7

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-01T03:45:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E602B0E.5A36DE21@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<2573793.1046215399@dbforums.com> <3e5cbe97.18507712@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302261142.3a10eded@posting.google.com> <3e5ead7e.97103717@news.optonline.net> <3E5ED99F.F42D96B0@shaw.ca> <b3mofi$3a$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3e600256.184372586@news.optonline.net>`

```


Robert Wagner wrote:

> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> Robert

Robert,

Here you go again. I have always used the D-E-F-A-U-L-T format without any prior
knowledge of what that is - and checking the Net Express on-line help - this tells
me that the Default *IS* :-

The IDXFORMAT parameter specifies the format to use when creating indexed files.

IDXFORMAT=integer

Default: 3

0 Default format for the operating system (same as 3 on Windows)
1 C-ISAM
3 Micro Focus default indexed file format
4 Optimized format for fast duplicate key handling
5 Btrieve with ANSI conformance
6 Btrieve without ANSI conformance
8 Large indexed file format

And that being the case - there is no way I can view ISAMs as Ascii Text !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reading MF Files from Powercobol7

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-01T18:32:16+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3pgo7$g1r$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<2573793.1046215399@dbforums.com> <3E5ED99F.F42D96B0@shaw.ca> <b3mofi$3a$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3e600256.184372586@news.optonline.net> <3E602B0E.5A36DE21@shaw.ca>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
 
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> format.

You have already been told, and you can confirm it by reading the manual, 
that _ALL_ _indexed_ files on DOS, Windows and OS/2 whether 16 or 32 bit 
since Cobol/2 have used EXTFH to access the files of the above type. If you 
read the manual, or even WMKs extract, you will that it tells you that code 
is generated by the compiler to call the EXTFH module.

For example in the archaic version 2.5 Toolset Reference manual (p 3-2):

"The file handler [EXTFH] is called by the run-time system to handle I/O 
operations on all files with organization indexed."

There are a few cases where sequential and relative files may use the 'run 
time', but indexed files use EXTFH.

As you have been told the IDXFORMAT controls the format that files are 
_created_.  The EXTFH module is able to create and use all the above 
formats (in all versions in the last decade).

> Vanilla, default Micro Focus indexed files are formatted like ASCII text.

No. WRONG. You have been told this before.

The default is IDXFORMAT'3'.  These are structured files with binary file 
and record headers and are nothing like 'ASCII text'. Go and read what it 
tells you in the manual.

If you have Level II (IDXFORMAT'2') files (by accident or design), while 
yours may superficially look like ASCII files, it has been explained to you 
why they cannot be treated as such, even if the design avoids binary or 
packed data, which it need not.

It may be that your compiling environment has a .DIR directives file that 
has IDXFORMAT'2' in it, go and ask someone in your site that has two clues 
to rub together to explain this to you.

> They can be read by utilities such as sort and text editors. 

Not in any useful way in the general case.

> Only files designated IDXFORMAT"4" are in an ISAM-like format.

No that is simply not true, it is a lie, it is false.  In fact it 
specifically states in the manual that IDXFORMAT'4' is "an optimised form 
of [IDXFORMAT'3'].  And IDXFORMAT'3' is the default (read the manual).

The actual differences between '3' and '4' are in the index file and not in 
the data file, in both cases this has headers with binary data for the file 
and for each record.

Older versions of MF (eg version 2.5 of 1991) did require that for the use 
of IDXFORMAT'4' an additional package be bought, but this was rolled into 
the normal EXTFH, probably around version 3.0.

What actual version are you using, how out of date are you ?

> The other formats referenced above are foreign to MF. If a shop has 'MF
> format files', they are either vanilla or IDXFORMAT"4".  

No The formats above are the ones that MF will create and maintain (in 
recent versions, certainly since 1994).  It is only necessary to specify 
the appropriate IDXFORMAT and this will be used when the file is created.

_NONE_ of them are 'vanilla'.

> In a typical Micro
> Focus shop, all or a vast majority of indexed files will be the former,

You have absolutely no idea about what 'typical MF shops' do.  Many of 
them, for example, will be using versions that are less than a dozen years 
old.

The default is _not_ Level II, it is a format that is a 'non-optimised' 
form of IDXFORMAT'4'.  
 
> which means you don't need conversion software, you can process them as
> flat .. files.

No you cannot, unless you are prepared to lose data and have junk:

          The file may have binary or packed data.
          Deleted records are left in the file and may 'reappear',
                or worse.
          Slack space at end of file will give junk.

And this is only if they are specifically Level II which is the closest to 
'ordinary' (though C-ISAM is much the same).

> or 'line sequential' files.

And that is possibly your worst piece of advice ever.  Even if they are 
Level II format any binary or packed data will cause records to be junked.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reading MF Files from Powercobol7

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWS0202@comcast.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-01T01:55:00-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Zg2dnau0rLnBxP2jXTWc3Q@comcast.com>`
- **References:** `<2573793.1046215399@dbforums.com> <3E5ED99F.F42D96B0@shaw.ca> <b3mofi$3a$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3e600256.184372586@news.optonline.net> <3E602B0E.5A36DE21@shaw.ca> <b3pgo7$g1r$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
> No that is simply not true, it is a lie, it is false.  In fact it
> specifically states in the manual that IDXFORMAT'4' is "an optimised form
> of [IDXFORMAT'3'].  And IDXFORMAT'3' is the default (read the manual).
>
> The actual differences between '3' and '4' are in the index file and not
in
> the data file, in both cases this has headers with binary data for the
file
> and for each record.
>

    Actually, the type 4 data files are slightly different from the
type 3 data files.  They have some extra information in the data
file.  The major difference is that the type 4 allows more than
65,535 records with the same exact value in an alternate key
that allows duplicate values.  It also is much faster when this
is true (Example - a phone number is placed in a key field, but
many people do not give you their phone number.

    Most of the data files in microfocus indexed files can be accessed
without using the index, but it is finicky work.  Use rebuild if you
can.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reading MF Files from Powercobol7

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-02T08:53:56+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3r380$ae3$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<2573793.1046215399@dbforums.com> <b3mofi$3a$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3e600256.184372586@news.optonline.net> <3E602B0E.5A36DE21@shaw.ca> <3e6088e0.218819360@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:


>> and checking the Net Express 
                    ^^^^^^^^^^^
>>Default: 3
>>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> file before conversion.

Now that you have revealed that you are talking about a different product 
entirely we can see the difficulty you are having.

This specifically states 'Net Express', others have stated 'DOS, Windows, 
OS/2'.  

Solaris has a default of C-ISAM (which is much the same as Level II).

MF Solaris is not Net Express. Solaris is not 'DOS, Windows or OS/2'. 

IDXFORMAT'3' files are _NOT_ 'ASCII text', they are very much like 
IDXFORMAT'4' - which you should know 

C-ISAM are not 'ASCII text' (though superficially they may look like it).

Indexed files have no restriction on having binary or packed data, and thus 
may not be at all like ASCII files.

At least you finally have noticed that deleted records are a problem, that 
is one clue you have grown.

You really can't understand that the world outside your office maight be 
different.  You had advised some one that they could just read the file 
into a sort or as a flat file.  You completely failed to notice that some 
conditions needed to be added:

     _IF_ you want to convert (not even implied)
     _IF_ you use Solaris (0.01% chance)
     _IF_ you use only DISPLAY ASCCI text in records.
     _IF_ you have reorganised
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reading MF Files from Powercobol7

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-01T23:28:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e613944.3185692@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2573793.1046215399@dbforums.com> <b3mofi$3a$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3e600256.184372586@news.optonline.net> <3E602B0E.5A36DE21@shaw.ca> <3e6088e0.218819360@news.optonline.net> <b3r380$ae3$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>>> and checking the Net Express 
>                    ^^^^^^^^^^^
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>OS/2'. 

The Micro Focus Solaris manual contains the same language. I thought my files
were format 3. 

>Solaris has a default of C-ISAM (which is much the same as Level II).

I did not know that. Thanks for the education. 

>C-ISAM are not 'ASCII text' (though superficially they may look like it).

If a file contains no packed/binary fields, the only difference is deleted
records. When viewed with a text editor, they look like non-deleted records. 

>At least you finally have noticed that deleted records are a problem, that 
>is one clue you have grown.

I said to reorganize the file to get rid of them before conversion.

>     _IF_ you use Solaris (0.01% chance)

It's very likely to be the same on other brands of Unix. Make that 3%. 

I spent a year writing MF COBOL on HP-UX, but didn't use indexed files there. It
was all database. 

<mention of database causes light bulb to come on>

ODBC! Why didn't I think of it first? Why didn't anyone else? That's the answer
to the original question, which was how to access MF files from Fujitsu. Micro
Focus offers an ODBC driver called Correlate. A Web search found at least six
other sources, such as:

http://www.parkway-software.com/sql/funktion_1.html

Another solution requires no additional software. Write an access program in
Micro Focus and 'call' it from Fujitsu. Trying to do this with a COBOL CALL
would be asking for trouble because Micro Focus requires an extensive run-time,
and Fujitsu has a small run-time as well. I'd run them as two tasks
communicating through a pipe. See API functions CreateNamedPipe and CreateFile
to get started. Two other unattractive approaches come to mind: shared memory
(high overhead) and passing the data through a dll, which is amateurish but does
have the attraction of an all-COBOL solution. IMO, pipe is the way to go .. if
you are comfortable talking to the Windows API. 

Richard (and others): don't ask me to post code samples showing how to call
Windows from MF and Fujitsu. It can be done with a little 'glue'.

Robert
```

news:3e61602d.13147988@news.optonline.net...
>> 
>> RAF conjures pictures of guys in white ascots.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Excluding yourself, of course?

Touched a nerve, did I? I disrespect flag-wavers who get their patriotism from
tabloid newspapers. How ignoble. How unsophisticated. I respect guys like you,
who actually did something. 

FWIW, the failure at Bay of Pigs was blamed on "bad intelligence". I know the
intelligence was correct because I wrote the 'bad' report. What did them in was
someone from our side leaked the plan to the bad guys (published it in Stern)
and the CIA's amateur 'air force' kept getting lost. But we (Recon) got blamed
for screwing up. Reading this newgroup caused me to recall other bum raps.  

Robert
```

themselves.  However, Ho Chi Minh did not forget the U.S. snub.  Also
remember that Ho Chi Minh and Vietnam provided much more intelligence
against Japan during WWII than almost anyone else in Southeast Asia.

SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote:

>On Sun, 02 Mar 2003 01:41:38 GMT, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
>enlightened us:

>>>An actual letter home from a marine with the multinational force in 
>>>Bosnia:

>I've seen this on the internet somewhere, but can't recall where.  Has
>your daughter (real or imagined) posted this elsewhere?

I didn't claim she was _my_ daughter. Someone forwarded it to me. As you say,
the letter is going around the internet. It doesn't rign true to my ear as an
actual letter, but does accurately portray the attitude of Marines. 

>A you wonder why they think Americans are "cowboys" .

Real cowboys in West Texas, where I lived for 20 years, are paragons of modesty
and civility .. 180 degrees opposite from the cocky, reckless image assigned to
the word. 

Robert
```

#### ↳ Re: Reading MF Files from Powercobol7

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

Carlos lages <member129@dbforums.com> wrote:

>
>I am trying to install Power 7 ( i have already use 6.1)
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>because Fujtisu dont name it.

Use an ODBC driver. You can get one from Micro Focus, where it is called
Correlate, or at least six other sources. The access language will be SQL.

Call an access module written in Micro Focus. See my posting to Richard for more
on this. 

Robert
```

### DB2 LIKE QUERY ON VARCHAR

- **From:** "JK" <karthik@nospam.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-01T03:45:54+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<S1W7a.47773$ep5.32730@nwrddc02.gnilink.net>`

```
Can i do a LIKE on VARCHAR field ? It works in Spufi but when i code it as a
cursor it doesn't work. I tried passing value say GST, %GST% and still it
doesn't work. My question is would a LIKE query be sucessful on a VARCHAR
field in batch mode ?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DB2 LIKE QUERY ON VARCHAR

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-02T22:07:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e627c65.85919477@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<S1W7a.47773$ep5.32730@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <fcd09c56.0303010009.7ecc8d8f@posting.google.com>`

```
>"JK" <karthik@nospam.net> wrote in message
news:<S1W7a.47773$ep5.32730@nwrddc02.gnilink.net>...
> Can i do a LIKE on VARCHAR field ? It works in Spufi but when i code it as a
> cursor it doesn't work. I tried passing value say GST, %GST% and still it
> doesn't work. My question is would a LIKE query be sucessful on a VARCHAR
> field in batch mode ?

Try describing the host variable as a variable-length string:

01  search-key.
     05  search-length    comp-5  pic s9(04).
     05  search-text.
          10  filler occurs 1 to 8000 depending on search-length pic x.

or this might work:

01  search-key usage is sql type is varchar(n).
```

preprocessor accepted your original SQL (or COBOL SQL is very different from
assembler SQL).

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-02T23:28:34+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1ct46vkl267jgc0idfmkrkrupfkn9h2hoj@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<S1W7a.47773$ep5.32730@nwrddc02.gnilink.net> <13p36vc24q5r4vcsn91vqcdh71niacr0l4@4ax.com> <o7t8a.25839$V42.23434@nwrddc03.gnilink.net> <f5p46vcjs4np061qgbnageocghl01ootaa@4ax.com> <abu8a.18267$ES3.9467@nwrddc04.gnilink.net>`

```
On Sun, 02 Mar 2003 20:53:26 GMT "JK" <karthik@nospam.net> wrote:

:>No errors in compile. I just get sql +100. But when i spufi the same SQL i
:>get a whole bunch of rows.

Is WS-COL-LIKE-NAME defined as a VARCHAR?

Is the length correct?

Do you have a trailing %?

I found that using _ without a trailing % did not match. For example, 

 LIKE 'ABCD' (note - no masking characters) matched 'ABCD       '
 LIKE 'A_CD' (with a masking character) did not
 LIKE 'A_CD %' did match  

What mask are you using?     

:>"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message

#### ↳ Re: Cobol Program (URGENT and IMPORTANT)

- **From:** yusufjee <member25738@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-01T06:06:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2591142.1046498777@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<2586396.1046409100@dbforums.com>`

```

IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID. Compute.

*===========================================
* This program computes the area of circle
* area of  rectangle
* and the area of triangle
*===========================================

ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.

DATA DIVISION.

WORKING STORAGE SECTION.

01 T-OR-C-OR-A PIC X.
01 CALCULATE-CIRCLE PIC 9(5)V99.
        03 CIRCLE PIC 9(5)V99.
        03 RADIUS PIC 9(5)V99.

01 CALCULATE-RECTANGLE PIC 9(5)V99.
        03 HEIGHT PIC 9(5)V99.
        03 WIDTH PIC 9(5)V99.

01 CALCULATE-TRIANGLE 9(5)V99.
        03 BASE PIC 9(5)V99.

01 REPEAT-OR-EXIT PIC X.


PROCEDURE DIVISION.

PROGRAM-BEGIN.


    PERFORM DISPLAY-MSG.
    PERFORM T-OR-C-OR-A
    PERFORM CALCULATE-CIRCLE.
    PERFORM CALCULATE-RECTANGLE.
    PERFORM CALCULATE-TRIANGLE.
    PERFORM REPEAT-OR-EXIT.

PROGRAM DONE.

    STOP RUN.

DISPLAY-MSG.
    "THIS PROGRAM COMPUTES THE AREA OF CIRCLE, RECTANGLE AND TRIANGLE.".

T-OR-C-OR-A.

    DISPLAY "TYPE T FOR TRIANGLE OR C FOR CIRCLE OR A FOR RECTANGLE".
    ACCEPT T-OR-C-OR-A.
    IF T-OR-C-OR-A = "T" OR T-OR-C-OR-A = "t"
        PERFORM CALCULATE-TRIANGLE
    ELSE IF T-OR-C-OR-A = "C" OR T-OR-C-OR-A = "c"
        PERFORM CALCULATE-CIRCLE
    ELSE IF T-OR-C-OR-A = "A" OR T-OR-C-OR-A = "a"
        PERFORM CALCULATE-RECTANGLE
    ELSE
        PERFORM PROGRAM-BEGIN.

CALCULATE-CIRCLE.

* PERFORMS CALCULATION OF THE CIRCLE BY GIVEN VALUES

    DISPLAY "ENTER THE RADIUS TO CALCULATE".
    ACCEPT RADIUS.

* THE FORMULA TO COMPUTE CIRCLE
    COMPUTE CIRCLE = pi * RADIUS ** 2.

DISPLAY-CIRCLE.

    DISPLAY "THE AREA OF CIRCLE IS: " CIRCLE.

CALCULATE-RECTANGLE.

* PERFORMS CALCULATION OF THE RECTANGLE BY GIVEN VALUES *

    DISPLAY "ENTER THE WIDTH".
    ACCEPT WIDTH.
    DISPLAY "ENTER THE HEIGHT".
    ACCEPT HEIGHT.

* FORMULA TO COMPUTE RECTANGLE

    COMPUTE RECTANGLE = WIDTH * HEIGHT.

DISPLAY-RECTANGLE.

    DISPLAY "THE AREA OF RECTANGLE IS: " RECTANGLE.

CALCULATE-TRIANGLE.

* PERFORMS CALCULATION OF THE TRIANGLE BY GIVEN VALUES *

    DISPLAY "ENTER THE BASE".
    ACCEPT BASE.
    DISPLAY "ENTER HEIGHT".
    ACCEPT HEIGHT.

* FORMULA TO CALCULATE TRIANGLE

    COMPUTE TRIANGLE = 1/2 * BASE * HEIGHT.

DISPLAY-TRIANGLE.

    DISPLAY "THE AREA OF TRIANGLE: " TRIANGLE.

REPEAT-OR-EXIT.

    DISPLAY "TYPE R TO REPEAT OR E TO EXIT".
    ACCEPT REPEAT-EXIT.
    IF REPEAT-EXIT = "R" OR REPEAT-EXIT = "r"
        PERFORM PROGRAM-BEGIN
    ELSE
        PERFORM PROGRAM-DONE.



so wats wrong.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Program (URGENT and IMPORTANT)

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-01T23:28:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e614140.5229466@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2586396.1046409100@dbforums.com> <2591142.1046498777@dbforums.com>`

```
yusufjee <member25738@dbforums.com> wrote:

>so wats wrong

I'll limit these comments to errors which will not be caught by the compiler. 


>    PERFORM CALCULATE-CIRCLE.
>    PERFORM CALCULATE-RECTANGLE.
>    PERFORM CALCULATE-TRIANGLE.

These are performed from T-OR-C-OR-A. They do not belong here.

>T-OR-C-OR-A.
> ...
>    ELSE
>        PERFORM PROGRAM-BEGIN.

This, and the one at the end, are recursive performs. At the top you need
"perform until repeat-exit not equal to 'R'' and 'r' "

>    COMPUTE CIRCLE = pi * RADIUS ** 2.

When doing two different operations, it is best to use parentheses, especially
if you are a beginner: pi * (Radius ** 2). Alternatively: pi * Radius * Radius.
In this case, it will work as expected. Next time you might not be as lucky.

>DISPLAY-CIRCLE.
>
>    DISPLAY "THE AREA OF CIRCLE IS: " CIRCLE.

You did not perform this paragraph. Just remove the paragraph name.

>    COMPUTE TRIANGLE = 1/2 * BASE * HEIGHT.

The more normal form is: (BASE * HEIGHT) / 2. 

>    DISPLAY "TYPE R TO REPEAT OR E TO EXIT".
>    ACCEPT REPEAT-EXIT.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>        PERFORM PROGRAM-DONE.

See above, then delete the IF.

Put decision-making at the top of your structure and mundane tasks at the
bottom. Your structure is inverted (upside-down). 

Robert
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Program (URGENT and IMPORTANT)

- **From:** "Paul Raulerson" <praulerson@hot.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-03T01:46:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Lty8a.46166$Tz6.2057411@twister.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<2586396.1046409100@dbforums.com> <2591142.1046498777@dbforums.com>`

```
For some reason, I find myself believing that you are just trying to get some help. You do realize, of course, that this is one of
the most common programs assigned in schools/classes that teach COBOL, so most people are going to look at this
and say riiiighhhhttttt.....

I think it is the errors that convince me you probably did do this yourself. So I will offer a little bit of help to see where it
takes you. You should be able to get this to the point where the compiler will tell you what is wrong with it.

"yusufjee" <member25738@dbforums.com> wrote in message news:2591142.1046498777@dbforums.com...
>
> IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
> PROGRAM-ID. Compute.
                                           ^--- Problem number one. COMPUTE is a reserved word. Pick another name for the
                                                   program. For example, PROG1 or something equally boring.

>
> *===========================================
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> 01 T-OR-C-OR-A PIC X.
             ^---- You have this defined as both a data item and a paragraph name.
                       Change one of them to a better name. For example, this one might be better named
                        WS-USER-CHOICE or something like that.

> 01 CALCULATE-CIRCLE PIC 9(5)V99.
>         03 CIRCLE PIC 9(5)V99.
>         03 RADIUS PIC 9(5)V99.
>
            ^--- You missed something here. What you got is not what your indenting says you intended to get.
                    Also, that naming this is going on here again. This is data, not executable code.
                    You might have meant something like this:

                    01 CIRCLE-DATA.
                         01 CIRCLE-RADIUS                      PIC 9(5)V9(2).
                         01 CIRCLE-AREA                          PIC 9(5)V99.

            Do you see the difference? If not, figure out how many bytes of storage you will use in your original,
            and how many I would use in the same above.

> 01 CALCULATE-RECTANGLE PIC 9(5)V99.
>         03 HEIGHT PIC 9(5)V99.
…[102 more quoted lines elided]…
> Posted via http://dbforums.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Program (URGENT and IMPORTANT)

```
Is there a compiler that has defined variables such as pi?


What compiler and environment were you using?   What were your errors?  What did
you think the errors meant?

Sometimes compiler listings aren't very clear when they list compiler errors.  
We would be glad to clarify them.

Sometimes run-time listings aren't very clear when they list run-time errors.  
We will be glad to clarify them.

Sometimes, you just get results that don't make sense.  We will help you
determine where your mistakes are - but it doesn't appear that you are near this
far by looking at the code.
```

- **Date:** 2003-03-03T15:22:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3vrv9$1fr$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<2586396.1046409100@dbforums.com> <2594191.1046615474@dbforums.com>`

```

On  2-Mar-2003, yusufjee <member25738@dbforums.com> wrote:

> what the hell is wrong with u people. I m not copying and pasting
> anything. This is what i have done myself. If u dont want to answer
> dont but please dont say i this is not my work cuz it is mine. And by
> the way if i had tp copy and paste, i would have never came to the
> forum for help :(

It's probably best to be polite, and also edit your messages when you are asking
for help.   The more it looks as though you are working hard, and appreciate a
bit of help, the more likely it will be for you to get that help.

We are trying to get you to perform the same kind of debugging that we do
ourselves - that is the way to learn.  That's why we have asked for you to tell
us what error messages you have been getting, and what you have tried to do to
resolve them.    We also need to know your compiler and its environment, as they
sometimes change some factors.

BTW, it is obvious that your code wasn't copied from a textbook example.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-02T22:07:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e625c34.77676673@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <16e2f010.0302262023.271f085a@posting.google.com> <3e5eb28e.98399852@news.optonline.net> <3E5F0492.F511246E@worldnet.att.net> <b3nfmp$93$1@panix1.panix.com> <bfdfc3e8.0302281001.7ff1075a@posting.google.com>`

```
thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) wrote:

>docdwarf@panix.com wrote in message news:<b3nfmp$93$1@panix1.panix.com>...
>> 
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>afraid to touch it -let alone MOVE it.

I wrote a very similar program in Realia COBOL under MSDOS. It used two modems
per phone line. One modem had an A/D converter on the output side, for speech,
and a DTMF decoder on the input side. The other was a vanilla 1200 baud data
modem. 

It answered the phone "Welcome (blah, blah). Please enter your store number."
After they did, it said, "Store one two three in Roswell. Press one to send an
order, two to report your order will be late, (other features), or five to check
on the status of all orders." Five was for the store manager, checking from
home. If they pressed one, it prompted for the department (these were
supermarkets), then disconnected the voice modem and connected the data modem
without losing the line. At the end, it went back to the voice modem, waited for
them to press a key, and read them a hash total appearing on the screen of their
Telxon handheld. 

Each machine could handle three phone lines in parallel. Multi-tasking under
DOS! The dispatcher was in the main COBOL program. 

Nowadays one can buy audio tools off-the-shelf, from Dialogic et al. In 1984, I
wrote my own wave editor and A/D driver .. first in Compiled Basic (CB), then
rewrote it a year later in Realia COBOL.  

They were still using the order entry program a few years ago. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWS0202@comcast.net>

easier.  For statements (like sort) that do not posses an END, you
can substitute a CONTINUE.  Put period after the continue.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-03T02:08:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e62b8c5.10396698@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <bfdfc3e8.0302250806.7c4bb2f9@posting.google.com> <3e5c0336.100308295@news.optonline.net> <dcedb8d0.0302260822.2487d09@posting.google.com> <3e5d524d.8208933@news.optonline.net> <16e2f010.0302262023.271f085a@posting.google.com> <3e5eb28e.98399852@news.optonline.net> <b3nuql$pd2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0302281109.3941d608@posting.google.com> <b3oehr$2m9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3ohmm$4kv$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>4) "liberal" use of scope terminators (with or without periods and with or
>without Next Sentence in the code - either in conforming or extension
>places) seems like a "good idea" to most people.  (Whether to use them on
>statements WITHOUT conditional phrases is still controversial)

I don't see why it is controversial. We don't write programs like English prose;
we write one statement per line. Neither I nor any sane programmer advocates
changing that style. Thus line breaks delimit statements. Coding a period at the
end of a statement, thereby promoting it to a sentence (COBOL definition), adds
no information. The period becomes just another 'noise word'. I want my programs
to be succinct, not cluttered with noise. 

As we all know, COBOL is disparaged for being 'too verbose'. I find the charge
bogus, especially coming from supporters of C-like languages such as Java that
require full-path qualification when it is unnecessary. Eliminating
end-of-statement periods from COBOL would be one step to defusing the criticism.
Then we could turn the tables and watch defenders of C-like languages defend
their need for a semi-colon at the end of every statement. 

These terminators were invented for the convenience of compiler writers, not
language practitioners. They made parsing easier. That was fine in the 1960's
but we're long past the point where languages should be 'easy to compile'.
Today, easy to maintain is economically much more important. Cluttering programs
with non-functional punctuation doesn't make them easier to maintain. It creates
an opportunity for 'style police', armed with the precept 'every statement must
end with a period when outside a conditional', to nit-pick perfectly valid code.


<end of rant. I feel better now.>

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-02T22:02:55-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3uk5g$d3g$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <bfdfc3e8.0302250806.7c4bb2f9@posting.google.com> <3e5c0336.100308295@news.optonline.net> <dcedb8d0.0302260822.2487d09@posting.google.com> <3e5d524d.8208933@news.optonline.net> <16e2f010.0302262023.271f085a@posting.google.com> <3e5eb28e.98399852@news.optonline.net> <b3nuql$pd2$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <dcedb8d0.0302281109.3941d608@posting.google.com> <b3oehr$2m9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3ohmm$4kv$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3e62b8c5.10396698@news.optonline.net>`

```
The controversy that I was referring to (in case it wasn't clear) was
whether to code something like:

     Call "ABC"
     End-Call

(with no ON EXCEPTION phrase).

Many (probably MOST) programmers who do use scope terminators, do NOT use
them (where perfectly valid) for the unconditional format of statements.
(Only the inline perform requires this).

There are two advantages to *always* coding the scope terminator

1) If you eventually (in matainance) do add a conditional, you already have
the "scope" of the statement defined - and it will remain an imperative
statement, e.g.

 Read XYZ
   At End
      Call "ABC"
      End-Call
   Not At End
      Perform big-loop
End-Read

  >>> might need maintenance to change  it to

 Read XYZ
   At End
      Call "ABC"
        On Exception
             Display "Program 'ABC' wasn't found or loaded"
      End-Call
   Not At End
      Perform big-loop
End-Read

Having the END-CALL already there, might make maintenance easier.

2) The 2nd reason that ALWAYS coding the scope terminator may avoid
"unexpected" matching of conditional situations

Suppose you have

 If  A = "A"
   Add 1 to NumField
      On Size Error
                 Add 3 to NumField2
Else
   Display "A isn't 'A'"
End-If

Now you want to "expand" the logic for your first add to:

 If  A = "A"
   Add 1 to NumField
      On Size Error
                 Add 3 to NumField2
      Not On Size Error
         Perform NumField-is-small-enough
Else
   Display "A isn't 'A'"
End-If

This *might* seem to work the way that the indentation says it should - but
it wouldn't.  To get the "logic" that the indentation requires, you would
need to code:

 If  A = "A"
   Add 1 to NumField
      On Size Error
                 Add 3 to NumField2
                 End-Add
      Not On Size Error
         Perform NumField-is-small-enough
Else
   Display "A isn't 'A'"
End-If

There are a number of other cases like this (including some where it isn't
even the same statement with erroneously matched conditional phrases).
Therefore, I (and I think I am QUITE unusual in doing this) TEND to use
scope terminators for all statements where they can be used - even if it is
an unconditional statement.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-03T17:14:10+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3ukuu$qvk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <b3oehr$2m9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3ohmm$4kv$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3e62b8c5.10396698@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> I don't see why it is controversial.

No.  But your not being able to see this is irrelevant.

> we write one statement per line. 

I frequently write one statement over several lines.

> Neither I nor any sane programmer advocates changing that style. 

> Thus line breaks delimit statements. 

No. Line breaks delimit phrases, comments, blank lines, multiple 
destinations, and statements.  In short they delimit lines.

        READ thisfile
            NEXT RECORD
            WITH LOCK
            AT END
                 MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO thiskey
        END-READ

One statement, 6 line breaks.  The line breaks do _not_ delimit the 
statement.

        MOVE ZERO           TO PR-Ssubs
                               PR-Ccount
                               PR-Total-Qty
                               PR-Total-Value

4 Line breaks, one statement, the line breaks do _not_ delimit the 
statement.

One can also write several statements on a line, the line breaks do _not_ 
delimit the statement.

> Coding a period at the end of a statement, thereby promoting it to a
> sentence (COBOL definition), adds no information. 

Maybe not to _YOU_, maybe not to _ME_, but it _MAY_ add information to 
those who are expecting it to be there.

> The period becomes just another 'noise word'. 

And so is 'THEN', comma, semi-colon, and a whole heap of other words. 
People use them if they want to, or not if they don't.  It is a matter of 
_choice_.

> I want my programs to be succinct, not cluttered with noise.

No one is trying to change the way that you write a program.  However, you 
seem to want to change what others do.

These things are a matter of _choice_.  You appear to want to be a 
control-freak.

> Eliminating end-of-statement periods from COBOL would be one step to
> defusing the criticism. 

   a) who cares
   b) it won't stop criticism

> Then we could turn the tables and watch defenders
> of C-like languages defend their need for a semi-colon at the end of every
> statement.

Do you feel the need to go and criticise 'other languages' ? Why is this ?

And why are you trying to recruit us into your crusade against C ?

When I write in C, I am perfectly happy to use semi-colons, and whatever 
other syntax the language is designed with.

Your understanding of C is also flawed.  While the ';' is a statement 
terminator, that is analogous to the full stop being a sentence terminator. 
 A 'statement' in C does not use the definition from Cobol, it has its own 
definition.  For example a statement may be:

             a = 1,
             b = ( c * 2 ) + 6,
             myfile = open(...),
             7;

Sometimes it is useful to write this way, but usually it is simpler to just 
end each expression and make it a statement.

The ';' is also a control point and resolving a control point after each 
expression execution is safer.

> Cluttering programs with non-functional punctuation doesn't ..

It is only your _opinion_ that it is clutter, not some truth revealed to 
'believers'. 

'Clutter' or 'Clarity' depends _entirely_ on 'what you are used to'. You 
prefer to see no full stops, others prefer to see them.

> It creates an opportunity for 'style police', ... to nit-pick perfectly
>  valid code.

Whereas you would like to be _the_ global 'style police' (as here), nit 
picking about 'noise words' and how people should be coding in _your_ style.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-03T09:11:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e630abb.31381691@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <b3oehr$2m9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3ohmm$4kv$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3e62b8c5.10396698@news.optonline.net> <b3ukuu$qvk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>And so is 'THEN', comma, semi-colon, and a whole heap of other words. 
>People use them if they want to, or not if they don't.  It is a matter of 
>_choice_.

Suppose this is a forum on proper use of English. Suppose someone posts a
message advocating the bureaucratic writing style -- all nouns rather than
verbs, missing commas, no personal pronouns, never say please, an air of
superiority over the reader. Suppose I point out his style is _not_ good
English. Would you then jump to his defense, saying there is no such thing as
good and bad, people can write any way they wish?

There _is_ a difference between good and bad programming. This forum is an
appropriate place to advocate good and criticize bad. 

>No one is trying to change the way that you write a program.  

Ah, but they do .. not here, but in the real world. Most organizations have a
stylebook describing their practices, whrerein are listed primarily don'ts and
very few dos. Some have 'style police' to enforce the rules. In most cases, the
book describes programming style from the '70s. 

>These things are a matter of _choice_.  You appear to want to be a 
>control-freak.

I want to be an educator and advocate of Best Practices. 

>'Clutter' or 'Clarity' depends _entirely_ on 'what you are used to'. You 
>prefer to see no full stops, others prefer to see them.

Opinions can change. I used to write with full stops, because I had no choice.
When explicit terminators became available, in the mid-80s, I changed my style. 
Similarly, when evaluate became available, I stopped writing nested ifs.
Actually, I still write nested ifs occasionally, then have to correct myself. 

Robert
```

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> The fault rests
> on the shoulders of traditionalists, who dominate this newsgroup. They'd
> rather be marginalized than learn to program well. 

It is only your _opinion_, and a rather insulting one, that the majority of 
the group here "program badly".

You seem to base this almost entirely on the extremely superficial view 
that if they use a few more full-stops than the absolute minimum, they they 
must also have very poor design skills, must be using pre-'74 standard 
code, and probably their parents were cousins.

In fact it is often you that comes up with archaic and obsolete ideas and 
seems to know very little of what is outside your playpen.

I doubt that you have seen more than a few lines of anyone's code, 
certainly not mine, and have no basis at all, other than your own 
prejudices, for judging whether they write good software or bad.  Nor, in 
fact, have you defined 'bad' in anything other than the most superficial 
basis of appearance.

From an earlier message:

RW> Having worked in production support, where 400,000 people wouldn't get
RW> paid unless the problem was fixed NOW, I know that problems are seldom 
in
RW> source code. They're usually in transaction records. Delete the 
offending
RW> record and the system runs.

'Bad programming' is where the program crashes from unexpected data in the 
input. The problem _is_ in the source code.

'Good programming' survives this and posts an error log.

The criteria that you are using for 'good or bad' is poorly chosen.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T12:51:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e648dae.130455042@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3e630abb.31381691@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303031047.3dc3a55b@posting.google.com> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b417ac$udc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
>> The fault rests
>> on the shoulders of traditionalists, who dominate this newsgroup. They'd
>> rather be marginalized than learn to program well. 

>I doubt that you have seen more than a few lines of anyone's code, 
>certainly not mine, and have no basis at all, other than your own 
>prejudices, for judging whether they write good software or bad.  Nor, in 
>fact, have you defined 'bad' in anything other than the most superficial 
>basis of appearance.

The sentence's subject was traditionalists, not posters to this newsgroup. I've
seen millions of lines of their bad code. 

I described good vs. bad at some length in Best Practices. Focussing on periods
was the newsgroup's decision. For instance, I wrote:

"Write a procedure like a story -- with a beginning, middle and end. In the
beginning you clear counters, acquire memory, construct objects, open files,
etc. In the middle, you loop through whatever you're doing. In the end you print
out totals, release memory, destroy objects, close files, etc. Initialization
means 'done at the beginning'. Programs that 'clean up' at the end of a
procedure rather than the beginning are poorly designed."

I imagine they teach precepts like this in Comp Sci 101, yet I see it violated
in COBOL programs every day. 

>From an earlier message:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>The criteria that you are using for 'good or bad' is poorly chosen.

In that shop, production support changes to source code had to go through _all_
the methodology stuff. "if foo not numeric move zero to foo" had to be reviewed
by 'subject matter experts', a project created in version control, the whole
system re-tested by the test team. In short, management butt-covering made it
impractical to fix the problem correctly. 

This was not an isolated example. In my experience, most big shops ham-string
support the same way. One shop even required a team of Big Five auditors to be
called in at 3am to sign off on the change before it could be used in
production.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T00:46:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e6548db.178379301@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3e630abb.31381691@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303031047.3dc3a55b@posting.google.com> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b417ac$udc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e648dae.130455042@news.optonline.net> <b42hfd$g7r$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On  4-Mar-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >> rather be marginalized than learn to program well.

> The millions of lines of their bad code when was that code written?

I'm now sitting on a pile of recently inherited Bad Code written with the last
five years. It's full of GO TOs, cut-and-paste redundant code, and whole
programs duplicated with only a few lines changed. The author never worked on a
mainframe. He programmed by intuition, favoring short-term fixes over long-term
solutions. 

As I said in the introduction to Best Practices, "Teaching someone the grammar
of a programming language and expecting well-written code is like teaching
someone to type and expecting literature. Neophyte programmers all make the same
mistakes." That's a statement about Human Nature which applies to all platforms.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T08:14:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e65abed.203745949@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b417ac$udc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e648dae.130455042@news.optonline.net> <b42tot$m5i$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>> I described good vs. bad at some length in Best Practices. 
>
>You gave your opinions about you didn't like, yes.  You seem to treat it as 
>if it was some revelation carved on gold plates.

No, just my _experienced_ opinion. 

>> Focusing on
>> periods was the newsgroup's decision. For instance, I wrote:
>
>You have subsequently initiated and argued at length focussing on full 
>stops.

I was responding to posts from others. 

I know why it's such a hot issue to mainframers. They see the push for no
periods as an assault on their Culture. If periods fall, so too will PERFORM ..
THRU and other indefensible features. As a result, they defend periods tooth and
nail. 

>> "Write a procedure like a story -- with a beginning, middle and end. In
>> the beginning you clear counters, acquire memory, construct objects, open
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>process, end" in the same way at all.  

Yes they do, internally. You have a bunch of 'callbacks', but within them are
procedures. I wrote, 

" Use the company as a design model for your program or system. High level code
should make high level decisions; low level code should do the grunt work and
refer exceptions to higher levels for handling. 

For real-time applications, use the network model, in which modules pass
messages to each other in response to events. Think of each module as a company
department, within which there can still be a hierarchy."

A procedure isn't necessarily tied to batch or on-line. It can be, for example,
a parser which receives a line of input and returns a list of words. I just
wrote one today. The function services both batch and on-line. Its internal
structure follows the above precent of begin-middle-end.

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T15:07:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b453s8$l94$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b417ac$udc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e648dae.130455042@news.optonline.net> <b42tot$m5i$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e65abed.203745949@news.optonline.net>`

```

On  5-Mar-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> I know why it's such a hot issue to mainframers. They see the push for no
> periods as an assault on their Culture. If periods fall, so too will PERFORM
> ..
> THRU and other indefensible features. As a result, they defend periods tooth
> and nail.

I was convinced long ago that PERFORM THRU should fall.   But not being a
believer in the domino effect, I didn't assume that now that I use END-IF, and
that now periods were not necessary for clear code when we don't have
pre-compilers inserting logic - that their use was automatically BAD.

Many new things came about from the experiences of us old timers.   We demanded
and got improvements.   But just because a style feature is new doesn't mean
that feature is good.   Just because a style feature is the style feature I am
comfortable doesn't mean it is good.

And putting in periods isn't "good".  And taking periods out isn't "good".   
They are simply preferences that normally won't make any difference at all with
our coding practices.    And when someone has a coding practice where periods DO
make a difference, then that coding practice is the problem.

We agree that terminators are good.   We agree that structured programs are
good.   We agree that when we code this way periods are not necessary.  Neither
is white space, indentation, nor the ADD command.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Software Archeology

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T15:02:18-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<16e2f010.0303051502.4a0f6cdd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b417ac$udc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e648dae.130455042@news.optonline.net> <b42tot$m5i$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e65abed.203745949@news.optonline.net> <b453s8$l94$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message news:<b453s8$l94$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...
> I was convinced long ago that PERFORM THRU should fall.   But not
> being a believer in the domino effect, I didn't assume that now that I 
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> ADD command.

I wonder if the Cambodians still use full-stop periods...

Many new things did indeed come about because of the experiences of
"old timers". One of the most obvious ones is that somewhere between
the origin of Cobol and the Cobol-85 standard a consensus arose that
the period as the end-all-be-all scope terminator was lacking in some
things.

Maybe they wanted to nest statements, maybe the period was just too
APL-like in its power, maybe there were visual problems.  Except for a
few decks in classes, I wasn't using Cobol pre-85, so I'll shut up and
let the consensus of the "old timers" speak for me.

Change doesn't happen without conflict.  Somewhere in the pre-85 days
there must have been enough belief and agreement between the compiler
manufactures, the standards body and compiler users that a more
granular scope terminator was necessary.  They certainly did not
up-end the entire language for a cosmetic change.  There had to be
some substance behind the move.  Otherwise they would have happily
continued using full-stop periods and the Cobol world would never have
heard of END-Anything.

You suggest that there is nothing wrong with periods and that they are
the equal of explicit scope terminators.  That the choice between the
two is a mere preference.  This idea is directly at odds with whatever
reasons the Cobol community had 2 decades ago for enhancing scope
termination options in the first place.

It isn't (as I am often accused) a case of 'old == bad; new == good'. 
It is a case of 'old == ok; new == improved'.  That we did not see a
move for a change in scope termination in the 2002 standard seems to
indicate that the Cobol-85 standard fixed whatever problems existed
with scope termination before.

The fact that the Cobol community at large decided they needed
enhanced scope termination is evidence enough that periods are not as
good as explicit scope terminators.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Software Archeology

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T17:45:33-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0303051745.b01619c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b417ac$udc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e648dae.130455042@news.optonline.net> <b42tot$m5i$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e65abed.203745949@news.optonline.net> <b453s8$l94$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303051502.4a0f6cdd@posting.google.com>`

```
psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote

> > And putting in periods isn't "good".  And taking periods out isn't 
> > "good".  They are simply preferences that normally won't make 
> > any difference at all with our coding practices.    And when someone
> >  has a coding practice where periods DO make a difference, then
> > that coding practice is the problem.
 
> You suggest that there is nothing wrong with periods and that they are
> the equal of explicit scope terminators.  That the choice between the
> two is a mere preference.  

No. He didn't say that _at_all_.

I do wish that people would criticise based on what is written rather
than criticising what their own prejudice makes them think is written.

He didn't say that full stops are the _equal_ of scope terminators,
nor that he _chooses_ between them.

He says that once some code is written (with scope terminators) then
having full stops at scope level zero makes no difference either way.

It is not what I would do, but at least I understand what he says.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Software Archeology

_(thread depth: 23)_

- **From:** psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T09:10:03-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<16e2f010.0303060027.6e3ede1f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b417ac$udc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e648dae.130455042@news.optonline.net> <b42tot$m5i$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e65abed.203745949@news.optonline.net> <b453s8$l94$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303051502.4a0f6cdd@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0303051745.b01619c@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote in message news:<217e491a.0303051745.b01619c@posting.google.com>...
> psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> No. He didn't say that _at_all_.


The above quoted paragraph look quite like "it doesn't make any
difference, just a mere preference" to me.

The only way for periods in a coding practice to make a difference is
by their presence.  So I suggest that 'taking periods out' is quite
"good"'.  Change the first half -- that all preferences are created
equal -- and the need for the last part -- fix problem periods in
coding practices -- becomes unnecessary.

> I do wish that people would criticise based on what is written rather
> than criticising what their own prejudice makes them think is written.


I thought I was commenting on the use of full-stop periods as a
preference.  And disagreeing with what was written, that 'taking
periods out isn't good'.  What exactly does my prejudice make me think
was written?
 
> He didn't say that full stops are the _equal_ of scope terminators,
> nor that he _chooses_ between them.


No, _I_ said the two were not equal in response to the 'simply a
preference' statement.

The problem is that the compiler has quite well defined rules for what
it will accept, but as is often said, it is quite dumb -- it does
EXACTLY what the human tells it to do.  The preferences of the human
writter have an extreme impact on the readability and maintainability
of that source by other humans.

Style is more than just a preference -- it is everything.

Hmm, consider the code, written with a preference for compactness:

PROCEDURE DIVISION USING J. PERFORM VARYING I FROM 1 BY 1
    UNTIL I > J COMPUTE K = I - I / 2 * 2 IF I = 1 DISPLAY I "
VARIABLE I"
    '' EQUAL TO ONE" ELSE IF I = 2 DISPLAY I "VARIABLE I EQUAL TWO"
   END-IF END-IF END-PERFORM STOP RUN.

Is this coding practice a problem?  It works after all.  Where is the
line?
 
> He says that once some code is written (with scope terminators) then
> having full stops at scope level zero makes no difference either way.

The oft quoted number is that 10% of original source is the first
implementation.  90% is maintenance over the lifetime.  That would
suggest that a zero level period has a 1 in 10 chance of staying zero
level over the life of the product -- or, to look at it another way, a
90% chance of 'making a difference'.

> It is not what I would do, but at least I understand what he says.

I understand what Howard said.  And I even agree with most of it --
all except that part about most periods not mattering one way or the
other.  But mostly the entire email was blasted off quickly so that I
could make my Cambodian Domino wisecrack.  Then I felt I needed to add
some meaningful Cobol content. ;->
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Software Archeology

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T18:57:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b485me$c77$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b417ac$udc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e648dae.130455042@news.optonline.net> <b42tot$m5i$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e65abed.203745949@news.optonline.net> <b453s8$l94$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303051502.4a0f6cdd@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0303051745.b01619c@posting.google.com> <16e2f010.0303060027.6e3ede1f@posting.google.com>`

```

On  6-Mar-2003, psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote:

> The above quoted paragraph look quite like "it doesn't make any
> difference, just a mere preference" to me.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> coding practices -- becomes unnecessary.

With terminators and CONTINUE, they don't make any difference by their presence
or by their absence.  Sort of like white space.
Without these they make a difference by their presence or by their absence.

So a standard that says remove periods or a standard that says add periods is
not "good", but is neutral.

"Good" is what makes the program work better, be less likely to have errors in
it, and which is more clear for the maintenance programmer down the road.  
"Bad" is what makes the program work wrong, and be hard for the maintenance
programmer to know what you are doing and what you were intending to do.

Slightly bad is errors that get caught by the compiler - but those are minor
concerns.

"Neutral" are what aren't either of the above.


> The oft quoted number is that 10% of original source is the first
> implementation.  90% is maintenance over the lifetime.  That would
> suggest that a zero level period has a 1 in 10 chance of staying zero
> level over the life of the product -- or, to look at it another way, a
> 90% chance of 'making a difference'.

So do you suggest moving code into an inner level should not be edited?  I
indent in those rare cases.  Editing the code for readability is important.  
But the important thing is that if you use END-IF's the compiler will catch your
error, not your customer.

I am in an unusual environment where periods are actually "good".   But normally
the use or non-use of periods is a neutral issue.   So I save my preaching for
stuff that matters.
```

>difference, just a mere preference" to me.

I wonder .. if periods don't make any difference, why does the subject cause so
much heated, emotional debate? It comes from both sides, but primarily from
traditionalists wanting to keep their periods. Why do they hang on so tightly to
a cause that seems, to impartial observers, an obvious lose? 

>The only way for periods in a coding practice to make a difference is
>by their presence.  So I suggest that 'taking periods out' is quite
>"good"'.  Change the first half -- that all preferences are created
>equal -- and the need for the last part -- fix problem periods in
>coding practices -- becomes unnecessary.

When the metrics are good vs. bad, you're into emotion. When the metrics are
correct vs. incorrect, you're into reason. 

>The problem is that the compiler has quite well defined rules for what
>it will accept, but as is often said, it is quite dumb -- it does
>EXACTLY what the human tells it to do. 

Everyone who's worked with computers knows the falsity in 'The computer only
does what you tell it.'  War stories abound. :)

>The preferences of the human
>writer have an extreme impact on the readability and maintainability
>of that source by other humans.
>
>Style is more than just a preference -- it is everything.

You are correct. When we write source code, we're not talking to the compiler,
we're talking to the next programmer who must understand our code. Old-time
COBOL programmers never understood this concept. They thought they were talking
to the machine. 

>Hmm, consider the code, written with a preference for compactness:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>line?

Another damn C programmer trying to write COBOL. ;->

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

Howard Brazee has posted dozens of messages defending periods. So has Donald
Tees, by posting sample code using periods.

The Old Guard just won't let go of it. 

I posted my Best Practices here a month ago. It talked about a whole lot more
than avoiding periods, yet that's what the newsgroup latched onto as the central
talking point. Why? It wasn't my decision. 

I wish it had lauched a debate about program structure or expressiveness. Those
topics could have opened the discussion to levels of discourse higher than
personal insults. Now that my iron is luke-warm, the opportunity for exploration
has been lost. 

Chalk the failure up to the dynamics of newsgroups. The medium has no
communication defect. The problem is it attracts a sub-standard audience. 

Robert

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Killfile revisited (was: Software Archeology

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T22:32:55-06:00

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Robert

I think that this last sentence is the final "straw".  Unless some
REASONABLE participant in this group can give me a good reason not to do so,
I plan on expanding my "killfile" tomorrow.

Anyone who DOES continue to read the "drivel" coming from that source and
wants a response based on actual compiler or Standards documentation -
please feel free to reply to any of his future posts with YOUR questions and
I'll be happy to reply either with "fact" when (as so often in the past he
is simply wrong) or my personal opinion (based - apparently on a wider view
of what is and should be done in COBOL today) for "opinion-oriented"
discussions.

My personal suggestion would be that as many truly COBOL aware people
(unlike this apparently "isolated" egotist) who participate in this
newsgroup as possible also stop responding to his posts.  I don't think that
it will shut him up - but being ignored MIGHT sink into his mind where
nothing else has let him understand  that it his attitude even more than his
errors that upset many CLC readers.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Software Archeology

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T23:35:32-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E682FA3.E6D4485A@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b417ac$udc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e648dae.130455042@news.optonline.net> <b42tot$m5i$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e65abed.203745949@news.optonline.net> <b453s8$l94$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303051502.4a0f6cdd@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0303051745.b01619c@posting.google.com> <16e2f010.0303060027.6e3ede1f@posting.google.com> <3e67c807.122578794@news.optonline.net> <b48rtl$9oa$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3e680fdf.140973358@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:>

>
> Howard Brazee has posted dozens of messages defending periods. So has Donald
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Robert

But didn't you have at least an idea that this is what would happen?  I've never
seen any such discussion that didn't degenerate into "this is how I do it so
everybody else should too".  I suspect this happens because of a lack of what I call
"perspective" classes in programming/systems training.  It only takes a little while
for people to settle into ways of doing things - based upon their own training and
what facilities they have available at the time - then when someone else comes along
(who doesn't share their background in this sense) and castigates them for doing it
that way, well of course they're going to jib.  As far as I'm concerned, all
language training should include a three or four week overview of the history of the
development of programming languages generally as well as the one in question.  Then
maybe we'd avoid smug nonsense about other languages (such as the old canard from VB
wonks that you can't do event oriented programming in COBOL) and scorn from newbies
in their own;  as example, the school that is against paragraph names.

PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Software Archeology

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-09T22:46:01
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e6bbe80_1@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b417ac$udc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e648dae.130455042@news.optonline.net> <b42tot$m5i$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e65abed.203745949@news.optonline.net> <b453s8$l94$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303051502.4a0f6cdd@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0303051745.b01619c@posting.google.com> <16e2f010.0303060027.6e3ede1f@posting.google.com> <3e67c807.122578794@news.optonline.net> <b48rtl$9oa$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3e680fdf.140973358@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e680fdf.140973358@news.optonline.net...

>
> Chalk the failure up to the dynamics of newsgroups. The medium has no
> communication defect. The problem is it attracts a sub-standard audience.
>
ROFL!!

Robert, I haven't read your "Best Practices" (recently I just haven't had
time to frequent CLC) but I have been staggered at the vitriol your posts
have evoked.

I don't believe the audience is sub-standard and I don't believe that you
are guilty of many of the things you have been  accused of.

(I have to believe your last sentence above was written tongue-in-cheek).

The problem is that if you represent the "new breed" of COBOL programmer,
you will be just as passionate about things like NOT using full stops etc.,
as the "old guard" are about coding the way they always have.

Why do you suppose there is a huge thread objecting to free format because
of the effects it will have on printing source?
It is because the people objecting haven't thought about using different
fonts and orientations, in the same way that a guy asking for examples of
using WORD from COBOL gets referred to a "white paper" like it is some
mysterious art instead of a technology that has been around for 4 years
now... On the other hand, someone who NORMALLY thinks of printed output in
terms of WORD (or some other Word Processor) instinctively realises that
printing source or anything else is not a problem. From COBOL or Java or
whatever...

None of this means that you are an arrogant egotist, OR that everyone who
disagrees is some kind of mental deficient.

It all comes down to mind set.

I've been writing COBOL all my working life (coming up for 38 years) and I
still do so and I still love it. Like you, I have taught many others to do
so (and SOME of them DID go on to be Managers, so I guess that makes them
failures in Richard's book...<G>). The COBOL I write today is not like the
COBOL I wrote 30 years ago (it would be an insult to all the dedicated and c
lever people who I worked with, if I had learned nothing from them). And it
is also true that I no longer make my living out of writing COBOL. (These
days I do it out of curiosity to see what the new stuff is like and because
I still get a kick out of it) . I have moved on to Management so maybe
Richard's theory is not so wrong <G>.

I do see the future of COBOL (like all Programming) as being OO, but there
are many here who are not convinced, and I have enough respect for them to
think carefully on their arguments. Over the years we have had heated
debates and "Religious Wars" in this NewsGroup. Some of it has been
acrimonious but that is (thankfully) usually short lived. I can honestly say
I have learned much from contributing here, and for the most part, it is
fun, too.

Here's my suggestion:

1. Lighten up. A sense of  humour is essential if you intend to post here
regularly (and I, for one, hope you will...)

2. It is a Public unmoderated Newsgroup. Nobody controls or runs it and
there are technically no rules. That means we all have to exercise some
responsibility in what we post.

3. There is no RIGHT or WRONG in COBOL programming when it comes to STYLE.
(There is definitely RIGHT or WRONG when it comes to results <G>). One
person's "Best Practice" is another person's anathema. It all comes down to
preferences.

I call myself a COBOL Programmer. Show me broken COBOL and I'll fix it.
That's what I do. I don't care if you write spaghetti, use PERFORM...THRU,
insert full stops, don't insert full stops, write SECTIONS, don't write
SECTIONS, write Classes with obscure inheritance, or whatever...if COBOL can
compile it; I can fix it. A very wise COBOL man taught me this many years
ago and he was right.

(As a footnote to the ideas expressed in the last paragraph, I was greatly
tempted to post in the thread on First Time switches...ALTER makes a
FANTASTIC "first time" switch (highly efficient, much more so than an 88 or
IF/EVALUATE), but I realised it was only mischief on my part so I
didn't...<G>).

Pete.






> Robert
>
> Robert

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert, I haven't read your "Best Practices" (recently I just haven't had
>time to frequent CLC) but I have been staggered at the vitriol your posts
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
