I worked with a guy who programmed on paper, followed by  'keypunching' his
changes using .. what else .. vi. He became defensive when I pointed out we had
SAMBA and better editors on the PC side. He would have become livid if I'd said
we stopped using paper listings thirty years ago. Of course, being a diplomat, I
didn't say that. :>

>It is because the people objecting haven't thought about using different
>fonts and orientations, in the same way that a guy asking for examples of
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>whatever...

Ironically, the same shop published all reports as Word documents, which were
distributed via email. As you say, it's fairly common practice. He thought
ephemeral media that was fine for Users, but not programmers. 

>Here's my suggestion:
>
>1. Lighten up. A sense of  humour is essential if you intend to post here
>regularly (and I, for one, hope you will...)

1. I'm not artful enough to make a point with humor. See the example I posted in
another thread.

2. Being serious pisses them off more. 

"We do not make people humble and meek when we show them their guilt and cause
them to be ashamed of themselves. We are more likely to stir their arrogance and
rouse in them a reckless aggressiveness. There is a guilty conscience behind
every brazen word and act and behind every manifestation of self-righteousness."

Eric Hoffer

>2. It is a Public unmoderated Newsgroup. Nobody controls or runs it and
>there are technically no rules. That means we all have to exercise some
>responsibility in what we post.

I hope poopiehead is reading. 

>I call myself a COBOL Programmer. Show me broken COBOL and I'll fix it.
>That's what I do. I don't care if you write spaghetti, use PERFORM...THRU,
>insert full stops, don't insert full stops, write SECTIONS, don't write
>SECTIONS, write Classes with obscure inheritance, or whatever...if COBOL can
>compile it; I can fix it. 

Same here. But when working as an employee or contractor, shop standards and
'scope of the project' block such fixes. I was once told to reinstate spelling
errors in comments because fixing them was 'outside the scope'.  My present
project is a delight because there's no code-level oversight. Nobody else knows
COBOL. I can program _well_ for the first time in five years. 

Robert

"The essence of tragedy lies in the deafness of its heroes."
                           -Albert Camus

On  6-Mar-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> >The above quoted paragraph look quite like "it doesn't make any
> >difference, just a mere preference" to me.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> a cause that seems, to impartial observers, an obvious lose?

As the primary person on the other side of this debate - all I am arguing is
that there isn't significant reason to call periods obsolete, and to create
standards against them.   Especially since I am in an uncommon environment where
they are useful.

You're the person on the other side who wants to force everybody to your side.

The rest of the input seemed to be by people who don't use periods, but don't
mind them either.

I think it is obvious to everybody except you who is emotionally hanging on to
his style.


> >The only way for periods in a coding practice to make a difference is
> >by their presence.  So I suggest that 'taking periods out' is quite
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> correct vs. incorrect, you're into reason.

Semantics.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Software Archeology

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T15:20:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b47p0i$6nb$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b417ac$udc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e648dae.130455042@news.optonline.net> <b42tot$m5i$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e65abed.203745949@news.optonline.net> <b453s8$l94$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303051502.4a0f6cdd@posting.google.com>`

```

On  5-Mar-2003, psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote:

> You suggest that there is nothing wrong with periods and that they are
> the equal of explicit scope terminators.  That the choice between the
> two is a mere preference.  This idea is directly at odds with whatever
> reasons the Cobol community had 2 decades ago for enhancing scope
> termination options in the first place.

No I didn't.  I stated that if I use explicit scope terminators (when I can see
the scope), and if I eschew NEXT SENTENCE, periods are meaningless and harmless
in compiled code.   The standards should be to use scope terminators and to
proscribe NEXT SENTENCE.


> The fact that the Cobol community at large decided they needed
> enhanced scope termination is evidence enough that periods are not as
> good as explicit scope terminators.

And I don't propose that we keep them as scope terminators without good reason
(most environments do not have such a good reason).   But I  think it is silly
to decide that they should be eliminated just because they can.   Or because
"old code had periods, so new code should not".  Insufficient reason.

The shop standards should allow periods or non-periods and address itself to
things that make a difference - such as explicit scope terminators, NEXT
SENTENCE, and drop through code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Software Archeology

_(thread depth: 23)_

- **From:** psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-03-07T07:44:04-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<16e2f010.0303070744.55577870@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b417ac$udc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e648dae.130455042@news.optonline.net> <b42tot$m5i$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e65abed.203745949@news.optonline.net> <b453s8$l94$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303051502.4a0f6cdd@posting.google.com> <b47p0i$6nb$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message news:<b47p0i$6nb$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...
> On  5-Mar-2003, psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote:
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> proscribe NEXT SENTENCE.

If I misunderstood the two items being compared in the 'simple
preference' statement, please forgive me.  I really did just blast off
a quick rant of a Cobol nature as life-support (topic-support?) for my
domino theory wisecrack.  I'll turn up the semantic analysis option in
the future.

However, this idea that a zero level period is harmless is what I
would question.  Your exception list is at least 1/3 incomplete.  In
addition to scope terminators and no next sentences you also need to
completely avoid copy code (I know some places never use it -- other
places use it everywhere).  There may be other exceptions I'm not
aware of.

On the one side, that of the use of zero-level periods, you have three
requirements to maintain if they are to be harmless.  On the other
side, that of no zero-level periods, you have no requirements to
maintain.

Since three requirements are not equal to no requirements it seems
that there is a clear difference.  And if any of those three
requirements fail, there is actual harm in the use of the zero level
period.  The fewer possible points of failure, the less the likelihood
of a failure.  That is why I don't think it is a 'simple preference',
but good reason to eschew them all together.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Software Archeology

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-07T16:40:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b4ai1o$hcr$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b417ac$udc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e648dae.130455042@news.optonline.net> <b42tot$m5i$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e65abed.203745949@news.optonline.net> <b453s8$l94$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303051502.4a0f6cdd@posting.google.com> <b47p0i$6nb$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303070744.55577870@posting.google.com>`

```

On  7-Mar-2003, psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote:

> However, this idea that a zero level period is harmless is what I
> would question.  Your exception list is at least 1/3 incomplete.  In
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> aware of.

Agreed, if you use copy code, it is a good idea to include periods in the main
source.  When you can't be sure what is in your code, you can guarantee a full
stop period where you expect.

> On the one side, that of the use of zero-level periods, you have three
> requirements to maintain if they are to be harmless.  On the other
> side, that of no zero-level periods, you have no requirements to
> maintain.

I only saw two requirements, eliminate NEXT SENTENCE, and terminate your
commands.

But it is very easy to illustrate how when you do NOT use periods, failure to
have these requirements hurts.
IF A=B
    NEXT SENTENCE
ELSE
    PERFORM B
PERFORM C
COPY MYCODE
PERFORM D
PERFORM E
.

Does this look like safe code to you?	

How about:
IF A=B
    NEXT SENTENCE
ELSE
    PERFORM B.
PERFORM C.
COPY MYCODE.
PERFORM D.
PERFORM E.

If we don't use terminators and use NEXT SENTENCE, this latter code looks much
more trustworthy to me.

> Since three requirements are not equal to no requirements it seems
> that there is a clear difference.  And if any of those three
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> but good reason to eschew them all together.

I think your conclusion is backwards.  If you fail to meet these requirements,
there is a definite disadvantage in eschewing periods.   It is only when you do
have these requirements that a style without periods is harmless.

That said, I am all in favor of having standards that require terminators and
disallow NEXT SENTENCE.   In that case, a style of no unnecessary periods is
fine with me - except after inserted (copymember or precompiler) code which
isn't completely known.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Software Archeology

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-07T12:02:47-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Bf4aa.4943$6z.971370@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b417ac$udc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e648dae.130455042@news.optonline.net> <b42tot$m5i$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e65abed.203745949@news.optonline.net> <b453s8$l94$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303051502.4a0f6cdd@posting.google.com> <b47p0i$6nb$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303070744.55577870@posting.google.com> <b4ai1o$hcr$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
There is another place where lack of periods is a killer, that nobody has
mentioned yet.  That is when you are debugging, and trying to single step.
What it does, is make each paragraph "one step" instead of each sentence.  I
stated earlier that using, or not using, periods is a matter of granularity
within the code. That granularity is crucial to stepping through a program
to find errors.

If you really want scope terminators to replace periods, then we need an
"end-sentence" scope terminator to do it right. Sentences *are* the basic
unit of code at the compiled level, and debuging with any kind of rational
methodoly requires knowing where each sentence ends.

Donald


Donald

to

…[21 more quoted lines elided]…

much

requirements,

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Software Archeology

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-03-07T14:32:30-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<16e2f010.0303071432.2cdc8648@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b417ac$udc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e648dae.130455042@news.optonline.net> <b42tot$m5i$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e65abed.203745949@news.optonline.net> <b453s8$l94$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303051502.4a0f6cdd@posting.google.com> <b47p0i$6nb$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303070744.55577870@posting.google.com> <b4ai1o$hcr$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message news:<b4ai1o$hcr$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...
> On  7-Mar-2003, psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote:
> 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> commands.

I added the third requirement -- no copy code.  

The problem with a zero-level period is that it can be COPYed in at a
non-zero-level scope.  Something like:

	IF A=B
         Copy ABSTMTS.
      END-IF

Will break if you have any zero-level periods in "ABSTMTS".

I've had this happen with some vendor code before.  The did some
updates for a module that used ABSTMTS once and added a zero-level
period.  Worked great.  But several of their other programs were of
the flavor:

       PERFORM VARYING MONTH-NBR FROM 1 BY 1
         UNTIL MONTH-NBR > 12
         COPY ABSTMTS.
       END-PERFORM

and 

       IF need-abstmts
	   COPY ABSTMTS.
       END-IF

It became hard to explain how modules listed in source control as
unchanged suddenly couldn't compile.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Software Archeology

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWS0202@comcast.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-10T01:18:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cNicnUkKqMzcs_GjXTWcqw@comcast.com>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b417ac$udc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e648dae.130455042@news.optonline.net> <b42tot$m5i$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e65abed.203745949@news.optonline.net> <b453s8$l94$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303051502.4a0f6cdd@posting.google.com> <b47p0i$6nb$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303070744.55577870@posting.google.com> <b4ai1o$hcr$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

> I only saw two requirements, eliminate NEXT SENTENCE, and terminate your
> commands.
>
> But it is very easy to illustrate how when you do NOT use periods, failure
to
> have these requirements hurts.
> IF A=B
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> If we don't use terminators and use NEXT SENTENCE, this latter code looks
much
> more trustworthy to me.
>

    It all seems to boil down (again) to eliminate NEXT SENTENCE, and
terminate
all commands.  It would be very helpfull if we could have the compiler flag
missing END's,
such as with the following.

IF A = 1                            *> note endents (Use courier?)
  IF B = 2
     DISPLAY "A1B2"
END-IF
DISPLAY "IF SHOULD BE OVER NOW".       *> period here

NEW-PARAGRAPH.

        The above will compile just fine, but it is clearly wrong.
    ANSI flagging is of no value, as it flags too much.  An error
(flag or informational level) for missing END's would be nice.

    The existence of this capability would, it seems to me, encourage
periods whenever you come to a definite stop.
```

```
Russell,

comments below are not intended to be personal.

I have used "you" as I always feel uncomfortable with the impersonal "one".

My use of "you" is intended for the forum at large, not you personally.

Pete.

"Russell Styles" <RWS0202@comcast.net> wrote in message
news:cNicnUkKqMzcs_GjXTWcqw@comcast.com...
>
> > I only saw two requirements, eliminate NEXT SENTENCE, and terminate your
> > commands.
> >
> > But it is very easy to illustrate how when you do NOT use periods,
failure
> to
> > have these requirements hurts.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

No, it looks like crap. But so does the stuff with periods in it...<G>

> > How about:
> > IF A=B
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> > If we don't use terminators and use NEXT SENTENCE, this latter code
looks
> much
> > more trustworthy to me.
> >

 Nope. Not to me, it doesn't...<G>. (I'm smiling because I don't really
care...)

What would you say to:

if NOT A = B
   perform B
end-if
perform C
*>
*> Replace Procedure Division COPY with external module...
*>
call 'mycode'
      on exception
           <some abort routine >
end-call
perform D
perform E
.

Don't like it?

Fair enough... It just goes to show that when it comes to style there is
really no right or wrong, just preference.

I would code it exactly as above. I expect you would have no trouble
maintaining it (whether or not you actually LIKED it..), just as I would
have no trouble maintaining either of your examples.

>
>     It all seems to boil down (again) to eliminate NEXT SENTENCE, and
> terminate
> all commands.  It would be very helpfull if we could have the compiler
flag
> missing END's,
> such as with the following.
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The only time I use periods is when I HAVE to. But I really don't care
whether you use them or not.

I don't like code in UPPER CASE because it shouts at me and is more
difficult to read, but you haven't seen me arguing this here.

It is easy in style wars to be side-tracked into obsession with form at the
expense of content.

It is COBOL.

If you call yourself a COBOL programmer, deal with it.

Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Software Archeology

_(thread depth: 27)_

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWS0202@comcast.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-13T01:47:58-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7ZednUUNzpUrtO2jXTWcqA@comcast.com>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b417ac$udc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e648dae.130455042@news.optonline.net> <b42tot$m5i$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e65abed.203745949@news.optonline.net> <b453s8$l94$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303051502.4a0f6cdd@posting.google.com> <b47p0i$6nb$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303070744.55577870@posting.google.com> <b4ai1o$hcr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cNicnUkKqMzcs_GjXTWcqw@comcast.com> <3e7007d1_2@127.0.0.1>`

Huh?  None of my comments (except for my name) are
quoted below.  I was not insulted (at least not on this occasion).

I personally use END's, and try to put a period whenever
I come to a logical end.  This treats the period as sort of
a super END.  Naturally, no NEXT SENTENCE.  No exceptions.

    I would support deleting NEXT SENTENCE personally.  It
is just too dangerous to be allowed to live.

    I might add that I don't feel like this subject is important enough
to preach about. You can come up with examples that demonstrate
how to get in trouble either way.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Software Archeology

_(thread depth: 28)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-24T09:11:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5ne8d$2hlo$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b417ac$udc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e648dae.130455042@news.optonline.net> <b42tot$m5i$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e65abed.203745949@news.optonline.net> <b453s8$l94$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303051502.4a0f6cdd@posting.google.com> <b47p0i$6nb$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303070744.55577870@posting.google.com> <b4ai1o$hcr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cNicnUkKqMzcs_GjXTWcqw@comcast.com> <3e7007d1_2@127.0.0.1> <7ZednUUNzpUrtO2jXTWcqA@comcast.com>`

```
The only contexts in which standard COBOL allows NEXT SENTENCE are in IF and
SEARCH statements, and ISO/IEC 1989:2002 designates its use in those
contexts (which, in standard COBOL, is everywhere, though some
implementations extend its use beyond these two verbs) as an "archaic
language element".

From Annex G of that document:  "The purpose of the archaic language element
designation is to discourage the use in new programs of some features ...
These elements are classified as archaic rather than obsolescent because
their use in existing programs might be too extensive to warrant removal in
the next revision of standard COBOL ... There is no plan for the removal of
archaic elements; however, should their use in program inventories become
insignificant, they may be considered for designation as obsolete by future
architects of standard COBOL."

I for one am not really comfortable with demanding that all programs
containing NEXT SENTENCE suddenly become uncompilable on that basis alone.
While the contract programmers might love the opportunity to make lots and
lots of money reengineering existing code to avoid the constructs, I am not
convinced that requiring the companies to whom that code belongs to expend
those funds fixing code that would otherwise work just fine is defensible on
economic grounds.

    -Chuck Stevens

news:7ZednUUNzpUrtO2jXTWcqA@comcast.com...

> quoted below.  I was not insulted (at least not on this occasion).
…[146 more quoted lines elided]…
> > ---= 19 East/West-Coast Specialized Servers - Total Privacy via
Encryption
> =---

```

- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b417ac$udc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e648dae.130455042@news.optonline.net> <b42tot$m5i$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e65abed.203745949@news.optonline.net> <b453s8$l94$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303051502.4a0f6cdd@posting.google.com> <b47p0i$6nb$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303070744.55577870@posting.google.com> <b4ai1o$hcr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cNicnUkKqMzcs_GjXTWcqw@comcast.com> <3e7007d1_2@127.0.0.1> <7ZednUUNzpUrtO2jXTWcqA@comcast.com> <b5ne8d$2hlo$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

Chuck Stevens <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message news:b5ne8d$2hlo$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...

> The only contexts in which standard COBOL allows NEXT SENTENCE are in IF and
> SEARCH statements

NEXT SENTENCE is an imperative statement.

As such, it can be used in the AT END and/or INVALID KEY clauses
of READ, WRITE, RETURN, RECEIVE, REWRITE, DELETE and START statements,
as well as in ON SIZE ERROR and ON OVERFLOW clauses.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Software Archeology

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-24T16:29:45-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5o0sp$t9n$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b417ac$udc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e648dae.130455042@news.optonline.net> <b42tot$m5i$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e65abed.203745949@news.optonline.net> <b453s8$l94$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303051502.4a0f6cdd@posting.google.com> <b47p0i$6nb$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303070744.55577870@posting.google.com> <b4ai1o$hcr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cNicnUkKqMzcs_GjXTWcqw@comcast.com> <3e7007d1_2@127.0.0.1> <7ZednUUNzpUrtO2jXTWcqA@comcast.com> <b5ne8d$2hlo$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <blLfa.22703$ja4.1555906@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Next Sentence" most definitely is *NOT* an imperative statement in the
current, past, or recently approved 2002 COBOL Standard.

At least one vendor (Micro Focus) *does* include an extension of a "next
sentence" imperative statement.  Some other vendors allow this PHRASE to be
used with other verbs - besides IF and SEARCH.

If you have a compiler that accepts this as an imperative statement, run a
compile with its ANSI flagging option turned on (such as FLAGSTD(H) with
Micro Focus) and you will see that this is a non-conforming (and therefore
non-portable) use.

This was all "discussed" in a recent thread (or two) in comp.lang.cobol.
Check the archives for all the "gruesome" details.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Software Archeology

_(thread depth: 31)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-24T14:51:42-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5o25v$2vdo$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b417ac$udc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e648dae.130455042@news.optonline.net> <b42tot$m5i$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e65abed.203745949@news.optonline.net> <b453s8$l94$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303051502.4a0f6cdd@posting.google.com> <b47p0i$6nb$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303070744.55577870@posting.google.com> <b4ai1o$hcr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <cNicnUkKqMzcs_GjXTWcqw@comcast.com> <3e7007d1_2@127.0.0.1> <7ZednUUNzpUrtO2jXTWcqA@comcast.com> <b5ne8d$2hlo$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <blLfa.22703$ja4.1555906@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <b5o0sp$t9n$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I agree, Bill.  I checked back as far as the '74 standard, and NEXT SENTENCE
is documented for and allowed in ONLY in IF and SEARCH...WHEN there, and is
definitely not in the list of "imperative statements".   All indications are
that '68 COBOL is similar in this respect.  It's referred to as the NEXT
SENTENCE *phrase* in the indices of the '74, '85 and 2002 standards.  All
indications are that '68 is similar.

    -Chuck Stevens

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Software Archeology

_(thread depth: 30)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-25T22:50:01+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5o261$b8g$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <7ZednUUNzpUrtO2jXTWcqA@comcast.com> <b5ne8d$2hlo$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <blLfa.22703$ja4.1555906@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Hugh Candlin wrote:

>> The only contexts in which standard COBOL allows NEXT SENTENCE are in IF
                              ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
>> and SEARCH statements
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> as well as in ON SIZE ERROR and ON OVERFLOW clauses.

No, that is not true.  In __STANDARD__ Cobol (ie '61, '68, '74, '85, '02) 
NEXT SENTENCE is a clause of the IF and SEARCH statements.

Some compilers, such as MicroFocus, also allow it to be used as a statement 
when standard conformance is not set on.

In standard Cobol the NEXT SENTENCE clause also cannot be used if the IF or 
SEARCH has a scope terminator END-IF or END-SEARCH.

Some compilers also allow this if standard conformance is not set on.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Software Archeology

_(thread depth: 32)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-25T10:50:55-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5q1dh$999$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <7ZednUUNzpUrtO2jXTWcqA@comcast.com> <b5ne8d$2hlo$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <blLfa.22703$ja4.1555906@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <b5o261$b8g$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b5pqi0$73i$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
A matter of "terminology" (I think)

All Standard conforming compilers *do* have a "switch" for CHECKING
conformance of source code to ANSI/ISO rules.  However, some have methods of
"limiting" syntax to conforming code - while others only "flag" extension
syntax.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b45kje$r27$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <b417ac$udc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e648dae.130455042@news.optonline.net> <b42tot$m5i$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e65abed.203745949@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:


>>You have subsequently initiated and argued at length focussing on full
>>stops.
> 
> I was responding to posts from others.

What you have done repeatedly was to make a direct relationship between 
'using full stops' and 'bad programming, poor design, use of GO TO and 
PERFORM THRU'.

In fact you even went further accusing those that used full stops of 
_removing_ scope terminators.

Your attitude has been specific as to each use of that key pushing back 
programming 10 years.

Complete arrant nonsense.  You have this fault in your logic that goes from 
the specific to the general.  The best that you could argue is:

  "I saw this really bad programmer and he used full-stops"

(or even several or many) but you conclude:

  "Therefore everyone who uses full-stops is a bad programmer".

You have done this in several discussions: "My site doesn't do new 
development in Cobol, therefore all sites are using C/C++".

> I know why it's such a hot issue to mainframers. They see the push for no
> periods as an assault on their Culture. 

Actually, I am not a mainframer, I do not use full stops beyond the 
minimum, I do not have a 'culture'.

> If periods fall, so too will
> PERFORM .. THRU and other indefensible features. As a result, they defend
> periods tooth and nail.

I argue against PERFORM THRU, GO TO, SECTIONs and other older features.

My argument with you is that you insult people with no justification at all 
and you attempt to be a dictator.

>>That is your _opinion_.  It may well be useful for churning out batch
>>programs.  My programs are not batch programs, they don't have "start,
>>process, end" in the same way at all.
> 
> Yes they do, 

Well there you go, then.  All this time I was quite wrong about my 
programs, and all it took was someone who has never seen any of them to 
reeal the 'truth'.

> internally. You have a bunch of 'callbacks', 

Do I ? I don't seem to be able to find them, perhaps you could quote some 
line numbers so that I can find them.

> " Use the company as a design model for your program or system. High level
> code should make high level decisions; low level code should do the grunt
> work and refer exceptions to higher levels for handling.

Thank you for the lecture.  In which way do the think that my systems don't 
actually take appropriate actions.

I know: I don't use GOBACK - proof positive that I do a STOP RUN in _every_ 
program, especially when it can't cope anymore.

> For real-time applications, use the network model, in which modules pass
> messages to each other in response to events. Think of each module as a
> company department, within which there can still be a hierarchy."

Thank you for the lecture.  Rearrange the following words into a well known 
phrase or saying: 'eggs, grandma, suck ...'
```

On  3-Mar-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> I did .. in Best Practices. Most mainframe managers yawn because they regard
> COBOL as maintenance-only, not for new development. The fault rests on the
> shoulders of traditionalists, who dominate this newsgroup. They'd rather be
> marginalized than learn to program well. Problem is, they're dragging me down
> with themselves.

What evidence do you have that we do not use CoBOL for new development?  
Because we don't all use your style of coding?

How is our failure to be convinced that your way is best for us to use "dragging
you down with ourselves"?

How is it that when we change our coding and programming styles - but not in
step with the way you code - that we are "traditionalists"?
```

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e6548d1.178369372@news.optonline.net>`

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On  3-Mar-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Because we don't all use your style of coding?

My evidence is large organizations where I have worked, where all new
development is in C and managers are primarily C programmers who regard COBOL as
Legacy. 

>How is our failure to be convinced that your way is best for us to use
"dragging
>you down with ourselves"?

Because COBOL has been marginalized in these organizations. When I suggest
writing a new application in COBOL, they turn up their noses and say 'why don't
you just write it in C?'. They also say, 'if we have to make extensive changes,
it's easier to rewrite the whole thing in C++'

You 'defenders of the period' are to blame. But for you, COBOL would be a modern
OO-enabled language
```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e65c305.209658431@news.optonline.net...


> I thought posting on CLC was 'public review'. Not good enough, huh?
>
> I used to answer newbie questions on now-defunct askme.com, where I was #1
rated
> COBOL expert. I know .. amateur hour.
>
> I'm not plugged into the standards process. Your point is well taken ..
being an
> insider is the only way to effect real change.

The nice thing about being "plugged into the standards process" is that in
many ways doing so pretty much consists of "suit up and show up".  If you
are willing to commit to participating in the process, I would say the
primary criterion for being a participatory member of the process is
participation.

That's one reason I resent so deeply the actions of those who are sitting on
the sidelines and throwing tomatoes at the players, the process or the
results in preference to actually walking out onto the field and
participating.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>The nice thing about being "plugged into the standards process" is that in
>many ways doing so pretty much consists of "suit up and show up".  If you
>are willing to commit to participating in the process, I would say the
>primary criterion for being a participatory member of the process is
>participation.

That's a problem for us independent contractors. We get paid for hours worked,
period. No sick time, no vacation, no sojurns to committee meetings. 

>That's one reason I resent so deeply the actions of those who are sitting on
>the sidelines and throwing tomatoes at the players, the process or the
>results in preference to actually walking out onto the field and
>participating.

Being a hands-on kinda guy, I whole-heartedly agree with your sentiment. 

In this 'age of the internet', one would think committee business could be
accomplished over the network, with no need for in-person meetings. To the best
of my knowledge (I could be wrong), that's not the case. It looks like there are
annual meetings at vacation resorts. Correct me if I'm wrong.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T08:23:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b47sof$13se$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <b3oehr$2m9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3ohmm$4kv$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3e62b8c5.10396698@news.optonline.net> <b3ukuu$qvk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e630abb.31381691@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303031047.3dc3a55b@posting.google.com> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b42h14$g14$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6548d1.178369372@news.optonline.net> <b43jbp$rp3$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3e65c305.209658431@news.optonline.net> <b455nd$29p5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e66a2e4.47524270@news.optonline.net>`

```
(responses interspersed):

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e66a2e4.47524270@news.optonline.net...
> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
> >The nice thing about being "plugged into the standards process" is that
in
> >many ways doing so pretty much consists of "suit up and show up".  If you
> >are willing to commit to participating in the process, I would say the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> That's a problem for us independent contractors. We get paid for hours
worked,
> period. No sick time, no vacation, no sojurns to committee meetings.

There are and historically have been several such independent contractors on
the J4 committee.  Some of them get corporate sponsorship for the costs of
their participation not from their employers but from other companies who
value their expertise; others pay out of their own pockets.  All seem to
have regarded participation as worthwhile, so I don't see the problem as
being a universal one so much as a personal one.

> Being a hands-on kinda guy, I whole-heartedly agree with your sentiment.

Well, then, suit up and show up.

> In this 'age of the internet', one would think committee business could be
> accomplished over the network, with no need for in-person meetings.

That's been explored, and even experimented with, but the experiments were
less than 100% successful.

>To the best of my knowledge (I could be wrong), that's not the case.

Documents are circulated, examined, and responded to outside of the
meetings.  Some of the business is handled electronically.  See
http://www.cobolportal.com/j4/ .  All the recent J4 documents and papers are
there.

> It looks like there are annual meetings at vacation resorts. Correct me if
I'm wrong.

Consider yourself corrected.

J4 meets every two months, usually for a week.  The meetings usually run
from 8:30AM to 6PM, except on the day we have a J4-TAG meeting to recommend
positions for INCITS / ANSI to carry forward to WG4.

The J4 meetings for this year were/are:  Carmel (January); San Antonio (this
month); Kokomo (June); Los Angeles area  (July); Chicago (most likely
northern suburbs; last time was in Lincolnshire) (September); and Las Vegas
(December).

Some (but hardly all) of the J4 meetings have been and will be held at what
arguably could be described as resort areas, but my experience has been that
there is rarely time during the course of the meeting to enjoy the offerings
of the resorts.

And in general a meeting site is chosen because the necessary facilities to
support the meeting (a variety of places to eat, an identified location for
copying and such, and food stores and pharmacies) are close by, because it
is in a location that is convenient for the person organizing the meeting to
make the necessary arrangements, including last-minute ones involving the
likes of the setup of the meeting room.  Usually the site is a relatively
short distance from the meeting host's home, precisely so that the organizer
can do everything possible to ensure that the meeting is properly supported
by the facility.

That was certainly the case when I hosted the July 2002 meeting in an
Embassy Suites in Anaheim.  I chose the location because the staff was more
interested in getting our business than any of the other Orange County
locations I contacted, because the costs were reasonable, and because it was
a ten-minute drive from my house (and therefore I knew where to locate the
likes of restaurants, copy centers, food stores and pharmacies).  I did not
choose it because it was close to Disneyland; in fact, I rejected the ones
that were convenient to Disneyland (or to anything else that might be
considered a resort, unless you think of the Richard Nixon Library and
Birthplace as being a resort!) on the grounds of cost.

The location I had initially preferred was actually several miles further
away from any of the resort venues.  The main reason I would have preferred
it were that there was a wide choice of restaurants (from fast-food to the
best French restaurant in Orange County) and ample shopping, all within
walking distance of the hotel.   However, the costs were higher and the
staff was apathetic.

I believe the priorities of others who have arranged J4 meetings are
similar.  I seriously doubt, for example, that the organizer of the June
2003 meeting has agonized for hours about facilitating the participants'
access to all the world-class resorts nearby to Kokomo.  I think one of the
reasons for the choice of the specific hotel in Kokomo is that there's a
Schlotzky's sandwich shop across the alleyway, convenient for lunch.  Works
for me.   Worked for me last time I was in Kokomo for a J4 meeting, too.

Closest thing I saw to a resort on the Kokomo trip was when I drove by what
had been the old Studebaker and South Bend watch factory sites when I went
up to visit friends in South Bend.  Walked to Union Square for dinner a time
or two during the San Francisco meeting.  Actually drove by the mission in
Carmel while I was there in January.  Ooooh!  Ahhhh!

   -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

_(thread depth: 24)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-07T11:27:58-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b4arru$4ki$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <b3oehr$2m9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3ohmm$4kv$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3e62b8c5.10396698@news.optonline.net> <b3ukuu$qvk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e630abb.31381691@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303031047.3dc3a55b@posting.google.com> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b42h14$g14$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6548d1.178369372@news.optonline.net> <b43jbp$rp3$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3e65c305.209658431@news.optonline.net> <b455nd$29p5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e66a2e4.47524270@news.optonline.net> <b47sof$13se$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e67e4f9.129989508@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e67e4f9.129989508@news.optonline.net...
> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

<<A meeting every two months is outside the parameters of my contractor
status. If I were 'senior staff' at Accenture or one of the other Big Five
contractors, it might be doable.>>

The contractors I am thinking of who do participate aren't "senior staff" at
Accenture or any of the other Big Five contractors, either, nor are they so
wealthy that the costs of membership and participation are trivial to them.
They just feel it's worth it.

<<I'm 'in the trenches' doing what I love best: cranking beautiful COBOL
code. >>

Well, I'm sure that's the way you view it, but that comment reminds me a
little too much of what I read about the styles of one Gilderoy Lockhart for
my comfort.  ;-)

Be that as it may, deciding what "beautiful COBOL code" is and writing down
and defending those rules so that everybody can follow them also seems to be
rather clearly something you like doing a whole lot.

If you didn't, I can't but help but wonder why you're bothering to post all
these messages instead of staying "in the trenches" writing COBOL code of
such transcendent glory as to send even the most casual reader into
paroxysms of admiration and adulation!  ;-)   Certainly helping compose the
rules that whole International Standards bodies vote upon as to what
constitutes admirable, or even beautiful, COBOL has to have more attraction
for you than mucking about down here in comp.lang.cobol with hoi polloi ...
;-)

There are also ways of participating in the process that don't require
membership in the committee.  Comments, suggestions, questions, comments and
the like are submitted all the time to J4, and discussed and acted upon by
the members.  Sometimes all it takes is an e-mail to the chair.

Or would you rather sit on the sidelines and throw tomatoes?   ;-)

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

_(thread depth: 25)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-08T00:43:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e693cab.62433118@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <b3oehr$2m9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3ohmm$4kv$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3e62b8c5.10396698@news.optonline.net> <b3ukuu$qvk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e630abb.31381691@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303031047.3dc3a55b@posting.google.com> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b42h14$g14$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6548d1.178369372@news.optonline.net> <b43jbp$rp3$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3e65c305.209658431@news.optonline.net> <b455nd$29p5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e66a2e4.47524270@news.optonline.net> <b47sof$13se$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e67e4f9.129989508@news.optonline.net> <b4arru$4ki$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3e67e4f9.129989508@news.optonline.net...

><<I'm 'in the trenches' doing what I love best: cranking beautiful COBOL
>code. >>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>my comfort.  ;-)

For the benefit of the incognosenti, Gilderoy Lockhart is an attention-seeking
ego-maniac in Harry Potter. I disagree with the premise that all who seek beauty
are driven by ego. 

>Be that as it may, deciding what "beautiful COBOL code" is and writing down
>and defending those rules so that everybody can follow them also seems to be
>rather clearly something you like doing a whole lot.

Yes, it is. I spent 20+ years mentoring programming teams on how to program
well. Nearly every individual went on to become programming manager at a big
shop. 

>If you didn't, I can't but help but wonder why you're bothering to post all
>these messages instead of staying "in the trenches" writing COBOL code of
>such transcendent glory as to send even the most casual reader into
>paroxysms of admiration and adulation!  ;-)   

I write Really Good Stuff every day. Perhaps I should post clips here so others
could learn. 

I know you're being sarcastic/ironic; I hope not patronizing/mocking. 

>Certainly helping compose the
>rules that whole International Standards bodies vote upon as to what
>constitutes admirable, or even beautiful, COBOL has to have more attraction
>for you than mucking about down here in comp.lang.cobol with hoi polloi ...
>;-)

Now it does sound mocking. 

>There are also ways of participating in the process that don't require
>membership in the committee.  Comments, suggestions, questions, comments and
>the like are submitted all the time to J4, and discussed and acted upon by
>the members.  Sometimes all it takes is an e-mail to the chair.

Good idea. I'll consider writing some emails.But I don't have much quarrel with
the standards. My complaints are against practitioners who insist on writing
COBOL as they did in the '70s and who codify their mediocrity as 'shop
standards'. Thou will'st prefix every paragraph name with a four-digit number;
thou must do perform thru. Gimme a break. Their calendar is running thirty years
slow.

>Or would you rather sit on the sidelines and throw tomatoes?   ;-)

I've thrown few tomatoes at the standard. I throw them at the Old Guard when
they defend periods, static linking and other holdovers from the Good Old Days.
```

see the same thing in America in politics.  People complain about the
government and the politicians but when it comes time to vote and try

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-09T21:49:11
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e6bb12e_1@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <b3oehr$2m9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3ohmm$4kv$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3e62b8c5.10396698@news.optonline.net> <b3ukuu$qvk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e630abb.31381691@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303031047.3dc3a55b@posting.google.com> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b42h14$g14$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6548d1.178369372@news.optonline.net> <b43jbp$rp3$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3e65c305.209658431@news.optonline.net> <b455nd$29p5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
news:b455nd$29p5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...

> That's one reason I resent so deeply the actions of those who are sitting
on
> the sidelines and throwing tomatoes at the players, the process or the
> results in preference to actually walking out onto the field and
> participating.
>
>

Sitting here with my case of tomatoes, I have to make the following
observations <G>:

1. The game is not open; it costs you to play. (However, some players have
noted that being a player is worth another $15 an hour on their contract
charge out rate...I see this as a measure of how sad  (and easily impressed)
some of the fans are.)

2. There is no profit from the Gate; even though a ticket in the bleachers
costs $200, and to be a player really requires Corporate sponsorship and
$800 up front. The "non-profit" governing body have no selection criteria on
players, other than that they can pay the fees. The non-profits are not
channelled back into promoting the game as a whole, because there aren't
any...yeah, right....

3. Despite the enthusiasm of the players, there is little direction and it
it is very reminiscent of the croquet game in "Alice in Wonderland" where
flamingos were used for mallets. The result is that  a huge amount of energy
and talent (not to mention Corporate funding) is simply wasted.

4. The game moves so slowly that it takes 18 years to get around the
diamond.

5. As many of the spectators rely on the result of the game in order to make
a living, you can hardly be surprised if they throw tomatoes. In fact, most
of us have become so bored with it that we can't even be bothered to throw
tomatoes any more...and many of us have watched our jobs disappear as the
game has fallen into disrepute, so that we can't even afford to buy tomatoes
and throw them away...

6. I have acquired a taste for tomato sandwiches and have decided this is a
much better use for them than wasting them on an irrelevance that has little
remaining credibility <G>. Who cares? Apart from the vested interests and
the "non-profit" Standards body, have we seen people queueing up to get into
the game? How many $200 tickets have been sold?

Thank God we have some enlightened Vendors  who have kept the game going
OUTSIDE the official stadium. Listening to the people in the street and
watching what is happening in the Marketplace. These are the people I'd
invite to share a tomato sandwich...

But then, I would say that...<G>

Pete.




-----------== Posted via Newsfeed.Com - Uncensored Usenet News ==----------
   http://www.newsfeed.com       The #1 Newsgroup Service in the World!
-----= Over 100,000 Newsgroups - Unlimited Fast Downloads - 19 Servers =-----
```

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>2. There is no profit from the Gate; even though a ticket in the bleachers
>costs $200, and to be a player really requires Corporate sponsorship and
>$800 up front. 

Much more. A week's lost wages $2K plus expenses $1K+ prices a season ticket at
$12-15K per year. That's small potatoes for Corporate; a new Saturn every year
for us grunts. If you're younger than me, make that 11 cups of overpriced
Starbucks coffee per day, 30 bottles of water per day,  a new Beemer every two
years,or a Gap shirt every day.  

> Listening to the people in the street and
>watching what is happening in the Marketplace. These are the people I'd
>invite to share a tomato sandwich...

Lots of verbs and imagery. You can write. You should author a book instructing
neophytes on how to write Good COBOL. 

"A man never shows his own character so plainly as by the way he portrays
another's."  Jean Paul Richter

"Press Enter to exit." (a technodweeb)

Robert
```

Robert Wagner wrote:

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

That is not evidence that _Howard_'s company is not writing new development

coded in a way that everyone else can understand.

freedom of choice.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

_(thread depth: 20)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T11:35:16-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2B8418D4DAF56B45.35FAE32A0E2CDFF1.1052D080769F84A5@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b42h14$g14$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6548d1.178369372@news.optonline.net> <b43nlu$1c0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e65c369.209758959@news.optonline.net>`

```
On Wed, 05 Mar 2003 09:30:13 GMT, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
enlightened us:

>Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>imagination; it happens in the real world every day. 

Maybe in *YOUR* world it does, but it doesn't in mine or many, many
others who participate in this newsgroup.  At my company we would
never even consider using C or C++ for new development on our
mainframe.  I believe it is sparingly used on our server applications,
but even there the developers may use intel assembler or other less
buggy languages.  But on the mainframe, 95% of our new development in
batch and online programs is done in Cobol.  The other 5% is in
Assembler.  There are no discussions to do anything different.  Don't
make generalizations you can't prove or which are just not true.

As I recall two years ago when Mr. Klein and others were asking for
comments on the as yet to be voted on standard, I don't recall seeing
you pushing your comments or agenda in here or in comments to the
committee.  Now you come around acting all huffy and puffy and trying
to belittle everyone else.  And you wonder why you've gotten the
comments you've received.  The simple answer is, you've earned them.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Bumper Sticker seen in Homer, Alaska:

"Homer, Alaska: A Quaint Little Drinking Village, 
With a Fishing Problem."
 ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T03:50:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e66a63c.48380647@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b42h14$g14$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6548d1.178369372@news.optonline.net> <b43nlu$1c0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e65c369.209758959@news.optonline.net> <2B8418D4DAF56B45.35FAE32A0E2CDFF1.1052D080769F84A5@lp.airnews.net>`

```
SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote:

>On Wed, 05 Mar 2003 09:30:13 GMT, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
>enlightened us:
>
>>Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>> The marginalization of COBOL is not a figment of my
>>imagination; it happens in the real world every day. 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>make generalizations you can't prove or which are just not true.

Where I heard those comments most was at US Department of Education, where we
originated half the 16M student loans in the US. The other half were originated
by small private sector lenders who complained they couldn't compete because our
fees were so low. 

Our fees covered expenses plus a small 'profit'. They were lower than private
sector's because we were more efficient. We originated a loan 'untouched by
human hands' and 'paperless' via a Web site. Even the all-important signature on
the loan document was electronic.  Private sector lenders still required paper
documents and human approval. I imagined them running old-fashioned COBOL which
couldn't be changed to keep pace with us. 

I'm as cynical as most about government, but this was one case where government
got it right and private sector got it inefficient.

FWIW, our default rate was less than one percent. Most people are incorrect when
they imagine a high default rate on student loans. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

_(thread depth: 23)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-07T00:35:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e67dd75.128065165@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b42h14$g14$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6548d1.178369372@news.optonline.net> <b43nlu$1c0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e65c369.209758959@news.optonline.net> <2B8418D4DAF56B45.35FAE32A0E2CDFF1.1052D080769F84A5@lp.airnews.net> <3e66a63c.48380647@news.optonline.net> <b47tcn$149h$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message

>> I imagined them running old-fashioned COBOL which couldn't be changed to
>keep pace with us.
>
>You imagined this?   You *imagined* this?   And from that you drew
>conclusions?   I suppose I shouldn't be surprised, after all.

I draw these conclusions from seeing COBOL code at banks, credit unions and
businesses in general. 

>> FWIW, our default rate was less than one percent. Most people are
>incorrect when
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>default rate is much higher than that for student loans.  What is "high"?

I should have been more precise in terminology. A 'default', by government
definition, occurs when a payment is missed within the year. Using that
definition, the 'default rate' is between 8 and 10 percent. 

That doesn't mean the debt was written off as bad. Government and other lenders
can pursue collection for years. Government has an advantage in this regard
because it can garnishee tax refunds to repay the debt. After the dust settles,
government's 'bad debt write-off' is less than one percent. 

Telechek's 'uncollectables' were also less than one percent. So too were a major
credit card company where I used to work. 

I'd say high is five percent. I'd expect to see that at credit card companies
such as Providian, which cater to bad risk customers and then piss them off with
unreasonable fees. Capitol One has been successful catering to the same customer
base and treating them with respect. 

When I worked at the major credit card company, we were rejecting 50% of
applications .. at great cost for credit checks. I proposed reaching into the
input hopper, grabbing 100 applications at random, and approving them
sight-unseen. There was only one criterion: they had to be of legal age.
Management accepted my experiment. I put them in a special billing cycle so we
could compare their 'performance' against the average of all cardholders. Guess
what .. it was exactly the same. Our 50% rejects contained no predictive power. 

Did we stop doing it? Of course not. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Opinions from the veterans or experts

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T15:16:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b454d3$lk7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <b3oehr$2m9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3ohmm$4kv$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3e62b8c5.10396698@news.optonline.net> <b3ukuu$qvk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e630abb.31381691@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303031047.3dc3a55b@posting.google.com> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b42h14$g14$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6548d1.178369372@news.optonline.net>`

```

On  4-Mar-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> >What evidence do you have that we do not use CoBOL for new development?
> >Because we don't all use your style of coding?
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> as Legacy.

Large organizations where I have worked have new CoBOL development.   Sure there
are Java programmers with these same attitudes - but that doesn't have anything
to do with the reality that new business development needs to get done in the
best platform available.   These guys also believe C is obsolete - and to tell
the truth in our shops it pretty much is.


> >How is our failure to be convinced that your way is best for us to use
> "dragging
> >you down with ourselves"?
>
> Because COBOL has been marginalized in these organizations.

But not in organizations I have worked in.

> When I suggest
> writing a new application in COBOL, they turn up their noses and say 'why
> don't
> you just write it in C?'. They also say, 'if we have to make extensive
> changes, it's easier to rewrite the whole thing in C++'

It is much easier to maintain CoBOL than C or C++.

> You 'defenders of the period' are to blame. But for you, COBOL would be a
> modern OO-enabled language

So how did we stop it from being a modern OO-enabled language?   By having
periods in our code?  By figuring that removing periods did nothing to make the
code better?

But even still, there is LOTS of new CoBOL being written.  I suspect most of it
isn't OO, as the advantages of OO for mainframe business applications are not as
obvious as the costs.   But that is not at all related to our not accepting your
mandate that we remove periods from our otherwise well-written code.
```

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e66ab61.49697771@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b3dpmd$8fa$1@ctb-nnrp2.saix.net> <b3oehr$2m9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b3ohmm$4kv$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3e62b8c5.10396698@news.optonline.net> <b3ukuu$qvk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e630abb.31381691@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303031047.3dc3a55b@posting.google.com> <3e63f77a.91997621@news.optonline.net> <b42h14$g14$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6548d1.178369372@news.optonline.net> <b454d3$lk7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>It is much easier to maintain CoBOL than C or C++.

Having maintained both, I'd say it's about the same. If anything,  easier in
C/C++ because code quality is better. SOME C programmers try to write beautiful
code whereas all COBOL programmers write 'just get the job done with minimal
effort' code, which often means cut-and-paste redundancy. 

I was awe-struck by the beauty of one C program I had to maintain. I wished I
could shake the author's hand. I've never seen a COBOL program which evoked that
feeling.
```

I said nothing about Unisys, pro or con, because I know little about it. 

>That characterization also strikes me as not a little demeaning of my

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

Per your request, I added this to my Best Practices paper:

Unix and PC) and Fujitsu (primarily PC). Omitting platforms such as Unisys,
