###### ↳ ↳ ↳ IBM and COMP-3 (was: Interesting change in "bounds checking"

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-01T14:40:50-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3r5so$t0h$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3jb1e$2g8k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3jp3j$9fq$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e5ecf44.105751068@news.optonline.net> <b3ooe1$3c4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e60f6fb.247010783@news.optonline.net>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e60f6fb.247010783@news.optonline.net...
> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
> >"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
> >news:3e5ecf44.105751068@news.optonline.net...
> >
 <snip>
>
> I said we practitioners cannot write ANSI-conforming code because we'd
have to
> give up comp-3 and pointers. That wasn't a reference to Unisys. I was
thinking
> of IBM mainframes in re comp-3, and all platforms in re pointers.
>
 <snip>

All ANSI/ISO '85 Standard compilers (including *all* IBM compilers) have
supported the "portable"

     USAGE PACKED-DECIMAL

for 17 years now (or so).  Therefore, "reliance" on COMP-3 should be a
non-issue.

  USAGE POINTER

is an extension - but a fairly common one - today. It is part of the 2002
Standard.  Interestingly enough, the rules for its use (as an extension)
have changed over the years and have NOT been portable from implementation
to implementation.  The biggest non-portable issue was whether or not you
could do a

    Set pointer-data-item to Address of Working-Storage-data-item

statement or not.  Micro Focus introduced this fairly early - IBM has only
supported it in the last few years.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM and COMP-3 (was: Interesting change in "bounds checking"

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-02T11:05:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e61d7a4.43735350@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3jb1e$2g8k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3jp3j$9fq$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e5ecf44.105751068@news.optonline.net> <b3ooe1$3c4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e60f6fb.247010783@news.optonline.net> <b3r5so$t0h$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3e60f6fb.247010783@news.optonline.net...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> >news:3e5ecf44.105751068@news.optonline.net...

>All ANSI/ISO '85 Standard compilers (including *all* IBM compilers) have
>supported the "portable"
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>non-issue.

Fair enough, although I've never seen PACKED-DECIMAL used in a mainframe shop.

>  USAGE POINTER
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>to implementation.  

I've found it to be very portable. Fujitsu tried to introduce the irregular
notation 'func addr(x)', but found they had to support 'address of x'. To the
best of my knowledge, I could be wrong, their manuals still describe the former
and neglect mentioning that the latter is available. 

>The biggest non-portable issue was whether or not you
>could do a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>supported it in the last few years.

Yes, how stupid. IBMers were forced to write a 'do nothing' function which
returned its argument in order to get addressability to working-storage. 

Here is an example of why a program would reasonably want to do this. Suppose
you're going for an environment variable. If it doesn't exist, you want to set
it to a default in working-storage. You'd write:

call 'getenv' using variable-name, pointer-to-variable
if  pointer-to-variable equal to null
    set pointer-to-variable to address of default-value
end-if

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting change in "bounds checking"

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-03T13:55:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b40j0a$283e$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3jb1e$2g8k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3jp3j$9fq$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e5ecf44.105751068@news.optonline.net> <b3ooe1$3c4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e60f6fb.247010783@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e60f6fb.247010783@news.optonline.net...

> I said nothing about Unisys, pro or con, because I know little about it.

You have, however, repeatedly described what "all mainframes" do and used
that as a reason for your "best practice".   Quite often, Unisys
"mainframes" don't do what "all mainframes" do in at least a significant
number of the cases in which you defend your practice on the grounds of its
universality.   That strikes me as insulting, perhaps even contemptuous,
both to Unisys and to the users who find Unisys equipment useful tools in
their business.

> I said we practitioners cannot write ANSI-conforming code because we'd
have to
> give up comp-3 and pointers.

You have to give up both of these features if you want to write COBOL that
can be generally presumed to be portable in the first place.

The first is an extension everywhere it appears.  USAGE PACKED-DECIMAL
requires a radix of 10, and minimum storage for each digit (which pretty
much means 4 bits, I think).  That's pretty close to COMP-3, though it might
not be identical in all implementations.  Still, its standard COBOL and is a
better choice for portability.

> That wasn't a reference to Unisys. I was thinking
> of IBM mainframes in re comp-3, and all platforms in re pointers.

Unisys doesn't have USAGE POINTER either; nobody's asked for this feature.
I suspect the 2200 OOCOBOL compiler may have it, but no user has even hinted
at a desire for this feature so far as I know.

Are you certain that Unisys MCP/AS platforms are the *only*
currently-supported and actively-marketed platforms anywhere on this planet
that don't support USAGE POINTER in '85- (or even '74-)compliant COBOL
compilers?

> Per your request, I added this to my Best Practices paper:
> "This paper describes COBOL on IBM (primarily mainframe), Micro Focus
(primarily
> Unix and PC) and Fujitsu (primarily PC). Omitting platforms such as
Unisys,
> Tandem and others implies no disparagement of those cultures."

No, that's not much of a caveat.  You appear to be trying to produce an
"industry best practices" paper.  It's your use of words like "all" and
"every" to describe your preferred list of implementations that's
disparaging of those implementations whether that disparagement is intended
by you or not.

For example, consider the fact that Unisys doesn't support USAGE POINTER
like "all mainframes" do.

One could characterize this as "Because USAGE POINTER is not part of
standard COBOL (until the 2002 standard), if portability is a significant
consideration this construct should not be used on implementations
conformant with earlier standards".   One could even say " ... consideration
this construct should be used with careful in-line documentation indicating
its implementation-specific nature and the possibility some code would need
to be changed if taken to a different platform."

But one could also write "No programmer in his right mind would ever use
USAGE POINTER;all real implementations avoid it like the plague because no
meaningful implementation provides support for it."

The latter is a "reductio ad absurdum" of what you have had to say about the
feature content of Unisys compilers.

Thus, your repeated discussions of what "all platforms" and "all mainframes"
provide is not much mitigated by your suggestion at the beginning that the
likes of Unisys and Tandem aren't worthy of your specific attention in this
paper.

That's my point.   The use of "all" and "every" in these discussions has
dismissed outright those that don't meet your criteria.

Your explicit *intent* may not be to disparage Unisys, Tandem, or any other
implementation that fails to meet your criteria of what "mainframes" do, but
by implicitly excluding them from eligibility in your "best practices" list
you do treat them, and their user base, with contempt.  When you add this
suggested text, what you end up doing is changing the sense to something
like "when I say 'all', I mean 'everybody that's anybody', when I say
'mainframe', I mean 'every real mainframe'.", and that *demonstrates* your
contempt, whatever your intent was.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting change in "bounds checking"

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T10:08:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e647b03.125676020@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3jb1e$2g8k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3jp3j$9fq$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e5ecf44.105751068@news.optonline.net> <b3ooe1$3c4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e60f6fb.247010783@news.optonline.net> <b40j0a$283e$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>their business.

I understand your feeling because I worked on non-IBM mainframes for 15 years,
from 1965 through 1979. Two were Burroughs Medium Systems, where I rewrote the
print spooler (my version was adopted by at least ten other installations) and
the communications module of the OS (in COBOL, for the first Telechek system). I
worked with the designers of MCPV (in Pasadena) and was on a user-group (BOMP)
committee evaluating enhancement requests. I also worked on platforms made by
Honeywell (as a Honeywell systems engineer), CII/Bull and RCA. 

During that period, I resented the attitude of IBMers. They marginalized us by
talking as though IBM mainframes were the only 'real' computers. And they made
it difficult to find a job when they discounted non-IBM experience as being
irrelevant. For instance, it didn't matter that the RCA Spectra was a drop-in
clone of the IBM-360, and that I knew its systems internals inside and out,
having written some of them. That experience didn't count because it wasn't
'real' IBM. 

Now I hear you expressing the same resentment I felt then. 

The Best Practices paper had only two references to 'mainframe'. One said IBM
COBOL runs primarily on mainframes; the other said free-form was available on
most compilers except mainframe. The latter is incorrect because free-form is
not available on _any_ IBM compiler. I changed the sentence to say so. 

>Are you certain that Unisys MCP/AS platforms are the *only*
>currently-supported and actively-marketed platforms anywhere on this planet
>that don't support USAGE POINTER in '85- (or even '74-)compliant COBOL
>compilers?

I have limited the scope of the paper to IBM, Micro Focus, Fujitsu and Realia. I
have run benchmarks on others, but don't claim to have in-depth knowledge. 

>> Per your request, I added this to my Best Practices paper:
>> "This paper describes COBOL on IBM (primarily mainframe), Micro Focus
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>by you or not.

The paper has no references to "mainstream", nor to "all" or "every" in a
disparaging, exclusionary context. If it once did, I changed that language weeks
ago. 

>For example, consider the fact that Unisys doesn't support USAGE POINTER
>like "all mainframes" do.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>to be changed if taken to a different platform."

I'm sensitive to portability, but I write about _practice_ rather than theory.
Because pointers use exactly the same syntax on Micro Focus, IBM and Fujitsu,
and are on the horizon for others, that's good enough for me. It is unreasonable
to avoid such a useful feature solely because it lacks the imprimatur of
ANSI/ISO. 

>Your explicit *intent* may not be to disparage Unisys, Tandem, or any other
>implementation that fails to meet your criteria of what "mainframes" do, but
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>contempt, whatever your intent was.

I'm not here to glorify IBM mainframes. That role is played by many other
posters. They are easily identified when they reference four-letter mainframe
acronyms with accompanying attitude those tools are universally known to
Professional Programmers. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting change in "bounds checking"

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T08:14:40-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b42jdg$hv7$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3jb1e$2g8k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3jp3j$9fq$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e5ecf44.105751068@news.optonline.net> <b3ooe1$3c4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e60f6fb.247010783@news.optonline.net> <b40j0a$283e$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e647b03.125676020@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e647b03.125676020@news.optonline.net...

> The Best Practices paper had only two references to 'mainframe'.

I was referring more to your recent postings in CLC and their implications
as to what "real mainframes" were.

 I have limited the scope of the paper to IBM, Micro Focus, Fujitsu and
Realia. I
> have run benchmarks on others, but don't claim to have in-depth knowledge.

> The paper has no references to "mainstream", nor to "all" or "every" in a
> disparaging, exclusionary context. If it once did, I changed that language
weeks
> ago.

Your recent postings have such references, however.

> I'm sensitive to portability, but I write about _practice_ rather than
theory.
> Because pointers use exactly the same syntax on Micro Focus, IBM and
Fujitsu,
> and are on the horizon for others, that's good enough for me. It is
unreasonable
> to avoid such a useful feature solely because it lacks the imprimatur of
> ANSI/ISO.

It is unreasonable to use such a feature in code designed for portability if
there is any detail difference in the *implementation and behavior* (not
just the syntax) on various platforms.  The specific comment to which I was
referring is "I was thinking of IBM mainframes in re comp-3, and all
platforms in re pointers" in this thread.  *We* have certainly been talking
about platforms other than those you list above.

 > I'm not here to glorify IBM mainframes. That role is played by many other
> posters. They are easily identified when they reference four-letter
mainframe
> acronyms with accompanying attitude those tools are universally known to
> Professional Programmers.

You are, as your stated purpose, here to promulgate (and perhaps glorify)
your own world view as to what is and is not appropriate as industry-wide
practice, and what is and is not useful on all platforms.   Some of the
features you insist are industry-wide and therefore suitable for use
everywhere started life as IBM extensions and were copied by SOME other
implementations, as being industry-wide practice.  There exist "platforms"
that don't have pointers.  The fact that "all platforms" have them says
something specific to this readership about the offerings of any
manufacturer of systems or operating environments that don't.

Look to your *behavior*, not to your *intentions* for clues as to the
message you are presenting.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting change in "bounds checking"

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T10:04:09-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b42pqq$mjv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3jb1e$2g8k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3jp3j$9fq$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e5ecf44.105751068@news.optonline.net> <b3ooe1$3c4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e60f6fb.247010783@news.optonline.net> <b40j0a$283e$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e647b03.125676020@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e647b03.125676020@news.optonline.net...

> I understand your feeling because I worked on non-IBM mainframes for 15
years,
> from 1965 through 1979. Two were Burroughs Medium Systems, where I rewrote
the
> print spooler ...

Well, let's see.  I would be surprised if it were the V Series offering that
was compliant with ANSI X3.23-1974, which I don't believe was released until
after you left non-IBM environments (I may be wrong on this, but in any
event it wasn't out for very long by the end date).  And no compiler
compliant with ANSI X3.23-1985 ever reached the market for the V Series
(though one was originally planned and significant development effort
expended on it).  That leaves the most likely candidate as "COBOLL",
replaced by the still-'68-compliant "COBOLV" sometime in the 1980's, I
think.

Those were the days.  You could do a division by zero and the machine
wouldn't hiccup.  You could, as I recall, do decimal arithmetic on some
*very* large fields.  You could (and actually still can, on MCP systems) put
alphanumeric data into numeric fields and treat it numerically with nary a
whimper from the hardware or software (such a situation should not pass an
IF NUMERIC test, then or now).  These extensions and these characteristics
have implications that suggest their use might not be portable.

You have previously mentioned what the Medium System did for certain
constructs in COBOL as your reason for eschewing those constructs.  I
believe almost all implementations still marketed today are based on *at
least* the '74 dialect of COBOL, and most on the '85 version.   I can't
imagine that the peculiarities of a particular dialect of 1968 COBOL would
remain an issue, one way or another, for "best practices" in 2003, and
certainly not for constructs whose behavior is well and carefully defined in
all the later standards.

> During that period, I resented the attitude of IBMers. They marginalized
us by
> talking as though IBM mainframes were the only 'real' computers.

Exactly as you have done in your recent correspondence in this forum,
marginalizing anyone whose implementation does not match IBM mainframes,
Fujitsu or Micro Focus in its extensions beyond the standards.

The point of writing ANSI-standard COBOL is to ensure portability, as I see
it.

To insist that "best practice" makes use of extensions like COMP-3 and USAGE
POINTER on "all platforms" is a clear indication to me of one of two things:
either platforms that don't provide them do not qualify as "all platforms",
or that it's "bad practice" even to use a machine that doesn't have them.

Personally I believe the provisions of "best practice" in COBOL should be
developed as a *subset* of standard COBOL, not using a number of extensions
from a particular subset of implementations.  Moreover, I believe that
subset should be one that is implemented in a compatible way in the widest
possible number of implementations.

If the follower of a COBOL "best practices" document that insists on the
universal value of an IBM (or Fujitsu or Micro Focus or Unisys) extension
makes use of that extension, he is making the possibility of migrating to
*any arbitrary* platform supporting standard COBOL potentially more costly
than if the author of the "best practices" document had limited his
"practices" list to those from a particular, widely- (or universally-)
implemented COBOL standard, eschewing the extensions insofar as practical.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting change in "bounds checking"

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T03:50:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e669641.44288594@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3jb1e$2g8k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3jp3j$9fq$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e5ecf44.105751068@news.optonline.net> <b3ooe1$3c4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e60f6fb.247010783@news.optonline.net> <b40j0a$283e$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e647b03.125676020@news.optonline.net> <b42pqq$mjv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>think.

Yes, I used COBOLL.

>Those were the days.  You could do a division by zero and the machine
>wouldn't hiccup.  You could, as I recall, do decimal arithmetic on some
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>have implications that suggest their use might not be portable.

I said in another thread that letting a called program do a STOP RUN was like
empowering a file clerk to declare the company bankrupt because the handle on
the file cabinet was jammed.  Allowing the hardware to abort a job because of
non-numeric data takes the simile a step lower. It's like empowering a
janitorial contractor to declare the company bankrupt because it was unhappy
with something found in a trash can. 

Unix systems and PCs don't have this feature. They raise an exception or treat
the field as zero. Only mainframes permit low-level hardware to make high-level
decisions. It is poor design. 

>You have previously mentioned what the Medium System did for certain
>constructs in COBOL as your reason for eschewing those constructs.  I
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>all the later standards.

That's why I don't claim to know anything about Unisys COBOL, even though I had
fifteen years' experience with obsolete versions. 

>> During that period, I resented the attitude of IBMers. They marginalized
>us by
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Fujitsu or Micro Focus in its extensions beyond the standards.

I refuse to limit Best Practices to the Lowest Common Demominator. Given that
pointers are available to >95% of practitioners, they have a place in the
tutorial.

>To insist that "best practice" makes use of extensions like COMP-3 and USAGE
>POINTER on "all platforms" is a clear indication to me of one of two things:
>either platforms that don't provide them do not qualify as "all platforms",
>or that it's "bad practice" even to use a machine that doesn't have them.

Let the reader/consumer make his own judgement .. with knowledge of what's
widely available. 

>Personally I believe the provisions of "best practice" in COBOL should be
>developed as a *subset* of standard COBOL, not using a number of extensions
>from a particular subset of implementations.  Moreover, I believe that
>subset should be one that is implemented in a compatible way in the widest
>possible number of implementations.

There is always a tradeoff between portability and 'full use' of the compiler.
You think portability should be sine qua non. I think the decision should be
based on cost vs. benefit. 

>If the follower of a COBOL "best practices" document that insists on the
>universal value of an IBM (or Fujitsu or Micro Focus or Unisys) extension
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>implemented COBOL standard, eschewing the extensions insofar as practical.

The _only_ non-standard practice I advocated was pointers, which will be
available 'tomorrow' on 2002-conforming compilers and are available today on the
most popular. Omitting reference to pointers would be a disservice to today's
practitioners. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting change in "bounds checking"

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T11:58:09+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<k96e6vcbfgao0euibbfo0pfr1cu3stac2d@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3jb1e$2g8k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3jp3j$9fq$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e5ecf44.105751068@news.optonline.net> <b3ooe1$3c4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e60f6fb.247010783@news.optonline.net> <b40j0a$283e$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e647b03.125676020@news.optonline.net> <b42pqq$mjv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e669641.44288594@news.optonline.net>`

```
On Thu, 06 Mar 2003 03:50:17 GMT robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

:>"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

:>>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
:>>news:3e647b03.125676020@news.optonline.net...

:>> That leaves the most likely candidate as "COBOLL",
:>>replaced by the still-'68-compliant "COBOLV" sometime in the 1980's, I
:>>think.

:>Yes, I used COBOLL.

:>>Those were the days.  You could do a division by zero and the machine
:>>wouldn't hiccup. 

How does one store infinity in a PIC S9(5) field?

Why is it good for a divide by zero to not raise an exception?

:>>                  You could, as I recall, do decimal arithmetic on some
:>>*very* large fields.  You could (and actually still can, on MCP systems) put
:>>alphanumeric data into numeric fields and treat it numerically with nary a
:>>whimper from the hardware or software (such a situation should not pass an
:>>IF NUMERIC test, then or now).  These extensions and these characteristics
:>>have implications that suggest their use might not be portable.

Their use would be stupidity.

As a side point, the IBM mainframe will allow an alphabetic to be used as a
numeric, i.e., is a PIC 9(3) field has 'AAA', it would be treated as 111 in a
numeric operation. If the value is 'AA ', is will cause an exception.

:>I said in another thread that letting a called program do a STOP RUN was like
:>empowering a file clerk to declare the company bankrupt because the handle on
:>the file cabinet was jammed. 

I agree that Stop Run is bad.

An abend/exception, on the other hand, should be allowed.

For example, what should a called program do if the parameter list is bad? 

How can in intelligently notify the caller as the caller is not using the
correct protocol?

:>                              Allowing the hardware to abort a job because of
:>non-numeric data takes the simile a step lower. It's like empowering a
:>janitorial contractor to declare the company bankrupt because it was unhappy
:>with something found in a trash can. 

Using invalid data to make business decisions may cause a company to become
bankrupt.

You case is more like the janitor seeing a pile of money in the garbage and
questioning whether it should be disposed of.

:>Unix systems and PCs don't have this feature. They raise an exception or treat
:>the field as zero. Only mainframes permit low-level hardware to make high-level
:>decisions. It is poor design. 

The hardware IS raising an exception. 

The application program can choose, stupidly of course, to treat it as zero.

Or it can treat it as invalid data.

Best of all, of course, is for the program to check the data to make sure it
is valid and not rely on the hardware to treat it as zero or raise an
exception.

   [ snipped ]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting change in "bounds checking"

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T09:40:09-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b4815p$17hk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3jb1e$2g8k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3jp3j$9fq$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e5ecf44.105751068@news.optonline.net> <b3ooe1$3c4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e60f6fb.247010783@news.optonline.net> <b40j0a$283e$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e647b03.125676020@news.optonline.net> <b42pqq$mjv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e669641.44288594@news.optonline.net> <k96e6vcbfgao0euibbfo0pfr1cu3stac2d@4ax.com>`

```

"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message
news:k96e6vcbfgao0euibbfo0pfr1cu3stac2d@4ax.com...

> How does one store infinity in a PIC S9(5) field?

Dunno.  Wasn't there.  Was told that division by zero didn't necessarily
cause a fault by someone who was there.

> Why is it good for a divide by zero to not raise an exception?

Doesn't matter if the program makes sure it doesn't happen first.




>> You could, as I recall, do decimal arithmetic on some
>>*very* large fields.  You could (and actually still can, on MCP systems)
put
>>alphanumeric data into numeric fields and treat it numerically with nary a
>>whimper from the hardware or software (such a situation should not pass an
>>IF NUMERIC test, then or now).  These extensions and these characteristics
>>have implications that suggest their use might not be portable.

> Their use would be stupidity.

Well, in the general case I might even agree with you; that's why I strongly
suggest that people edit their data.  As far as large numeric fields goes, I
have run into circumstances in which neither the 18 digits provided for by
ANSI X3.23-1974 and -1985, nor the 23 digits allowed by Unisys MCP/AS
hardware, were enough for accurate numeric representation.  That's one
reason, for example, why ISO/IEC 1989:2002 provides for 31 digits in
standard arithmetic.  Banks quite often found a 98-digit numeric limit
useful on the V Series, though I quite agree injudicious use of that
particular extension was a definite barrier to portability.

> As a side point, the IBM mainframe will allow an alphabetic to be used as
a
> numeric, i.e., is a PIC 9(3) field has 'AAA', it would be treated as 111
in a
> numeric operation. If the value is 'AA ', is will cause an exception.

Which is why my recommended best practice is to do an IF NUMERIC test first.
I don't think "AAA" or "AA " should be considered valid NUMERIC data.  Its
presence in a numeric field should be considered indicative of a problem,
and the programmer's failure to detect it via an IF NUMERIC test (or
equivalent) a violation of "best practices" and an error on the part of the
programmer.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting change in "bounds checking"

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T17:55:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e678abd.106886026@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3jb1e$2g8k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3jp3j$9fq$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e5ecf44.105751068@news.optonline.net> <b3ooe1$3c4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e60f6fb.247010783@news.optonline.net> <b40j0a$283e$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e647b03.125676020@news.optonline.net> <b42pqq$mjv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e669641.44288594@news.optonline.net> <k96e6vcbfgao0euibbfo0pfr1cu3stac2d@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote:

>:>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message

>:>                              Allowing the hardware to abort a job because of
>:>non-numeric data takes the simile a step lower. It's like empowering a
>:>janitorial contractor to declare the company bankrupt because it was unhappy
>:>with something found in a trash can. 

>:>Unix systems and PCs don't have this feature. They raise an exception or
treat
>:>the field as zero. Only mainframes permit low-level hardware to make
high-level
>:>decisions. It is poor design. 
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Or it can treat it as invalid data.

In practice:
..Batch payroll job crashes at 3am
..Production support is called
..Worker finds the offending transaction and deletes it. Looks at the next few
to insure they do not have the same error. Doesn't bother to write down the
employee id. 
..Files are rolled back. These procedures are well rehearsed because crashes are
frequent. 
..Job is rerun. Worker prays there isn't another bad transaction.
..One paycheck out of 400K will probably be wrong. 

That could have been accomplished much more conveniently by treating the
non-numeric field as zero. 

Suppose your automobile's computer ran like a mainframe. Upon receiving bad data
from a sensor, it would report the error and shut down the entire vehicle until
a technician arrived. 

Space Shuttle to Mission Control during re-entry: "Say, what does ASRA mean?"

>Best of all, of course, is for the program to check the data to make sure it
>is valid and not rely on the hardware to treat it as zero or raise an
>exception.

It is considered a waste of time for every program to revalidate the same data.
They check it when it enters the system. Bad data usually slips in through a
side entrance, such as a 'feed' from another computer system. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting change in "bounds checking"

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T20:06:44+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<l83f6vge482q6eskbm9mc6sjqhtu8asont@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3jb1e$2g8k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3jp3j$9fq$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e5ecf44.105751068@news.optonline.net> <b3ooe1$3c4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e60f6fb.247010783@news.optonline.net> <b40j0a$283e$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e647b03.125676020@news.optonline.net> <b42pqq$mjv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e669641.44288594@news.optonline.net> <k96e6vcbfgao0euibbfo0pfr1cu3stac2d@4ax.com> <3e678abd.106886026@news.optonline.net>`

```
:>>The hardware IS raising an exception. 

:>>The application program can choose, stupidly of course, to treat it as zero.

:>>Or it can treat it as invalid data.

:>In practice:
:>..Batch payroll job crashes at 3am
:>..Production support is called
:>..Worker finds the offending transaction and deletes it. Looks at the next few
:>to insure they do not have the same error. Doesn't bother to write down the
:>employee id. 
:>..Files are rolled back. These procedures are well rehearsed because crashes are
:>frequent. 
:>..Job is rerun. Worker prays there isn't another bad transaction.
:>..One paycheck out of 400K will probably be wrong. 

:>That could have been accomplished much more conveniently by treating the
:>non-numeric field as zero. 

How would anyone know the error? Silence is very bad in this context.

Your procedures are missing a very important step - open a problem ticket so
that the next time this occurs an exception report is generated rather than a
crash.

Of course if your management was that bad, perhaps incorrect checks were
accepted as the cost of doing business - until the business went bankrupt, of
course.

:>Suppose your automobile's computer ran like a mainframe. Upon receiving bad data
:>from a sensor, it would report the error and shut down the entire vehicle until
:>a technician arrived. 

For certain errors? Sure.

For others? Not.

:>Space Shuttle to Mission Control during re-entry: "Say, what does ASRA mean?"

That is why the code is debugged rather than accepting invalid data as zero.

:>>Best of all, of course, is for the program to check the data to make sure it
:>>is valid and not rely on the hardware to treat it as zero or raise an
:>>exception.

:>It is considered a waste of time for every program to revalidate the same data.
:>They check it when it enters the system. Bad data usually slips in through a
:>side entrance, such as a 'feed' from another computer system. 

Which should be validated.

If bad data comes from an edited source a crash is a very good idea as there
is earlier corruption - which may have been fed to other processes as well.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting change in "bounds checking"

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T09:28:11-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b480fb$16pt$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3jb1e$2g8k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3jp3j$9fq$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e5ecf44.105751068@news.optonline.net> <b3ooe1$3c4$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e60f6fb.247010783@news.optonline.net> <b40j0a$283e$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e647b03.125676020@news.optonline.net> <b42pqq$mjv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e669641.44288594@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e669641.44288594@news.optonline.net...

> I said in another thread that letting a called program do a STOP RUN was
like
> empowering a file clerk to declare the company bankrupt because the handle
on
> the file cabinet was jammed.  Allowing the hardware to abort a job because
of
> non-numeric data takes the simile a step lower. It's like empowering a
> janitorial contractor to declare the company bankrupt because it was
unhappy
> with something found in a trash can.

Based on my experience, some programmers, shops, customers and
implementations like the equivalent of SOC7, others don't.  Is 135792 a
univerally-accepted representation of a PIC 9(6) DISPLAY field that actually
contains 4"C1C3C5C7C9D2" ( = "ACEGIK" in an EBCDIC environment)?  What's the
numeric value of  4"5A5B7B7C5D"
( =  4"]$#@)%" )? Should it be 999999?  011232?  What?  And why?

That's why IF ... NUMERIC tests are important.

> Unix systems and PCs don't have this feature. They raise an exception or
treat
> the field as zero. Only mainframes permit low-level hardware to make
high-level
> decisions. It is poor design.

Neither do *some* mainframes.  And my personal preference is for the
programmer to be provided the data-verification tools (e.g. IF NUMERIC) and
to be expected to use them.  If garbage gets past the edit, the programmer
is responsible for whatever the consequences are.  My personal opinion is
that whether a nonnumeric value is silently included in a program's
calculation or the cause of a crash-and-burn should actually be the choice
of the management of the company, not the programmer, and that the inclusion
of class tests is one good way to make the programs more portable
regardless.

> That's why I don't claim to know anything about Unisys COBOL, even though
I had
> fifteen years' experience with obsolete versions.

I refer you back to the "mainframes" and "all platforms" discussion.

> I refuse to limit Best Practices to the Lowest Common Demominator. Given
that
> pointers are available to >95% of practitioners, they have a place in the
> tutorial.

Funny.  The implementations you seem to view as "mainstream" all point to
these very same Best Practices and say, in one form or another, "Hey, folks.
This isn't portable."  and tell you how to write portable code.

And, just out of curiosity, where did you get your >95% figure?

 The _only_ non-standard practice I advocated was pointers, which will be
> available 'tomorrow' on 2002-conforming compilers and are available today
on the
> most popular. Omitting reference to pointers would be a disservice to
today's
> practitioners.

What about COMP-3?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-02T09:34:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hek8a.77272$zF6.5509479@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3jb1e$2g8k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3jp3j$9fq$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e5ecf44.105751068@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302281854.19994b0a@posting.google.com> <3e6098b1.222869392@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303011239.3283841a@posting.google.com> <01c2e06a$c1ceb5a0$92ccf943@chottel> <3E61A560.80D5BA30@worldnet.att.net>`

```

Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message news:3E61A560.80D5BA30@worldnet.att.net...
> 
> I've never had occasion to define a variable as USAGE INDEX, 

It is primarily used, in my experience, for capturing intermediate Index Name values
for later use, either to reset an Index Name value to a previous setting,
or perhaps to be passed to another module as part of a file record,
or to assist with program debugging using the DISPLAY statement
(you cannot access index items as normal data items.)

The important thing to bear in mind is that, where a subscript value 
would be incremented by 1 each time to process a table entry-by-entry, 
an Index Name value would be incremented by the size of one table entry.
In other words, while a subscript's value reflects the table entry's occurrence
(5 represents the 5th occurrence, etc), an Index Name contains an offset value,
reflecting the sum displacement of the target table entry from the start of the table.       

> even though COBOL requires me to use indexes for SEARCH and SEARCH ALL.

Not the COBOL I use.  The identifier varied can be a Subscript or an Index Name.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting change in "bounds checking"

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-03T08:19:23-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3vvac$1qa8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3jb1e$2g8k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3jp3j$9fq$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e5ecf44.105751068@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302281854.19994b0a@posting.google.com> <3e6098b1.222869392@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303011239.3283841a@posting.google.com> <01c2e06a$c1ceb5a0$92ccf943@chottel> <3E61A560.80D5BA30@worldnet.att.net> <hek8a.77272$zF6.5509479@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

"Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote in message
news:hek8a.77272$zF6.5509479@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...

> > even though COBOL requires me to use indexes for SEARCH and SEARCH ALL.
>
> Not the COBOL I use.  The identifier varied can be a Subscript or an Index
Name.

Ummm... I think that's a bit of an oversimplification.

There's no identifier varied in SEARCH ALL because there's no VARYING clause
in that format; the varying is done "behind the user's back" in the INDEXED
BY item.

For SEARCH, if you have a VARYING clause, it can reference an index data
item or a numeric integer (but not an index-name).  Use of the index-name
associated with the INDEXED BY clause in the table being SEARCHed is
accomplished (arguably most efficiently) by simply dropping the VARYING
clause.

Now, your compiler *might* allow a SEARCH on a table that doesn't have an
INDEXED BY clause at all, and that most definitely would be an extension to
standard COBOL.

For SEARCH ALL, though, I don't see offhand where the compiler would be
expected to indicate where the "match" occurred because there's no VARYING
clause to begin with.  There'd have to be some "extra syntax" over what the
standards specify in the Format 2 SEARCH to indicate where to put that
location to accomplish that.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting change in "bounds checking"

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-03T17:52:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gDM8a.3615$3g.530984@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3jb1e$2g8k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3jp3j$9fq$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e5ecf44.105751068@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302281854.19994b0a@posting.google.com> <3e6098b1.222869392@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303011239.3283841a@posting.google.com> <01c2e06a$c1ceb5a0$92ccf943@chottel> <3E61A560.80D5BA30@worldnet.att.net> <hek8a.77272$zF6.5509479@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <b3vvac$1qa8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
news:b3vvac$1qa8$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
> There's no identifier varied in SEARCH ALL because there's no VARYING
clause
> in that format; the varying is done "behind the user's back" in the
INDEXED
> BY item.

SEARCH VARYING only makes sense in the context of a multi-dimensioned table;
in which case SEARCH ALL cannot make sense, because the table cannot be
sorted simultaneously by more one key field.

(Oh, well, I "suppose" you could re-sort the table prior to each search and
allow a SEARCH ALL, but now you're back to a linear search - search with a
small s, not the COBOL verb - anyway just to sort the table.)

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting change in "bounds checking"

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-03T07:16:50+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3thu9$bmf$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3e6098b1.222869392@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303011239.3283841a@posting.google.com> <3e61dce1.45076655@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> "... are you sure it's COBOL?"

So, if I was to say that your car didn't have as many dials on the dash as 
mine then you would take that to be a personal insult of you?
 
> If EXIT PROGRAM had worked right in the first place,
> there would have been no need to create GOBACK.

EXIT PROGRAM did not _exist_ when IBM created GOBACK.

EXIT PROGRAM was introduced in ANSI'74, prior to that each vendor had their 
own mechanism to return from a CALLed program.  Some had to do a 'CALL 
RETURN', others invented EXIT PROGRAM, IBM created GOBACK.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting change in "bounds checking"

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-03T08:26:07-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3vvn0$1qfb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3jb1e$2g8k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3jp3j$9fq$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e5ecf44.105751068@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302281854.19994b0a@posting.google.com> <3e6098b1.222869392@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303011239.3283841a@posting.google.com> <3e61dce1.45076655@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e61dce1.45076655@news.optonline.net...
> riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> If EXIT PROGRAM had worked right in the first place, there would
> have been no need to create GOBACK.

EXIT PROGRAM works just fine. What leads you to believe it behaves any
differently from the way it is described as behaving?   None of the MCP/AS
COBOL compilers have EVER had GOBACK.  None of our customers have had a
problem distinguishing between EXIT PROGRAM and STOP RUN; they are both
clearly documented.   We have had no indication of any user desire whatever
that we provide GOBACK in our '74- or '85-compliant compilers.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting change in "bounds checking"

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-03T17:34:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e639249.66088941@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3jb1e$2g8k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3jp3j$9fq$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e5ecf44.105751068@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302281854.19994b0a@posting.google.com> <3e6098b1.222869392@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303011239.3283841a@posting.google.com> <3e61dce1.45076655@news.optonline.net> <b3vvn0$1qfb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3e61dce1.45076655@news.optonline.net...
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>differently from the way it is described as behaving?   

Forgive the sloppy phrasing. I should have said 'had been designed right.' The
'74 standard required EXIT PROGRAM to be in a paragraph by itself. I found this
so irritating, I stopped using it in the late '70s. Because I wasn't using it, I
didn't notice the '85 standard removed the requirement for a paragraph name. 

>None of the MCP/AS
>COBOL compilers have EVER had GOBACK.  None of our customers have had a
>problem distinguishing between EXIT PROGRAM and STOP RUN; they are both
>clearly documented.   We have had no indication of any user desire whatever
>that we provide GOBACK in our '74- or '85-compliant compilers.

I like the symmetry of a single exit mechanism. Avoiding STOP RUN prevents a
called program from terminating the run unit, which is like a file clerk
declaring the company bankrupt. Also, it reinforces the idea of programs being
'called' by an OS or shell. On many platforms, the command line is passed to the
main program as a parameter, so it seems appropriate to have main exit like a
called program. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting change in "bounds checking"

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-03T12:36:51-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b40ed3$24p6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<b3ja2j$7rr$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b3jb1e$2g8k$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <b3jp3j$9fq$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e5ecf44.105751068@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0302281854.19994b0a@posting.google.com> <3e6098b1.222869392@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303011239.3283841a@posting.google.com> <3e61dce1.45076655@news.optonline.net> <b3vvn0$1qfb$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3e639249.66088941@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e639249.66088941@news.optonline.net...

> >None of the MCP/AS
> >COBOL compilers have EVER had GOBACK.  None of our customers have had a
> >problem distinguishing between EXIT PROGRAM and STOP RUN; they are both
> >clearly documented.   We have had no indication of any user desire
whatever
> >that we provide GOBACK in our '74- or '85-compliant compilers.
>
> I like the symmetry of a single exit mechanism.

I understand that is one of the benefits of the verb.   But none of our many
users have felt strongly enough about this even to suggest such an addition
to our compilers.  Moreover, many of our users are sensitive to
execution-time performance considerations, and I have some trouble viewing
determination at execution time as to whether to execute the equivalent of a
STOP RUN or an EXIT PROGRAM as optimal in terms of performance, which is why
I can see that others might choose to use the "right verb" in the first
place.

> Avoiding STOP RUN prevents a
> called program from terminating the run unit, which is like a file clerk
> declaring the company bankrupt.

Well, since we're into similes, a file clerk discovering that the company's
General Ledger is on fire might reasonably need to be able to take some
action to put out the fire before it spreads rather than wait for the
message to be propagated up the line of command via inter-office memos to
the Chief Safety Officer who was empowered to issue the appropriate memos to
order someone on the Security Staff to make his way down to the file room
and take appropriate action!

I can see a called program reasonably doing a STOP RUN on a serious database
error, for example.  There may indeed be other ways to do it, but this is
indeed one way, and it is a reasonable and valid way in certain contexts.
GOBACK has its uses, but it is no substitute for STOP RUN in the contexts in
which STOP RUN is appropriate and produces useful results, and that set of
contexts is not a null set.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

<a id="t-375cdb3d50"></a>
## Thread: External Table Addressability using Procedure-Pointer — 3 msgs

### External Table Addressability using Procedure-Pointer

- **From:** wobconsult@sprynet.com (WOB Consulting)
- **Date:** 2003-03-01T05:00:55-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<173f80ae.0303010500.288929f8@posting.google.com>`

```
In a COBOL/BATCH program (Compiled using the DYNAM option), we want to
establish addressability to a Static/External ASSEMBLER Table (CSECT),
using a PROCEDURE-POINTER, which was Assembled as AMODE(31)
RMODE(ANY).

Our question is whether the RETURN-CODE Special-Register is set to a
non-zero value AND/OR the PROCEDURE-POINTER itself is set to NULL if
the External Table address could not be resolved?

In reading about PROCEDURE-POINTER, its usage that we are attempting
resembles that of an MVS "LOAD" Macro, although we're unaware whether
there an underlying differences.

Example code -

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
03 WS-PP PROCEDURE-POINTER.
03 WS-PP-X REDEFINES WS-PP PIC X(08).
03 WS-TABLE PIC X(08) VALUE 'EXTERNAL'.
03 WS-PTR POINTER.
03 WS-PTR-X REDEFINES WS-PTR PIC X(04).
LINKAGE SECTION.
01 LS-TABLE-REC.
   03  LS-TABLE-NBR-ENTRIES PIC 9(09) BINARY.
   03  LS-TABLE-TBL OCCURS 1 TO 1024 TIMES
                    DEPENDING ON LS-TABLE-NBR-ENTRIES
                    INDEXED BY LS-TABLE-IDX,
                               LS-TABLE-IDX-MAX.
       05  LS-TABLE-ENTRY PIC X(12).
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
SET WS-PP TO NULL.
MOVE ZERO TO RETURN-CODE.
SET WS-PP TO ENTRY WS-TABLE.
MOVE WS-PP-X TO WS-PTR-X.
SET ADDRESS OF LS-TABLE-REC TO WS-PTR.
SET LS-TABLE-IDX-MAX TO LS-TABLE-NBR-ENTRIES.

Thanks in Advance....

Bill
```

#### ↳ Re: External Table Addressability using Procedure-Pointer

- **From:** psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-03-01T15:50:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<16e2f010.0303011550.3e35e99@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<173f80ae.0303010500.288929f8@posting.google.com>`

```
wobconsult@sprynet.com (WOB Consulting) wrote in message news:<173f80ae.0303010500.288929f8@posting.google.com>...
> In a COBOL/BATCH program (Compiled using the DYNAM option), we want to
> establish addressability to a Static/External ASSEMBLER Table (CSECT),
…[37 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill

Cool!  I think you may have actually found a use for IBM's
implementation of procedure pointer.

I cannot say for certain if this is proper and documented behavior,
but I know on the 2.1 and 2.2 versions of Cobol for OS/390 this will
work.  The procedure pointer is the address of the entry point
followed by 4 bytes of nulls (or is it 4 bytes of nulls followed by
the address).

Thanks for the great idea, I can now throw away my LOAD macro
assembler module.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: External Table Addressability using Procedure-Pointer

- **From:** wobconsult@sprynet.com (WOB Consulting)
- **Date:** 2003-03-02T21:27:11-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<173f80ae.0303022127.5325d0c4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<173f80ae.0303010500.288929f8@posting.google.com> <16e2f010.0303011550.3e35e99@posting.google.com>`

```
psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote in message news:<16e2f010.0303011550.3e35e99@posting.google.com>..
> Cool!  I think you may have actually found a use for IBM's
> implementation of procedure pointer.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> assembler module.

Joe,

I searched Google before posting and found several previous postings,
indicating that the PROCEDURE-POINTER, when resolved, is
left-justified with low-order X'00's.

We're going to try this ourselves, making sure that the external-table
is not in the Batch Loadlib and see what we get.

Yeah, the idea was to also get rid of our generic LOAD Macro
sub-program, so this would be a big help.

We intend on addressing these tables (as we do currently) in CICS,
using the "LOAD PROGRAM" command, so that will remain.

I'll keep you posted if something turns up with a non-zero
RETURN-CODE, a NULL value in PROCEDURE-POINTER or both. I'm also going
to check for the ever popular IBM invalid address-value of X'FF000000'
in the first 4-bytes of the PROCEDURE-POINTER. Hopefully, the
RETURN-CODE will be the way to go.

Bill
```

<a id="t-36b31bf6c7"></a>
## Thread: EXIT PROGRAM ('74 vs '85 Standard) — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### EXIT PROGRAM ('74 vs '85 Standard)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-01T15:08:46-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3r7h6$u5$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
For those interessted, the following "substantive change" item is listed in
the '85 Standard on page XVII-46

"(70) EXIT PROGRAM statement (1 IPC). The EXIT PROGRAM statement need not be
the only statement in a paragraph."

Therefore, anyone who hasn't changed compilers or programming techniques
MIGHT still have a restriction similar to this.  Of course, even their '74
Standard compiler *might* have allowed this restriction to be ignored - as a
perfectly valid extension.  Certainly, I know of no currently supported
(when run in '85 Standard mode) compiler that doesn't follow this rule.
There are, however, some '74 Standard compilers still being supported (or
'85 Standard compilers that have a '74 Standard option) such compilers
*might* still have this restriction - but again, I don't personally know of
any.

FYI - this was the "argument" against adding the GOBACK statement to the
Standard. In an '85 Standard conforming program, it was legal to code a
paragraphs such as the following:

  Leave-Program-Para.
       Display "about to this program and/or stop the run-unit"
       Exit Program
          .
       Display "Leaving the run-units main program"
       Stop Run
         .

Clearly (ignoring lower-case) this would NOT have been conforming to the '74
Standard - but was in the '85 Standard.
```

```
opposite view below

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:b3g0dg$a5k$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
> Speaking of making things easy to edit (removing periods to aid in cutting
and
> pasting)...
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> It makes it easy to add or comment out conditions as each condition is on
the
> same line as it's OR or AND.

And just to have one more thing noone can agree on, I prefer:
IF    VAR1 = VAR2    OR
        VAR3 = VAR4    OR
        E = F

My rationale: It is easier to read with the conditions lined up under each
other. (Note: I used tabs in the note, but would use spaces in real code.)
I considered your way, for your reason, but decided ease of reading was more
important (to me) than ease of debugging. Also, for mixed conditions, I
often code as follows, beacuse I believe it makes the meaning clearer:
IF    ERROR-FLAG NOT = 'Y'
        AND
        (VAR1 = VAR2   OR
        VAR3 = VAR4   OR
        E = F)
```

```
SkippyPB wrote:
> On Thu, 27 Feb 2003 18:56:40 -0600, "DBuck" <dbuck@prairieinet.net>
> enlightened us:
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> binary as 1111 0001.

Only if you're working with Display fields:

binary	= x'00000001'
packed	= x'1c'

The x'F1' is a COBOLism applied after calculations, there are no 
instructions to perform calculations on external decimal, i.e., Display, 
fields.
```

- **References:** `<egM7a.7762$kf7.1072047@news20.bellglobal.com> <b3o4d4$bg$1@panix1.panix.com> <TEM7a.7769$kf7.1078145@news20.bellglobal.com> <8NN7a.13935$os6.1058798@news20.bellglobal.com> <A4O7a.7952$kf7.1094850@news20.bellglobal.com> <217e491a.0302281435.283988c5@posting.google.com>`

```

On 28-Feb-2003, riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

> I suggest that you remove all the line number in columns 1-6 and leave
> these blank (they don't do anything useful) and ensure that you don't
> have spurious characters in cols 7 and 8 (which seems to be the case).

Line numbers can be useful in environments which use lots of copy members.  
That way we can cross reference a line in the compiler listing with a source
code line.


  002002         050800     IF CC-UPDATE-SW = 'Y'
PP 5648-A25 IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM  2.2.1                   SIPR486   Date 02
  LineID  PL SL  ----+-*A-1-B--+----2----+----3----+----4----+----5----+----6---
  002003      1  050900         DISPLAY 'READYING AREAS IN UPDATE MODE'
  002004         051000*        READY IASAR102-REGION USAGE-MODE UPDATE
  002005      1                   MOVE 2 TO DML-SEQUENCE
  002006      1                   CALL 'IDMS' USING SUBSCHEMA-CTRL
  002007      1                           IDBMSCOM (36)
  002008      1                           IASAR102-REGION
  002009      1                     PERFORM IDMS-STATUS;
  002010         051100*        READY IASAR108-REGION USAGE-MODE UPDATE
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: what is the best way to debug a cobol program

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-03-03T15:08:57-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0303031508.676783cd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<egM7a.7762$kf7.1072047@news20.bellglobal.com> <b3o4d4$bg$1@panix1.panix.com> <TEM7a.7769$kf7.1078145@news20.bellglobal.com> <8NN7a.13935$os6.1058798@news20.bellglobal.com> <A4O7a.7952$kf7.1094850@news20.bellglobal.com> <217e491a.0302281435.283988c5@posting.google.com> <b3vuin$2qb$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote 

> > I suggest that you remove all the line number in columns 1-6 and leave
> > these blank (they don't do anything useful) and ensure that you don't
> > have spurious characters in cols 7 and 8 (which seems to be the case).

I later realised that the lines had been wrapped and the cols 73-80
were appearing on the next line.
 
> Line numbers can be useful in environments which use lots of copy members.  
> That way we can cross reference a line in the compiler listing with a source
> code line.

Maybe.  But you are using it on Linux which has different tools. In
fact the line numbers, cols 73-80 and the comments are in fact the
problem.  The Open Cobol documentation has:

""" -----------------------------------------------------------
Source Format

OpenCOBOL supports both fixed and free source format.

The format can be explicitly specified by giving one of the following
options to cobc:

-free
    Free format.
-fixed
Fixed format.

If non of these options is given, the format is automatically
determined by the following rule.

The compiler inspects the first six characters of the first line in
the source file. If the characters are all digits or spaces, the
format is considered to be fixed format. Otherwise, it is free format.
----------------------------------------------- """

Compile with -fixed option, or put '000000' in cols 1-6 of the first
line of the program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: what is the best way to debug a cobol program

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-02T22:44:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e62895e.89240127@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<egM7a.7762$kf7.1072047@news20.bellglobal.com> <b3o4d4$bg$1@panix1.panix.com> <TEM7a.7769$kf7.1078145@news20.bellglobal.com> <8NN7a.13935$os6.1058798@news20.bellglobal.com> <A4O7a.7952$kf7.1094850@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
"Paul" <paulhbliu@hotmail.com> wrote:

>Hi Don
>
…[57 more quoted lines elided]…
>what's the error message tell me ?

It tells you the compiler is not recognizing comment lines. It should not be
parsing comment lines. The old, fixed-form, notation for comments was * in
column 7; the new, free-form, is *>

Because you are switching from free-form, in the main program, to fixed-form, in
the copybook, you need a compiler directive before and after the copybook. In
the compiler's manual, look for options named something like FIXED and FREE,
then figure out how to imbed them in your program. Every compiler does it
differently; most use a character such as $ or @, often followed by as word such
as SET or OPTIONS. 

Alternatively, change the copybook to free-form by removing line numbers and
numbers at the ends of lines. While you are at it, delete 'eyecatch'. It is an
obsolete holdover from the Good Old Days, when COBOL programmers debugged by
reading memory dumps. 

Robert
```
