<a id="t-c407b510e9"></a>
## Thread: A Matter of Style — 84 msgs

### A Matter of Style

- **From:** Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@Sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-02T21:29:32-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca>`

```
I want to start a new thread here, partially in response to some of the 
bad feeling that has been evolving, and partially in response to the 
many interesting issues that the thread has raised.  I think that every 
veteran member of this newsgroup will agree that styles of cobol have 
evolved, and that this is a good thing. Being concious of style, and 
willing to spend time learning to code with style is the mark of a good 
programmer.

On the other hand, it has spawned many a bitter battle, and the 
definition of "good style", is perhaps a problem that Grace herself 
would have delegated to a committe dooomed to eternal bickering.

I would propose that style is not a matter of following rules, and that 
if reaches that point, the code is probably doomed to perdition anyway. 
I think many different styles serve the purpose of making code readable 
and maintainable, the most important aspects of source code.

Having said all that, I think that we need to re-look at style 
frequently.  With the addition of OOP, stylistict methods of the past 
may nor be as effective, and new methods may have to evolve.

I am going to post some code ... after some pride induced reflection, 
unretouched, as I put in in my library a week ago.  It is a version 1.00 
of a new system, and is a method within a ledger object.  It does a 
search of ledger items within a ledger object, based on date, account, 
reference number, etc.  It starts off by creating a screen object, then
well ... I'll comment in the code.

Donald
```

#### ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

- **From:** Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@Sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-02T21:51:15-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca>`

```
the code:

For those not use to OOP, the following exists within an object, with 
it's own working storage section.  This working storage starts fresh 
each time the method is invoked, however the data in the other portion 
remains in effect from the creation of the object to the completion.

This is invoked when the user presses a "search" menu item or button. At 
thet time, it inherits the object it is in, which is a ledger.  The 
ledger exists as an array at the next level up.  That array is what is 
actually being searched.

        METHOD-ID. DO-SEARCH.
        DATA DIVISION.
        WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
        77 END-FLAG                 PICTURE X.
        77 SCREEN-OBJECT            USAGE OBJECT REFERENCE LEDDLL1.
        77 ERROR-OBJECT             USAGE OBJECT REFERENCE LEDDLL1.
        77 LOCAL-KEYSTROKE          PICTURE S999.
        77 REFERENCE-TYPE           PICTURE X.
        77 SEARCH-START             PICTURE 9999.
        77 SEARCH-INCREMENT         PICTURE S99.
        77 SEARCH-END-FLAG          PICTURE X.
        77 SUB1                     PICTURE 9999.
        77 SUB2                     PICTURE 9999.
        LINKAGE SECTION.
        PROCEDURE DIVISION.
        MAIN-LINE.
            PERFORM STARTUP.
            PERFORM MAINLOOP
                UNTIL END-FLAG IS EQUAL TO "Y".
            PERFORM CLOSEOUT.
            EXIT METHOD.

This routine creates a screen, and sets the starting search criteria. 
They are set from the main object ... that is, they retain their value 
while th current ledger is open.  That allows one search to start where 
the last one left off.  You'll notice that I use periods, but also use 
indentation to draw the eye to lines with abnormal termination. I also 
use all uppercase .. a matter of personal choice.  My editor 
colour-codes all that stuff, BTW.

        STARTUP.
            MOVE "N" TO END-FLAG.
            INVOKE LEDDLL1 "NEW" RETURNING SCREEN-OBJECT.
            INVOKE SCREEN-OBJECT "SEARCH-OPEN-THE-SCREEN".
            MOVE LEDGER-TITLE-TWO TO LOCAL-TITLE OF SCREEN-OBJECT.
            INVOKE SCREEN-OBJECT "SEARCH-SET-THE-TITLE".
            MOVE "Y" TO SEARCH-SEARCHA OF SCREEN-OBJECT.
            MOVE START-SEARCH-DATE TO SEARCH-DATE OF SCREEN-OBJECT.
            MOVE START-SEARCH-ACCOUNT TO SEARCH-SACCOUNT OF 				 
SCREEN-OBJECT.
            MOVE START-SEARCH-R-TYPE TO REFERENCE-TYPE.
            MOVE START-SEARCH-R-NUMB TO SEARCH-RNUMB OF SCREEN-OBJECT.
            MOVE START-BY-DATE TO SEARCH-SDATE OF SCREEN-OBJECT.
            MOVE START-BY-ACCOUNT TO SEARCH-SEARCHA OF SCREEN-OBJECT.
            MOVE START-BY-REF TO SEARCH-SREF OF SCREEN-OBJECT.
            MOVE START-BY-BAD TO SEARCH-SBAD OF SCREEN-OBJECT.
            PERFORM SEARCH-ACCOUNT.

The main loop runs a screen.  It contains four radio buttons, which 
determine the search type ... by account, by date, by reference, and by 
account number that is not in the chart of accounts.

As each radio button is pressed, it greys/enables the fields associated 
with the button.  For example, if the search is by date, the from date 
and the two date are enabled, and the from/to account are greyed out.

Editing and auditing of the fields is done in generated code (Generated 
by the SP2 screen system, in Cobol)

The buttons/menu items on the screen are search forward, backward, from 
current, from the start, home, jump to end etc.

        MAINLOOP.
            IF LAST-SEARCH-RECORD IS EQUAL TO 1
                MOVE LOW-VALUES TO SEARCH-NEXT-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE "g" TO SEARCH-PREVIOUS-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
            ELSE
            IF LAST-SEARCH-RECORD IS EQUAL TO DEBIT-CREDIT-COUNT
                MOVE "g" TO SEARCH-NEXT-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE LOW-VALUES TO SEARCH-PREVIOUS-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
            ELSE
            IF LAST-SEARCH-RECORD IS EQUAL TO ZERO
                MOVE "g" TO SEARCH-NEXT-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE "g" TO SEARCH-PREVIOUS-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
            ELSE
                MOVE LOW-VALUES TO SEARCH-NEXT-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE LOW-VALUES TO SEARCH-PREVIOUS-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT.
            MOVE REFERENCE-TYPE TO SEARCH-RTYPE OF SCREEN-OBJECT.
            INVOKE SCREEN-OBJECT "SEARCH-SHOW-THE-DATA".
            INVOKE SCREEN-OBJECT "SEARCH-GET-THE-DATA".
            MOVE SCREEN-KEYSTROKE OF SCREEN-OBJECT TO LOCAL-KEYSTROKE.
            IF LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO ZERO
                CONTINUE
            ELSE
            IF LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO 13
                CONTINUE
            ELSE
            IF LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO 500
                MOVE 1 TO SEARCH-START
                MOVE 1 TO SEARCH-INCREMENT
                MOVE "Y" TO END-FLAG
                PERFORM DO-THE-SEARCH
            ELSE
            IF LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO 501
                MOVE "Y" TO END-FLAG
                MOVE 1 TO SEARCH-RECORD
            ELSE
            IF LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO 502
                MOVE "Y" TO END-FLAG
                MOVE DEBIT-CREDIT-COUNT TO SEARCH-RECORD
            ELSE
            IF LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO 503
                ADD 1 TO LAST-SEARCH-RECORD GIVING SEARCH-START
                MOVE 1 TO SEARCH-INCREMENT
                MOVE "Y" TO END-FLAG
                PERFORM DO-THE-SEARCH
            ELSE
            IF LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO 504
                SUBTRACT 1 FROM LAST-SEARCH-RECORD GIVING SEARCH-START
                MOVE -1 TO SEARCH-INCREMENT
                MOVE "Y" TO END-FLAG
                PERFORM DO-THE-SEARCH
            ELSE
            IF LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO 27
                MOVE "Y" TO END-FLAG
            ELSE
            IF (LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO -4
                OR
               LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO -2)
                PERFORM CHECK-TYPE
            ELSE
                DISPLAY "KEYSTROKE -->" LOCAL-KEYSTROKE.

        CLOSEOUT.
            INVOKE SCREEN-OBJECT "SEARCH-CLOSE-THE-SCREEN".
            SET SCREEN-OBJECT TO NULL.

        CHECK-TYPE.
            IF SEARCH-LAST-FLD-ID OF SCREEN-OBJECT IS EQUAL TO
                SEARCH-SEARCHA-I OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                    PERFORM SEARCH-ACCOUNT
            ELSE
            IF SEARCH-LAST-FLD-ID OF SCREEN-OBJECT IS EQUAL TO
                SEARCH-SDATE-I OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                    PERFORM SEARCH-DATE
            ELSE
            IF SEARCH-LAST-FLD-ID OF SCREEN-OBJECT IS EQUAL TO
                SEARCH-SREF-I OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                    PERFORM SEARCH-REFERENCE
            ELSE
            IF SEARCH-LAST-FLD-ID OF SCREEN-OBJECT IS EQUAL TO
                SEARCH-RTYPE-I OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                    PERFORM RESET-ITYPE
            ELSE
            IF SEARCH-LAST-FLD-ID OF SCREEN-OBJECT IS EQUAL TO
                SEARCH-SBAD-I OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                    PERFORM SEARCH-BAD.

        SEARCH-ACCOUNT.
            IF SEARCH-SEARCHA OF SCREEN-OBJECT IS EQUAL TO "Y"
                MOVE LOW-VALUES TO SEARCH-SACCOUNT-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE "g" TO SEARCH-DATE-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE "g" TO SEARCH-RTYPE-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE "g" TO SEARCH-RNUMB-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE "N" TO SEARCH-SDATE OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE "N" TO SEARCH-SREF OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE "N" TO SEARCH-SBAD OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE SEARCH-SACCOUNT-I OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                    TO SEARCH-NEXT-FLD-ID OF SCREEN-OBJECT.

        SEARCH-DATE.
            IF SEARCH-SDATE OF SCREEN-OBJECT IS EQUAL TO "Y"
                MOVE "g" TO SEARCH-SACCOUNT-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE LOW-VALUES TO SEARCH-DATE-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE "g" TO SEARCH-RTYPE-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE "g" TO SEARCH-RNUMB-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE "N" TO SEARCH-SEARCHA OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE "N" TO SEARCH-SREF OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE "N" TO SEARCH-SBAD OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE SEARCH-DATE-I OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                    TO SEARCH-NEXT-FLD-ID OF SCREEN-OBJECT.

        SEARCH-REFERENCE.
            IF SEARCH-SREF OF SCREEN-OBJECT IS EQUAL TO "Y"
                MOVE "g" TO SEARCH-SACCOUNT-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE "g" TO SEARCH-DATE-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE LOW-VALUES TO SEARCH-RTYPE-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE LOW-VALUES TO SEARCH-RNUMB-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE "N" TO SEARCH-SEARCHA OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE "N" TO SEARCH-SDATE OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE "N" TO SEARCH-SBAD OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE SEARCH-RTYPE-I OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                    TO SEARCH-NEXT-FLD-ID OF SCREEN-OBJECT.

        SEARCH-BAD.
            IF SEARCH-SBAD OF SCREEN-OBJECT IS EQUAL TO "Y"
                MOVE "g" TO SEARCH-SACCOUNT-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE "g" TO SEARCH-DATE-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE "g" TO SEARCH-RTYPE-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE "g" TO SEARCH-RNUMB-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE "N" TO SEARCH-SEARCHA OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE "N" TO SEARCH-SDATE OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE "N" TO SEARCH-SREF OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                MOVE SEARCH-GO-I OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                    TO SEARCH-NEXT-FLD-ID OF SCREEN-OBJECT.

        RESET-ITYPE.
            IF SEARCH-RTYPE OF SCREEN-OBJECT IS EQUAL TO "*"
                MOVE "*" TO REFERENCE-TYPE
            ELSE
            IF (SEARCH-RTYPE OF  SCREEN-OBJECT IS NOT LESS THAN "A"
                AND
                SEARCH-RTYPE OF  SCREEN-OBJECT IS NOT GREATER THAN "Z")
                    MOVE SEARCH-RTYPE OF SCREEN-OBJECT TO REFERENCE-TYPE.

        DO-THE-SEARCH.
            MOVE SEARCH-DATE OF SCREEN-OBJECT TO START-SEARCH-DATE.
            MOVE SEARCH-SACCOUNT OF SCREEN-OBJECT TO 					 
START-SEARCH-ACCOUNT.
            MOVE SEARCH-RTYPE OF SCREEN-OBJECT TO START-SEARCH-R-TYPE.
            MOVE SEARCH-RNUMB OF SCREEN-OBJECT TO START-SEARCH-R-NUMB.
            MOVE SEARCH-SDATE OF SCREEN-OBJECT TO START-BY-DATE.
            MOVE SEARCH-SEARCHA OF SCREEN-OBJECT TO START-BY-ACCOUNT.
            MOVE SEARCH-SREF OF SCREEN-OBJECT TO START-BY-REF.
            MOVE SEARCH-SBAD OF SCREEN-OBJECT TO START-BY-BAD.
            MOVE "N" TO SEARCH-END-FLAG.
            PERFORM SEARCH-THE-TRANSACTIONS
                VARYING SUB1 FROM SEARCH-START BY SEARCH-INCREMENT
                    UNTIL SEARCH-END-FLAG IS EQUAL TO "Y".

        SEARCH-THE-TRANSACTIONS.
            IF (SEARCH-SEARCHA OF SCREEN-OBJECT IS EQUAL TO "Y"
                AND
                TRANS-ACCOUNT (SUB1) IS EQUAL TO 
SEARCH-SACCOUNT OF 					SCREEN-OBJECT)
                    MOVE SUB1 TO SEARCH-RECORD
                    MOVE "Y" TO SEARCH-END-FLAG
            ELSE
            IF (
                    SEARCH-SDATE OF SCREEN-OBJECT IS EQUAL TO "Y"
                    AND
                    TRANS-DATE (SUB1) IS EQUAL TO SEARCH-DATE OF 				 
SCREEN-OBJECT
               )
                        MOVE SUB1 TO SEARCH-RECORD
                        MOVE "Y" TO SEARCH-END-FLAG
            ELSE
            IF  (   (
                    SEARCH-SREF OF SCREEN-OBJECT IS EQUAL TO "Y"
                    AND
                    SEARCH-RTYPE OF SCREEN-OBJECT IS EQUAL TO "*"
                    AND
                    TRANS-REF-NUMB (SUB1) IS EQUAL TO 
SEARCH-RNUMB OF 					SCREEN-OBJECT
                    )
                OR
                    (
                    SEARCH-SREF OF SCREEN-OBJECT IS EQUAL TO "Y"
                    AND
                    TRANS-REF-TYPE (SUB1) IS EQUAL TO 
SEARCH-RTYPE OF 					SCREEN-OBJECT
                    AND
                    TRANS-REF-NUMB (SUB1) IS EQUAL TO 
SEARCH-RNUMB OF 					SCREEN-OBJECT
                    )
                )
                    MOVE SUB1 TO SEARCH-RECORD
                    MOVE "Y" TO SEARCH-END-FLAG
            ELSE
            IF SEARCH-SBAD OF SCREEN-OBJECT IS EQUAL TO "Y"
                PERFORM CHECK-BAD-ACCOUNT
                    VARYING SUB2 FROM 1 BY 1
                UNTIL
                    (
                     SUB2 IS GREATER THAN ACCOUNT-COUNT
                        OR
                     TRANS-ACCOUNT (SUB1) IS EQUAL TO 
ACCOUNT-NUMBER 					(SUB2)
                    )
                IF SUB2 IS GREATER THAN ACCOUNT-COUNT
                    MOVE SUB1 TO SEARCH-RECORD
                    MOVE "Y" TO SEARCH-END-FLAG
                    .
            IF SUB1 IS LESS THAN 1
                PERFORM BAD-SEARCH
            ELSE
            IF SUB1 IS GREATER THAN DEBIT-CREDIT-COUNT
                PERFORM BAD-SEARCH.

        CHECK-BAD-ACCOUNT.
            CONTINUE.

        BAD-SEARCH.
            INVOKE LEDDLL1 "NEW" RETURNING ERROR-OBJECT.
            INVOKE ERROR-OBJECT "YESNO-OPEN-THE-SCREEN".
            MOVE SHORT-TITLE TO LOCAL-TITLE OF ERROR-OBJECT.
            INVOKE ERROR-OBJECT "YESNO-SET-THE-TITLE".
            MOVE SPACE TO YESNO-MESSAGE-A OF ERROR-OBJECT.
            MOVE "Search Failed !" TO YESNO-MESSAGE-B OF ERROR-OBJECT.
            MOVE "Search again?" TO YESNO-MESSAGE-C OF ERROR-OBJECT.
            INVOKE ERROR-OBJECT "YESNO-SHOW-THE-DATA".
            INVOKE ERROR-OBJECT "YESNO-GET-THE-DATA".
            MOVE SCREEN-KEYSTROKE OF ERROR-OBJECT TO LOCAL-KEYSTROKE.
            IF LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO 500
                INVOKE ERROR-OBJECT "YESNO-CLOSE-THE-SCREEN"
                SET ERROR-OBJECT TO NULL
                MOVE "N" TO END-FLAG
                MOVE "Y" TO SEARCH-END-FLAG
            ELSE
            IF LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO 501
                INVOKE ERROR-OBJECT "YESNO-CLOSE-THE-SCREEN"
                SET ERROR-OBJECT TO NULL
                MOVE "Y" TO END-FLAG
                MOVE "Y" TO SEARCH-END-FLAG
            ELSE
                MOVE "N" TO END-FLAG
                MOVE "Y" TO SEARCH-END-FLAG
                INVOKE ERROR-OBJECT "YESNO-CLOSE-THE-SCREEN"
                SET ERROR-OBJECT TO NULL.

        END METHOD DO-SEARCH.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

- **From:** Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@Sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-02T22:08:31-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E62F15F.9030404@Sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca>`

```
And some comments on my own style.

You will notice that I write very old fashioned code, with narry an 
endif in sight.  The main reason for that is that I consider the 
architecture of the system far more important stylisticly than the 
actual code.  With the actual code, I tend to prefer the lowest common 
demoninator, and rely on paragraph naming and fairly small modules to 
make reading it simple.

That also includes screen loading, to an extent.  The simple fact is 
that all those extra words, particualrly when you put them on a line by 
themselves, take up screen real estate.  By using OOP to do the main 
chunking, each module is reasonably well isolated from the others, and 
local vs global data is kept under control.

You'll notice I like redundent brackets when entering complex logic. I 
think that they do make the logic much more easily read by the human, 
even if they make no difference post compilation.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com>

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>

```


Donald Tees wrote:

> the code:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

And for one of those dabbling in OOP - you threw me a little <G>. Not
necessarily the whole layout - but the format for LEDDLL1 that's giving you
the returned SCREEN-OBJECT. (Seriously - you may be using a feature of OO I
haven't yet considered - my current mindset being you MOVE Intrinsics (pic
x/9's) but SET for objects).

On style, I would be thrown by your IF ELSE constructs; to me I'm only happy
when they are indented :-

if.......

else    if.....

        else.....

And of course the above starts to run off the end of the page.

Doesn't make it any more effective than what you have coded - but I'm happier
reading it that way - plus I use the End-if. However your MAIN LOOP, I truly
wouldn't be happy until I turned that into a set of EVALUATES, hopefully
giving me a more condensed set of code.

Also this is where I need 'info' on the SCREEN-OBJECT seeing as you keep
saying 'MOVE X TO Y OF SCREEN-OBJECT - and if possible I would prefer to use
SET rather than MOVE values if possible, particularly as they become search
criteria, condition tests.

Purely OO - I would try and 'chop' this method down by making your perform
PARAGRAPHS into 'PRIVATE' methods invoked by "DO-SEARCH". Perhaps not in what
you have quoted, but there's always a chance that treated as 'PRIVATE'
methods they could be invoked by another method within this class/program,
OR, an entirely separate program could invoke "DO-SEARCH-2", (which might
have a separate set of prime rules different from 'DO-SEARCH'), which in turn
references *some* or *all* of the 'PRIVATE' methods. .

I didn't have a problem reading your Uppercase or Periods/Full Stops where
you have used them. (As you are aware I work on the opposite).

Anyway let's see what the others have to say first. Brave man for posting
source - Gallant effort.. Hope this triggers a useful discussion - no
nonsense about rules like the Ten Commandments - but just STYLE to get the
message across..

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-03T18:23:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E639E1E.1F27F442@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca> <3E62F2D2.B8575B30@shaw.ca> <3E638346.6030703@Sympatico.ca>`

```


Donald Tees wrote:

> > Purely OO - I would try and 'chop' this method down by making your perform
> > PARAGRAPHS into 'PRIVATE' methods invoked by "DO-SEARCH". Perhaps not in what
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Donald

Still on this OO topic - who do you and I ask ? There are as yet no OO COBOL gurus
that I'm aware of, and I don't think you can really look to other languages for a
principle.

As a very broad brush statement :-

A COBOL method is a piece of self functioning code, (mini program), with the
ability to be invoked or to invoke other methods. If the method contains 'sub
functions' - consider writing these as 'PRIVATE' methods if there is the
possibility that they can be used by more than one PUBLIC method.

PS: For non-OO-ers, There's no such animal as PRIVATE or PUBLIC in OO COBOL. The
PUBLIC methods are the ones you invoke like a non-OO CALL. The 'PRIVATES' are only
invoked from within the current class, e.g. :-

    invoke self "doThisSubFunction" using x return y.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

- **From:** psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T16:46:53-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<16e2f010.0303041646.1f11642c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca>`

```
Let me prefix this by saying I'm not trying to start another shouting
match about periods.  I do have a few comments about indentation,
whitespace and magic numbers that I ran across that I think hurt
readability.  Since you used a consistent style throughout the program
I was only specific on the MAINLOOP, I would have gotten repetitive in
my repetition if I continued to repeat.

It is nice, clear and concise.  But if I got a 3am call wanting me to
identify a problem with it I would have three concerns about
understand what this source file was doing, my concerns about its
style would be:


-- Whitespace is a great thing.  In some cases you have statements
slammed together without any blank lines, in others they are nicely
set off by line breaks and indentation, i.e. the IFs and UNTILs in
SEARCH-THE-TRANSACTIONS.  I like whitespace because it helps me group
things easily before I have to read them.  A compiler doesn't care
about it but it can really make a huge difference for a human reader.


-- Magic Numbers are a bad thing.  I still don't know what "g" and "N"
do, I just don't.  Most of the others I've made pretty good guesses at
(some I'm sure I'll be wrong on), but those two I just don't get.  I'm
a big fan of 88-levels because the the assure the reader that the
number/string being tested is what the programmer intended, they may
name the 88 badly, but at least those number/strings went through
their testing.  Comments have no such assurances -- I can imagine (and
have seen) code that starts out with a comment like "502 is the date
search button" that changes so much that a 502 and date searching no
longer exist.  But the comments never seem to stay up to date.  At
least with an 88-level you know anyone maintaining the code had to
consider them when making revisions.

Additionally, if you decide to add a phone number button between date
and account you find yourself renumbering the rest of the radio group
in several places (depending on window manager, some will let you add
a 505 in tab order 2).  My guess is that it is harder to change all
the right literals in all the right places than it is to insert a
502-radio-srch-phn 88-level and adding one to the other three
88-levels.  The 88-level one you can usually get right the first time
with ease and little effort.


-- Indentation is a really helpful aid to comprehension.  Butting
every IF and ELSE up against the same left margin makes for a great
deal of stress to read.  Especially with the single period used to
terminate the entire nested run.  One is forced to look ahead to find
the first period just to know the scope of the nested IF.  Indentation
can make this a non-issue, even when used with periods.


What follows is what I though as I was reading the code, it was
written first, but is posted last.  I hope this doesn't cause any
tense errors.

MAINLOOP-- This routine was a bit hard to read.  Without indentation I
had a hard time picking out where the 4-way nested IF ends and the
9-way nested IF begins.  The 4 statements buried between can easily be
lost if one doesn't catch the tiny full stop at the end of the 4th
group of statements.

I'm on a big monitor right now and I couldn't catch it instantly, on a
small screen it would be very easy to page between these two and miss
the break, thus confusing it with a 13-way nested IF.  At 80X24, even
a large one, it would have been quite hard.

This is really where indentation helps out.  Use of END-IFs would
promote readability, but really, are not both of these nested IFS
perfect cases (pun intended) for an EVALUATE?  This really is a place
where it could excel.  In the case of the second set of nested IFs the
indentation of END-IFs could get a bit annoying, but in the first it
would also look nice.  I offer an alternative suggestion using both:


   MAINLOOP.


       IF LAST-SEARCH-RECORD IS EQUAL TO 1
           MOVE LOW-VALUES TO SEARCH-NEXT-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
           MOVE "g" TO SEARCH-PREVIOUS-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
       ELSE
           IF LAST-SEARCH-RECORD IS EQUAL TO DEBIT-CREDIT-COUNT
              MOVE "g" TO SEARCH-NEXT-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
              MOVE LOW-VALUES TO SEARCH-PREVIOUS-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
           ELSE
              IF LAST-SEARCH-RECORD IS EQUAL TO ZERO
                 MOVE "g" TO SEARCH-NEXT-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                 MOVE "g" TO SEARCH-PREVIOUS-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
              ELSE
                 MOVE LOW-VALUES TO SEARCH-NEXT-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
                 MOVE LOW-VALUES TO SEARCH-PREVIOUS-T OF SCREEN-OBJECT
              END-IF
           END-IF
       END-IF


* The END-IFs are not really necessary, this could be indented with a
* full-stop period, but my personal observations is that the use of
* period to terminate IFs discourages the indentation, while END-IF
* promotes it.
*
* Not personal observation, but studies previously quoted in this
* forum, is the idea that indentation that follows the logical flow of
* the program aids in comprehension -- even for programmers that code
* without indentation. (btw, I was one of those programmers until I
* saw that and did a few tests on myself)

       MOVE REFERENCE-TYPE TO SEARCH-RTYPE OF SCREEN-OBJECT.
       INVOKE SCREEN-OBJECT "SEARCH-SHOW-THE-DATA".
       INVOKE SCREEN-OBJECT "SEARCH-GET-THE-DATA".
       MOVE SCREEN-KEYSTROKE OF SCREEN-OBJECT TO LOCAL-KEYSTROKE.


* Whitespace is also helpful when reading.  These 4 statements
* were sandwiched in between the two nested IFs.  It required a
* careful reading to see where the ELSE ended and the always executed
* statements began.  A syntax highlighting editor helps on this, but
* that is not always a luxury one has.  For example, google does not
* highlight its Cobol, nor do black and white printers.
*
* A line before the first move and after the last move would really
* offer a nice visual clue.


       EVALUATE TRUE

          WHEN LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO ZERO
                CONTINUE


          WHEN LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO 13
                CONTINUE


          WHEN LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO 500
                MOVE 1 TO SEARCH-START
                MOVE 1 TO SEARCH-INCREMENT
                MOVE "Y" TO END-FLAG
                PERFORM DO-THE-SEARCH


          WHEN LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO 501
                MOVE "Y" TO END-FLAG
                MOVE 1 TO SEARCH-RECORD


          WHEN LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO 502
                MOVE "Y" TO END-FLAG
                MOVE DEBIT-CREDIT-COUNT TO SEARCH-RECORD


          WHEN LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO 503
                ADD 1 TO LAST-SEARCH-RECORD GIVING SEARCH-START
                MOVE 1 TO SEARCH-INCREMENT
                MOVE "Y" TO END-FLAG
                PERFORM DO-THE-SEARCH


          WHEN LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO 504
                SUBTRACT 1 FROM LAST-SEARCH-RECORD GIVING SEARCH-START
                MOVE -1 TO SEARCH-INCREMENT
                MOVE "Y" TO END-FLAG
                PERFORM DO-THE-SEARCH


          WHEN LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO 27
                MOVE "Y" TO END-FLAG


          WHEN (LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO -4
                OR
               LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO -2)
                PERFORM CHECK-TYPE


          WHEN OTHER
                DISPLAY "KEYSTROKE -->" LOCAL-KEYSTROKE

          END-EVALUATE

* This could also have been an EVALUATE LOCAL-KEYSTROKE instead
* of TRUE, but then it would require paging up to the top to see
* the entire condition, and I would have had to retype more stuff...
*
* In this case they are indented similar to the nested IFs, but it
* is easy to see at a glance that it is only a single structure.  The
* nested IFs pretty much require checking each one for a period to
* ensure this.
*
* If you feel strongly about the IF/ELSE/IF/ELSE style, why not
* at least put the terminal period on a separate line.  I would
* find:
*
*   IF condition
*      statement
*   ELSE
*      statement
*   ELSE
*      statement
*   .
*
* easier to read than
*
*   IF condition
*      statement
*   ELSE
*      statement
*   ELSE
*      Statement.
*
* because it provides some visual clues to my brain before I
* actually read the thing.


For other comments, I can tell pretty much what 'MOVE "Y" TO END-FLAG'
does, that usage is pretty standard.  But what in the world does a
lower-case "g" do???  An upper-case "N"???  And I think that 0, 13,
and 27 are nul, cr, and esc respectively, but I'm not sure.  I am
certain that I don't know what -4, -2, 500, 501, 502, 503, or 504 are.

From the comments in your post I can guess that the 500-numbers are
the resource ids for your radio buttons, but from your source I have
no clue whatsoever.  I'm guessing that -2 and -4 are magic numbers
that get assigned to the default button and a cancel button.  Not
because of anything in the program, but because I've used various
window managers and that seems to be common.  And I still cannot get
"g" or "N"?

This is the great value of conditionals, If I saw LOCAL-KEYSTROKE
defined with something like:

      88 LCL-KEY-NUL                        VALUE 0.
      88 LCL-KEY-CR                          VALUE 13.
      88 LCL-KEY-ESC                        VALUE 27.
      88 LCL-RADIO-SRCH-DATE    VALUE 501.
      88 LCL-RADIO-SRCH-ACCT    VALUE 502.
      88 LCL-RADIO-SRCH-REF       VALUE 503.
      88 LCL-RADIO-SRCH-UNCHART  VALUE 504.
      88 LCL-BTN-SEARCH                VALUE -2.
      88 LCL-BTN-CANCEL                VALUE -4.

It would be easier to wade in and know what the object is doing.  A
conditional is worth a 1000 comment lines.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T03:44:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E657312.B387CE4C@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca> <16e2f010.0303041646.1f11642c@posting.google.com>`

```


Joe Zitzelberger wrote:

> Let me prefix this by saying I'm not trying to start another shouting
> match about periods.  I do have a few comments about indentation,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

<snip>

Donald,

While I could read your stuff quite well, I can't but help agree with
Joe's detailed comments - he has made a very positive contribution. Again,
he too has latched on to EVALUATES to replace IF ELSE constructs, and
CONDITION names to replace the "g" and "N".

Now when it comes to exactly what you are doing, and I'm referring to your
Screen Object, I didn't look deeply into what the code was doing until I
had Screen-Object figured. Would you care to send me that privately so I
see where it fits in - that may then cause me to make/not make further
comment.

I think Joe has contributed what you were looking for, and what I was also
hoping for, from others - without all that bloody semantic nonsense about
Periods, EXITS, GO TOs, blah, blah, blah !

Forgive me, and let me digress and wax enthusiastic about CONDITIONS and
EVALUATES. Those as a pair, I would venture, are probably one of the most
significant features introduced to COBOL ('85 ???). Compare their power to
the ugly CASE syntax in other languages - where a set of 'cases' can
consume a page of source. We've got 'em beat !

Two features of using CONDITIONS instead of move "g" or 'N".

(1) Editing a record and meeting error conditions :-

Global W/S in a class :-

01 ValidationFlag                     pic x(4) comp-5 value 0.
   88 ValidationOK                    value 0.
   88 Error-PROBEID                   value 001.
   88 Error-PROBE-MAKE                value 002.
   88 Error-PROBE-FREQUENCY           value 003.
   88 Error-PROBE-DIAMETER            value 004.
   88 Error-PROBE-TESTBLOCK           value 005.
   88 Error-PROBE-CALIBRATION         value 006.
   88 Error-DATE                      value 007.
   88 Error-ELEMENT                   value 008.
   88 OKtoDelete                      value 009.
   88 Error-Logic                     value 010.
   88 ValidationError                 value 1 thru 99.

I've got every input field repeated her, plus some. Now when it comes to
triggering my messagebox as a result of above error conditions :-

Method-id. "messageBox".
*>-----------------------------------------------------------
Working-storage section.
78 MC                          value 9.
78 ML                          value 35.
01 wa.
  05 pic x(ML) value "   ".                            *>  1
  05 pic x(ML) value "Make can't be blank   ".         *>  2
  05 pic x(ML) value "Frequency can't be zero ".       *>  3
  05 pic x(ML) value "Diameter can't be zero".         *>  4
  05 pic x(ML) value "Test Block can't be blank ".     *>  5
  05 pic x(ML) value "Calibration can't be blank  ".   *>  6
  05 pic x(ML) value "Invalid Date  YYmm ".            *>  7
  05 pic x(ML) value "Element must be Dual or Single". *>  8
  05 pic x(ML) value "OK to delete this record ?".     *>  9
01 wb redefines wa.
   05 ws-message occurs MC pic x(ML).

The error (88) also takes me to the appropriate text for the messagebox.
Not shown in this example, but I can resequence the errors to tell me if I
want to have

         when Ok
           invoke os-MessageBox "OK"
         when YesNo
           invoke os-MessageBox "YesNo"
         when YesNoCancel
           invoke os-MessageBox "YesNoCancel"
         when AbortRetryIgnore
           invoke os-MessageBox "AbortRetryIgnore"
         when OkCancel
           invoke os-MessageBox "OKCancel"
         when other
           invoke os-MessageBox "RetryCancel"

(2) Identifying Fields/Controls

I don't know how it works with SP2 - but probably in a fairly similar
fashion. I identify all my windows controls by a Field-Number (my way of
doing it, and not necessarily the best way of doing it - but it works OK
for me). I sure as heck don't want the different control objects uniquely
identified in the Dialog Program. So using the Field Numbers I put all the
objects into a collection sequenced by FIELD-NUMBER. One of the prime
reasons for doing this currently with Net Express is the interim technique
for destroying objects - to avoid memory leaks. I can kill all the objects
when the dialog is finished with one line :-

    invoke os-ControlCollection "deepFinalize" returning
os-ControlCollection

When it gets the imprimatur, the J4 TR(Technical Report) on "Finalizer"
should obviate the necessity of me worrying about garbage collection.

Again back to the 88 Conditions which is what my Field-Numbers are - to
display an existing record - Method "displayExistingRecord" invokes the
following with a PERFORM varying.....  :-

*>------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "passEntryField".
*>------------------------------------------------------------
Local-storage section.
copy "displayF.cpy".
*> this has the picture format fordecimal numbers

Procedure Division.

 Evaluate true
   when Field-EF-PROBEID
     compute Dialog-Length = function length (Probe-ProbeID)
     move Probe-ProbeID to Dialog-text

   when Field-EF-PROBE-MAKE
     compute Dialog-Length = function length (Probe-Make)
     move Probe-Make to Dialog-text

   when Field-EF-PROBE-FREQUENCY
     move Probe-Frequency to ls-display12
     compute Dialog-Length = function length (ls-display12)
     move ls-display12 to Dialog-text

   when Field-EF-PROBE-DIAMETER
     move Probe-Diameter to ls-display13
     compute Dialog-Length = function length (ls-display13)
     move ls-display13 to Dialog-text

   when Field-EF-PROBE-TESTBLOCK
     etc......

 End-evaluate

 invoke os-Dialog "setEntryfield" using Dialog-Params

End Method "passEntryField".
*>------------------------------------------------------------

I think using the Level 88's makes it pretty clear what I'm doing above.

As a final, let's get a little *fussy* with Joe's EVALUATE - I don't think
he missed it, just that he was trying to illustrate a point :-

>
>        EVALUATE TRUE
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

you can delete above CONTINUE and consider the previous and next as a
*pair*

>
>           WHEN LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO 13
>                 CONTINUE
>
>           WHEN LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO 500

The next could be either, one line :-

    MOVE 1 TO SEARCH-START, SEARCH-INCREMENT   OR :-

    MOVE 1 TO SEARCH-START,
    ......................SEARCH-INCREMENT  (the 'dots' are to line it up)

- above gives you just less to read

- but would be two lines if you had :-

                SET SEARCH-BEGIN TO TRUE
                SET SEARCH-INCREMENTZERO TO TRUE

- Although if your record's default values were zeroes, you could use
INITIALIZE, and only MOVE or SET different values, when appropriate.

>
>                 MOVE 1 TO SEARCH-START
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>                 MOVE 1 TO SEARCH-RECORD

                    etc.....

But I think you get the idea. Doing the same thing but less verbose.

I hope I'm not putting you off with the above. I can follow your code -
but like Joe I find there is a tremendous advantage to using the
Condition/Evaluate combination.

My programming - Perfecto ? Nah. Still working at it !

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Don Leahy" <xdb2imsN@nospam.whatever.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T11:12:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nlp9a.2857$6z.630505@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca> <16e2f010.0303041646.1f11642c@posting.google.com> <3E657312.B387CE4C@shaw.ca> <b4535q$ku7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:b4535q$ku7$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
> 88 levels will be better when I can set a value to false.

I agree with the concept, but there are some practical difficulties.

01  WS-FLAG            PIC X.
       88  FLAG-OKAY      VALUE 'Y'.
       88  FLAG-I-DUNNO VALUE SPACE.

Obviously, SET FLAG-OKAY TO TRUE  will set WS-FLAG to 'Y'.

But, what would be in WS-FLAG in the case of SET FLAG-OKAY TO FALSE ?  You
can't use spaces, because then we'd be setting FLAG-I-DUNNO to TRUE, which
may not be what we want.

I guess what we need in COBOL is a true "logical" data type that can *only*
have a value of 'true' or 'false'.
```

- **From:** "Calin @ Work" <dontemailme@work.com>

```
Probably a MF extension, but:
01  WS-FLAG                    PIC X.
       88  FLAG-OKAY         VALUE 'Y'
                                WHEN FALSE "N".
       88  FLAG-I-DUNNO   VALUE SPACE.

Usable?  You bet!

Calin, TORONTO
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ SET TO FALSE (was: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T12:07:25-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b45ed2$tc8$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca> <16e2f010.0303041646.1f11642c@posting.google.com> <3E657312.B387CE4C@shaw.ca> <b4535q$ku7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <nlp9a.2857$6z.630505@news20.bellglobal.com> <jWp9a.2874$6z.635176@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
For those who might be confused by the other notes in this thread, this is
the EXACT format used in the recently approved 2002 Standard. Micro Focus
simply did an "early" (pre-approval) implementation of it.

P.S. - also "enhanced" (???) in the 2002 Standard is ability to SET a series
of true/false values, e.g.

   Set This That Other to True
          He She They to False
          I-Forgot to True

(all in one SET statement)

Not a "biggy" - but a "nice to have" in some programs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T07:53:29-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b456hq$2akr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca> <16e2f010.0303041646.1f11642c@posting.google.com> <3E657312.B387CE4C@shaw.ca> <b4535q$ku7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <6Lp9a.7052$3g.873271@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```

"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:6Lp9a.7052$3g.873271@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com...

> > 88 levels will be better when I can set a value to false.
>
> You know, the inability to do this is really an AMAZING oversight by the
> 'standards recommenders.'

Ummm...  yeah, I guess you can chastise the committees for their outrageous
failure to include this, and it's true, the '85 standard doesn't allow SET
<88-level> TO FALSE.

So, go right out there and harangue to your hearts' content.  But before you
go too far with castigation and tirade, you might want to check out what the
differences are between General Format 4 as shown by ANSI X3.23-1985 on page
VI-127 in 6.23.2, SET statement general formats, and General Format 4 as
shown by ISO/IEC 1989:2002 Page 516, 14.8.35 SET statement general formats.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T11:13:48-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b457ns$2u7$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E657312.B387CE4C@shaw.ca> <b4535q$ku7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <6Lp9a.7052$3g.873271@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
In article <6Lp9a.7052$3g.873271@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>,
Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
>"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
>news:b4535q$ku7$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>'standards recommenders.'

Well... a possibility might have been the ever-popular 'Don't break 
something else in the process of fixing something'... what would a field 
with multiple 88s be set to for a FALSE?  A logical, but absurd, condition 
might be generated with:

01  WS-CHAR PIC X.
    88  RANGE-ONE VALUES X'00' THRU X'7F'.
    88  RANGE-TWO VALUES X'80' THRU X'FF'.
    88  RANGE-ALL VALUES X'00' THRU X'FF.

...

EVALUATE TRUE
    WHEN COND01
        SET RANGE-ONE TO FALSE
    WHEN COND02
        SET RANGE-TWO TO FALSE
    WHEN COND03
        SET RANGE-ALL TO FALSE
END-EVALUATE.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T17:14:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b45bac$pk1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E657312.B387CE4C@shaw.ca> <b4535q$ku7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <6Lp9a.7052$3g.873271@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <b457ns$2u7$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
> Well... a possibility might have been the ever-popular 'Don't break
> something else in the process of fixing something'... what would a field
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>     88  RANGE-ALL VALUES X'00' THRU X'FF.

We need to expand the 88 definition to allow a FALSE= clause. (or something
similar)  Any code without that clause works the same as current.  Compile
errors would result without it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T09:49:51-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b45dbv$2fge$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E657312.B387CE4C@shaw.ca> <b4535q$ku7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <6Lp9a.7052$3g.873271@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <b457ns$2u7$1@panix1.panix.com> <b45bac$pk1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
I simply can't resist.

<<We need to expand the 88 definition to allow a FALSE= clause. (or
something similar)  Any code without that clause works the same as current.
Compile errors would result without it.>>

We do, huh?   Imagine it.  Done.

(not that I had much to do with it!)

In current standard COBOL, it's true that if you're going to do a "SET
<condition-name> TO FALSE" you must have a corresponding "WHEN SET TO FALSE
IS <literal>" in the VALUE clause of the <condition-name>, but in all
honesty I don't find that an overwhelmingly onerous requirement of the
language.

Note that as far as I know it's still possible to download a copy of the
DRAFT of the new standard, for free even.  Check out Page 369, 13.16.61.1,
VALUE clause general format 3 (condition-name) as well as the
previously-mentioned citation for SET statement Format 4 and its associated
rules for supporting information.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>

```
Hmm, I never considered it might be IMPOSSIBLE to set something to FALSE

My tiny mind just assumed no one would ever use a 'flag' or 'switch'  or
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T11:53:53-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b45a31$ha0$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <6Lp9a.7052$3g.873271@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <b457ns$2u7$1@panix1.panix.com> <Szq9a.7060$3g.880455@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
In article <Szq9a.7060$3g.880455@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>,
Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:b457ns$2u7$1@panix1.panix.com...
>> Well... a possibility might have been the ever-popular 'Don't break
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>(e.g., RANGE-ALL).

I *did* call it 'absurd'... but also notice that setting RANGE-ONE to 
FALSE sets both RANGE-TWO and RANGE-ALL to TRUE.

Then, a bit deeper... consider what happens when the THRU of RANGE-TWO is 
changed to X'FE'.  SET RANGE-ONE TO FALSE will set RANGE-ALL to TRUE... 
but what about RANGE-TWO?

>
>My tiny mind just assumed no one would ever use a 'flag' or 'switch'  or
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>problem...

Truly it is a Wonderful World that has New Things to Learn in It.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
>
> Then, a bit deeper... consider what happens when the THRU of RANGE-TWO is
> changed to X'FE'.  SET RANGE-ONE TO FALSE will set RANGE-ALL to TRUE...

Oh, I have no problem with setting condition-1 to (value) and having that
affect another condition:"overlapping conditions" (sheesh, that almost
sounds like an 'official' term!) should be the programmer's responsibility.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T14:07:19-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b45ht7$7jk$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <Szq9a.7060$3g.880455@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <b45a31$ha0$1@panix1.panix.com> <Zqr9a.7072$3g.888639@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
In article <Zqr9a.7072$3g.888639@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>,
Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:b45a31$ha0$1@panix1.panix.com...
>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>sounds like an 'official' term!) should be the programmer's responsibility.

These 'overlapping conditions' (I call them 'multiple conditions') can 
have a bit of practical application... consider:

01  WS-STORE PIC 9(6) VALUE ZEROES.
    88  EASTERN-REGION-STORE       VALUES 0700 THRU 0900
    88  WESTERN-REGION-STORE       VALUES 1000 THRU 1100
    88  ORIGINAL-ACQUISITION-STORE VALUES 0725, 0813, 1020, 1090.
    88  STORE-IN-DISCOUNT-PGM      VALUES 0725, 0810, 1075, 1090.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T21:16:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E666992.D7276108@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <Szq9a.7060$3g.880455@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <b45a31$ha0$1@panix1.panix.com> <Zqr9a.7072$3g.888639@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <b45ht7$7jk$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```


docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> These 'overlapping conditions' (I call them 'multiple conditions') can
> have a bit of practical application... consider:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

And expanding on your example we could also give Howard *implied*  TRUE and
FALSE :-

01  WS-STORE PIC 9(6) VALUE ZEROES.
     88 Valid-Stores   0700 thru 1100.                         *> True Condition

     88 Invalid-Stores 0 thru 0699, 1101 thru 9999.     *> False Condition
    88  EASTERN-REGION-STORE       VALUES 0700 THRU 0900
    88  WESTERN-REGION-STORE       VALUES 1000 THRU 1100
    88  ORIGINAL-ACQUISITION-STORE VALUES 0725, 0813, 1020, 1090.
          1090.
    88  STORE-IN-DISCOUNT-PGM      VALUES 0725, 0810, 1075, 1090.

Now let me be provocative <G>. I think you could write programs without IF /
ELSE constructs just by substituting EVALUATES.

if     a = b
        then......

else ......
End-if

Becomes :-

Evalute true
  when a = b then.....
  when other ....
End-Evaluate

Other than one more line of code, for the EVALUATE  :-

if ErrorFound
    set CancelProgram to true
    EXIT METHOD/EXIT PROGRAM
End-if

----- I *do* use the above, BUT.......

becomes :

Evaluate true
    when ErrorFound
         set CancelProgram to true
         EXIT METHOD/EXIT PROGRAM
End-Evaluate

??????

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T21:57:06-05:00

```
In article <3E666992.D7276108@shaw.ca>, James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>Now let me be provocative <G>.

I'd say that you already were right there.  You've established a situation
which would increase the amount of maintenance for adding; what happens
when (not 'if') the NORTHERN-REGION-STOREs (values 1300, 1429, 1498, 1551) 
need to be dealt with?

(This is one of the times when negative conditionals - despite the 
abhorrence some have of them - prove mighty valuable.  Consider:

05  WS-MONTH PIC 99 VALUE ZEROES.
    88  VALID-MONTH VALUES 01 THRU 12.
...
MOVE INPUT-MONTH TO WS-MONTH.
IF NOT VALID-MONTH... )

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T03:48:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E66C30D.9F6EE880@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <Zqr9a.7072$3g.888639@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <b45ht7$7jk$1@panix1.panix.com> <3E666992.D7276108@shaw.ca> <b46de2$mpv$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```


docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> In article <3E666992.D7276108@shaw.ca>, James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
> >
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

Actually I wouldn't touch the above with a barge pole, (just expanding on your
example to indicate flexibility of 88s). As Richard correctly pointed out for
flexibility the above should be a flag in the DB. And in my non-coding capacity ,
(one of those awful Systems people), we used the flag technique back in the sixties
onwards when designing.

Example - Debenhams has departments numbered 1000 to in theory 1999, for central
buying. But the snotty nosed stores (Harvey Nichols - couple of blocks from Harrods -
and echoes of 'Are You Being Served' ) want to buy their own 'Fashions' - OK so
designate them 2000 to 2999. Thank God - if 1022 meant Furs (centrally bought), so
did 2002 Furs (locally bought). The bean counters got that bit right.

Within reason the ranges of numbers represented merchandise divisions, Fashions,
Fashion Accessories, Children's' Wear etc. Can't remember their specific numbers or
where they were originally categorized but we had say, Dept 1355 - Cigarettes/Tobacco
and Dept 1367 - Candies. OK.

The last stage of central buying was to buy centrally for food - so  we had the
Foodhalls, Depts 1440 -  1450. Easy - we *know* Food is range 1440 - 1450. Snag is,
and logically, somebody says, "Swing the Cigs and Candy over to Food Division" -
POOOF !

The *only* way, (bugger it, I'm getting as adamant as 'you know who') is to have
flags in your DB that can be manipulated at will.

Think back a year or so ago - Eileen (YukonMama), highlighted this exact problem with
a set of hard coded ranges built into the source.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T08:54:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b47jvf$1ar$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E666992.D7276108@shaw.ca> <b46de2$mpv$1@panix1.panix.com> <3E66C30D.9F6EE880@shaw.ca>`

```
In article <3E66C30D.9F6EE880@shaw.ca>, James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
>
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
>example to indicate flexibility of 88s).

Well... it doesn't bother me... but it might offend Helmsman Kowalski.

>As Richard correctly pointed out for
>flexibility the above should be a flag in the DB.

What 'should be' and what is are not, of course, always the same... even 
assuming the existence of a database on-site it might be one of those 
horrid 'imitation-of-a-set-of-files' architectures.

>And in my non-coding capacity ,
>(one of those awful Systems people), we used the flag technique back in the sixties
>onwards when designing.

As my Sainted paternal Grandfather, may he sleep with the angels, used to 
say, 'Never use yourself as a comparative... you'll only get 
disappointed.'

[snip of numbers remembered from Decades Past]

>Think back a year or so ago - Eileen (YukonMama), highlighted this exact 
>problem with
>a set of hard coded ranges built into the source.

Probably so... this can happen, though, when a system grows beyond the 
size of its original design.  The progression I was taught, e'er-so-long 
ago, was small system = 88s, medium system = WORKING-STORAGE table 
lookups, large system = hit the files/database.

Doing it that way may result in Something Wrong happening, of course, just 
as doing something *any* way may result in Something Wrong happening... 
but if one is to be wrong it is not such a bad thing to be wrong based on 
a textbook model; that way a correction might be a bit easier than being 
wrong based on an idiosyncratic one.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T03:50:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e66bb58.53784847@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <Szq9a.7060$3g.880455@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <b45a31$ha0$1@panix1.panix.com> <Zqr9a.7072$3g.888639@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <b45ht7$7jk$1@panix1.panix.com> <3E666992.D7276108@shaw.ca>`

```
James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:


>Now let me be provocative <G>. I think you could write programs without IF /
>ELSE constructs just by substituting EVALUATES.
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>??????

I have seriously considered doing that. My tentative practice is: one condition,
use IF; more than one, use EVALUATE. The problem comes in maintenance. The
original code says: 

If  current-byte equal to space
    perform end-word
else 
    perform start-word
end-if

Now we want to change it to:

if  current-byte equal to space 
    perform end-word
else
if  current-byte equal to ')'
    perform end-word
    perform start-word
else
    perform start-word
end-if
end-if

That's ugly, so we rewrite:

evaluate current-byte
    when space
        perform end-word
    when ')'
        perform end-word
        perform start-word
    when other
        perform start-word
end-evaluate

The maintenance job would have been easier if the IF had been written as an
EVALUATE. 


Robert
```

```
is

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

Oh, I agree with that. However - and your example is perfect to demonstrate
this - there is no good business reason in this case to 'SET (any one of
these condition) TO anything. '

If a certain store number means it's both Eastern Region and in the discount
program, the (admittedly subjective) "correct" way to code this is to update
the appropriate VALUE clauses, not code something in-line like, "IF Store-no
= '12345' SET STORE-IN-DISCOUNT-PROGRAM to TRUE."

MCM
```

```
In article <2lu9a.7115$3g.916172@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>,
Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:

>= '12345' SET STORE-IN-DISCOUNT-PROGRAM to TRUE."

Consider the possibility that... a bit of filler reserved For Future Use
gets used for a Managerial Override so that a specifically-included store
can be made to participate in the discount program.  A two-year coder
might interpret this to...

IF MANAGERIAL-OVERRIDE-DATA-IN-FILLER NOT = SPACES AND LOW-VALUES
    SET STORE-IN-DISCOUNT-PGM TO TRUE
END-IF.

... and hoo boy, things can get unpleasant.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T14:51:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b47n9i$5vc$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <Zqr9a.7072$3g.888639@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <b45ht7$7jk$1@panix1.panix.com> <2lu9a.7115$3g.916172@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <b45ph7$kn0$1@panix1.panix.com> <Jtw9a.7149$3g.941244@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
I forgot which thread this came up in, but I got a reply from my CC to Charles
Hottel:

Brazee wrote:
>
> On  3-Mar-2003, "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Among other reasons, this rule eliminates one possible reason to use GO
TO.
>
> I don't understand this - what relationship is there between the SORT verb
and
> GO TO?

In at least one older dialect (OS/VS COBOL, if I remember correctly), an
input or
output procedure had to be a section, not a mere paragraph.  With
non-trivial
sections, it can be awkward to avoid using a GO TO to leave the section
-- unless
of course you use sections *instead of* paragraphs, which can invite
other
problems if you accidentally omit the "SECTION" keyword on a procedure
header.

With later dialects a paragraph can be an input or output procedure, so
the
issue becomes moot.

> I occasionally will have a job where internal sorts are applicable, but
have
> never been tempted to put a GO TO in those programs.
>
> =======================================================
>
> I would argue with him that it should be "hardly ever use the SORT verb -
and
> only do so after analysis of the problem indicates that this will offer a
real
> improvement over using an external sort".

That's pretty much what I do say, if you go beyond the summary page for
control
structures and peruse:

	http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/style/sort.html

> And if table sorts get added to the language, make sure to exclude them
from
> this style request.

I heartily agree.  I used a table sort once or twice, as a vendor
extension, and
was glad to have it available.  I should add a note to that effect.

I presume that the posting above is from comp.lang.cobol, which I
haven't
visited for some time now.  For one thing, my news reader stopped
talking to
my ISP's news server for some reason and I never figured out how to
reconcile them.  More importantly, about five years ago I moved from MVS
to
Unix, and I haven't used COBOL much.  That also explains why the website
is
so antiquated, as Mr. Klein has pointed out in another posting.

Feel free to quote this email in the news group if you like.  Thanks for
the
note.

Michael C. Kasten	mck9@swbell.net
http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
```

```
On  6-Mar-2003, "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

> chottel@cpcug.org


> In at least one older dialect (OS/VS COBOL, if I remember correctly), an
> input or
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> header.

All of the compilers I worked with until recently had this requirement.    But I
don't remember not being able to use:
INPUT-SORT SECTION.
INPUT-SORT.
       PERFORM 1100-READ-LOOP UNTIL EOF-FINDER.
REST-OF-THE-PROGRAM SECTION.
1100-READ-LOOP.
...

I had sections because the compiler required them, but put all the code in
paragraphs after REST-OF-THE-PROGRAM SECTION.

I'm glad these aren't necessary anymore.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T08:50:12-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b47u85$154t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <Zqr9a.7072$3g.888639@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <b45ht7$7jk$1@panix1.panix.com> <2lu9a.7115$3g.916172@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <b45ph7$kn0$1@panix1.panix.com> <Jtw9a.7149$3g.941244@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <b47n9i$5vc$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:b47n9i$5vc$1@peabody.colorado.edu...

> In at least one older dialect (OS/VS COBOL, if I remember correctly), an
> input or
> output procedure had to be a section, not a mere paragraph.

That would be any COBOL compiler compliant with ANSI X3.23-1974, and the
procedure could also (with THRU section-name-2) be a group of CONTIGUOUS
sections.  Allowing paragraphs or groups of contiguous paragraphs was a
reasonably common extension.

ANSI X3.23-1985 relaxed that restriction, with some side-effects (I've
mentioned that elsewhere in this forum).

> With later dialects a paragraph can be an input or output procedure, so
> the
> issue becomes moot.

More to the point, there's no restriction in '85 COBOL on branching out of
the paragraph to anywhere else in the program, so long as you come back to
the terminus of the procedure.  In '74 and prior, an INPUT or OUTPUT
PROCEDURE was defined by a block of code at compile time; in '85 and
subsequent, the INPUT or OUTPUT PROCEDURE is defined by whatever path the
program actually takes at execution time.

> > And if table sorts get added to the language, make sure to exclude them
> from
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> was glad to have it available.  I should add a note to that effect.

Since SORT ... INPUT PROCEDURE ... OUTPUT PROCEDURE ... need not necessarily
involve files at all (in our implementation, for example, the "SD" ends up
being vaporware, a convenient place to store information at compile time),
as others have pointed out it's possible to construct a "table sort" in
earlier dialects of COBOL.  I would expect, though, that it would at least
in theory be possible for the compiler to generate more efficient code for a
true "table sort" than for its procedural equivalent, but I would also point
out that the 2002 standard does not obligate the implementor to do so.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-07T00:35:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e67d75d.126505490@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <Zqr9a.7072$3g.888639@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <b45ht7$7jk$1@panix1.panix.com> <2lu9a.7115$3g.916172@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <b45ph7$kn0$1@panix1.panix.com> <Jtw9a.7149$3g.941244@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <b47n9i$5vc$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b47u85$154t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>Since SORT ... INPUT PROCEDURE ... OUTPUT PROCEDURE ... need not necessarily
>involve files at all (in our implementation, for example, the "SD" ends up
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>out that the 2002 standard does not obligate the implementor to do so.

The compiler could generate _much_ more efficient code for a list sort.
Hopefully, we'll get that in the next release, although I'm not holding my
breath. Exchanging entries in a list sort requires six pointer operations, as
opposed to moving 200 byte entries around. On timing tests, list sorting runs
2-5 times faster than table sorting. 

Problem is, there is no standard structure for a list entry. That would have to
be defined in the language.Or, better yet, in the Standard Class Library for
Lists.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T11:03:05+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b45sbc$ufd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <b45a31$ha0$1@panix1.panix.com> <Zqr9a.7072$3g.888639@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <b45ht7$7jk$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> These 'overlapping conditions' (I call them 'multiple conditions') can
> have a bit of practical application... consider:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 88ï¿œï¿œSTORE-IN-DISCOUNT-PGMï¿œï¿œï¿œï¿œï¿œï¿œVALUESï¿œ0725,ï¿œ0810,ï¿œ1075,ï¿œ1090.

Building data into the source code is seldom 'practical'.  In this 
particular case if they want more than 100 stores in the west or 200 in the 
east, or if they simply want to change a store's discount program then the 
programmer has to do it and this involves all the testing procedures.

It is also extremely non-reusable.  What if your company aquires another 
and you want to reuse your programs for the new company.

These 88 levels should be attributes of the store's data record, not of the 
program.

In very many cases, maybe all, where overlapping conditions occur it is 
probably using the wrong data model.  

I also dislike 88 levels because I can't search for all uses of 'WS-Store', 
I have to find all aliases for it and serach for each of those.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T22:10:56-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b46e80$qa7$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <Zqr9a.7072$3g.888639@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <b45ht7$7jk$1@panix1.panix.com> <b45sbc$ufd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
In article <b45sbc$ufd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>,
Richard Plinston  <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Building data into the source code is seldom 'practical'.

Building data into WORKING-STORAGE in this manner can be seen as far more 
'practical' than hardcoding values... and values get hardcoded all the 
time.  An ugly situation, true, but one that I, at least, have encountered 
frequently.

>In this 
>particular case if they want more than 100 stores in the west or 200 in the 
>east, or if they simply want to change a store's discount program then the 
>programmer has to do it and this involves all the testing procedures.

It was never stated by me that it would be otherwise.

>
>It is also extremely non-reusable.  What if your company aquires another 
>and you want to reuse your programs for the new company.

Corporate acquisitions are the function of the Executives... and if 
another company is acquired then *our* shop better have the more funky 
code so we wind up doing the processing and not the acquirees.

>
>These 88 levels should be attributes of the store's data record, not of the 
>program.

I've seen situations like that, sure... just like I've seen situations 
like the one coded here.  Hope for the chicken, prepare for the sausage... 
hope for the breast, prepare for the wurst.

>
>In very many cases, maybe all, where overlapping conditions occur it is 
>probably using the wrong data model.  

Have you been taking lessons for a proponent of Best Practices?  Never 
mind the why and wherefore, these situations - *and* uglier - show up all 
the time; this is why one learns to evaluate them carefully and deal with 
them efficiently.  Doesn't matter if the last project leader was the CFO's 
nephew, doesn't matter if the President's idiot brother-in-law sold the 
company a worthless software package, doesn't matter if the data architect 
- or maybe even just the last coder to touch the program - was hung-over 
or maybe still drunk.

The code's there... so it might be well to discuss how one deals with it.

>
>I also dislike 88 levels because I can't search for all uses of 'WS-Store', 
>I have to find all aliases for it and serach for each of those.

This increases one's understand of the various vocabularies used in the 
corporation... it gets to a point where you can read stuff and know 
whether the Engineers or the Accountants were providing the 'specs'.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T12:19:46-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<16e2f010.0303051219.185eda55@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca> <16e2f010.0303041646.1f11642c@posting.google.com> <3E657312.B387CE4C@shaw.ca> <b4535q$ku7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message news:<b4535q$ku7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...
>
> 88 levels will be better when I can set a value to false.
>

False is nice, but hardly the end-all, be-all.  In the case of this
object, I was suggesting replacing the literals in the source with a
condition name.  In that case the condition name would never be
changed to true or false, just as you would never move a value to a
literal.  It would only be used to test the value.  The conditions
"LCL-RADIO-SEARCH-DATE" and "NOT LCL-RADIO-SEARCH-DATE" are fully
valid.  In fact, the "NOT condition-name" style is a test for falsity
(falseness?).

In the case of boolean conditions you can always define your own
falses.  Some random examples I have sitting on my desktop:

   05 Pic X           value space.
     88 File-Closed   value space.
     88 File-Opened   value 'Y'.

   05 Pic X           value space.
     88 More-Data     value space.
     88 End-Of-Date   value 'Y'.

Even without the false option 88's are extraordinarily useful in many
cases.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T14:59:34+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b47nom$604$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca> <16e2f010.0303041646.1f11642c@posting.google.com> <3E657312.B387CE4C@shaw.ca> <b4535q$ku7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303051219.185eda55@posting.google.com>`

```

On  5-Mar-2003, psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote:

> > 88 levels will be better when I can set a value to false.
> >
>
> False is nice, but hardly the end-all, be-all.

Yep.  But they will be better.   I use 88 levels to show contrary conditions
now, and when someone does maintenance, they can either assume from my naming
conditions that FILE-OPEN and FILE-CLOSED are opposites, or they can double
check on the top because they have been bitten by making obvious assumptions in
the past.

But the following doesn't require such:

If FIRST-TIME
     Set FIRST-TIME to FALSE
     Perform FIRST-TIME-ROUTINE
End-IF.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-07T00:35:24+00:00

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

> I use 88 levels to show contrary conditions
>now, and when someone does maintenance, they can either assume from my naming
>conditions that FILE-OPEN and FILE-CLOSED are opposites, or they can double
>check on the top because they have been bitten by making obvious assumptions in
>the past.

I use 'not file-open', and I always use low-value as false. I always initialize
structures, whether in working-storage or dynamically allocated,  to low-values

     88 file-open value 'o' false low-value.
>
>But the following doesn't require such:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>End-IF.

If you need a first-time flag, there's probably something wrong with your
program's structure. I haven't fealt the need in twenty years.
```

```
> > I use 88 levels to show contrary conditions
> >now, and when someone does maintenance, they can either assume from my naming
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

But someone has to know your standards, or check to make sure of his
assumptions.

> >But the following doesn't require such:
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> program's structure. I haven't fealt the need in twenty years.

Then change this to FILE-OPEN.   I just wrote something from the top of my head.

Something like a FIRST-TIME switch can be useful when you are walking a database
to pick up transactions for a particular customer - and if you find a qualifying
transaction, you want to write a customer header record before writing the first
transaction.   Sometimes we don't have any choice about what how the output
should be formatted.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-03-07T08:42:31-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<16e2f010.0303070842.7de7a25b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca> <16e2f010.0303041646.1f11642c@posting.google.com> <3E657312.B387CE4C@shaw.ca> <b4535q$ku7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <16e2f010.0303051219.185eda55@posting.google.com> <b47nom$604$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e67d53e.125961415@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message news:<3e67d53e.125961415@news.optonline.net>...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> 
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> program's structure. I haven't fealt the need in twenty years.

I find myself doing this alot lately.  In a case where a program
cannot be "INITIAL" because it needs multiple calls to complete a unit
of work -- A SAX document handler for example.

This technique can save the caller the trouble of CANCELing after a
unit of work when a CANCEL is always required, just turn the
FIRST-TIME flag back on at the end of each UOW, or document in this
example.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-08T07:55:33+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b4aq4d$205$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <16e2f010.0303051219.185eda55@posting.google.com> <b47nom$604$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e67d53e.125961415@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> If you need a first-time flag, there's probably something wrong with your
> program's structure. I haven't fealt the need in twenty years.

No.  There are many reasons why a first time flag is required and can't be 
done in other useful ways, though it may be be specifically called 
'first-time'.

For example if printing an exception report one _could_ initialise file 
(open), initialise printer (open + headings) and then then run through the 
file printing report.  Simple.

However, if a more sophisticated approach is required, such as printing 
_nothing_ unless there are exceptions then there needs to be a flag of some 
sort that indicates the printer initialise is required when the first 
exception is found.  This may be something obvious such as 'first-found' or 
something less obvious such as a null object pointer, or a zero page-number.

'First-time' flags are not necessarily a sign of falws in the program 
structure, but are often a sign of delayed decisions that may indicate a 
higher level of sophistication.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-08T01:12:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
>Robert Wagner wrote:

…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>higher level of sophistication.

I have no objection to 'if not file-open'. In the case of writing a customer
header, mentioned by someone else, I'd write 'if customer-id not equal to
last-customer-header  perform write-customer-header.' That's not an indicator or
'flag', but it accomplishes the same end. 

I was objecting to logic that trips over itself by making first-time conditional
rather structural. 

Robert
```
