##### ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-07T22:39:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E69201A.1A0299EC@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca>`

```


Donald Tees wrote:

> the code:
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>         77 ERROR-OBJECT             USAGE OBJECT REFERENCE LEDDLL1.

>            etc.......

>         PROCEDURE DIVISION.
>         MAIN-LINE.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> SCREEN-OBJECT.

etc....

Just a follow-up on the above - and I don't think Donald was looking for a
dissertation on use/non use of full stops/periods <G>

It threw me for a loop where he was using :-

            MOVE START-SEARCH-DATE TO SEARCH-DATE OF
                  SCREEN-OBJECT.

"Hey ! You can't do that !", I said to myself, "Moving to an object". It got
even worse when he sent me two demo programs doing exactly that without
specifying the format of the Screen-Object in Program 1, which is where the
MOVEs were occurring..

The short answer is you CAN. Fujitsu already has this COBOL 2002 feature,
(based on the then draft at the time they got cracking),  associated with
Repository and usage of Properties. Via M/F's Answer Exchange I confirmed Net
Express will have exactly the same in Version 4, (due out soon). And you can
do it with an interim approach in Version 3.1 - snag with the latter - it
isn't mentioned in the on-line help BUT is covered in the on-line LRM book.
(Whoever looks at the LRM, if you are comfortable with the language ???). I
know, I know - WMK - you are going to say "Well, you bloody well should !").

Typically at the moment I'll have a method :-

copy "CustomerRecord.cpy". *> a typical Level 01 text record
invoke os-CustomerFile "getRecord" using PrimeKey returning CustomerRecord

Similarly CustomerFile method "getRecord" also contains CustomerRecord.cpy"

I haven't yet figured this use of Properties but it does appear to have
distinct advantages over passing a linked-record - in effect, it is allowing
you to reference the record from one place 'in situ' by its object reference.
(Perhaps some of my copyfile techniques could become obsolescent ?).

As I've commented before OO is some 90% plus, traditional non-OO syntax. OO
takes you into a different mindset learning 'concepts', (techniques, if you
like), containing a richness in flexibility. And the last is completely
without reference to handling GUIs.

Even if you are not into OO you probably by now have some glimmer about
ClassC inherits from ClassB, ClassB inherits from ClassA. Too complex to
detail here, but in fact you can go north, south, east and west with this
class invoking - and Class A, (at the top of the tree) has the ability to
invoke subordinate Classes B and C.. As an extreme - if you had Classses A
through Z, each can invoke the other 25 - and you do it with 26
object-references or pointers..

I've no doubt the above features are covered in J4's COBOL 2002 - but you
would be hard challenged to find it easily, because it is a "technical" and
not a "conceptual" or "example" document.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>

```
news:3E69201A.1A0299EC@shaw.ca...

> I've no doubt the above features are covered in J4's COBOL 2002 - but you
> would be hard challenged to find it easily, because it is a "technical"
and
> not a "conceptual" or "example" document.

Pages 768-784 inclusive, E.17, "Object Oriented Concepts", are intended to
provide an overview and introduction; this section also includes some code
fragments and a rudimentary application example.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-08T03:36:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E69646C.78C7F6BE@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca> <3E69201A.1A0299EC@shaw.ca> <b4b7s7$dar$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```


Chuck Stevens wrote:

> > I've no doubt the above features are covered in J4's COBOL 2002 - but you
> > would be hard challenged to find it easily, because it is a "technical"
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> fragments and a rudimentary application example.

Many thanks Chuck. Just as well you said E.17 . I start at Page 789 - but I
think that's an Adobe problem. As they say, 'Been there, seen that, done that'.
Billl zeroed me in on the same reference about two years ago.

As it goes, it is very well presented, and I know somewhere in there I've read
about multiple inheritance plus the caveats about using same. However, the
examples really only scratch the surface, as does one of Bill's 'favourites',
Artur's introductory book for Fujitsu OO.

Some of the J4 examples are beyond my ken at the moment, because I don't have a
compiler that currently handles them  I'm fairly certain I can't use E.17.4.2
".... with the FINAL clause" - neither on-line help nor the LRM contain the
word 'FINAL' - so you don't absorb and skip over the ones you can't use..

Probably OO in general, but OO COBOL in particular -  it's like the saying
"Thar's gold in them thar' hills". You can either research it yourself or hope
that some assayer, like Will Price, comes along and puts it into perspective
with examples. Still even he ducks out in his text, giving a broad coverage on
collections and concludes, "Collections/Dictionaries are a topic that would
fill a separate book". True, true, true.

On the collection angle, currently there's a Czech in M/F's Answer Exchange
asking, "I know about the Default Sort, and understand from your example how to
write my own Sort. But if I use my own Sort how can I switch back to the
Default Sort ?".
Innocent question, but a bit tricky. I bet there are about six solutions,
including things like overriding the class method that has the Default Sort, or
perhaps storing the DefaultSort and OwnSort as separate methods invoked as
callbacks.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-07T18:34:40-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<T_9aa.4433$Xo3.805815@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca> <3E69201A.1A0299EC@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3E69201A.1A0299EC@shaw.ca...
> Donald Tees wrote:
> >         77 SCREEN-OBJECT            USAGE OBJECT REFERENCE LEDDLL1.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> "Hey ! You can't do that !", I said to myself, "Moving to an object". It
got
> even worse when he sent me two demo programs doing exactly that without
> specifying the format of the Screen-Object in Program 1, which is where
the
> MOVEs were occurring..
>
> The short answer is you CAN. Fujitsu already has this COBOL 2002 feature,
> (based on the then draft at the time they got cracking),  associated with
> Repository and usage of Properties. Via M/F's Answer Exchange I confirmed
Net
> Express will have exactly the same in Version 4, (due out soon). And you
can
> do it with an interim approach in Version 3.1 - snag with the latter - it
> isn't mentioned in the on-line help BUT is covered in the on-line LRM
book.
> (Whoever looks at the LRM, if you are comfortable with the language ???).
I
> know, I know - WMK - you are going to say "Well, you bloody well should
!").
>
> Typically at the moment I'll have a method :-
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Similarly CustomerFile method "getRecord" also contains
CustomerRecord.cpy"
>
> I haven't yet figured this use of Properties but it does appear to have
> distinct advantages over passing a linked-record - in effect, it is
allowing
> you to reference the record from one place 'in situ' by its object
reference.
> (Perhaps some of my copyfile techniques could become obsolescent ?).
>

Probably not, but they will be affected. I would probably code the sequence
a bit differently. Instead of:

     invoke os-CustomerFile "getRecord" using PrimeKey returning
CustomerRecord

I would code (in the main routine)

    MOVE PrimeKey TO KeyField OF OS-CUSTOMER-FILE.
    IF ERROR-FLAG OF OS-CUSTOMER-FILE IS EQUAL TO "Y"
        MOVE ERROR-MESSAGE OF OS-CUSTOMER-FILE TO
            ERROR-MESSAGE OF ERROR-WINDOW
        INVOKE ERROR-WINDOW "SHOW-MESSAGE-WAIT"
    ELSE
        PERFORM DO-THE-NEXT-STEP.

DO-THE-NEXT-STEP.
        MOVE CUSTOMER-RECORD OF OS-CUSTOMER-FILE TO LOCAL-CUSTOMER-RECORD.

KeyField would be set up as a SET style property, which contained the file
read procedure, looked up any appropriate status codes and set an an error
message in the literal field ERROR-MESSAGE, and then set a flag (ERROR-FLAG)
before exiting. Note that by doing it with a SET statement, you cannot *look
at" the field "KeyField" ... it s a write only field <G>.  You would have to
set it up with a GET method as well to read it. That way, setting it invokes
specific code, while getting it is a simple move.  Note, though, that you
would still need the copy ... it would be copied under the 01 level data
name LOCAL-CUSTOMER-RECORD.

Donald
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

- **From:** bdhobbs18@acm.org (Bill Hobbs)
- **Date:** 2003-03-17T19:28:58-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74ca5032.0303171928.38d98b3e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca>`

```
Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@Sympatico.ca> wrote in message news:<3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca>...
>         PROCEDURE DIVISION.
>         MAIN-LINE.
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>             PERFORM SEARCH-ACCOUNT.

The comments made by others about 88s, evaluates, end-if, and
indenting nested ifs agree with my style.  A part of my personal style
that I'd like to throw to the wolves is paragraphs and performs.

If the code is contained within a loop or the same code would be
executed in a number of locations, I'll put it in a paragraph and
perform that.  But initialization, termination, and run once code - I
put that in-line.

About the only other time I'll use a paragraph and perform are for
large amounts of code that would be contained within another statement
(if, evaluate, at end, etc.).  Depending on my mood, fifty lines may
be a large amount.

Personally, I find it much easier to view sequentially executed code
that is written sequentially, not jumping around, running out of
fingers to hold my place in listings or trying to maintain a LIFO
queue in my wetware at oh-dark-thirty.

From my point of view, why bury code, that may be relevant to a
problem, in paragraphs that are performed only one time?  Bring the
code up where it can be easily reviewed.

One argument that I've heard is that a paragraph should not be more
than X lines long.  Is that X executable lines, or are comments and
blank lines included?  If my paragraph is X + 1 long, what is the
penalty?  If no penalty, how about X + 2?  X + 3 ... Then I'll ask
another manager the same question.  Who decided X in the first place? 
X seems to be very flexible and rarely written down.

One person told me that the paragraph name can tell what the paragraph
does.  I explained to them that COBOL supports this really neat
statement called a comment - it can be longer than a paragraph name,
extend over multiple lines, and can be used in more places than a
paragraph name.

I've yet to have a fellow programmer tell me that their head exploded
because one of my paragraphs was hundreds of lines long and they
couldn't deal with it ... but I guess if their head did explode, they
would have difficulty telling me about it.  Well, the police would
probably have a clue from the blood-soaked listing with my name in the
comments.  ;-)

Well wolves, what are your opinions on this?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-18T05:23:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e76aa51.50801856@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca> <74ca5032.0303171928.38d98b3e@posting.google.com>`

```
bdhobbs18@acm.org (Bill Hobbs) wrote:

>Donald Tees <Donald_Tees@Sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:<3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca>...
>>         PROCEDURE DIVISION.
>>         MAIN-LINE.
…[74 more quoted lines elided]…
>Well wolves, what are your opinions on this?


You got it essentially right. The other guys want to group sentences having
'temporal cohesion' (using Yourdon/Constantine terminology) into a paragraph.
For instance, all the startup stuff.That's not right. Isolation should be based
on 'functional cohesion'. Temporal stuff should be in the structure rather than
paragraph.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-18T15:01:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b57cbl$h5v$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca> <74ca5032.0303171928.38d98b3e@posting.google.com>`

```
If I can group functions together, I will do so.

My-Program.
    Perform Get-Parms.
    Perform Initialize-database.
    Perform Process-records until INFILE-EOF.
    Perform Housekeeping.
    Goback.


If Get-Parms is big and complicated, and the maintenance programmer doesn't is
not interested in checking it right now - it is quick for him to get to the part
of the code that he is checking on.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-18T10:19:32-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QMGda.1735$sK6.389936@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca> <74ca5032.0303171928.38d98b3e@posting.google.com> <b57cbl$h5v$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
I follow pretty much the same rule.  Another poster stated that paragraph
names are not as good as comments, but I tend to disagree.  I prefer well
chosen paragraph names because they conserve space, and comments do not tend
to be updated when there is a rush job.  I reserve comments for either
generalized explanation blocks, or in the odd case, where I feel actual code
might be rather obscure. To me, they are more in the nature of "notes to the
next guy".

Donald

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message

the part
```

```
Often I will put a comment block in for the simple reason that I know I am
going to have to come back and redo a patch correctly at a later date ... I
am dealing with an emergency at the time.  In cases like that, I will delete
the comments when I have had time to finish the job correctly.

Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

- **From:** psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-03-18T07:54:53-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<16e2f010.0303180754.5fa5e607@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca> <74ca5032.0303171928.38d98b3e@posting.google.com>`

```
bdhobbs18@acm.org (Bill Hobbs) wrote in message news:<74ca5032.0303171928.38d98b3e@posting.google.com>...
<snip>
> Personally, I find it much easier to view sequentially executed code
> that is written sequentially, not jumping around, running out of
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> paragraph name.

For an especially complicated statement, then X might well be 1 or 2
lines.  For a simple series of MOVEs then X could well exceed hundreds
of lines.  The decision of what X is is a heuristic judgement on the
part of the author.  Some programmers have firm rules like "no more
than one screen" or "no more than 50 lines", while some approach the
problem with a 'gut-feeling' of "this routine is doing too much".

The general idea is to keep a separately executable section of code
(paragraph/procedure/function/subroutine/whatever) small enough to be
understandable at a quick glance.  When I see a paragraph called
"MUNGE-WIDGETS", if it is small enough, and clear enough that I can
grasp what it does in a glance, then I can forget about that details
of that section of code and treat "MUNGE-WIDGETS" as an atomic verb. 
This approach supports the 'brick-laying' metaphor of programming. 
Once a brick is built and tested it can be attached to other bricks to
build completely different structures without changes.  Those
structures can then be treated as single bricks and assembled to build
even more complex structures.

This is why the paragraph (function/procedure/subroutine/whatever)
should be named with exactly what the function does.  Not only does it
help clarify for any future reading, but it discourages the original
author from putting unrelated statements in the paragraph (sometimes
called "side effects").  A paragraph named
"A123456789-CENTRAL-LOOP-PARA" can contain ANYTHING and will require
very detailed reading to grasp its purpose.  A paragraph named
"B123456789-READ-FILE-X" usually gives a clue to the reader that it is
going to read file X and nothing more.  A quick glance at READ-FILE-X
will verify if the author kept up this convention or if he did lots of
unrelated things in the paragraph.

As for comments, well, they are usually worse than useless, sometimes
very helpful, and often out of date.  I cannot count the times I have
seen entire programs filled with comments like:

   *   Add amount  outstanding to total outstanding
       ADD XYZ-AMOUNT-OUTSTANDING TO ABC-TOTAL-OUTSTANDING

Such comments are worse than useless as they just clutter up the code
with a nonstandard restatements of the Cobol.

Where I do find a great deal of value is narratives for each
paragraph/section/nested program that states what the author is trying
to accomplish, what they expect in, what they do with it and how they
put it out.  While comments of this style are often great and helpful,
it is easy for them to get out of date as maintenance changes things. 
So unless the program is unchanged from the original implementation I
usually don't trust them without careful verification.

Just my humble $0.02...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-18T17:03:13

- **Message-ID:** `<3e7735ed_1@text-west.newsgroups.com>`

```

"Joe Zitzelberger" <psychedelic-harry@mindless.com> wrote in message
news:16e2f010.0303180754.5fa5e607@posting.google.com...
> bdhobbs18@acm.org (Bill Hobbs) wrote in message
news:<74ca5032.0303171928.38d98b3e@posting.google.com>...
> <snip>
>  I cannot count the times I have
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I agree completely with what you said Joe.

However, as I DO use comments in COBOL, I was interested in your last
sentence. Having verified the comment is true (or not) would you then amend
the comment?

I write sectionalised code (yes, I know...I don't intend to respond to any
argument about this. It is a preference and nobody has ever come to me and
said "Jeez Pete, I really can't maintain your code because that
sectionalization makes it so hard to follow...". I don't care whether YOU
(all) use paragraphs or sections, so please grant me the same courtesy...)
and because the code is written in very clearly defined sections (analogous
to the "bricks" you mentioned), I do put a  comment at the beginning of each
section (seldom more than 5 lines) explaining what this particular section
is trying to accomplish. I also "Line Comment" (*>) where I am doing
something a bit unusual or not immediately obvious.(And I do use meaningful
Section names...).

As I use these comments when I come back to do maintenance, and I DO
maintain them if they require it, I just wondered if anybody else here
actually uses comments and would maintain them also...???

Pete.
```

```
> As I use these comments when I come back to do maintenance, and I DO
> maintain them if they require it, I just wondered if anybody else here
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-18T19:32:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BvKda.12369$ja4.852312@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca> <74ca5032.0303171928.38d98b3e@posting.google.com> <16e2f010.0303180754.5fa5e607@posting.google.com> <3e7735ed_1@text-west.newsgroups.com>`

```

Peter E.C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message news:3e7735ed_1@text-west.newsgroups.com...
> 
> I just wondered if anybody else here actually uses comments and would maintain them also...???

I do.  I use program comments aka remarks, section comments, paragraph comments,
and inline comments, and keep them up-to-date.

I also use sections, and lettered-and-numbered paragraphs, and Perform...Thru, 
and periods, and Y and N as program indicator values.

I never use Alter, or Go To (with or without Depending On), or Evaluate, 
or Initialize, or True.

I always code the ELSE path in an IF statement, to demonstrate that I have
performed the necessary "What if?" evaluation of that condition.  

I avoid the NOT construct like the plague, simply because junior staff
tend to make a lot of mistakes when maintaining such code.

I avoid program abends.  Online, they irritate the users and infuriate the systems guys.
During Batch, which runs overnight, well, need I say more. 

I do not cloud the clarity of main process routines with support procedures,
as they have a habit, sooner or later, of needing to be executed from/in 
more than place in the program.  That's my style, like it or not, your choice.

Fundamentally, it is in my brain that the program's construction is formulated,
so it needs to be and will be done using a methodology which I understand
and am most comfortable working with, which does not violate acceptable usage
and/or cause others difficulties when working with the program.

I should add that I tend to get assigned major, complex systems and programs, 
so that does have a bearing on my approach, and the need for it.  

If that is apposed to someone else's methodology, then we are going to have
a problem regardless of whose methodology, right or wrong, is used,
when maintenance is required, unless each methodology is comprehensible.

A company I worked for once contemplated the purchase of a program optimizer.  
Along with several others, I was asked to submit several programs for evaluation
to assist in evaluating the optimizer.  My programs graded out at 100%.
Make of that whatever you will.

In summary, if someone else's programming style does not reflect my own,
I don't give a flying toenail, as long as it works and I can easily understand it.
I consider this to be a demonstration of my willingness and ability
to function in a team environment in a spirit of co-operativeness.
You also demonstrate that trait.  Not everyone does.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

```
On 18-Mar-2003, "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote:

> I avoid program abends.  Online, they irritate the users and infuriate the
> systems guys.  During Batch, which runs overnight, well, need I say more.

I recently modified an existing batch program (which gets run around 2:00 each
morning), so that it will abend.   It seems that the vendor which wrote this
program to copy our database into our older KSDS VSAM file didn't check the
status code after an open.   The error message that came out was not noticed by
the operators.   I changed the program to do a display and then a standard
abend, and then wrote up instructions to the operators about what to do when the
abend occurred before restarting.   (Go to CV 10 and vary the file off-line)

Abends have their place.
```

```
program if I feel it necessary to explain why something is being done.  Not

your head and wonder why in the world....  but we are supposed to put a quick
comment at the beginning of the program about what was changed when we do
maintenance - something like 'Corrected calculation of standard cost' or
'changed value of xyz' along with the date and our name.  I do delete comments
from the beginning of a program that are more than 5 years old :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-03-18T14:30:45-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<16e2f010.0303181430.6c2a11b3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca> <74ca5032.0303171928.38d98b3e@posting.google.com> <16e2f010.0303180754.5fa5e607@posting.google.com> <3e7735ed_1@text-west.newsgroups.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3e7735ed_1@text-west.newsgroups.com>...
>
> However, as I DO use comments in COBOL, I was interested in your last
> sentence. Having verified the comment is true (or not) would you then amend
> the comment?

Of course.  If I were changing the code related to the incorrect
comment I would update the entire comment to reflect the new state of
the program.

If I were altering other code not related to the comment in error I
would put a little note after the comment to the effect of "This seems
to have been changed -- the current behavior is..."

There is one exception to this -- if I were researching something and
came across a bad comment in a module I was not intending to change I
would likely send the owner of said module a note about it and do
nothing.  But this is only because of the vast amount of paperwork,
approvals and permission required by change control software and the
departments that manage change control software to actually effect the
change.

> I write sectionalised code (yes, I know...I don't intend to respond to any
> argument about this. It is a preference and nobody has ever come to me and
> said "Jeez Pete, I really can't maintain your code because that
> sectionalization makes it so hard to follow...". <snip>

No arguments intended, just curiosity.

Other than maintaining a few sorts in some old vendor code I've never
encountered sections (and thus have no strong feelings either way). 
As far as I ever knew, a section was a way of grouping paragraphs into
an overlay -- and if storage was ever so tight that I needed to work
in phases I would rewrite the program in assembler.

Do sections do more than this?  What benefit do you get from their
use?  Is it the grouping that is the benefit?

If they are just like paragraphs I don't really see why it would make
a difference to use them or not.  Do sections follow the same flow of
control rules as paragraphs?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-20T16:06:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5cou4$bnd$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca> <74ca5032.0303171928.38d98b3e@posting.google.com> <16e2f010.0303180754.5fa5e607@posting.google.com> <3e7735ed_1@text-west.newsgroups.com> <16e2f010.0303181430.6c2a11b3@posting.google.com>`

```

On 18-Mar-2003, psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote:

> If they are just like paragraphs I don't really see why it would make
> a difference to use them or not.  Do sections follow the same flow of
> control rules as paragraphs?

Sections seem to be more popular in Europe than in the US.   I don't like them
because they are "perform thru" commands.  I have seen cases where someone has
added a paragraph after a section without realizing that they were changing the
section.  I believe it is safer to use the most atomic performs available.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-19T05:44:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E78043A.2D7DF21@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca> <74ca5032.0303171928.38d98b3e@posting.google.com> <16e2f010.0303180754.5fa5e607@posting.google.com> <3e7735ed_1@text-west.newsgroups.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" wrote:
> (snip)
> As I use these comments when I come back to do maintenance, and I DO
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

I maintain comments in COBOL programs.  If I'm in the program to
change it for any reason, comments are fair game, too.  My shop has
the usual "revision history" comments in the ID Division, and
frequently has brief comments at the beginning of a paragraph. 
Occasionally, there may be comments embedded in the middle of a
paragraph.  

I've deleted comments that merely echoed the paragraph name, or
otherwise did not add any useful information.  I've corrected
egregious typing, spelling, and syntax errors in comments.  If the
business rules have changed, I change the comments to match the
current business rules, and sometimes to explain the reason for, or
history of, business rule changes.  When I am feeling particularly
fiendish, I expand the initials of previous maintenance programmers to
restore their full name in the comments.  It's my belief that everyone
should sign their work.

One of my previous bosses absolutely detested leaving "commented-out"
code in a production program.  His philosophy was, if you need to know
what it used to do, then go dig it out of the version control system. 
Our style-checker program (which I wrote) flags commented-out code in
the Data Division and the Procedure Division.  It also flags
non-referenced data names and procedure names.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-19T12:53:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fLZda.715$U96.217509@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca> <74ca5032.0303171928.38d98b3e@posting.google.com> <16e2f010.0303180754.5fa5e607@posting.google.com> <3e7735ed_1@text-west.newsgroups.com> <3E78043A.2D7DF21@worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Arnold Trembley" <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:3E78043A.2D7DF21@worldnet.att.net...
>
> One of my previous bosses absolutely detested leaving "commented-out"
> code in a production program.  His philosophy was, if you need to know
> what it used to do, then go dig it out of the version control system.

I am one of the few programmer-types I know who can hit the 'delete' key
without a single guilt pang - version control or no version control.

I accept that many are uncomfortable with this, but I will guarantee you I
am uncomfortable with...

==========
BEFORE
=========
   IF  WS-FIELD-ONE = 'XYZ'
         MOVE  FR-VALUE-1 TO    DEST-FIELD-1
         MOVE  GT-VALUE-1 TO    DEST-FIELD-2
  ELSE
         MOVE 1     TO WS-FLAG.
=========
AFTER
=========
   IF  WS-FIELD-ONE = 'XYZ'
         MOVE  FR-VALUE-1 TO    DEST-FIELD-1.
         MOVE  GT-VALUE-1 TO    DEST-FIELD-2
* ELSE
         MOVE 1   TO WS-FLAG .

(Please note the full stop added in line 2 of 'after')

Also much worse if above are nested several levels deep and the "*" in
column seven is fifteen or or so columns distant from the COBOL verbs....


If you're going to write like this, better to just delete the lines no
longer executed. (IMNSHO).


MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-20T01:31:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e78ffcb.30060115@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca> <74ca5032.0303171928.38d98b3e@posting.google.com> <16e2f010.0303180754.5fa5e607@posting.google.com> <3e7735ed_1@text-west.newsgroups.com> <3E78043A.2D7DF21@worldnet.att.net> <fLZda.715$U96.217509@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:

>"Arnold Trembley" <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
>news:3E78043A.2D7DF21@worldnet.att.net...
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>column seven is fifteen or or so columns distant from the COBOL verbs....

They do it because mainframe shops have a Programming Standard which says never
delete a line, comment it out. These standards were written before we had CM
(version control) software. They should have expired when we got CM, but they
didn't. 

Here's what happens in practice. When a section of code, usually a big
conditional,  becomes impossible to read, someone rewrites it as a new
paragraph, then completely comments out the old paragraph. The old paragraph is
useless because you can't tell which changes replaced others, but the
programmers haven't violated the 'commandment' about not deleting code. 

I comment out code when I think I might need to recover it, and when it serves
as a bad example of 'pitfalls to beware', with a comment explaining why. If it's
just dead code, I delete it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-19T23:02:32-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g2bea.1875$DA4.279475@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca> <74ca5032.0303171928.38d98b3e@posting.google.com> <16e2f010.0303180754.5fa5e607@posting.google.com> <3e7735ed_1@text-west.newsgroups.com> <3E78043A.2D7DF21@worldnet.att.net> <fLZda.715$U96.217509@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <3e78ffcb.30060115@news.optonline.net>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>
> I comment out code when I think I might need to recover it, and when it
serves
> as a bad example of 'pitfalls to beware', with a comment explaining why.
If it's
> just dead code, I delete it.

Again with the proviso that I maintain mostly my own code, I will often
comment it out until the next time I open that particular can of worms.
Generally, if it has been in production for a few months, next time I start
by taking out all the old shit, and cleaning it up to "current state".

The reasoning is that if the fix causes new problems, I want to see where I
came from.

Donald
```

```
It's the 'clever' line-by-line deletion/modification stuff which gets me

and a period.")

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A Matter of Style

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-19T03:29:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e77d3e3.15930197@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3E62E83C.6060507@Sympatico.ca> <3E62ED53.6050004@Sympatico.ca> <74ca5032.0303171928.38d98b3e@posting.google.com> <16e2f010.0303180754.5fa5e607@posting.google.com>`

```
psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote:

>As for comments, well, they are usually worse than useless, sometimes
>very helpful, and often out of date.  I cannot count the times I have
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>with a nonstandard restatements of the Cobol.

I haven't seen here the very real issue of screen real estate. If you can see a
section of code on a screen or two, you can understand it. Commenting techniques
like the above reduce the amount of useful information on your screen. In other
languages (C), putting punctuation({, })  on a line by itself does the same.

We need to see what a section of code is doing. Editing constructions which work
against that are counter-productive.
```

```
Hi:

As most people might have noticed, Mr. Gates is the richest person
on the planet again this year.

I wouldn't mind Mr. Gates making all that money if his company
wrote software which was marginally acceptable.  But, when you make
that much money selling horrid, unimaginative, unintuitive,
bug-riddled
junk that has taken them years to develop, well!

Has anyone considered the cost to the world's economy of the poor
quality of Microsoftware?

Perhaps someone with a calculator could calculate how much Mr. Gates
might have if he had to pay a dime for every minute wasted in the
past 20 or so years by every human being (not just us) who ever used
their software when:

a. Waiting for your PC to boot.
b. Sitting there wondering if your machine has crashed before you
reboot.
c. Trying to figure out why your program works just fine on one
machine
   but not on another.
d. Re-installing Windows because you don't know what else to do.
c. Trying to make everything that used to work work again after
installing
   the latest 'improved' version of something.
d. Downloading and installing the latest bug-fixes.
e. Waiting for your machine to shut down and reboot numerous times
   when installing software.
f. Looking for something in the 'Help' and not finding it.
g. Doing something according to the 'Help' which doesn't work.
h. Trying to make your Plug 'n' Play gadget Plug 'n' Play.
i. Re-doing what you just did because the stupid thing crashed.
j. Doing 'End Task' nineteen times before it actually ends.

I could go on and I am sure there are tons of other things to add to
the list.

So, how much would Bill be worth if he had to hand out all those
dimes?

(I recently bought XP but am too scared to install it!)

Thanks

Tony Dilworth
```

```
1. Virtually all the problems you list go away with XP (or are drastically
reduced).
2. You should review your hardware list against
requirements/compatibilities. Most of the problems reported on the XP
newsgroup have to do with a)old hardware, b)high-performance games, c)crappy

without an un-scheduled re-boot.

rather than upgrade.

dimes from the programmers responsible, don't look in the mirror.

dividend since I bought.
```

```
He wrote his last commercial line of code 20 years ago, for Windows 1.0.
```

#### ↳ Re: OT Microsoft

- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0303031144.2a0cee55@posting.google.com>`

```
In message <93dfc7c7.0303031144.2a0cee55@posting.google.com>, Anthony 
Dilworth <foodman123@aol.com> writes
>I wouldn't mind Mr. Gates making all that money if his company
>wrote software which was marginally acceptable.  But, when you make
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>quality of Microsoftware?

Yes, but what about all the other shite that was/is written and is not 
Microsoft spawned. Do you really think that they are worse than anyone 
else? Do you think that if they spent more time testing that the 
software would really be any better? It would certainly cost more.

>
>Perhaps someone with a calculator could calculate how much Mr. Gates
>might have if he had to pay a dime for every minute wasted in the
>past 20 or so years by every human being (not just us) who ever used
>their software when:

Perhaps you would prefer to use a quill pen and ledger? How much time is 
Microsoft software saving you? How long would it take you to write, 
address and post the 100,000+ copies to reach each reader of this group? 
How much would it cost you?

>
>a. Waiting for your PC to boot.

Perhaps you prefer 640k memory and command line interpreters? At least 
you knew where you stood in those days (in the mire).

>b. Sitting there wondering if your machine has crashed before you
>reboot.

Live life on the edge, reboot more frequently.

>c. Trying to figure out why your program works just fine on one
>machine
>   but not on another.

Same as my company's IBM S/390 box. What's new?

>d. Re-installing Windows because you don't know what else to do.

Never had to do that, W95, W98 or XP. I have had to re-install windows 
because some a**e-wit screwed it up, but not because of the software.

>c. Trying to make everything that used to work work again after
>installing
>   the latest 'improved' version of something.

Keep a list of required actions to restore your machine or get one of 
those packages that does it all for you.

>d. Downloading and installing the latest bug-fixes.

What software doesn't have bug fixes?

>e. Waiting for your machine to shut down and reboot numerous times
>   when installing software.

Could be better, but if you got it right first time then it wouldn't be 
a problem, would it?

>f. Looking for something in the 'Help' and not finding it.

Read the F****** Manual. Whoops, they don't provide those any more. Try 
reading the Readme.txt files.

>g. Doing something according to the 'Help' which doesn't work.

Since when? Whenever I have found disparities between help and result it 
has always been my fault, not the software.

>h. Trying to make your Plug 'n' Play gadget Plug 'n' Play.

Let's go back to DOS and see how well you get on there.

>i. Re-doing what you just did because the stupid thing crashed.

Hardware, not Bill Gates at fault.

>j. Doing 'End Task' nineteen times before it actually ends.
>

Don't be so impatient. It does work. But if you were prepared to pay the 
price for real software then you wouldn't have to re-install, re-boot, 
plug-n-pray, read inadequate help text,etc. But then you wouldn't have a 
machine capable of half that which yours can do today.

>I could go on and I am sure there are tons of other things to add to
>the list.

I could go on too. But I'd only get bored or choke to death in an 
apoplectic fit..

>
>So, how much would Bill be worth if he had to hand out all those
>dimes?

How much would you be worth if you had bought the original os? How much 
could you be worth if you were to get up and write the definitive, 
working, usable, reliable and CHEAP operating system. Have you 
considered what it would take? Stop bleating and get to it!

>
>(I recently bought XP but am too scared to install it!)

Coward.

>
>Thanks
>
>Tony Dilworth
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT Microsoft

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T14:00:25+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b40tvo$qdo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0303031144.2a0cee55@posting.google.com> <aOI1xKBmZ+Y+EwDX@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
Alistair Maclean wrote:

> Perhaps you would prefer to use a quill pen and ledger? How much time is
> Microsoft software saving you? How long would it take you to write,
> address and post the 100,000+ copies to reach each reader of this group?
> How much would it cost you?

Excuse me: Microsoft vs. Quill pen and ledger ?

Microsoft did _not_ invent the computer, nor word processing, nor the 
internet, nor EMail.  In fact _none_ of those.

Microsoft didn't invent BASIC, or Personal Computers (the Apple II was so 
called), MS-DOS (MS-DOS 1 was essentially a clone of DRI's CP/M for 8086), 
or networking, or well, just about nothing actually.

The first edition of Bill Gates "The Way Ahead" in 1994 (?) made 
_no_mention_ of the internet at all, even though many of us were using it 
at the time (with OS/2 and Unix).  Windows 95 initially had no Internet 
connection. These were 'corrected' in later editions, and ever since MS has 
tried to reinvent history.

If Microsoft had never existed computers wouldn't be much different from 
what they are now, they may be running different software, but not 
significantly different: RISCOS, BeOS, Amiga, X-GEM, CDE were all developed 
independently with no refernce to MS products.  Even OS/2 was not developed 
from MS products, quite the reverse actually:  Windows 386 (which developed 
into Windows 3.0) used IBM code to utilise 'enhanced' 386 mode and OS/2 NT 
became Windows NT.

> Perhaps you prefer 640k memory 

Microsoft did not invent extended memory or the 386, but it did write 
MS-DOS so that it was _impossible_ to make it run in procted mode - 
Microsoft is the _cause_ of limiting MS-DOS to single tasking and real mode 
(limiting to 640Kb).

It was DesqView and DR-DOS 5 (nearly 2 years befor MS-DOS 5) that brought 
in memory management and task switching.  MultiTasking and Multiuser on PCs 
was available before MS-DOS could even use hard drives.

> and command line interpreters? At least
> you knew where you stood in those days (in the mire).

Microsoft did not invent GUIs, there were several that existed well before 
Windows 1 was released: X, Star, Lisa, Macintosh, GEM, Perq, 

Several more arrived before MS was able to make a 'good' one (Win 3.1). 

What _is_ significant is that 'command lines' _can_ be nice and usable, but 
MS seemed to go out of its way to make theirs horrible to use deliberately 
so that users would willingly forgo it.  For example there is 'DOSKEY' that 
provides command line editing but MS hides it away and makes it difficult 
to even find it (it is still not nice).

But MS has at last seen the light and has a project that will make all 
their tools available from command lines so that server and networking 
tasks can, at last, be automated instead of having to point and click every 
time on each machine.

> Live life on the edge, reboot more frequently.

My systems (except Windows) only get rebooted for power failures.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Microsoft

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-03T22:15:03-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rh6dnRB-RqkquvmjXTWcjA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0303031144.2a0cee55@posting.google.com> <aOI1xKBmZ+Y+EwDX@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <b40tvo$qdo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:b40tvo$qdo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz...
> Alistair Maclean wrote:
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> connection. These were 'corrected' in later editions, and ever since MS
has
> tried to reinvent history.
>
> If Microsoft had never existed computers wouldn't be much different from
> what they are now, they may be running different software, but not
> significantly different: RISCOS, BeOS, Amiga, X-GEM, CDE were all
developed
> independently with no refernce to MS products.  Even OS/2 was not
developed
> from MS products, quite the reverse actually:  Windows 386 (which
developed
> into Windows 3.0) used IBM code to utilise 'enhanced' 386 mode and OS/2 NT
> became Windows NT.

Phooey!

Rockefeller didn't invent oil. Rockefeller didn't invent kerosene. What
Rockefeller did was lower the price of kerosene from $3.00/gallon to less
than fifty cents (in 1900 money). In three years. His effort eliminated the
whale-oil industry and brought cheap lighting to the world.

A few years later, he did a similar thing with gasoline.

Had it not been for what he did, we'd be driving horse-drawn carriages in
the dark.

Complain that Gates didn't invent anything novel. Postulate that had he not
been around, we'd have something equivalent (prove it!). The fact remains
there are as many desktop computers in America as there are people and 90%+
of these computers run a Micros~1 operating system.

You can't say that about toasters or toothpaste or TV sets. And Micros~1
became ubiquitous solely on its own merit, by persuading willing buyers to
part with cash for something of value. Even in the silly legal machinations
of recent note, no one ever showed as single customer was harmed.

No, Micros~1 (and Gates) got where they are today the old fashioned way.

But, as I said earlier, I've more than doubled my money in Micros~1 stock,
so I'm a happy computor for several reasons, one of which is that I've put
away my green eye-shades and sleeve garters.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Microsoft

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T18:31:01+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b41h2d$2uo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0303031144.2a0cee55@posting.google.com> <aOI1xKBmZ+Y+EwDX@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <b40tvo$qdo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <rh6dnRB-RqkquvmjXTWcjA@giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:

> Had it not been for what he did, we'd be driving horse-drawn carriages in
> the dark.

To quote yourself:  prove it!   

> Complain that Gates didn't invent anything novel. 

I wasn't 'complaining', I was reporting.

> Postulate that had he
> not been around, we'd have something equivalent (prove it!). 

Most of the Microsoft products were not written in-house but were 'bought 
in', either by buying the product, or by buying the company that produced 
it. In other cases the MS developed product was based on other similar 
products.

If MS had not been around then the originators of the current MS products 
may still be selling them instead of them being MS branded.

For example:    

  MS Xenix       - a version of Unix from AT&T
  MS-DOS         - written by SCP, based on CP/M
  SQL Server     - Sybase
  IE             - written by Spyglass
  FrontPage      - FrontPage was a company
  MSC            - Lattice C                
  SMB networking - licenced from IBM
  NT Advanced Server - originated in IBMs LAN Manager
  TCP/IP         - taken from BSD

In other cases MS drove competitors out of the market.  If MS didn't exist 
we may still be using Word Perfect 10, Quattro 8.

> The fact
> remains there are as many desktop computers in America as there are people
> and 90%+ of these computers run a Micros~1 operating system.

And if MS didn't exist there may be just as many but running a variety of 
systems that are just as capable, or even more so.

> You can't say that about toasters or toothpaste or TV sets. 

Which is simply not a problem at all.  All brand of toasters take the same 
bread and produce the same colour and taste.  All brands of TV get the same 
channels and can plug in any VCR.  

Imagine if MS produced TV, you would get the MSN channels in full colour 
and sound.  Other channels would be received black and white only or 
without sound, and the adverts would be replaced by 'approved' ones from a 
MSN channel.

If it wasn't a MS branded VCR then the next TV upgrade would mean that it 
wouldn't work anymore and you need to buy a new MS one.

It is exactly because there is competition that they all TVs work with each 
other so well. It is only MS that stops other systems working with them.

> And Micros~1 became ubiquitous solely on its own merit, by persuading 
> willing buyers to
> part with cash for something of value. 

No. By persuading the OEM machine vendors that they should pay MS whether 
they shipped a machine with MS product or not (per box pricing) or that 
only MS OSes should be shipped otherwise the discount will be lost.

At the retail level this made MS based machine the only game in town.

But of course there aren't 250 million computers in use.  There may have 
been that many sold, but MS software makes recent computers obsolete, only 
the latest will run current MS products.

I have 10 year old machines doing useful work running Linux, anything older 
than last year (or maybe two) won't load XP.  MS has announced that 
Longhorn won't run any current applications and it also probaly won't run 
on anything less than 2 MHz with a GByte of RAM.

90% of your 'one per person' (OK probably 75%) have been junked or put in 
the closet because they can't run current MS software.  

If MS hadn't been around then many of those junked machines would still be 
in use saving the buyers the cost of replacement machines.

> Even in the silly legal machinations of recent note, 

The 'silly' machinations proved that MS did illegal practices on a massive 
scale. What was entirely silly was that no effective penalty was ever made.

> no one ever showed as single customer was harmed.

Then where did the 42 billion come from ?

Now that Linux is indeed proving to be a competitor for MS products MS is 
talking about reducing prices.

Over the last few years MS managed to eliminate all competition in a very 
effective way and thus could maintain prices that were well above what 
could be maintained in a competitive environment.

This is proven by MS having to reduce prices now that there actually is 
competition again.

Now it may be that the 42 billion only represents a 'few dollars' harm for 
each customer.

> No, Micros~1 (and Gates) got where they are today the old fashioned way.

Exactly, greed, litigation, predatory practices, all the 'old fashioned' 
ways that they passed laws to try to prevent in a monopoly.
 
> But, as I said earlier, I've more than doubled my money in Micros~1 stock,
> so I'm a happy computor for several reasons, one of which is that I've put
> away my green eye-shades and sleeve garters.

Which had happened well before Microsoft was any more than just another 
computer software company.  Most of the software that I have used was not 
MS, and did not need MS.  

If MS never existed or ceased to exist today, the world would still be the 
same, just the brand names would be different.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Microsoft

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T12:51:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e64942f.132121263@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0303031144.2a0cee55@posting.google.com> <aOI1xKBmZ+Y+EwDX@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <b40tvo$qdo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Microsoft didn't invent BASIC, or Personal Computers (the Apple II was so 
>called), MS-DOS (MS-DOS 1 was essentially a clone of DRI's CP/M for 8086), 
>or networking, or well, just about nothing actually.

MS began writing MS-DOS 2 within a week of getting the IBM contract. MS-DOS 3
was also 100% Microsoft code. 

>The first edition of Bill Gates "The Way Ahead" in 1994 (?) made 
>_no_mention_ of the internet at all, even though many of us were using it 
>at the time (with OS/2 and Unix). 

Using hard-to-configure communications software and an overly complex winsock.
None of that stuff was simple enough for the masses to use. 

> Even OS/2 was not developed from MS products, 

That's true. It was developed from MVS. Examining OS/2 internals makes this
apparent. 

>Microsoft did not invent extended memory or the 386, but it did write 
>MS-DOS so that it was _impossible_ to make it run in protected mode - 

Because MS had decided Windows would be their 32-bit OS, stopped developing DOS,
turned it over to IBM.

>Microsoft is the _cause_ of limiting MS-DOS to single tasking and real mode 
>(limiting to 640Kb).

Sixteen bit addresses limited it to 1M and the IBM BIOS further reduced that to
640K.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Microsoft

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T10:55:45-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0303041055.3bfc9844@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0303031144.2a0cee55@posting.google.com> <aOI1xKBmZ+Y+EwDX@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <b40tvo$qdo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e64942f.132121263@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> MS began writing MS-DOS 2 within a week of getting the IBM contract. MS-DOS 3
> was also 100% Microsoft code. 

Not true.  It began writing PC-DOS 1 based on SCP's 86-DOS (QDOS)
'within a week', actually most was done by Tim Paterson of SCP.

Various versions of MS/PC-DOS contained IBM written code.  For example
IBM rewrote much of PC-DOS 1.0 to result in PC-DOS 1.1 after DRI
threated to sue MS, IBM and SCP over copyright issues and IBM accepted
that it was proven. Other settlements included IBM selling CP/M-86 and
that DRI could freely write any clone of PC-DOS features, which is why
they were never sued over DR-DOS.
 
> Using hard-to-configure communications software and an overly complex winsock.
> None of that stuff was simple enough for the masses to use. 

Actually many ISP put out easy install disks, such as AOL and IBM. 
Stick the disk in and answer a few questions, how hard is that ?
 
> >Microsoft is the _cause_ of limiting MS-DOS to single tasking and real mode 
> >(limiting to 640Kb).
> 
> Sixteen bit addresses limited it to 1M and the IBM BIOS further reduced that to
> 640K.

Actually MS did try to make a multi-tasking version of DOS that should
have been able to use 286 protected mode.  It derived from 3.1 and was
version 4.0 and later 4.1 (not to be confused with the much later
4.01).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Microsoft

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T08:14:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e65aca2.203926654@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0303031144.2a0cee55@posting.google.com> <aOI1xKBmZ+Y+EwDX@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <b40tvo$qdo$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e64942f.132121263@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303041055.3bfc9844@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

>Actually MS did try to make a multi-tasking version of DOS that should
>have been able to use 286 protected mode. 

It thunked the machine into and out of protected mode by general reset via the
keyboard contoller. What an inelegant design. It wasn't Microsoft's fault, it
was Intel's. 

I wrote a GEM-like OS in 1990-92. It was OO at its core, having no file system,
just collection classes. A directory was a collection of files and other
directories. A file was a collection of records. A record was a collection of
objects.  I thought the design was beautiful. It would have been called New
Dawn.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Microsoft

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T07:39:06+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b45gct$pag$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0303031144.2a0cee55@posting.google.com> <3e64942f.132121263@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303041055.3bfc9844@posting.google.com> <3e65aca2.203926654@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> 
>>Actually MS did try to make a multi-tasking version of DOS that should
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> fault, it was Intel's.

No.  While it was possible to change between real and protected mode using 
a trick, the reason that one _needed_ to swap back to real mode was because 
of the way that Microsoft (ab)used memory segment registers.  Not just in 
DOS itself but in the language libraries and its own programs.

Many OSes and systems, such as Xenix, went from 8086 to 80286 protected 
mode with few problems.  MS had a flawed implementation that could not be 
mapped to the 286 selectors.

OS/2 1.x used 286 protected mode and could run real mode programs in the 
DOS box.  Many programs could be recompiled for protected mode, or even 
'family mode', such as Microfocus cobol 3.x system which ran in protected 
mode on OS/2 or as a DOS program.

Microsoft software was too problematical.

> I thought the design was beautiful. It would have been called New Dawn.

Well you ould do.  It didn't seem to impress anyone with two clues though.
```

```
>Robert Wagner wrote:
>>>Actually MS did try to make a multi-tasking version of DOS that should
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>a trick,

The trick was using the keyboard controller to reset the machine. 

>Many OSes and systems, such as Xenix, went from 8086 to 80286 protected 
>mode with few problems.  MS had a flawed implementation that could not be 
>mapped to the 286 selectors.

Win 3.x did the same. 

The 286 was fatally flawed in design. The 386 got it right.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT Microsoft

- **From:** foodman123@aol.com (Anthony Dilworth)
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T05:16:53-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93dfc7c7.0303040516.58a057c7@posting.google.com>`

```
> Yes, but what about all the other shite that was/is written and is not 
> Microsoft spawned. Do you really think that they are worse than anyone 
> else? Do you think that if they spent more time testing that the 
> software would really be any better? It would certainly cost more.
> 
    No, I don't think that spending more time testing it would be
    be better. I think that more time spent designing something better
    would make their testing easier and their products better.

> 
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
       As I have discussed in prior topics, the use of a command line
       interpreter and the use of the keyboard is superior to combined
       use of mouse and keyboard. I can compile, compile and link,
       compile, link and execute with ONE keystroke (plus CR).  I suppose that
       you consider that inefficient and archaic? I guess you enjoy using
       stuff like the laughably mis-named Fujitsu Programming Staff?

> >b. Sitting there wondering if your machine has crashed before you
> >reboot.
> 
> Live life on the edge, reboot more frequently.

      Why should I EVER have to reboot. May I infer that you spend your
      time rebooting often as a matter of course instead of doing 
      something productive?

> >c. Trying to figure out why your program works just fine on one
> >machine
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

       Just because you have problems on other platforms does not
       make it alright. Your What's New attitude suggests that you
       accept crap without complaint.


> >d. Re-installing Windows because you don't know what else to do.
> 
> Never had to do that, W95, W98 or XP. I have had to re-install windows 
> because some a**e-wit screwed it up, but not because of the software.
> 

     It should not be possible for some a**e-wit to screw it up.
     If some a**e-wit screwed it up it is because the software allowed
     itself to be screwed up.  I think that Microsoft is currently
     talking about self-healing software. Surely, self-healing software
     can only be required by software which is self-injuring.


> >c. Trying to make everything that used to work work again after
> >installing
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

     Why should I have to keep a list of required actions to restore the
     machine?  Why should it need to be restored?  Why should I buy extra
     software to support it? 

> >d. Downloading and installing the latest bug-fixes.
> 
> What software doesn't have bug fixes?
> 
     Very few, but not on the massive scale of Microsoft. And, we are
     not just talking about bugs. We are talking about poor design,
     and poor programming and appalling inefficiency and wasted time.

     
> >e. Waiting for your machine to shut down and reboot numerous times
> >   when installing software.
> 
> Could be better, but if you got it right first time then it wouldn't be 
> a problem, would it?

      In case you have not noticed, there are software systems,
      which, during normal and trouble-free installation, shut down and
      re-boot. And, sometimes more than once.  Also, after installing
      software why should I have to re-boot? The very fact that one has
      to often re-boot after installing something points to the poor
      basic design of Windows.

> >f. Looking for something in the 'Help' and not finding it.
> 
> Read the F****** Manual. Whoops, they don't provide those any more. Try 
> reading the Readme.txt files.

     All software should be self-documented and require NO external printed
     documention.  It is also the height of amateurism to provide readme
     files.

> 
> >g. Doing something according to the 'Help' which doesn't work.
> 
> Since when? Whenever I have found disparities between help and result it 
> has always been my fault, not the software.

     Your experience is different from mine.
> 
> >h. Trying to make your Plug 'n' Play gadget Plug 'n' Play.
> 
> Let's go back to DOS and see how well you get on there.
> 
       Another fatuous comment. Going back is not a solution. Writing
       good, dependable, self-documented software is.


> >i. Re-doing what you just did because the stupid thing crashed.
> 
> Hardware, not Bill Gates at fault.
> 
      Oh, Really???

> >j. Doing 'End Task' nineteen times before it actually ends.
> >
> 
  
> Don't be so impatient. It does work. But if you were prepared to pay the 
> price for real software . . .

      Can you suggest a source for such software?
      Why should I have patience with amateurish junk?

> 
> >I could go on and I am sure there are tons of other things to add to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
      Do go on then and I will hope for the latter<g>.

Thanks 

Tony Dilworth
```

#### ↳ Re: OT Microsoft

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T01:03:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e63fabd.92832361@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0303031144.2a0cee55@posting.google.com>`

```
foodman123@aol.com (Anthony Dilworth) wrote:

>I wouldn't mind Mr. Gates making all that money if his company
>wrote software which was marginally acceptable.  But, when you make
>that much money selling horrid, unimaginative, unintuitive,
>bug-riddled
>junk that has taken them years to develop, well!

You would probably prefer using beautiful, creative, intuitive and stable
software produced by IBM during the Good Old Days ('60s through mid-'80s). You'd
particularly enjoy programming IMS, an antidiluvian database where each call
required 10-20 highly cryptic parameters and produced numeric return codes that
NObody could figure out. <polishing fingernails on lapel> Help screens? Hah, we
laugh at help screens. RTFM! Only a wimp would return an error message in
English. Where's the challenge in that? If it was easy, anybody could do it. You
should hire us over-paid professionals to get the job done right. 

As to Mr. Gates' so-called GUI, we've adopted a wait-and-see attitude. It might
catch on or it might not. Character mode 25x80 screens have powered the world's
largest companies for decades. There's no arguing with success. As an
alternative to GUI, we've developed 43x80. You cannot believe the delighted look
on users' faces when they see screens containing nearly twice as much
information! It is fully supported by 3270 protocols, allowing you to leverage
your investment in $2,000 dumb terminals to new heights of productivity. 

Now Mr. Gates wants to upset our traditions with something called OOPS. It
should be obvious for the name alone that it's a mistake. Gates didn't invent
it, but he made it available to the masses. The 'enfant terrible'  even browbeat
his minions into rewriting every blasted Microsoft application -- Word, IE,
Excel, et al. -- into OOPS things called COM or OLE or .NET or ??  We don't
understand it, but it sounds rotten to us. Why can't we just write 'PERFORM
2000-ACCUM-TOTALS THRU 2999-ACCUMS-TOTALS-EXIT. MOVE ZERO TO WS-TOTALS.' like we
always have?
```

- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0303031144.2a0cee55@posting.google.com> <3e63fabd.92832361@news.optonline.net> <R7ycnciCzLTDhPmjXTWc2Q@comcast.com> <01c2e1fb$d88c27e0$a593f243@chottel> <3e649fbf.135081439@news.optonline.net> <sdednTfRIbviVvmjXTWciA@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>split, would be worth about $48 dollars today (minus inflation).

There have been nine splits:

  Sept. 18, 1987  2 for 1 
  April 12, 1990  2 for 1 
  June 26, 1991 3 for 2     
  June 12, 1992  3 for 2    
  May 20, 1994   2 for 1 
  Dec. 6, 1996  2 for 1 
  Feb. 20, 1998  2 for 1 
  March 26, 1999 2 for 1 
  Feb. 14, 2003  2 for 1 

One original share is now 288 * $23 (recent price) = $6624 / 24 = 276 x 
In 1999, one share had become 144 * $92  (3/29/99) = $13248 / 24 = 552 x

 $1,000 would have become $552,000, is now $276,000 (plus dividends). I was off
on both numbers, must have remembered $160K from earlier.
```

- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0303031144.2a0cee55@posting.google.com> <3e63fabd.92832361@news.optonline.net> <R7ycnciCzLTDhPmjXTWc2Q@comcast.com> <01c2e1fb$d88c27e0$a593f243@chottel> <3e649fbf.135081439@news.optonline.net> <b42j64$gtu$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:b42j64$gtu$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  4-Mar-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
>
> > If you had bought $1K of MSFT at the IPO price (1986?), it would have
been
> > worth
> > $160K in 1999. Today it would be worth about $90K.
> >
> > The only stock with more appreciation was Keane, which would have grown
to
> > $250K. It is now well under half that. I used to work for Keane.
>
> If you pick and choose starting and ending points, there are several
stocks that
> have out-performed Microsoft.
>
> But if I had bought MicroSoft back then, I would have sold it when I
bought my
> last house.  (I sold my EDS stock).   Microsoft never paid dividends until
now,
> and stock that doesn't pay dividends is only worth money when we sell it.

Here's why (I think): Dividends are taxed as ordinary income. Big
stockholders would rather see the stock price increase (taxed at capital
gains rates). To the extent the stock price can rise, by using the dividend
money to grow the company, big stockholders are better off with a stock
price increase.

Now if dividends were not taxed at all (the Bush plan), there would be
contrary pressure to keep the stock price down AND pay dividends.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<NDSdncYoXq2RnPujXTWcig@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0303031144.2a0cee55@posting.google.com> <3e63fabd.92832361@news.optonline.net> <R7ycnciCzLTDhPmjXTWc2Q@comcast.com> <01c2e1fb$d88c27e0$a593f243@chottel> <3e649fbf.135081439@news.optonline.net> <b42j64$gtu$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <XymdnfR6zOg_vPijXTWcig@giganews.com> <3e65a8e7.202971710@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e65a8e7.202971710@news.optonline.net...
> "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:
>
> >Here's why (I think): Dividends are taxed as ordinary income. Big
> >stockholders would rather see the stock price increase (taxed at capital
> >gains rates). To the extent the stock price can rise, by using the
dividend
> >money to grow the company, big stockholders are better off with a stock
> >price increase.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> market practices. But it didn't deter IBM, who paid lots of dividends.

A lot of people think monopolies are bad - mostly because (they think)
monopolies raise prices. In the classic cases (Standard Oil, Microsoft),
prices drop. In Standard Oil's case prices dropped to gain more consumers.
In IBM and Microsoft's cases, they are their own competitor.

Once everbody had a copy of Win95, Microsoft's revenue stream would stop -
unless MS itself comes out with a better product for less money. If MS
doesn't offer something new and improved, they're out of business!

IBM continued developing newer and faster computers even while they were a
monopoly.

No, in most cases, consumers benefit from monopolies. This is, obviously,
not the case for competitors.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Microsoft

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T08:05:35+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b45hui$q0m$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0303031144.2a0cee55@posting.google.com> <XymdnfR6zOg_vPijXTWcig@giganews.com> <3e65a8e7.202971710@news.optonline.net> <NDSdncYoXq2RnPujXTWcig@giganews.com>`

```
JerryMouse wrote:

> A lot of people think monopolies are bad - mostly because (they think)
> monopolies raise prices. In the classic cases (Standard Oil, Microsoft),
> prices drop. In Standard Oil's case prices dropped to gain more consumers.
> In IBM and Microsoft's cases, they are their own competitor.

In MS case the prices haven't dropped.  In fact XP is more expensive than 
2000 or 98.

But not only are the numbers bigger, but the cost of computer hardware has 
dropped enormously while the price for MS component has remained the same.  
This makes MS takes a much larger percentage of the total cost.

When computers were $2000 the MS software was just 10 - 20 % (Windows plus 
Works or Office).

Now computers can be down to $200 for a bare machine, one of these with 
Windows and Office may be $500, 50% - 60% to MS.

In fact now that there is a viable competitor MS _are_ talking about 
reducing prices.

> Once everbody had a copy of Win95, Microsoft's revenue stream would stop -
> unless MS itself comes out with a better product for less money. 

'98 wasn't less money, 2000 wasn't less money, XP isn't less money.

> If MS
> doesn't offer something new and improved, they're out of business!

No wrong.  By ensuring that OEM _only_ build machines with MS software 
under penalty of fines* or other sanctions, then every new machine has 
Windows installed and revenue to MS.

By selling businesses on the 'advantage' of a corporate maintenence scheme 
they also ensure that when a new computer has been bought the existing 
Windows licence that they paid for is discarded and a new corporate licence 
is used thus paying twice.
 
> No, in most cases, consumers benefit from monopolies. This is, obviously,
> not the case for competitors.

No.  That is like saying that citizens benefit from dictatorship.  In 
Russia their leaders were mostly seen as benefactors.  This was supported 
by propaganda which the poulation believed.  Now with Microsoft ...


*'fines': MS offers a 'special retrospective discount' on all products if 
the OEM does not offer products from any competitor, or for example Linux.  
This may be, say $5 or so dollars per system.  If they have sold 100,000 
machines this is worth $500,000 or more.  If they offer a machine with a 
competitive product they lose that accumulated discount and have to pay the 
half million to MS.
```

```
Hugh Candlin wrote:

> To present a more unbiased argument, you must also criticize
> video card manufacturers, and case manufacturers,
> both guilty of jacking-up prices at a consistent  rate.
> $450 for a video card is predatory.

If you don't want to buy a Rolls Royce then just buy a Ford.

In fact I can buy a new video card for considerably less than I could 10 
years ago, about $US 10 now against 2 or 3 times that for a basic VGA card. 
20 years ago a CGA card cost $US 300, high end graphics cards ran to over a 
thousand dollars.

I just dug out a 1996 catalogue that has a 'Diamond Edge 3D 4Mb' at $NZ 
799.00, while currently a GeForce 4 128Mb (8xAGP, TV in/out) is $NZ 315.

From PC Magazine 1993:
   ATI Ultra Pro 2Mb (no 2D or 3D)     $US 549
   
If you wanted one with TV in/out (as your $450 has) then:

   TrueVision Targa+ 64                $US 1699

Actually I get my 'video cards' for free as they are built into the M/B 
along with LAN, Modem, multi-IO and all the other stuff that used to extra 
cost add on cards, yet the M/B itself is much cheaper than previous years.
 
Cases ?  The cheapest case in 1996 was $NZ 79, today $NZ 46. Of course you 
were probably happy with a beige box 5 years ago but now want one of those 
see through with laser light and triple cooling.

If you compare in an 'unbiased' way you will compare between past and 
present low-end systems and between past and present high-end systems.

> The video card manufacturers are also guilty of heeding to
> an obscene upgrade schedule, piling on feature after feature
> while game developers plod along in their dust.

Then don't buy it.  Get one of the cards that is _much_ cheaper, or 'free' 
on the M/B. It is game players wanting to throw money at game writers and 
video cards that is encouraging them to compete in giving game players 
'bragging rights'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Microsoft

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T15:28:51-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HDidnc5ftNW48PujXTWckw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0303031144.2a0cee55@posting.google.com> <3e63fabd.92832361@news.optonline.net> <R7ycnciCzLTDhPmjXTWc2Q@comcast.com> <01c2e1fb$d88c27e0$a593f243@chottel> <3e649fbf.135081439@news.optonline.net> <b42j64$gtu$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <XymdnfR6zOg_vPijXTWcig@giganews.com> <b454il$lkc$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
>
> Even with taxes - I don't get any return from my investment unless I get
> dividends or unless I sell stock.

Sure you do. Here's how:

Suppose you come into $100k that you could use in your business netting a
20% return.

By just using the $100k, at the end of the year you have the original $100k
+ $20k profit. You'll pay income tax on the $20k.

Alternative:
Buy $100k worth of stock in which you (and others) have a great deal of
confidence. Borrow against the stock from your bank at, say, 6%. At the end
of a year, what have you got?

1. The original $100k
2. Increased value of the stock, say $10k
3. Any dividend, say $2k
4. Profit from the use of $100K, $20k
5. Minus interest, $6k
Result, $26k. But you'll only pay tax on $20k because the $6k interest is
deductible.

All in all, you're 30% better off (assuming everything goes OK).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Microsoft

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b45sdo$65t$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0303031144.2a0cee55@posting.google.com> <3e63fabd.92832361@news.optonline.net> <R7ycnciCzLTDhPmjXTWc2Q@comcast.com> <01c2e1fb$d88c27e0$a593f243@chottel> <3e649fbf.135081439@news.optonline.net> <b42j64$gtu$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <XymdnfR6zOg_vPijXTWcig@giganews.com> <b454il$lkc$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <HDidnc5ftNW48PujXTWckw@giganews.com>`

```

On  5-Mar-2003, "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:

> Alternative:
> Buy $100k worth of stock in which you (and others) have a great deal of
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> All in all, you're 30% better off (assuming everything goes OK).

Unless you get a dividend or sell the stock, you can't use 2) or 3).

So you have:

> 1. The original $100k
> 4. Profit from the use of $100K, $20k
> 5. Minus interest, $6k
> Result, $14k. But you'll only pay tax on $20k because the $6k interest is
> deductible.

How is this better than:  ???

> Suppose you come into $100k that you could use in your business netting a
> 20% return.
>
> By just using the $100k, at the end of the year you have the original $100k
> + $20k profit. You'll pay income tax on the $20k.


It is only better if the amount of cash you get from that stock is greater than
$100, for instance if you get a $10 dividend and/or you sell it for $110.  
Until such happens, it isn't doing you a bit of good.  (as people discovered
when their internet stocks values dropped when the bubble burst).

Of course, if you bought Microsoft when it was first available, you could be
selling off as much as you paid for now, and next year, and next year, and the
following year...   But it is the selling or the dividends that you can spend.
```

```
>Suppose you come into $100k that you could use in your business netting a
>20% return.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…

in flames. You make 26K vs. 20K because of that higher risk.
```

```
higher than the individual rate).
```
