##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT Microsoft

- **From:** foodman123@aol.com (Anthony Dilworth)
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T05:41:03-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<93dfc7c7.0303040541.2457ba49@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0303031144.2a0cee55@posting.google.com> <3e63fabd.92832361@news.optonline.net>`

```
> 
> You would probably prefer using beautiful, creative, intuitive and stable
> software produced by IBM during the Good Old Days 

     Actually, I began my career in 1960 and worked with IBM mainframes
     until the early 70's so I know about the IMS junk. I abandoned the 
     IBM world and pursued employment using something called BLIS COBOL which
     ran on DG minis and was vastly superior to anything IBM had at the
     time. Does anyone else remember BLIS (Business Language Integrated System)?
     When the PC came out, I switched to them.

Thanks 

Tony Dilworth
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT Microsoft

- **From:** "Don Leahy" <xdb2imsN@nospam.whatever.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T09:48:24-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_039a.2508$Or5.435297@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0303031144.2a0cee55@posting.google.com> <3e63fabd.92832361@news.optonline.net>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e63fabd.92832361@news.optonline.net...
>
> You would probably prefer using beautiful, creative, intuitive and stable
> software produced by IBM during the Good Old Days ('60s through mid-'80s).
You'd
> particularly enjoy programming IMS, an antidiluvian database where each
call
> required 10-20 highly cryptic parameters and produced numeric return codes
that
> NObody could figure out.
>
I know you were being humourous, but for the benefit of the non-mainframers
in the group,  I'd like to point out that the average IMS call requires only
about 4 parameters, not 10-20, and the return codes are alphanumeric, not
numeric.  And they're not cryptic at all.  Take 'GE' for example.
*Obviously* that means 'segment not found', right?  <g>  Just like SQLCODE
+100 obviously means 'row not found'.

It may be an old product, but it's far from obsolete.  New releases come out
every couple of years, and many banks and insurance companies run their
businesses on it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Microsoft

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T08:14:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e65acda.203982865@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0303031144.2a0cee55@posting.google.com> <3e63fabd.92832361@news.optonline.net> <_039a.2508$Or5.435297@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
"Don Leahy" <xdb2imsN@nospam.whatever.net> wrote:

>[in re IMS]

>It may be an old product, but it's far from obsolete.  New releases come out
>every couple of years, and many banks and insurance companies run their
>businesses on it.

In the Good Old Days, when machines were anemic, they chose it for speed. It was
faster than DB2 aka 'the MIPS sink'. A lot of IMS access is written in assembly
language. Now that machines are fast, there is no rationale for using it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ IMS (was: OT Microsoft

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T11:57:26-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b45dqc$74l$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0303031144.2a0cee55@posting.google.com> <3e63fabd.92832361@news.optonline.net> <_039a.2508$Or5.435297@news20.bellglobal.com> <3e65acda.203982865@news.optonline.net>`

```
Ignoring (for the moment) that there are two sides of IMS (DB and DC - using
their older designations), what you are saying is that there is no rationale
for using an EXISTING "hierarchical" database when you COULD convert your
entire business logic to a relational database.

I would LOVE to see that logic presented to any serious cost-aware
management <G>

P.S.  Have you actually seen any studies comparing performance of IMS
Fastpath with DB2 on the same hardware/software configuration?

Again, if I were creating a NEW application in a business with no existing
"database" - I would probably use a relational database rather than an
hierarchical one - but how often does that happen?
```

```
When IDMS added the ability to have multiple parents to its database, they
wanted to distance themselves from IMS by changing its description from
"hierarchical" to "network".    But if I say I use a "network database", nobody
knows what I mean.

So I use the term people understand, and put quotes around it if I don't care
for the term.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ IMS vs DB2 was Re: OT Microsoft

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-18T11:39:17-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E773DA5.753B0433@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0303031144.2a0cee55@posting.google.com> <3e63fabd.92832361@news.optonline.net> <_039a.2508$Or5.435297@news20.bellglobal.com> <3e65acda.203982865@news.optonline.net>`

```
As always it depends.  IMS may well fit certain models of application
better.  While DASD and CPU speeds have increased dramatically since the
early days of DB2 and main memory increases overcome some of the other
slowdowns, IMS can still be faster in some high volume applications
where speed matters.  Many shops run both DB2 and IMS.  Rewriting of
complex applications isn't cheap and a large number of the IMS
applications are very complex.  Incidentally, I would suspect most IMS
DB as well as IMS DB/DC (data base/data communications) programs are
written in COBOL with EXEC DLI statements that are preprocessed or
co-processed depending on the compiler level. 

Robert Wagner wrote:
> 
> "Don Leahy" <xdb2imsN@nospam.whatever.net> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> language. Now that machines are fast, there is no rationale for using it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IMS vs DB2 was Re: OT Microsoft

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** B Berman <bberman@netbox.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-18T21:37:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E779332.3CBEDDC6@netbox.com>`
- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0303031144.2a0cee55@posting.google.com> <3e63fabd.92832361@news.optonline.net> <_039a.2508$Or5.435297@news20.bellglobal.com> <3e65acda.203982865@news.optonline.net> <3E773DA5.753B0433@istar.ca>`

```
"Clark F. Morris, Jr." wrote:

> As always it depends.  IMS may well fit certain models of application
> better.  While DASD and CPU speeds have increased dramatically since the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> co-processed depending on the compiler level.

The only instance where a Cobol pgm would have EXEC-DLI code would be in A CICS
program that was accessing a DLI database.  A standard batch DLI program or an IMS DC
online program would use the CALL 'CBLTDLI' USING... construct, not EXEC-DLI.

Bob
```

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick<Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>

```
According to the DL/I manuals you can use EXEC DLI in batch programs.  Of
course you must pre-compile with the CICS translator before compiling with
COBOL.

Our shop uses CBLTDLI for both batch and CICS DL/I calls.


--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications Development
FirstBank Data Corporation

CICS
program that was accessing a DLI database.  A standard batch DLI program or
an IMS DC
online program would use the CALL 'CBLTDLI' USING... construct, not
EXEC-DLI.

Bob
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IMS vs DB2 was Re: OT Microsoft

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick<Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-24T11:48:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5nju5$2bgkmh$1@ID-184804.news.dfncis.de>`
- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0303031144.2a0cee55@posting.google.com> <3e63fabd.92832361@news.optonline.net> <_039a.2508$Or5.435297@news20.bellglobal.com> <3e65acda.203982865@news.optonline.net> <3E773DA5.753B0433@istar.ca> <3E779332.3CBEDDC6@netbox.com> <b5fcck$28ph2q$1@ID-184804.news.dfncis.de> <3E7B5550.145397FE@netbox.com>`

```
Other than reading the documentation a bit several years ago I don't have
any rela experience with using the "EXEC DLI" calls.  But I know there are a
few differences.  For one, you cannot mix CBLTDLI calls and EXEC DLI
commands in the same program.  An EXEC DLI does *not* translate to a "CALL
'CBLTDLI'".  I just did a translate/compile to get some information.  Just
like an EXEC CICS, it translates them to several MOVE statements followed by
"CALL 'DFHEI1' USING ...".  You also don't code the PCB status areas in to
the LINKAGE SECTION.  The translator seems to define one called DLZDIB, but
it's structure is a bit different than the one used for the CALL interface.

As for why you have to use the CICS pre-processor for a batch program with
EXEC DLI commands, it's because "EXEC DLI" is not valid COBOL.  The COBOL
compiler does not know what it means.  Only the CICS pre-processor knows. 
The CICS pre-processor translates the EXEC DLI commands in to COBOL CALL
statements as I described above.

That's about all I know.  I was never able to get a program to run properly
using EXEC DLI calls, so I gave up.



--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications Development
FirstBank Data Corporation

>>> B Berman<bberman@netbox.com> 03/21/03 11:02AM >>>
Frank Swarbrick wrote:

> According to the DL/I manuals you can use EXEC DLI in batch programs.  Of
> course you must pre-compile with the CICS translator before compiling
with
> COBOL.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> The only instance where a Cobol pgm would have EXEC-DLI code would be in
A
> CICS
> program that was accessing a DLI database.  A standard batch DLI program
or
> an IMS DC
> online program would use the CALL 'CBLTDLI' USING... construct, not
> EXEC-DLI.

Frank - in a CICS pgm, is there any practical difference between using
EXEC-DLI
vs CALL 'CBLTDLI' ?  and in a batch pgm, if you use EXEC-DLI, you say it
must
be compiled using the CICS preprocessor, so what for ?

Bob
```

```
Your best bet, your very best bet, is to look in the bottom of your next
Cracker-Jacks box for a new hard drive (40G < $80).

Here's just one reason for doing a clean install of XP, thereby guaranteeing
a satisfying computing experience:

After you get XP installed, you'll probably want to run NTFS. Updating
Win9x, or installing over a Win9x system will leave you with a FAT-32 file
system. (A clean install gives you choices) While you can convert FAT-32 to
NTFS with a native XP utility, you will be stuck with small cluster sizes,
necessitating something like Partition Magic to re-size those suckers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Microsoft

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T16:18:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b42jl9$gvn$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<93dfc7c7.0303031144.2a0cee55@posting.google.com> <16e2f010.0303031937.20b2ab7f@posting.google.com> <93dfc7c7.0303040351.26d35769@posting.google.com>`

```

On  4-Mar-2003, foodman123@aol.com (Anthony Dilworth) wrote:

> I have a small POS software business. I have three physical hard drives
> on my main machine. I don't think I have enough space on C: to install XP
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> installing it so I am postponing the inevitable.

On my home machine, I do not have an operating system on C:.   I stopped putting
Windows on C: with Windows 3.14. (Back when I was using OS/2 on another
partition).

When you do the installation, you do need to not take defaults without checking,
but there's no problem installing on a different drive.   There is an advantage
in doing so, as you can leave your old Windows working for a while (there will
be a menu you can select change which drive you boot to).  Wait until
everything's moved over before cleaning up your original.   Windows DOES use a
couple of files on C: in order to boot up - so if you ever format C:, you will
have to repair your installation.

If you want to upgrade your Windows instead of getting a clean install (not
recommended), it doesn't cost that much to get yourself a new hard drive.  This
will usually come with a program to move your existing C: to the new hard drive.
  Make sure your current Windows can see the whole drive, or plan on buying
Partition Magic to expand it once you go to XP.
```

```
On 3 Mar 2003 11:44:19 -0800, foodman123@aol.com (Anthony Dilworth)
wrote:

>
>(I recently bought XP but am too scared to install it!)

Install it.  It is rock solid.  

With that said, make sure all your hardware has WHQL certified
drivers. XP can be a bit picky about driver quality.  Win2k drivers
*should* work in a pinch, but don't count on it.  XP also loves ram.
Give it enough, it will absolutely fly compared to previous versions,
skimp, and it will run like crap.  I'm very happy with 1GB...

Also, the detonator drivers that ship with XP are messed up, if your
monitor turns off due to your windows(but not bios) power saving
settings, XP will spontaneously reboot.  That might have been partly
due to my dual displays, but it happened to me until I updated to the
latest WHQL Detonator drivers.  OF course, if you don't have an NVIDIA
card, this doesn't apply.
```

<a id="t-f3f786848b"></a>
## Thread: If *I* were to write a "best pracice" document — 13 msgs

### If *I* were to write a "best pracice" document

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-03T16:35:35-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b40lbq$4hs$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Somewhat a "follow-up" on several recent and ongoing threads, I thought I
would say what I think I would do if I were to create a "best practices"
document (for new COBOL programmers -and for shops considering new or
revision to existing in-house standards).

1) Start by stating which compilers and environments I was most familiar
with - and what others I know about "slightly". Finally list other compilers
and O/S that I do not have personal experience with - so my comments may be
INCORRECT for such environments.

2) Say that the most important thing that should be done in creating ANY new
COBOL program or doing "significant" modification to existing code is to
find out what your goals and requirements are.  Some things to find out:

 A) Is your program intended for "online" (or interactive) use - or "batch"
processing.  If the former, what type of response times are
expected/required - if the latter, what is your "window" for completion?

 B) Related to the above, what is your expected "volume" (transactions or
"records") and what type of "hardware" power will you have?  Is this
expected to change (grow) over time and if so how rapidly?

 C) Is it currently expected that the source code will need to be ported to
other O/S environments?  other compilers on the same environment?  (How many
times in the past has your "shop" - or customers - claimed it wouldn't need
to be ported, but then requested it? How many times have they asked for
"portable" code that actually remains on the same O/S with the same compiler
for its full "life-time"?)

D) Is either the input or output must be shared across platforms (operating
systems) or across programming languages (including to/from other COBOL
compilers)  Are you expected to interface with some Database system?  If so,
is that system "portable" and/or its access available across systems.

3) Indicate that when doing "minor" maintenance on an existing program, it
is almost always best to use the STYLE and practices of that program and NOT
try and throw "new" techniques into an existing and functioning program.

4) Does your shop use a "proprietary" Operating System (primarily?
exclusively?) if so, is there a "proprietary" COBOL product set for that
environment sold by the same vendor?   If not, is there a "de facto"
compiler for the O/S or environment?

5)  Do you expect to sell this application to other sites or to customers?
If so,
 A) Is it safe to "assume" that the purchasing sites already own the
"run-time" for your compiler (e.g. IBM and LE) or .NET with "new" Windows
environments - or must each customer machine get a "new" copy of the
run-time?
 C) Will you be distributing your application as source code or object code?
If source code, are there any government requirements (such as *must* be
ANSI conforming)

6) In general, what are the "performance" versus "ease of maintenance"
trade-offs for this application?  For the site in general?  (Is it likely
that you "personally" will ALWAYS be doing the maintenance on this
application? Alternatively, will all maintenance go thru a "tight" audit
system for conformance to "shop standards")

7) If the "new" program is supposed to be a "quick&dirty" program for a
single use, what is the track record of your shop eventually moving such
programs "into regular production and maintenance"?

  * * * *

There are probably BUNCHES (no "pun intended") of other questions that I
think impact "best practices".  However, once these questions have been
answered, I would probably include such statements as:

 "-  Use of INDEXES versus SUBSCRIPTS ...
   Whether you use a subscript (data-item) or an index (INDEXED BY phrase)
to access a specific element may depend on what your shop (and maintenance
programmers) are most used to as well as what your performance requirements
are.  In a case where the specification of which element is to be accessed
relates "naturally" to some other data item that you are already
manipulating, then probably it is easiest to use this data item directly as
a subscript - rather than setting an index to that item's value.  On the
other hand, if you are "moving thru" the table elements in some algorithmic
way, the use of an INDEX may well be most self documenting.  When using a
subscript in a "tight loop" or heavily used procedure, it is usually
important to optimize the USAGE and PICTURE of the data item.   In many
systems, using a "full word binary" item performs the best for such a use.
However, you should check the performance information for your specific
compiler to determine what is the best definition for it - and what the
overhead is in "moving" a value to such an item.  For infrequent references
to a table element (within an "actual run of a program), there is probably
minimal performance difference between indexes and subscripts.  In such
cases, using the "most  well known and understood" technique in your shop
will probably be your best choice.

  NOTE:   Read your language reference material for further information on:
  - Index data-items versus Index-names
 - Relative subscripting and indexing
 - Search versus Search ALL and index requirements"
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: If *I* were to write a "best pracice" document

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T15:28:23+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<b42gmm$fqb$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b40lbq$4hs$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <01c2e1f2$ad742340$a593f243@chottel>`

```

On  3-Mar-2003, "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote:

> Mine would have been similar to this web site:
>
>  http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html

Just browsing through this document:

Don't use the SORT verb.
Among other reasons, this rule eliminates one possible reason to use GO TO.


I don't understand this - what relationship is there between the SORT verb and
GO TO?

I occasionally will have a job where internal sorts are applicable, but have
never been tempted to put a GO TO in those programs.

=======================================================

I would argue with him that it should be "hardly ever use the SORT verb - and
only do so after analysis of the problem indicates that this will offer a real
improvement over using an external sort".

And if table sorts get added to the language, make sure to exclude them from
this style request.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If *I* were to write a "best pracice" document

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T09:40:16-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b42hd3$19g$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b40lbq$4hs$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <01c2e1f2$ad742340$a593f243@chottel> <b42gmm$fqb$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
There is an HISTORICAL (not current with the '85 Standard) reason for this -
that is also unknown to IBM mainframe programmers from their '74 compiler.

In the '74 Standard, one wasn't able to "transfer control" outside an Input
or Output procedure.  Therefore, as the Input or Output Procedure itself was
executed "only once" - the only '74 Standard way of "looping" thru the
procedure (which, of course you probably wanted to do) was to GO TO the
start of the input/output procedure from the "end" if there were more
records to release/return.  ALTERNATIVELY, you could code a PERFORM THRU
within the Input/Output procedure, but in that case you had to "GO TO" some
sort of exit after the last record to avoid "falling thru" to the performed
procedure after the "mainline" of your procedure was completed.

Many programmers never "noticed" that this restriction was removed with the
'85 Standard (and '85 Standard compilers) and you may now PERFORM THRU and
"outside procedure" from within your Input/Output procedure without ever
doing a GO TO.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If *I* were to write a "best pracice" document

```
> In the '74 Standard, one wasn't able to "transfer control" outside an Input
> or Output procedure.  Therefore, as the Input or Output Procedure itself was
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> doing a GO TO.

Thanks.  I suppose one could use the GO TO to go to the end just as easily (ANSI
'74 also required that these procedures be SECTIONs).

Why am I sure I used ANSI 68 & ANSI 74 CoBOL on IBM mainframes that had the
INPUT PROCEDURE call paragraphs in the REST-OF-THE-PROGRAM SECTION?

Any rate, a historical reason shouldn't be listed in a set of current "best
practices" recommendations, without being noted as such.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If *I* were to write a "best pracice" document

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T14:31:25-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b439ft$11es$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<b40lbq$4hs$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <01c2e1f2$ad742340$a593f243@chottel> <b42gmm$fqb$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b42hd3$19g$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:b42hd3$19g$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net...

> Many programmers never "noticed" that this restriction was removed with
the
> '85 Standard (and '85 Standard compilers) and you may now PERFORM THRU and
> "outside procedure" from within your Input/Output procedure without ever
> doing a GO TO.

This relaxation has an interesting side-effect.  It's no longer reasonable
to determine for certain *at compile time* whether a RELEASE or RETURN is
being executed from within such a procedure (as the SYNTAX rules for these
verbs require).  The 2002 standard changes this to a GENERAL rule to correct
this; in any event, if it's to be unconditionally prevented either the
compiler or the operating environment has to take specific steps to do so at
execution time.

It is also not reasonable to determine for certain *at compile time* whether
a SORT or MERGE is executed from within such a procedure (illegal), but this
is correctly a GENERAL rule and need not be enforced at compile time;
likewise, if "nested SORTs" are a problem, "somebody" must actively prevent
them at execution time.

(Note that I originally had "possible" where I now have "reasonable".  It is
*possible*, at least in theory, to detect these violations at compile time,
but in complex programs it can be done only by predicting all the possible
execution-time paths through the program -- just as it is *possible* to make
sure that all execution-time paths that read a file are preceded by an
execution-time path that opens the file appropriately -- but I for one think
doing such analyses is far beyond what one should reasonably expect from a
compiler!).

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If *I* were to write a "best pracice" document

- **From:** psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T09:55:04-08:00

##### ↳ ↳ Re: If *I* were to write a "best pracice" document

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T09:59:44-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b42iik$olu$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b40lbq$4hs$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <01c2e1f2$ad742340$a593f243@chottel>`

```

"Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in message
news:01c2e1f2$ad742340$a593f243@chottel...
> Mine would have been similar to this web site:
>
>  http://home.swbell.net/mck9/cobol/cobol.html
>
> except this site is better than anything I envisioned.  Which is why my
web
> site still says "Charlie Hottel's web pages  will be hear real soon now
> ..." It seems hard to come up whith original material, although I notice
> that does not seem to stop a lot of other people.
>

(Posted to comp.lang.cobol - with CC's to the web-page author)

I have *not* reviewed all (or even much) of this work, but I would say that
it is "pretty out-of-date" on its IBM Mainframe COBOL knowledge.  You should
notice that the highest "knowledge" level product that is mentioned is
"COBOL II" (by which, I assume he means the now out-of-service VS COBOL II
product - about 4 IBM COBOL products "ago". I did se, however, at least one
reference to "COBOL/370" which only about 5 years out-of-date)

 - Its OO-COBOL stuff is all pre-Enterprise COBOL - where there was a change
in lBM's OO COBOL direction.

 - Statements such as
   "MVS does not support environmental variables."
is correct in the sense that the "now defunct" MVS operating system never
did this, but z/OS and OS/390 do.

I also worry (slightly) about the technical accuracy - consider the
"recommendation" in the style section,

"Don't use commas when you don't need them. They add nothing but clutter.
They may even be misleading when the reader mistakes a comma for a period,
due to poor print quality, a poor choice of fonts, or poor eyesight."

To me, this implies that there are SOME places where commas are needed.
There is *no* syntax in '85 Standard COBOL where commas are needed.

Or another example is the section on "Multiple Entry Points" which doesn't
mention some of the (easily missed) restriction on using the ENTRY statement
with dynamic CALLs as documented at:

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/4.1.6.3

   ***

As I say, I have NOT reviewed this page in detail.  It does start out with a
CLEAR statement of the environment that the author knows and furthermore
requests comments - and has links to dissenting views.  My initial
impression is that it is fairly good for IBM mainframe programmers - but
certainly not a "best pracices" for general COBOL use (and doesn't CLAIM to
be such)
```

#### ↳ Re: If *I* were to write a "best pracice" document

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T03:02:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E640DCD.9533475C@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<b40lbq$4hs$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> Somewhat a "follow-up" on several recent and ongoing threads, I thought I
> would say what I think I would do if I were to create a "best practices"
> document (for new COBOL programmers -and for shops considering new or
> revision to existing in-house standards).
>

<snip>

Lots of good ideas, but I foresee problems, and some of these have become
transparent following the appropriate threads.

Given the diversity, mainframes v PCs - is it realistic to come up with a text
that
can cover all situations. Example, PC-wise you can say "Handle COBOL files this
way..........". Depending upon the particular mainframe it *might* also be
possible to use the same quote - but when I look at some of the (IBM) mainframe
techniques  for file selection, which have nothing to do with COBOL syntax
.....?

To date I think the nearest we got to this was "COBOL Unleashed", published in
'98, where different authors produced chapters on their speciality. It is highly
unlikely that any one of those contributors would have accepted the challenge of
writing the whole book. Somebody even ventured to write a short and fairly good
introductory chapter on OO.

It seems to me -  the only possible way to win would be a co-operative of
contributors like "COBOL Unleashed". Still, seeing that *someone* is a
specialist on 'How to win friends and influence enemies' ...........?

This one was ticking away in my mind from the word 'go'. I think I've got it
right - the intent was 'Best Practices" based on COBOL '85. WHY ?  'This is how
you do it..',  based on something which is 18 years old.  Doesn't it make much
more sense to do it based on COBOL 2002  ? Example : 'This you can definitely do
NOW with your existing compiler, and these features illustrate new syntax
available from COBOL 2002".

Even the above would give you indigestion if you tried to embrace OO. Forget it
- that would need to be a totally separate book, making an acknowledgement of
Book I.

Jimmy Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: If *I* were to write a "best pracice" document

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T03:50:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e668e13.42194326@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b40lbq$4hs$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3E640DCD.9533475C@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
>Given the diversity, mainframes v PCs - is it realistic to come up with a text
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>.....?

It sounds like you are referring to JCL, where the COBOL program associates a
file with a 'dd name' and the JCL associates that with an actual file name. PC
and Unix COBOLs allow you to do the same. You set environment variables like
'custfile = /(directory)/"My Customer file" and say SELECT .. ASSIGN TO
$custfile. 

>This one was ticking away in my mind from the word 'go'. I think I've got it
>right - the intent was 'Best Practices" based on COBOL '85. WHY ?  'This is how
>you do it..',  based on something which is 18 years old.  Doesn't it make much
>more sense to do it based on COBOL 2002  ? Example : 'This you can definitely
do
>NOW with your existing compiler, and these features illustrate new syntax
>available from COBOL 2002".

Few have access to a 2002 compliant compiler, but most of the 'good stuff' is
available _now_ in '85 compilers from Micro Focus, Fujitsu, Realia and, in some
cases, IBM. I advocated using what's available today.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: If *I* were to write a "best pracice" document

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T15:05:30+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b47o3q$67i$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b40lbq$4hs$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3E640DCD.9533475C@shaw.ca> <3e668e13.42194326@news.optonline.net>`

```

On  5-Mar-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> It sounds like you are referring to JCL, where the COBOL program associates a
> file with a 'dd name' and the JCL associates that with an actual file name. PC
> and Unix COBOLs allow you to do the same. You set environment variables like
> 'custfile = /(directory)/"My Customer file" and say SELECT .. ASSIGN TO
> $custfile.

And VAX did one better - we normally did the same thing within DCL, but if we
didn't assign a file name, the system used the name in the CoBOL program.
```

#### ↳ Re: If *I* were to write a "best pracice" document

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T03:50:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e667e20.38111150@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b40lbq$4hs$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>Somewhat a "follow-up" on several recent and ongoing threads, I thought I
>would say what I think I would do if I were to create a "best practices"
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>INCORRECT for such environments.

An inventory of COBOL compilers is nearly impossible to obtain. I'd read about
650 and Tiny COBOL, but I've seen references here to compilers I'd never heard
of. 

>2) Say that the most important thing that should be done in creating ANY new
>COBOL program or doing "significant" modification to existing code is to
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>expected to change (grow) over time and if so how rapidly?

That used to be at the top of my list, as well. Twenty years ago I would have
agreed, perhaps even ten years ago. In the old days, our system designs were
heavily influenced by hardware performance issues. The tail was wagging the dog.
Nowadays, hardware is fast enough that we can usualy design for application
requirements while ignoring machine constraints. An exception is database
access, were some features such as unions are still too slow.

If performance is an issue, write prototypes to measure speed of alternative
approaches. Real-world data is more valuable than projections based on
experience which may be obsolete. 

> C) Is it currently expected that the source code will need to be ported to
>other O/S environments?  other compilers on the same environment?  (How many
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>for its full "life-time"?)

I concur. There is no way of knowing whether code must be portable. The
pragmatic solution is use generally accepted practices. Usually, that means
standard-compliant code, but don't ham-string yourself by avoiding generally
available features solely because they not in the standard. If they're widely
used, they will be on the next iteration. 

>D) Is either the input or output must be shared across platforms (operating
>systems) or across programming languages (including to/from other COBOL
>compilers)  Are you expected to interface with some Database system?  If so,
>is that system "portable" and/or its access available across systems.

Again, nobody knows what will happen in the future. The safest course is make
all files and database calls as generic as possible. Don't put binary numbers in
files because it saved milliseconds twenty years ago. 

>3) Indicate that when doing "minor" maintenance on an existing program, it
>is almost always best to use the STYLE and practices of that program and NOT
>try and throw "new" techniques into an existing and functioning program.

Agreed. 

>5)  Do you expect to sell this application to other sites or to customers?
>If so,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>run-time?

Assume they don't, so distribute the run-time. If the compiler company wants
royalties for the run-time, find a compiler that doesn't. 

> C) Will you be distributing your application as source code or object code?
>If source code, are there any government requirements (such as *must* be
>ANSI conforming)

Nobody distributes source code today, except in the mainframe world. At best,
they put source in escrow. 

Government agencies' internally-developed code is more non-compliant than
businesses'. Regulations requiring compliance are unenforced and unenforcable. 

>6) In general, what are the "performance" versus "ease of maintenance"
>trade-offs for this application?  For the site in general?  (Is it likely
>that you "personally" will ALWAYS be doing the maintenance on this
>application? Alternatively, will all maintenance go thru a "tight" audit
>system for conformance to "shop standards")

The original author sets the de-facto standard. As you said above, maintenance
should follow his style. The 'style police' are usually called in for
maintenance rather than original development. When there is a conflict between
his style and the 'shop standard', they will accede to following his style. The
alternative is a complete rewrite, which they lack the power to authorize. 

Hopefully the new style will become a precedent to change the shop standard. Not
because it is better, solely because it is 'in production'. 

>7) If the "new" program is supposed to be a "quick&dirty" program for a
>single use, what is the track record of your shop eventually moving such
>programs "into regular production and maintenance"?

Happens all the time. Version control is one way to draw the distinction
between production and one-time. A better way is execution logging. When I see a
program run ten times in a month, that's not a 'one time' program. It needs to
be ramped up to production quality, documented and designated production. 

Robert
```

<a id="t-71e46e7cbc"></a>
## Thread: OT - Humor (for those interested in "logic" and "science" — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT - Humor (for those interested in "logic" and "science"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-03T18:34:08-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b40sa2$qe1$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
 The following is an actual question given on a University of Washington
Chemistry mid term. The answer by one student was "so profound" that the
professor shared it with colleagues, via the Internet, which is, of course,
why we now have the pleasure of enjoying it as well.

 Bonus Question: Is Hell exothermic (gives off heat) or endothermic (absorbs
heat)? Most of the students wrote proofs of their beliefs using Boyle's Law,
(gas cools off when it expands and heats). One student wrote the following
 answer:

 First, we need to know how the mass of Hell is changing in time. So we need
to know the rate that souls are moving into Hell and the rate they are
leaving. I think that we can safely assume that once a soul gets to Hell, it
will not leave. Therefore, no souls are leaving. As for how many souls are
entering Hell, let's look at the different religions that exist in the world
today. Some of these religions state that if you are not a member of their
religion, you will go to Hell. Since there are more than one of these
religions and since people do not belong to more than one religion, we can
project that all souls go to Hell. With birth and death rates as they are,
we can expect the number of souls in Hell to increase exponentially.

 Now, we look at the rate of change of the volume in Hell because Boyle's
Law states that in order for the temperature and pressure in Hell to stay
the same, the volume of Hell has to expand proportionately as souls are
added.

This gives two possibilities:

1. If Hell is expanding at a slower rate than the rate at which souls enter
Hell, then the temperature and pressure in Hell will increase until all Hell
breaks loose.

 2. If Hell is expanding at a rate faster than the increase of souls in
Hell, then the temperature and pressure will drop until Hell freezes over.

 So which is it? If we accept the postulate given to me by Teresa during my
Freshman year that: "it will be a cold day in Hell before I sleep with you,"
and take into account the fact that I still have not succeeded in having
sexual relations with her, then #2 cannot be true, and thus I am sure that
Hell is exothermic and will not freeze.

 The student received the only "A" given.


 --
 Bill Klein
  wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com
```

#### ↳ Re: OT - Humor (for those interested in "logic" and "science"

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-03-03T21:43:53-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b413t9$qi7$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<b40sa2$qe1$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

> The following is an actual question given on a University of Washington
>Chemistry mid term. The answer by one student was "so profound" that the
>professor shared it with colleagues, via the Internet, which is, of course,
>why we now have the pleasure of enjoying it as well.

From http://www.snopes.com/college/exam/hell.htm :

--begin quoted text:

Commonly, the piece will begin with a statement meant to authenticate the 
story. "An actual question given on University of Washington chemistry 
midterm," "from a Yale professor," and "Dr. Schambaugh, of the University 
of Oklahoma School of Chemical Engineering, Final Exam question for May of 
1997" have so far been spotted. 

--end quoted text

DD
```

```
I thought it very amusing too. Here's one related to the topic, that Irish priests
love to tell with a twinkle in their eye - you may possibly have heard it before.

Joe Flannagan having done his three score and ten on terra firma, eventually made
it to Heaven. His guardian angel took him on a quick tour, pointing out the
different 'rooms' and occupants. 'Those are the Mormons, that room has Jews, this
one Baptists. Those people chanting are Hindus. Shhh.... ! Quiet as we go past
this one. It's the Catholics and they think they are the only ones up here !".

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

## Thread: IBM OO COBOL — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo)_

### IBM OO COBOL

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T03:11:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c2e1fb$b16dd000$a593f243@chottel>`

```
Since IBM dropped SOM and now has OO COBOL with Java interoperability, does
anyone know which version of the compiler was the first to implement this
new OO COBOL.  I want to get some idea of how far away my current compiler
is. I have just read up on OO in the Programmer's Guide and seemed to
understand most of it pretty well.  I am anxious to start experimenting
with  OO. Thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM OO COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-03T23:06:31-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b41c9p$fns$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<01c2e1fb$b16dd000$a593f243@chottel>`

```
It was the change from IBM COBOL for OS/390 & VM (V2R2) to
                                    IBM Enterprise COBOL for z/OS & OS/390
(or is it OS/390 & z/OS)

where the "switch" from SOM to "Java Interoperability" was introduced.

Enterprise COBOL is now on its second release (still "version 3") - but
either of them include this functionality.  The XML support was enhanced
between releases of Enterprise COBOL.
```

```
Jimmy
```

<a id="t-13fe03de1f"></a>
## Thread: AS/400 (I Series) Support — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`AS/400, iSeries, RPG`](topics.md#as400)_

### AS/400 (I Series) Support

- **From:** Floyd Johnson <fjohnso5@rochester.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T19:23:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E64FD2B.5050904@rochester.rr.com>`

```
Friends:

Some of you may remember when I wrote last fall about receiving an 
AS/400 as a gift from our college bookstore.  We finally received the 
machine last Christmas and are now planning to deploy it sometime during 
the summer months.

IBM has offered to help us get it "up and running". They understand, 
through the brief contact that we have had with them, that we have the 
hardware but no software.

My anticipated use of the hardware is to support our File Structures 
course (using COBOL) and our upper division DBMS course (using ???).

My question for you - what software to I need to be looking for.  I know 
nothing about the AS/400 - and we will need to build this thing from the 
OS on up to support the above mentioned courses.

Thank you for any hints, hand holding, or suggestions you can provide.
I am a good learner - I spent the last two years installing LINUX for 
the first time and have a reasonably stable network working, though I 
had used UNIX for most of the previous four years.

I suspect that we will get to the same place with the AS/400.  Some 
years ago I was using an IBM 9370 for an extended period of time (alas, 
using FORTRAN), so I have been exposed to the IBM platform to some extent.

Thanks again.

Floyd Johnson
Assistant Professor
     Computer Science
```

#### ↳ Re: AS/400 (I Series) Support

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T22:21:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<738a6v0959iho4l45a2mk16pbg725je3uo@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3E64FD2B.5050904@rochester.rr.com>`

```
On Tue, 04 Mar 2003 19:23:25 GMT, Floyd Johnson
<fjohnso5@rochester.rr.com> wrote:

>
>IBM has offered to help us get it "up and running". They understand, 
>through the brief contact that we have had with them, that we have the 
>hardware but no software.
When they say they will help does that mean that they will supply the
software free of charge or at a very low price, or just help to
install/configure whatever you buy?
If it is free try and get your hand in (at least) the following
WebSphere
COBOL/400 and ILE-COBOL
RPG/400
CL/400 (just mandatory if you whish to do anything usefull)
C/C++ compiler
JAVA Development Kit (the full thing, not just the runtime)
And try and get licenses of Client Access and Personal Comunications
also.
(there may be other things but I don't remember).

>
>My anticipated use of the hardware is to support our File Structures 
>course (using COBOL) and our upper division DBMS course (using ???).
You will get IBM DB2 as a base installation. (not UDB). This may be
enough for what your course requires.

COBOL/400 or ILE-COBOL are both separately licensed, and you may need
to buy a license for either of them. (bear in mind that COBOL/400 does
NOT have all the intrinsic functions that ILE-COBOL has, this just in
case these are part of your course).

Also depending on the operating system version that you can install in
that specific model, you may also be able to install at any time, and
for a period of 70 days any of the development tools available for
that platform which is very handy sometimes. (whether you can do this
more than once I don't know!!)
>
>My question for you - what software to I need to be looking for.  I know 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>had used UNIX for most of the previous four years.
Be very carefull with authorities.
Some "managers" give to much authority because they don't know how to
do things and they get some big surprises sometimes.
Use group authorities as much as possible.
Use Menu/programs with `"owner" authorities, and don't give individual
authorities higher than "*use" unless required. (e.g. you can set a
menu/program (B) with authority to change a file (C), give a user (A)
authority to use that program (B), even though the user may not have
any authority to file (C).
Don't give "*allobj" authorities to anyone 
Use a "security level" of at least 20.

The AS/400 has lots of help in each individual command, but sometimes
it is still not clear what a particular command requires. Old manuals
were great to search, but the new ones requires that you install all
the manuals in the AS400, configure the HTTP server and that Java
runtime is also installed. Try and get some help from IBM in this
area.



Regards

FF
Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

#### ↳ Re: AS/400 (I Series) Support

## Thread: fixed format — 9 msgs

### fixed format

- **From:** "Paul" <paulhbliu@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T15:47:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8i89a.2736$Or5.487415@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Hi

In the fixed format cobol code I got compiler complain: parse error said
below line(begin of the program) expecting '$'
000100 01 CSCSCD.
00001510

what's the meaning of that, is it related to the format ?

thanks
Paul
```

#### ↳ Re: fixed format

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T13:07:21-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b434i9$u79$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<8i89a.2736$Or5.487415@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Don't know the implementation / platform but an error here is not at all
unreasonable.

Sequence numbers in fixed-form reference format format* COBOL are in Columns
1 - 6.  In that format Column 7 may contain a space, a hyphen, a slash, a
"D" or a "*".  Column 7 here contains "1", which is not on that list.

It's sort of tough to figure out what the author meant by the sequence
numbers here; one possibility is
    000100 01 CSCSCD.
    000150 10  ...
although I'm not a personal fan of the relaxation of "area A / Area B"
restrictions when using fixed-form reference format, so I would prefer
something more like
    000100 01 CSCSCD.
    000150     10  ...
if that's what was meant in the first place.

Also,
    000100 01 CSCSCD.
    001510 ...
is a viable possibility in that format.

    -Chuck Stevens


"Paul" <paulhbliu@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:8i89a.2736$Or5.487415@news20.bellglobal.com...
> Hi
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: fixed format

- **From:** "Paul" <paulhbliu@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T16:26:23-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6S89a.2971$Or5.494411@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<8i89a.2736$Or5.487415@news20.bellglobal.com> <b434i9$u79$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
about the format
the one should look like this(with space number):
000100  01  CSCSCD.                        00015000
12345678901234567890123456789012345678

000100 is first 6 space
space is 7th
00015000 is the final 8 spaces in this line

BTW, what's the most possible reason that parser complain that "expecting
'$'"

thanks
Paul

Columns
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: fixed format

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T13:57:52-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b437h1$105t$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<8i89a.2736$Or5.487415@news20.bellglobal.com> <b434i9$u79$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <6S89a.2971$Or5.494411@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
If the "00015000" actually occurs beginning in Column 31, the only way I can
think of offhand that this would be valid syntax in  fixed-form reference
format is if your implementation defined Margin R (which must be fixed at a
specific location in any given implementation) as occurring between Columns
30 and 31, which I think is extremely unlikely.  There's also the
theoretical possibility that the implementation allows the location of
Margin R explicitly to be varied by the user, and somehow that location has
gotten set to an incorrect or inappropriate value.  Barring these
possibilities, what this fragment is supposed to mean or accomplish I
haven't a clue.

    -Chuck Stevens

"Paul" <paulhbliu@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:6S89a.2971$Or5.494411@news20.bellglobal.com...

a
> > "D" or a "*".  Column 7 here contains "1", which is not on that list.
> >
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…

said
> > > below line(begin of the program) expecting '$'
> > > 000100 01 CSCSCD.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: fixed format

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-04T15:12:31-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b434s3$i03$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8i89a.2736$Or5.487415@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
It looks to me as if in your second source line the "1" from "10" is in
column 7.  The only valid characters for column 7 (in the ANSI'85 Standard)
are

   " "  (space)
  "D"  (or 'd' for debugging line)
  "*"  (for a comment)
  "/"  (for a comment and a "page throw")

My guess is that your compiler ALSO allows a "$" - probably indicating a
compiler directive or option.

    ***

NOTE:
  In addition, it also looks as if you have found out about the A-margin and
the B-margin in ANSI'85 Standard "fixed" format.  I suggest that you look
these up in your text book or language reference.   Certain parts of the
COBOL ('85 Standard) language must begin in columns 8-11 - other parts of
the language must begin in columns 12 - thru the R-margin (which may be
after Column 72, Column 80 - or wherever your compiler thinks it is)
```

```
Paul,

Which compiler are you using ?  If Micro Focus is that "$" related to a set
directive such as the following :-

$set sql (dbman=ODBC)

The dollar sign has to be  in Column 7.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
>Hi
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>what's the meaning of that, is it related to the format ?

As explained before, you are including a fixed-form copybook into a free-form
program. Doing so requires a compiler directive before and after the copybook,
OR blanking out the sequence numbers.
```

```
write some Cobol programs, having once been a
Cobol analyst/programmer (circa 1980).

questions based on this product? What is the legal
status of the spftware itself - is it still "protected"

David Kinston
melbourne.au
```

#### ↳ Re: Microsoft Cobol V5

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T18:19:33+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b441hh$5ha$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3e65848d$0$10451@echo-01.iinet.net.au>`

```
David K wrote:

> Are there people here who would be able to answer
> questions based on this product? What is the legal
> status of the spftware itself - is it still "protected"
> given that Microsoft abandoned it many years ago?

MS Cobol version 5 was a repackaged version of MicroFocus Cobol/2 3.0.  MF 
may well have something to say if you have 'stolen' it or gave it away.

However, based on an assumption that you bought the product yourself years 
ago then neither MS nor MF could complain about you continuing to use it 
even if you had shelfed it for a decade or so.

The licence for MS Cobol 5 includes right to distribute the programs you 
compile with this product without any run time charges.

It can produce DOS and Windows 3.x programs.

Windows XP apparently does not support this version of Cobol, but Win98 and 
W2K should be OK.
```

<a id="t-380cf6a549"></a>
## Thread: Netexpress 3.1/Dialog System:Validate — 1 this month / 2 total

_Thread started [2003-02](2003-02.md#t-380cf6a549)._

#### ↳ Re: Netexpress 3.1/Dialog System:Validate

- **From:** mfcobol2002 <marcos_as@terra.com.br>
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T11:59:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2605999.1046865557@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<2557677.1045826677@dbforums.com>`

```

User-defined validation allows you to specify a program name which
will be called as part of the normal validation process of an entry
field or a Window.

This allows you greater flexibility in validating options such as "does
the value exist in a file?".

solution of the problem. my gratefulness to all.


When you specify a user-defined validation program, you must write the
program and it should include the copy file "dsusrval.cpy" along with
your standard screenset datablock copy file.

The callout will be made from Dialog System as follows:

CALL "USER-VALIDATION-PROGRAM"
using DS-USER-VALIDATION-LINKAGE
DATA-BLOCK

so the called program must provide a linkage section and procedure
division using statement that match this description.

When your program has done its validation it then sets the DS-UV-
RETURN-STATUS to one of the following values:

78 DS-UV-FIELD-VALID value 0.
78 DS-UV-FIELD-INVALID value 1.
78 DS-UV-USE-ERROR-MSG-NO value 2.
78 DS-UV-USE-ERROR-MSG value 4.

If the field is valid then move DS-UV-FIELD-VALID to DS-UV-RETURN-
STATUS. If it is invalid then move DS-UV-FIELD-INVALID to DS-UV-
RETURN-STATUS.

If you want to specify an error message to be displayed other than the
default message defined in the data definition then you can override
this by adding the DS-UV-USE-ERROR-MSG-NO or DS-UV-USE-ERROR-MSG value
to the DS-UV-FIELD-INVALID value and placing the result in DS-UV-
RETURN-STATUS.

If you are overriding the message number then the number passed back to
Dialog System in the DS-UV-ERROR-MSG-NO field will be used to identify
the correct error-message to display.

If you are overriding the message text then the text passed back to
Dialog System in the DS-UV-ERROR-MSG field will be used as the error
message text to display.

I have attached an example program which demonstrates the use of
this feature.

TESTVAL.GS contains a window with an entry field and a push button. The
only values allowed in the entry field by the validation routine are
"12345" and "ABCDE". Enter an invalid value and then press the
"Validate" button. This will cause the validation program "dsusrval.dll"
to be called to validate the contents of the entry field. An error
message will then be displayed in a message box.
```

```

On  5-Mar-2003, poopiehead <member15404@dbforums.com> wrote:

> I AM RUNNING POWER COBOL 6.0.  I WOULD LIKE TO COMPARE A RECORD READ IN
> FROM A FILE TO A FORM FEED.  BASICALLY:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> ANY IDEAS OR KNOW OF A WAY TO COMPLETE THIS TASK PLEASE HELP.

I hated it when I used compilers that don't allow me to initialize a variable to
hex values.  I used to have on-line programs that I couldn't read on the screen
(as they would act on the screen).   Do you have such a compiler?

This is valid code in my compiler.
MOVE X'0000021C'                TO DDN-TCB
      IF L05-DMI-BYTE > X'79' AND < X'90'
      IF L05-DMI-BYTE > X'19' AND < X'30'
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Form Feed

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-05T16:36:33-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E665FD1.760EE3F6@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<2606427.1046875381@dbforums.com> <b45bsn$pus$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
See below for an 85 standard solution, pun intended.
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> On  5-Mar-2003, poopiehead <member15404@dbforums.com> wrote:
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>       IF L05-DMI-BYTE > X'19' AND < X'30'

COBOL 85 (including IBM VS COBOL II and successors and the POWER COBOL)
allow you to state in the SPECIAL-NAMES paragraph of the ENVIRONMENT
DIVISION - SYMBOLIC [CHARACTERS] FORM-FEED [IS/ARE] integer-1 where
integer-1 is the decimal equivalent of the hex value of a form feed +
1.  If a form feed is x'0A' then integer-1 would be 11. I have forgotten
what a form feed is in ASCII.  This is more self documenting than a hex
value.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Form Feed

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T15:03:16+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b47nvl$673$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<2606427.1046875381@dbforums.com> <b45bsn$pus$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3E665FD1.760EE3F6@istar.ca>`

```

On  5-Mar-2003, "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:

> COBOL 85 (including IBM VS COBOL II and successors and the POWER COBOL)
> allow you to state in the SPECIAL-NAMES paragraph of the ENVIRONMENT
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> value.

I switched to hex values as soon as I could.    If you put the hex value as a
working-storage constant, it is just as documenting, and all beginning
programmers understood it.

Actually, I worked in a compiler where the SPECIAL NAMES had the hard coded hex
value that messed up the screen display.  That was the worst of both worlds.
```

```
TO CURB ANY ARGUEMENTS I DECLARED THE RECORD AS SO.
 01 SPECIAL-CHARACTERS              comp pic s9(02).
    88 form-feed                     value 12.
    88 carriage-return              value 10.

I CHECK TO SEE IF IT IS EITHER CHARACTER.  AFTER READING THE POST I
REMEMBERED THAT DOS ALWAYS PUTS A 10 AND 12 AT THE END OF A LINE
(ASSEMBLER DAYS).

THE WIERD THING IS THAT I CAN WRITE A FORM FEED TO A FILE BUT I CAN'T
COMPARE THE FILE'S INPUT TO A FORM FEED USING THE SAME METHOD.

THE COMMENT ON A FF CONSTANT MAKES SENSE.  I GOT THE SAME IDEA IN MY
HEAD AND COULDN'T GET IT OUT.  I SEARCHED ALL MY FUJITSU AND POWER COBOL
BOOKS AND ALL THE HELP FILES FOR A CONSTANT FOR FORM FEED.  TO MY
SURPRISE THERE WAS NONE.  THIS IS SOMETHING POWER COBOL SHOULD ADD AS A
CONSTANT OR WRITE BETTER STINKING BOOKS IF IT EXISTS.

I WILL CONTINUE TO TRY THIS METHOD AND HOPEFULLY I CAN FIGURE SOMETHING
ELSE OUT.  IF THERE ARE ANY OTHER IDEAS PLEASE SHARE THEM ANY HELP IS
APPRECIATED.

JERRYMOUSE, THE PRICE JUST MADE THAT SO FUNNY.  $12.00 JUST WASN'T
FUNNY, BUT $13.00 I CAN'T CONTAIN MYSELF.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Form Feed

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T10:03:09-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0303061003.66a1317f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2606427.1046875381@dbforums.com> <3e66b463.52003587@news.optonline.net> <b46sva$cmh$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b46vqj$vin$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <2611501.1046961957@dbforums.com>`

```
poopiehead <member15404@dbforums.com> shouted:

> TO CURB ANY ARGUEMENTS I DECLARED THE RECORD AS SO.
>  01 SPECIAL-CHARACTERS              comp pic s9(02).
>     88 form-feed                     value 12.
>     88 carriage-return              value 10.

Hey, you have managed to find the shift keys, progress at last.

> I CHECK TO SEE IF IT IS EITHER CHARACTER.  AFTER READING THE POST I
> REMEMBERED THAT DOS ALWAYS PUTS A 10 AND 12 AT THE END OF A LINE
> (ASSEMBLER DAYS).

End of line in CP/M-DOS-Windows is cr/lf x"0D0A"  (13/10).
 
> THE WIERD THING IS THAT I CAN WRITE A FORM FEED TO A FILE BUT I CAN'T
> COMPARE THE FILE'S INPUT TO A FORM FEED USING THE SAME METHOD.

There may be several reasons for that.  If the default of BINARY(WORD)
is set then the PIC S9(2) will be two bytes with value x"0C00" (byte
reversed).  Thus it will be _two_ bytes that are checked and the
second may be, say, space which will not match x"00".

Setting BINARY(BYTE) as WMK pointed out will set single byte values.

The next problem is that if you are reading the file as LINE
SEQUENTIAL then it may be stripping out control characters and so you
woul;d never see the form feed.

cr/lf characters are stripped when reading and tab characters are
expanded.  Other controls may be stripped too.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Form Feed

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-07T00:35:25+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e67cda6.124017391@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2606427.1046875381@dbforums.com> <3e66b463.52003587@news.optonline.net> <b46sva$cmh$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b46vqj$vin$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <2611501.1046961957@dbforums.com>`

```
poopiehead <member15404@dbforums.com> wrote:

>
>TO CURB ANY ARGUEMENTS I DECLARED THE RECORD AS SO.
> 01 SPECIAL-CHARACTERS              comp pic s9(02).
>    88 form-feed                     value 12.
>    88 carriage-return              value 10.

You might want to change the terminology to 

    88 line-feed                        value 10.
    88 form-feed                       value 12. 
    88 carriage-return               value 13. 

Your equivocation derailed Richard. 

Form feed is a thing we send to printers to tell them to skip to the next page.
Line feed (aka New Line) tells a screen or printer to skip to the next line. 
Carriage return moves the 'cursor' back to postition 1. 

PC text file records (not just DOS) are terminated by cr/lf. Unix text file
records are terminated by nl, which is the same as lf. 

When you read a text file as LINE SEQUENTIAL, you do not see cr nor lf. If it
contained form feeds, you _would_ see them. 

If you want to see the 'raw' file contents, do this:

 environment division.
 input-output section.
 file-control.
    select input-file assign to (whatever)
        organization is record sequential
        file status is file-status-code.

 data division.
 file section.
 fd  input-file.
 01  sector-record.
     05  sector-byte occurs 512 pic x.

Hope this clears up any confusion. 

>THE COMMENT ON A FF CONSTANT MAKES SENSE.  I GOT THE SAME IDEA IN MY
>HEAD AND COULDN'T GET IT OUT.  I SEARCHED ALL MY FUJITSU AND POWER COBOL
>BOOKS AND ALL THE HELP FILES FOR A CONSTANT FOR FORM FEED.  TO MY
>SURPRISE THERE WAS NONE.  THIS IS SOMETHING POWER COBOL SHOULD ADD AS A
>CONSTANT OR WRITE BETTER STINKING BOOKS IF IT EXISTS.

We share your frustration. Next time, come here first. We know it all.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Form Feed

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-03-06T10:03:14-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0303060658.72c4d81f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2606427.1046875381@dbforums.com> <3e66b463.52003587@news.optonline.net> <b46sva$cmh$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard - Hopefully my buddy Poopiehead will read this far.

1 - Pic S9(2) COMP can be a single byte if BINARY(BYTE) is utilized -
I use this all the time when porting MF code to simulate COMP-X.

2 - If the file is opened and read as LINE SEQUENTIAL the Form Feed is
stripped from the input and that is likely the problem here.  Two
possible solutions:

Solution A - Unless you use a directive or Varying record lengths,
Fujitsu COBOL pads line sequential records so you might be able to
read them as sequential instead of line sequential.

Solution B - Pre-Process the file sequentially one byte at a time
converting the Form Feed character to another character such as a ~ or
a ^ then read it as a line sequential file (assuming it truly has
trailing spaces stripped) an search for the replacement character.


Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message news:<b46sva$cmh$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>...
> Robert Wagner wrote:
> 
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>        on this system PIC 9(02) COMP is not one byte.
```

```
and later cater for deleting trailing spaces like MF by specifying 
CBR_TRAILING_BLANK_RECORD=REMOVE.

which then makes the sequential file unaligned ?
```

```
Yes - which is why I suggessted the pre-process solution.  I do this
kind of processing but changed my "WRITE AFTER 1" etc to instead use
control codes in the record --- "Move Write-After-1 to Rec-cc" Write
....

The I post process the records into Flexus Form Print.  

This is much like the IBM Mainframe COBOL '74 - "After Positioning"
extension.
```

```

I DID NOTICE THAT THE LINE SEQUENTIAL STRIPPED THE FORM FEEDS OUT LATE
YESTERDAY.  WHEN I DISCOVERED THAT FUJITSU CHANGED FORM FEEDS TO SPACES
I TRIED TO THINK OF ANOTHER WAY TO GENERATE GOOD CODE FOR MY PROGRAM.
FOR MY GIVEN SITUATION NONE OF THE ABOVE POSTS APPLY.  I THOUGHT OF
CALLING A MICROFOCUS EXE OR DLL TO STRIP OUT FORM FEEDS AND REPLACE THEM
WITH ANOTHER CHARACTER, BUT THE RUN TIME WOULD TAKE TO LONG.  I HAVE TO
OPEN DIFFERENT REPORTS AND MANY VARY IN LENGTH.  CHARACTER BY CHARACTER
WAS ANOTHER THOUGHT BUT A LOOP THAT REPEATS 133 TIMES FOR A SINGLE LINE
AND CONTINUES FOR 500 PAGES IS NOT A GOOD OPTION EITHER.  I AM CURRENTLY
LOOKING AT TEXT OR SOME OTHER WAY TO FIND THE BEGIN OR END OF A PAGE.
THE REPORTS ALSO VERY IN LINE COUNTS.  SO IF I DON'T FIND TEXT I WILL BE
FORCED TO GO BACK AND JUST CHANGE THE FORM FEED TO ANOTHER CHARACTER
SUCH AS '~', '*', ETC.  NOT A BIG THING BUT A PAIN IN THE ASS FOR THE
AMOUNT OF REPORTS OUR SOFTWARE GENERATES.  THANKS AGAIN FOR YOUR HELP.
IF THERE ARE MORE IDEAS I STILL WELCOME THEM.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Form Feed

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-08T00:43:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e693cb3.62440919@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2606427.1046875381@dbforums.com> <2615982.1047043612@dbforums.com>`

```
poopiehead <member15404@dbforums.com> wrote:

>
>I DID NOTICE THAT THE LINE SEQUENTIAL STRIPPED THE FORM FEEDS OUT LATE
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>AND CONTINUES FOR 500 PAGES IS NOT A GOOD OPTION EITHER.  

Speed is will not change much between alternative solutions. Whether you do it
yourself or let a COBOL file system do it, SOME software SOMEwhere will examine
every byte in the file.

The cleanest way is to do it yourself. Then you're not guessing what the file
system may or may not do. I posted the code to do that earlier. It was
cut-and-pasted from my production system which processes thousands of reports
daily, in a few milliseconds per. Some reports are over 500 pages long. 

My concern is tab expansion, for which there are two 'standards'. Text editors
and COBOL file systems jump to the next (column - 1) divisible by  eight.
Spreadsheets such as Excel put a single tab between fields. As a result, letting
COBOL expand a report file produced by a spreadsheet produces columns that don't
line up. Because my input could be using either convention, I must detect the
convention at run-time and automatically adapt to it. 

Worse, some reports contain a mixture. The 'legal verbiage' follows text-editor
conventions while the mutual fund holdings section follows spreadsheet
conventions. No general-purpose tool can figure that out and get it right. 

Another problem is FTPing files between PC and Unix with the default Binary
option rather than ASCII. That's usually not a problem because COBOL file
systems handle either line terminator. But if someone edits the PC-style file
with a Unix editor such as vi, it becomes double-spaced. If my record processor
did not detect and delete these, downstream logic would be seriously screwed up.
In your case, the reports would be printed double-spaced. 

For the reasons illustrated above, I recommend you read the raw file image and
process it a byte at a time. You don't need 'byte stream'; you can do it more
simply with 'record sequential'. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Form Feed

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-03-08T09:47:03-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0303080947.65522add@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2606427.1046875381@dbforums.com> <2615982.1047043612@dbforums.com> <3e693cb3.62440919@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert - you mentioned Tab expansion.  Here's some code (Fujitsu) that
expands tabs on Windows - using the "windows" and "unix" method.  I
know there are two different ways - this one has worked in every real
case I have encountered.


This is probably pretty ugly - but it works - it's purpose was to take
a file, expand tabs and remove extra 0D characters then replace the
original file with the modfied.  Works well.  Passing ,BSAM on the end
of the input file name speeds it up considerably.  This will work with
other compilers if you strip the ,BSAM.

000010 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000030 PROGRAM-ID. SSEXPAND IS INITIAL.
000130* Called program used to expand tabs and to remove extra x"0D"
characters.
000140* NOTE: ,BSAM is fujitsu specific - you need to remove it for
other compilers
000142*       ,BSAM speeds reading and creation of sequential files
000150 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000160 CONFIGURATION SECTION.
000230 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
000250 FILE-CONTROL.
000251     select pre-expand assign to input-file.
000252     select post-expand assign to "in1.fil,BSAM".
000515 DATA DIVISION.
000520
000530 FILE SECTION.
000531 FD  pre-expand.
000532 01  pre-byte pic x.
000533 FD  post-expand.
000534 01  post-byte pic x.
000793 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000794 01  last-byte pic x value spaces.
000820 01  done-flag pic x value spaces.
000830     88 all-done value "Y".
000840 01  current-column pic 9(3) value 1.
000850 01  remm pic 9(3) value zeros.
000900 LINKAGE SECTION.
000910 01  INPUT-FILE PIC X(80).
005560 PROCEDURE DIVISION USING INPUT-FILE.
005910 MAIN-LOGIC.
005911     move space to last-byte done-flag
005912* expand tabs, remove extra X"0D" characters.
005913     open input pre-expand
005914          output post-expand
005915     move space to done-flag
005916     perform until all-done
005917         read pre-expand
005918              at end set all-done to true
005919              not at end
005920                  evaluate pre-byte also last-byte
005921                     when x"09" also any
005922                          move space to last-byte
005923                          compute remm = 8 - (function rem
(current-column 8))
005924                          if remm = 8
005925                             move zeros to remm
005926                          end-if
005927                          add 1 to remm
005928                          perform remm  times
005929                             write post-byte from last-byte
005930                             add 1 to current-column
005931                          end-perform
005932                     when x"0D" also X"0D"
005938                          continue
005939                     when x"0A" also x"0D"
005940                          move pre-byte to last-byte
005941                          write post-byte from pre-byte
005942                          move 1 to current-column
005943                     when other
005944                          move pre-byte to last-byte
005945                          write post-byte from pre-byte
005946                          add 1 to current-column
005947                  end-evaluate
005948          end-read
005949     end-perform
005950     close post-expand pre-expand
005951     move space to done-flag
005952     open input post-expand output pre-expand
005953     perform until all-done
005954       read post-expand at end set all-done to true
005955            not at end write pre-byte from post-byte
005956       end-read
005957     end-perform
005958     close post-expand pre-expand
005959     GOBACK
005960     .



robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message news:<3e693cb3.62440919@news.optonline.net>...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Form Feed

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-08T20:27:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e6a3fc1.128768484@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2606427.1046875381@dbforums.com> <2615982.1047043612@dbforums.com> <3e693cb3.62440919@news.optonline.net> <bfdfc3e8.0303080947.65522add@posting.google.com>`

```
thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) wrote:

>Robert - you mentioned Tab expansion.  Here's some code (Fujitsu) that
>expands tabs on Windows - using the "windows" and "unix" method.  I
>know there are two different ways - this one has worked in every real
>case I have encountered.

The program is expanding tabs 'the word processor way', i.e. a tab stop every
eight positions. Spreadsheets don't follow that convention. They put a single
tab between fields. The name for this is "tab delimited". My inputs use both
conventions, and occasionally a mixture of the two within a single document.

I figure out which they are using by first trying the most common. In this
application, that's spreadsheet. If I detect columns aren't lining up, I back up
and try it the word processor way. When that doesn't work, I try treating 'all
spaces' as tabs. 

A little background. My inputs are not in a standard format. They are in
thousands of report-like formats designed by tens of thousands of secretaries
using spreadsheets, Crystal Reports, canned software, etc. I see the raw file
they submitted. Logical pages are sometimes sliced down the middle, with the
right side of the page stuck at the bottom. In other cases, every line is
'folded' onto two. Or only big lines are folded.  Sometimes they're rotated 90
degrees, with columns running down the page rather than across. Some reports are
'two up', where the right side of the page _belongs_ at the bottom. In the case
of bonds, interest rate and maturity (which are needed to identify the specific
bond) way be in separate columns or embedded in the description, sometimes in US
format (mdy), other times international. Some reports are .pdf format.
Unbelievably, 8% contain footing errors because the secretary forgot to hit
Recalc. 

Programming languages were designed for deterministic formats. Handling this
sort of ambiguity _with 100% accuracy_ is challenging.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol Form Feed

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-03-08T09:39:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0303080939.327df8ad@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2606427.1046875381@dbforums.com> <2615982.1047043612@dbforums.com>`

```
While 1 byte sequential reads seem to border on "Silly" - Fujitsu's
sequential access speed is without peer if you use ,BSAM in your
assign.

ie  select some-file assign "the.txt,BSAM".

You'll find it quite fast.

However, if you are using v4.2 or later the same bytestream IO as
supported with MicroFocus is supported by Fujitsu - but the data
declarations are slightly different.  I'm talking about the
CBL_OPEN_FILE, CBL_READ.... etc.

Here are my two copybooks for each - Fujitsu first, then MicroFocus:

      *> Fuj Values
       01 infile-name pic x(64).
       01 access-mode PIC 9(4) BINARY.
       01 deny-mode PIC 9(4) BINARY.
       01 P-device PIC 9(4) BINARY.
       01 infile-handle PIC X(4).
       01 status-code PIC S9(4) COMP-5.
       01 infile-details.
          03  infile-len pic 9(18) binary.
          03  filler      pic x(14).
       01 infile-offset PIC 9(18) BINARY.
       01 in-buff-end PIC 9(9) BINARY.
       01 flags PIC 9(4) BINARY.

MF:


       78 fals value 0.
       78 tru  value 1.

       78 in-buff-len      value 2048.
       78 out-buff-len     value 2048.
       01 in-buff          pic x(in-buff-len) value low-values.
       01 out-buff         pic x(in-buff-len) value low-values.

       01 cmd-line         pic x(128) value low-values.
       01 infile-name      pic x(64) value spaces.
       01 infile-handle    pic x(4).
       01 infile-offset    pic x(8) comp-x value 0.
       01 infile-details.
          05 infile-len       pic x(8) comp-x.
          05 filler           pic x(8).
       01 outfile-name     pic x(64).
       01 outfile-handle   pic x(4).
       01 outfile-offset   pic x(8) comp-x value 0.

       01 out-buff-ptr     pic 9(4) comp-x.
       01 out-buff-end     pic x(4) comp-x value out-buff-len.
       01 out-line-ptr     pic 9(4) comp-x.
       01 in-buff-ptr      pic 9(4) comp-x.
       01 in-buff-end      pic x(4) comp-x value in-buff-len.
       01 next-byte        pic x.

       01 eof-flag         pic 9(2) comp-x value fals.
           88 eof                  value tru.
           88 not-eof              value fals.
       01 last-block-flag      pic 9(2) comp-x value fals.
           88 last-block           value tru.
       01 first-block-flag      pic 9(2) comp-x value tru.
           88 first-block               value tru.
           88 not-first-block           value fals.

       01 deny-mode        pic 9(2) comp-x value 0.
       01 P-device           pic 9(2) comp-x value 0.
       01 flags            pic 9(2) comp-x value 0.
       01 access-mode      pic 9(2) comp-x value 1.
```
