```
One other place I used to see COPY code used in the Procedure Division
was in mainframe CICS programs.  In our applications we had a standard
way of checking certain security options (which were over and above
what CICS could do) and we coded this in a COPY book which was
included in all of our online 1st screen processing modules. 

But that was abandoned about 5 years ago when we beefed up our user
security and used a different front end to check such things.

Regards,
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-11T13:56:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<A116DE01CB5F088D.A5BBECD18893463D.D7BC9707658DAB7C@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b4b2e9$rtu$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6ba7ca_1@127.0.0.1> <b4gbtd$r44$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <104A1D324DFAE16C.34A63F8DEC3F8901.205C85A5A0AD8D07@lp.airnews.net> <3e6d3e63_2@127.0.0.1>`

```
On Tue, 11 Mar 2003 02:03:58 -0000, "Peter E.C. Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> enlightened us:

>Fair enough. Can you see any reason why this code could not have been
>compiled and then CALLed?
…[62 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Calling, linking etc. from CICS programs adds overhead and can cause a
transaction to slow down.  In many of the sites where our software
runs we are obligated by contract to have no more than 7 second
response time on a teller transaction.  Anything we could do in the
code to speed things along insured that compliance.  Not so much of a
trade off to have code in a copy book as opposed to calling or linking
to another program.  The code is standardized either way.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-12T00:03:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e6e6ed9.139024112@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b4b2e9$rtu$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6ba7ca_1@127.0.0.1> <b4gbtd$r44$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <104A1D324DFAE16C.34A63F8DEC3F8901.205C85A5A0AD8D07@lp.airnews.net> <3e6d3e63_2@127.0.0.1> <A116DE01CB5F088D.A5BBECD18893463D.D7BC9707658DAB7C@lp.airnews.net>`

```
SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote:

>Calling, linking etc. from CICS programs adds overhead and can cause a
>transaction to slow down.  In many of the sites where our software
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>to another program.  The code is standardized either way.

Seven seconds!! The mainframe standard used to be _two_ seconds. In the worlds
of client-server, PC and Unix, 100 milliseconds is thought to be the threshold
of acceptable performance. Some Web sites, such as Google, deliver
sub-millisecond response to millions of non-trivial transactions per day. 

In the '80s, running on an entry-level 4361 with 1 MIPS speed (roughly 10 MHz,
equivalent to a 286), I had average CICS response time of .9 seconds with 100
terminals attached and 20-30 active. The speed of today's mainframes is
300-1,000 MIPS .. roughly  500 times faster,  Why has the bar been lowered to
three times slower? It should have been raised to at least match competing
technologies. 

FWIW, the contract should specify standard deviation as well as average. Users
are irritated more by erratic response times than by slow. My .9 had a standard
deviation of .2. Generally, sigma should be no more than 25% of mean. 

Robert
```

```
> deliver sub-millisecond response to millions of non-trivial transactions
> per day.

'No more than' implies a never to be exceeded maximum.  'standard', 
'usual', 'average' are discussing something completely different.

> FWIW, the contract should specify standard deviation as well as average.

Please try again when you have noticed the actual wording.
```

- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3e6e6ed9.139024112@news.optonline.net> <b4m0f4$128$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e6e988f.149703503@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
> 
>>> SkippyPB wrote:
>>>>runs we are obligated by contract to have no more than 7 second
>>>>response time on a teller transaction.

>>'No more than' implies a never to be exceeded maximum.  'standard',
>>'usual', 'average' are discussing something completely different.
> 
> I would hope that management at least took Statistics 101. If not, what's
> the point in requiring a college degree?

As usual for you, you make an error in reading and then lash out at 
everyone else because it's their fault.

>>Please try again when you have noticed the actual wording.

> The wording was written by an innumerate dumb-ass who didn't understand

As usual for you, you make an error in reading and then lash out at

Regardless of how you _think_ that performance should be specified, there 
are different ways that it can be done.  It seems that in SkippyPB's case a 
contractural maximum response was used.

In fact a similar way of specifying response times are used in other 
industries.  For example in outsourced warehousing it is typical to specify 
a maximum time in working hours from a despatch order being received to the 
goods being despatched.  Averages and deviation may be interesting, but are 
not particularly useful.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-12T10:45:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3e6e6ed9.139024112@news.optonline.net> <b4m0f4$128$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e6e988f.149703503@news.optonline.net> <b4mbqo$5vj$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>everyone else because it's their fault.

SkippyPB didn't originate the language; it came from his clients. I was lashing
out at their lack of sophistication. 

>As usual for you, you make an error in reading and then lash out at 
>everyone else because it's their fault.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>not particularly useful.

Average and deviation are the only valid statistical measures. By specifying
maximum time at 3-5 sigma, they are guaranteeing acceptable performance within a
limited range of observations. They are giving the appearance of being 'tough'
while, in fact, being quite permissive. 

I'd prefer to see valid metrics. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-13T19:55:07+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b4nsbr$qfr$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b4m0f4$128$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e6e988f.149703503@news.optonline.net> <b4mbqo$5vj$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e6f0913.178511760@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> Average and deviation are the only valid statistical measures. 

What arrant nonsense.  As usual, Mr Wagner, you claim that your ideas are 
the only valid ones and anything different 'lacks sophistication', or is 
just wrong.

> By
> specifying maximum time at 3-5 sigma, they are guaranteeing acceptable
> performance within a limited range of observations. They are giving the
> appearance of being 'tough' while, in fact, being quite permissive.
 
You didn't seem to think that 7 seconds was 'tough', in fact you raved 
about how you could run the whole banking operation in 100 milliseconds, or 
something - I wasn't paying much attention to your usual bragging.

By specifying a particular measurement one is determining, not only what 
the outcome should be, but also how it may be arrived at.

If an average is given then there may be a very large number of 
transactions of one type, by improving these there is a reduction in the 
average.  If there are a very small number of transactions of another type 
they can be quite slow without affecting the average and without making 
much impact on the deviation.

The customer has probably considered this, and by setting a maximum there 
are two effects: it ensures that the slowest transactions are made fast; 
and queueing theory would demonstrate that improving all transactions is 
required to achieve this.

> I'd prefer to see valid metrics.

You prefer to see everyone agreeing with you. and being grateful for the 
opportunity to do so, otherwsie you pick up your toys and go home 
complaining that 'they quibble'.

It never even occurs to you that other viewpoints are valid.
```

```
On 12-Mar-2003, Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

> If an average is given then there may be a very large number of
> transactions of one type, by improving these there is a reduction in the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> required to achieve this.

On any on-line transaction, the customer virtually always prefers for us to
speed up the transactions where he notices a wait over speeding up the many
transaction that are fast enough that he doesn't notice a significant delay.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-13T01:48:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e6fcb1c.228192610@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b4m0f4$128$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e6e988f.149703503@news.optonline.net> <b4mbqo$5vj$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e6f0913.178511760@news.optonline.net> <b4nsbr$qfr$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>just wrong.

In all the traditional (e.g. weights and measures) QA work I did, the government
standard was always about averages rather than worst cases. For instance, when
evaluating a container labeled '16 oz', it did not say 'no sample shall be less
than 15.9 oz', it said 'the average weight shall be 16 oz with a standard
deviation of .1 oz'. That permitted a 15.5 oz containter to pass if it was the
only one out of 200 million. 

Perhaps you should write letters of complaint about the arrant nonsense of such
standards. You might reinvent the field of statistics ... while basking in the
glory of watching them use 'Richard's law'.

It was done once before, by the person who invented the 'Student's T Test'. He
posted the thing anonymously, signing it 'A Student'. In fact, he was a
professional statistician working for the Guiness brewery. He didn't reveal his
name out of fear he'd get into trouble with his bosses, who might accuse him of
wasting time developing such an abstraction. He was Dilbert in the 18th century.


>> By
>> specifying maximum time at 3-5 sigma, they are guaranteeing acceptable
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>something - I wasn't paying much attention to your usual bragging.

Consumers will surely notice the difference between 100ms and 7 seconds (or 2
seconds). 

Do try to pay attention. Your ripostes will be more damaging if they rebut my
stupid postings directly rather just calling me names. 

>By specifying a particular measurement one is determining, not only what 
>the outcome should be, but also how it may be arrived at.

Your cause is not advanced by dangling prepositions.

>If an average is given then there may be a very large number of 
>transactions of one type, by improving these there is a reduction in the 
>average.  If there are a very small number of transactions of another type 
>they can be quite slow without affecting the average and without making 
>much impact on the deviation.

Slow transactions will show up in the deviation if they are more than 2%.

>The customer has probably considered this, and by setting a maximum there 
>are two effects: it ensures that the slowest transactions are made fast; 
>and queueing theory would demonstrate that improving all transactions is 
>required to achieve this.

You credit the customer with more sophistication than he possesses. In truth,
he's a dumb-ass who doesn't know how to measure software performance. 

>> I'd prefer to see valid metrics.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>It never even occurs to you that other viewpoints are valid.

Did it ever occur to you that all viewpoints are not equally valid? Some are
simply sub-standard. People who say 'it's just a matter of style' are usually
defending an otherwise defensless sub-standard technique. They try to allay
criticism by calling it style rather than a technique. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-13T14:58:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b4q6ak$4n8$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b4m0f4$128$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e6e988f.149703503@news.optonline.net> <b4mbqo$5vj$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e6f0913.178511760@news.optonline.net> <b4nsbr$qfr$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e6fcb1c.228192610@news.optonline.net>`

```

On 12-Mar-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> >It never even occurs to you that other viewpoints are valid.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> criticism by calling it style rather than a technique.

But which ones are sub-standard are not constant in all environments.

A government making sure you are not intentionally cheating millions of
customers out of � of a cent in interest has a different goal - and different
measurements than does a customer who could care less about that half penny.

A systems programmer who wants to make his batch jobs run before midnight has
different measurements to be concerned with than the data entry operator who
simply does not want to wait for a response before typing in the next line of
data.

Which is the fastest way to travel?   It depends on the goal.   A rocket can get
me around the world in a hurry, provided I am willing to train for 2 years and
wait for a flight time.   But if all I want is to pick up a shirt at the sale on
the other side of town, a bicycle would have been quicker.

One can raise the average speed on the local freeway by increasing the speed
limit - but the people complaining about rush hour deadlock will still be
complaining with this new higher average not helping them a bit.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-15T01:56:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e726489.398567284@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b4m0f4$128$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e6e988f.149703503@news.optonline.net> <b4mbqo$5vj$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e6f0913.178511760@news.optonline.net> <b4nsbr$qfr$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e6fcb1c.228192610@news.optonline.net> <b4q6ak$4n8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e714ba7.326650338@news.optonline.net> <b4sq17$osr$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 13-Mar-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>statistical measurement.   While I didn't ask which definition of "average",
you
>meant - I was showing that there are valid reasons to be concerned with all
>sorts of response times.   Use the statistic which is useful to determine how
to
>make your customers happy.

In statistical circles, the widely accepted norm is mean (average) and standard
deviation. Using non-standard measures makes it impossible to compare across
platforms, or between two applications on the same platform. Also, it leads to
miscommunication, because readers assume you're using the norm when you're
actually using some other measure.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-14T22:39:37-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<texca.349$yX1.97849@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b4m0f4$128$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e6e988f.149703503@news.optonline.net> <b4mbqo$5vj$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e6f0913.178511760@news.optonline.net> <b4nsbr$qfr$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e6fcb1c.228192610@news.optonline.net> <b4q6ak$4n8$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e714ba7.326650338@news.optonline.net> <b4sq17$osr$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e726489.398567284@news.optonline.net>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
> In statistical circles, the widely accepted norm is mean (average) and
standard
> deviation. Using non-standard measures makes it impossible to compare
across
> platforms, or between two applications on the same platform. Also, it
leads to
> miscommunication, because readers assume you're using the norm when you're
> actually using some other measure.

So fucking what?  You can run in statistical circles all you want, but the
rest of us are getting sick and tired of your nonsense.  A *LIMIT* is just
that.  Do they post road signs saying "mean speed 60 with a standard
deviation 12.6?"  I am sure a cop would be real impressed if you told them
that a speed limit was posted wrong, and that unless he issued it with a
standard deviation he was a fool, and not worthy of being a cop.

You cannot compare the figures for two machines when you only run it on one,
regardless of what you measure. Measuring stuff for the sake of a rule, then
throwing it out after collecting it, strikes me as aparticularly inane way
to spend your time ... though maybe in your case, it is time well spent.

Did it ever occur to you that you have been getting this sort of work for
years becasue nobody wanted you anywhere near a computer that did anything
usefull?

Donald
TGIF.


Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

```
>Robert Wagner wrote:

…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

In that case, see my response to Howard Brazee. 

>> You will defend that as a 'question of style'; 
>

Of course not, nor could I easily find them in DejaNews .. if I cared to spend

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>bullying with "lacks sophistication" or "dumb-ass", or "101".

Which invites them to rebut. Not by name-calling but by posting cogent
argumentation. I admit to having emotions, like everyone else. When I see
something stupid, I sometimes react the easy way -- by calling it 'stupid' or
'dumb-ass' -- rather than constructing a well-reasoned argument against it. In
this ephemeral media, that's sometimes more cost-effective than spending the
time to research all alternatives. Besides, the cheap shot is more gratifying ..
as you have amply demonstrated here. 

>It seems that you stonewall all opposing arguments until they give up, 
>and/or just insult you (as you have indicated several times).  You them 
>claim another victory (as you had indicated).  I was just wondering how 
>many of them called you a fascist.

>Perhaps you could work out why I asked.

I think I have it worked out. You're asking how many of my adversaries were

imbicile or nazi, no matter how I responded. The answer is very few on the
internet, more in real-world corporate politics, where the real pros practice
their trade. 

Are you using a Socratic approach to educating me by asking questions?

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-14T21:06:44+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b4qkvc$nf$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b4mbqo$5vj$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e6f0913.178511760@news.optonline.net> <b4nsbr$qfr$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e6fcb1c.228192610@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> In all the traditional (e.g. weights and measures) QA work I did, the
> government standard was always about averages rather than worst cases. For
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> containter to pass if it was the only one out of 200 million.

What you failed to understand is that there is a differene between 'Quality 
Control' and 'Quality Assurance'.

If there was QC in the factory they would have a weighing device in the 
production line and any under weight would be discovered and rejected.  The 
rejects would be used for promotion or recycled.

As this QC is mechanistic there is no requirement to involve any 
statistical work, so you, or indeed any computer people, would not be 
involved at all, and thus would not know it occurred.

With QA, as you say, the under weights are not discarded but are 
distributed.  Samples are taken and analysed, the calculations are used to 
determine how many should have been rejected, and more importantly, the 
probability of anyone noticing and complaining about it.

Both mechanisms have costs.  The QC has the direct cost of the rejects, the 
QA has the direct cost of the sampling and the indirect costs of the 
complaints, possible government fines, and customer dissatisfaction.

With QC there may still need to be some sampling as proof that the QC is 
working.

Both are _valid_ means of obtaining quality (of the labeling),  the 
_choice_ of which to use depends on the manufacturer and which costs he is 
prepared to bear.

In the case of the response times, the customer also has a choice of having 
the system give some approximation of QA figures, or of having a quality 
control mechanism.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-14T03:54:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e714119.323947559@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b4mbqo$5vj$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e6f0913.178511760@news.optonline.net> <b4nsbr$qfr$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e6fcb1c.228192610@news.optonline.net> <b4qkvc$nf$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>If there was QC in the factory they would have a weighing device in the 
>production line and any under weight would be discovered and rejected.  The 
>rejects would be used for promotion or recycled.

Yes I have seen that on production lines runnning up to 2K units per minute. 

>With QA, as you say, the under weights are not discarded but are 
>distributed.  Samples are taken and analysed, the calculations are used to 
>determine how many should have been rejected, and more importantly, the 
>probability of anyone noticing and complaining about it.

No, QA is ideally brought in when the line is started. It measures deviation of
early outputs and suggests adjusting filling heads to minimize deviation. 

The best solution is preventing errors rather than defending them after the
fact.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-15T09:33:03+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b4s0lh$j9c$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b4nsbr$qfr$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e6fcb1c.228192610@news.optonline.net> <b4qkvc$nf$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e714119.323947559@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> No, QA is ideally brought in when the line is started. It measures
> deviation of early outputs and suggests adjusting filling heads to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> the fact.

Well of course it is best to adjust the line to make as few as possible 
that are under weight.  Making them over weight is less of an issue, first 
of all there is no room for the extra, and secondly there will not be many 
complaints.

However there is still a difference in what hapens to the small number that 
are under a specified figure.  QC rejects them, QA distributes them because 
it never knows exactly which ones they are, only a statistical 'guess' at 
how many there will be.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-13T01:53:03
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e6fdecf_2@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b4b2e9$rtu$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6ba7ca_1@127.0.0.1> <b4gbtd$r44$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <104A1D324DFAE16C.34A63F8DEC3F8901.205C85A5A0AD8D07@lp.airnews.net> <3e6d3e63_2@127.0.0.1> <A116DE01CB5F088D.A5BBECD18893463D.D7BC9707658DAB7C@lp.airnews.net>`

```

"SkippyPB" <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote in message
news:A116DE01CB5F088D.A5BBECD18893463D.D7BC9707658DAB7C@lp.airnews.net...
> On Tue, 11 Mar 2003 02:03:58 -0000, "Peter E.C. Dashwood"
> <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> enlightened us:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> transaction to slow down.

Well, it CAN, but it doesn't HAVE to...

It depends on how you set up your environment. There ARE overheads if you
use CICS commands to CALL and LINK (like XCTL) but there is NO overhead if
you simply CALL a statically linked module (it is preloaded along with your
other code).
> In many of the sites where our software
> runs we are obligated by contract to have no more than 7 second
> response time on a teller transaction.

Most of the mainframe TP systems I worked on required 4 second response as
stated in the SLA. (And we achieved it. In CICS and IMS/DC environments.
(TaskMaster was faster and so was Shadow but these TP monitors went the way
of all things not IBM...)

>Anything we could do in the
> code to speed things along insured that compliance.  Not so much of a
> trade off to have code in a copy book as opposed to calling or linking
> to another program.  The code is standardized either way.

I disagree. However, I DO agree that the difference is slight enough (in the
mainframe environment) not to argue about. The main advantage of using
compiled code (as opposed to the COPY book) in this environment has nothing
to do with performance (which will be much the same either way). The
advantage is that the code is encapsulated and requires no other COPY books
(like a set of WS variables). This means less "bits and pieces" to be
maintained, version controlled, etc.

I remain unconvinced of the need for COPY books in Procedure Division under
NORMAL CIRCUMSTANCES (I accept that generated code and Declaratives
(maybe...) are special cases. <G>

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-13T10:33:32-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<C979C2129A337B0D.F710B3E9532B6494.785F413C7E02438D@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b4b2e9$rtu$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6ba7ca_1@127.0.0.1> <b4gbtd$r44$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <104A1D324DFAE16C.34A63F8DEC3F8901.205C85A5A0AD8D07@lp.airnews.net> <3e6d3e63_2@127.0.0.1> <A116DE01CB5F088D.A5BBECD18893463D.D7BC9707658DAB7C@lp.airnews.net> <3e6fdecf_2@127.0.0.1>`

```
On Thu, 13 Mar 2003 01:53:03 -0000, "Peter E.C. Dashwood"
<dashwood@enternet.co.nz> enlightened us:

>
>"SkippyPB" <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote in message
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I guess I should have been more specific.  First, we don't use COPY
books in our Procedure Division any more.  Next, the case I was
discussing was from the early 80's, we were running on a 4381 with
VSE/VM and VSAM was our main file system.  The particular contract
that required 7 seconds was negotiated that way because we were
running 17 banks each with a various number of branches through the
system.  Most were using Bunker Ramo teller devices and the
controllers (which were located in the branches) could be slowed by
things out of our control.  Our typical response time from mainframe
to controller was 2-3 seconds.  However, those controllers could hold
up sending the response on to the proper teller for up to 4 seconds,
hence to protect ourselves, we negotiated and got the 7 seconds put in
the contract.

Also, the CICS and VSE under VM that we were running at the time could
not do straight calls.  You had to do LINK and/or XCTL.  Hence the use
of a COPYBOOK instead.

As the technology got better and we upgraded hardware and operating
systems, the copybooks went away and an entirely, more effecient front
end that did what was in the copybook for all of our transactions was
put in place.  Also newer teller equipment in the form of PCs plugged
into a Novell network and network/gateway devices eased many of those
old problems with response time.  Our teller stuff is currently
topping out at 1 second response time at our biggest client (550
branches).  

>Most of the mainframe TP systems I worked on required 4 second response as
>stated in the SLA. (And we achieved it. In CICS and IMS/DC environments.
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>---= 19 East/West-Coast Specialized Servers - Total Privacy via Encryption =---

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Micro$oft Haiku Error Message #109

Program aborting:
Close all that you have worked on.
You ask far too much.

~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e7130f6.319815971@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b4b2e9$rtu$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6ba7ca_1@127.0.0.1> <b4gbtd$r44$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <104A1D324DFAE16C.34A63F8DEC3F8901.205C85A5A0AD8D07@lp.airnews.net> <3e6d3e63_2@127.0.0.1> <A116DE01CB5F088D.A5BBECD18893463D.D7BC9707658DAB7C@lp.airnews.net> <3e6fdecf_2@127.0.0.1> <C979C2129A337B0D.F710B3E9532B6494.785F413C7E02438D@lp.airnews.net>`

```
kippyPB <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote:

> the case I was
>discussing was from the early 80's, we were running on a 4381 with
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>the contract.

That makes more sense. Assuming the Bunker Ramo 'cluster controllers' were like
3274s, they were programmable devices, although programming them was an Excedrin
headache. I considered programming 3274s several times, but never did. Then I
found a third-party 3274 drop-in replacement which used two Intel 8088s running
MSDOS. It was a PC (board) programmed to act like a 3274. Programming it was a
breeze. 

>Also, the CICS and VSE under VM that we were running at the time could
>not do straight calls.  You had to do LINK and/or XCTL.  Hence the use
>of a COPYBOOK instead.

They could have done straight calls to statically linked programs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-14T13:09:35-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4F1BE9EA5A8DB7A3.66F66713BD3BD8BF.C08FBF955231E923@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b4b2e9$rtu$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6ba7ca_1@127.0.0.1> <b4gbtd$r44$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <104A1D324DFAE16C.34A63F8DEC3F8901.205C85A5A0AD8D07@lp.airnews.net> <3e6d3e63_2@127.0.0.1> <A116DE01CB5F088D.A5BBECD18893463D.D7BC9707658DAB7C@lp.airnews.net> <3e6fdecf_2@127.0.0.1> <C979C2129A337B0D.F710B3E9532B6494.785F413C7E02438D@lp.airnews.net> <3e7130f6.319815971@news.optonline.net>`

```
On Fri, 14 Mar 2003 03:54:53 GMT, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
enlightened us:

>kippyPB <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote:
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't remember what the model number was but I do no the code for
the controller was written in Fortran of all things.  I was the only
programmer in a shop of 30 that knew Fortran, so guess who got to make
the changes?  As I recall, you made your changes and compiled and
linked the program.  You then transmitted the object code to the
controller which wrote it onto a 5" (or maybe it was 8) floppy which
then got booted into the controller's memory by pushing certain
buttons on the controller.  Needless to say, not many changes were
done to the teller system at that end of it.


>>Also, the CICS and VSE under VM that we were running at the time could
>>not do straight calls.  You had to do LINK and/or XCTL.  Hence the use
>>of a COPYBOOK instead.
>
>They could have done straight calls to statically linked programs. 

I may be wrong, but I'm almost certain in VSE CICS 1.6 and lower, you
could not issue a CALL from a command level program.  Far too long ago
to remember all the specifics.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Micro$oft Haiku Error Message #109

Program aborting:
Close all that you have worked on.
You ask far too much.

~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

```
SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote:

>On Fri, 14 Mar 2003 03:54:53 GMT, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
>enlightened us:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>>That makes more sense. Assuming the Bunker Ramo 'cluster controllers' were
like
>>3274s, they were programmable devices, although programming them was an
Excedrin
>>headache. I considered programming 3274s several times, but never did. Then I
>>found a third-party 3274 drop-in replacement which used two Intel 8088s
running
>>MSDOS. It was a PC (board) programmed to act like a 3274. Programming it was a
>>breeze. 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>done to the teller system at that end of it.

Sounds very familiar. IBM used 8" floppies which ran 24/7, eventually wearing a
bald spot into the disk. 

My point was, you could have eliminated the 4 sec controller delay by fixing the
problem in the controller. Then politicians could have negotiated a more
reasonable 3 second max response in place of 7 seconds. 

>>>Also, the CICS and VSE under VM that we were running at the time could
>>>not do straight calls.  You had to do LINK and/or XCTL.  Hence the use
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>to remember all the specifics.

EXEC CICS statements all translated to calls. Yes, you could do calls from CICS
programs. 

Robert
```

```
That's true.  But if you need to execute a CICS API command from the

right now.
```

```
>Anyway, If anybody is still using COPY in the Procedure Division of their

I use Procedure Division COPY members for the same reason that I was 
coding two-digit year fields in dates in 1997: because the person who 
signed/signs my timesheets indicates that This Is What To Do.
```

```
Mr Dashwood, please note that I did not say I refrained from commenting, I 
did not say that I refrained from offering an alternative method and I did 
not say that I refrained from questioning the intelligence of folks who 
told me to code this way... I simply said that I coded as my client 
requested that I do (at least to a point where what I call my 
'professional integrity' remains uncompromised; there *have* been things 
I've refused to code).

As for 'model emoloyee'... I've found that the best 'employee spirit' in 
terms of dedication to work and searching for innovative ways to solve 
problems is often found in 'employees' who have - as I did - turned 
consultant/contractor/hired gun.
```

```
On 10-Mar-2003, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> >All very well, but at the end of the day you do what they ask.
>
> That's what I'm usually paid to do, aye.

I have a co-worker who found it much easier to be a contractor for two reasons:

1.   He doesn't have to fight being promoted to being one of them.
2.   He doesn't feel as strong ownership - making it easier to do what they ask
(after recommending a better way of doing it).

I worked with him as an employee (we both worked for Bill Gate's future
sister-in-law), and we have both been contractors and employees since then.  Now
he's very happy being a contractor and taking months off if he wants.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

```
In article <b4il59$5b1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
>On 10-Mar-2003, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>1.   He doesn't have to fight being promoted to being one of them.

This was a Very Large Factor in my deciding to go The Consulting Route... 
I found out that somewhere - I think in the Book of Leviticus - it is 
written 'Thou shalt not pay a good programmer more than a bad manager.'

A consultant/contractor/hired gun, however, is another story, entire... 
and I wanted to reach my middle age doing something I do well, doing 
something that showed results and doing something that I enjoy... all the 
while providing value *and* making a decent living at, as well.

Make sure the papers get from one side of the desk to the other?  Nah.  
Don the mantle of (echo chamber on) CCCAAAPPPTTTAAAAIIIINNNNN... COBOL... 
OBOl... OBol... Obol and go from contract to contract?  Yeah, sure, why 
not.

(Might soon be time to start pavement-pounding again, though... at my 
present site there are two departments where the managers love my work... 
and no departments where the managers seem willing to continue paying for 
it.)

>2.   He doesn't feel as strong ownership - making it easier to do what they ask
>(after recommending a better way of doing it).

I feel *very* strong ownership, even after being told to write utter 
garbage-code... damnation, at least it is going to be the best 
garbage-code one can hope to find.

>
>I worked with him as an employee (we both worked for Bill Gate's future
>sister-in-law), and we have both been contractors and employees since then.  Now
>he's very happy being a contractor and taking months off if he wants.

Months off?  Hmmmm... in addition to the tawdry financial matters involved 
I don't think I am yet creative enough to do such things... gotta *do* 
something and all that.

DD
```

```
In article <b4ioa1$6i1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
>On 10-Mar-2003, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>He's got horses, a machine shop, and hunts.

Well, then... all he needs is a boat (def'n: 'A hole in the water that you 
throw money into') and he's all set.

>
>You and I will need to find advocations when we win the lottery and retire from
>our vocations.  We either continue living (some kind of "work"), or we start
>dying.   He already has a life that keeps him busy enough.

I was taught, long ago, that it takes a very creative mind to handle 
unlimited leisure without self-destructing... granted that I, personally, 
haven't been able to test this but it seems to make sense.

There was a song I heard, long ago - the Georgia Sea Island Singers?  the 
Real Bahamas? - called, I think, 'Take Me Over the Tide'; it contained the 
line of 'When my work on earth has ended and I cannot work any longer, 
Lord, take me over the tide'... so as long as I'm breathing there's work 
to be done.

What *kind* of work could I do as a lottery-winner?  No idea... and in 
order to find that out I'd have to pay Stupid Tax... errrr, buy lottery 
tickets in order to find out.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

```
On 10-Mar-2003, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> What *kind* of work could I do as a lottery-winner?  No idea... and in
> order to find that out I'd have to pay Stupid Tax... errrr, buy lottery
> tickets in order to find out.

Not me.  I've got a wife who enjoys them.   I don't see what she spends on them,
but have gone to nice restaurants a couple of times when she has won.   But
really - it's no worse than if she had spent the money on a Big Gulp.   Tickets
are essentially free when we don't change our budget to buy them.

Personally, I am ahead on lottery.  I went to an expo to take a look at the
brand new Commodore 64 computer and was given some lottery tickets at the door.
One of them put me a couple of dollars ahead, and I aim to stay that way.
```

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-11T02:04:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e6d43c2.62444934@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3e6bbfbc$1_1@127.0.0.1> <b4gv1q$akh$1@panix1.panix.com> <b3638c46.0303100645.7e7266cb@posting.google.com> <b4ik00$qa6$1@panix1.panix.com> <b4il59$5b1$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

> Now he's very happy being a contractor and taking months off if he wants.

Contractors get to take months off, even if they _don't_ want to. 

In the good old days (before Y2K), I could find the next adventure within a week
or two. Now it takes months, and each time the pay rate (but not the bill rate)
is lower. I know .. supply and demand. I've had the good fortune to draw a
mid-to-high rate, but I know it cannot last. 

In the old days, contractors made a lot more money then employees .. about
double if they did it right (using per diem to tax-shelter half their income).
Now they make about the same, because of unemployment between gigs. Putting a
price on risk, uncertainty and stress makes contracting _less_ remunerative than
employment. 

I used to say, "My future lies not on the good-will of one employer; it rests on
the vibrancy of the marketplace." Well, the market has stopped vibrating.

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

- **From:** "Don Leahy" <xdb2imsN@nospam.whatever.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-10T09:17:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<X71ba.1443$4q6.242214@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b4b2e9$rtu$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6ba7ca_1@127.0.0.1>`

```

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3e6ba7ca_1@127.0.0.1...
> I've watched this bewilderment. <G>
>
> Can anybody clarify for me why you would WANT to COPY into the Procedure
> Division?
>
<Snip>

Years ago we made heavy use of copy codes in our Telon programs.  For that
product, it was the path of least resistance because it uses procedure
division copybooks to embed your hand-coded Cobol into its generated Cobol.

Another use is to standardize parameter lists, as in

    CALL 'WHATEVR' USING COPY LWHATEVR.

In subroutine WHATEVR:

     PROCEDURE DIVISION USING COPY LWHATEVR.

This helps to ensure that the parameter lists always match.

Yet another use is to standardize calls to frequently used subroutines.  As
in:

     PERFORM Z999-LOG-ERROR
      . . . .

     COPY Z999LERR.

The DB2 Smart/Restart product from Relational Architects provides you with
copycodes that you embed in the Procedure Division in order to invoke its
modules.  No doubt there are other vendors that do the same thing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood)
- **Date:** 2003-03-11T02:05:26-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3638c46.0303110205.4d04e2d6@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b4b2e9$rtu$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6ba7ca_1@127.0.0.1> <X71ba.1443$4q6.242214@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Thanks for very interesting response Don. Comments below...

"Don Leahy" <xdb2imsN@nospam.whatever.net> wrote in message news:<X71ba.1443$4q6.242214@news20.bellglobal.com>...
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
> news:3e6ba7ca_1@127.0.0.1...
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I can see a use for COPY when we are talking code generators, because
it is kind of part of the "Pre-compile" process. I can't see a use for
it when cutting our own code. And Telon is in the twilight of its
years now, just like a number of other COBOL code generators... These
things consistently failed to deliver what they promised. The one I
most "love to hate" is Jackson...<G>


 
> Another use is to standardize parameter lists, as in
> 
>     CALL 'WHATEVR' USING COPY LWHATEVR.
>

Well, I've never seen that one (and I have seen most things
COBOL...<G>)

It's an interesting idea but severely limited in the dynamic
environments I normally work in. I try to avoid static linkage,
although I realise there are arguments for both sides of that coin.

It seems to me that the logical extension of this would be to make the
whole CALL...USING statement a COPY, then it is at least consistent.
And anyone looking through COPY books would know what module it refers
to.

 
> 
> Yet another use is to standardize calls to frequently used subroutines.  As
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Yes, I know it is "standard practice" on many sites. I really don't
like this because it means huge duplication of code. If a "subroutine"
is "frequently used" it is a good candidate for being compiled and
CALLed. The argument for using COPY code is based around ease of
maintenance, but this is spurious. A compiled module could do the job
just as well and it could be maintained in ONE place. (This, of
course, is the underlying driver that led to the development of
components.)


> The DB2 Smart/Restart product from Relational Architects provides you with
> copycodes that you embed in the Procedure Division in order to invoke its
> modules.  No doubt there are other vendors that do the same thing.

Now this one I can see the sense in... It still sounds like very tired
technology to me.

Thanks for the points, Don.

I remain unconvinced...<G>.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Don Leahy" <xdb2imsN@nospam.whatever.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-11T11:17:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3_nba.3633$4q6.457888@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b4b2e9$rtu$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6ba7ca_1@127.0.0.1> <X71ba.1443$4q6.242214@news20.bellglobal.com> <b3638c46.0303110205.4d04e2d6@posting.google.com>`

```
"Peter E. C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:b3638c46.0303110205.4d04e2d6@posting.google.com...
> Thanks for very interesting response Don. Comments below...
>
> "Don Leahy" <xdb2imsN@nospam.whatever.net> wrote in message
news:<X71ba.1443$4q6.242214@news20.bellglobal.com>...
> > "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
> > news:3e6ba7ca_1@127.0.0.1...
> > > I've watched this bewilderment. <G>
> > >
> > > Can anybody clarify for me why you would WANT to COPY into the
Procedure
> > > Division?
> > >
> > <Snip>
> >
> > Years ago we made heavy use of copy codes in our Telon programs.  For
that
> > product, it was the path of least resistance because it uses procedure
> > division copybooks to embed your hand-coded Cobol into its generated
Cobol.
> >
> I can see a use for COPY when we are talking code generators, because
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Has *any* code generator *ever* delivered on all of its promises?  In many
shops Telon was introduced as a 'stopgap' solution to help batch Cobol
programmers write IMS/DC (as it was known then) and CICS programs.  I know
of one site that grew disenchanted with Telon and brought in Netron/Cap as a
"replacement".  10 years later, they are still actively using both products.
New screens are being written all the time (though not as many as in the
olden days) using both generators, so I don't see either product going away
any time soon.  Applications targeted at customers are being written as Web
applications, of course, but many internal applications are still green
screen.

>
> > Another use is to standardize parameter lists, as in
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> although I realise there are arguments for both sides of that coin.

By 'static linkage' are you referring to the parameter list being passed?
Or to the CALL itself?

>
> It seems to me that the logical extension of this would be to make the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes, sometimes it was done that way.   CALL 'WHATEVER' USING COPY LWHATEVER
is often in its own copybook.

> >
> > Yet another use is to standardize calls to frequently used subroutines.
As
> > in:
> >
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> components.)

These copybooks were typically used to set up the parameters to call a
subroutine (an error logger that inserts messages to an IMS queue for
example).

>
> > The DB2 Smart/Restart product from Relational Architects provides you
with
> > copycodes that you embed in the Procedure Division in order to invoke
its
> > modules.  No doubt there are other vendors that do the same thing.
>
> Now this one I can see the sense in... It still sounds like very tired
> technology to me.

Tired technology?  Checkpoint/restart is an essential part of any
non-trivial batch process.   No matter what platform you're on.

> Thanks for the points, Don.
>
> I remain unconvinced...<G>.

So do I actually.  I don't necessarily advocate any of the techniques
described.  I was just sharing what I've seen over the years.

>
> Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-10T15:57:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b4ickc$19f$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b4b2e9$rtu$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6ba7ca_1@127.0.0.1>`

```

On  9-Mar-2003, "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> Can anybody clarify for me why you would WANT to COPY into the Procedure
> Division?

I have an interesting example - we have a called program that calculates a
balance against our IDMS database.   Well, if the program is called from an ADSO
screen, it needs to be compiled with DC CoBOL and has to abort differently than
the DB CoBOL program that batch programs use.

Most of the Procedure division is one big copy statement.

We actually have 3 versions of this program - one version is using CoBOL 2
because it is used by a third system that is called by a C program and we
haven't been able to figure out how to get it working with a current compiler.


We also have IDMS COPY statements that get filled out differently depending on
the IDMS records and subschemata we use.  We also have copy statements for
standard aborts and for the paragraphs that IDMS calls to check on status codes.


There are a few other cases where a copy statement sets up some complicated
procedures before calling a different program to create new database records.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-10T17:32:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e6cc8f3.31001775@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b4b2e9$rtu$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6ba7ca_1@127.0.0.1> <b4ickc$19f$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>On  9-Mar-2003, "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>balance against our IDMS database.   Well, if the program is called from an
ADSO
>screen, it needs to be compiled with DC CoBOL and has to abort differently than
>the DB CoBOL program that batch programs use.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>standard aborts and for the paragraphs that IDMS calls to check on status
codes.

Sounds like what you really want is the conditional compilation provided in 2002
COBOL:

evaluate platform
    when ADSO
        ......
    when DB
        ......
end-evaluate
```

```
> >We also have IDMS COPY statements that get filled out differently depending
> >on
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> end-evaluate

Not applicable to the above IDMS copy.

But I'm not quite understanding how it works in my other code.   The three
programs have a few lines different, but their logic is primarily identical. 
One system calls one program name, another system calls a different program
name.  I don't know how the compiler knows the platform (I don't know if the
platform has a name).   But I still need three sets of compiled code, and making
them three different programs seems pretty simple.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-11T02:04:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e6d43cf.62458572@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b4b2e9$rtu$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6ba7ca_1@127.0.0.1> <b4ickc$19f$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6cc8f3.31001775@news.optonline.net> <b4ijr8$4n0$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>On 10-Mar-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
>
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>platform has a name).   But I still need three sets of compiled code, and
making
>them three different programs seems pretty simple.

As in every other language offering conditional compilation, you set platform=x
in the compiler-invoking statement or JCL. This allows you to produce three
different .obj files while maintaining a single source file. Assembly language
programmers have been doing it for 40 years.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood)
- **Date:** 2003-03-11T02:22:16-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3638c46.0303110222.3ab79f0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b4b2e9$rtu$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6ba7ca_1@127.0.0.1> <b4ickc$19f$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6cc8f3.31001775@news.optonline.net> <b4ijr8$4n0$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6d43cf.62458572@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message news:<3e6d43cf.62458572@news.optonline.net>...
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
> 
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> programmers have been doing it for 40 years.

As an old time Assembler programmer I agree that conditional
compilation COULD be an option. But I hated it then and I hate it even
more now. I am very sorry that the new standard has included
conditional COBOL compilation. (It is not like me to actively be
against a feature; usually my attitude would be "OK, I won't use it
but there's no reason why someone else shouldn't..." In this case I
reckon it is positively dangerous).

I remember having terrible trouble with other people's conditional
Assemblies back in the sixties. Maybe I was inexperienced and couldn't
handle it. What ever the reason, the scars have stayed to this day...

Having said all of that, I have to agree that it might be a solution
for Howard's intriguing example.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** dashwood@enternet.co.nz (Peter E. C. Dashwood)
- **Date:** 2003-03-11T02:11:06-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b3638c46.0303110211.4a8f2e2b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b4b2e9$rtu$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6ba7ca_1@127.0.0.1> <b4ickc$19f$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message news:<b4ickc$19f$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...
> On  9-Mar-2003, "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> 
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> procedures before calling a different program to create new database records.

This one is a very good example, Howard. In effect, if I understand
this correctly, you are saying that the same code has to run in
different environments, so it makes sense to maintain it from a common
(COPYed) source.

I think this is the only response I've seen so far that actually makes
the case. I can't think of a better alternative, other than to NOT get
in that situation in the first place <G>, but I realise that in
Reality, we don't always have a choice...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

- **From:** psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-03-10T10:43:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<16e2f010.0303101043.2a79247d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b4b2e9$rtu$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6ba7ca_1@127.0.0.1>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3e6ba7ca_1@127.0.0.1>...
> I've watched this bewilderment. <G>
> 
> Can anybody clarify for me why you would WANT to COPY into the Procedure
> Division?

Because some vendors might package it that way.

In the case of the vendor I was thinking of when I posted it, the
style goes back to the early 1980's.  Even though the source has been
almost completely replaced in the intervening two decades it was done
incrementally and without changes to the packaging style.

> This is like negative evolution...(I think they call it "retarded" in the
> trade...<G>).

Let me say that I agree with you wholeheartedly on this one.  But it
doesn't make the problem go away.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-10T19:19:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b4iogh$6iu$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b4b2e9$rtu$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6ba7ca_1@127.0.0.1> <16e2f010.0303101043.2a79247d@posting.google.com>`

```

On 10-Mar-2003, psychedelic-harry@mindless.com (Joe Zitzelberger) wrote:

> > Can anybody clarify for me why you would WANT to COPY into the Procedure
> > Division?
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> incrementally and without changes to the packaging style.

Which is a primary reason for not assuming that the code within a COPY member
meets shop standards (and thus we can safely not include a full stop period
after such a copy).  Sometimes contracts upgrade copy members - let's say if it
was tax code logic.  They send over the changed copymember and tell us to
recompile the programs that use it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Periods and COPY statements

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-10T11:57:39-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Is3ba.1849$VT.242087@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<b4avqs$b8p$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b4b2e9$rtu$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6ba7ca_1@127.0.0.1>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> >

> Can anybody clarify for me why you would WANT to COPY into the Procedure
> Division?
>

While I use to use three copy statements for each file (select, fd, and all
the procedures), I no longer do.  A few years back, this was the easiest way
to ensure correct methodology accross an entire system, when such things as
file/record locking were required.  Now, of course, I use OOP objects
instead, and the "copy" is done at the object level instead by creating a
new instance of the file handling instance.

However, I still use copy statements, even in OOP, and in one particular
case for procedures.

The SP2 screen system generates copy items for all of the screen parameters
used in any particular screen. Normally, and in the supplied samples, they
are working storage copy elements only.  They are, however, generated by a
scripting language that is designed to produce Cobol code.

I have modified that to also create standard methods for opening and closing
screens, setting the title, updating the screen with an immediate exeit
(write only) and entering the screen, waiting for input, and exiting with a
status code.  These are complete methods, from the method-id statement to
the end-method statement, so the question of periods is irrelevant.

My standard screen handling object simply has some common methods at the
top, and two copy statements per screen.  The first is a working storage
copy that sets up data fields for the screen as Cobol properties.  The
second is a procedural copy that includes the basic methods for each screen.

Here is some code that uses such an object.  Note that the cobol for the
ERROR-OBJECT is copletely generated, and can be moved from system to system
unchanged.  By modifying the code generator, I should be able to generate
code for any cobol on the market that has the ability to compile OOP
comliant code, recompile the error-object, and leave the below code
completely unchanged.  Once eveyone is up to the new standard, I will be
able to leave the code generator alone as well.

The only last thing required, is to make all the Cobol compilers link
compatable.  Then I should be able to put my error-handling OOP DLL on a
system as an application, and use it from within any cobol compiler.  That
is, if the language was specified right in that new standard .... can we do
that, and if not, why not?

       BAD-SEARCH.
           INVOKE LEDDLL1 "NEW" RETURNING ERROR-OBJECT.
           INVOKE ERROR-OBJECT "YESNO-OPEN-THE-SCREEN".
           MOVE SHORT-TITLE TO LOCAL-TITLE OF ERROR-OBJECT.
           MOVE SPACE TO YESNO-MESSAGE-A OF ERROR-OBJECT.
           MOVE "Search Failed !" TO YESNO-MESSAGE-B OF ERROR-OBJECT.
           MOVE "Search again?" TO YESNO-MESSAGE-C OF ERROR-OBJECT.
           INVOKE ERROR-OBJECT "YESNO-SHOW-THE-DATA".
           INVOKE ERROR-OBJECT "YESNO-GET-THE-DATA".
           MOVE SCREEN-KEYSTROKE OF ERROR-OBJECT TO LOCAL-KEYSTROKE.
           IF LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO YES-HIT
               MOVE "N" TO END-FLAG
               MOVE "Y" TO SEARCH-END-FLAG
           ELSE
           IF LOCAL-KEYSTROKE IS EQUAL TO NO-HIT
               MOVE "Y" TO END-FLAG
               MOVE "Y" TO SEARCH-END-FLAG
           ELSE
               MOVE "N" TO END-FLAG
               MOVE "Y" TO SEARCH-END-FLAG.
           INVOKE ERROR-OBJECT "YESNO-CLOSE-THE-SCREEN".
           SET ERROR-OBJECT TO NULL.

Donald
```

## Thread: Selecting specific printer — 4 msgs

### Selecting specific printer

- **From:** pearson@infonegocio.com (Pamela)
- **Date:** 2003-03-09T16:00:23-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a3146a5b.0303091600.59ec6f80@posting.google.com>`

```
Is it possible to select a specific printer within a 
Cobol program running under DOS?
I have tried assigning a printer file and moving
"LPT1" or "LPT2" to it but no output is produced.
Any help would be greatly appreciated.
```

#### ↳ Re: Selecting specific printer

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-10T13:28:17+13:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b4gmcq$mm8$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<a3146a5b.0303091600.59ec6f80@posting.google.com>`

```
Pamela wrote:

> Is it possible to select a specific printer within a
> Cobol program running under DOS?
> I have tried assigning a printer file and moving
> "LPT1" or "LPT2" to it but no output is produced.
> Any help would be greatly appreciated.

Have a look on your disk and se if there is a file 'LPT1' now.

It may be that you have to use LPT1: with a colon.

If you are running in a DOS box under Windows you may also have to ensure 
that the printers are set up to capture print data from DOS programs.
```

#### ↳ Re: Selecting specific printer

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-09T18:50:11-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dGmdnfOy7KopfPajXTWciQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<a3146a5b.0303091600.59ec6f80@posting.google.com>`

```

"Pamela" <pearson@infonegocio.com> wrote in message
news:a3146a5b.0303091600.59ec6f80@posting.google.com...
> Is it possible to select a specific printer within a
> Cobol program running under DOS?
> I have tried assigning a printer file and moving
> "LPT1" or "LPT2" to it but no output is produced.
> Any help would be greatly appreciated.

If your program is running in a DOS box under Windows, RPV (Report Program
Viewer) is a free re-director to take a DOS print file (on disk) and
redirect it through the Windows print routine.
```

## Thread: Heads Up — 23 msgs

### Heads Up

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-10T01:46:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<D7Saa.11894$Oz1.680451@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Someone is trolling this group for email addresses and attempting
to send what I imagine is a hostile attachment via email.
(I checked some of the other 30-plus addressees - they had also posted to CLC.)

The subject of the email is variable, but usually ends in "Microsoft".
The subject of mine was FW: Look at the pack from Microsoft.

In my case, the perp wasted his/her time, as the ATT server 
stripped the email clean.  My email simply read

[Filename: q391562.exe, Content-Type: application/octet-stream]
The attachment file in the message has been removed by eManager

I know most of you are knowledgeable and protected 14 ways from Sunday.
This is just a heads up to those who might not be so prepared.
```

#### ↳ Re: Heads Up

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-10T14:07:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1%0ba.158$Xq4.139395@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<D7Saa.11894$Oz1.680451@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

news:D7Saa.11894$Oz1.680451@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
> Someone is trolling this group for email addresses and attempting
> to send what I imagine is a hostile attachment via email.
> (I checked some of the other 30-plus addressees - they had also posted to
CLC.)
>
> The subject of the email is variable, but usually ends in "Microsoft".
> The subject of mine was FW: Look at the pack from Microsoft.

I got one of those which was addressed as "from" Microsoft, alledgedly
containing some kind of service pack. Very nicely done, too. (I got the one
you described also).

Fortunately I remembered that Microsoft does not send service packs, they
simply announce  and send a link, so  I never even gave my AV software a
chance to prove its worth.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Heads Up

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-11T06:05:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<U0fba.25395$Oz1.834775@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<D7Saa.11894$Oz1.680451@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <1%0ba.158$Xq4.139395@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <iLmdnY7iUfN4c_GjXTWcoA@comcast.com>`

```
Russell Styles <RWS0202@comcast.net> wrote in message news:iLmdnY7iUfN4c_GjXTWcoA@comcast.com...
> 
> I got that, but the attachment seems to be missing.
> 
> Looks legit as far as I can tell.  Might not be the same
> thing you are talking about.


Be assured, it is not legit.  No way.

Microsoft has a stated policy.  They do NOT broadcast attachments via email.

The most likely reason for the attachment being missing is that your ISP
vetted your email, detected the virus, and stripped the attachment from the email.
```

```
TATA Teleservices (India) who acquired it from Hughes. This cannot be faked. 

Hughes Tele.com (I) Ltd
   Ispat House BG Kher Marg Worli
   Mumbai, Maharas 400018
   India

Administrative Contact:

   [same address]

   Jaydeep Sampat (KKUEV) jsampat@hil.hns.com

rich quick' site registered to:

 Serious|Net
 P.O. Box 1300
 Artesia, CA 90702-1300
 US

 Administrative Contact:
    Owner, Domain  rdonald@serious.net
   [same address, no phone]
 Technical Contact:
    Contact, For-Info  contact@serious.net

message originated in India (or Pakistan?), and vnsl is fake. I will write to
Tata and post the results, if any. 
    

Robert
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<D7Saa.11894$Oz1.680451@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <b3638c46.0303110239.5e779188@posting.google.com> <Vdyba.1061$ja4.79875@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote:

>Peter E. C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:b3638c46.0303110239.5e779188@posting.google.com...
>> Thanks for that, Hugh.
>> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>If it was traceable to the originator, surely the authorities would have done
so by now?

The only 'authorities' are network administrators. Governments can't be bothered
to chase bad guys unless they threaten national security. 

The SMTP mail system makes it difficult to completely hide the poster's
identity. A message can almost always be tracked to the mailserver where it was
inserted/originated. The administrator, if he's interested, can look through
logs to identify the individual perp. There used to be 'SMTP anonymizers' who
bypassed controls by using the 'forwarding' feature. That abusive practice has
now been extinguished almost completely. 

The system's weakness is dependence on the good will of administrators. There
are thousands of sites that are poorly administered. Some fly the flag of
familiar, big name companies such as Bell Canada. Complaints supported by
corrigible evidence go into the bit bucket. SMTP authorities should yank
privileges from such sites, but they don't.

In this case, the perp appears to know which big name sites are poorly
administered. I haven't received a reply from Tata nor Bell Canada. 

>> You have to wonder what kind of sad bastard would derive satisfaction
>> out of attacking te contributors to a COBOL forum?
>
>I dunno, but there are a lot of them.  My virus scanner now claims to be
checking
>for over 66,666 viruses.

Nine out of ten are clones of other virii. Egotistical teens wanting to show how
'brilliant' they are slightly modify an existing virus and claim authorship. 

>> I would've thought we must be one of the least threatening and
>> innocous groups on the Internet..<G>
>
>Probably because COBOL isn't the language of choice in the virus development
world?

There's a concept! Write a virus in COBOL that attacks Java programs. It would
be easy because the JVM is not encrypted. :>

Robert
```

- **Message-ID:** `<_yadnURzhbDPvPKjXTWcig@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<D7Saa.11894$Oz1.680451@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <b3638c46.0303110239.5e779188@posting.google.com> <Vdyba.1061$ja4.79875@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3e6efa57.174739082@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net>
>
> The SMTP mail system makes it difficult to completely hide the poster's
> identity. A message can almost always be tracked to the mailserver where
it was
> inserted/originated. The administrator, if he's interested, can look
through
> logs to identify the individual perp. There used to be 'SMTP anonymizers'
who
> bypassed controls by using the 'forwarding' feature. That abusive practice
has
> now been extinguished almost completely.

Not exactly. The only line that can be trusted is the last 'Received from'
address. Spammers often use open relays as injection points with everything
faked. So, all you can trace is back to the open relay.
```

```
"SkippyPB" <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote in message
news:5DAD4C54CEBA4259.1848D2EA89D177CB.CA1DC09BD728588F@lp.airnews.net...
> On Wed, 12 Mar 2003 13:26:16 -0600, "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
> enlightened us:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >The only 'authorities' are network administrators. Governments can't
be
> >bothered
> >> >to chase bad guys unless they threaten national security.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> >AOL gets 1 billion spams a day. The AG of NY filed and won one case in
two
> >years.
> >
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
See: http://itmanagement.earthweb.com/secu/article.php/2091641
(March 5th, 2003)

"Just two weeks after reporting progress in its war on spam, AOL told
e-marketers that spam levels had doubled in the last six months and the AOL
Time Warner (Quote, Company Info) Internet unit would soon block over a
_billion_ unsolicited bulk e-mail messages a day. "

In the MonsterHut/Paetac case, Paetac TRIED to drop MonsterHut. However,
Paetac had executed a "Pink" contract with Monsterhut to allow some
percentage of complaints per day. As Paetec got more and more blocked,
Paetec tried to disconnect MonsterHut. MonsterHut got an injuction to force
Patec to live up to the contract. The AG's involvement with MonsterHut had
nothing to do with MonsterHut's dispute with PaeTec.

As for the claim that "spammers get taken care of when someone goes after
them," it is not possible to "go after them" if the spammer is in Korea,
Brazil, Japan, Hong Kong, Italy, China, yak-yak-yak.

"Legal" solutions to spam don't work, cannot be made to work, and can cause
significant harm if attempted. Here's some downside to legislative
'solutions:'

1. None can work against foreign-host injected spam.
2. Because of legal delay, none can work against a spam spew in progress.
3. Significant bureaucracy (an attendant cost) will be created to deal with
spam.
4. Defining "Spam" is nigh impossible.
5. By legislative fiat a flagged spam ("ADV" "ADV-ADULT" etc) will
"legalize" some forms of spam. From there, it's only a small step to
mandating delivery.
6. Inevitably there will be exceptions in the legislation (charity,
political, etc.).

There ARE ways, however, to deal with spam. One of the best is
www.spews.org.

(Note that spews.org is hosted in Siberia and has no telephone or email
address)

By blocking ISPs who harbor spammers and provide spam-support services (web
sites, DNS, etc.), we can divide the internet into the spam-friendly part
and the spam-free part. Then the spammers can spend their lives spamming
each other and not fsck with normal folk.

You want to have some fun? Visit the news.admin.net-abuse.email newsgroup.
You never saw such passionate people. Examples:

"Why am I being blocked? I don't send spam." - You live in a crack house and
Domino's don't deliver.

"I'm not the spammer at my ISP" - You want us to believe you're in a whore
house just to hear the piano player?

"It would cost too much to change ISPs" - And you think we give a shit?

"You are blocking (American Bar Association, NY Philharmonic,
Sloane-Kettering Cancer Center, etc.)" - And you think we give a shit?
```

```
On Thu, 13 Mar 2003 11:13:19 -0600, "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
enlightened us:

>
>"SkippyPB" <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote in message
…[43 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That's amazing.  I guess the spammers prefer to steal or maybe it is
easier to steal AOL email addresses than anyone else (except maybe
hotmail).

>In the MonsterHut/Paetac case, Paetac TRIED to drop MonsterHut. However,
>Paetac had executed a "Pink" contract with Monsterhut to allow some
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

As it was explained to me by the AG's office, that is not true.
Paetac first complained to Monsterhut about their spamming and warned
them they would be disconnected per Paetac's TOS if they did not stop.
Monsterhut responded that people complaining about receiving spam were
really trying to "unsubscribe".  Paetec bought this at first until
more and more complaints started coming in and some ISP's threatened
Paetec with a DNS if they didn't stop the spam originating from their
site.  Paetec went back to Monsterhut and told them they were going to
diconnect them.  Monsterhut filed suit against Paetec and got a court
injunction against Paetec.  Somewhere around this time (and I'm not
sure exactly when) the AG of New York got involved and started looking
at it.  They accused them of some business prastice (sorry but the
exact wording of the indictment eludes me).  Part of the testimony I
had to provide and swear to involved the spam I got and my reporting
of it.  Basically, I had to swear that yes I was complaining about
spam from Monsterhut; no I was not trying to unsubscribe and no I had
never given Monsterhut permission to email me anything nor had I ever
done business with them.  The case may not have been directly related
to Paetec's dispute, but it was as a result of it.

>As for the claim that "spammers get taken care of when someone goes after
>them," it is not possible to "go after them" if the spammer is in Korea,
>Brazil, Japan, Hong Kong, Italy, China, yak-yak-yak.
>

You can complain (and I do) to the ISP in the foreign countries where
the Spam originates.  Many follow through and disable the accounts.
Many others don't.  I feel the more I get disabled, the less spam I
get.

>"Legal" solutions to spam don't work, cannot be made to work, and can cause
>significant harm if attempted. Here's some downside to legislative
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It is a puzzle that is for sure.  But even if it just eliminates spam
from within the U.S., it is worth the effort.

>There ARE ways, however, to deal with spam. One of the best is
>www.spews.org.
>

An excellent site.

>(Note that spews.org is hosted in Siberia and has no telephone or email
>address)
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Heads Up

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcNOSPAMewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 2003-03-15T21:05:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iAMca.2567$jA2.239965@newsread2.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<b3638c46.0303110239.5e779188@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<D7Saa.11894$Oz1.680451@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <b3638c46.0303110239.5e779188@posting.google.com>`

```
People, the message in question is a virus!

This means that unless you just happen to be the first person in the 
world to receive it (NOT likely!), you are not getting it from a 
perpetrator, or a spammer,or a troller of newsgroups, but from a victim. 
  Granted, it is 95% the immediate senders fault for not keeping an 
up-to-date antivirus program, or for being dumb enough to  execute an 
unsolicited attachment, but yet the sender of your message is still a 
victim.

Many of the viruses out now days are so persistent because the infected 
sender's Email address is suppressed, so although you may be able to 
deduce the victim's ISP, there is no obvious way to even contact a naive 
victim and tell him to purge his system of the virus.  If the virus 
bypasses the victim's Email client when sending out infected Email, the 
victim may even be totally unaware his system is infected.

Tracking down the true "perp", the originator of the virus, requires 
global Internet traffic analysis to isolate the earliest occurrence of 
the virus, and considerable luck to be able to track down the 
responsible individual.

For those who have never done so, it would be instructive to go to a 
site such as the Symantec Anti-Virus Center 
http://www.symantec.com/avcenter/
and examine the attributes of some of the current virus threats to 
better understand how these things spread and what they do.  The most 
popular technique for virus propagation is for the virus to send Email 
to everyone in the victim's Address book.  If many in this group are 
receiving this virus, this probably means that some victim is a reader 
of this newsgroup and had managed to populate his address book with 
posters to this newsgroup prior to infecting his system.

To satisfy my curiosity, I occasionally save an unsolicited attachment 
in a TEMP folder to force my Norton A/V program to scan the file and 
identify the virus type (You NEVER, NEVER want to execute - it might be 
a virus too recent for your A/V software to detect!).  The most recent 
"Microsoft Updates" virus I received was identified by Norton A/V as 
"W32.GibeB@mm".  Looking up the characteristics of this virus at the 
above site shows that it does indeed propagate using Outlook address 
books using a built-in SMTP client (no "send" evidence left in Outlook).

Peter E. C. Dashwood wrote:
...
> Now I'm wishing I had kept it as it may have given a clue or been
> traceable back to the sender.
> 
> You have to wonder what kind of sad bastard would derive satisfaction
> out of attacking te contributors to a COBOL forum?
...
> "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote in message news:<D7Saa.11894$Oz1.680451@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>...
>
```
