<a id="t-bf9e2557a0"></a>
## Thread: Flaming procedure — 4 msgs

### Flaming procedure

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-10T12:48:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e6c7a9f.10946789@news.optonline.net>`

```
Quoted from another newsgroup, ten years ago, author unknown:

"As the new year heats up with the list's customary
misunderstandings I wanted to make a few suggestions so that we
never have to go without. The list nearly died of neglect over
the holidays when people went out of town and forgot to post
parting shots at their favorite adversaries. Without those little
arrows of rancor and wit the digital fires nearly expired and the
communications depended on humor and sexual innuendo to keep any
kind of passion in play.

In order to avoid such a bleak period again, empty of the juicier
emotions and wittier, sharp edged repartee, I offer the
following. The principles have been successfully tested time and
time again as many of you have witnessed. Please feel free to
draw on them when bored, feeling malicious but uninspired, when
your self esteem needs a hit of self righteousness to get
straight, or anytime you're in the mood for a brawl. The
principles applied with thought, or especially without thought,
are guaranteed to give you a rush of indignation and adrenalin.
Develop your skill and you can enjoy not only the contest with
it's delicious sense of meaningful conflict but also the pleasure
of crushing the opponent's pride. (And how sweet it is.)

1) Make sure you bring up your views on abortion, capital
punishment, Christianity, and the political party you least like,
regularly. When you do so, do so with vigor, enthusiasm, and
scorching wit. Who knows, you may eventually beat those who
embrace the opposite (wrong) side of the argument into submission
and they will convert to your views, thereby making the world a
better place.

2) Refer to your opponents in arguments (or debates that hold the
promise of becoming arguments) as facists as often as possible.
Suggest that their views parallel the Nazi's at least once in any
good flame war.

3) Point out the shortcomings of the opposite gender. Use
tasteless jokes. Base all characterizations of the opposite
gender on the very worst traits of your ex or current spouse, or
your ex or current love interest. If you haven't personally been
mistreated while remaining spotlessly innocent use other people's
histories.

4) When you've managed to get a good heated exchange going try to
score points by using a vocabulary that will drive your opponent
to the dictionary. Mock any attempts on their part to do the
same. If possible humiliate them with ironic references to
misspellings, ill conceived sentence construction, or, best,
inappropriate word usage. Winning in this area makes you sexier.

5) Never apologize. If you make an error in thinking blame it on
the muddy post to which you are responding.

6) When inspired make sure you word your attacks and
counterattacks so that you leave no opening for your adversary to
capitulate to your view except in disgrace. Try to make certain
that every avenue of response is a path of shame.

7) If you start to slip in an argument attack the person. It's
most helpful to know something personal about them but the medium
has restrictions in this area. I recommend keeping a catalog of
stupid, awkward, or embarrassing posts that people make so you
can throw them in their face later as proof of their deficiencies
as a human.

8) If someone does the same to you respond that in their reliance
on ad hominem attacks the argument has deteriorated to a level
that no longer warrants your participation. This can be a winning
blow if played properly. Be subtle here, and clever; try to
convey the sense of your opponent as dim-witted, ethically
degenerate, desperate, and outmanuevered by your overwhelming
intellectual superiority. The real joy here is that you can
neatly do away with any respect due your opponent, slander his
character, lacerate his pride, and, if done properly and with
elan, simultaneously represent yourself as a man or woman whose
ethics and moral sensitivity make it impossible for you to do
what you just did. This one is a real gem and when executed
gracefully an act of art.

9) Remember that you are always right. And I do mean always. No
matter what forces are marshalled against you, no matter how
reasonable, humble, or generous, don't give an inch, don't be
swayed. You are always right. It's the other side that caused
this ruckus and keeps it going.

10) Always insist on the last word. The only honorable finish is
unconditional capitulation by your adversaries or their defeated
silence.

I trust that these guidelines will be of aid. I find myself too
often relegated to the sideline carrying as I do the sandwich
board for a business on my digital body. So onward comrades!
Don't flag! Don't apologize! Draw out your rapier wit, your
unshielded anger, your superiority, your sarcasm, your electric
selves! Think of your great beauty as you weave back and forth,
the words your blades. Eventually I'll get a second internet
account and, being then anonymous and free of reflecting
negatively on the business, will join you. How my blood cries
out!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Flaming procedure

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-11T02:04:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e6d409b.61637960@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3e6c7a9f.10946789@news.optonline.net> <yu4ba.37426$sf5.23138@rwcrnsc52.ops.asp.att.net> <35WdnZVioo67cPGjXTWcog@comcast.com>`

```
"Russell Styles" <RWS0202@comcast.net> wrote:

>    It hardly matters.  Somebody is going to be find an excuse to
>flame Mr Wagner, no matter what he says.

The article makes an effective point against logical fallacies by describing
them in common English rather than Latin phrases so beloved to logicians. It
uses wit, rather than rancor, to defuse the 'arguments' of would-be flamers. It
is brilliant on several levels. 

I really wish I could write that well. The few times I have, I was apeing the
style of original authors. Doing so is close to plagerism. 

Ten years ago I wrote in alt.food.veg (pro), alt.flame and alt.smokers (pro,
principal writer and keeper of the FAQ).  Flammage here is nothing compared to
those groups. Consider this fairly typical exchange on alt.flame: 

Dave Williams (davew@apds.demon.co.uk) wrote:
: In article <3f6kur$khr@nntp1.u.washington.edu>, pkf@u.washington.edu
: (Paula Freeman) wrote:
: : Jack aka Pookie aka The Incredible Mister Limpie isn't *busy* being a
: : lameass at all because it requires absolutely no effort whatsoever on his
: : part.  You might say he's an old hand at it.  Hardy fuckin har.

: You sound a touch bitter, Paula. He couldn't get it up last time he
: visited, eh?

  In a sense, this statement has truth to it; I very seriously doubt that I
could get excited by somebody as aged and as phony as our darling Mrs.
Allison... not to mention that I ain't likely to go for any of that ol'
adultery shit. However, I'm sure she has *plenty* of reasons to be bitter.

-- end quote --

That's first-class flammage .. original and witty. We don't have the resources
here. We'd do better to stick to the subject at hand .. COBOL. 

Robert
```

## Thread: Fujitsu NetCobol v7.0 - using scrollable cursor in ESQL (dynamic or static) — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Fujitsu NetCobol v7.0 - using scrollable cursor in ESQL (dynamic or static)

- **From:** "fulvio" <nospam@abuse.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-11T21:03:51+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b4lf4d$kak$1@grillo.cs.interbusiness.it>`

```
Hi,

my problem is:

     how can i do to define the cursor_type (dynamic or static) using esql
in NetCobol 7.0?

My need is to have the ability to declare static/dynamic scrollable cursor
type, like DBAccess Control do.

My scenario:
Server Linux with MySQL ver. 4.0.10
Clients Win2000Pro with MySql ODBC driver ver. 3.51.06
Fujitsu NetCobol for Win 7.0 Enterprise Ed

When I try to declare a scrollable cursor and then execute fetch
next/prior/first/last using the
methods of  the DBAccess Control, all it works well.
When I try to do the same with the ESQL statements (without using
DBcontrol), I can't rich the same result.
The cursor_type always seems to be setup like a FORWARD_ONLY cursor.

We have also tried to insert one option in the @ODBC_inf  (not documented)
to try to
declare the cursor type to use   (@SQL_CURSOR_TYPE=STATIC), like DBAccess
Control
do in his property section (Others>CursorType) but without result.

This is the our @ODBC_inf created by SQLODBCS.EXE and the statements:

[SERVER LIST]
MYSQL=MySQL Server
[MYSQL]
@SQL_DATASRC=MYSQL
@SQL_USERID=fabio
@SQL_PASSWORD=NOCHGJKBACSNHHIOADBECFEO
@SQL_ACCESS_MODE=READ_WRITE
@SQL_COMMIT_MODE=MANUAL
@SQL_CONCURRENCY=READ_ONLY
@SQL_ODBC_CURSORS=USE_DRIVER
@SQL_QUERY_TIMEOUT=0
[CONNECTION_SCOPE]
@SQL_CONNECTION_SCOPE=PROCESS

...
EXEC SQL DECLARE cust-cur SCROLL CURSOR FOR SELECT * FROM customer END-EXEC.
EXEC SQL OPEN cust-cur END-EXEC.
...
EXEC SQL FETCH cust-cur NEXT INTO :db-cust-rec END-EXEC.
...
EXEC SQL FETCH cust-cur PRIOR INTO :db-cust-rec END-EXEC.

...
EXEC SQL FETCH cust-cur FIRST INTO :db-cust-rec END-EXEC.

...
EXEC SQL FETCH cust-cur LAST INTO :db-cust-rec END-EXEC.
...

What's wrong?
Thanks

Fulvio
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu NetCobol v7.0 - using scrollable cursor in ESQL (dynamic or static)

- **From:** pvieira@emporsoft.pt (Paulo Vieira)
- **Date:** 2003-03-12T01:04:38-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5b8d7c7.0303120104.4975eaa0@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b4lf4d$kak$1@grillo.cs.interbusiness.it>`

```
"fulvio" <nospam@abuse.com> wrote in message news:<b4lf4d$kak$1@grillo.cs.interbusiness.it>...
> Hi,
> 
…[60 more quoted lines elided]…
> Fulvio

Hi,
I am not using MySql or Fujitsu 7 for Linux, so this post may be a
total waste of everybody's time, but last time I checked, there wasn't
any cursor support built into the SQL engine of MySql; therefore, I
think your best shot is to use the Fujitsu provided classes to emulate
that behaviour as a client side cursor (provided that the compiler has
them. I know that NetCobol for Windows 6.1 and 7.0 have them). Browse
your documentation for "DB Access Class Library" or take a look at
http://www.adtools.com/download/v7manuals/DBAccessClassLibrary.zip

Best Regards,

Paulo Vieira, Emporsoft
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu NetCobol v7.0 - using scrollable cursor in ESQL (dynamic or static)

- **From:** maross@texoma.net (Mike)
- **Date:** 2003-03-12T23:10:27-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2763b81a.0303122310.6ffd4a48@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b4lf4d$kak$1@grillo.cs.interbusiness.it> <b5b8d7c7.0303120104.4975eaa0@posting.google.com> <b4nvba$qvi$1@grillo.cs.interbusiness.it>`

```
Fulvio, I'm using FSC-6.1 on (W2k) with MySQL servers on RH7.3 and W2k
and transactions work.
However you may try some of these hints?
What is your sqlState/sqlCode saying?
Does your my.ini have the InnoDB paths and directories setup right?
I have the *max drivers setup on my systems(Redhat 7.3 and
W2k(4.0.1-alpha-max-nt)) and you need these MySql *max drivers for
InnoDB  transactional queries(commits).
Try testing a commit with a scripting language like PHP to verify your
problem is in your COBOL logic/or *.inf files and/or try the simplest
1 row table till it works.

    Are you using a MySql client that can help you diagnose your
table's integrity?
I use SQLyog and MySQL-Front(dated and End_of_life) and both can
convert MyISAM(not for transactional COBOL) to  InnoDb files.
Here is a good SQLyoug review
http://www.databasejournal.com/features/mysql/article.php/1558731
I've had to rebuild my InnoDB tables before that crashed and would not
function      transactions properly till fixed?

Make sure your cobol85.cbr has a @ODBC_Inf=*.inf line pointing to Your
*.inf

Here is an old test *.inf I used that uses dynamic SQL correctly (just
retested).

(in COBOL program *EXEC SQL CONNECT TO 'was_DEFAULT'  END-EXEC.)

[was_Default] 
@SQL_DATASRC=testDB
@SQL_USERID=**hideThis
@SQL_PASSWORD=**hideThis
@SQL_ACCESS_MODE=READ_WRITE
@SQL_COMMIT_MODE=AUTO  
***** NOTE I use AUTO and it works for MySQL trans** as InnoDB sets to
Manual anyway?

@SQL_CONCURRENCY=READ_ONLY
@SQL_ODBC_CURSORS=USE_DRIVER
@SQL_QUERY_TIMEOUT=0

[SQL_DEFAULT_INF]
@SQL_SERVER=default
@SQL_USERID=**hideThis
@SQL_PASSWORD=**hideThis

[CONNECTION_SCOPE]
@SQL_CONNECTION_SCOPE=THREAD

and here is one module from that COBOL program for your reference.

pUpdate.
           display 'Enter record # to upDate from database='.
           accept acceptNum. 
           move acceptNum to ageSv.
           display 'Enter NEW record # to upDate from database='.
           accept acceptNum. 
           move acceptNum to ageSvU.
           EXEC SQL OPEN cur1  END-EXEC.
           EXEC SQL UPDATE testt Set age = :ageSvU Where age = :ageSv 
                END-EXEC. 
           EXEC SQL COMMIT WORK END-EXEC.
           EXEC SQL CLOSE cur1  END-EXEC

I'm using PHP for MySQL as there is plenty of support and it is OS
independent. FSC-COBOL has very little MySQL knowledge base anywhere
including adtools.com as they don't admit support for MySQL nor could
they ever help me.
    Good Luck Mike
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu NetCobol v7.0 - using scrollable cursor in ESQL (dynamic or static)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "fulvio" <nospam@abuse.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-13T21:47:03+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b4qqd5$l8m$1@grillo.cs.interbusiness.it>`
- **References:** `<b4lf4d$kak$1@grillo.cs.interbusiness.it> <b5b8d7c7.0303120104.4975eaa0@posting.google.com> <b4nvba$qvi$1@grillo.cs.interbusiness.it> <2763b81a.0303122310.6ffd4a48@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Mike,

we use a server with Linux RH7.3 with MySQL MySQL ver. 4.0.10-gamma-Max-log
with InnoDB
on client W2K the driver version is MySql ODBC driver ver. 3.51.06

I think that the feature about the static,dynamic,forward_only cursor is
delegate to client.
During the execution of the statement only at the cursor open time something
trip on the lan.

I give you an example.

I need to select a couple of record from a table and I want to read them in
different way and I don't care if the
extracted records are affected about some change made from someone else
during my session.

So I need a SCROLLABLE and STATIC cursor.

These are the esql statements:

declaring a cursor...
EXEC SQL DECLARE cust-cur SCROLL CURSOR
                 FOR SELECT * FROM customer
END-EXEC.

opening the cursor...
EXEC SQL OPEN cust-cur END-EXEC.

reading one record in forward, and the record
returned is the next-one: correct.
EXEC SQL FETCH cust-cur NEXT INTO :db-cust-rec END-EXEC.

reading one record behind, and the returned record
instead is the next-one: ERROR!!!
EXEC SQL FETCH cust-cur PRIOR INTO :db-cust-rec END-EXEC.

reading the first record of the recordset, and the
returned record instead is again the next-one: ERROR!!!
EXEC SQL FETCH cust-cur FIRST INTO :db-cust-rec END-EXEC.

reading the last record of the recordset, and the
returned record instead is again the next-one: ERROR!!!
EXEC SQL FETCH cust-cur LAST INTO :db-cust-rec END-EXEC.

SQLSTATE and SQLCODE are always zero.

I had tryed to log the comunication from the ODBC and the DataBase during
two database comunication sessions,
one made with a PowerCobol sample program that use the DBAccess control and
that perform all the reads in the
right manner, and one made with a sample program build with the Cobol
Project Manager that mistakes the way
to read.

In the first case the DBAccess control send to the ODBC the instructions to
declare a STATIC cursor
(<SQL_CURSOR_TYPE>), and the ODBC drivers relay the command to the database.

In the second case these instructions are missing.

These are the logs.
==============================================
...
...
ESQL_CURSOR 560-154 ENTER SQLSetStmtOption
 HSTMT 02361858
UWORD 7 <SQL_CONCURRENCY>
SQLLEN 1

ESQL_CURSOR 560-154 EXIT SQLSetStmtOption with return code 0 (SQL_SUCCESS)
HSTMT 02361858
UWORD 7 <SQL_CONCURRENCY>
SQLLEN 1

ESQL_CURSOR 560-154 ENTER SQLSetStmtOption
HSTMT 02361858
UWORD 6 <SQL_CURSOR_TYPE>          !!!!!!!! this is missing in the other log
SQLLEN 3

ESQL_CURSOR 560-154 EXIT SQLSetStmtOption with return code 0 (SQL_SUCCESS)
HSTMT 02361858
UWORD 6 <SQL_CURSOR_TYPE>         !!!!!!!! this is missing in the other log
SQLLEN 3

ESQL_CURSOR 560-154 ENTER SQLExecDirectW
HSTMT 02361858
WCHAR * 0x00172AFC [ 20] "SELECT*FROM anagrcli"
SDWORD 20

ESQL_CURSOR 560-154 EXIT SQLExecDirectW with return code 0 (SQL_SUCCESS)
HSTMT 02361858
WCHAR * 0x00172AFC [ 20] "SELECT*FROM anagrcli"
SDWORD 20
...
...
==============================================

==============================================
...
...
ESQL_CURSOR 668-508 ENTER SQLSetStmtOption
HSTMT 02362120
UWORD 7 <SQL_CONCURRENCY>
SQLLEN 1

ESQL_CURSOR 668-508 EXIT SQLSetStmtOption with return code 0 (SQL_SUCCESS)
HSTMT 02362120
UWORD 7 <SQL_CONCURRENCY>
SQLLEN 1

ESQL_CURSOR 668-508 ENTER SQLExecDirect
HSTMT 02362120
UCHAR * 0x01E75FE8 [ 20] "SELECT*FROM anagrcli"
SDWORD 20

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu NetCobol v7.0 - using scrollable cursor in ESQL (dynamic or static)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** maross@texoma.net (Mike)
- **Date:** 2003-03-13T22:53:08-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2763b81a.0303132253.20aca6bb@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b4lf4d$kak$1@grillo.cs.interbusiness.it> <b5b8d7c7.0303120104.4975eaa0@posting.google.com> <b4nvba$qvi$1@grillo.cs.interbusiness.it> <2763b81a.0303122310.6ffd4a48@posting.google.com> <b4qqd5$l8m$1@grillo.cs.interbusiness.it>`

```
Fulvio wrote
>I need to Know if this feature is supported only from the DBAccess
control
>in PowerCOBOL that can declare the cursor type in his property, or
not.

NO Fulvio it doesn't work! I tried your "Prior" argument without
success on W2k-MySQ FSC6.1 (forward scrolls only like your results).
You know your COBOL-SQL well. I hate to suggest gimmicky code however,
you might read the fetched data(keys) into a COBOL table (for prior
records) . This method worked for me, however you have to open/
perform set to desired table-key.

  pQuery.
     move 1 to ageTabIndex.
     move zeroes to ageTab.
     move 0 to recCount recNum.
     move 0 to ageSVu ageSV.
     EXEC SQL OPEN cur1  END-EXEC.
     perform pQueryLoop 
         until recCount > 8. *> till 8 for test or eof
     EXEC SQL close cur1  END-EXEC.
If you don't close above and reopen as below you will get eof (or just
next record)
     EXEC SQL OPEN cur1  END-EXEC. 
     move ageTabIndex to ageSvu. *> set from my previous Seq reads
     EXEC SQL FETCH cur1 INTO :ageSv where age = :ageSvU END-EXEC.
     EXEC SQL close cur1  END-EXEC.

   pQueryLoop. 
      ***** sequential read loop**************
OR try to declare two cursors on same file with read-only then for
updates close both and re-open for specific record/s updates?
 Since you have it working in PowerCOBOL the problem must be in
FSC-COBOL drivers. They had a problem with SQLutility in 6.1 that
needed a patch (they didn't post to their site) and it looks like 7
has problems too (PHP is free and does better than that). When I have
the time I will test your statements in PHP-MySQL or maybe an
FSC-COBOL-MySQL guru will intervene or you might try the DBforums.
Nice to see someone using MySQL-COBOL other than the free versions. If
you have serviceWise you might see if your methods work with Oracle
and FSC. Sorry I could not help more.
   Good Luck Mike
```

Thanks Mike for your support,

I would like to try the DB Access Class Library for COBOL (API)
included with the FJCobol 7.0., but I think that these API are only
for DB mentionend in the documentation (SymfoWARE, Oracle (using Oracle Call
Interface)
and SQL Server).

Today I finaly receive the reply from the FJ Support, this is what they told
me:
**************************************
Fujitsu COBOL does not support FETCH PRIOR or FETCH FIRST by design. In this
implementation FETCH = FETCH NEXT.
If any SQL command is not supported by COBOL (there are a few of those in
addition to FETCH, such as CREATE and TRUNCATE), use Dynamic SQL
(PREPARE/EXECUTE).
[Example]
:
01 STMVAR PIC X(100).
:
MOVE "FETCH cust-cur PRIOR INTO :db-cust-rec" TO STMVAR
EXEC SQL
PREPARE STMIDT FROM :STMVAR
END-EXEC.
EXEC SQL
EXECUTE STMIDT
END-EXEC.

For more information see COBOL User's Guide, Chapter 19, section "Using
Dynamic SQL".
************************************

If I will discover something about the next test, I will post in the
newsgroup.
Thanks to all. (sorry for my english)
Fulvio
fruber@tin.it

Fulvio wrote:
>If I will discover something about the next test, I will post in the
>newsgroup.

Don't try to recreate the wheel Fulvio. MySql supports Dynamic Cursors
"forward only" as per here
http://www.mysql.com/products/myodbc/faq_7.html#Dynamic_Cursor.
I read in PHPfreaks.com that the "Scroll" argument in the Declare Verb
is interpreted as a comment in MySQL and PostgreSql(set to default).
It appears your Support from FSC is correct and their SQL connect
modules are working properly. I'm guessing Dbaccess emulates/Loads a
range of data keys that emulates prior behavior. I just wish FSC would
set up a forum at their adtools.com so everyone could see solutions to
developers problems (many php forums are free). When I has serviceWise
they only took 1-3 days to respond compared to 4 weeks from
Macromedia, however I found my Macromedia problem quickly on their
well hosted user forums instead.

If you are using Linux you might try FlashMX as a persistent front end
instead of PowerCOBOL. It works From PPC to server and can compile to
*.exe with 3rd party tools.
Fujitsu needs to stop following M$ so closely and stop putting Net
prefix into every product. Even M$ is renaming its next server
"Windows Server 2003". Mike
```

## Thread: What are they talking about...... — 24 msgs

### What are they talking about......

- **From:** "A Farzanegan" <ahmad.farzanegan@virgin.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-11T23:40:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b4ls6a$ml1$1@helle.btinternet.com>`

```
Dear

I am off to an interview on Thursday. They have provided me with a list of
question to answer. This one has snookered me. Can anyone help.
I've ckeck IBM book manager & look at some of the manuals with no luck. I
have 15 years mainframe experience, however this new to me.


Here is the question.

Is the logic implied by the indentation in the examples below the same as
the logic as interpreted by the COBOL compiler?

a. IF C1
              S1
              IF C2
                   S2
              ELSE
                       IF C3
                            S4
 ELSE
            S5.



b. IF C1
          IF C2
               IF     C3
                        S1
               ELSE
                        S2
          ELSE
               S3
 ELSE
          S4.



c. IF  C1
          S1
    ELSE
          IF C2
                   S2
          ELSE
                   S3.



d. IF C1
         S1
          IF C2
               S2
          ELSE
               S3
          S4
 ELSE
          S5.



e. IF C1
        IF C2
             S1
        ELSE
             IF C3
                  S2
             ELSE
                  S3
 ELSE
          S4.
```

#### ↳ Re: What are they talking about......

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-03-11T23:47:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c2e828$879f5fe0$1095f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<b4ls6a$ml1$1@helle.btinternet.com>`

```
The answer is no.  The indentation implies tha "ELSE S5" is matched with
"IF C1" but the compiler will match it with the closest preceding IF which
is "IF C3".

A Farzanegan <ahmad.farzanegan@virgin.net> wrote in article
<b4ls6a$ml1$1@helle.btinternet.com>...
> Dear
> 
> I am off to an interview on Thursday. They have provided me with a list
of
> question to answer. This one has snookered me. Can anyone help.
> I've ckeck IBM book manager & look at some of the manuals with no luck. I
…[67 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

"A Farzanegan" <ahmad.farzanegan@virgin.net> wrote:

>Dear
>
…[67 more quoted lines elided]…
>          S4.

That's easy. 
a. no
b. yes
c. yes
d. no
e. yes

This is the level to which 'defenders of the period'  have brought us. It speaks
elequently to the superiority of explicit delimiters over periods. 

Robert
```

On 11-Mar-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> That's easy.
> a. no
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> elequently to the superiority of explicit delimiters over periods.

How did "defenders of the period" bring us to this?   You make such incredible
claims.   Name one such defender and tell us how that one defender contributed
to whatever "this" is.

Do you know of any "defenders of the period" that don't advocate using END-IFs
at all levels in this example?   e.g. some "defender of the period" who didn't
recognize the utility of END-IF.

How would this have been more or less clear after my putting in END-IFs with a
period after the final one, over your putting in END-IFs without a period after
the final one?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What are they talking about......

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-13T01:48:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e6fc3bd.226304896@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b4ls6a$ml1$1@helle.btinternet.com> <3e6e7a7b.142002608@news.optonline.net> <b4nlf1$qkj$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 11-Mar-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>How did "defenders of the period" bring us to this?  

The reason why they are asking these questions is because they have thousands of
programs containing nested IFs which aren't explicitly terminated. The reason
why they didn't ask the test taker to supply explicit terminators to make the
IFs work as intended, indicated by indentation, is because they intend to
continue writing the old way. 

There are two kinds of 'defenders of the period'. Your type accedes to explicit
terminators, defending periods only at 'level zero'. The retro type opposes
explicit terminators. I was excoriating the retro type, who wrote the test which
framed the context of the discussion. 

>Do you know of any "defenders of the period" that don't advocate using END-IFs
>at all levels in this example?   e.g. some "defender of the period" who didn't
>recognize the utility of END-IF.

As I said above, the people who wrote the test. 

>How would this have been more or less clear after my putting in END-IFs with a
>period after the final one, over your putting in END-IFs without a period after
>the final one?

There would be no difference syntactically. Stylistically, mine would be
internally consistant whereas yours would appear to have one foot in the boat
and the other on the dock.

Robert
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> The reason why they are asking these questions is because they have thousands of
> programs containing nested IFs which aren't explicitly terminated.

You have no idea at all that they have 'thousands' of programs.  They
may have only a small amount of code from before the time they adopted
an '85 compiler.

Working out the examples is a reasonable test of Cobol skills
_regardless_ of how much code they have of that form.

> The reason
> why they didn't ask the test taker to supply explicit terminators to make the
> IFs work as intended, indicated by indentation, 

That is possibly the most clueless thing that you have said yet. 
There is no indication that the indentation indicates the 'correct'
intention.  If these are in existing code then it would be more likely
that the code works as written and has passed tests to show that, the
indentation is what is incorrect.

> is because they intend to continue writing the old way. 

Yet another of your totally unfounded assumptions based entirely on
your own prejudices and complete ignorence of their actual intent.

It may well be that they do have some older code from the 80s still in
production and want it brought up to date, hense finding someone who
can demonstrate this particular skill.

Given that you would follow the indentation then consider that you
failed the test miserably.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What are they talking about......

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-15T01:56:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e727d7e.404957050@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b4ls6a$ml1$1@helle.btinternet.com> <3e6e7a7b.142002608@news.optonline.net> <b4nlf1$qkj$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3e6fc3bd.226304896@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303141248.1a46de61@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

>Working out the examples is a reasonable test of Cobol skills
>_regardless_ of how much code they have of that form.

If you want to test logic skills, ask questions about logic. Such as 'what's the
inverse of' if a not = b and c > d and e

>> The reason
>> why they didn't ask the test taker to supply explicit terminators to make the
>> IFs work as intended, indicated by indentation, 
>
>That is possibly the most clueless thing that you have said yet. 

Oh good, a nadir.

>There is no indication that the indentation indicates the 'correct'
>intention.  If these are in existing code then it would be more likely
>that the code works as written and has passed tests to show that, the
>indentation is what is incorrect.

Tests don't 'prove' anything is correct. If you want proofs, go to mathematics
or legal. The best we can do is provide corrigible evidence to support
reasonable propositions. I've seen tons of obviously flawed code which made it
thru QA and UAT testing. 

In the context of automated conversion, I would argue that indentation is
correct >90% of the time. The tool should report report discrepencies for manual
investigation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What are they talking about......

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-16T04:24:37+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b4u6fs$h60$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<b4ls6a$ml1$1@helle.btinternet.com> <3e6fc3bd.226304896@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303141248.1a46de61@posting.google.com> <3e727d7e.404957050@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>Working out the examples is a reasonable test of Cobol skills
>>_regardless_ of how much code they have of that form.
> 
> If you want to test logic skills, ask questions about logic. Such as
> 'what's the inverse of' if a not = b and c > d and e

What has that got to do with it ?  The particular questions were about 
testing understanding of IF structure not logic.  I am sure that if the 
questioner wanted to test logic skills of that type then they would have 
included a question.

In fact there is no indication that _haven't_ included many different 
questions, including ones of the type you indicate.
 
>>That is possibly the most clueless thing that you have said yet.
> 
> Oh good, a nadir.

No, it is only the most clueless _so_far_, I am sure that you can manage 
worse with almost no effort at all.
 
> The best we can do is provide corrigible evidence to
> support reasonable propositions. 

And I would suggest that your _guess_ that the indentation is the correct 
interpretation is _not_ a reasonable proposition.

It may simply be that your editor, or the printer, has different tab 
settings, and/or some lines are space filled and some have tabs.

Now go and provide 'evidence' of any type to support your proposition that 
it is the indentation that should take precedence.

> I've seen tons of obviously flawed code
> which made it thru QA and UAT testing.

Having seen your assertions about initialise being 'wrong' and your guess 
about what the code really means, I would have serious doubts about any 
claims that you make about what is flawed or not.
 
> In the context of automated conversion, I would argue that indentation is
> correct >90% of the time. 

Who mentioned 'automated conversion' ?

Indentation has no meaning at all in Cobol, C, Pascal, or many other 
languages.  While it is useful if programmers follow consistent indentation 
rules there is no language restriction that requires this.

You can 'argue' all you like, but I certainly wouldn't trust you with any 
source code at all if you would change the logic of how it works based on 
indentation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What are they talking about......

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-15T21:47:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e736822.465034310@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b4ls6a$ml1$1@helle.btinternet.com> <3e6fc3bd.226304896@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303141248.1a46de61@posting.google.com> <3e727d7e.404957050@news.optonline.net> <b4u6fs$h60$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>testing understanding of IF structure not logic. 

IF structure _is_ logic. See my companion article for an illustration of
transforming logic into IF structures. 

>I am sure that if the 
>questioner wanted to test logic skills of that type then they would have 
>included a question.

Tests of this type too often demonstrate the author's (self-rated) skill rather
than assessing the applicant's ability. When I write tests, I benchmark them on
people of known ability, then run a regression analysis. If test scores do not
correlate with ability, the test goes into the trash.

The _highest_ correlation I've seen was a test that had nothing to do with
programming. It was non-verbal. It measured the testee's ability to fold and
mentally rotate three-dimensional paper patterns with various marks on panels.
Speed was included in the score. It had >90% correlation with programming skill,
and errors were 'one tailed' i.e. it flunked a few good programmers, but didn't
pass any mediocre ones. That's the kind of error you want. 

>>>That is possibly the most clueless thing that you have said yet.
>> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>worse with almost no effort at all.

A few more glasses of wine will get the job done. :>

>Now go and provide 'evidence' of any type to support your proposition that 
>it is the indentation that should take precedence.

APS uses indentation. So does SQR, the Oracle report writer. I assume my own
Beautifier doesn't count here. 

>> I've seen tons of obviously flawed code
>> which made it thru QA and UAT testing.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>claims that you make about what is flawed or not.

Good use of the logical principle 'if one dog has spots, all dogs have spots.'
You should break out the long-forgotten logic text moldering in your attic. Look
under 'accident' and 'hasty'. 

>> In the context of automated conversion, I would argue that indentation is
>> correct >90% of the time. 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>rules there is no language restriction that requires this.

I'm not a compiler; I'm reading as a human. 

The mistake of extra or missing periods is more common than 'accidentally'
indenting or outdenting. 

>You can 'argue' all you like, but I certainly wouldn't trust you with any 
>source code at all if you would change the logic of how it works based on 
>indentation.

Now you don't believe in indentation. Perchance, are you crossposting from the
Smalltalk newsgroup?

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What are they talking about......

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-16T23:40:59+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b50a89$iks$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<b4ls6a$ml1$1@helle.btinternet.com> <3e727d7e.404957050@news.optonline.net> <b4u6fs$h60$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e736822.465034310@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> IF structure _is_ logic. 

IF structure is structural logic, not conditional logic.

Am I surprised that you didn't respond to my mentioning that the one 
question presented was just one of many and that others may well cover 
areas that you complained about ?

No.  You just bulldoze on that you know everything and you would have done 
much better than the ones not even shown.

>>Now go and provide 'evidence' of any type to support your proposition that
>>it is the indentation that should take precedence.
> 
> APS uses indentation. So does SQR, the Oracle report writer. 

Python, too relies on indentation, none of these are Cobol.

> I assume my own Beautifier doesn't count here.

Your assumption is correct, useful 'beautifiers' will _create_ correct 
indentation and not change the program code to match tab stops.

>>Having seen your assertions about initialise being 'wrong' and your guess
>>about what the code really means, I would have serious doubts about any
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> spots.' 

Actually, it was use of the logic, 'the dog has bitten me before and may do 
so again'.  Your analogies are quite off the mark.

It is usually you that does the 'I saw one bad programmer and he used full 
stops, so every programmer that uses full stops is bad'.

> You should break out the long-forgotten logic text moldering in
> your attic. Look under 'accident' and 'hasty'.

Are you claiming that all your errors of fact are typing mistakes ?
 
>>Indentation has no meaning at all in Cobol, C, Pascal, or many other
>>languages.  While it is useful if programmers follow consistent
>>indentation rules there is no language restriction that requires this.
> 
> I'm not a compiler; I'm reading as a human.

Hmmmmm. let it pass, let it pass     ;-)
 
> The mistake of extra or missing periods is more common than 'accidentally'
> indenting or outdenting.

As I said, it may be an artifact of different tab stop settings.  It may be 
a faulty 'beautifier', it may be that the code works correctly but the 
previous programmer 'fixed' the indentation incorrectly.

>>You can 'argue' all you like, but I certainly wouldn't trust you with any
>>source code at all if you would change the logic of how it works based on
>>indentation.
> 
> Now you don't believe in indentation. 

Bleeep!! Logic error.  In Cobol I treat the code as if it follows the 
indentation in exactly the same way that the compiler does.

Not believing a single instance of indentation does not mean that I 'don't 
believe _in_ indentation'.  You were following your one spot dog = all 
analogy. 

Code should be indented correctly, if it is not then the code should be 
examined against the spec and either the code adjusted or the indentation.  
Your short cut of just following the indentation is liable to flaws in 
many, possibly most, cases.

> Perchance, are you crossposting from the Smalltalk newsgroup?

Maybe from Python.
```

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Python, too relies on indentation, none of these are Cobol.

APS _is_ COBOL. It's a preprocessor that translates 'easy' APS code into
compliant COBOL. If it were up to me, I'd run it once and thereafter maintain
the COBOL. But some shops maintain APS code and preprocess it every time. 

>> I assume my own Beautifier doesn't count here.
>
>Your assumption is correct, useful 'beautifiers' will _create_ correct 
>indentation and not change the program code to match tab stops.

Forget tab stops. Tab stops are a minefield of incompatibility. My text editor
stores nothing but spaces. 

>>>Having seen your assertions about initialise being 'wrong' and your guess
>>>about what the code really means, I would have serious doubts about any
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>stops, so every programmer that uses full stops is bad'.

I've seen thousands of mediocre programmers, and known a few hundred in real
life. 

>> You should break out the long-forgotten logic text moldering in
>> your attic. Look under 'accident' and 'hasty'.
>
>Are you claiming that all your errors of fact are typing mistakes ?

You misunderstood my response. Accident and hasty do not refer to typos; they
refer to logical errors. Read a logic text to understand what I said. 

>>>You can 'argue' all you like, but I certainly wouldn't trust you with any
>>>source code at all if you would change the logic of how it works based on
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>indentation in exactly the same way that the compiler does.

We all do that.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What are they talking about......

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-17T20:13:59+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b52igg$hae$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<b4ls6a$ml1$1@helle.btinternet.com> <b4u6fs$h60$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e736822.465034310@news.optonline.net> <b50a89$iks$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e73c053.487614219@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> APS _is_ COBOL. It's a preprocessor that translates 'easy' APS code into
> compliant COBOL. 

APS is _NOT_ COBOL.

It may be a Cobol generator, it may be a Cobol tool, it may even have some 
passing resemblence to Cobol in some ways, it may even pass actual Cobol 
through unchanged, but it is not _Cobol_.

The specification of the APS language cannot be found in the Cobol standard.
```

> Forget tab stops. Tab stops are a minefield of incompatibility. My text editor

But you didn't write that code in the first place.   Do you know which editor

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What are they talking about......

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-15T20:21:05-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b50n2k$sc9$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b4ls6a$ml1$1@helle.btinternet.com> <3e727d7e.404957050@news.optonline.net> <b4u6fs$h60$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e736822.465034310@news.optonline.net> <b50a89$iks$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
As someone who DOES try and get my indentation to follow my logic (obviously
no "sane" person would try and get their logic to follow their indentation),
it sounds like my "favorite" <NOT> expert on all things COBOL would probably
believe the comments in source code as reflecting what the program was
supposed to do - rather than what the code actually did - even for a program
that had been "running fine" for years.

The original question (long ago and far away) was trying to determine (in a
test) whether a programmer COULD understand the rules of COBOL as far as
logic goes - regardless of what the indentation might "hint at" - and to
determine when the two were not in sync.

To me, that is a useful tool in any programmer who must maintain existing
programs.  Just as I think that it is a useful skill to be able to create
MODIFIED comments to reflect what a program's logic actually does.
```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 14-Mar-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>It seems to me that I remember someone telling me about how onerous of a task
it
>was to remove periods while cutting and pasting logic into IF logic.    If
>removing periods is a chore, then certainly aligning the code is a chore.
>
>Also, in some of these atrocities, we have so many levels of indention that
when
>some new code is added, there is no way to align it correctly.

Difficulty of indentation depends on your editor. With mine, I select the first
and last line, hit 'shift right' four times, and it's done. To remove periods
within the 'scoped' block, I hit 'change', from=., to=nothing, and it's done. 

If you're using an editor that requires shifting each line individually, it
would be a major headache.
```

On 12-Mar-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> The reason why they are asking these questions is because they have thousands
> of programs containing nested IFs which aren't explicitly terminated. The
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> continue writing the old way.

It could be.   But if I were teaching CoBOL, I would include such code in a test
I would give them.   This test would do more than test their knowledge, it would
also give them a better appreciation of good coding methods.

Still - a programmer HAS to be able to understand what that code does.  If he
gets hired to maintain such code, he needs to be able to verify that it works
correctly, and be able to clean it up without breaking it.
```

#### ↳ Re: What are they talking about......

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick<Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-11T17:17:48-07:00

```
It looks like their indentation is an implied 'end-if'  I'll insert them
inline so you can see.  But here are my answers.

a) no
b) yes
c) yes
d) no
e) yes

By the way, it's probably best to change to a fixed-length font for best
readability.  My reader by default does a scalable font, which is annoying!

Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications Development
FirstBank Data Corporation

>>> A Farzanegan<ahmad.farzanegan@virgin.net> 03/11/03 04:40PM >>>
Dear

I am off to an interview on Thursday. They have provided me with a list of
question to answer. This one has snookered me. Can anyone help.
I've ckeck IBM book manager & look at some of the manuals with no luck. I
have 15 years mainframe experience, however this new to me.


Here is the question.

Is the logic implied by the indentation in the examples below the same as
the logic as interpreted by the COBOL compiler?

a. IF C1
              S1
              IF C2
                   S2
              ELSE
                       IF C3
                            S4
*                      END-IF
*             END-IF                       
 ELSE
            S5.
*END-IF



b. IF C1
          IF C2
               IF     C3
                        S1
               ELSE
                        S2
*              END-IF
          ELSE
               S3
*         END-IF
 ELSE
          S4.
*END-IF



c. IF  C1
          S1
    ELSE
          IF C2
                   S2
          ELSE
                   S3.
*         END-IF
*   END-IF



d. IF C1
         S1
          IF C2
               S2
          ELSE
               S3
*         END-IF
          S4
 ELSE
          S5.
*END-IF



e. IF C1
        IF C2
             S1
        ELSE
             IF C3
                  S2
             ELSE
                  S3
*            END-IF
*       END-IF
 ELSE
          S4.
*END-IF
```

#### ↳ Re: What are they talking about......

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-11T18:43:57-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Z6idnSBUmeC7HvOjXTWchA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<b4ls6a$ml1$1@helle.btinternet.com>`

```

"A Farzanegan" <ahmad.farzanegan@virgin.net> wrote in message
news:b4ls6a$ml1$1@helle.btinternet.com...
> Dear
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Here is the question.

Here's the answer:

They are all wrong. The fact that you have to ask such questions implies,
within the question, the ambiguity and potential error of the technique. You
might as well be asking how to determine what the compiler will do with a
NOTE paragraph or ALTER GO TO.

The need for the question disappears when using modern constructs of the
language, in this case, scope delimiters.
```

AF

<a id="t-a58656fdd0"></a>
## Thread: COPY and Period - New Thread — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### COPY and Period - New Thread

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-11T18:51:12-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<toKcnZ-IybTqHPOjXTWciA@giganews.com>`

```
In one of the longest threads in this newsgroup, ample discussion has come
forth over COPY books and periods within and without, best practice,
ambiguity, appeals to various standards, and a fascination with process.

Admitting in advance to not being schooled (or interested) in the nuances of
what seems to be an angles/pin debate, I thought I might restart the
discussion for those who are addicted with this question:

Can a COPY book consist on only a period? If not, why not? If yes, can you
use COPY REPLACING?

Inquiring minds want to debate.
```

---

- **From:** Steve Barrie <member26402@dbforums.com>

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Follow up on RM/85 to Fujitsu file system

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-12T21:07:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v77v6v4g4dun0v6laqjja0fck3e1lvqem2@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<at84om$srg$1@reader08.wxs.nl> <2633929.1047472821@dbforums.com>`

```
On Wed, 12 Mar 2003 12:40:21 +0000, Steve Barrie
<member26402@dbforums.com> wrote:

>
>We are commencing a project to migrate RM/85 Cobol indexed files (with a
>DAT file extension) to C-ISAM. I would be interested in any tool that
>would help with this conversion, or at least to generate text files as
>the first step.
If the files really are RM/85 as you say, and if you are converting to
Fujitsu then you may not need to do anything but to run Fujitsu
recovery program on the files, as they use the same filesystem.

Whether or not you can do this will depend on the type of information
you hold on the file.

If you have a FD that contains any COMP-6 (and eventually some of the
other COMP-xxx) then you will not be able to use the data directly,
and will need to do a program in RM to convert to a plain file, and 
then another one to load the info into the new file (with the correct
COMP-XX fields as supported by Fujitsu). Fujitsu File Utility program
may also be used to load the file from the plain file to a Indexed
file (never used it though).

If you need help with this conversion my company has loads of
experience in doing this type of conversions and we would gladly help
you doing it if you posses the File descriptions.

Regards

Frederico Fonseca
Sys Soft Integrated Ltd.

Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico _ fonseca at syssoft-int . com
```

leave them ASCII. The next conversion will be much simpler.

<a id="t-df0bc5c7d1"></a>
## Thread: What's Missing in a COBOL Style Guide? — 239 this month / 258 total

### What's Missing in a COBOL Style Guide?

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-12T15:51:08-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E6FC7EC.8EF66036@attglobal.net>`

```
Folks,
You provided lots of great input when I posted a proposed COBOL
Programming Standard a few days ago.  One thing that came up repeatedly,
both in this newsgroup and within my company was "show us examples of
how to code, not just the prohibitions about what we cannot code".

So, I'm dusting off a Style Guide that has been laying around, probably
largely ignored.  I think I can sneak it into the standard without
alarming the management too badly.

How about looking it over and telling me what else it needs to say, what
it says wrong, etc.?  It is unabashedly IBM Mainframe COBOL oriented.
I'd be willing to have suggestions that apply to other platforms,
though.

Here's the document:
                1985 ANSI/ISO COBOL -- Style Guide
                                1
                          Introduction

From: Colin Campbell

Date: 12 March 2003

This Style Guide for writing 1985 ANSI/ISO COBOL is intended to
suggest ways that programmers can write more readable and more
maintainable programs.  Nothing in this guide needs to be taken
as a rule.  Programmers are free to follow some suggestions and
ignore others.  Programmers are, however, encouraged to think
about the suggestions and how they might help in programming;
also, they should read the entire document, because they may see
programs in the future which use some of these suggestions, and
need to understand the code they're seeing.

Contributions to the Style Guide are welcome from anyone coding
COBOL at CoX-ORG; they may be sent to:

Colin Campbell at xxx

                       General Suggestions

1.Use of comment lines:

  Both the '/' and '*' in Column 7 (the 'Continue Column', in
  COBOL terminology) can be used to signify comments.  The '/'
  causes a page eject on the output listing of any compiler,
  according to the 1985 ANSI standard.  Many programmers use the
  '/' without any other text to start a new page, then put
  comment lines starting with '*' after it, to tell what a set
  of data items is for or what a procedure does.  Since the '/'
  is also a comment line, go ahead and start the 'comment text'
  on that line, and continue with '*' lines.

  Comments are often interspersed within source code, especially
  in the Procedure Division, to explain to a reader the intent
  of some code.  Such comments should be general in their nature
  (such as 'This is the case of a keyword coded without its
  value'), and the code itself should be clear enough to be
  understandable once the 'general idea' is given by the
  comment.  If the comment has to tell the reader a lot of
  detail, this may mean that the code should be changed -
  perhaps to improve the names of data items or conditions.
  Watch for this situation.

  Programmers sometimes put code in a program which they do not
  intend to use in the production version of the program, such
  as debugging lines, or logic which it is later decided is not
  needed (but the programmer thinks might be wanted in the
  future).  The normal thing to do in this case is to turn
  thecode into comments by putting '*' in the continue column.
  Two variations on this practice might be useful.

  One is to put a distinctive string, perhaps '**' or '*==>' on
  such lines.  This makes it easy to find all of them when
  editing a program.  When the saved lines prove to be needed,
  you'll save time locating them.  You could even use different
  'comment strings' for different related groups, since there
  are often data items and procedure statements which both
  relate to a feature.

  If you've used 'debugging lines' (usually DISPLAY verbs, with
  perhaps some data items to allow conversion of indexes, etc.)
  in testing your program, you may want to keep them for future
  testing.  COBOL contains a little used feature called the
  Debug Module which can help with this.  Change these lines to
  true 'debug lines' by coding 'D' in the continue column.  Such
  lines are treated as comments unless the SOURCE-COMPUTER
  paragraph contains the phrase 'WITH DEBUGGING MODE '.  The
  following method of coding the SOURCE-COMPUTER paragraph is
  suggested:

        *SOURCE-COMPUTER. IBM-390 DEBUGGING MODE.

  By changing the '*' to a space, all of the 'D' lines in the
  program are activated (that is, compiled and executed); by
  changing the space to '*', they are all deactivated (that is,
  ignored by the compiler).  Since the SOURCE-COMPUTER paragraph
  has no other purpose except to set the debugging mode on or
  off, it may be commented or omitted.
                       General Suggestions

2.Use of EJECT and SKIP1/2/3:

  These compiler directives are IBM extensions to COBOL, while
  comment lines are defined as part of the language by ANSI.
  Therefore, it would be better to use the standard method to
  control the listing.

  If an existing program containing EJECT and/or SKIPn
  directives is being converted to ANSI 1985 COBOL, it is
  relatively simple to change all of the EJECT lines to the '/'
  in the continue column.  The process using the ISPF/PDF Editor
  would be to exclude all lines and find all occurrences of
  EJECT, change all space in column 7 to '/' in the non-excluded
  lines, and finally, remove all occurrences of EJECT.  The
  editor commands would be:

        X ALL;
 F ALL ' EJECT ';
 C ALL NX ' ' '/' 7;
 C ALL NX ' EJECT ' ''

  A similar sequence could be used for SKIP1, SKIP2, and SKIP3
  replacement (these would be changed to '*' in the continue
  column).

  While this not a document to teach ISPF, command sequences
  like the one shown above can be retrieved and modified easily
  by setting one of the PF keys to the value 'RETRIEVE' or
  'CRETRIEV'.  (Type 'KEYS' on any ISPF command line to get
  access to the key meanings.)

3.Coding a COBOL Program in Mixed Case:

  Beginning with 1985 ANSI COBOL, the 26 lower case alphabetic
  characters may be used anywhere the 26 upper case alphabetic
  characters can be used, and the lower case and upper case
  equivalents are considered equal.

  This means that reserved words and user defined names may be
  coded in either case - "identification", "Identification",
  "IdEnTiFiCaTiOn", and "IDENTIFICATION" are all equivalent and
  valid.  A procedure name may be defined as "READ-A-RECORD" and
  referenced as "read-a-record".

  Should you use upper case, as in the past, or start using
  lower case, or use a mixture?  This is your choice.  To the
  compiler, it makes no difference, because every reserved word
  or name is translated to upper case for the purposes of
  matching.  To you, however, there is a difference.  First, non-
  numeric literals are NOT translated, so if you wish them to
  appear in  upper case on output, for example, you must enter
  them in upper case.  Second, while the ISPF Editor's FIND
  command will find strings in either case, there may be
  utilities that will not.  Third, there is the question of
  readability.  You must decide, but the vast majority of COBOL
  programs now in existence are written in upper case, so it may
  be hard for a programmer to read a lower or mixed case program
  as quickly.

  All in all, it may be better to continue to code COBOL in
  upper case, only using lower case characters in places where
  they are needed, such as to compare against input, to create
  mixed case messages, or the like.

                    Procedure Division Ideas

4.Creating Data and Procedure Names:

  It is usually a good idea to choose user defined names, such
  as data names and procedure names, which contain at least one
  hyphen ('-') to reduce the chance that they may conflict with
  current or future COBOL reserved words.

  Avoid very simple words, and common words, especially if they
  are Data Processing terms.

  Very short names are desirable for use as subscripts and
  indexes, but make them at least  two characters long, to
  simplify finding them when editing a program.  (Think how
  difficult it would be to find the occurrences of the words 'I'
  or 'X' as opposed to the word 'IX'!)

  If you've tried to convert an old program to 1985 ANSI COBOL
  and find that the program used what is now a reserved word,
  one technique for converting it might be to append '--X' to
  the name (assuming it is short enough).    There are no COBOL
  reserved words with consecutive hyphens, and very few user
  defined names.

                    Procedure Division Ideas

1.Coding of Periods

  Code periods very sparingly in the Procedure Division.
  Periods are required only at the end of a procedure header
  (paragraph name, or section name followed by the word SECTION)
  and at the end of a procedure (paragraph or section).  The
  period at the end of a procedure should be coded on a new line
  in column 12 (the start of 'margin B' in COBOL terms).  Since
  periods are easy to overlook, this helps make it easier to
  check visually that they are present.

  The practice of coding a period after each statement makes
  changing a program harder.  For example, a series of MOVE
  statements might have started out being unconditional, but
  later need to be executed only if a condition is met.  If each
  MOVE ends with a period, then the periods must be removed to
  make the whole group execute only under the condition.

  One of the most common errors in COBOL programming is
  misplacing periods, either putting one where it shouldn't be,
  or leaving one out that is needed.  Most often, this is
  related to use of the IF statement.  Instead of terminating IF
  statements with a period, use the END-IF 'scope terminator',
  and line it up with the IF to which it corresponds.

  When coding nested IF's, it is not always practical to use END-
  IF, because it may require two, three, four, or more to 'end'
  all of the conditions.  In this case, the period is a great
  convenience, so it should be used, but code it on a new line
  at the start of margin B.  Doing this is valid COBOL syntax,
  and it makes modification of the IF statement simpler, because
  the period doesn't have to be removed from the 'old' last line
  and replaced on a 'new' last line.

2.Aligning procedural statements:

  For a set of statements in a single procedure, try to align
  the word 'TO' and all of the relational operators (think of
  them as being two characters long, since there are now '<='
  and '=>' as well as  '= ', etc.) for improved readability.
  For example:

              MOVE     DATE--NAME              TO DATA--NAME-2
              IF       DATA--NAME-3            =  DATA--NAME-4
                       MOVE     DATA--NAME-2   TO DATA--NAME-3
              END-IF

  In addition, code verbs in 'fixed' columns, leaving room for
  the longer verbs (EVALUATE, SUBTRACT, and UNSTRING are each
  eight characters).  This generally helps the eye see each
  statement more easily.  Most editors can be set up to provide
  tab characters so that entering the statements doesn't require
  excessive use of the space bar.

  The ISPF Edit and View facilities offer an aid to looking at
  COBOL and other types of source code.  This is the HILITE
  command.  In order to use it, the PC 3270 Emulator tool must
  support 'Extended Attributes', and the user must choose a
  terminal type that includes extended attributes.  Then, when
  editing a COBOL member, type "HI AUTO" on the commend line.
  The result should be that the COBOL source becomes colored.
  Immediately after, type "HI", which will bring up a dialog
  where there are choices that can be selected using a "/".
  Select all of them.  Then, while still in this dialog, use the
  Action Bar choice "Languages", and type the number for COBOL,
  then press Enter.  Yet another dialog will be started to allow
  you to specify color choices for various COBOL elements.  The
  important one right now is "Special Characters".  Choose a
  distinctive color and under hilighting, type "Reverse".  Then,
  go to the next field, "Special Characters to highlight" and
  enter the period and the semicolon.  Save the changes by
  pressing the End function key.  When you return to viewing
  your program, you'll see that all of the periods are enclosed
  in a character box, and are extremely visible.  These settings
  will be saved in the user's profile data set for use with
  every Edit or View of a COBOL program.

3.Using NOT:
  If you've ever coded 'NOT >' or 'NOT <' relational operators,
  it is time to stop, because 1985 ANSI COBOL allows coding '<='
  (less than or equal to, the equivalent of not greater than)
  and '>=' (greater than or equal to, the equivalent of not less
  than).  Many programmers and theorists believe that humans
  have a hard time understanding the use of NOT.
                    Procedure Division Ideas

4.Improving PERFORM Readability:

  Sequences of code which require repetitive (loop controlled)
  execution and are used only once in a program used to have to
  be coded as a separate procedure  (paragraph or section).  In
  1985 ANSI COBOL, they may be written as part of an 'in line
  PERFORM'.  Using this option allows the programmer to avoid
  creating another procedure name and also puts the code where
  it is actually used, like a FORTRAN DO-loop.  Here is a 1974
  to 1985 ANSI COBOL comparison:
  Old:
        PERFORM  MAKE-ROOM-IN-TABLE
                 VARYING 'index'       FROM 'index' BY -1
                 UNTIL   'index'         <  1
                    OR   'new-entry-key' >  'entry-key(index)'
        MOVE     'new-entry'             TO 'entry(index + 1)'
        ...
    MAKE-ROOM-IN-TABLE  SECTION.
        MOVE     'entry(index)'          TO 'entry(index + 1)'
        .
  New:
        PERFORM
                 VARYING 'index'       FROM 'index' BY -1
                 UNTIL   'index'         <  1
                    OR   'new-entry-key' >  'entry-key(index)'
          MOVE   'entry(index)'          TO 'entry(index + 1)'
        END-PERFORM
        MOVE     'new-entry'             TO 'entry(index + 1)'
        ...

5.Using 'switches' or 'flags':

  Define the item where such a 'state variable' is stored
  without a data name, and change  the state with SET
  statements.  One example of needing such a variable might be
  in loading a table in a subprogram when it is called for the
  first time.  Testing for zero occurrences in the table
  wouldn't take into account the possibility of an empty input
  file, so some type of first time indicator is needed.  Here is
  a 1974 to 1985 ANSI COBOL comparison:
  Old:
    77    LOAD-TABLE-FLAG       PIC X(01) VALUE 'N'.
      88  TABLE-LOAD-REQD                 VALUE 'N'.
      88  TABLE-LOADED                    VALUE 'Y'.
        ...
        IF       TABLE-LOAD-REQD
                 PERFORM  TABLE-LOAD
                 MOVE     'Y'          TO LOAD-TABLE-FLAG

  New:
    77                          PIC X(01) VALUE 'N'.
      88  TABLE-LOAD-REQD                 VALUE 'N'.
      88  TABLE-LOADED                    VALUE 'Y'.
        ...
        IF       TABLE-LOAD-REQD
                 PERFORM  TABLE-LOAD
                 SET      TABLE-LOADED TO TRUE
        END-IF
  As you can see, there are two advantages to this technique.
  First, the need to invent another data name is avoided.
  Second, the values associated with the 'state' of the variable
  don't have to be known in the Procedure Division; in fact,
  they could be changed to '0' and '1' or anything else without
  requiring any change to the Procedure Division.

                    Procedure Division Ideas

6.   Writing compound conditional expressions:

  Compound conditional expressions (multiple simple conditions
  connected by AND or OR) can be the source of problems in
  programs, especially when maintenance is done by someone not
  familiar with a program.

  One reason is that the AND or OR words usually 'hide' at the
  end of a line.  Consider moving them to the start of the next
  line, and limiting the number of conditions per line to one.
  This will make the 'AND' and 'OR' more visible, and if they
  are aligned under the related 'IF' or 'WHEN', there will be
  more room on the line to write the condition.  Finally, if one
  of the conditions is deleted, or a new condition is added, it
  will usually only be necessary to delete or insert one line,
  and the danger of leaving a 'hanging' AND or OR or forgetting
  to put one in is reduced.  Here is an example:
        IF       TABLE-LOAD-REQD
          AND    TABLE-STATUS          =  '00'
                 PERFORM  TABLE-LOAD
                 SET      TABLE-LOADED TO TRUE
        END-IF

7.Writing expressions:

  Expressions used in COMPUTE, IF, and elsewhere are handled by
  COBOL compilers according to a set of rules which control the
  order of evaluation, such as those for AND and OR in
  conditional expressions and those for '**', '*', '/', '+', and
  '-' in arithmetic expressions.

  Parentheses are required only to force expressions to be
  evaluated in a different order than the rules of COBOL would
  normally cause them to be evaluated.  Some programmers use
  them to 'clarify' a program, so that there will be no doubt
  about the order of evaluation.  You might consider whether
  code which 'needs' such clarification is what you want to
  write.  Sometimes, however, the parentheses are required.  In
  such cases, consider coding each parenthesis on a line by
  itself, or no more than one set of left and right parentheses
  on a line.  The first technique makes the parentheses more
  visible, and the second makes it easier to be sure that they
  are balanced, especially when changing the expression.
  Here is an example:
        COMPUTE  ANSWER =  (
                            C
                        +  (A + 1)
                        *  (B - 2)
                           )
  Remember that the ISPF Editor now can help with this task.
  There is a "HILITE" command available, which can be used to
  colorize COBOL code and check for balanced parentheses.  You
  must have your terminal emulator set up to use "Extended
  Attributes".
```

#### ↳ Re: What's Missing in a COBOL Style Guide?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-13T00:41:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E6FD423.3F821009@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<3E6FC7EC.8EF66036@attglobal.net>`

```


Colin Campbell wrote:

> Folks,
> You provided lots of great input when I posted a proposed COBOL
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Colin, As it's Mainframe - just a couple of pointers which are fairly
universal :-

>
> 5.Using 'switches' or 'flags':
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
>   requiring any change to the Procedure Division.

My preference, use numerics for 88s. Agreed you can do it with alpha, but :-

01 MYFLAG                          PIC 9(03) VALUE ZERO..
    88 VALIDATION-OK        VALUE 0.
    88 ERROR-NAME             VALUE 1.
    88 ERROR-ZIPCODE     VALUE 2.
    88 ERROR-                         VALUE 3. ETC....

     88 WARNING-ERRORS   VALUE 1 THRU 9
     88 CRITICAL-ERRORS     VALUE 20 THRU 35.

Groupings like VALIDATION-OK, WARNING-ERRORS and CRITICAL-ERRORS allow you
to make specific decisions.

Doesn't always work avoiding that "need to invent another data name". (OK -
so now I'm going back to lowercase - phew !). Here's one I'm currently
testing with sorting collections into different sequences:-

01 SequenceFlag                pic 9.
   88 NewByName                value 1.
   88 NewByCountry             value 2.
   88 NewByType                value 3.
   88 ValidSequence            value 1 thru 3.
01 Old-SequenceFlag            pic 9.
   88 OldByName                value 1.
   88 OldByCountry             value 2.
   88 OldByType                value 3.
................
display "Phase 2 - Default Sort using MOVE CORRESPONDING"
move 9 to SequenceFlag
perform CHOICE-PARA until SequenceFlag = zeroes
EXIT METHOD.

CHOICE-PARA.

display " "
display "Select sequence :"
display "(1) By name (2) By Country (3) By Type (0) Quit routine"
accept SequenceFlag

Evaluate true
  when Old-SequenceFlag = SequenceFlag
       display "*** Already in sequence you requested ***"
  when ValidSequence
       move zeroes to Kounter
etc..........

Note that when I have finished doing the callback which displays the
re-sorted list I then move SequenceFlag to Old-SequenceFlag.

>
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Some have a tendency to 'bracket' the conditions - and I think that's a good
idea for clarity :-

        IF      ( TABLE-LOAD-REQD)  AND
                  ( TABLE-STATUS  =  '00')
                    etc.,,,,,,

Just my $0.02

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

"James J. Gavan" wrote:

>
>
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB

I'm sorry, I cannot see any "clarity" gain by placing parentheses around a
condition-name or a simple condition, just because there is a logical connector
between them.  You can't fool the compiler, who won't mind either way of writing
it.  But I don't think this would help a human reader, either.

Even if a program coded the IF this way, it would still be my preference to put
the logical connector at the start of the line, to make it more visible.
Colin
```

I'll restrict these comments to what I see wrong rather than missing. 

>2.Aligning procedural statements:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

No, no. Goofy spacing doesn't make a program more readable.

>3.Using NOT:
>  If you've ever coded 'NOT >' or 'NOT <' relational operators,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

It's true that humans have a hard time understanding NOT. We programmers who
understand Boolean algebra don't. Constructs such as <= contain an unnecessary
OR whereas not > is a single condition.  Please delete this paragraph. 

>7.Writing expressions:

Forget the 'rules of COBOL'. Parentheses should always be written when a
statement contains a mixture of AND and OR, or +/- and multiply or divide.

Robert

divide))

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What's Missing in a COBOL Style Guide?

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-13T16:14:41
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e709310_1@text-west.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<3E6FC7EC.8EF66036@attglobal.net> <3e7005d6.243228562@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert/Colin,

I simply haven't had time to read the whole Guide, however I have quickly
browsed the responses.

I agree with Thane that the TONE is right.

30 years ago I had to formulate exactly such a guide for a COBOL shop in New
Zealand. (I still have a copy of it somewhere in the attic <G>...was so
proud of it I kept it). I remember that then as now, the secret is to
ENCOURAGE programmers (rather than COERCE them) into agreeing a consistent
coding style.

Colin's document, in its introduction, does this.

Programmers are usually intelligent people who don't react well to words
like "MANDATORY" "REQUIRED" or "YOU WILL DO THIS".

In hindsight ,with broader and longer experience, having seen many flame
wars over style, I have a much more relaxed attitude now than I had even
then. But I can afford that indulgence; most corporate shops can't. We do
need standards and gudelines for consistency, but we need to be open minded
and gentle in enforcing them.

I have to say that I also endorse 100% Robert's comments  on points 3 and 7
below. (I don't really care about "goofy spacing"...)

I too am from the school where programmers were required to learn Logic, and
Boolean Algebra and Propositional Calculus are both very dear to my heart
(as previous posts here have demonstrated.)

There is nothing difficult about learning the proper Boolean use of
Negation, and a single day is more than enough for any programmer who wants
to learn the basics of  AND, OR, NOT, De Morgan's Laws (which many old hands
learn by bitter experience without even knowing they learned it <G>) and the
use of Boolean simplification to take the knots out of complex conditions
and nested IFs. I have run such courses in-house on countless occasions. If
anyone here is interested in a tutorial or would like more on this, simply
indicate your interest and I'll gladly provide it.

I think it would be a pity to see NOT being banned in relational conditions
because it is considered by "humans" <G> to simply be a means of saying
"greater than or equal to" or "less than or equal to" or "unequal to"...

For the same reason, I hate to see flags being set to "Y" or "N" when 1
indicates TRUE and 0 indicates FALSE. (I hate it even more when certain
languages corrupt this pure concept into letting a negative value indicate
FALSE, just because it is easier to implement on certain hardware...). But
that's just me, and those are simply MY preferences. I don't think it is
RIGHT or WRONG to set flags to "Y" or "N" (as Paul Simon says, "...I have no
opinion about that...").

Pete.

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e7005d6.243228562@news.optonline.net...
> Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net> wrote:
>
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> It's true that humans have a hard time understanding NOT. We programmers
who
> understand Boolean algebra don't. Constructs such as <= contain an
unnecessary
> OR whereas not > is a single condition.  Please delete this paragraph.
>
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> Robert

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>I remember that then as now, the secret is to
>ENCOURAGE programmers (rather than COERCE them) into agreeing a consistent
>coding style.

The shops I managed for 20+ years had no published programming standards. Yet
style was so standardized that you couldn't tell from reading code who wrote it.
I accomplished it through social pressure (pride) and deliberately assigning
maintenance to someone other than the original author. They knew the code they
wrote today would need to be understood and modified by a cohort tomorrow. 

>Colin's document, in its introduction, does this.
>
>Programmers are usually intelligent people who don't react well to words
>like "MANDATORY" "REQUIRED" or "YOU WILL DO THIS".

That's ok if the Standards author knows what he's talking about, and
substantiates strictures with examples. If he doesn't, we'll find ways around
the Standard. 

>In hindsight ,with broader and longer experience, having seen many flame
>wars over style, I have a much more relaxed attitude now than I had even
>then. But I can afford that indulgence; most corporate shops can't. We do
>need standards and gudelines for consistency, but we need to be open minded
>and gentle in enforcing them.

Mainframe shops are seldom 'gentle in enforcing them'. 

>There is nothing difficult about learning the proper Boolean use of
>Negation, and a single day is more than enough for any programmer who wants
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>indicate your interest and I'll gladly provide it.

I wrote one in Best Practices. Nobody commented on it. 

>I think it would be a pity to see NOT being banned in relational conditions
>because it is considered by "humans" <G> to simply be a means of saying
>"greater than or equal to" or "less than or equal to" or "unequal to"...

Computer: a machine not as intelligent as a human being but more intelligent
than a programmer.  :)

>For the same reason, I hate to see flags being set to "Y" or "N" when 1
>indicates TRUE and 0 indicates FALSE. (I hate it even more when certain
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>opinion about that...").

I use flags extensively. I always use low-value as false. True value may be 'y'
or may be a letter representing the proposition. It is never 1.  I don't see
what difference it makes.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What's Missing in a COBOL Style Guide?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-14T12:38:32
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e71b1e6_2@text-west.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<3E6FC7EC.8EF66036@attglobal.net> <3e7005d6.243228562@news.optonline.net> <3e709310_1@text-west.newsgroups.com> <3e71366b.321213406@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e71366b.321213406@news.optonline.net...
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> I use flags extensively. I always use low-value as false. True value may
be 'y'
> or may be a letter representing the proposition. It is never 1.  I don't
see
> what difference it makes.
>
The difference is that 1 represents the multiplicative identity  and 0
represents the additive identity in ANY algebra. In a Boolean Algebra,  the
multiplicative identity represents universal truth; and the additive
identity represents the negation of this.

Like I said, these are preferences... it really doesn't matter.

Pete.
