<a id="t-16a931fc96"></a>
## Thread: Standard COBOL contrasted with OOCOBOL — 34 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo) · [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Standard COBOL contrasted with OOCOBOL

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-03-17T23:13:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c2ecda$c3c839e0$74c2f943@chottel>`

```
A while back someone asked to see a standard COBOL program and an OOCOBOL
program that solved the same problem.

Mike Murach's Structured COBOL book chapter 17 has an inquiry program,
Inq1000.cbl, written in MF COBOL using MF extensions for processing screen
I-O. I will post the listing as a reply to this posting.

The same book chapter 22 has an OOCOBOL solution to the same problem. It
consists of 5 classes: Bookinq.cbl, Bookmng.cbl, Bookfrm.cbl, Bookui.cbl,
and Bookinv.cbl. I will post them in another reply to this posting.

Quoting from th book page 740:

"After reviewing the oo program presented you may be wondering whether oo
programming is worth the trouble. The 4 page program presented in chapter
17 took nine pages of code when converted to OO code. Keep in mind that if
you design your classes with resuability in mind, you should be able to use
the same classes and methods in other applications.

Also keep in mind that oo COBOL in not an all or nothing proposition. You
can use it when it makes sense and use standard COBOL when it does not. 
.....

Two situations where oo programming almost always makes sense were
illustrated. First a file manager class could be used to perform file i/o
for any file. .....

Another situation is illustrated by the book inventory class. A class like
this can hold the data for a single record from any file.  Then its methods
can perform any required operations on that data, ......"

I hope this gives a sample flavor of the differences.  Of course the
explanations in the book would be of great help but typing in 2 whole
chapters is too much for me at this time. You could always get the book
from the library if you don't want to buy it.
```

#### ↳ Re: Standard COBOL contrasted with OOCOBOL

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-03-17T23:15:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c2ecda$f3204840$74c2f943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<01c2ecda$c3c839e0$74c2f943@chottel>`

```
Here is Inq1000.cbl:
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.

       PROGRAM-ID. INQ1000.

       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.

       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.

       FILE-CONTROL.

           SELECT INVMAST  ASSIGN TO "c:\data\cobol\invmast.dat"
                           ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
                           ACCESS IS RANDOM
                           RECORD KEY IS IM-ITEM-NO.

       DATA DIVISION.

       FILE SECTION.

       FD  INVMAST.

       01  INVENTORY-MASTER-RECORD.

            05  IM-ITEM-NO              PIC X(5).
            05  IM-DESCRIPTIVE-DATA.
                10  IM-ITEM-DESC        PIC X(40).
                10  IM-UNIT-COST        PIC 999V99.
                10  IM-UNIT-PRICE       PIC 999V99.
            05  IM-INVENTORY-DATA.
                10  IM-REORDER-POINT    PIC S9(5).
                10  IM-ON-HAND          PIC S9(5).
                10  IM-ON-ORDER         PIC S9(5).

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

       78  BLACK           VALUE 0.
       78  WHITE           VALUE 7.
       78  BRIGHT-WHITE    VALUE 15.

       01  SWITCHES.

           05  END-OF-INQUIRIES-SWITCH     PIC X   VALUE "N".
               88  END-OF-INQUIRIES                VALUE "Y".
           05  VALID-ENTRY-SWITCH          PIC X   VALUE "N".
               88  VALID-ENTRY                     VALUE "Y".
           05  ITEM-FOUND-SWITCH           PIC X   VALUE "Y".
               88  ITEM-FOUND                      VALUE "Y".
           05  NEXT-ITEM-SWITCH            PIC X   VALUE "Y".
               88  NEXT-ITEM                       VALUE "Y".

       01  SCREEN-DISPLAY-FIELDS.

           05  ERROR-MESSAGE       PIC X(80)   VALUE SPACE.
           05  UNIT-COST           PIC ZZZ.ZZ.
           05  UNIT-PRICE          PIC ZZZ.ZZ.
           05  REORDER-POINT       PIC ZZ,ZZZ.
           05  ON-HAND             PIC ZZ,ZZZ.
           05  ON-ORDER            PIC ZZ,ZZZ.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.

       000-PROCESS-INVENTRY-INQUIRIES.

           OPEN INPUT INVMAST.
           PERFORM 100-PROCESS-INVENTORY-INQUIRY
               UNTIL END-OF-INQUIRIES.
           CLOSE INVMAST.
           STOP RUN.

       100-PROCESS-INVENTORY-INQUIRY.

           PERFORM 110-DISPLAY-HEADING-LINES.
           MOVE "N" TO VALID-ENTRY-SWITCH.
           MOVE SPACE TO IM-ITEM-NO.
           PERFORM 120-GET-ITEM-NUMBER
               UNTIL VALID-ENTRY.
           IF NOT END-OF-INQUIRIES
               PERFORM 180-DISPLAY-ITEM-DATA
               MOVE "N" TO VALID-ENTRY-SWITCH
               PERFORM 190-GET-NEXT-ITEM
                   UNTIL VALID-ENTRY.

       110-DISPLAY-HEADING-LINES.

           DISPLAY "Inventory inquiry" AT LINE 1 COLUMN 1
               WITH BLANK SCREEN
               FOREGROUND-COLOR BLACK
               BACKGROUND-COLOR WHITE.
           DISPLAY "Type an item number. Then press Enter."
               AT LINE 3 COLUMN 1.

       120-GET-ITEM-NUMBER.

           MOVE "Y" TO VALID-ENTRY-SWITCH.
           PERFORM 130-ACCEPT-ITEM-NUMBER.
           PERFORM 140-EDIT-ITEM-NUMBER.
           IF NOT VALID-ENTRY
               PERFORM 160-DISPLAY-ERROR-MESSAGE
           ELSE
               PERFORM 170-DISPLAY-BLANK-LINE.

       130-ACCEPT-ITEM-NUMBER.

           DISPLAY "Item number. . . . . " AT LINE 5 COLUMN 1.
           DISPLAY "Enter 99999 to end." AT LINE 24 COLUMN 1.
           ACCEPT IM-ITEM-NO AT LINE 5 COLUMN 22
               WITH PROMPT.

       140-EDIT-ITEM-NUMBER.

           IF IM-ITEM-NO = "99999"
               MOVE "Y" TO END-OF-INQUIRIES-SWITCH
           ELSE IF IM-ITEM-NO = SPACE
               MOVE "N" TO VALID-ENTRY-SWITCH
               MOVE "An item number is required." TO ERROR-MESSAGE
           ELSE
               MOVE "Y" TO ITEM-FOUND-SWITCH
               PERFORM 150-READ-INVENTORY-RECORD
               IF NOT ITEM-FOUND
                   MOVE "N" TO VALID-ENTRY-SWITCH
                   MOVE "Inventory record not found."
                       TO ERROR-MESSAGE.

       150-READ-INVENTORY-RECORD.

           READ INVMAST
               INVALID KEY
                   MOVE "N" TO ITEM-FOUND-SWITCH.

       160-DISPLAY-ERROR-MESSAGE.

           DISPLAY ERROR-MESSAGE AT LINE 23 COLUMN 1
               WITH FOREGROUND-COLOR BRIGHT-WHITE BEEP.

       170-DISPLAY-BLANK-LINE.

           DISPLAY SPACES AT LINE 23 COLUMN 1.

       180-DISPLAY-ITEM-DATA.

           MOVE IM-UNIT-COST TO UNIT-COST.
           MOVE IM-UNIT-PRICE TO UNIT-PRICE.
           MOVE IM-REORDER-POINT TO REORDER-POINT.
           MOVE IM-ON-HAND TO ON-HAND.
           MOVE IM-ON-ORDER TO ON-ORDER.
           DISPLAY "Description:"   LINE 7  COLUMN 1.
           DISPLAY IM-ITEM-DESC     LINE 7  COLUMN 22.
           DISPLAY "Unit cost:"     LINE 8  COLUMN 1.
           DISPLAY UNIT-COST        LINE 8  COLUMN 22.
           DISPLAY "Unit price:"    LINE 9  COLUMN 1.
           DISPLAY UNIT-PRICE       LINE 9  COLUMN 22.
           DISPLAY "Reorder point:" LINE 10 COLUMN 1.
           DISPLAY REORDER-POINT    LINE 10 COLUMN 22.
           DISPLAY "On hand:"       LINE 11 COLUMN 1.
           DISPLAY ON-HAND          LINE 11 COLUMN 22.
           DISPLAY "On order:"      LINE 12 COLUMN 1.
           DISPLAY ON-ORDER         LINE 12 COLUMN 22.

       190-GET-NEXT-ITEM.

           MOVE "Y" TO VALID-ENTRY-SWITCH.
           PERFORM 200-ACCEPT-NEXT-ITEM.
           PERFORM 210-EDIT-NEXT-ITEM.
           IF NOT VALID-ENTRY
               PERFORM 160-DISPLAY-ERROR-MESSAGE
           ELSE
               PERFORM 170-DISPLAY-BLANK-LINE.

       200-ACCEPT-NEXT-ITEM.

           DISPLAY "Do you want to display another item?"
               LINE 24 COLUMN 1.
           ACCEPT NEXT-ITEM-SWITCH AT LINE 24 COLUMN 38
               WITH FOREGROUND-COLOR BRIGHT-WHITE UPPER.

       210-EDIT-NEXT-ITEM.

           IF NOT NEXT-ITEM
               IF NEXT-ITEM-SWITCH = "N"
                   MOVE "Y" TO END-OF-INQUIRIES-SWITCH
               ELSE
                   MOVE "N" TO VALID-ENTRY-SWITCH
                   MOVE "You must enter 'Y' or 'N'" TO ERROR-MESSAGE.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Standard COBOL contrasted with OOCOBOL

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-18T01:04:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E766EF2.6AF8512B@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<01c2ecda$c3c839e0$74c2f943@chottel> <01c2ecda$f3204840$74c2f943@chottel>`

```


Charles Hottel wrote:

> Here is Inq1000.cbl:
>        IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.

<snip>

>
> >
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> >

Charles,

Will be interested to see his OO programs - when you've typed them <G>

Above makes good sense, but ignoring all the $10 words, (encapsulation,
polymorphism etc.) , the most important word above is R-E-U-S-A-B-I-L-I-T-Y. If
you accept that OO is the 'easiest' tool for GUIs/Web etc., that's only part of
the OO story. Why switch to OO just as a gimmick ? As developers we have to be
looking for 'speedier' development - and it's the repetition from Reusability
that counts, apparent somewhat in the short term but definitely there in the
long term.

I've found it advantageous to stick with class format rather than intermingling
'Procedural' programs. Too detailed to support here, but I definitely find the
class approach has additional benefits. (But again nothing's wrong - your
style, use the approach that you are comfortable with).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standard COBOL contrasted with OOCOBOL

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-18T08:16:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vBAda.11785$ja4.810404@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<01c2ecda$c3c839e0$74c2f943@chottel> <01c2ecda$f3204840$74c2f943@chottel> <3e76a6ae.49870000@news.optonline.net>`

```

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message news:3e76a6ae.49870000@news.optonline.net...
> "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote:
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> What is the deal with three digit numbers prefixing paragraph names?

Enumeration facilitates the grouping of functionally similar paragraphs,
giving a structured nature and overall cohesiveness to the program.

I use an alphanumeric prefix scheme, A-0000- thru Z-9999.
This allows me to easily group similar paragraphs, and also helps me 
to quickly determine if I have already coded a specific piece of performed code,
and also helps me, or anyone else, to quickly locate the particular section
which contains this logic, and, of equal if not more importance, all similar logic.

I can use a specific letter to preface all of my database call logic,
another for checkpoint/restart, another for exception processing, etc.
I can restrict inline logic to paragraphs A through whatever, 
and use the remaining characters for common performed routines,
such as database calls, exception processing, etc.

Coupled with consistent paragraph-naming practices, this makes it much
easier for me to control the construction of a program.  I rarely have to wonder 
what Z-0250-ORDERSLI-MISS is supposed to do, or Z-0680-CREDITCK-FAIL, 
or where in the program they should be/are located.

> Wouldn't they mean the same without the prefixes?

It's a matter of choice.  I could have multiple associated paragraphs in my program, 
A-1260-CREDITCK-FAIL, B-1320-CREDITCK-FAIL, C-1140-CREDITCK-FAIL etc,
and Z-0680-CREDITCK-FAIL, and instantly recognize that the A-1260/B-1320/C-11140 routines
are in-line processing paragraphs, each executed for one of several input transaction types, 
while the associated Z-0680 routine is my common exception processing for all transaction types,
performed from each of the aforementioned inline paragraphs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standard COBOL contrasted with OOCOBOL

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standard COBOL contrasted with OOCOBOL

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-18T16:36:37
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e772fb1_1@text-west.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<01c2ecda$c3c839e0$74c2f943@chottel> <01c2ecda$f3204840$74c2f943@chottel> <3e76a6ae.49870000@news.optonline.net>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e76a6ae.49870000@news.optonline.net...
> "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote:
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Wouldn't they mean the same without the prefixes?

It's a fair question.

The 3 digits are useful for a human to locate a PERFORMed paragraph. They
generally follow the "flow" of the program and you can be reasonably sure
that  400-whatever will appear in the source code AFTER 200-something. Some
people extend this to encompass all sorts of extra stuff (like a special
"group" for "mainline" paragraphs/sections, another "group" for
"subroutines" that are activated from "above", and so on.)

The main reason for using them is that if you are called in on the late
shift (or have given birth 20 minutes previously <G>) you don't have to
think too hard about where a given paragraph/section is located in source.

Despite JerryMouse's laconic assertion to the contrary, the fact is that
these prefixes make no difference to the actual "meaning" of the name, as
interpreted by the computer.

Pete.
```


#### ↳ Re: Standard COBOL contrasted with OOCOBOL

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-03-17T23:19:19+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c2ecdb$82b6b480$74c2f943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<01c2ecda$c3c839e0$74c2f943@chottel>`

```
Bookinq.cbl:

       Identification Division.

       Program-id. Bookinq.

       Environment Division.

       Object Section.

       Class-Control.
          BookMng is class "bookmng".

       Data Division.

       Working-Storage Section.

       01  handles.
           05  bookMngObj       object reference.

       Procedure Division.

           Invoke BookMng "New" returning bookMngObj.
           Invoke bookMngObj "process-inquiries".
           Stop Run.

Bookmng.cbl:

       Identification Division.
       Class-id.  BookMng
                  inherits from Base.

       Environment Division.
       Object Section.
       Class-Control.
           BookMng     is class "bookmng"
           BookUI      is class "bookui"
           BookFM      is class "bookfm"
           BookInv     is class "bookinv"
           Base        is class "Base".

       Object.
           Data Division.
           Working-Storage Section.

           01  handles          object reference.
               05  bookUIObj.
               05  bookFMObj.
               05  bookInvObj.

           01  switches.
               05  end-of-inquiries-switch     pic x(01)  value "N".
                   88  end-of-inquiries                   value "Y".

           01  error-message                   pic x(50).

           01  book-data.
               05  bk-item-number              pic x(05).
               05  bk-descriptive-data.
                   10  bk-item-desc            pic x(40).
                   10  bk-unit-cost            pic 9(03)v99.
                   10  bk-unit-price           pic 9(03)v99.
               05  bk-inventory-data.
                   10  bk-reorder-point        pic s9(05).
                   10  bk-on-hand              pic s9(05).
                   10  bk-on-order             pic s9(05).

           Procedure Division.
      **********************************************************
           Method-ID. "process-inquiries".
           Procedure Division.

               invoke BookUI "New" returning bookUIObj
               invoke BookFM "New" returning bookFMObj

               invoke bookFMObj "open-file"
               invoke bookUIObj "display-heading"

               perform until end-of-inquiries
                  invoke bookUIObj "get-item-number"
                      returning bk-item-number
                  if bk-item-number = "99999"
                      set end-of-inquiries to true
                  else
                      invoke bookFMObj "create-book"
                          using bk-item-number
                          returning bookInvObj
                      if bookInvObj = null
                          move "Inventory record not found."
                              to error-message
                          invoke bookUIObj "display-error-message"
                              using error-message
                      else
                          invoke bookUIObj "display-blank-line"
                          invoke bookInvObj "get-desc-info"
                              returning bk-descriptive-data
                          invoke bookUIObj "display-desc-info"
                              using bk-descriptive-data
                          invoke bookInvObj "get-inv-info"
                              returning bk-inventory-data
                          invoke bookUIObj "display-inv-info"
                              using bk-inventory-data
                          invoke bookUIObj "get-next-item"
                              returning end-of-inquiries-switch
                          if not end-of-inquiries
                              invoke bookUIObj "display-heading"
                          end-if
                      end-if
                  end-if
               end-perform

               invoke bookFMObj "close-file"

           End Method "process-inquiries".

      ***********************************************************
       End Object.
       End Class BookMng.

Bookfm.cbl:

       Identification Division.
       Class-id.  BookFM
                  inherits from Base.
       Environment Division.
       Input-Output Section.
       File-Control.
           select INVMAST assign to "c:\cobol\data\invmasti.dat"
                          organization is indexed
                          access is random
                          record key is im-item-no.

       Object Section.
       Class-Control.
           BookFM  is class "bookfm"
           BookInv is class "bookinv"
           Base    is class "Base".

       Data Division.
       File Section.
       fd  invmast.
       01  inventory-master-record.
           05  im-item-no              pic x(05).
           05  im-descriptive-data     pic x(50).
           05  im-inventory-data       pic x(15).

       Object.

           Procedure Division.
      ***********************************************************
           Method-ID. "open-file".
           Procedure Division.
               open input invmast.
           End Method "open-file".
      ***********************************************************
           Method-ID. "close-file".
           Procedure Division.
               close invmast.
           End Method "close-file".
      ***********************************************************
           Method-ID. "create-book".
           Data Division.
           Linkage Section.
           01  ls-item-no     pic x(05).
           01  ls-bookInvObj  object reference.
           Procedure Division using ls-item-no
                              returning ls-bookInvObj.
               move ls-item-no to im-item-no
               read invmast
                   invalid key
                       set ls-bookInvObj to null
                   not invalid key
                       invoke BookInv "New"
                           returning ls-bookInvObj
                       invoke ls-bookInvObj "set-book-info"
                           using inventory-master-record
               end-read.
           End Method "create-book".
      ***********************************************************
       End Object.
       End Class BookFM.

Bookui.cbl:

       Identification Division.
       Class-id.  BookUI
                  inherits from Base.
       Environment Division.
       Object Section.
       Class-Control.
           BookUI is class "bookui"
           Base   is class "Base".

       Object.

           Data Division.
           Working-Storage Section.
           78  black           value 0.
           78  white           value 7.
           78  bright-white    value 15.

           Procedure Division.
      ***********************************************************
           Method-ID. "get-item-number".
           Data Division.

           Local-Storage Section.
           01  switches.
               05  valid-entry-switch        pic x(01) value "N".
                   88  valid-entry                     value "Y".

           01  error-message                 pic x(50).

           Linkage Section.
           01  ls-item-number                pic x(05).

           Procedure Division returning ls-item-number.

               move "N"    to valid-entry-switch.
               move spaces to ls-item-number.
               perform until valid-entry
                 display "Item number. . . . . " at line 5  column 1
                 display "Enter 99999 to end."   at line 24 column 1
                 accept ls-item-number at line 5 column 22
                     with prompt
                 if ls-item-number = space
                    move "An item number is required." to
                         error-message
                    invoke self "display-error-message"
                        using error-message
                 else
                    set valid-entry to true
                 end-if
               end-perform.

           End Method "get-item-number".
      ***********************************************************
           Method-ID. "get-next-item".
           Data Division.

           Local-Storage Section.
           01  switches.
               05  next-item-switch          pic x(01) value " ".
                   88  next-item                       value "Y".

           01  error-message                 pic x(50).

           Linkage Section.
           01  ls-end-of-inquiries-switch    pic x(01)  value "N".

           Procedure Division returning ls-end-of-inquiries-switch.

               move " " to next-item-switch.
               perform until next-item-switch = "Y" or "N"
                 display "Do you want to display another item?"
                     line 24 column 1
                 accept next-item-switch at line 24 column 38
                     with foreground-color bright-white upper
                 evaluate next-item-switch
                    when "Y"
                       move "N" to ls-end-of-inquiries-switch
                    when "N"
                       move "Y" to ls-end-of-inquiries-switch
                    when other
                       move "You must enter a 'Y' or 'N'." to
                            error-message
                       invoke self "display-error-message"
                           using error-message
                 end-evaluate
               end-perform.

           End Method "get-next-item".
      ***********************************************************
           Method-ID. "display-heading".
           Procedure Division.

               display "Inventory inquiry" at line 1 column 1
                   with blank screen
                   foreground-color black
                   background-color white.
               display "Type an item number. Then press Enter."
                   at line 3 column 1.

           End Method "display-heading".
      ***********************************************************
           Method-ID. "display-desc-info".
           Data Division.

           Local-Storage Section.
           01  screen-display-fields.
               05  unit-cost             pic zzz.zz.
               05  unit-price            pic zzz.zz.

           Linkage Section.
           01  ls-descriptive-data.
               05  ls-item-desc        pic x(40).
               05  ls-unit-cost        pic 9(03)v99.
               05  ls-unit-price       pic 9(03)v99.

           Procedure Division using ls-descriptive-data.

               move ls-unit-cost  to unit-cost.
               move ls-unit-price to unit-price.
               display "Description:"       line 7  column 1.
               display ls-item-desc         line 7  column 22.
               display "Unit cost:"         line 8  column 1.
               display unit-cost            line 8  column 22.
               display "Unit price:"        line 9  column 1.
               display unit-price           line 9  column 22.

           End Method "display-desc-info".
      ***********************************************************
           Method-ID. "display-inv-info".
           Data Division.

           Local-Storage Section.
           01  screen-display-fields.
               05  reorder-point         pic zz,zzz.
               05  on-hand               pic zz,zzz.
               05  on-order              pic zz,zzz.

           Linkage Section.
           01  ls-inventory-data.
               05  ls-reorder-point    pic s9(05).
               05  ls-on-hand          pic s9(05).
               05  ls-on-order         pic s9(05).

           Procedure Division using ls-inventory-data.

               move ls-reorder-point to reorder-point.
               move ls-on-hand       to on-hand.
               move ls-on-order      to on-order.
               display "Reorder point:"     line 10 column 1.
               display reorder-point        line 10 column 22.
               display "On hand:"           line 11 column 1.
               display on-hand              line 11 column 22.
               display "On order:"          line 12 column 1.
               display on-order             line 12 column 22.

           End Method "display-inv-info".
      ***********************************************************
           Method-ID. "display-error-message".
           Data Division.

           Linkage Section.
           01  ls-error-message           pic x(50).

           Procedure Division using ls-error-message.

               display ls-error-message at line 23 column 1
                   with foreground-color bright-white beep.

           End Method "display-error-message".
      ***********************************************************
           Method-ID. "display-blank-line".
           Procedure Division.

               display spaces at line 23 column 1.

           End Method "display-blank-line".
      ***********************************************************
       End Object.
       End Class BookUI.

Bookinv.cbl:

       Identification Division.
       Class-id.  BookInv
                  inherits from Base.
       Environment Division.
       Object Section.
       Class-Control.
           BookInv   is class "bookinv"
           Base      is class "Base".

       Object.
           Data Division.
           Working-Storage Section.
           01  book-data.
               05  bk-item-no                    pic x(05).
               05  bk-descriptive-data.
                   10  bk-item-desc              pic x(40).
                   10  bk-unit-cost              pic 9(03)v99.
                   10  bk-unit-price             pic 9(03)v99.
               05  bk-inventory-data.
                   10  bk-reorder-point          pic s9(05).
                   10  bk-on-hand                pic s9(05).
                   10  bk-on-order               pic s9(05).

           Procedure Division.
      ***********************************************************
           Method-ID. "set-book-info".
           Data Division.
           Linkage Section.
           01  ls-book-data.
               05  ls-item-no              pic x(05).
               05  ls-descriptive-data     pic x(50).
               05  ls-inventory-data       pic x(15).
           Procedure Division using ls-book-data.
               move ls-book-data to book-data.
           End Method "set-book-info".
      ***********************************************************
           Method-ID. "get-desc-Info".
           Data Division.
           Linkage Section.
           01  ls-descriptive-data.
               05  ls-item-desc        pic x(40).
               05  ls-unit-cost        pic 9(03)v99.
               05  ls-unit-price       pic 9(03)v99.
           Procedure Division returning ls-descriptive-data.
               move bk-descriptive-data to ls-descriptive-data.
           End Method "get-desc-info".
      ***********************************************************
           Method-ID. "get-inv-info".
           Data Division.
           Linkage Section.
           01  ls-inventory-data.
               05  ls-reorder-point    pic s9(05).
               05  ls-on-hand          pic s9(05).
               05  ls-on-order         pic s9(05).
           Procedure Division returning ls-inventory-data.
               move bk-inventory-data to ls-inventory-data.
           End Method "get-inv-info".
      ***********************************************************
       End Object.
       End Class BookInv.
```

```
Perhaps, but at least two of the five classes are reuseable so they are
only overhead the first time that you write them. That is why I quoted a
little from the book because I anticpated knee jerk comments about the
relative sizes of the code.

I personally am hoping for comments from those more experienced in OO COBOL
who might have other ways of implementing this that provide even more
reusability.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standard COBOL contrasted with OOCOBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-18T17:43:42-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b58avg$k0v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<01c2ecda$c3c839e0$74c2f943@chottel> <3e76a3ca.49129905@news.optonline.net> <01c2eda1$0b6cea40$7f95f243@chottel> <3E77AD33.1BEB130C@shaw.ca>`

```
I know that Jimmy - and almost everyone else - will "cringe" at this, but in
case anyone missed it and would really WANT to do it, page 244 of the 2002
Standard *does* explicitly say

"Within a class definition, the screen section may be specified only in a
factory definition or an instance definition, but not in a method
definition."

i.e. it IS possible to use the "character-based" Accept/Display Screen
Section "stuff" within a 2002 conforming program.  OTOH, the entire Screen
Section and its Accept/Display syntax *is* in the "processor dependent"
section, so this ould only be valid in an implementation that support Screen
Section et al in non-OO environments.
```

```
Bill,

Understand what you are saying, and probably most would not WANT to use Screen
Section - other than how Richard and Donald respectively use Flexus SP2, each in
their own way. Recall, (without knowing the specifics), Richard has built
routines that let him 'assemble' Screen Sections.

It's a useless exercise, but just *searching* to see if some sort of reusability
could be built into the example's Screen Module (bookUI).

He'll correct me if I'm wrong, but I don't think Donald has got his screen stuff
in a method - just the filling of screen fields, referenced from the 'global'
Screen Object.
contained in the Instance.

Based on your quote above :-

"Within a class definition, the screen section may be specified only in a
factory definition or an instance definition, but not in a method
definition."

I'm guessing, without checking, that  the above quote also applies to Files and
Report Writer. As regards files, that ties in with Thane's observation and
recommendation about "killing" W/S and definition of files in methods.

- I just double-checked on what I am doing with COBOL files (ISAM, Seq, LineSeq
and Relative) - I have the file definition in the Instance (Object Section)
.
Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standard COBOL contrasted with OOCOBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-19T12:52:19+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b58f5g$30r$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<01c2ecda$c3c839e0$74c2f943@chottel> <01c2eda1$0b6cea40$7f95f243@chottel> <3E77AD33.1BEB130C@shaw.ca> <b58avg$k0v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3E77B64F.F231D4DE@shaw.ca>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:

> Understand what you are saying, and probably most would not WANT to use
> Screen Section - other than how Richard and Donald respectively use Flexus
> SP2, each in their own way. Recall, (without knowing the specifics),
> Richard has built routines that let him 'assemble' Screen Sections.

My code generators aren't related to SP/2 at all, the two are in  
completely different systems.  They also don't use 'SCREEN SECTION' (though 
I gues they could be modified to do so), but create W-S records of the 
screen layouts and code to do field by field ACCEPT and DISPLAYs using 
X-Open ACCEPT ... AT nnnn.  

I dislike SCREEN SECTION because it just doesn't fit the way I like to have 
user interfaces.

The W-S records show another use of FILLER as 'not for this field'. When a 
group is used in a DISPLAY ... AT statement, only the non-FILLER fields are 
displayed so the screen record may be redefined to have just the 
output-only fields or just the i-o fields:

      WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
      01  Screen-01.
          03  FILLER           PIC X(20)  VALUE "   Customer Code:  [".
          03  S1-Customer      PIC X(6)   VALUE SPACES.
          03  FILLER           PIC X(4)   VALUE "]   ".
          03  S1-CustName      PIC X(32)  VALUE SPACES.
          03  FILLER           PIC X(18)  VALUE SPACES.
          .....
      01  Screen-01-All REDEFINES Screen-01    PIC X(1920).

    
            PERFORM Screen-BG               *> set background colour 
                                            *>     eg black on cyan
                                            *>       user selectable
            DISPLAY Screen-01-All  AT 0101  *> displays all 1920 characters
            PERFORM Screen-FG               *> set foreground colour
                                            *>     eg blue on cyan
            DISPLAY Screen-01      AT 0101  *> only displays non-filler
                                            *>      in blue/cyan
            ....
            PERFORM Screen-IO               *> sets i-o colour eg white/cyan
            DISPLAY S1-Customer AT 0121     
            ACCEPT S1-Customer  AT 0121
            etc

It gets really boring keying in this code, so the generator clicks it off 
in a few seconds.

It is very rarely used these days.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standard COBOL contrasted with OOCOBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-19T04:38:48
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e77d8f6_2@text-west.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<01c2ecda$c3c839e0$74c2f943@chottel> <3e76a3ca.49129905@news.optonline.net> <01c2eda1$0b6cea40$7f95f243@chottel> <3E77AD33.1BEB130C@shaw.ca>`

```

"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message
news:3E77AD33.1BEB130C@shaw.ca...
>
>
>
> Meanwhile let me see if I can trigger another aspect which I recently
mentioned
> here. Donald or Pete, can you improve on the Screen Section class by using
the
> Properties technique for objects as per Donald's  :-
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Jimmy, I'd like to help here but there are several things stopping me...

1. It would take more time than I have at the moment or would be prepared to
spend on it.
2. I don't approve of using  Screen Section code anyway. (If you do, it
should be separated from the Business logic; like, the application code
receives and sends a buffer from and to the separate Screen Section code.
The application contains NO Screen Section code.)
3. I would not write a Class to handle screens, using Screen Section.
4. If the object of this exercise is to demonstrate to Robert that OO is
good, then frankly, it just isn't worth it.

Many years ago (back in the days when I still used MicroFocus COBOL (Version
3, I think...)), I wrote a system that had many screens. I used a separate
module for each screen and the interface was via a buffer, as described
above. Each screen module was specific to that particular screen and
contained all the fields, defined in a Screen Section. The interface buffer
was exactly the same as an IMS/DC buffer with a two byte attribute preceding
each field and the same values used for these attributes as were used on the
mainframe. So you could set an attribute to NUMERIC-HIGH-BRIGHTNESS (for
example...) and the field would appear on the screen intensified with
numeric data. You could also insert the cursor through the attributes and
you could set colours. All of the code to do this was provided by a single
skeleton of the called module. The "screen specific" parts were generated by
a program I wrote called ACEPAINT. This let you "draw" the screen as you
wanted it to look, with coded symbols for ""start fixed" and "start
variable". When you were happy with it you generated it.

The generation phase generated a Screen section, an IMS/DC buffer (like a
combined MID/MOD, but for use on the PC), complete with attributes, a CICS
BMS buffer with attributes (this was really just as a Sales point and
because it was easy to do it...we only had a very small investment in CICS
and the application this was done for did not use it...), and the necessary
DIF/DOF and MID/MOD code to run the screen under IMS/DC on a mainframe. (My
plan was to use the PC to build and debug mainframe code, at a time when
this was still a very novel idea...<G>).  The generated bits were then
merged with the skeleton code and the resulting module compiled. (It could
then be CALLed from the application when required).I had a single process
that could do the whole lot from painting the screen right through to the
compiled screen support module. You could design a screen and generate
support for it in minutes... I thought it was pretty cool...(So did the
people I was working for..)

It all worked beautifully and it was kind of spooky to see a PC sitting
alongside a Mainframe 3270 with the two of them showing identical screen
formats, as the same COBOL application code ran on both machines. (In those
days they were not connected, and were each running the application as a
standalone app. using their own files (In one case, VSAM/KSDS and the other
ISAM...<G>) The only difference was that the mainframe didn't call the
screen support module, it used IMS/DC and DIF/DOF/MID/MOD instead, but the
application code and the interface to the screens was identical.

Today (just like Screen Section in COBOL) it is obsolete.

(Having said that, I s'pose I could revamp it now as OO...stop it! You're
getting me at it!)

The User Interface has come a long way since the early 1980s.

In conscience, I can't be party to perpetuating 20 year old technology as a
Class, even if it DID use Properties properly...<G>, and certainly not to
win an argument that isn't worth the effort. (I would've entered Foodman's
Great Programming Competition if I had that much time to waste...<G>)

Pete.

http://www.newsfeed.com The #1 Newsgroup Service in the World! >100,000 Newsgroups
---= 19 East/West-Coast Specialized Servers - Total Privacy via Encryption =---
```

#### ↳ OOCOBOL "dialects" - MicroFocus vs. IBM (and no doubt more)

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-17T21:52:25-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E76B419.ADFCC02C@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<01c2ecda$c3c839e0$74c2f943@chottel>`

```
Charles,
I've wanted to learn OO COBOL for years.  I tried reading the MicroFocus book.
I've read articles in the computing press.  I quickly copied and downloaded the
program(s) you posted.

I handed them straight into IBM VisualAge COBOL V2.2, and as has happened
before, they wouldn't compile.  MicroFocus COBOL and IBM COBOL don't speak the
same OO language.  Would you, or anyone else here, know how to translate the
code?

If it's needed, I can supply a compiler listing showing the syntax
difficulties.  I hesitate to call them errors, since the compilers all predate
the 2002 standard.  IBM language reference manuals are out there on the Web, so
it should be possible for someone knowledgeable to do a translation.  I wish I
were that person!
Colin

Charles Hottel wrote:

> A while back someone asked to see a standard COBOL program and an OOCOBOL
> program that solved the same problem.
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> chottel@cpcug.org
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OOCOBOL "dialects" - MicroFocus vs. IBM (and no doubt more)

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-18T10:12:39
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b56qv1$7j8$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<01c2ecda$c3c839e0$74c2f943@chottel> <3E76B419.ADFCC02C@attglobal.net>`

```
Colin,

Up until very recently there has been no standard for OO Cobol so each
vendor's implementation was bound to be different. Now that a standard has
been agreed you will find that programs coded to that standard will compile
across different platforms as each vendor falls in line with that standard.
Micro Focus have commited to supporting the standard, and have indeed been
right at the heart of the standards making process.

The Micro Focus documantation can be found at supportline.microfocus.com

Regards,
Paul
www.microfocus.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OOCOBOL "dialects" - MicroFocus vs. IBM (and no doubt more)

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-18T19:52:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E7779C0.5E334A74@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<01c2ecda$c3c839e0$74c2f943@chottel> <3E76B419.ADFCC02C@attglobal.net>`

```


Colin Campbell wrote:

> Charles,
> I've wanted to learn OO COBOL for years.  I tried reading the MicroFocus book.
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I intend to comment separately on what Charles posted. Now in response to your
query. So far as I know VisualAge is an 'early' version of OO. (If he's looking in,
perhaps Lorne up here in the Great White North can confirm). So were Micro Focus
and Hitachi.

Fujitsu followed slightly later, so they were able to pick up on 'future' 2002
features which J4 had already firmed up on in their successive drafts. F/J's
latest offerings were very close to 2002 before it was released. In the case of
M/F, Net Express V4.0 will contain more compatibility with 2002. IBM using
Enterprise - the COBOL OO syntax is fine, just that you use JavaBase and invoke
Java for support utilities..

I don't know if it will help - send me your listings and errors - I can at least
try and see if I can 'bend' the M/F code to suit VisualAge. (Just in case - is
there a VisualAge on-line manual I can refer to about OO syntax ?)

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Standard COBOL contrasted with OOCOBOL

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-18T15:20:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b57dgg$hpm$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<01c2ecda$c3c839e0$74c2f943@chottel>`

```

On 17-Mar-2003, "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote:

> "After reviewing the oo program presented you may be wondering whether oo
> programming is worth the trouble. The 4 page program presented in chapter
> 17 took nine pages of code when converted to OO code. Keep in mind that if
> you design your classes with resuability in mind, you should be able to use
> the same classes and methods in other applications.

But to make this an advantage, you need to work in an environment that doesn't
require extensive testing whenever maintenance is done on an object.    This
isn't hard when the object is some GUI - but is much less likely when the object
is a business rule.

But there are other advantages to OO besides reusability.  If your objects are
language neutral, for instance - you have a huge advantage in interfacing your
"program" with the rest of your world.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Standard COBOL contrasted with OOCOBOL

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-03-18T23:10:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c2eda3$7f1db260$7f95f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<01c2ecda$c3c839e0$74c2f943@chottel> <b57dgg$hpm$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
I don't see where it takes anymore testing to test a class that changes
than it does to test a CALLed program that changes. There are ways of
creating new versions of objects without destroying the functionality of
the  the old object, at least I thought this was one of the problems that
MS COM solved.
```

#### ↳ Re: Standard COBOL contrasted with OOCOBOL

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-21T00:41:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E7A5F73.21CEE5FD@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<01c2ecda$c3c839e0$74c2f943@chottel>`

```


Charles Hottel wrote:

> A while back someone asked to see a standard COBOL program and an OOCOBOL
> program that solved the same problem.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> the same classes and methods in other applications.

First thing to note about Mike Murach's code is that he is using the 'basics' -
he is not invoking any support utilities to do this - other than the reference
to Class Base - which you *must* have, somewhere.

OK Charles you subsequently asked about Reusability - so let's keep it in that
vein.
I could spend hours just writing an application to show this, but I'll
paraphrase, hopefully getting the message across.

First the non-OO program you quoted. Not much retrievable from that to use
elsewhere, other than using copy filess for the FILE-CONTROL and FILE-SECTION
entries.

Following three tier system approach, Mike gives us :-

Trigger Program to get into OO - BookInq - which invokes the BookManager

Class-Control.   BookManager     is class "bookmng"
                         BookUI               is class "bookui" = User
Interface(Screen)
                         BookFile             is class "bookfile"
                         BookRecord        is class "bookrecd".

Note : I've changed those names BookFM and BookInv - I had to read the source
to find out that they meant respectively 'File" and "Records"

Not part of the exercise, but we have to have a data file to test his classes.
So Stage 1 of Reusability - I have to create a test file of data, Line
Sequential, with a text editor. The next thing I do is revamp his BookInquiry
program :-

*>-----------------------------
Program-id. BookInquiry.

Class-Control.     BookManager    is class "bookmng"
                   EventManager   is class "p2emgr"
                   FileCheck      is class "filechck"
                   .
Working-Storage Section.

01 bookManagerObj                object reference.
01 os-Desktop                object reference.
01 os-EventManager           object reference.

01 LineSeqFilename pic x(100) value "c:\book.txt".
01 IsamFilename    pic x(100) value "c:\book.dat".

Procedure Division.

    perform PHASE-1-HOUSEKEEPING
    perform PHASE-2-CHECK-ISAM-EXISTS
    perform PHASE-3-RUN-APPLICATION
    Stop Run.

PHASE-1-HOUSEKEEPING.

*> I need the Desktop, because (Reusability) classes already
*> written need os-Parent for window messaging

 call "apigui"            *> needed for GUIs
 invoke EventManager      "new" returning os-EventManager
 invoke os-EventManager   "initialize"
 invoke os-EventManager   "getDesktop" returning os-Desktop
 .
PHASE-2-CHECK-ISAM-EXISTS

*> pseudo code :-

    invoke Filecheck "checkFileExists" using IsamFilename
         returning result

    if result <> zeroes then write ISAM file using input
       LineSeqFile (LineSeqFilename)

       *> REUSABILITY - the following already exist:-
       *> a) FileCheck
       *> b) ErrorMessages - Windows Error Messaging
       *> c) LineSeq  - see source below
       *> d) ISAMfile - see source below
       *> e) FileErrors -  which contains a comprehensive list of messages
       *>                           for file status used by (c) and (d)

PHASE-3-RUN-APPLICATION.

    Invoke BookManager "New" returning bookManagerObj.
    Invoke bookManagerObj "process-inquiries".

*>--------------------------

The source for the generic LineSequential and ISAM files follows. In the case
of the ISAM there is an *assumption* that all keys can fit into 20 characters.
(Not big enough ? - then make it 50 or whatever). For LineSequential,
Sequential or Relative no such problem - just the Relative Key for the latter.

Just a comment tied into the above 'generic' IsamFile - PrimeKey - would have
to change the record format copyfile to look as follows :-

       01  INVENTORY-MASTER-RECORD.
            05 PRIME-KEY.
                 10  IM-ITEM-NO               PIC X(5).
            `    10  FILLER                         PIC X(15).
            05  IM-DESCRIPTIVE-DATA.
                10  IM-ITEM-DESC        PIC X(40). etc........

Don't know why he chose Pic x(05) for an Item Number - I would have gone with a
pic 9(05). (Maybe he's harking back to the past - in RM/COBOL '74 keys had to
be Pic x ).

*>-----------------isamfile.cbl----------------------------------

Class-id.          ISAMfile
                   inherits from FileErrors.

Class-control.     ISAMfile                is class "isamfile"
                   FileErrors              is class "filerror"
                   .

*> Note : All lines marked +++ MUST HAVE the same value as
*> the variable MaxRecordSize

*>----------------------------------------------------------
FACTORY.
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "new".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 lnk-self                object reference.
Procedure Division returning lnk-self.
   invoke super "new" returning lnk-self
End Method "new".
*>------------------------------------------------------------
End FACTORY.
*>------------------------------------------------------------
OBJECT.
*>------------------------------------------------------------
FILE-CONTROL.

     Select               Data-File
     assign               Data-filename
     organization         indexed
     access               dynamic
     record key           Data-PrimeKey
     file status          ws-FileStatus.

*>---------------------------------------------------------------
FILE SECTION.

FD  Data-File
    record varying from 20 to 1500 characters        *> +++
       depending on ws-RecordSize.
01  Data-record.
    05 Data-PrimeKey       pic x(20).
    05 Data-info           pic x(1480).              *> +++

*>---------------------------------------------------------------
OBJECT-STORAGE SECTION.
copy "LinkErr.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==ws==.
01 Data-filename                      pic x(100).
78 MaxRecordSize                      value 1500.
01 ws-RecordSize                      pic 9(04) value 1.
01 OpenFlag                           pic 9(01) value 0.
   88 FileClosed                      value 0.
   88 FileOpened                      value 1.

*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "closeFile".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==01 lnk==.

Procedure Division returning lnk-SqlResult.

  set ResultOK to true

  if FileOpened
     close Data-file

     if    ws-fileStatus = "00"
           set FileClosed to true

     else  set FileError to true
           invoke super "showFileError" using ws-ErrorParams
     End-if
  End-if

End Method "closeFile".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "deleteFile".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Local-storage section.
01 ls-result                             pic x(4) comp-5.
Linkage section.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==01 lnk==.

Procedure Division returning lnk-SqlResult.

  set ResultOK to true
  call "CBL_DELETE_FILE" using     Data-Filename
                         returning ls-result

  if ls-result <> "00"
     set FileError to true
     invoke super "showFileError" using ws-ErrorParams
  End-if

End Method "deleteFile".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "getFilename".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 lnk-filename                  pic x(100).
Procedure Division returning lnk-filename.
 move Data-filename to lnk-filename
End Method "getFilename".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "openInputFile".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==01 lnk==.

Procedure Division returning lnk-SqlResult.

  set ResultOK to true
  open input Data-file

  if   ws-fileStatus = "00"
       set FileOpened to true

  else set FileError to true
       invoke super "showFileError" using ws-ErrorParams
  End-if

End Method "openInputFile".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "openIOfile".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==01 lnk==.

Procedure Division returning lnk-SqlResult.

  set ResultOK to true
  open I-O Data-file

  if   ws-fileStatus = "00"
       set FileOpened to true

  else set FileError to true
       invoke super "showFileError" using ws-ErrorParams
  End-if

End Method "openIOfile".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "openOutputFile".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==01 lnk==.

Procedure Division returning lnk-SqlResult.

  set ResultOK to true
  open output Data-file

  if   ws-fileStatus = "00"
       set FileOpened to true

  else set FileError to true
       invoke super "showFileError" using ws-ErrorParams
  End-if
End Method "openOutputFile".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "readNext".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 lnk-ReturnValues.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==05 lnk==.
05 lnk-record.
   10 occurs 1 to MaxRecordSize
      depending on ws-RecordSize     pic x.

Procedure Division returning lnk-ReturnValues.

  initialize lnk-ReturnValues
  Read Data-File next record
    At End
      Set fileFinis to True
    Not at End
      Move  Data-record (1:ws-RecordSize)
      to    lnk-record  (1:ws-RecordSize)
  End-Read

  if   ws-fileStatus = "00" or "10"
       continue

  else set FileError to true
       invoke super "showFileError" using ws-ErrorParams
  End-if

End Method "readNext".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "readRecord".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 lnk-PrimeKey                     pic x(20).
01 lnk-Values.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==05 lnk==.
05 lnk-record.
   10 occurs 1 to MaxRecordSize
      depending on ws-RecordSize     pic x.

Procedure Division using lnk-PrimeKey
                   returning lnk-Values.

  initialize lnk-Values
  move lnk-PrimeKey to Data-PrimeKey
  Read Data-File
    key is Data-PrimeKey
        invalid key
             set RecNotFound to true
        not invalid key
             move Data-record(1:ws-RecordSize)
             to   lnk-record (1:ws-RecordSize)
  End-Read

  if   ws-fileStatus = "00" or "23"
       continue

  else set FileError to true
       invoke super "showFileError" using ws-ErrorParams
  End-if

End Method "readRecord".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "rewriteRecord".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 lnk-record.
   10 occurs 1 to MaxRecordSize
      depending on ws-RecordSize     pic x.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==01 lnk==.

Procedure Division using lnk-record
                   returning lnk-SqlResult.

  set ResultOK to true
  rewrite Data-record from lnk-record

  if   ws-fileStatus <>"00"
       set FileError to true
       invoke super "showFileError" using ws-ErrorParams
  End-if

End Method "rewriteRecord".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "setFileInfo".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 lnk-Filename                  pic x(100).
01 lnk-Parent                    object reference.
01 lnk-recordSize                pic 9(04).
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==01 lnk==.

Procedure Division using     lnk-filename
                             lnk-Parent
                             lnk-RecordSize
                   returning lnk-SqlResult.

  set ResultOK          to true
  initialize               ws-ErrorParams
  move lnk-filename     to Data-filename
                           ws-filename
  set ws-Parent         to lnk-Parent
  move lnk-recordSize   to ws-RecordSize

  if   ws-RecordSize > MaxRecordSize
       move "RS" to ws-fileStatus
       set FileError to true
       invoke super "showFileError" using ws-ErrorParams
  End-if

End Method "setFileInfo".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "startPrimeKey".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 lnk-Key                                  pic x(20).
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==01 lnk==.

Procedure Division using lnk-Key returning lnk-SqlResult.

 set ResultOK to true
 move lnk-Key to Data-PrimeKey

 Start Data-File key is > Data-PrimeKey
       invalid key     set FileFinis to true
       not invalid key continue
 End-start

  if   ws-fileStatus = "00" or "10"
       continue

  else set FileError to true
       invoke super "showFileError" using ws-ErrorParams
  End-if

End Method "startPrimeKey".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "writeRecord".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 lnk-record.
   10 occurs 1 to MaxRecordSize
      depending on ws-RecordSize     pic x.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==01 lnk==.

Procedure Division using lnk-record
                   returning lnk-SqlResult.

  set ResultOK to true
  write Data-record from lnk-record

  if   ws-fileStatus <>"00"
       set FileError to true
       invoke super "showFileError" using ws-ErrorParams
  End-if

End Method "writeRecord".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------

End OBJECT.
End CLASS ISAMfile.

*>---------------------------------------------------------------

*>-----------------lseqfile.cbl----------------------------------

Class-id.          LineSequentialFile
                   inherits from FileErrors.

Class-control.     LineSequentialFile  is class "lseqfile"
                   FileErrors          is class "filerror"
                   .

*> Note : All lines marked +++ MUST HAVE the same value as
*> the variable MaxRecordSize.

*> This class is used for Line Sequential records which are
*> of either fixed or variable length.
*>
*> See the separate methods "readNextVariable" and
*> "writeRecordVariable"

*>---------------------------------------------------------------
FACTORY.
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "new".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 lnk-self                object reference.
Procedure Division returning lnk-self.
   invoke super "new" returning lnk-self
End Method "new".
*>------------------------------------------------------------
End FACTORY.
*>------------------------------------------------------------
OBJECT.
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
FILE-CONTROL.

     Select               Data-File
     assign               Data-filename
     organization         LINE SEQUENTIAL
     access               sequential
     file status          ws-FileStatus.

*>---------------------------------------------------------------
FILE SECTION.

FD  Data-File
    record varying from 1 to 1500 characters        *> +++
       depending on ws-RecordSize.
01  Data-record           pic x(1500).              *> +++

*>---------------------------------------------------------------
OBJECT-STORAGE SECTION.
copy "LinkErr.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==ws==.
01 Data-filename                      pic x(100).
78 MaxRecordSize                      value 1500.
01 ws-RecordSize                      pic 9(04) value 1.
01 OpenFlag                           pic 9(01) value 0.
   88 FileClosed                      value 0.
   88 FileOpened                      value 1.

*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "closeFile".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==01 lnk==.

Procedure Division returning lnk-SqlResult.

  set ResultOK to true

  if FileOpened
     close Data-file

     if    ws-fileStatus = "00"
           set FileClosed to true

     else  set FileError to true
           invoke super "showFileError" using ws-ErrorParams
     End-if
  End-if

End Method "closeFile".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "deleteFile".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Local-storage section.
01 ls-result                             pic x(4) comp-5.
Linkage section.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==01 lnk==.

Procedure Division returning lnk-SqlResult.

  set ResultOK to true
  call "CBL_DELETE_FILE" using     Data-Filename
                         returning ls-result

  if ls-result <> "00"
     set FileError to true
     invoke super "showFileError" using ws-ErrorParams
  End-if

End Method "deleteFile".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "getFilename".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 lnk-filename                  pic x(100).
Procedure Division returning lnk-filename.
 move Data-filename to lnk-filename
End Method "getFilename".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "openInputFile".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==01 lnk==.

Procedure Division returning lnk-SqlResult.

  set ResultOK to true
  open input Data-file

  if   ws-fileStatus = "00"
       set FileOpened to true

  else set FileError to true
       invoke super "showFileError" using ws-ErrorParams
  End-if

End Method "openInputFile".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "openOutputFile".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==01 lnk==.

Procedure Division returning lnk-SqlResult.

  set ResultOK to true
  open output Data-file

  if   ws-fileStatus = "00"
       set FileOpened to true

  else set FileError to true
       invoke super "showFileError" using ws-ErrorParams
  End-if

End Method "openOutputFile".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "readNext".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------

*> Used for FIXED LENGTH records

Linkage section.
01 lnk-ReturnValues.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==05 lnk==.
05 lnk-record.
   10 occurs 1 to MaxRecordSize
      depending on ws-RecordSize     pic x.

Procedure Division returning lnk-ReturnValues.

  initialize lnk-ReturnValues
  Read Data-File next record
    At End
      Set fileFinis to True
    Not at End
      Move  Data-record (1:ws-RecordSize)
      to    lnk-record  (1:ws-RecordSize)
  End-Read

  if   ws-fileStatus = "00" or "10"
       continue

  else set FileError to true
       invoke super "showFileError" using ws-ErrorParams
  End-if

End Method "readNext".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "readNextVariable".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------

*> Used for VARIABLE LENGTH records

Linkage section.
01 lnk-ReturnValues.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==05 lnk==.
05 lnk-RecordSize                    pic 9(04).
05 lnk-record.
   10 occurs 1 to MaxRecordSize
      depending on ws-RecordSize     pic x.

Procedure Division returning lnk-ReturnValues.

  initialize lnk-ReturnValues
  Read Data-File next record
    At End
      Set fileFinis to True
    Not at End
      Move  Data-record (1:ws-RecordSize)
      to    lnk-record  (1:ws-RecordSize)
      Move  ws-RecordSize to lnk-RecordSize
  End-Read

  if   ws-fileStatus = "00" or "10"
       continue

  else set FileError to true
       invoke super "showFileError" using ws-ErrorParams
  End-if

End Method "readNextVariable".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "setFileInfo".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 lnk-Filename                  pic x(100).
01 lnk-Parent                    object reference.
01 lnk-recordSize                pic 9(04).
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==01 lnk==.

Procedure Division using     lnk-filename
                             lnk-Parent
                             lnk-RecordSize
                   returning lnk-SqlResult.

  set ResultOK          to true
  initialize               ws-ErrorParams
  move lnk-filename     to Data-filename
                           ws-filename
  set ws-Parent         to lnk-Parent
  move lnk-recordSize   to ws-RecordSize

  if   ws-RecordSize > MaxRecordSize
       move "RS" to ws-fileStatus
       set FileError to true
       invoke super "showFileError" using ws-ErrorParams
  End-if

End Method "setFileInfo".
*>---------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "writeRecord".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------

*> used for FIXED LENGTH records

Linkage section.
01 lnk-Record.
   10 occurs 1 to MaxRecordSize
      depending on ws-RecordSize      pic x.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==01 lnk==.

Procedure Division using lnk-record
                         returning lnk-SqlResult.

  set ResultOK to true
  write Data-record from lnk-record (1:ws-RecordSize)

  if   ws-fileStatus <> "00"
       set FileError to true
       invoke super "showFileError" using ws-ErrorParams
  End-if

End Method "writeRecord".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "writeRecordVariable".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------

*> used for VARIABLE LENGTH records

Linkage section.
01 lnk-RecordSize                     pic 9(04).
01 lnk-Record.
   10 occurs 1 to MaxRecordSize
      depending on lnk-RecordSize        pic x.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==01 lnk==.

Procedure Division using lnk-RecordSize, lnk-record
                         returning lnk-SqlResult.

  set ResultOK to true
  write Data-record from lnk-record (1:lnk-RecordSize)

  if   ws-fileStatus <> "00"
       set FileError to true
       invoke super "showFileError" using ws-ErrorParams
  End-if

End Method "writeRecordVariable".
*>--------------------------------------------------------------

End OBJECT.
End CLASS LineSequentialFile.

*>---------------------------------------------------------------

I've used the above to create the test data and now I use the ISAM File to
replace Mike's  class BookFile and I need the equivalent of his BookRecord,
which I'll repeat here :-

*************************************************************
Identification Division.
Class-id.  BookInv (BookRecord)
           inherits from Base.
Environment Division.
Object Section.
Class-Control.
    BookInv   is class "bookinv" *> BookRecord is class "bookrecd"
    Base      is class "Base".

Object.
    Data Division.
    Working-Storage Section.
    01  book-data.
        05  bk-item-no                    pic x(05).
        05  bk-descriptive-data.
            10  bk-item-desc              pic x(40).
            10  bk-unit-cost              pic 9(03)v99.
            10  bk-unit-price             pic 9(03)v99.
        05  bk-inventory-data.
            10  bk-reorder-point          pic s9(05).
            10  bk-on-hand                pic s9(05).
            10  bk-on-order               pic s9(05).

    Procedure Division.
**********************************************************
    Method-ID. "set-book-info".
    Data Division.
    Linkage Section.
    01  ls-book-data.
        05  ls-item-no              pic x(05).
        05  ls-descriptive-data     pic x(50).
        05  ls-inventory-data       pic x(15).
    Procedure Division using ls-book-data.
        move ls-book-data to book-data.
    End Method "set-book-info".
**********************************************************
    Method-ID. "get-desc-Info".
    Data Division.
    Linkage Section.
    01  ls-descriptive-data.
        05  ls-item-desc        pic x(40).
        05  ls-unit-cost        pic 9(03)v99.
        05  ls-unit-price       pic 9(03)v99.
    Procedure Division returning ls-descriptive-data.
        move bk-descriptive-data to ls-descriptive-data.
    End Method "get-desc-info".
**********************************************************
    Method-ID. "get-inv-info".
    Data Division.
    Linkage Section.
    01  ls-inventory-data.
        05  ls-reorder-point    pic s9(05).
        05  ls-on-hand          pic s9(05).
        05  ls-on-order         pic s9(05).
    Procedure Division returning ls-inventory-data.
        move bk-inventory-data to ls-inventory-data.
    End Method "get-inv-info".
**********************************************************
End Object.
End Class BookInv.
**********************************************************.

I introduce a new element between BookManger and IsamFile :-

[bookManager] ---------->[BookDBI - DB Interface}------->[ISAMFile]

BookDBI would be equivalent to Mike's BooInv(BookRecord) above, but handling
only one record at a time, (which is what I normally do). However I can see the
advantage of using the BookRecord class which ties in with comments from Donald
and Pete about object properties. Having multi(instances) copies of BookRecord
dovetails nicely into something like the following :-

The travel agent brings up various vacation options in the West Indies on his
screen, which include photos of hotel, beaches etc., cruise line/air line
schedules, booking vacancies, room costs etc., perhaps visuals taking you
through a virtual hotel. He/she bamboozles you with all this data - then to
recap you ask the agent to take you back through some of the options - the
'Record' Class approach  does it for you..

Doesn't affect the demo but there currently is a time bomb in his 'BookRecord"
class. Item Number is picx(05) =  pic 9(05). The inventory clerk starts at '1'
and eventually keys in '99998' - if he gets that far !  N/E doesn't have
automatic garbage collection as yet,  (Eventually - J4's TR(Tech Report)
Finalizer) - so when finished with the various BookRecords, currently you
better have :-

    invoke BookRecordObj(n) "finalize" returning BookRecordObj(n)

The alternative - going belly up with memory leaks.

One more change, if I was going whole hog. Go full GUI and replace Mike's
BookUI - the control logic is in BookManager, I would have a parameter class
passing the GUI info to MyDialog class. His screen is 'no sweat' for any GUI
approach - and same Dialog can be used for (a) Editing (Add, Change, Delete)
with (b) Enquiries separate, if you so wish - using a switch to say whether
user has access to (a) or (b).

Yes above is a LOT of classes to do what he has in one non-OO program. BUT....

a) New or changed programs

1. BookTriigger
2. BookManager (business logic)
3. BookDBI (interface to COBOL files/SQL and DB)
4. BookDialog (screen parameters referred to above)

b) Programs *already* written and RE-USED :-

5. LineSeqFile
6. IsamFile
7. ErrorMessages
8. FileErrors
9. Filecheck
10.MyDialog (generic class to create GUIs, display data and return Windows
     events)

Write 4 programs out of 10 - not a bad batting average - and remember - Items
5 through 10, having been thoroughly tested, can be used over and over again.

Non OO. Sure you can emulate some of the above but not with quite the same
dexterity as OO.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```
