###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Standard COBOL contrasted with OOCOBOL

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-27T04:37:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E827ACA.C273E67F@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<01c2ecda$c3c839e0$74c2f943@chottel> <3E7A5F73.21CEE5FD@shaw.ca> <01c2f3eb$aeeb1020$c38bf243@chottel>`

```


Charles Hottel wrote:

> Jimmy,
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Now you've lost me <G>,  because it seems you've asked the same question twice
but in a slightly different way. Mike's programs above are the ones he has
written. MF Base, FJBase, Hitachi Base or JavaBase in the case of IBM
Enterprise are required to trap errors - you invoke a class or a method which
doesn't exist, OO either (a) tells you the class or object doesn't exist or
(b) 'trundles' up through the hierarchy with an invalid method name(or typo),
until it hits Base and it is 'Base" (or a sub-class) which will report that
'Method x' doesn't exist. You may also get an error because you try and invoke
with a object that isn't applicable, e.g. "getGallons" when the class is
Avoirdupois weights !.

Ask again if I have supplied the wrong answer <G>

>
> <snip>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

All the above written by me but note they use support -

 a) FileCheck - a convenient way to get at CBL_FILE  routines in M/F which are
also in F/J.

 b) ErrorMessages - Windows Error Messaging - The Business Program checks for
logic errors and uses a table of messages, passes parameters to ErrorMessages
which is a  SUB CLASS of the support GUI Class - Messagebox. Again there is a
similar support mechanism in POWERCOBOL

c) LineSeq
d) ISAMfile

(c) and (d) written by me. Looks easy doesn't it ? But it did take me a long
time to figure it out and it developed from an original idea by Will Price.
Initially I had a SEPARATE class for each file - just like Mike has. At that
time I didn't understand the true significance of INSTANCES (many different
files using the same class but with data SPECIFIC to each instance - but check
back on the file classes - that means the file data is SPECIFIC to an instance
and NOT the data that appears in methods). Although not sold on OO, it was our
very own WMK who supplied the missing link - specifying the record as VARYING
FROM A TO B DEPENDING ON C.

Again check the source for LineSequentialFile (SequentialFile is a 'carbon'
copy) - a couple of things I've added. The first should slightly pique
Howard's interest - maintenance. It wasn't until I pasted the source that I
remembered I had methods for 'Fixed' and 'Variable' length Line Sequentials or
Sequentials. As originally envisaged, "new" files are automatically created as
VARIABLE as soon as you do an OPEN OUTPUT. That's fine - but when I wanted to
read old RM/COBOL files - belly up. My RM's are all ISAMs but I read them as
Sequentials ignoring the index - BUT - created by RM/COBOL they are also fixed
length ! Hence the reason you see (in LineSequentialFile and SequentialFile),
the methods :-

Method-id. "readNext".
Method-id. "readNextVariable".
Method-id. "writeRecord".
Method-id. "writeRecordVariable".

A quick fix to LineSeq and Seq, recompile and away to the races. No other
programs are affected.

You might be curious about the following :-

*>------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "closeFile".
*>------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==01 lnk==.

Procedure Division returning lnk-SqlResult.

  set ResultOK to true

  if FileOpened
     close Data-file

     if    ws-fileStatus = "00"
           set FileClosed to true

     else  set FileError to true
           invoke super "showFileError" using ws-ErrorParams
     End-if
  End-if

End Method "closeFile".
*>------------------------------------------------------------

Typically of course, as part of your program close-down you ensure files get
closed.
Depending upon what you are doing you might open/close, and open/close again
in a particular Business program. Now the file class is accessed,  at 'arm's
length' as it were.( i.e. ENCAPSULATED - hidden, and you don't have the source
- just the method names and paramaeters). Given a particular situation you
might find that you attempted to CLOSE a file which is already CLOSEd.

At the current time I'm switching to SQL with MS Access - but for those
RM/COBOL files I still need the COBOL Class SequentialFile to get my CSVs to
feed into Access ! (And I need those RM files as input - there's nothing
better to test a system than REAL data !)

e) FileErrors -  which contains a comprehensive list of file status messages.
This is nothing more than a set of tables with a brief indication of the error
type which includes ANSI codes and the M/F extensions for the '9x' series. Get
the error description which is passed to (b) ErrorMessages above.

>
> <snip>
…[87 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I had hoped there was sufficient but to spell it out - but a dummy DBI
(DataBase Interface) and let's stick with Mike's BookInv  (BookRecord). His
screen shows display of the whole record. But he also shows accessing
specific sub elements in the record, Methods "get-desc-Info" and
"get-inv-info". It's daft as it reads, because you can access all the screen
info in one gulp. He is merely demonstrating that you can strip off bits of a
record.

Now how I would currently do it (and by no means the ONLY way it can be done)
:-

[bookManager] ---------->[BookDBI - DB Interface}------->[ISAMFile]

Instead of invoking the ISAMFile as "new", the bookManager invokes BookDBI
"new" returning os-BookDBI :-

The bookManager then has three possibilities of invoking :-

a) One Screen only - invoke os-BookDBI "readPrimeKey" using
        PrimeKey returning ls-values - note that this method completely
        bypasses BookDBI and uses the method in the ISAMFile

b) Separate screens
    (1) Where we just want "descrip-info" -
        invoke os-BookDBI "get-desc-Info"
            using PrimeKey returning ls-values
    (2) Where we just want "inv-info" -
         invoke os-BookDBI "get-inv-Info"
            using PrimeKey returning ls-values

#2 doesn't really make sense - you want both description and inventory data -
so in the real world you would use (a) above.

Note that (b) 1 and 2 above both invoke super (ISAMFile) FROM the BookDBI
Class - and then strip off the information to be returned to the Business
Class.

*>-------------------------------------------------------------
*>*** compiled cleanly but I haven't  tested it

Class-id.        BookDBI inherits from ISAMFile.

Class-Control.   BookDBI  is class "BookDBI"
                 ISAMFile is class "isamfile"

*>-------------------------------------------------------------
FACTORY.
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "new".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage section.
01 lnk-self                     object reference.
Procedure Division returning lnk-self.
 invoke super "new" returning lnk-self
End Method "new".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
END FACTORY.
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
OBJECT.
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
OBJECT-STORAGE SECTION.
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
01 Book-Info.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==05 ws==.
copy "bookrecd.cpy" replacing ==(tag1)== by ==05 bk==,
                              ==(tag)==  by ==bk==.
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Method-ID. "get-desc-Info".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage Section.
01 lnk-PrimeKey             pic x(20).
01 lnk-values.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==05 lnk==.
05  lnk-descriptive-data.
    10   lnk-item-desc      pic x(40).
    10   lnk-unit-cost      pic 9(03)v99.
    10   lnk-unit-price     pic 9(03)v99.

Procedure Division returning lnk-values.

set ResultOK to true
invoke super "readPrimeKey"
       using lnk-PrimeKey returning Book-Info

if ResultOK
    move bk-descriptive-data to lnk-descriptive-data

else set FileError to true
End-if

End Method "get-desc-info".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Method-ID. "get-inv-info".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
Linkage Section.
01 lnk-PrimeKey             pic x(20).
01 lnk-values.
copy "sqlreslt.cpy" replacing ==(tag)== by ==05 lnk==.
05  lnk-inventory-data.
    10  lnk-reorder-point    pic s9(05).
    10  lnk-on-hand          pic s9(05).
    10  lnk-on-order         pic s9(05).
Procedure Division returning lnk-values.

set ResultOK to true
invoke super "readPrimeKey"
       using lnk-PrimeKey returning Book-Info

if ResultOK
    move bk-inventory-data to lnk-inventory-data

else set FileError to true
End-if

End Method "get-inv-info".
*>-------------------------------------------------------------
*> Additionallly, assuming I am using a listbox or a collection
*> for some other reason I would have methods for following :-
*>
*> createcollection
*> addToCollection
*> changeCollection
*> delete from collection

End Object.
End Class BookDBI.
*>-------------------------------------------------------------

Now as to screen handling, currently, having returned the data to the Business
class (bookManager) I would have it invoke the Screen/Dialog Class to display
the data. It could be done in the BookDBI as in Mike's BookInv - but at this
point in time, my preference is for the Business Logic to instruct other
classes what to do. (It's debatable - there is more than one approach).

I mentioned Object Properties - switch to a class which holds one bookrecord
per book object then the procedure is slightly different.

> Could you explain to me in a little more detail what purpose that you
> see for BookDBI? The whole multiple record business has me a little lost.

Hopefully above clarifies it a little. Now to your 'Why more than one object
?".
I would have to carefully think out my DBI approach versus the BookRecord
class, but you could have :-

Object-storage.
01 os-BookDBI occurs 10 object reference value null.

move 6 to n
 invoke BookDBI "new" returning os-BookDBI(n)

- obviously you would have to carefully track which "n" object you were
currently accessing. That's why the Object Properties approach, with a
separate object per book  is probably safer.

Another aspect of multi-objects. No good using books - let's take ladies'
fashions.
Spring Styles identified to a product group, sub-group etc. The central
buyer/merchandiser wants to view the performance of similar styles before
re-ordering  I would tend to give him a listbox of records to choose from -
hence my reference to collections above. He's curious how Style A from
Manufacturer X is going compared to Style B from manufacturer Z - bearing in
mind seasonal merchandise has a very short life. The DressRecord (equivalent
of the BookInv record) may contain a picture of the Style to remind him of
what it looks like, and summarized at corporate level, quantity, colours,
sizes. Additionally what is the performance at each retail branch. Now I'm not
suggesting this is ALL stored in the DressRecord but you can also have
sub-classes of DressRecord for different properties.

So he selects Styles A and B and C from the Listbox creating separate
DressRecord objects. Now he/she can hop, skip and jump between the various
style objects to make buying decisions.

If it still leaves a blank - sing out. Any of you other OO folk want to add
anything ?

As a closing note - THE BIG PROBLEM - there just isn't the sample code around
to demonstrate all the possibilities of using OO COBOL in the real world.

Jimmy, Calgary AB

>
```

classes looked so well thought out that I thought the vendor might have
provided them:-)

You are right about there not being enough real world examples. Once I have
a compiler that does OO I will revisit Price's book.  So far I have just
read it and it is not the same as doing it. I find that when I don't "use

Charles Hottel wrote:

> Thanks. I think I get it now. You should take it as a compliment that your
> classes looked so well thought out that I thought the vendor might have
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> <snip>

Thanks for the compliment - and naturally I *am* pleased to receive it, but I'm
not going to preen like a peacock. Put it down to SHEER BLOODY HARD WORK
figuring it out on my own. But note, this was achieved with info from others,
Arranga/Coyle, Wilson Price and dear old Bill Klein, two young go-getters using
N/E, Calin in Toronto and Simon in the UK Midlands; Pete Dashwood pointing me
at DB and SQL, (although I suspect he will absolutely *hate* the approach I've
taken - though it does work). On occasion tech advice from M/F - particularly
Steve Biggs when he was there, and now people like Chris Glazier etc..

Might raise a few hairs amongst the bits and bytes crowd here - but with a
career starting in admin in the RAF I did have a fascination with 'systems' -
no, not the stars, planets etc, nor combustion engines - God forbid !  The
first 12/13 years of my EDP career were as a systems analyst. My starting point
is systems design, (fitting the pieces of the jigsaw together), supplemented by
that elegant language - COBOL..

Jimmy, Calgary AB

## Thread: copybook transform — 2 msgs

### copybook transform

- **From:** "Paul" <paulhbliu@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-18T17:04:14-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BJMda.1610$lQ4.516172@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Hi

another assignment is to do transformation from cobol copybook
(with values) that the customer wants to transform into XML data
will the XSLT be the best tool ?
as I can't find any reference on this, could give any suggestion here ?

thanks
Paul
```

#### ↳ Re: copybook transform

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-03-18T22:40:55-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0303182240.462c5ab5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<BJMda.1610$lQ4.516172@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
I'm not certain I understand the request.

Do you want to represent the copybook as XML or the CONTENT of the
data area as XML - where you might have multiple "records" each with
the associated and appropriate XML tags?

XLST might be a way to take an XML formatted document and presesent it
as a COBOL copybook, but It can't go the other direction.

If the goal is to convert "COBOL data" (hate that term - data is data)
into XML formatted data using the copybook the generate the tags for
said data, then you might want to look at the new XML output feature
from the new version of Fujitsu COBOL.  It takes a copybook (really
record layout) and converts a data file to XML based on the layout.

"Paul" <paulhbliu@hotmail.com> wrote in message news:<BJMda.1610$lQ4.516172@news20.bellglobal.com>...
> Hi
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Paul
```

Express), but will not run properly. The ouput of the
code simply only has the headings and no output of the
data.

The program is simple take the data do some
calcualtions and output them.

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. Program6.
       AUTHOR. DAN J.
       INSTALLATION.

       DATE-COMPILED.

***************************************************

*

       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT IN-EMPLOYEE-FILE  ASSIGN TO
'data.dat'
               ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
           SELECT OUT-SALARY-FILE ASSIGN TO
'PRINT-FILE.OUT'
               ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  IN-EMPLOYEE-FILE
           RECORD CONTAINS 80 CHARACTERS.
       01  IN-EMPLOYEE-REC.

           05  EMP-NAME-IN                     PIC
X(15).
           05  HOURS-WRK-IN                    PIC
9(3).
           05  RATE-IN                         PIC
9(3)V99.
           05                                  PIC
X(57).

       FD  OUT-SALARY-FILE
           RECORD CONTAINS 80 CHARACTERS.
       01    OUT-SALARY-REC                    PIC
X(80).
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
      *01  LINE-CT                             PIC 99
   VALUE 0.
       01  ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS              PIC XXX
   VALUE 'YES'.
       01  WS-DATE.
           05 WS-YEAR                          PIC
9999.
           05 WS-MONTH                         PIC 99.
           05 WS-DAY                           PIC 99.

X(4)   VALUE SPACES.
           05                                  PIC
X(5)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  EMP-NAME-OUT                    PIC
X(15).
           05                                  PIC
X(4)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  GROSS-PAY                       PIC
9(6)V99.
           05                                  PIC
X(4)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  FICA                            PIC
9(5)V99.
           05                                  PIC
X(4)   VALUE SPACES.
           05  NET-PAY                         PIC
9(6)V99.

       01  HDR-1.
           05                                  PIC
X(15)  VALUE SPACES.
           05                                  PIC
X(26)
                 VALUE 'CREATED FROM SAMPLE INPUT'.
           05                                  PIC
X(22)  VALUE SPACES.
           05  DATE-OUT.
               10 MONTH-OUT                    PIC XX.
               10                              PIC X
   VALUE '/'.
               10 DAY-OUT                      PIC XX.
               10                              PIC X
   VALUE '/'.
               10 YEAR-OUT                     PIC
XXXX.
           05                                  PIC
X(11).
           05                                  PIC
X(5)   VALUE 'PAGE'.
           05  PAGE-NO                         PIC 99
   VALUE ZERO.
       01  HDR-2.
           05                                  PIC
X(2)   VALUE SPACES.
           05                                  PIC
X(20)
                VALUE 'NAME    '.
           05                                  PIC
X(58)
                VALUE 'GROSS PAY        FICA
NET PAY'.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.

*****************************************************
      *     PROGRAM LOGIC
   *

*****************************************************
       100-MAIN-MODULE.
           OPEN INPUT  IN-EMPLOYEE-FILE
                OUTPUT OUT-SALARY-FILE
           MOVE FUNCTION CURRENT-DATE TO WS-DATE
           MOVE WS-MONTH TO MONTH-OUT
           MOVE WS-DAY TO DAY-OUT
           MOVE WS-YEAR TO YEAR-OUT
           PERFORM 200-HEADING-RTN
           PERFORM UNTIL ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS = 'NO
'
              READ IN-EMPLOYEE-FILE
                 AT END
                    MOVE 'NO ' TO
ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS
                 NOT AT END
                    PERFORM 300-CALC-RTN
              END-READ
           END-PERFORM
           CLOSE IN-EMPLOYEE-FILE
                 OUT-SALARY-FILE
           STOP RUN.

*****************************************************
      *      Headings are printed from 200-HEADING-RTN
   *

*****************************************************
       200-HEADING-RTN.
           ADD 1 TO PAGE-NO
           WRITE OUT-SALARY-REC FROM HDR-1
                AFTER ADVANCING PAGE
           WRITE OUT-SALARY-REC FROM HDR-2
                AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES
           MOVE 0 TO LINE-CT.

*****************************************************
      *  CALCULATIONS
   *
      *
   *

*****************************************************
       300-CALC-RTN.
           IF LINE-CT >= 25
              PERFORM 200-HEADING-RTN
           END-IF
           MOVE EMP-NAME-IN TO EMP-NAME-OUT
           COMPUTE GROSS-PAY = HOURS-WRK-IN * RATE-IN
           COMPUTE FICA = GROSS-PAY * .0765
           COMPUTE NET-PAY = GROSS-PAY - FICA
           WRITE OUT-SALARY-REC FROM DETAIL-LINE
               AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES
           ADD 1 TO LINE-CT.

Here is the data file, data.dat
P NEWMAN       050525
R REDFORD      040810
E TAYLOR       035925
N STERN        070615
K ROGERS       032785
R STERN        012345
C HAMMEL       157577
M STERN        070654
L STERN        100987
S SMITH        097667

I'm at a loss can anybody tell me why, please!

-Dan J

Danny Jovasevic wrote:

> Hi,
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Danny,

Your program works ! Just to play it safe I called your data file
"c:\dan.txt" I'm assuming you compiled and got a clean copy. The only
problem I had was with your data file., where you say :-

05  HOURS-WRK-IN                    PIC 9(3).
05  RATE-IN                                  PIC 9(3)V99.

That's EIGHT digits and you are showing in the data file :-

P NEWMAN       050525 = SIX digits

So I got a 'non numeric error'.

To make it work, I "shoved" two zeroes on the end of the records. The
calculations make no sense but here are the results I got :-

               CREATED FROM SAMPLE INPUT
03/18/2003

  NAME                GROSS PAY        FICA  NET PAY

         P NEWMAN           02625000    0200812    02424188

         R REDFORD          03240000    0247860    02992140

         E TAYLOR           03237500    0247668    02989832

         N STERN            04305000    0329332    03975668

         K ROGERS           02512000    0192168    02319832

         R STERN            00414000    0031671    00382329

         C HAMMEL           09058900    0693005    08365895

         M STERN            04578000    0350217    04227783

         L STERN            09870000    0755055    09114945

         S SMITH            06469900    0494947    05974953

I'm guessing you haven't yet used the Animator. Do so, then like the old
American Express ad you will say, "Don't leave home without it" <G>.

Try this with your program - Bring up the Animator and step through the
program line-by-line. You will see how the program *actuallly* flows as
opposed to how you *think* it flows. Want to query a variable - click on
it and the Animator brings up a window showing the variable's contents.

Jimmy, Calgary AB

>
>        IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
…[179 more quoted lines elided]…
> -Dan J
```

input file definition and the data.  If I look at HOURS-WRK-IN and
RATE-IN, you have it defined as a total of 8 numeric digits.  Your
input data is only showing 6 numeric digits.

### Cobol: SQL

- **From:** TJTonyTJ@EarthLink.net (Tony Johnson)
- **Date:** 2003-03-19T13:33:08+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e786cf9.2639044@news.newsguy.com>`

```
Everyone,

I would like to know anyone's opinion concerning SQL programming.  Late in the
game for me comes tackling SQL and so I've been looking at the various
opinions of using SQL with cobol.  I would like to know which is the best
methodology in utilizing SQL in my cobol programs. I'm familiar with embedded
SQL, SQL CLI and SQL ODBC, etc.  Is one better than the other?  What is the
prefered usage in the real world?  Thanks!!




See ya!
TJ
03/19/03  08:32 am
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol: SQL

- **From:** bdhobbs18@acm.org (Bill Hobbs)
- **Date:** 2003-03-19T11:01:43-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<74ca5032.0303191101.26f40348@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3e786cf9.2639044@news.newsguy.com>`

```
TJTonyTJ@EarthLink.net (Tony Johnson) wrote in message news:<3e786cf9.2639044@news.newsguy.com>...
> I would like to know anyone's opinion concerning SQL programming.  Late in the
> game for me comes tackling SQL and so I've been looking at the various
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> prefered usage in the real world?  Thanks!!

I've always been handed embedded SQL, specifically Ingres embedded in
VMS COBOL.  From what little I know of the others, I think the
differences are small - just different interfaces to stuff your SQL
from here to there and suck the data from there to here.

A bigger change is how you handle the data.  I've done battle with
code that extracts a bunch of data from the database, stores it in
memory or a file, steps thru the records one at a time, sending the
data back to the database as part of a query to get more data, etc.. 
Uses lots of resources: database, network, memory, disk, CPU, time.

I've rewritten these pigs to create temporary tables in the database,
populate them from the other tables, then retrieve the data from the
database for final processing into a report or file.  Compared to the
original program, I may use more database resources, but I use a whole
lot less of the other resources.  I can also execute multiple user
requests for a report at one time, even if their parameters are
different.

So I say don't sweat the interface to the database, try to learn how
to make the SQL do most of the work for you.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol: SQL

- **From:** "Richard Maher" <maher_rj@hotspamnotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-19T19:40:14+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<b5ah2u$pc2$1@titan.btinternet.com>`
- **References:** `<3e786cf9.2639044@news.newsguy.com>`

```
Hi,

If you're fortunate enough to have a database provider that supports the
ANSII Standard SQL Module Language then I strongly recommend using that.
Just call the SQL as a subroutine. No bollocks pre-compiling and
pre-compiler barf that makes your debugger sessions difficult. COBOL
compiler for COBOL - SQL Compiler for SQL. Makes sense to me.

Cheers Richard.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol: SQL

- **From:** "Richard Maher" <maher_rj@hotspamnotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-22T09:20:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5h9t5$54t$1@hercules.btinternet.com>`
- **References:** `<3e786cf9.2639044@news.newsguy.com> <b5ah2u$pc2$1@titan.btinternet.com> <3e7917c1.36194836@news.optonline.net>`

```
Hi,

> How is that different from PREPARE followed by EXECUTE?  PREPARE compiles
> a SQL statement at execution time (on the server) and EXECUTE runs it.

Well one's done at compile-time and the other is done at run-time. But then
you knew that right?

I believe "Whether of not to use Dynamic SQL" to be a seperate question. I
was merely pointing out and apples and apples alternative to the usual
pre-compiler "exec-sql . . .blah. . . end-exec" approach. I'll include a
brief example below.

With Rdb/VMS dynamic SQL is supported with either the pre-compiler or
the SQL Module Language interfaces. Personally I would only *ever* use
dynamic SQL when statement syntax is dependant on run-time discovery
of environmental variables or user input. Which in my expeience is very
very infrequently.

Some people don't seen to have a problem with interpreters (and let's face
it, at least in Rdb's case, compiled SQL is only Binary Language
Representation and a long way from machine code) but, where possible,
I like to shift CPU utilization and memory allocation to the compiler rather
than the run-time. But then I love full stops, minimise the use of
END-verbs, think INITIALIZE is the dog's bollocks (especially for
arrays), perform paragraphs in sections and then go to fini, and reserve
the use of upper-case to literals that need it - so what do I know?

As far as ODBC goes, it is easy to use (well if you can't say anything nice
:-)

Regards Richard Maher.

Example Module language:-

module    dist_sql
language  cobol
parameter colons

declare pers alias filename mf_personnel

procedure declare_connection_name
        sqlcode,
        :db_spec        char(50);

        attach :db_spec;

procedure set_trans_rw
        sqlcode;

        set transaction read write
                reserving pers.departments for shared write;

procedure update_dept
        sqlcode
        :dept_name    char(30);

        update pers.departments
                set department_name = :dept_name
                where department_code= 'PHRN';

procedure disconnect_db
        sqlcode;

        disconnect default;


Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e7917c1.36194836@news.optonline.net...
> "Richard Maher" <maher_rj@hotspamnotmail.com> wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> How is that different from PREPARE followed by EXECUTE?  PREPARE compiles
a SQL
> statement at execution time (on the server) and EXECUTE runs it.
>
>
```

Nobody could disagree, in principle. But in some _very common_ environments
(DB2, Oracle), PREPARE has been found to run faster than pre-compiled ESQL.
Shops have converted all their programs in order to speed them up.

>END-verbs, think INITIALIZE is the dog's bollocks (especially for
>arrays), perform paragraphs in sections and then go to fini, and reserve

>As far as ODBC goes, it is easy to use (well if you can't say anything nice
>:-)

MSQUERY talking through ODBC runs circles around high-priced tools such as
FILE-AID. It comes 'free' with Excel, so everyone already has it.

compiles

>
 Nobody could disagree, in principle. But in some _very common_ environments
> (DB2, Oracle), PREPARE has been found to run faster than pre-compiled
ESQL.
> Shops have converted all their programs in order to speed them up.

You cannot compare PREPARE and "precompiled ESQL."  They are not of the same
species: PREPARE is an ESQL command. ESQL is not "precompiled" anything,
it's a programming techinque.

I think you may have meant to compare the two ESQL sequences..

1. PREPARE, EXECUTE
2. CALL (or appropriate DBMS command to invoke a stored procedure).

MCM

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol: SQL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T05:41:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e7d2832.302588049@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3e786cf9.2639044@news.newsguy.com> <b5ah2u$pc2$1@titan.btinternet.com> <3e7917c1.36194836@news.optonline.net> <b5h9t5$54t$1@hercules.btinternet.com> <3e7c9810.265684485@news.optonline.net> <j%6fa.3183$U96.969045@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3e7c9810.265684485@news.optonline.net...
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>it's a programming techinque.

You are mistaken. The precompiler strips out ESQL, compiles it, stores it in the
database (much like a stored procedure), assigns it a unique id, and replaces
the ESQL code with a CALL referencing that unique id. At execution time the
program invokes the 'binary SQL' in the same way it would invoke a stored
procedure. I know for sure that's how it works on DB2, and am fairly confident
it works the same on other databases. 

You can tell it works that way because, when things get screwed up, you have to
do a BIND PLAN to reassociate the precompiler code with the table.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol: SQL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T13:45:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zTifa.3557$U96.1067146@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3e786cf9.2639044@news.newsguy.com> <b5ah2u$pc2$1@titan.btinternet.com> <3e7917c1.36194836@news.optonline.net> <b5h9t5$54t$1@hercules.btinternet.com> <3e7c9810.265684485@news.optonline.net> <j%6fa.3183$U96.969045@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <3e7d2832.302588049@news.optonline.net>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e7d2832.302588049@news.optonline.net...
> "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> >> >> a SQL statement at execution time (on the server) and EXECUTE runs
it.
> >
> >You cannot compare PREPARE and "precompiled ESQL."  They are not of the
same
> >species: PREPARE is an ESQL command. ESQL is not "precompiled" anything,
> >it's a programming techinque.
>
> You are mistaken. The precompiler strips out ESQL, compiles it, stores it
in the
> database (much like a stored procedure), assigns it a unique id, and
replaces
> the ESQL code with a CALL referencing that unique id. At execution time
the
> program invokes the 'binary SQL' in the same way it would invoke a stored
> procedure. I know for sure that's how it works on DB2, and am fairly
confident
> it works the same on other databases.

Well, you are getting fairly compiler/OS/DBMS-specific here, but my point
was that both these sequences
are (E)mbedded (SQL):

== sequence one ===
EXEC SQL
     PREPARE stmt
END-EXEC

EXEC SQL
   EXECUTE stmt
END -EXEC
================

=== sequence two=====
EXEC SQL
    CALL storedprocedure
END-EXEC
==================

"At the end of the day"  these get to the same place, they just get there
using different routes.

In sequence one, the statment is parsed and a plan prepared at run-time  In
sequence two, the statement is parsed and a plan prepared when
'storedprocedure' is created.

It's been a while since I worked with the IBM-brand mainframe ESQL product;
it's possible, I guess, that if 'stmt' is sufficiently simple the
precompiler might be able to prepare 'stmt' at compile time, but that would
IMO qualify as a brand-specific optimization.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol: SQL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T07:48:53-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0303230748.b1941dc@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3e786cf9.2639044@news.newsguy.com> <b5ah2u$pc2$1@titan.btinternet.com> <3e7917c1.36194836@news.optonline.net> <b5h9t5$54t$1@hercules.btinternet.com> <3e7c9810.265684485@news.optonline.net> <j%6fa.3183$U96.969045@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <3e7d2832.302588049@news.optonline.net>`

```
One Correction...

robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message news:<3e7d2832.302588049@news.optonline.net>...
> "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
> 
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

The Oracle PreCompiler does not store anything relating to the SQL in
the database.  Instead, the SQL gets embedded in the native Oracle
calls that the precompiler compiles the EXEC SQL syntax into.  So - it
doesn't work the same on other databases.
```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e7c9810.265684485@news.optonline.net...
> "Richard Maher" <maher_rj@hotspamnotmail.com> wrote:
>
>
> Nobody could disagree, in principle. But in some _very common_
environments

Robert,

am assuming you mean the SAME SQL statement?

Certainly dynamic SQL has come a long way in the last few years but I
seriously doubt that, comparing like for like, it is quicker to prepare,
late bind, and execute a statement rather than to early bind and simply load
it.

If you have hard evidence for this, or even an explanation as to why it is
so, I'd very much like to see it.

Having said that, I am not advocating that ESQL is the right way to go,
either. Recent experience with some very clever people has persuaded me that
stored procedures and triggers have a lot more merit than I previously
believed and some of what I have seen over the last year makes COBOL and
ESQL (or even dynamic SQL for that matter...) look pretty primitive.

It is ironic that a few years ago I was a Voice in the Wilderness in this
forum crying for the use of Relational Database to keep COBOL systems open,
and receiving volumes of scathing mail extolling the virtues of ISAM files
(which I already knew but had seen beyond), and suggesting that Relational
Databases would never catch on because of their inherent "inefficiency"
(funny how they're all quiet now <G>), and now I am fairly sure that the use
of static SQL with COBOL is pretty primitive also...<G>

Pete.




----== Posted via Newsfeed.Com - Unlimited-Uncensored-Secure Usenet News==----
http://www.newsfeed.com The #1 Newsgroup Service in the World! >100,000 Newsgroups
---= 19 East/West-Coast Specialized Servers - Total Privacy via Encryption =---
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol: SQL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T18:02:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e7dc433.10214908@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3e786cf9.2639044@news.newsguy.com> <b5ah2u$pc2$1@titan.btinternet.com> <3e7917c1.36194836@news.optonline.net> <b5h9t5$54t$1@hercules.btinternet.com> <3e7c9810.265684485@news.optonline.net> <3e7d1f4d_1@text-west.newsgroups.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>am assuming you mean the SAME SQL statement?

Yes, of course. 

>Certainly dynamic SQL has come a long way in the last few years but I
>seriously doubt that, comparing like for like, it is quicker to prepare,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>so, I'd very much like to see it.

A statement is compiled once; it is executed thousands to millions of times.
Compilation speed is insignificant compared to execution speed. Executing a
PREPAREd statement is faster than executing a SELECT or FETCH (especially)
because of reduced parameter passing overhead and better statement cache
management.

see:
http://www.ericsson.com/cslab/projects/erlang/sqlerlang/node66.html

also:
http://www.carumba.com/talk/perl/DBI_Talk3_1999/tsld031.htm
http://www.carumba.com/talk/perl/DBI_Talk3_1999/tsld032.htm

>Having said that, I am not advocating that ESQL is the right way to go,
>either. Recent experience with some very clever people has persuaded me that
>stored procedures and triggers have a lot more merit than I previously
>believed and some of what I have seen over the last year makes COBOL and
>ESQL (or even dynamic SQL for that matter...) look pretty primitive.

Technically true, but there is a political problem.  ESQL is controlled by the
application programmer whereas stored procedures and triggers are controlled by
the DBA. Filling out paperwork and getting the change onto the always-too-busy
DBA's schedule may take unreasonably long. It is easier and faster for us grunts
to create a quasi-stored procedure via PREPARE. 

>It is ironic that a few years ago I was a Voice in the Wilderness in this
>forum crying for the use of Relational Database to keep COBOL systems open,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>of static SQL with COBOL is pretty primitive also...<G>

I look forward to seeing, a few years hence, COBOL programmers extolling linked
lists and calling tables obsolete. <G>

Robert
```

<a id="t-12b7a3be6f"></a>
## Thread: Reporting defects in the ANSI/ISO COBOL Standards — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards) · [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### Reporting defects in the ANSI/ISO COBOL Standards

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-19T13:31:35-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5agiq$4f7$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
As a follow-up on another thread, I thought that I would give some GUIDANCE
on submitting "defect reports" on ANSI/ISO COBOL Standards. Just as this is
an unmoderated newsgroup, readers of this note may do as they please with
its recommendations/information.

1) Although I have seen nothing to indicate that the '85 Standard (with its
approved 2 amendments) has been withdrawn as either an ANSI or an ISO COBOL
Standard, I *strongly* recommend against submitting defect reports against
it - unless the same defect exists in the 2002 Standard.  It is *highly*
unlikely that either J4 (ANSI-ish group) or WG4 (ISO-ish group) will do much
more than reply that this (older) Standard is no longer being "updated".

2) For those outside the US, it is possible for you to submit your defect
reports to *EITHER* your national Standards body or to ANSI/J4.  There are
PROs and CONs of each approach. I won't go into them here, but if you have
questions about them, please email me privately.  (Check the archives of CLC
on how to find out how to contact your "national standards body".)  It is
true that J4 will do the actual "grunt work" on any defect report no matter
WHERE it comes from, but it is also true that WG4 will review (and
approve/modify) it before it becomes final - even if it comes from within
the US.

3) The "easiest" (IMHO) way to submit a defect report to J4 is to send it
(by email) to the J4 chair at:
   Don.Schricker <at> microfocus.com
It is valid, however, to send it directly to ANSI indicating for which
Standard you are submitting the defect report.  See:
  http://www.ansi.org/
for contact information for them.

4) "Defect reports" are *not* intended as a way to submit
   "Wouldn't it have been better if ..."
        or
   "Why didn't you use this approach ..."
        or
   "Please expand the Standard to include ..."
        or
   "Vendor XYZ does it this way, why didn't you make the standard say ..."
comments.

 Defect reports are for reporting DEFECTS in what the Standard actually
says. This may include "omissions" where there is something "critical" left
out of the specification that makes it ambiguous or unclear what the
Standard requires. It also, DEFINITELY includes cases where two or more
separate parts of the Standard conflict with each other.  For examples of
what defects have already been reported and BEGUN their processing for the
2002 Standard, see:
   http://www.cobolstandard.info/defect.htm

You will notice that NONE of these documents is marked "DF" meaning that
they have reached final approval status.  However, these documents do show
the type of "problem" reported and how J4 drafts *tentative* responses.

If you are interested in *final* defect and interpretation (an "entirely
different kettle of fish") documents for the 85/89/91 ANSI Standard, you can
go to (long URL - combine to get to correct page):

http://global.ihs.com/doc_detail.cfm?currency_code=USD&customer_id=212547254
C0A&shopping_cart_id=2725385B2E4B202041594020240A&country_code=US&lang_code=
ENGL&item_s_key=00267482&item_key_date=020417&input_doc_number=&input_doc_ti
tle=cobol

and order (for $28 (USD)) a copy of CIB-28.  Members of J4 or WG4 *might* be
able to provide you with a "soft" copy of the document depending upon their
interpretation of the "Acknowledgement" versus "Copyright" requirements.

5)  The important information to include in a Defect Report are:
 -   Your name and contact information (company/organization affiliation is
OPTIONAL)
 -  Which version of the Standard you are reporting a problem with
 - Specific pages/sections (title and number) within the Standard where the
problems "appear"
 -  *EXACTLY* what the defect is that you are reporting

        and OPTIONALLY

   what solution (fix) you would suggest

6) I cannot give you ANY estimate on when you should expect a tentative or
final response to your report.  I will say, however, that it is HIGHLY
likely that J4 will at least begin processing your report within the next 2
meetings after they receive it (no guarantee, just likely).  If you wish to
"follow" their processes, you should contact the J4 chair for information on
seeing working documents related to your report (or on when/where meetings
are if you wish to attend).

        ***

Hopefully, any others who have participated in or are currently
participating in the standardization process will correct me, if I have
misspoken above - or add to this if I missed something.
```

#### ↳ Re: Reporting defects in the ANSI/ISO COBOL Standards

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-19T13:52:55-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5ahqq$sju$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b5agiq$4f7$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
(Speaking to myself <G>)

In addition to what I say below, you do NOT need to use "defect reports" to
let J4/WG4 know about strictly editorial issues (misspelling, punctuation,
font problems, etc).  Send them to the J4 chair directly.  If he disagrees
with your evaluation - or thinks it is NOT editorial, he may suggest that
you DO submit it as a "defect report" - but there is no need to start that
way.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0303202114.4d71ce6f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b5agiq$4f7$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<b5agiq$4f7$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>...
> 
> Hopefully, any others who have participated in or are currently
> participating in the standardization process will correct me, if I have
> misspoken above - or add to this if I missed something.

The only thing I will add is that some Reports are at DF Status
currently --- and I will be (As the maintainer of the site) be adding
more in the next week or two.

http://www.cobolstandard.info/defect.htm

I will also be posting a suggested defect reporting form.

## Thread: two questions — 25 msgs

### two questions

- **From:** mfcobol2002 <marcos_as@terra.com.br>
- **Date:** 2003-03-20T11:59:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2664878.1048161545@dbforums.com>`

```

Respected Gentlemen,

First question
When executing a program for the first time he already executes the
declaration COMMIT in I begin it.
I always used after the declarations Write/Rewrite/Delete. What problems
can have if it executes COMMIT soon of I begin?

Second question
When to use EXIT PROGRAM and when to use GO BACK?

Does anybody have an example of the use of multiple threads with Web?


Marcos Antonio de Souza
Brasil
```

#### ↳ Re: two questions

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-03-20T21:40:11-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0303202140.3863130f@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2664878.1048161545@dbforums.com>`

```
mfcobol2002 <marcos_as@terra.com.br> wrote 

> Second question
> When to use EXIT PROGRAM and when to use GO BACK?

Some will tell you to use GOBACK _all_ the time.

I don't use GOBACK at all, I use EXIT PROGRAM in a CALLed program and
STOP RUN in a main program.  I can always tell the difference.  With
Fujitsu you have to mark the program as MAIN (or -M) or not, so it is
easy to know when to use which.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: two questions

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-22T05:34:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YBSea.138$qr4.17976647@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<2664878.1048161545@dbforums.com> <217e491a.0303202140.3863130f@posting.google.com>`

```
I would use GOBACK in all cases for the following reasons:
1.  If a called program ever becomes a calling program, the EXIT PROGRAM
statement functions like a CONTINUE statement (not good).
2.  If a calling program ever becomes a called program, the STOP RUN
statement will not return to the calling program, but will end the run unit.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: two questions

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-22T18:07:27+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5guo2$5bd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<2664878.1048161545@dbforums.com> <217e491a.0303202140.3863130f@posting.google.com> <YBSea.138$qr4.17976647@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Terry Heinze wrote:

> I would use GOBACK in all cases for the following reasons:
> 1.  If a called program ever becomes a calling program, 

CALLed programs can also be 'CALLing' at the same time. That is there is no 
restriction on the use of the CALL statement within a program whether it 
has been CALLed or not.

The distinction is between CALLed program and MAIN programs.  Some systems 
make no distinction at compile time, only at run time as whether they have 
been 'CALLed' from another COBOL program or from the JCL (or equivalent).  

Other systems make a firm distinction such that a program compiled as a 
'MAIN' _cannot_ be CALLed and a non-MAIN can _only_ be CALLed and cannot be 
directly activated.

In such systems the programmer is _never_ confused as to which of EXIT 
PROGRAM or STOP RUN to use.

> 2.  If a calling program ever becomes a called program, the STOP RUN
> statement will not return to the calling program, but will end the run
> unit.

If the program is written so that it can be executed or CALLed then the 
solution should be obvious, the behaviour is designed so that the two can 
be used as:

              program code
              ...
              EXIT PROGRAM
              STOP RUN
              .

If the EXIT PROGRAM does not work (because the code was not CALLed) then 
the STOP RUN will work.

> the EXIT PROGRAM
> statement functions like a CONTINUE statement (not good).

No.  This behaviour is _good_, it is designed that way, it allows the above.

Unlike GOBACK, it also allows code to be between the EXIT PROGRAM and STOP 
RUN that will only be executed if the program was not CALLed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: two questions

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-22T11:17:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e7c447e.244287707@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2664878.1048161545@dbforums.com> <217e491a.0303202140.3863130f@posting.google.com> <YBSea.138$qr4.17976647@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <b5guo2$5bd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Terry Heinze wrote:
>
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
>RUN that will only be executed if the program was not CALLed.

This behavior is _not good_, but what a silly squabble. The correct word is
RETURN. Because of historical antecedents, we're stuck with EXIT PROGRAM, STOP
RUN and GOBACK. Given such a limited choice, the one closest to RETURN is
GOBACK.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: two questions

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-22T08:05:03-05:00

- **Message-ID:** `<v7on4vjm1l2nc4@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<2664878.1048161545@dbforums.com> <217e491a.0303202140.3863130f@posting.google.com> <YBSea.138$qr4.17976647@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <b5guo2$5bd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e7c447e.244287707@news.optonline.net>`

```

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e7c447e.244287707@news.optonline.net...
[snip]
> This behavior is _not good_, but what a silly squabble. The correct word
is
> RETURN. Because of historical antecedents, we're stuck with EXIT PROGRAM,
STOP
> RUN and GOBACK. Given such a limited choice, the one closest to RETURN is
> GOBACK.

As in,

1050 GOSUB 6000
...
6000 ' Do something
6080 RETURN

or

6000 ' Do something
6080 GOBACK

GOSUB and GOBACK makes some sense. <G>

with what is clear and unambiguous. <G>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: two questions

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T07:23:46+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5idfc$tac$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<2664878.1048161545@dbforums.com> <YBSea.138$qr4.17976647@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <b5guo2$5bd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e7c447e.244287707@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>Unlike GOBACK, it also allows code to be between the EXIT PROGRAM and STOP
>>RUN that will only be executed if the program was not CALLed.
> 
> This behavior is _not good_, but what a silly squabble. 

Then why are you squabbling ?

If a particular behaviour is what you want then please do use that.

The questioner asked which to use, instead of just dictating one method, I 
have been explaining the differences and the issues.

> The correct word is RETURN. 

No, it is not 'correct' at all.  In C functions the syntax is:
     return value;  

where the implication is that it is 'giving back' or returning a result.  
Cobol does not return a value (except with extensions) and so EXIT is more 
appropriate.

> Because of historical antecedents, we're stuck with EXIT
> PROGRAM, STOP RUN and GOBACK. 

We're not 'stuck' with anything.  GOBACK is an IBM thing, the others are 
Cobol.  Before EXIT PROGRAM was introduced as the standard way of program 
exit, vendors had introduced their own pet methods, GOBACK is the last of 
these to survive.

> Given such a limited choice, the one closest
> to RETURN is GOBACK.

Well it does have the same number of characters, but none of them are the 
same.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: two questions

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T02:24:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e7d0724.294124851@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2664878.1048161545@dbforums.com> <YBSea.138$qr4.17976647@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <b5guo2$5bd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e7c447e.244287707@news.optonline.net> <b5idfc$tac$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>If a particular behaviour is what you want then please do use that.

The behavior I want is return to whoever called you, exit, whatever. There is no
reason to complicate it with 'issues'; it is a single and simple concept. 

>> The correct word is RETURN. 
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>appropriate.

I don't like the concept of a program returning a single value. What if it needs
to return two values, which is usually the case? Then it switches gears to
return values in 'parameters'. Why not always return values in parameters?
Because one value makes it convenient to make the program an in-line FUNC. Tail
wagging dog. 

If the standard allowed EXIT PROGRAM to return to the operating system (or
interpreter, or run-time, or CICS) which called it, I'd have no problem with
using EXIT PROGRAM always .. especially after learning (here) it no longer needs
to be in a paragraph by itself. But the standard has the (obsolete) concept of
"run unit", meaning 'executed from the operating system command line.'
The standard's definition of "run unit" reads:

4.167 run unit: One or more runtime modules that interact with one another and
that function, at execution time, as an entity to provide problem solutions.

That's pretty vague (not to mention, optimistic). A better definition is found
here:

"C.4.1.2 Runtime level organization
At the runtime level the most inclusive unit of organization is the run unit. A
run unit is a complete problem solution
consisting of a runtime module or of several inter-communicating runtime
modules. A runtime module results
from compiling a compilation unit. A run unit is an independent entity that may
be executed without
communicating with, or being coordinated with, any other run unit except that it
may process files and messages
or set and test switches that were written or will be read by other run units.
A runtime element results from the compilation of each function, method, or
program."

Suppose I fork five tasks and wait for them to complete. Are they five "run
units?" The standard appears to say they are not because they are certainly
"coordinated with" my task. Yet it wants each of them to terminate with STOP
RUN, in which case they _are_ "run units." Make up your mind. 

[helpful example]

"From each of the 3 compilation units, a separate runtime module is created by
the compiler. Typically, a linkage
editor is used to combine runtime modules, from one or multiple compilations,
into a run unit."

Maybe in 1980. Today we use dynamic linkage.

Here is the clincher:

C.4.5.1
"When control is transferred to a called program, execution proceeds from
statement to statement beginning with
the first nondeclarative statement of the called program. If control reaches a
STOP RUN statement, this signals the
logical end of the run unit. If control reaches an EXIT PROGRAM statement, this
signals the logical end of the called
program only, and control then reverts to the next executable statement
following the CALL statement in the calling
runtime element. If control reaches a GOBACK statement and the program has been
called, controls continues as
for the EXIT PROGRAM statement; otherwise, control continues as for the STOP
statement. Thus the EXIT
PROGRAM statement terminates only the execution of the program in which it
occurs, the STOP RUN statement
terminates the execution of a run unit, and the GOBACK statement returns from
whence it came, whether it was
another program or the operating system."

Here the standard explicitly says use GOBACK to get a generic return. That seals
it for me. 

>> Because of historical antecedents, we're stuck with EXIT
>> PROGRAM, STOP RUN and GOBACK. 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>these to survive.

It survived so well that it's included in the 2002 standard, quoted above.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: two questions

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T04:56:58+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<u8bfa.1021$5N7.902@newssvr19.news.prodigy.com>`

```

reasons for my preference.  It wasn't a silly squabble 'til some of you
folks turned it into one.  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: two questions

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T18:06:36+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5jj2e$dkn$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<2664878.1048161545@dbforums.com> <YBSea.138$qr4.17976647@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <b5guo2$5bd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e7c447e.244287707@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> 
> This behavior is _not good_,

All behaviour is good, as long as the programmer understands it and can 
accurately predict its actions ...

Oh, I see what you mean    ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ GoBack, Main programs, etc (was: two questions

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-22T12:24:59-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5i9pu$8uf$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<2664878.1048161545@dbforums.com> <217e491a.0303202140.3863130f@posting.google.com> <YBSea.138$qr4.17976647@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <b5guo2$5bd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard is correct and I almost said it myself that the difference is
between CALLed programs and MAIN programs.  A CALLing program may be either
a MAIN program or a CALLed program (or with the 2002 Standard either a
user-defined function or a method may CALL another program).

Speaking of the 2002 Standard, the use of GoBack becomes *more* "useful"
(and/or more ambiguous) in that it is valid to "replace"

   Exit Program (as IBM et al has always allowed it)
   Stop Run (again as commonly implemented today)
  Exit Method  (as IBM and Micro Focus document it today - and Fujitsu MAY
allow it)
        and
  Exit Function

As I stated in another thread, this means that if you have common "procedure
division" source code, you can code a GoBack and not worry about what type
of "compilation entity" uses it.  However, as others have pointed out this
is pretty unusual.

I have YET to see any good reason for NOT using GoBack.  I have seen several
reasons why it may not be NEEDED in some environments (where you MUST know
at compile time if you have a MAIN or CALLed program).  In the case where
you WANT to do additional "things" between a MAIN program's exit and a
CALLed program's exit (something I have never seen - but which is certainly
POSSIBLE), you can (using 2002 Standard code - where EXIT Program need not
be in a sentence by itself)

    Exit Program/Method/Function
        Perform  In-Main-Program
    GoBack
     .

And in this case one MUST use the "EXIT" statement.  As I have stated
before, I have worked primarily in IBM and IBM-emulation environments and
this simply is NOT something that I have had a functional need to do.  I
accept, however, that others may.

    ***

Concerning the Fujitsu (and potentially other vendor) requirement to KNOW at
compile-time (or at least linking time) whether a program (without a Linkage
Section) is intended to be used as a MAIN program or a CALLed program (and
to so mark it - so it can't be used as the other),  I have always felt that
this was NOT conforming to the ANSI/ISO Standard.  Others may know of such a
"get-out" - but as far as I have ever seen, the ANSI/ISO Standard *requires*
that a program (without a Linkage Section) *must* be usable as either a MAIN
program or a CALLed program (without parameters passed to it) - without
recompiling.

Therefore (IMHO), Fujitsu is simply non-conforming in this requirement.
That is NOT to say that there is anything "wrong" with a programmer knowing
at compile-time which type of program they are creating - but it is to say
that the compiler vendor REQUIRING this decision at compile-time is
NON-conforming (as far as I have ever seen).  I would be interested if
others can find some place in the ANSI/ISO Standard ('85 or 2002) that
allows this requirement.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GoBack, Main programs, etc (was: two questions

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-22T19:20:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v7puo6fg7ctf80@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<2664878.1048161545@dbforums.com> <217e491a.0303202140.3863130f@posting.google.com> <YBSea.138$qr4.17976647@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <b5guo2$5bd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b5i9pu$8uf$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill, you might want to review 14.5.1 Run unit organization, in
2002, which says, in part,

"A run unit may additionally contain runtime modules and data
storage areas derived from the compilation of compilation units
written in languages other than COBOL; in this case the
requirements for the relationship and interaction between the
COBOL and the non-COBOL modules are defined by the
implementor."

The first part of this quote has always been true, but was not,
as far as I know, recognized in prior standards. Understanding
this allows us to recognize that COBOL cannot impose its
requirements on the operating evnivironment, but the operating
environment may impose its requirements on every language,
including COBOL. (This has implications for
"processor-dependent" another of your favorite topics. <g>)

If Fujitsu uses "compilation units written in languages other
than COBOL" and, as a result of using those compilation units
in a particuar operating environment, requires that one program
be marked as MAIN; this cannot be contrary to the rules of
COBOL for any standard. However, once control has been
transferred to a COBOL program, then the rules for COBOL
apply.

William M. Klein <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:b5i9pu$8uf$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net...
[snip]
> Concerning the Fujitsu (and potentially other vendor) requirement to KNOW
at
> compile-time (or at least linking time) whether a program (without a
Linkage
> Section) is intended to be used as a MAIN program or a CALLed program (and
> to so mark it - so it can't be used as the other),  I have always felt
that
> this was NOT conforming to the ANSI/ISO Standard.  Others may know of such
a
> "get-out" - but as far as I have ever seen, the ANSI/ISO Standard
*requires*
> that a program (without a Linkage Section) *must* be usable as either a
MAIN
> program or a CALLed program (without parameters passed to it) - without
> recompiling.
>
> Therefore (IMHO), Fujitsu is simply non-conforming in this requirement.
> That is NOT to say that there is anything "wrong" with a programmer
knowing
> at compile-time which type of program they are creating - but it is to say
> that the compiler vendor REQUIRING this decision at compile-time is
> NON-conforming (as far as I have ever seen).  I would be interested if
> others can find some place in the ANSI/ISO Standard ('85 or 2002) that
> allows this requirement.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GoBack, Main programs, etc (was: two questions

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T12:49:28+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5j0fo$5rd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<2664878.1048161545@dbforums.com> <b5guo2$5bd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b5i9pu$8uf$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <v7puo6fg7ctf80@corp.supernews.com>`

```

WMK wrote:

>> That is NOT to say that there is anything "wrong" with a programmer
> knowing
>> at compile-time which type of program they are creating - but it is to
>> say that the compiler vendor REQUIRING this decision at compile-time is
>> NON-conforming (as far as I have ever seen).  

It is not the vendor that is requiring this, it is the environment: 
Windows. If an application is built as several program then either is is 
all linked together as a single .EXE with a specific entry point (the MAIN) 
or it is dynamic with one .EXE (the MAIN) and several .DLLs.

DLLs are CALLed, .EXEs are executed.

I can see mainframers getting a brain-lock on this because they may be used 
to not caring.  In practice it is just not an issue.  A simple dummy MAIN 
can be written that just reads the cammand line, CALLs the program name 
provided there and the does a STOP RUN.  Then every other program can be 
executed as if it were a MAIN.

Of course there are issues with linkage parameters, but so are there in any 
system.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GoBack, Main programs, etc (was: two questions

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-22T18:58:18-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5j0rd$kv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<2664878.1048161545@dbforums.com> <b5guo2$5bd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b5i9pu$8uf$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <v7puo6fg7ctf80@corp.supernews.com> <b5j0fo$5rd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
If this is an "operating system" requirement, then how does Micro Focus
(using the same M$ linker) manage to support both DLL's and EXE's withOUT
requiring that you specify *at compile time* whether a program is a MAIN or
not?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GoBack, Main programs, etc (was: two questions

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-22T21:37:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v7q6ouk7oqdube@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<2664878.1048161545@dbforums.com> <b5guo2$5bd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b5i9pu$8uf$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <v7puo6fg7ctf80@corp.supernews.com> <b5j0fo$5rd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b5j0rd$kv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

William M. Klein <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:b5j0rd$kv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net...
> If this is an "operating system" requirement, then how does Micro Focus
> (using the same M$ linker) manage to support both DLL's and EXE's withOUT
> requiring that you specify *at compile time* whether a program is a MAIN
or
> not?

I am not certain how to address this because we are talking
about something that applies to older products; but consider
the following.

As a developer using MF COBOL/2 3.2, writing for Windows 3.0,
I might include the following in COBOL.DIR.

OMF"OBJ"
TARGET"286"
DEFFILE
DEFFILETYPE"WIN"

Then in any main program I include the directive:

      $SET NODLL

NODLL means EXE. The default directive is DLL.

Thus when the code is compiled, a Windows definition file (.def)
will be created that is marked for either .exe or .dll. When the
object files are linked, the appropriate file, executable or library,
is created.

This arrangement, imposed by a developer, is no different than
a directive imposed by a development environment. Both are
required for creating COBOL applications for Windows.
Neither arrangement, necessarily makes the source code
non-standard.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GoBack, Main programs, etc (was: two questions

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T17:16:20+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5jg46$cdd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<2664878.1048161545@dbforums.com> <b5i9pu$8uf$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <v7puo6fg7ctf80@corp.supernews.com> <b5j0fo$5rd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b5j0rd$kv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

> If this is an "operating system" requirement, then how does Micro Focus
> (using the same M$ linker) manage to support both DLL's and EXE's withOUT
> requiring that you specify *at compile time* whether a program is a MAIN
> or not?

MF can produce run-units in several ways.  For example the source can be 
'compiled' to .int intermediate code or to .gnt generated code.  When this 
is done the 'main' program is the run-time cobrun.exe or application server 
or somesuch (cobrun on Unix/Linux too).  

Another way is to produce .EXEs and .DLLs and use the static or the shared 
run time code.  For DOS these can all be .EXEs even when CALLed.  For OS/2 
and Windows the 'main' program is a .EXE and the CALLed programs are .DLLs.

If a 'real' Windows API program is to be written then the 'main' program 
may need to have an actual Windows message loop in it, so there is no 
chance of it not being known to be 'main'.

So, with MF it may be possible to compile without knowing, but it may not 
be possible to link without knowing, and this depends on how the 
application is built and what run-time will be used.

If the MS LINK is used then either compiler options are required or the 
linker must be told which 'statup' object to link in.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GoBack, Main programs, etc (was: two questions

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T16:23:59-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v7s8p5f72hun70@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<2664878.1048161545@dbforums.com> <217e491a.0303202140.3863130f@posting.google.com> <YBSea.138$qr4.17976647@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <b5guo2$5bd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b5i9pu$8uf$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill, there is a similar situation with Micro Focus COBOL/2 3.2
concerning nested programs. The default compiler directive is
NONESTCALL. In order to compile any nested program, including
a fully conforming ANS85 nested progam; the developer must
specify the NESTCALL directive.

I have no problem with this. I just put the NESTCALL directive
on the first line of source for the nested program; but I thought
I would bring to your attention that another vendor requires
directives under some circumstances.

that

a
> "get-out" - but as far as I have ever seen, the ANSI/ISO Standard
*requires*
> that a program (without a Linkage Section) *must* be usable as either a
MAIN
> program or a CALLed program (without parameters passed to it) - without
> recompiling.
>
> Therefore (IMHO), Fujitsu is simply non-conforming in this requirement.
> That is NOT to say that there is anything "wrong" with a programmer
knowing

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: GoBack, Main programs, etc (was: two questions

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T15:56:41-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5laiu$vlp$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<2664878.1048161545@dbforums.com> <217e491a.0303202140.3863130f@posting.google.com> <YBSea.138$qr4.17976647@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <b5guo2$5bd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b5i9pu$8uf$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <v7s8p5f72hun70@corp.supernews.com>`

Actually, this relates to another thread.  Micro Focus requires "bunches" of
directives to be ANSI/ISO conforming (e.g. QUOTE, TRUNC, NOOLDNEXTSENTENCE,
etc).  HOWEVER, all of those are required for *EVERY* program to be
conforming.  My "concern" was about the requirement to decide which TYPE of
program you were creating at compile-time before deciding which directive(s)
to use.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: two questions

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

```

On 21-Mar-2003, Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

> 'MAIN' _cannot_ be CALLed and a non-MAIN can _only_ be CALLed and cannot be
> directly activated.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: two questions

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-25T19:11:36+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5nlcg$5cm$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<2664878.1048161545@dbforums.com> <YBSea.138$qr4.17976647@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <b5guo2$5bd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b5ncli$qts$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:


>> Other systems make a firm distinction such that a program compiled as a
>> 'MAIN' _cannot_ be CALLed and a non-MAIN can _only_ be CALLed and cannot
>> be directly activated.
> 
> I have never seen the advantage of this architecture.   Call everything!

It isn't an 'advantage', it is a requirement of the environment.  In 
practice it makes no difference at all - there would be no point in CALLing 
any of my 'main's, trying to directly execute any non-main would crash and 
burn with 'linkage parameter not supplied'.

If I wanted to write programs that could be CALLed from, say, an 
application menu selection, or executed by a command line, it is simple to 
arrange this, as in fact I do, it is just different from your environment.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: two questions

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

- **References:** `<2664878.1048161545@dbforums.com> <YBSea.138$qr4.17976647@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <b5guo2$5bd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b5ncli$qts$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b5nlcg$5cm$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```

On 25-Mar-2003, Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

> >> Other systems make a firm distinction such that a program compiled as a
> >> 'MAIN' _cannot_ be CALLed and a non-MAIN can _only_ be CALLed and cannot
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> It isn't an 'advantage', it is a requirement of the environment.

Doesn't C in all environments require a "main"?

OK, let me change my wording.   I have never seen the advantage of making this
"a requirement of the environment".


> If I wanted to write programs that could be CALLed from, say, an
> application menu selection, or executed by a command line, it is simple to
> arrange this, as in fact I do, it is just different from your environment.

Yep.   We can get around this limitation, but there was no good reason for that
limitation to be part of the design of the environment in the first place.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: two questions

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-03-24T17:22:03-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0303241722.57a129a3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2664878.1048161545@dbforums.com> <YBSea.138$qr4.17976647@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <b5guo2$5bd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b5ncli$qts$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b5nlcg$5cm$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b5nluc$21q$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote

> Doesn't C in all environments require a "main"?

Yes, though this is not where the binary starts executing.  The 'start
up' is the entry point into the binary, this sets up some data and
then invokes 'main()'.  The compiler knows to set the entry point
correctly.

With Cobol compilers, this could be done several different ways, and
often is.
 
> OK, let me change my wording.   I have never seen the advantage of making this
> "a requirement of the environment".

DLLs in OS/2 and Windows, and .so shared objects with Unix/Linux can
be set to be sharable such that one copy resides in memory no matter
how many users call it.  Executables are not shared as they will
contain, or attach, all the data areas.

This means that there will be a difference in the way that mains
(executables) and .DLL/.so will work internally.

The apparent advantage is savings in memory for multiple copies of the
same code.

However, these needn't be sharable, so there may not be any specific
'advantage' except that the OS can load them with simple API calls and
build the links correctly rather than the compiler writer having to
re-invent that mechanism.

MF had already built their own CALL management routines back in the
CP/M days, revised for MS-DOS and early Unix, so they could continue
to use this for .int/.gnt and also use .DLL/.so if the user wished to.
 
> Yep.   We can get around this limitation, but there was no good reason for that
> limitation to be part of the design of the environment in the first place.

Its not a 'limitation', its a 'feature'.
It doesn't 'limit' what I can do in any way, it just changes the way
that I may do it.

There are many aspects that are different between mainframes (say) and
Unix or PC, and between different vendor's Cobol systems.  They are
just differences, features, or requirements.  They are not
specifically advantages or limitations.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: two questions

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-28T12:42:53-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E84899D.3000104@newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<2664878.1048161545@dbforums.com> <YBSea.138$qr4.17976647@newssvr15.news.prodigy.com> <b5guo2$5bd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b5ncli$qts$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b5nlcg$5cm$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <b5nluc$21q$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0303241722.57a129a3@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard wrote:
> "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote
> 
>>Doesn't C in all environments require a "main"?
> 
> Yes,

No.  From C89 (ISO 9899-1999) onward, two execution environments are 
defined for C: hosted and freestanding.  In the freestanding 
environment, the startup function is defined by the implementation.

A lot of C code is written for the freestanding environment, typically 
for embedded processors.

> though this is not where the binary starts executing.

Whether it is or not is implementation-dependent.  For that matter, the 
existence of a "binary" is implementation-dependent, and what it means 
to be the place where the program "starts executing" is rather subjective.

All the world is not Windows and Unix.  The brave may want to look at 
the various C implementations IBM's provided for OS/400 over the years.

Michael Wojcik
Principal Software Systems Developer
Micro Focus International
```

#### ↳ Re: two questions

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-21T13:43:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5f4ti$llt$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<2664878.1048161545@dbforums.com>`

```

On 20-Mar-2003, mfcobol2002 <marcos_as@terra.com.br> wrote:

> Second question
> When to use EXIT PROGRAM and when to use GO BACK?

On an IBM mainframe, there is no reason to use anything except GOBACK anymore.
```

## Thread: Window 2000 NTVDM leaks memory when COBOL CALL X"91" using 35! — 18 msgs

### Window 2000 NTVDM leaks memory when COBOL CALL X"91" using 35!

- **From:** tom.qian@brinksinc.com (Tom Q)
- **Date:** 2003-03-20T08:02:30-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<408f5895.0303200802.4e215e22@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi all:

I am looking for help to aviod W2K NTVDM.EXE blocking the memory.

My Application:
It is MF COBOL 3.4 16 bit running on Windows 2000 with SP3.  It checks
incoming file(s) very second and copy the file(s) to a Temp folder.  I
am using 'CALL X"91" using FUNCTION 35' to excute "Dir C:\IN\*.* /OD
/B > TEMP.WRK"

Sympton:
Everytime COBOL CALL X"91" using FUNCTION 35, NTVDM.EXE blocks the
memory (1k per second / 86mg per day ) unless I exit the application. 
But the NTVDM.EXE in Windows NT 4 with sp4 dose not do this to the
same application.

Solution:
Microsoft dose not have a solution for this issue.

Help:
Is there any other MF COBOL 3.4 commend can do "Dir C:\IN\*.* /OD /B >
TEMP.WRK" instead of 'CALL X"91" using FUNCTION 35'???

Thank for any input!

Tom
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Window 2000 NTVDM leaks memory when COBOL CALL X"91" using 35!

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-03-20T18:56:17-08:00

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote

> >Everytime COBOL CALL X"91" using FUNCTION 35, 

> have you tried using a CANCEL after the CALL?

The MF hex-number routines are internal run-time routines and may confuse a CANCEL.
```
