- **References:** `<408f5895.0303200802.4e215e22@posting.google.com> <ItWdnWsCGr6GcOSjXTWcoA@comcast.com> <408f5895.0303281130.79a6e877@posting.google.com>`

```

"Tom Q" <tom.qian@brinksinc.com> wrote in message
news:408f5895.0303281130.79a6e877@posting.google.com...
> "Russell Styles" <RWS0202@comcast.net> wrote in message
news:<ItWdnWsCGr6GcOSjXTWcoA@comcast.com>...
> > "Tom Q" <tom.qian@brinksinc.com> wrote in message
> > news:408f5895.0303200802.4e215e22@posting.google.com...
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> >     Try x"91" function 69, and do a dir directly, without the system
call.
> >
> >     You will have to sort the data yourself of course, as
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> TOM

    Sure there is.  It works just like it does in ver 3.1.  It seems to be a
direct map
of the standard dos function calls from before windows.

    Here is a cut and paste from net express doc, it hasn't changed at all.


Searches a directory for a given file specification.

call X"91" using result

                 function-code
                 parameter

Parameters:

result Group item defined as:

f-error  pic x comp-x
 f-handle pic x(2) comp-x
 f-attrout pic x comp-x
 f-time  pic x(2) comp-x
 f-date  pic x(2) comp-x
 f-size  pic x(4) comp-x
 f-fileout  pic x(n)
function-code pic x comp-x
parameter Group item defined as:
 f-action  pic x comp-x
 f-attrin  pic x comp-x
 f-filein  pic x(<m>)

On Entry:

function-code Contains 69.

f-action Defines the action to perform:
 0 Find the first matching file
 1 Find the next matching file
 2 Terminate the search early
 3 Find one matching file
f-attrin Attribute byte. All normal files, plus those with the attributes
specified by setting this parameter, are to be included. To specify
attributes, set the bits as follows:
 bit 7 Unused
 bit 6 Unused
 bit 5 Archived file
 bit 4 Subdirectory
 bit 3 Unused
 bit 2 System file

bit 1 Hidden file
 bit 0 Read-only file
f-filein The space-terminated filename specification of the file(s)
required. This can contain a drive/directory or any wildcard characters.

On Exit:

f-error Contains a status of the result:
 0 Success/file found
 1 No more files
 2 Error
f-handle The find handle. This field is set by the find-first function (see
f-action above) and should not be altered until the corresponding terminate
search has been done.
f-attrout Attribute byte of file found:
 bit 7 Unused
 bit 6 Unused
 bit 5 Archived file
 bit 4 Subdirectory
 bit 3 Volume label
 bit 2 System file
 bit 1 Hidden file

bit 0 Read-only file
f-time Time file was created, in DOS format:
 bits 15-11 Hour, 0-23
 bits 10-5 Minute, 0-59
 bits 4-0 Bi-second, 0-29
f-date Date file was created, in DOS format:
 bits 15-9 Year, 0-119 (1980-2099)
 bits 8-5 Month, 1-12
 bits 4-0 Day, 1-31
f-size The size of the file in bytes.
f-fileout The name of the file found, space-terminated.

Comments:

To find a single file, use the find-one function.

To find several files, use the find-first function and then use the
find-next function repeatedly. When there are no more matching files,
find-next returns with 1 in f-error. If you do not keep calling find-next
until this happens, you must finish by calling the terminate-search
function.

The f-handle field should be set to zero before you execute the find-first
or find-one function. After that, if you are using the first/next/terminate
sequence this field should not be changed until after the terminate search.
If you are using the find-one function this field is automatically reset to
0 after that call.

If at any time f-error returns 1 or 2, a terminate search has been carried
out automatically, and you should not execute the terminate-search function.

This routine cannot be used to check the existence of a network share
resource, such as \\server1\share1, but it can be used to locate files or
directories on that share, such as \\server1\share1\*.

Copyright (C) 2002 Micro Focus International Ltd. All rights reserved.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Window 2000 NTVDM leaks memory when COBOL CALL X"91" using 35!

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-29T20:21:55+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b62au3$nuc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<408f5895.0303200802.4e215e22@posting.google.com> <ItWdnWsCGr6GcOSjXTWcoA@comcast.com> <408f5895.0303281130.79a6e877@posting.google.com>`

```
Tom Q wrote:

>     Try x"91" function 69, and do a dir directly, without the system
>>     call.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks for the info. but there is no function 69 available in MF COBOL 3.4

The paper manuals that I have for MF Cobol 3.4 are a complete set for 3.2 
plus an 'update' manual.  I don't know if MF produced a full set for 3.4. 
You may only have CD documentation.

Cobol/2 used to be divided into 3 products: Cobol compiler, Toolset add on 
and Workbench.  It used to be possible to buy just the compiler, I never 
used the Toolset and certainly not the Workbench. Consequently the manuals 
are in 3 sets with some subjects distributed.

X'91' Function 69 (and a few others) are documented in the Toolset 
Reference Volume 2 Appendix A page A-3.

I don't know about the CD documentation.

In theory, if you use just the Cobol compiler items (as listed in the 
licence terms in the manual) then you can distribute applications freely.  
If you use Toolset items then you should buy run-time licences for 
distributing your application.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Window 2000 NTVDM leaks memory when COBOL CALL X"91" using 35!

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-28T20:08:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E84ACAC.71C46490@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<408f5895.0303200802.4e215e22@posting.google.com> <3E7A057C.93F5768F@shaw.ca> <408f5895.0303281135.50e700cd@posting.google.com>`

```


Tom Q wrote:

> Jimmy:
> Thanks for the info. but there is no function 69 available in MF COBOL 3.4.
> TOM

Just one more shot - in case it helps - Win APIs - . contact Young Kim at  ysk@kimsoft.com
- he has produced lists of Win APIs to call from Net Express. Perhaps you *may* be able to
do the same from your V 3.4. (Alternatively Ken Mullins might be reading this and perhaps
he can offer a suggestion about Win APIs).

I've never used it but I have a copy of ''Win 98 API Programming for Dummies' and I see
reference to that FindFirst and FindNext in my on-line help quote :-

1. FindFirstFile - searches in a directory for the first file whose name matches the search
filename :-

HANDLE FindFirstFile(
  LPCTSR lpFilename        // pointer to name of file to look for
  LPWIN#@_FIND_DATA lpFindFileData
                                        // Pointer to info obtained by search
);

If function succeeds returns search handle that you can uise in a call to FindNextFile or
FileClose. If function fails returns INVALID_HANDLE_VALUE.

2. FindNextFile
3. GetFileInformatonByHandle

etc.....

Now - if you were using Net Express - I can give you the OO code to do it in a flash. There
is a class provided caled Filename  As an example, this is my sub-class Filename2.cbl :-

*>------------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "getFilenamesWithWildcard".
*>------------------------------------------------------------
Local-storage section.
01 n                         pic x(4) comp-5.
01 ls-bytes                  pic x(4) comp-5.
01 ls-length                 pic x(4) comp-5.
01 ls-size                   pic x(4) comp-5.
01 ls-string                 object reference.
01 ls-string2                object reference.
01 ls-TempCollection         object reference.
01 ls-text                   pic x(100).
01 ls-text2                  pic x(100).
Linkage section.
01 lnk-length                pic x(4) comp-5.
01 lnk-WildCardName.
   05 pic x occurs 1 to 50 depending on lnk-length.
01 lnk-collection            object reference.

Procedure Division using     lnk-length
                             lnk-WildCardName
                   returning lnk-collection.

 invoke CharacterArray "withLengthValue"
        using     lnk-length
                  lnk-WildCardName
        returning ls-string
 invoke FileName "expandwildcards" using     ls-string
                                   returning ls-TempCollection

 invoke ls-TempCollection "size" returning ls-size
 compute ls-length = function length (ls-text)
 invoke SortedCollection "ofReferences"
        using ls-size returning lnk-collection

 perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > ls-size
   invoke ls-TempCollection "at" using n returning ls-string
   invoke ls-string "sizeinbytes" returning ls-bytes
   invoke ls-string "getValue" returning ls-text
   move ls-text(1:ls-bytes) to ls-text2
   invoke CharacterArray "withLengthValue"
          using     ls-length, ls-text2
          returning ls-string
   invoke lnk-collection "add" using ls-string
 End-perform

End Method "getFilenamesWithWildcard".
*>------------------------------------------------------------


Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Window 2000 NTVDM leaks memory when COBOL CALL X"91" using 35!

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)

```
He's using 16 bit COBOL.  No API call is going to help in the least,
unless you want to try a 16 bit WINDOWS api call - which might work,
but I expect the whole system is built for DOS and linked to run as a
DOS program.  Give that, I think outside a COBOL upgrade he's out of
luck solving the memory leak.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
tom.qian@brinksinc.com (Tom Q) wrote 

> am using 'CALL X"91" using FUNCTION 35' to excute "Dir C:\IN\*.* /OD
> /B > TEMP.WRK"

<pre>
      *--Version: 4.10  21/03/03  10:29:02
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.                     getfile2.
      * ================================================================
       DATA DIVISION.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
      * ----------------------------------------------------------------
      *  Cobol 2 DOS areas
       01  DOS-INT21                   PIC X VALUE X"21".

       01  Register-Flag               PIC X.

       01  DOS-AX.
           03  DOS-AH                  PIC X.
           03  DOS-AL.
            05 DOS-AL-9                PIC 99 COMP.
       01  DOS-AX-9 REDEFINES DOS-AX   PIC S9(4) COMP.

       01  DOS-BX.
           03  DOS-BX-VAL              PIC 9(4) COMP.
       01  DOS-BX-9 REDEFINES DOS-BX   PIC S9(4) COMP.

       01  DOS-CX.
           03  DOS-CH                  PIC X.
           03  DOS-CL                  PIC X.
       01  DOS-CX-9 REDEFINES DOS-CX   PIC S9(4) COMP.

       01  DOS-DX.
           03  DOS-DH.
            05 DOS-DH-9                PIC 99 COMP.
           03  DOS-DL.
            05 DOS-DL-9                PIC 99 COMP.
       01  DOS-DX-9 REDEFINES DOS-DX   PIC S9(4) COMP.

       01  In-Index                    PIC S9(4) COMP-5.
       01  File-Name.
           03  FN-Char                 PIC X OCCURS 65.

       01  DIR-Attribute               PIC X VALUE X"10".
       01  R-Attribute                 PIC X VALUE X"01".
       01  A-Attribute                 PIC X VALUE X"20".
       01  S-Attribute                 PIC X VALUE X"04".
       01  H-Attribute                 PIC X VALUE X"02".

       01  WS-Attribute                PIC X.

       01  DTA.
           03  FILLER                  PIC X(21).
           03  DTA-Attribute           PIC X.
           03  DTA-Time                PIC  9(4) COMP-5.
           03  DTA-Date                PIC  9(4) COMP-5.
           03  DTA-Size                PIC  9(9) COMP-5.
           03  DTA-Name                PIC X(13).
           03  FILLER                  PIC X(75).

      * ================================================================
       LINKAGE SECTION.

           COPY "getfile.ws".  *> \az\include\getfile.ws

      * ================================================================
       PROCEDURE DIVISION USING Action File-Link Return-Data.
       Main SECTION.
       Program-Getfilex.

           MOVE SPACES         TO Return-Data
           EVALUATE Action
           WHEN "S"
               PERFORM Start-Search
               MOVE "N"        TO Action
               PERFORM Move-Return
           WHEN "N"
               PERFORM Next-Search
               PERFORM Move-Return
           WHEN "F"
               PERFORM Get-Free-Space
           END-EVALUATE

           EXIT PROGRAM
           .

       Move-Return.

           MOVE DTA-Attribute   TO WS-Attribute
           CALL "cbl_and"    USING DIR-Attribute
                                   WS-Attribute
                                   BY VALUE 1

           IF ( DTA-Name = SPACES )
               CONTINUE
           ELSE
           IF ( WS-Attribute = DIR-Attribute )
               MOVE "<DIR>"     TO Return-Data
           ELSE
               PERFORM Move-Filename
               MOVE DTA-Size    TO RT-Size
               PERFORM Move-Date
               PERFORM Move-Time
               PERFORM Move-Attributes
               .


      * ---------------------------------------------------------------
       Move-Attributes.

           MOVE "____"          TO RT-Attribute

           MOVE DTA-Attribute   TO WS-Attribute
           CALL "cbl_and"    USING R-Attribute
                                   WS-Attribute
                                   BY VALUE 1
           IF ( WS-Attribute = R-Attribute )
               MOVE "R"         TO RT-Attribute(1:1)
           END-IF

           MOVE DTA-Attribute   TO WS-Attribute
           CALL "cbl_and"    USING A-Attribute
                                   WS-Attribute
                                   BY VALUE 1
           IF ( WS-Attribute = A-Attribute )
               MOVE "A"         TO RT-Attribute(2:1)
           END-IF

           MOVE DTA-Attribute   TO WS-Attribute
           CALL "cbl_and"    USING S-Attribute
                                   WS-Attribute
                                   BY VALUE 1
           IF ( WS-Attribute = S-Attribute )
               MOVE "S"         TO RT-Attribute(3:1)
           END-IF

           MOVE DTA-Attribute   TO WS-Attribute
           CALL "cbl_and"    USING H-Attribute
                                   WS-Attribute
                                   BY VALUE 1
           IF ( WS-Attribute = H-Attribute )
               MOVE "H"         TO RT-Attribute(4:1)
           END-IF
           .

      * ----------------------------------------------------------------
       Move-Filename.

           UNSTRING DTA-Name
               DELIMITED BY "." OR X"00"
               INTO RT-Name
                    RT-Type
           IF ( RT-Type NOT = SPACES )
               MOVE "."    TO RT-Dot
           END-IF
           .

       MOVE-Date.

           DIVIDE 32   INTO DTA-Date
                               GIVING DTA-Date
                            REMAINDER RD-DD
           DIVIDE 16   INTO DTA-Date
                               GIVING RD-YY
                            REMAINDER RD-MM
           IF ( RD-YY > 19 )
               SUBTRACT 20       FROM RD-YY
           ELSE
               ADD 80              TO RD-YY
           END-IF
           MOVE "/"                TO RD-S1
                                      RD-S2
           .

       MOVE-Time.

           DIVIDE 32   INTO DTA-Time
                               GIVING DTA-Time
                            REMAINDER RT-SC
           MULTIPLY 2              BY RT-SC
           DIVIDE 64   INTO DTA-Time
                               GIVING RT-HR
                            REMAINDER RT-MN
           MOVE ":"                TO RT-S1
                                      RT-S2
           .

      * ----------------------------------------------------------------
       Start-Search.

           MOVE X"11"       TO Register-Flag
           MOVE X"1A00"     TO DOS-AX
           CALL x"84"
               USING DOS-INT21
                     Register-Flag
                     DOS-AX
                     DOS-BX
                     DOS-CX
                     DTA

           MOVE SPACES      TO DTA
           MOVE File-Link   TO File-Name

           MOVE 1           TO In-Index
           PERFORM
               UNTIL FN-Char(In-Index) = SPACE

               ADD 1        TO In-Index
           END-PERFORM

           MOVE X"00"       TO FN-Char(In-Index)

           MOVE X"11"       TO Register-Flag
           MOVE X"4E00"     TO DOS-AX
           MOVE X"0000"     TO DOS-CX
           CALL x"84"
               USING DOS-INT21
                     Register-Flag
                     DOS-AX
                     DOS-BX
                     DOS-CX
                     File-Name
           .

      * ----------------------------------------------------------------
       Next-Search.

           MOVE SPACES      TO DTA-NAME

           MOVE X"11"       TO Register-Flag
           MOVE X"4F00"     TO DOS-AX
           CALL x"84"
               USING DOS-INT21
                     Register-Flag
                     DOS-AX
                     DOS-BX
                     DOS-CX
                     DOS-DX
           .

      * ----------------------------------------------------------------
       Get-Free-Space.

           IF ( File-Link(2:1) = ":" )
               MOVE "A"              TO DOS-AL
               MOVE File-Link(1:1)   TO DOS-DL
               ADD  1                TO DOS-DL-9
               SUBTRACT DOS-AL-9   FROM DOS-DL-9
           ELSE
               MOVE X"00"            TO DOS-DL
           END-IF

           MOVE X"FF"       TO Register-Flag
           MOVE X"3600"     TO DOS-AX
           CALL x"84"
               USING DOS-INT21
                     Register-Flag
                     DOS-AX
                     DOS-BX
                     DOS-CX
                     DOS-DX

           MOVE ZERO               TO DTA-Size
           IF ( DOS-AX NOT = X"FFFF" )
               MOVE     DOS-AX-9   TO DTA-Size
               MULTIPLY DOS-BX-9   BY DTA-Size
               MULTIPLY DOS-CX-9   BY DTA-Size
           END-IF

           MOVE DTA-Size           TO RT-Size
           .

      * ----------------------------------------------------------------
      *            end of program file source
      * ----------------------------------------------------------------

getfile.ws

      *--Version: 4.10  21/03/03  10:22:14

       01  Action                      PIC X.
      *    Input Parameter:
      *           S = start
      *           N = next
      *           F = disk free space

       01  File-Link.
           03  File-Char               PIC X OCCURS 65.
      *    Input Parameter:
      *           file path eg: C:\Data\*.DAT

       01  Return-Data.
      *    Output Parameter:

           03  RT-Name-Ext.
            05 RT-Name                 PIC X(8).
            05 RT-Dot                  PIC X.
            05 RT-Type.
               07 RT-TChar             PIC X OCCURS 3.
           03  FILLER                  PIC X.
           03  RT-Attribute            PIC X(4).
      *                                    RASH
           03  RT-Att-RDF REDEFINES RT-Attribute.
            05 RT-Att-Char             PIC X OCCURS 4.
           03  RT-Size                 PIC ZZZ,ZZZ,ZZ9.
           03  FILLER                  PIC XX.
           03  RT-Date.

               05  RD-DD               PIC 99.
               05  RD-S1               PIC X.
               05  RD-MM               PIC 99.
               05  RD-S2               PIC X.
               05  RD-YY               PIC 99.
           03  FILLER                  PIC XX.
           03  RT-Time.
               05  RT-Hr               PIC 99.
               05  RT-S1               PIC X.
               05  RT-Mn               PIC 99.
               05  RT-S2               PIC X.
               05  RT-Sc               PIC 99.

      * ----------------------------------------------------------------
      *            end of program file source
      * ----------------------------------------------------------------



</pre>
```

## Thread: fujitsu cobol linkage problem — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### fujitsu cobol linkage problem

- **From:** "Paul" <paulhbliu@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-20T13:54:53-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<28oea.4156$lQ4.892565@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Hi

Using the Fujitsu cobol free compiler
In a subprogram: I want to link the multiple files which specified in
linkage section
also there are several subprograms in different levels needs to be done the
same thing(link)

when I enter all the link file name in the "Link Option", and build,
It complained:
LINK :fatal error LNK1104 cannot open file "D:\alib.cblD:\blib.cbl....."

I'm not sure if that's the proper way to link files in a project, when I
have mutiple files to be linked.
can anybody give me a suggestion ?

thanks for help
Paul
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: fujitsu cobol linkage problem

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-22T09:50:38+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5g1k4$ocq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<28oea.4156$lQ4.892565@news20.bellglobal.com> <eYvea.5647$K37.794291@news20.bellglobal.com> <SNCdnTKaZ9gNj-ajXTWckA@giganews.com> <8_Kea.4851$lQ4.1110582@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Paul wrote:

> Do I need to describe the copybook in the fujitsu compiler ( it seems
> NO_NEED )
>>
>> You can't link copybooks either.

In the Project Manager you can create 'folders' for each program (.EXE or 
.DLL).  These can be 'Source code', 'Object' or 'Libraries'.

Copy books can be added to the _source_code_ folder, but it is the COPY 
statement in the program that causes these to be included in the program.

Putting these into the folder is optional, but if included then if they are 
changed the manager will know that the program has to be recompiled to use 
the latest source version.

You seem to have added them to the 'Library' folder as they are being 
linked in.  What should go into the library are .LIB files which may be 
created if you have Cobol .DLLs that are CALLed, or are specific libraries 
(.LIB) required by your program.
```

#### ↳ Re: fujitsu cobol linkage problem

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-03-20T21:06:24-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0303202106.2f3096b7@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<28oea.4156$lQ4.892565@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
The COBOL FAQ and the FAQ on the Fujitsu site cover this.  You need to
specify the @OPTIONS MAIN to set which program is the main program.

…[15 more quoted lines elided]…

```

- **Message-ID:** `<V4rea.23790$jE3.559280@news.xtra.co.nz>`

```
Fujitsu Power Cobol 6. I can seem to get this to work. It say unresolved
external linkage. I have the kernel32.lib file included in the module. Any
ideas ?
```

#### ↳ Re: API GetFullPAthName

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-20T19:16:10-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LrydnXlxmJuP9OejXTWckw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<V4rea.23790$jE3.559280@news.xtra.co.nz>`

```

"James" <james@wasystems.co.nz> wrote in message
news:V4rea.23790$jE3.559280@news.xtra.co.nz...
> Fujitsu Power Cobol 6. I can seem to get this to work. It say unresolved
> external linkage. I have the kernel32.lib file included in the module. Any
> ideas ?


Make sure Script/Properties/Options includes:

ALPHAL(WORD)

Without this, Fujitsu folds called module names to upper-case.
```

```
Yes, I agree it SHOULD be Kernel32.lib. But he tried that and it didn't
work... Using the alias GetFullPathNameA is also a useful trick that has
worked for me on a few occasions.

However, I almost always include User32.lib in the project when I am using
the API and since I've been doing that I've had fewer problems...

I was trying to help <G>. As such, I suggested doing what I would do when
faced with the same problem. If it still doesn't work he is no worse off and
he has eliminated one more possibility...

Pete.
```

```
 ENVIRONMENT     DIVISION.
 DATA            DIVISION.
 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
 01 lpFileName       pic x(256).
 01 nBufferLength    pic s9(6) comp-5.
 01 lpBuffer         pic x(256).
 01 lpFilePart       pic x(256) value spaces.
 01 return-value     pic s9(6) comp-5.
 PROCEDURE       DIVISION.
     move "main.exe" to lpBuffer.
     move 256 to nBufferLength.
     call "GetFullPathNameA" with STDCALL using
                by reference lpFileName,
                by value nBufferLength,
                by reference lpBuffer,
                by reference lpFilePart
          returning return-value.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: API GetFullPAthName

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T21:52:22-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0303232152.4de0bbc7@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<V4rea.23790$jE3.559280@news.xtra.co.nz> <b5ev31$ktg$1@mailnews.kub.nl> <71pfa.29021$jE3.657664@news.xtra.co.nz>`

```
Are you using Binary(Byte) as a compiler directive?

If so - the Pic S9(6) COMP-5's will be 3 bytes (not good).

The MS docs indicate the following:

DWORD GetFullPathName(
  LPCTSTR lpFileName,  // file name
  DWORD nBufferLength, // size of path buffer
  LPTSTR lpBuffer,     // path buffer
  LPTSTR *lpFilePart   // address of file name in path
);


I didn't test this but try:

  01 lpFileName       pic x(256).
  01 nBufferLength    pic s9(9) comp-5.
  01 lpBuffer         pic x(256).
  01 lpFilePart       POINTER.
  01 return-value     pic S9(9) comp-5.
  PROCEDURE       DIVISION.
      move "main.exe" to lpBuffer.
      move 256 to nBufferLength.
      call "GetFullPathNameA" with STDCALL using
                 by reference lpFileName,
                 by value nBufferLength,
                 by reference lpBuffer,
                 by reference lpFilePart
           returning return-value.

I changed lpfilepart to Pointer because the docs say:

lpFilePart 
[out] Pointer to a buffer that receives the address (in lpBuffer) of
the final file name component in the path.

Since the buffer is receiving the address, it's a pointer.  Now, I
expect that you can't pass this pointer by reference - I think the
compiler will compain.

So change it to pic s9(9) comp-5 and redefine it with usage pointer. 
You'll have to use that pointer to get the path portion.


"James" <james@wasystems.co.nz> wrote in message news:<71pfa.29021$jE3.657664@news.xtra.co.nz>...
> ENVIRONMENT     DIVISION.
>  DATA            DIVISION.
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

```
Correcting my post its no a BUG its a Limitation of Power cobol, because
is impossible  to work  with a compiler that has this limitatios

see  Piece of Power cobol Manual

A COBOL picture edit string.When the RenderStyle property is "1 - COBOL
PICTURE", defines the COBOL picture string to be used for editing the
data.The CurrencySign and DecimalPointIsComma properties also affect the
data.PowerCOBOL supports up to 4 decimal digits and up to 14 digits in
the integer part. The total string length, whose picture is specified by
the PictureString property, must be shorter than 8192 bytes.

All cobol that I know you can define picture string up to
18 digits doent matter how decimal digits we have

in all othes cobol, you can have it  pic 9(02)v9(15)

I will give up to work with Power soon

carlos lages
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-22T18:56:46-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5j0oh$9r0$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<2669309.1048255110@dbforums.com> <2670353.1048265354@dbforums.com> <3e7b9906_1@text-west.newsgroups.com> <2674192.1048374308@dbforums.com>`

```
Others have given you various alternatives, but let me suggest another
modification of your code:

 working-storage Section.
 01  ws-campo  pic 9(04)v9999999  value zeros.
 01  Another-Field.
       05 Num-Edit  Pic 9(04).9(07)    Value Zero.
      05  Alpha redefines Num-Edit  Pic X(12)

 procedure Division.

    Compute  ws-campo = 10 / 3
    Move WS-Campo to Num-Edit
    move  Alpha   to  Pow-Text of  wcampo    (correctly defined in your
form)

P.S.  You may have already told us, but what type of field are you working
with that DOES require greater than 4 decimal places?
```

#### ↳ Re: Interest Calculation

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-22T19:39:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e7cb085.271946529@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<mtom7vo2g7arug9imj1d91tksmk1gibum7@4ax.com>`

```
Estev�o <ad2sanbr@yahoo.com.br> wrote:

>Hello,
>
>can anyone give me the algorythm in COBOL II of the transformation of
>the Nominal interest rate into Effective Interest rate (following the
>EU regulations).

ei = ((1 + (ni/f)) ** f) - 1  *> f = number of periods per year

If cash flows are irregular or bi-directional, you must converge on the answer:

compute precision = .0000005 / frequency
compute rate = 1 + (.1 / frequency)  *> initial extimate is 10%
move 1 to d, d-absolute
move zero to iterations
perform until d-absolute less than precision
    add 1 to iterations
    move present-value to b, c
    perform varying n from 1 by 1 until n > n-limit
        compute b = (b * rate) - payment (n)
        if  n less than n-limit
            compute c = b + (c * rate)
        end-if
    end-perform
    if  c not equal to zero
        compute d = b / c
    end-if
    subtract d from rate
    move d to d-absolute
    if  iterations greater than 9 and d-absolute not less than previous-d
        display 'does not compute'
        move zero to rate
        goback
    end-if
    move d-absolute to previous-d
end-perform
```

#### ↳ Re: Interest Calculation

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T07:17:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0303230717.7000cdef@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<mtom7vo2g7arug9imj1d91tksmk1gibum7@4ax.com>`

```
Estevï¿½o <ad2sanbr@yahoo.com.br> wrote in message news:<mtom7vo2g7arug9imj1d91tksmk1gibum7@4ax.com>...
> Hello,
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> TIA

How about something simpler than the other offering:

Taken from here: http://www.rwent.com/NominalvsEffective.html

There is a formula for finding the Effective Rate...  f = ( 1 + i )m - 1

 

where f is Effective Rate and m = number of compounds per year.

 

EXAMPLE

Find the effective rate for 18% compounded quarterly

 

i = .18 / 4 = 0.045

m = 4

 

f = ( 1 + i )m - 1

 

f = ( 1.045 )4 - 1

f = 1.192518601 - 1

f = 0.192518601

As a percent, f = 19.25%.
```

## Thread: IBM's "DB2 integrated coprocessor" — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### IBM's "DB2 integrated coprocessor"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-21T16:34:59-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5g42l$8r9$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
If you are currently - or plan in the near future - to use the "DB2
integrated coprocessor" which became available (via PTF) with IBM COBOL for
OS/390 & VM V2R2 and was "built-in" to Enterprise COBOL, I suggest that you
look at the following APARs.  I believe these are the "numbers" for COBOL
for OS/390 & VM - but they should show appropriate "sysroutes" for
Enterprise COBOL (either release)

PQ69319
PQ71089
PQ69652 (PE)
PQ71622 (for PQ69652)
PQ71463 (canceled as PQ71089 works without a Cobol fix)

    ***
Thanks to Roland Schiradin (customer)  and Tom Ross (IBM) for continuing to
pursue getting this feature "fully functional".

Personally, I am *not* aware of comparable APARs for the integrated CICS
support, but others may know if these need to be looked at as well.
```

<a id="t-767ad50899"></a>
## Thread: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification — 64 msgs

### "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-21T21:14:29-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
As a follow-up on another thread (discussion "ADDRESS OF" as part of both
Format 7 of the SET statement and in the data-address-identifier), I thought
I would give some other examples from older COBOL Standards of "similar"
cases of the same COBOL words being used within different syntactic
elements.

Consider:

A) the reserved word "zero" in the following 2 examples (valid in every
COBOL Standard that I know of)

  IF NUM-FIELD = ZERO

       versus

  IF NUM-FIELD ZERO

The first case uses the FIGURATIVE CONSTANT "zero" within a relational
expression.
The second case uses "zero" as a sign-test (as opposed to "positive" or
negative)

Although these two IF statements will (usually? always?) pass or fail, they
are significantly different tests. For example, the first case could be
expanded to

  IF NUM-FIELD = ZERO OR 2

while it would be invalid syntax to say

  IF NUM-FIELD ZERO OR 2

        ***

B) or possibly even more "obvious", consider

    USAGE DISPLAY

        versus

    DISPLAY "USAGE"

Clearly, these two uses of the reserved word "DISPLAY" have almost NOTHING
to do with each other.

        ****

The only reason that I am posting this note is to make it clear that the
same COBOL word may (and has been for the entire history of COBOL) been
valid within different contexts - as totally different syntactic units.
```

#### ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-22T12:33:12
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e7c5813_1@text-west.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net...
> As a follow-up on another thread (discussion "ADDRESS OF" as part of both
> Format 7 of the SET statement and in the data-address-identifier), I
thought
> I would give some other examples from older COBOL Standards of "similar"
> cases of the same COBOL words being used within different syntactic
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>   IF NUM-FIELD = ZERO

This is a relational condition.

>
>        versus
>
>   IF NUM-FIELD ZERO
>

This is a Class test. ("Class" is another word with multiple meanings since
the 2002 standard...<G>)

> The first case uses the FIGURATIVE CONSTANT "zero" within a relational
> expression.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Although these two IF statements will (usually? always?) pass or fail,
they
> are significantly different tests. For example, the first case could be
> expanded to
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Ah, these are implied objects (nothing to do with "object" as used in
"Object Oriented Programming"). Personally I don't use them because I think
they can lead to confusion. Do I take it from this that you endorse this
approach, Bill?

In the latter case I wouldn't be surprised if there is a compiler somewhere
that would compile this OK, as if it had been coded:

if num-field zero OR num-field = 2...

defaulting the implied relation to "=", but maybe not...

Your point about words being "re-used" in the standards is well taken. There
are several examples above.

Maybe it reflects on the lack of imagination of the Standard Writers? Or
maybe they just have little choice.

Pete.

>         ***
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
```

#### ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-22T19:39:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e7c9d08.266956898@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>As a follow-up on another thread (discussion "ADDRESS OF" as part of both
>Format 7 of the SET statement and in the data-address-identifier), I thought
>I would give some other examples from older COBOL Standards of "similar"
>cases of the same COBOL words being used within different syntactic
>elements.

You mean ambiguity resolved by context. I have no problem with that in
principle. In natural languages, we handle it routinely and with little effort. 
But computer language designers think ambiguity is BAD. They think programming
languages should be disambiguated by lexical analysis alone. That line of
thinking gave us "==" in C conditionals. Why? Because they've all studied math,
but few have studied linguistics. They are designing a math=like algorithmic
notation rather than a language. 

>Consider:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>negative)

That's a good example. 

I never understood the need for a sign test. When I want to know whether a
number is positive, I write 'num-field > 0'. 

>B) or possibly even more "obvious", consider
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>to do with each other.

A more confusing example is USAGE POINTER vs. STRING .. WITH POINTER.  Whoops,
the first is not in the standard. 

>The only reason that I am posting this note is to make it clear that the
>same COBOL word may (and has been for the entire history of COBOL) been
>valid within different contexts - as totally different syntactic units.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T08:40:32+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5iht0$v2v$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3e7c9d08.266956898@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> That line of thinking gave us "==" in C conditionals. Why? Because they've
> all studied math, but few have studied linguistics. They are designing a
> math=like algorithmic notation rather than a language.

FORTRAN = FORmula TRANslation

ALGOL = ALGOrithmic Language

Why would 'linguistics' have anything to do with it ?

COBOL, FORTRAN and BASIC like languages distinguish between assignment and 
equality test from context. They can do this because assignment is a 
statement (LET in BASIC).

In Algol like languages, such as C, assignment is an operator, not a 
statement and so a different symbol has to be used.

I do get this image from your 'hates' where you have failed somewhere and 
blame it on how the compiler was written or somesuch.

You failed to have correct output for the IRD (was it?) and so INITIALIZE 
is to blame.  Perhaps you had a C program with 'if ( x = 0 )' and so it is 
the fault of K&R's lack of linguistics.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T05:41:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e7d32e2.305323960@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3e7c9d08.266956898@news.optonline.net> <b5iht0$v2v$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Why would 'linguistics' have anything to do with it ?

We're talking about programming *languages*, not math-like languages. 

>COBOL, FORTRAN and BASIC like languages distinguish between assignment and 
>equality test from context. They can do this because assignment is a 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>statement and so a different symbol has to be used.

Language 'theorists' abhor context sensitivity. Read any of their writing and
you'll see "context-free" repeated over and over. Use of the postpostion "-free"
implies 'we used to suffer from it but now we have been liberated.' As in
"smoke-free'. It is an artifact of culture rather than a gesture toward
communicating truth.

>I do get this image from your 'hates' where you have failed somewhere and 
>blame it on how the compiler was written or somesuch.

My only failure was inspiring CLC readers to explore options they'd not
considered before, such as lists. 

> Perhaps you had a C program with 'if ( x = 0 )' and so it is 
>the fault of K&R's lack of linguistics.

That's a good example of poor linguistics. 

COBOL was designed in the late '50s by people who had nothing to rely on but
intuition and common sense. More modern languages, including C, were designed
with an eye toward Formal Definitions which could be processed by software. As
expected, the software favored context-free over context-sensitive because it
was easier to process. As a result, we have languages which are easy to parse
but difficult for a human to understand. 

I like COBOL because it's the most human-oriented programming language.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T19:55:26+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5jpeh$g9q$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3e7c9d08.266956898@news.optonline.net> <b5iht0$v2v$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e7d32e2.305323960@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>>Robert Wagner wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>> designing a math=like algorithmic notation rather than a language.

>>Why would 'linguistics' have anything to do with it ?
> 
> We're talking about programming *languages*, not math-like languages.

FORTRAN, Algol, C are math-like languages.  Or specifically, the 
expressions are.

> Language 'theorists' abhor context sensitivity. Read any of their writing
> and you'll see "context-free" repeated over and over. 

Who cares what language theorists like or abhor ?

> It is an artifact of culture rather
> than a gesture toward communicating truth.

Yeah, right, rant on.

If you can't be bothered reading the definition in the manual and want to 
use 'intuition' or just invent how it _should_ work from English or some 
other, then of course you will find it difficult.

> My only failure was inspiring CLC readers to explore options they'd not
> considered before, such as lists.

Yes, that was a failure, but that is because tables do exactly what is 
required already.

But you are too modest, you also failed to convince CLC about many other 
issues: that INITIALIZE was wrong, that the standard had errors, that SET 
would give a bound check error, ....
 
> COBOL was designed in the late '50s by people who had nothing to rely on
> but intuition and common sense. 

Actually they had several languages to use to form the basis of Cobol and 
much experience in the use of those.  This is why every Cobol manual has 
references to some the precursors.  Most of the original Cobol language was 
taken from predecessors.

> More modern languages, including C, 

Is C 'modern' ?  While Cobol is 40 odd years old, C is 30+ years (and C++ 
is 20+).

> were
> designed with an eye toward Formal Definitions which could be processed by
> software. 

Nonsence.  The 'formal definition' of C was 'what the current version of 
the compiler does' with additional notes in K&R's book.  It's 'design' 
criteria was "B with a few more types (B only had 'word' as a type)", and 
'B' was a simplified version of BCPL, BCPL was a basic (lower case) or cut 
down version of CPL where this was 'Combined Programming Language' - a 
synthesis of various languages around the Atlas Laboritory in Cambridge, 
England and London University, notably ACL and APL (not the IBM one).

CPL was a committe designed language much as PL/I was.  Each committee 
member had their own features they wanted to throw in.

Now Algol used a 'Formal Definition' as did all the Algol-68 contenders 
including Pascal.  But this had nothing to do with how 'easy' it would be 
to process. In fact the Algol-60 compiler writers 'went on strike' over 
parts of the formal definition because it was so hard to implement.

> As expected, the software favored context-free over
> context-sensitive because it was easier to process. 

No. Wrong.  Algol like languages (such as C) are like that for entirely 
different reasons.

BASIC, for example, is relatively easy to process, but it is not 
context-free.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T18:02:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e7deebe.21107077@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3e7c9d08.266956898@news.optonline.net> <b5iht0$v2v$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e7d32e2.305323960@news.optonline.net> <b5jpeh$g9q$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
:
>> Language 'theorists' abhor context sensitivity. Read any of their writing
>> and you'll see "context-free" repeated over and over. 
>
>Who cares what language theorists like or abhor ?

Programmers care, because we have to use the languages they create. 

>> My only failure was inspiring CLC readers to explore options they'd not
>> considered before, such as lists.
>
>Yes, that was a failure, but that is because tables do exactly what is 
>required already.

It is nearly impossible to write a reasonable parser using tables. Do you
allocate space for 1,000 words per statement, times 100,000 statements per
program? That's 100 megabytes of virtual memory. Parsers are not limited to
compilers, they are found in business applications. For instance, telephone
message processing and my current financial information project. 

>But you are too modest, you also failed to convince CLC about many other 
>issues: that INITIALIZE was wrong, that the standard had errors, that SET 
>would give a bound check error, ....

If there is nothing wrong with INITIALIZE, why did the 2002 standard 'improve'
it by adding FILLER? Because _other_ people complained about it not working
'right'. I've conceded my take on bounds checking was obsolete. 

>> COBOL was designed in the late '50s by people who had nothing to rely on
>> but intuition and common sense. 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>is 20+).

The topic is context 'freedom', not age. COBOL's ancestors were designed by
programmers. Ten years later, design had moved to Computer Scientists, who were
more concerned with 'purity' than ease of use. 

>> were
>> designed with an eye toward Formal Definitions which could be processed by
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>member had their own features they wanted to throw in.

Thank you for the history lesson, but you failed to address the point. C has but
a few minor instances of ambiguity resolved by context. They seem to have
slipped in by accident. C++ has _zero_  .. by design. 

>> As expected, the software favored context-free over
>> context-sensitive because it was easier to process. 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>context-free.

It is easy for a human programmer to process, when writing a
compiler/interpreter 'the old-fashioned way'. It is difficult to understand for
a rule-based automated compiler writer such as yacc. 

One reason 'they' hate COBOL is because it is so hard to define with rules.
Rather than admitting the inadequacy of their tools, they condemn the language
for being 'irregular'. Who says context-free is a good thing? Not us
programmers. Tool builders made that up out of necessity. 

It is fortunate they don't read CLC. When their tools are criticized, they howl
louder than Howard's defense of periods. 

Robert
```

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>Who cares what language theorists like or abhor ?
> 
> Programmers care, because we have to use the languages they create.

Specifically which 'language theorists' have 'created' the languages that I 
program in ?
 
> It is nearly impossible to write a reasonable parser using tables. 

And exactly how many in CLC want to write a 'reasonable parser' in Cobol ?

> Parsers are not limited
> to compilers, they are found in business applications.

ain't big, but it ain't hard.

> If there is nothing wrong with INITIALIZE, why did the 2002 standard

> not working 'right'. 

They added to the options and did not change the behaviour without these 
new options.

> The topic is context 'freedom', not age. 

Your claim was 'modern'.  Are you now 'defining' modern as 'context free' ? 
Previously you attempted to define it as '3 - 5 years'.

>>> were
>>> designed with an eye toward Formal Definitions which could be processed
>>> by software.

The 'points' that you attempted to make were: 

"""  .. including C 'were designed towards Formal Definitions which could 
be processed by software' """

C, and all precursors never went near a 'Formal Definition' until many 
years later.

Languages which had been designed with 'Formal Definitions' were not 'easy 
to process with software'.

> One reason 'they' hate COBOL is because it is so hard to define with
> rules. Rather than admitting the inadequacy of their tools, they condemn
> the language for being 'irregular'. 

Cobol is irregular, that is not 'condemning' it, it is describing it.

> Who says context-free is a good thing?
> Not us programmers. 

Context-free is useful in many cases.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-24T00:45:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e7e4835.44013283@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <b5iht0$v2v$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e7d32e2.305323960@news.optonline.net> <b5jpeh$g9q$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e7deebe.21107077@news.optonline.net> <b5l4rl$2rb$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>program in ?

C was developed by K & R at AT&T's Computing Science Research Center. Ritchie
has a PhD in math. 

Bjarne Stroustrup invented C++ while working at AT&T's Information and Systems
Software Research Lab. He is now a Computer Science professor at Texas A & M.

Guido van Rossum created Python at the National Research Institute for
Mathematics and Computer Science in the Netherlands.

People who work at institutions with "research" in the title are generally
theorists rather than practitioners. Therefore, all three of the languages you
mentioned here were created by language theorists. 

>> It is nearly impossible to write a reasonable parser using tables. 
>
>And exactly how many in CLC want to write a 'reasonable parser' in Cobol ?

At least one, probably more. 

>> Parsers are not limited
>> to compilers, they are found in business applications. 
>
>I do simple 'parsing' of template output files, of report scripts.  It 
>ain't big, but it ain't hard.

I parse 500 page reports. Could your parser handle that size? I thought not. 

If you are using tables, you have an arbitrary limit on maximum word size. Like
COBOL's 30 bytes. Modern parsers have no such limit. 

>> The topic is context 'freedom', not age. 
>
>Your claim was 'modern'.  Are you now 'defining' modern as 'context free' ? 

Yes. It's not my definition; it is that of language theorists. They talk about
LR(k) grammars, where k is the number of words a parser must look backward or
forward to disambiguate a word. LR(0) means context-free, which they think is
ideal. 

>Previously you attempted to define it as '3 - 5 years'.

That was another thread, where the topic was hardware cost/performance. It had
nothing to do with 'modern' in this context. 

>>>> were
>>>> designed with an eye toward Formal Definitions which could be processed
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>years later.

Which is why I said "with an eye toward" rather than 'by the use of'. A few
languages used formal definition in the design process. You mentioned Algol. But
in most cases, the formal definition is written by someone other than the
designer _after_ the language has been released. 

>Languages which had been designed with 'Formal Definitions' were not 'easy 
>to process with software'.

We appear to be reading two different pages. My 'formal definition' is a machine
readable file. It is not the human readable ground rules, description, specs or
standard. It is usually written in BNF notation or similar. It contains Rules in
the form antecedent:consequent, where antecedent often contains regular
expressions or complex conditionals. 

If such a definition is available, yes they are easy to process with software.
To my knowledge, no one has written a _complete_ one for COBOL. There are
subsets (e.g. TinyCOBOL) and there have been unfinished attempts. COBOL 2002
would be even more daunting. 

>> One reason 'they' hate COBOL is because it is so hard to define with
>> rules. Rather than admitting the inadequacy of their tools, they condemn
>> the language for being 'irregular'. 
>
>Cobol is irregular, that is not 'condemning' it, it is describing it.

'They' dislike irregularity because their tools cannot handle it well. I
understand the issue because I have similar difficulties in my analyzer. I
resolve it by re-iteration. Try it the most common way. If that doesn't work,
try including or excluding this form. If that doesn't work, try something else.

'They' don't like iteration, except a little backtracking. I suppose they think
it makes the analyzer appear unsure of itself. They want analysis on the first
pass. 

It's a cultural issue. When people mentally process natural language, they're
using re-iteration constantly, without being consciously aware of it. They must,
because life is 'irregular'. 

Robert

 Man would be fain be great and sees that he is little; would fain be
 happy and sees that he is miserable; would fain be perfect and sees
 that he is full of imperfections; would fain be the object of the
 love and esteem of men, and sees that his faults merit only their
 aversion and contempt. The embarrassment wherein he finds himself
 produces in him the most unjust passions imaginable, for
 he conceives a mortal hatred against TRUTH, which blames him and
 convinces him of his faults.
                            - Pascal, "Pensees"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-24T00:45:40+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e7e48a0.44120719@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3e7c9d08.266956898@news.optonline.net> <b5iht0$v2v$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e7d32e2.305323960@news.optonline.net> <-8idnWdcdpsSkOOjXTWckA@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Cold War, MAD, B-52, etc.).

I'm not so sure about the B-52. It is flying on airframes built in the 50's and
is expected to live until about 2030. Sure, avionics, engines and everything
else has changed, but the airframe hasn't.

Co-pilot to pilot: "What's it mean if I get an ASRA?"
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-24T23:39:37+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5lgmn$89c$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <b5iht0$v2v$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e7d32e2.305323960@news.optonline.net> <3e7e3b5a_1@text-west.newsgroups.com>`

```
Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:


>> I like COBOL because it's the most human-oriented programming language.
>>
> 
> Sadly, that is the very reason why most modern programmers DON'T like
> it...

What do you mean by 'modern' this time ?  3 - 5 years old, or 
'context-free' ?

But I think that your assertions is wrong.

COBOL is a business orientated language, it is mostly used for business 
applications: producing invoices, doing sales analysis, payrolls...

These applications are seen by many young people as boring, clerical, 
office type things to do, in any case they know almost nothing about these, 
and don't want to.

C, C++, PERL, C#, Java, are seen as being used for 'dynamic', exciting, 
graphical and web based things, for cryptology, for gaming.

The more that one knows about something, the more interesting it is. 
Conversely the less known the less interesting.

New programmers, generally, don't 'hate' Cobol, they know nothing about it 
or what it is used for and simply find other things much more interesting.

What is required is to teach the _applications_ first, such as 'business 
practice', then they may see the point of Cobol.
```

```
It is a demonstrable fact that COBOL is NOT popular amongst the programming
fraternity at large. I believe the reason for this is that most of them are
computer science graduates who prefer a programming language to be less
verbose than COBOL. That is the way they have been trained. Those of us who
love the language may bewail it, but it doesn't change the facts.

> But I think that your assertions is wrong.

Huh?! You are going to defend the position that  most modern programmers
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-25T02:24:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e7f91da.38026129@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <b5iht0$v2v$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e7d32e2.305323960@news.optonline.net> <3e7e3b5a_1@text-west.newsgroups.com> <b5lgmn$89c$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e7e586e_1@text-west.newsgroups.com> <217e491a.0303241051.23e001ca@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>being 'modern', while hating Cobol is evidence of being modern.

Everything modern is not ipso facto better. Modern hardware _is_ better, because
it delivers lower cost/performance (buck / bang). Modern programming software is
not necessarily better, because design is based on abstractions which are not
revealed  to the community of programmers. Sometimes it is better, as in the
case of OO. Often it is not, as in the plethora of languages which have
blossomed and failed within the last 20 years. 

My complaint is that language designers should favor human understanding over
software understanding. They don't concur and probably never will, because they
can't tweak their tools to match human kindergarten language skills. AI promised
to do this 30 years-ago; results have been very disappointing. 

As I said above, I love COBOL because it's the most human-oriented language. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-26T03:19:30+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5ohvb$hur$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3e7e586e_1@text-west.newsgroups.com> <217e491a.0303241051.23e001ca@posting.google.com> <3e7f91da.38026129@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> Everything modern is not ipso facto better. Modern hardware _is_ better,
> because it delivers lower cost/performance (buck / bang). 

I suspect here that, once again, you are confusing 'modern' and 'recent'. 
The IBM PC was not 'modern' when it was introduced, and is not modern now.

Intel/PCs are not cheaper because they are 'modern', but because of large 
scale production techniques.

> My complaint is that language designers should favor human understanding
> over software understanding. They don't concur and probably never will,
> because they can't tweak their tools to match human kindergarten language
> skills. AI promised to do this 30 years-ago; results have been very
> disappointing.

So you want to move towards the failures of 30 years ago.

As for agreeing with you.  No, they probably never will, but then that is 
probably because they don't want to bring in all the ambiguities that will 
occur, but you think will never happen.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-26T02:01:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e80ecf4.11054570@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3e7e586e_1@text-west.newsgroups.com> <217e491a.0303241051.23e001ca@posting.google.com> <3e7f91da.38026129@news.optonline.net> <b5ohvb$hur$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>I suspect here that, once again, you are confusing 'modern' and 'recent'. 

Agreed. I like your choice of word. 'Modern' is subjective and judgmental. 

>The IBM PC was not 'modern' when it was introduced, and is not modern now.

The Intel 8088 wasn't _terribly_ out of date when IBM introduced the PC. What
were the alternatives? The Motorola line was about the same. The TI-990 line was
better but unusable due to marketing policies (the TI 99/4A saw itself as a
competitor). IBM needed a product that could ship in less than a year. They made
pragmatic choices.

>Intel/PCs are not cheaper because they are 'modern', but because of large 
>scale production techniques.

True, but users don't care why, they just want to know 'which machine gives me
the most bang for the buck?' The answer is machines using RECENT Intel
processors. 

Did you know HP will soon abandon RISC in favor of Intel? HP made more than 50%
of RISC processors ever shipped. The HP-3000 line is now dead. 

I once sat through a half-hour monologue by a CS type who wanted to convince me
that RISC was inherently superior to CICS. He kept saying "it takes one-fifth
the silicon real-estate."  I knew he was wrong then, and the real world has
since confirmed my take on it. 
>
>> My complaint is that language designers should favor human understanding
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>So you want to move towards the failures of 30 years ago.

I didn't advocate AI .. then or now. 

>As for agreeing with you.  No, they probably never will, but then that is 
>probably because they don't want to bring in all the ambiguities that will 
>occur, but you think will never happen.

See related thread with Joe Zitzelberger,
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-03-26T09:14:34-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5scka$j11$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
In article <3e80ecf4.11054570@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>Agreed. I like your choice of word. 'Modern' is subjective and judgmental. 

Mr Wagner, this is wrong in that it clearly contradicts definitions
explicitly stated in the following commonly-accepted sources:

OED:  2. a. Of or pertaining to the present and recent times, as
distinguished from the remote past;

AHD: 1. a. Of or relating to recent times or the present: 

WRU: 1. Of or pertaining to the present time, or time not long past; late;
 not ancient or remote in past time; of recent period; 

MW:  2 : involving recent techniques, methods, or ideas 

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-03-26T16:15:44-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0303261615.30184e45@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3e80ecf4.11054570@news.optonline.net> <b5scka$j11$1@panix5.panix.com> <3e81e0b8.73467894@news.optonline.net> <b5spqk$n4s$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote 

> That has nothing to do with the fact, Mr Wagner, that each of these 
> citings defines 'modern' as 'recent' 

The citings are mostly '_of_ or _pertaining_to_ recent times'. 
'Modern' and 'recent' are not synonyms. For example a newly built desk
may not be 'modern', while one from the 1950s may be described as
such. They are building new Messerschmitt Me-262 jet fighters, these
are not 'modern' because they are not _of_ recent times.

> and does not, in any way, support 
> your assertion that ''Modern' is subjective and judgmental'.

The word 'modern' is often used in a subjective and judgemental way,
as it was by RW for example.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-03-26T20:58:17-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5tlrp$ps3$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3e81e0b8.73467894@news.optonline.net> <b5spqk$n4s$1@panix5.panix.com> <217e491a.0303261615.30184e45@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <217e491a.0303261615.30184e45@posting.google.com>,
Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote 
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>The citings are mostly '_of_ or _pertaining_to_ recent times'. 

Mr Plinston, that is the way adjectives work, at times.

>'Modern' and 'recent' are not synonyms.

This is wrong, at least according to 

http://thesaurus.reference.com/search?q=modern&db=roget and

http://www.m-w.com/cgi-bin/thesaurus?book=Thesaurus&va=modern

>For example a newly built desk
>may not be 'modern', while one from the 1950s may be described as
>such. They are building new Messerschmitt Me-262 jet fighters, these
>are not 'modern' because they are not _of_ recent times.

These are examples of how 'modern' and 'recent' are not always used 
synonymously; it does not change the fact that the Roget's Interactive and 
Merriam-Webster's thesaureses list them as such.

>
>> and does not, in any way, support 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>as it was by RW for example.

That a word can be used in a way does not mean it is defined in that 
way... in fact, it might be argued that doing so could be to the detriment 
of what is seen as more appropriate useage; after all, a torque-wrench can 
be used as a hammer... and this usually does not improve it's functioning 
as a torque-wrench.

As for Mr Wagner's uses, in particular... well, I am reminded of a passage 
penned over a century back by The Reverend Charles Lutwidge Dodgson:

from http://www.bartleby.com/66/25/10625.html -

"When I use a word," Humpty Dumpty said, in a rather scornful tone, "it 
means just what I choose it to mean-neither more nor less."

DD
```

```
yours <G>. I committed this quote to memory some 30 years ago...but no

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-26T14:41:33+00:00

```
On 25-Mar-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> True, but users don't care why, they just want to know 'which machine gives me
> the most bang for the buck?' The answer is machines using RECENT Intel
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> since confirmed my take on it.

The real world is not confirming which is technically superior.  There are lots
of reasons we make our choices - you mentioned a significant one above.    The
dominance of Microsoft/Intel  have made this choice cheaper, much the same way
as VHS beat Beta.

I very much dislike the way our phones are very low-fidelity.   But I don't see
any way that this will change in my lifetime, as manufacturers work hard at
lowering fidelity as low as they can to get more bandwidth.   If someone came
out with a superior hi-fi phone, would it sell?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-27T03:23:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e826056.106142517@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3e7e586e_1@text-west.newsgroups.com> <217e491a.0303241051.23e001ca@posting.google.com> <3e7f91da.38026129@news.optonline.net> <b5ohvb$hur$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e80ecf4.11054570@news.optonline.net> <b5se6s$e5b$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>I very much dislike the way our phones are very low-fidelity.   But I don't see
>any way that this will change in my lifetime, as manufacturers work hard at
>lowering fidelity as low as they can to get more bandwidth.   If someone came
>out with a superior hi-fi phone, would it sell?

In the music industry, higher fidelity always wins. In telephony, you're dealing
with an infrastructure built around 56K channels for digitized speech. No single
company can change that because they must be compatible with each other. 

Wireless offers the prospect of changing it. Next-generation wireless will offer
6-8Mb per channel, or up to 16M if you believe CDMA optimists. Preliminary
versions are already running in selected markets at 2M. 

Meanwhile, next-generation DSL is already running at 20M in selected small-town
locales thanks to Federal Tax subsidies. 

Hi Fi telophony is on the horizon. We'll have it within 1-3 years.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-26T13:11:47-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v83qki6rrojc32@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3e7e586e_1@text-west.newsgroups.com> <217e491a.0303241051.23e001ca@posting.google.com> <3e7f91da.38026129@news.optonline.net> <b5ohvb$hur$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e80ecf4.11054570@news.optonline.net>`

```

Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e80ecf4.11054570@news.optonline.net...
[snip]
> The Intel 8088 wasn't _terribly_ out of date when IBM introduced the PC.
What
> were the alternatives? The Motorola line was about the same. The TI-990
line was
> better but unusable due to marketing policies (the TI 99/4A saw itself as
a
> competitor). IBM needed a product that could ship in less than a year.
They made
> pragmatic choices.

Something I encountered while searching for something else.

---- Begin quote from < http://www.cs.mu.oz.au/313/stories > ------

As the architect and design team leader for the IBM PC project, I am
often asked why the 8088 microprocessor was selected for the original
PC design.  At the time of the PC's development, IBM viewed the major
competition as the Apple II and a growing family of Z80 CPM-based
systems, with Radio Shack the low-cost price leader.  The goal was a
better performing and expandable machine starting at the Apple II
price.  This goal immediately ruled out a number of competing
microprocessors, including all IBM proprietary designs, which at this
time were very expensive, and multichips, which had nonstandard power
requirements, required significant TTL glue chips to form a system
design, had few companion peripheral chips, and were difficult to
package using low-cost circuit board technology.

Some of the competitors were the IBM Series 1 processor, the System 3
mainstore processor, and the communication microprocessor in the IBM
37xx product line.  A major problem with these microprocessors was the
lack of IBM or third-party software development tools that ran on the
target microprocessors.  You needed an IBM mainframe to do software
development, which was somewhat beyond the target developer's means.
The 6502 family of microprocessors was ruled out due to its use in the
Apple product line; the last thing IBM wanted was to be viewed as an
Apple-compatible manufacturer.

The Z80 was a strong competitor with its high performance, large user
base, and third-party development tools, as well as its good language,
operating system (CPM), and application support.  But the Z80 had four
basic problems.  First, if IBM used the Z80, it would be viewed as a
follower endorsing again the architecture and operating system of a
number of small competitors.  Second, the Z80 architecture supported a
limited address space of 64K, without a paging scheme implemented
external to the microprocessors.  Third, there was no visible
migration path to higher performance, larger address space systems
using the Z80.  Fourth, the Z8000 microprocessor (next generation Z80)
had a totally different architecture.  IBM Japan proposed a
dual-processor PC using the Z80 to support existing applications and a
Z8000 for new applications, which was an expensive hardware solution
requiring the support of two software environments.  The Intel 8048
single-chip microprocessor used in a multiprocessor architecture was
also proposed by an IBM group in the Northeast.  This was never given
serious consideration due to the software limitations of the 8048.

We have now ruled out IBM microprocessors and the classic 8-bit
microprocessors of the time.  This left only one other competitor, the
Motorola 68000.  The 68000 supported a large linear address space and
a register-based instruction set that was very attractive to software
developers.  An excellent architecture chip, it has proven to be a
worthy competitor to the Intel-based architecture microprocessors.
But at the time, the 68000 was viewed as having many of the same
drawbacks as the IBM proprietary microprocessors.

There were four major concerns with the 68000.  First, because it was
a true 16-bit data and 24-bit address device, it would require a more
expensive system board design, including more bus buffer chips and TTL
glue chips, larger expansion slot connectors, and a more complex
circuit board design (more layers).  Second, the 16-bit bus required
twice the memory chips (ROM and RAM) for a minimum system
implementation; the minimum memory size and memory increments would
always be twice the size and cost of an 8-bit bus.  Third, benchmark
analysis of the 68000 indicated a performance edge over the 8086, but
it was not as memory efficient as the 8086 architecture, making a
small-system implementation less competitive.  Fourth, the 68000
lacked companion peripheral and support chips comparable to what was
provided by Intel and third-party vendors for the Intel bus compatible
microprocessors.

The most damaging concern with the 68000 was its lack of software
support, including development tools, languages, operating system, and
applications.  This was also somewhat true of the 8088/8086
processors.  Intel, however, provided a migration strategy from the
8080 architecture and software to the 8086 architecture.  The 8086
preserved the 8080 register architecture and condition codes and
provided a software utility that converted 8080-based code to 8086
code.  This provided the opportunity to convert the existing the 8080
software base to the 8086, and in actuality, most initial PC software
was converted 8080 software.

A number of other circumstances affected the decision to use the 8088.
The PC design is often viewed as IBM's first encounter with Intel, but
IBM had an ongoing relationship with Intel.  IBM was using Intel
processors in a number of products prior to the PC design.  The 8080
was used as a communications controller in the IBM 5100 portable
computer, the 8086 was used in the IBM Display writer, the 8085 was
used in the 5250 terminals for the System 3X mainframes and the IBM
Data Master, and the 8048 was used in IBM keyboards.  Thus, IBM had
extensive experience with Intel and Intel technology.  To be used in
IBM products, Intel parts had to pass IBM's quality standards and
tests.  Another strong motivation was Intel's capability to offer a
kit price for the majority of the PC chip set.

In summary, the 8088 was selected because it allowed the lowest cost
implementation of an architecture that provided a migration path to a
larger address space and higher performance implementations.  Because
it was a unique choice relative to competitive system implementations,
IBM could be viewed as a leader rather than a follower.  It had a
feasible software migration path that allowed access to the large base
of existing 8080 software.  The 8088 was a comfortable solution for
IBM.  Was it the best processor architecture available at the time?
Probably not, but history seems to have been kind to this decision.

---- End quote -----
```
