###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-24T02:00:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e7e582c.48101022@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <b5iht0$v2v$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e7d32e2.305323960@news.optonline.net> <3e7e3b5a_1@text-west.newsgroups.com> <b5lgmn$89c$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>applications: producing invoices, doing sales analysis, payrolls...

I don't accept that. I regard and use COBOL as a serious, general-purpose
programming language in par with C, C++, C#, Java and Python. 

By calling it business language, you're marginalizing it 

>These applications are seen by many young people as boring, clerical, 
>office type things to do, in any case they know almost nothing about these, 
>and don't want to.

Lack of knowledge does not prevent hatred. If anything, it encourages hatred,
supported by flimsily-concocted and uncritically examined rationales. Hasty
reading of any tabloid newspaper will confirm this. 

>C, C++, PERL, C#, Java, are seen as being used for 'dynamic', exciting, 
>graphical and web based things, for cryptology, for gaming.

That's true. Until recently, with good reason. The competition produced OO, then
Java, then dot-NET, while we sat on the sidelines writing PERFORM 100-INITIALIZE
THRU 100-EXIT. And the standards people wined and dined for seventeen years. 

If Micro Focus and Realia hadn't been pushing for progress, there would have
been none for seventeen years. I agree with Peter here. 

>New programmers, generally, don't 'hate' Cobol, they know nothing about it 
>or what it is used for and simply find other things much more interesting.

It's true they know nothing about it, but they were taught to hate it by their
college professors. Try talking to them. Their misinformation is unbelievable. 

>What is required is to teach the _applications_ first, such as 'business 
>practice', then they may see the point of Cobol.

Business applications can be implemented in any language which supports CURRENCY
or DECIMAL class. They can even be implemented with pure integers. The more
important issue is program maintenance over the long haul, and that's not
limited to business. COBOL is more readable and maintainable than other
languages. That's why it lives on and why they hate us so much. Because we make
the 'genius' look bad to his grandmother. Bottom line, that's what it's about. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-03-24T11:45:19-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0303241145.4012fe70@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <b5iht0$v2v$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e7d32e2.305323960@news.optonline.net> <3e7e3b5a_1@text-west.newsgroups.com> <b5lgmn$89c$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e7e582c.48101022@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> I don't accept that. I regard and use COBOL as a serious, general-purpose
> programming language in par with C, C++, C#, Java and Python. 
> 
> By calling it business language, you're marginalizing it 

Languages have particular strengths and certain weaknesses, there is
no such thing as a do-it-all language.  In particular, access to
libraries, objects, and other mechanisms is required.

While I do most programming in Cobol, including CGI servers and code
generators, I also use other languages where these are more
appropriate.

Where I need an ftp client, or an EMail processor, I can do this in 20
lines of Python and execute it from my Cobol system.

OTOH I wouldn't write an order processing system in any that you
mention above, others might.
 
> while we sat on the sidelines writing PERFORM 100-INITIALIZE
> THRU 100-EXIT. 

Did you ?  I haven't written like that since the 60s.

> COBOL is more readable and maintainable than other
> languages. 

What is 'readable', 'understandable' and 'maintainable' is _entirely_
dependant on what you are used to.  Cobol is, but only to Cobol
programmers.  To Java programmers Cobol is a mess with parts scattered
all over, it is not 'readable' to them.

This also affects your arguments over full stops.  To those who are
used to having full stops they make the code _more_ readable and
_more_ maintainable.  The same with numbering the paragraphs, or using
sections.  It is what they are used to and when presented with
something different their recognition of it is slower, but only until
they can train their brain to automatically pick up the recognition
points.

This is not to say that code should still should look like the 60s,
but when a site has a code legacy that goes back decades then there is
a good point that having uniformity of appearance is beneficial.

The 'de jour' programmers solve this problem by using a new language
every few years: Algol, Pascal, C, Modula2, Ada, C++, Java, C#, ...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-24T14:41:39-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5nqi3$kdd$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <b5iht0$v2v$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e7d32e2.305323960@news.optonline.net> <3e7e3b5a_1@text-west.newsgroups.com> <b5lgmn$89c$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e7e582c.48101022@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303241145.4012fe70@posting.google.com>`

```
IMHO, anyone who doesn't understand that *historically* COBOL *was* a
"business-oriented language" probably denies the fact of the holocaust and
maybe even that the world is "round" (or at least not flat).  (Needless to
say, they don't understand the origin of the acronym "COBOL" and probably
insist on calling the language "Cobol" to pretend that its origin has little
impact on its current uses.)

Furthermore, even with ALL the enhancements in the 2002 Standard (including
those which I used to call "C-ification" which allow COBOL to be used in
many/most of the places that "traditional" C was), it is STILL "business
oriented".  That is what its primary target is - and that is ONE of the
things that it is best at.  Having said that, especially with vendor and
operating system specific "extensions", it is entirely possible to do many
things with COBOL that are not "limited" to business applications.  In some
cases there are even good reasons for doing so (such as the few compiler
vendors that have written their compilers in COBOL in order to "self-test"
both the power and correctness of their own compilers).

There are, however, some things that are still almost (if not completely)
impossible to do in COBOL, for example "weather forecasting/emulation",
real-time game applications, any application requiring vector processing.

Again, in my opinion, there are a number of reasons that COBOL is "not
popular" with many programmers - who have begun their programming careers in
the last decade-plus.  I don't think that any SINGLE reason explains all of
it, but certainly its reputation among both "hackers" and "academics" (many
of whom don't even know what was introduced in the '85 Standard much less
what extensions are available) has to count for some of it.
```

```
>What is 'readable', 'understandable' and 'maintainable' is _entirely_

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

>but when a site has a code legacy that goes back decades then there is
>a good point that having uniformity of appearance is beneficial.

I'll buy that .. to a point. When legacy becomes an obstacle to progress, it's
time for a rewrite.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-26T03:28:15+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5oifp$i2u$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3e7e582c.48101022@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303241145.4012fe70@posting.google.com> <3e7fb831.47843001@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> They weren't born with these peferences. They voluntarily assimilated
> them. Why can't they drop them as readily as they adopted them?

Given that styles have changed over the last 40 years then there is no 
reason at all to assume that they won't continue to change over the next 
few decades.

While you consider that your 'best' is somehow an absolute, most would see 
it as just another way that will become archaic, and doesn't offer any 
particular advantages over other styles.  While _to_you_ it seems clarity 
and understanding itself, that is mainly because you wrote it and recognise 
it instantly.

Most do the same with their styles, they just don't claim that theirs are 
'the best' as you do, they know that it all personal preference and what 
one is used to.
```

```
In article <3e7c9d08.266956898@news.optonline.net>,
 robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> You mean ambiguity resolved by context. I have no problem with that in
> principle. In natural languages, we handle it routinely and with little 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> notation rather than a language. 

That is because we do not want our programs to do "sorta" what we wrote 
them to do.  Linguistics involve many shades of grey depending on the 
speaker, the listener, the relative humidity and the phase of the moon.

Algolrythmics involve doing exactly what is directed.  Nothing more, 
nothing less, nothing left to interpretation.

FTR, Cobol also has different assignment and equality operators, why is 
this bad in C but not in Cobol?
```

```
Hmmm.

MOVE ZERO TO A.
SET A TO +1.
ADD X, Y GIVING A.
DIVIDE X BY Y GIVING Z REMAINDER A.
IF A IS EQUAL TO ZERO

I believe in C/C++ one can write either "if (a=0)" or "if (a==0)". 
Both statements will compile correctly, but they will give different
results.  I could easily misread the intent of this kind of code.
```

```
> I believe in C/C++ one can write either "if (a=0)" or "if (a==0)".
> Both statements will compile correctly, but they will give different
> results.  I could easily misread the intent of this kind of code.

Experienced programmers use 'if ( 0 == x )', 
if this is miswritten as 'if ( 0 = x )' it gives a compilation error 
because a literal cannot be an lvalue.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-26T02:01:35+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e80f138.12146395@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3e7c9d08.266956898@news.optonline.net> <joe_zitzelberger-358101.09400124032003@corp.supernews.com> <3e7f382b.15064404@news.optonline.net> <joe_zitzelberger-9FD00C.08361725032003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

>In article <3e7f382b.15064404@news.optonline.net>,
> robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>if (a equal 0 ) or (b = 0)

This provides such an easily understood example, hopefully it will illuminate
the points I've been trying to make without much success. 

In COBOL, the equal sign is not context-free. In order to disambiguate it, the
compiler/parser must look one lousy word to the left. If it says "compute", the
equal sign is an assignment operator otherwise it is a relational operator.  The
rule is unambiguous. We do not capriciously assign meaning to the equal sign
depending on mood, phase of the moon, etc. 

C introduced "==" to make the language more reliable. It had the opposite
effect; it made the process LESS reliable. Why? Because humans are involved. You
cannot look solely at the language and say 'the language was clean; if it got
screwed up, blame it on Joe Programmer.' Blame him for what? For being human? I
blame the language designer for for laying traps wide open to Joe's
infalibilities. 

A better example is 'if (*a-string == "FOO"). Here the programmer obviously
intentended to compare the contents of a-string against the literal. The
compiler generated code to compare two pointers and returned unequal, always. 
The stupid programmer should have written 'if !(strcmp (a-string, "FOO")), which
is clear as mud. 

My point is: by concentrating on math-like precision and ignoring the human
factor, you and your CS friends are making the overall programming PROCESS
unreliable.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-27T03:14:04+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5r5u1$lie$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3e7f382b.15064404@news.optonline.net> <joe_zitzelberger-9FD00C.08361725032003@corp.supernews.com> <3e80f138.12146395@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> This provides such an easily understood example, hopefully it will
> illuminate the points I've been trying to make without much success.

It is not that we don't understand what you are saying, it is that we 
disagree with it.
 
> In COBOL, the equal sign is not context-free. In order to disambiguate it,
> the compiler/parser must look one lousy word to the left. If it says
> "compute", the
> equal sign is an assignment operator otherwise it is a relational
> operator.  

That is completely the wrong way around.  Cobol is a statement based 
language.  This is why the word COMPUTE must exist at the start of an 
assignment.  Once the statement type is known the meaning of the rest of 
the statement follows.

> The rule is unambiguous. We do not capriciously assign meaning
> to the equal sign depending on mood, phase of the moon, etc.

No computer language does that. 

> C introduced "==" to make the language more reliable. 

No. Wrong. Many languages use different symbols to indicate equality and 
assignment, only one of these can be the '=' symbol unless assignment is a 
statement (as it is in Cobol). Different languages make different choices.  
It happens that BCPL chose '==' for equality, and this went to B then C.  
Fortran chose .eq..  Other langauges changed assignment to, say, :=.

It has nothing to do with 'making it reliable', it has to do with the 
programmer being able to create code which does what he requires.

Both '=' and '==' can be used in one expression, and more than once, 
because assignment is an _operator_ not a statement.  If you think that it 
is confusing between '=' and '==' then how do you deal with precedence and 
associativity ?

> I blame the language designer for for laying traps
> wide open to Joe's infalibilities.

You would, it can't be _your_ fault when the program doesn't work.

> A better example is 'if (*a-string == "FOO"). Here the programmer
> obviously intentended to compare the contents of a-string against the
> literal. The compiler generated code to compare two pointers and returned
> unequal, always. The stupid programmer should have written 'if !(strcmp
> (a-string, "FOO")), which is clear as mud.

If you don't understand the language then get a manual, if you can't learn 
to use it then get a different language.  Perhaps you _will_ be happier 
with BASIC  ;-)

It is useful to be able to say:

        while ( ( bufpt = fgets(buffer, bufmax, infile) ) != NULL )
            {
            /* process record */
            }

> My point is: by concentrating on math-like precision and ignoring the
> human factor, you and your CS friends are making the overall programming
> PROCESS unreliable.

My 'overall programming process' is not unreliable, why is yours ?

Everything is _defined_ as to its results, there is no 'depending on mood, 
phase of the moon, etc.', if it seems to be like that to you it is because 
you cannot accurately predict, not because it is not predictable.

The problem is not 'the human factor' it is 'the wagner factor'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-27T03:23:40+00:00

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>the statement follows.

Software parsers don't necessarily think that way. When dealing with the atomic
equal sign, they want to 'traverse' to disambiguate it. 

>> The rule is unambiguous. We do not capriciously assign meaning
>> to the equal sign depending on mood, phase of the moon, etc.
>
>No computer language does that. 

I was responding to Joe Z's assertion they did. He said natural language syntax
opens the door to ambiguity. 

>Both '=' and '==' can be used in one expression, and more than once, 
>because assignment is an _operator_ not a statement.  If you think that it 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>You would, it can't be _your_ fault when the program doesn't work.

I have little trouble making my programs work, but I see others struggling. 

The difference between politics and policy is that politics assigns blame to
individual people whereas policy assigns blame to systems and institutions. You
are using a political solution by assigning blame to me, when the blame
correctly should be assigned to language design. 
>
>> A better example is 'if (*a-string == "FOO"). Here the programmer
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>with BASIC  ;-)

Suppose we applied the same design 'standards' to systems we delivered to
clients. Suppose we delivered a counter-intuitive system like WordPerfect, where
Shft-F3 means Help and F7 means Exit. They wouldn't buy it. They'd dump us and
go with a more mainstream product. We would protest 'It's a training issue'
without success. 

It seems ironic and sad that programmers must endure poor design that users
would reject out of hand. 

>It is useful to be able to say:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>My 'overall programming process' is not unreliable, why is yours ?

Mine isn't, but junior-level programmers have problems with the process. 

>The problem is not 'the human factor' it is 'the wagner factor'.

Again, you turn the debate into ad hominem. How ignoble. Please try to rebut the
points I make.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-28T02:34:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e83849f.180979545@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3e7f382b.15064404@news.optonline.net> <joe_zitzelberger-9FD00C.08361725032003@corp.supernews.com> <3e80f138.12146395@news.optonline.net> <b5r5u1$lie$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e82533e.102790445@news.optonline.net> <yJCga.5432$062.802333@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
"Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>level.  Polymorphism is polymorphism, whatever level it is applied to.

Very good point. If the parser was OO, overloading the assignment method for =
in a conditional would be easy. 

Sadly, yacc and other parser generators pre-date OO, which explains why they so
much difficulty with context-sensitivity. 

On a more general level, OO environments set Rules dynamically at execution
time, whereas the Formal Definition model casts them into stone before execution
begins. Is there a way to test an OO environment for completeness and lack of
self-contradiction, comparable to the tests we apply to Rules (and earlier
Decision Tables)? I'm sure theorists have wrestled with the issue. It doesn't
seem amenable to practical solution because the solution would require
simulating millions of execution paths.

If OO casts us back into the 'abyss', that's a good thing, because it
demonstrates inadequacies in the Formal Definition model. The idea that a model
can eliminate chaos by simply eliminating ambiguity as the lexical level is yet
another simple-minded, 'techie' solution to a more complex problem. If we're to
solve the ambiguity problem, we should understand it and employ appropriate
tools rather than 'quick fix' solutions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-03-27T12:03:47-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0303271203.5c3ab6bf@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3e7f382b.15064404@news.optonline.net> <joe_zitzelberger-9FD00C.08361725032003@corp.supernews.com> <3e80f138.12146395@news.optonline.net> <b5r5u1$lie$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e82533e.102790445@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> >> The rule is unambiguous. We do not capriciously assign meaning
> >> to the equal sign depending on mood, phase of the moon, etc.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> opens the door to ambiguity. 

You don't seem to have a consistent argument at all.

The 'rule is unambiguous' _because_ it is not 'natural language'.

Making a computerr language more of a 'natural language' will
introduce ambiguities.
 
> >> I blame the language designer for for laying traps
> >> wide open to Joe's infalibilities.

If Joe is infalible then the traps won't work.

> >You would, it can't be _your_ fault when the program doesn't work.
 
> are using a political solution by assigning blame to me, when the blame
> correctly should be assigned to language design. 

There may be a case for blaming the language design _if_ it wasn't
clearly defined, or was ambiguous, or was based on the phase of the
moon.

However, if the actual problem is that the user doesn't read the
manual, or fails to understand what is clearly written, or
mis-remembers what he 'learnt' 30 years ago, then it is the user who
should take the blame for failing programs.

> Suppose we applied the same design 'standards' to systems we delivered to
> clients. Suppose we delivered a counter-intuitive system like WordPerfect, where
> Shft-F3 means Help and F7 means Exit. They wouldn't buy it. They'd dump us and
> go with a more mainstream product. We would protest 'It's a training issue'
> without success. 

They would thank you for a system that was so easy to learn - if they
were WordPerfect users.

As it happened WordStar users had to relearn when they went to
WordPerfect and then relearn again when they went to Windows/Word. 
They relearnt with each new Windows system (though only small amounts)
and will relearn when Windows is replaced by the next thing.

> Mine isn't, but junior-level programmers have problems with the process. 

Junior level programmers will _always_ have problems, that is why they
are junior level.

All learning is 'trial and error'.  The errors are a necessary part of
the learning, attempts to remove errors also removes the opportunity
to learn.

Unfortunately there are those that make errors without learning.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-29T01:43:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e84d4da.267067567@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3e7f382b.15064404@news.optonline.net> <joe_zitzelberger-9FD00C.08361725032003@corp.supernews.com> <3e80f138.12146395@news.optonline.net> <b5r5u1$lie$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e82533e.102790445@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303271203.5c3ab6bf@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>> I was responding to Joe Z's assertion they did. He said natural language
syntax
>> opens the door to ambiguity. 
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>introduce ambiguities.

There, you just said the same thing Joe Z said. 

My argument is that designing a language superficially like English, with
unambiguous rules, makes the programming process more reliable. I don't see how
you can assert otherwise in a COBOL forum. That's the prime design tenet of
COBOL. 

>There may be a case for blaming the language design _if_ it wasn't
>clearly defined, or was ambiguous, or was based on the phase of the
>moon.

Or if it is counter-intuitive. For example, failing to at least issue a warning
on 'if (*astring == "foo")'. 

>However, if the actual problem is that the user doesn't read the
>manual, or fails to understand what is clearly written, or
>mis-remembers what he 'learnt' 30 years ago, then it is the user who
>should take the blame for failing programs.

Read the manual!? Anybody can do it with a manual in hand. Where's the challenge
in that? 

>> Suppose we applied the same design 'standards' to systems we delivered to
>> clients. Suppose we delivered a counter-intuitive system like WordPerfect,
where
>> Shft-F3 means Help and F7 means Exit. They wouldn't buy it. They'd dump us
and
>> go with a more mainstream product. We would protest 'It's a training issue'
>> without success. 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>and will relearn when Windows is replaced by the next thing.

Most WordStar/WordPerfect/Word users are secretaries who don't know other
software. They don't know common usage because they lack a reference base. Most
don't realize they can change key bindings to match their old tool, so they
memorize the new defaults thinking it's their only option. 

>> Mine isn't, but junior-level programmers have problems with the process. 
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>to learn.

I thought education was billed as a fast path to success. By replacing trial and
error with tutelage, students needn't repeat the errors we made. If that's not
the case, why bother going to school?

The cost of education is tuition _plus_ lost income. In the US, that can easily
come to $40-60K per year. Multiplied by four years, about $200K. 

I didn't make that mistake. If I had, I wouldn't have squandered $200K on
programming. I would have spent it on physics or The Classics. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: "Syntax" or not - in COBOL Lanuage specification

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-30T04:55:54+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6391s$4vc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<b5gken$d23$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3e82533e.102790445@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0303271203.5c3ab6bf@posting.google.com> <3e84d4da.267067567@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> There, you just said the same thing Joe Z said.
 
> My argument is that designing a language superficially like English, with
> unambiguous rules, makes the programming process more reliable. 

Yes, I know that is your argument, we understand it, we just disagree with 
it.

> I don't see how you can assert otherwise in a COBOL forum. 

No, you don't see that do you.

> That's the prime design tenet of COBOL.

It is not the _prime_ tenet of Cobol, The _prime_ tenet is to be useful for 
Business programmming and to be Common.  Being English-like is subservient 
to that.

There is, however, a difference between being superficially like English, 
and being 'more like a natural language'.  You want to introduce 
linguistics into computer languages, what a complete mess they would make 
of it.

> Or if it is counter-intuitive. For example, failing to at least issue a
> warning on 'if (*astring == "foo")'.

     testu.c:31: WARNING 121: ptr/int conversion:

Get a better compiler or learn how to use it.

And it is _not_ counter intuitive to anyone with two clues to rub together. 

>>All learning is 'trial and error'.  The errors are a necessary part of
>>the learning, attempts to remove errors also removes the opportunity
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> If that's not the case, why bother going to school?

You are working under the delusion that 'teaching' is an active verb. The 
best that can be hoped for is that teachers can provide an environment in 
which students can learn.

And no, you cannot replace 'trial and error' by tutelage. The best is that 
you get them to do appropriate trials and recognise that it _is_ an error 
when they make it.  Schools are there to ensure that the students _do_ 
repeat the errors we made, but in a place where the errors only result in 
learning.
 
> The cost of education is tuition _plus_ lost income. In the US, that can
> easily come to $40-60K per year. Multiplied by four years, about $200K.

Learning by trial and error is better than not trying or not recognising 
error or simply not learning from it.
 
> I didn't make that mistake. If I had, I wouldn't have squandered $200K on
> programming. I would have spent it on physics or The Classics.
```

<a id="t-0b7e601b59"></a>
## Thread: BINARY (and most COMP-n USAGEs) - not limited to integer PICTUREs — 6 msgs

### BINARY (and most COMP-n USAGEs) - not limited to integer PICTUREs

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-21T21:35:46-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5glml$972$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
It has come to my attention that at least one person is confused by the
numeric USAGEs in both Standard COBOL and as non-Standard extensions.

1) Easy case - both the USAGE COMP and USAGE BINARY are defined in the
ANSI/ISO '85 (and 2002) Standards.  There is *no* limitation on what the
picture clause may contain for such USAGEs - other than the fact that they
must define a numeric (not integer) data item.  See (for example), see ('85
Standard)  page VI-46,

     "(3) An elementary data item whose declaration contains, or an
elementary data item subordinate to a group item whose declaration contains,
a USAGE clause specifying BINARY, COMPUTATIONAL, or PACKED-DECIMAL must be
declared with a PICTURE character-string that describes a numeric item,
i.e., a PICTURE character-string that contains only the symbols 'P', 'S',
'V', and '9'. (See page VI-29, The PICTURE Clause.)"

2) There is nothing to limit any vendor from "limiting" an extension USAGE
(e.g. COMP-3, COMP-4, COMP-5, COMP-X) to only integer picture clauses.
However, when it comes to COMP-5 and COMP-X (when specified with "9's" in
the PICTURE clause), the most common extension definition that I am aware of
is the same as for COMP and BINARY usages, i.e. it must be numeric - not
just integer.  See for example

IBM - LRM

http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IGY3LR10/5.3.16.1

"The picture for a COMP-5 data item can specify a scaling factor (that is,
decimal positions or implied integer positions). In this case, the maximal
capacities listed in the table above must be scaled appropriately. For
example, a data item with description PICTURE S99V99 COMP-5 is represented
in storage as a binary half-word, and supports a range of values
from -327.68 to +327.67."

Micro Focus LRM
 http://supportline.microfocus.com/documentation/books/nx31sp1/lrpubb.htm

"8.25 The USAGE Clause
    ...

4. An elementary data item whose declaration contains, or an elementary data
item subordinate to a group item whose declaration contains, a USAGE clause
specifying COMPUTATIONAL-5, or COMPUTATIONAL-X must be declared with a
picture character-string that describes a numeric item or ..."

    ***

As I said, there MAY be some COMP-? extensions that do require an integer
picture clause, but it is certainly NOT true for the Standard "COMP" and
"BINARY" usages - nor for at least some common implementations of COMP-5 and
COMP-X.
```

#### ↳ Re: BINARY (and most COMP-n USAGEs) - not limited to integer PICTUREs

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-22T19:39:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e7ca471.268854028@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b5glml$972$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>It has come to my attention that at least one person is confused by the
>numeric USAGEs in both Standard COBOL and as non-Standard extensions.

<snip>
>http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/IGY3LR10/5.3.16.1
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>COMP-X.

Gee, I learn something new every decade. 

To see whether there is a speed difference, I added this to the timing test:

05  comp-5-number      comp-5         pic s9(09).      *> as before
05  comp-5-with-decimals comp-5       pic s9(10)v9(05).  *> new

Results were exactly the same .. and eight times faster than BINARY. I wonder
why Fujitsu punishes us for using standard notation.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: BINARY (and most COMP-n USAGEs) - not limited to integer PICTUREs

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-03-22T16:04:17-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0303221604.3dd97980@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b5glml$972$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3e7ca471.268854028@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> Results were exactly the same .. and eight times faster than BINARY. I wonder
> why Fujitsu punishes us for using standard notation.

Using the same source code (all S9(10)V999 with MULTIPLY and DIVIDE)
on the same slow machine:

Fujitsu 7 for Linux:
loop overhead           .08
display               12.83
packed                12.86
binary                 3.21
integer                3.26
comp-5                 2.80

MicroFocus on Linux:
loop overhead           .05
display                6.94
packed                 6.83
binary                 7.07
integer                7.18
comp-5                 6.67
Fujitsu is twice as bad with decimal and twice as good as MF for
BINARY.

Different systems have different characteristics. It is important to
know the characteristics of the _target_ syatem and not expect
'blanket cover' with one technique.
```

<a id="t-c8ae6135c9"></a>
## Thread: Hardware support for decimal (was something else) — 20 this month / 40 total

### Hardware support for decimal (was something else)

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-22T09:49:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e7c2b28.237800105@news.optonline.net>`

```
A timing test of Fujitsu COBOL running on a Pentium shows decimal arithmetic
slow on both DISPLAY and PACKED-DECIMAL formats, medium speed on BINARY with or
without digits right of decimal point, high speed on native COMP-5 integer. The
difference between DISPLAY and COMP-5 is 30:1. This supports my recommendation
to use comp-5 for 'bread and butter' variables. 

Robert 

identification division.
 program-id. test20.
*> author. Robert Wagner.
*>  Testing speed of arithmetic.
*>  Findings:
*>   loop overhead    .66
*>   display           16.54
*>   packed          11.42
*>   binary             2.58
*>   integer            1.48
*>   comp-5             .55
*>  Environment: Intel P100, Fujitsu COBOL V3.
 data division.
 working-storage section.
 01  unqualified-variables.
     05  display-number                    pic s9(10)v9(03).
     05  packed-number      packed-decimal pic s9(10)v9(03).
     05  binary-number      binary         pic s9(10)v9(03).
     05  integer-number     binary         pic s9(09).
     05  comp-5-number      comp-5         pic s9(09).

     05  test-name                         pic  x(20).
     05  start-time.
         10  start-hours                   pic  9(02).
         10  start-minutes                 pic  9(02).
         10  start-seconds                 pic  9(02).
         10  start-hundredths              pic  9(02).
     05  end-time.
         10  end-hours                     pic  9(02).
         10  end-minutes                   pic  9(02).
         10  end-seconds                   pic  9(02).
         10  end-hundredths                pic  9(02).
     05  repeat-factor value 10000000 binary pic s9(09).
     05  loop-overhead value zero binary   pic s9(09).
     05  elapsed-time             binary   pic s9(09).
     05  elapsed-time-edited               pic  z(04).99.

 procedure division.
 main.
     move 'loop overhead' to test-name
     perform start-timer
     perform repeat-factor times
         continue
     end-perform
     perform stop-timer
     move elapsed-time to loop-overhead

     move 'display' to test-name
     move zero to display-number
     perform start-timer
     perform repeat-factor times
         add 1 to display-number
     end-perform
     perform stop-timer

     move 'packed' to test-name
     move zero to packed-number
     perform start-timer
     perform repeat-factor times
         add 1 to packed-number
     end-perform
     perform stop-timer

     move 'binary' to test-name
     move zero to binary-number
     perform start-timer
     perform repeat-factor times
         add 1 to binary-number
     end-perform
     perform stop-timer

     move 'integer' to test-name
     move zero to integer-number
     perform start-timer
     perform repeat-factor times
         add 1 to integer-number
     end-perform
     perform stop-timer

     move 'comp-5' to test-name
     move zero to comp-5-number
     perform start-timer
     perform repeat-factor times
         add 1 to comp-5-number
     end-perform
     perform stop-timer

     goback

. start-timer.
     accept start-time from time
. stop-timer.
     accept end-time from time
     compute elapsed-time =
        (((((((end-hours * 60) +
           end-minutes) * 60) +
           end-seconds) * 100) +
           end-hundredths) -
        ((((((start-hours * 60) +
           start-minutes) * 60) +
           start-seconds) * 100) +
           start-hundredths))
     compute elapsed-time-edited rounded =
         (elapsed-time - loop-overhead) / 100
     display test-name elapsed-time-edited
```

#### ↳ Re: Hardware support for decimal (was something else)

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-22T07:38:26-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QJidnSAvnpG79eGjXTWciw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3e7c2b28.237800105@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e7c2b28.237800105@news.optonline.net...
> A timing test of Fujitsu COBOL running on a Pentium shows decimal
arithmetic
> slow on both DISPLAY and PACKED-DECIMAL formats, medium speed on BINARY
with or
> without digits right of decimal point, high speed on native COMP-5
integer. The
> difference between DISPLAY and COMP-5 is 30:1. This supports my
recommendation
> to use comp-5 for 'bread and butter' variables.
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> *>   comp-5             .55

Your observations are correct. However, the difference between 0.16 second
and .0055 second for ten million iterations, on a slow machine, with a
fairly-old compiler, might be within the margin of error of the measuring
tools and, if not, probably inconsequential in the real world.

At a factor of, say, 100 to get detectable differences (16 seconds vs .55
second), you'd be exercising 1 billion arithmetic operations.

You've demonstrated a difference and at the same time illustrated that
coding for micro-efficiency is almost always not worth the trouble.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Hardware support for decimal (was something else)

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-22T19:39:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e7cb786.273739367@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3e7c2b28.237800105@news.optonline.net> <QJidnSAvnpG79eGjXTWciw@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>tools and, if not, probably inconsequential in the real world.

The times are in seconds (you shouldn't have divided by 100) and the margin of
error is .05 sec (1/18.2). 

>At a factor of, say, 100 to get detectable differences (16 seconds vs .55
>second), you'd be exercising 1 billion arithmetic operations.

No. To get Confidence Interval greater than 95%, the smallest time must be 10
times greater than the margin of error. It is. Why not 20 times? Because errors
can only occur in one direction -- an extra clock tick. The test will never be a
clock tick too fast. Statisticians call such tests 'one-tailed'. If you don't
like that explanation, change the repeat factor to 20,000,000 or run it twice
and use the average. 

>You've demonstrated a difference and at the same time illustrated that
>coding for micro-efficiency is almost always not worth the trouble.

Generally, you're right. So why did IBM invent packed decimal?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Hardware support for decimal (was something else)

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-22T16:19:12-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F1adnXVmB7sYf-GjXTWcjQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3e7c2b28.237800105@news.optonline.net> <QJidnSAvnpG79eGjXTWciw@giganews.com> <3e7cb786.273739367@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e7cb786.273739367@news.optonline.net...
> "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:

>
> >You've demonstrated a difference and at the same time illustrated that
> >coding for micro-efficiency is almost always not worth the trouble.
>
> Generally, you're right. So why did IBM invent packed decimal?

Because the first IBM mass-marketed computers were sold to the accounting
departments. These computers had circuitry to do base-10 arithmetic and the
native code representation for these circuits was packed decimal. Much like
we think of with binary or COMP-5 (native representation).

I don't know whether contemporary computers have packed-decimal circuitry
(nor do I care); it's probably mostly a legacy thing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Hardware support for decimal (was something else)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T05:41:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e7d1bba.299396017@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3e7c2b28.237800105@news.optonline.net> <QJidnSAvnpG79eGjXTWciw@giganews.com> <3e7cb786.273739367@news.optonline.net> <F1adnXVmB7sYf-GjXTWcjQ@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>we think of with binary or COMP-5 (native representation).

By George, you got it! It's not about efficiency, it's about culture. When the
IBM salesman was pitching a computer to the company comptroller, who was
probably a CPA, decimal arithmetic sounded like a Good Idea .. because that's
what the customer was comfortable with. 

>I don't know whether contemporary computers have packed-decimal circuitry
>(nor do I care); it's probably mostly a legacy thing.

Yes, it is. That's why my Best Practices described packed decimal as obsolete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Hardware support for decimal (was something else)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T18:29:59+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5jke9$e7a$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3e7c2b28.237800105@news.optonline.net> <3e7cb786.273739367@news.optonline.net> <F1adnXVmB7sYf-GjXTWcjQ@giganews.com> <3e7d1bba.299396017@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> it's about culture. ...
> decimal arithmetic sounded like a Good Idea .. because
> that's what the customer was comfortable with.

No. Decimal arithmetic _is_ a good idea.  Or specifically scaled decimal 
arithmetic.  Trying to do business calculations in floating-point or in 
intergers gives _huge_ problems.

Given that most business applications read display format input (card 
packs, paper tape, input screens, web server forms) and produce display 
format output (printer, screens, html text) then catering for scaled 
decimal calculations is a good choice.  Otherwise a large amount of CPU 
time is spent converting between decimal and binary.

While the current performance measures are of _one_type_ of data, this 
leads to invalid conclusions about what is the 'best' to use for 
application data (ie other than loop variables and counters) because it 
takes no account of the neccesary conversions between internal and external 
formats.

As you also say, disk records should be display format.

On some systems this may result in best overall program performance being 
when internal calculations on appliocation data use display or packed 
formats.
```

```
Donald
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Hardware support for decimal (was something else)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T07:32:17-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AsidncTK8vdPKuCjXTWchA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<3e7c2b28.237800105@news.optonline.net> <3e7cb786.273739367@news.optonline.net> <F1adnXVmB7sYf-GjXTWcjQ@giganews.com> <3e7d1bba.299396017@news.optonline.net> <b5jke9$e7a$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <Z9ifa.228$Yz2.13526@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

"Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:Z9ifa.228$Yz2.13526@news20.bellglobal.com...
> "Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
> news:b5jke9$e7a$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz...
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> > On some systems this may result in best overall program performance
being
> > when internal calculations on appliocation data use display or packed
> > formats.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> program?

I don't think there is a good reason. Non-mainframe computers don't have
decimal circuitry and don't have decimal instructions (i.e., add-packed). On
PCs (for example), converting display to packed is a wasted step inasmuch as
the compiler will convert the packed data to binary before doing the
requested arithmetic.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Hardware support for decimal (was something else)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T18:02:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e7dd030.13284383@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3e7c2b28.237800105@news.optonline.net> <3e7cb786.273739367@news.optonline.net> <F1adnXVmB7sYf-GjXTWcjQ@giganews.com> <3e7d1bba.299396017@news.optonline.net> <b5jke9$e7a$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>formats.

Rather than speculate, I wrote a program to test your hypothesis.

 identification division.
 program-id. test21.
*> author. Robert Wagner.
*>  Testing speed of binary arithmetic vs. decimal,
*>  where input and output are display.
*>  Findings:
*>   loop overhead        .11
*>   decimal            17.52
*>   packed             35.15
*>   binary             12.24
*>  Environment: Intel P100, Fujitsu COBOL v3.
 data division.
 working-storage section.
 01  unqualified-variables.
     05  input-number-1 value 1234567      pic s9(09)v99.
     05  input-number-2 value 890          pic s9(09)v99.
     05  intermediate-number               pic s9(09)v99.
     05  output-number                     pic s9(09)v99.

     05  packed-number-1 packed-decimal    pic s9(09)v99.
     05  packed-number-2 packed-decimal    pic s9(09)v99.
     05  intermediate-packed-1 packed-decimal pic s9(09)v99.
     05  intermediate-packed-2 packed-decimal pic s9(09)v99.
     05  binary-number-1    comp-5         pic s9(09)v99.
     05  binary-number-2    comp-5         pic s9(09)v99.
     05  intermediate-binary-1 comp-5      pic s9(09)v99.
     05  intermediate-binary-2 comp-5      pic s9(09)v99.

     05  test-name                         pic  x(20).
     05  start-time.
         10  start-hours                   pic  9(02).
         10  start-minutes                 pic  9(02).
         10  start-seconds                 pic  9(02).
         10  start-hundredths              pic  9(02).
     05  end-time.
         10  end-hours                     pic  9(02).
         10  end-minutes                   pic  9(02).
         10  end-seconds                   pic  9(02).
         10  end-hundredths                pic  9(02).
     05  repeat-factor value 1000000 binary pic s9(09).
     05  loop-overhead value zero binary   pic s9(09).
     05  elapsed-time             binary   pic s9(09).
     05  elapsed-time-edited               pic  z(04).99.

 procedure division.
 main.
     move 'loop overhead' to test-name
     perform start-timer
     perform repeat-factor times
         continue
     end-perform
     perform stop-timer
     move elapsed-time to loop-overhead

     move 'decimal' to test-name
     perform start-timer
     perform repeat-factor times
         compute intermediate-number =
             input-number-1 / input-number-2
         compute output-number = intermediate-number * 123.45
     end-perform
     perform stop-timer

     move 'packed' to test-name
     perform start-timer
     perform repeat-factor times
         move input-number-1 to packed-number-1
         move input-number-2 to packed-number-2
         compute intermediate-packed-1 =
             packed-number-1 / packed-number-2
         compute intermediate-packed-2 =
             intermediate-packed-1 * 123.45
         move intermediate-packed-2 to output-number
     end-perform
     perform stop-timer

     move 'binary' to test-name
     perform start-timer
     perform repeat-factor times
         move input-number-1 to binary-number-1
         move input-number-2 to binary-number-2
         compute intermediate-binary-1 =
             binary-number-1 / binary-number-2
         compute intermediate-binary-2 =
             intermediate-binary-1 * 123.45
         move intermediate-binary-2 to output-number
     end-perform
     perform stop-timer
     goback

. start-timer.
     accept start-time from time
. stop-timer.
     accept end-time from time
     compute elapsed-time =
        (((((((end-hours * 60) +
           end-minutes) * 60) +
           end-seconds) * 100) +
           end-hundredths) -
        ((((((start-hours * 60) +
           start-minutes) * 60) +
           start-seconds) * 100) +
           start-hundredths))
     compute elapsed-time-edited rounded =
         (elapsed-time - loop-overhead) / 100
     display test-name elapsed-time-edited
```

#### ↳ Re: Hardware support for decimal (was something else)

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T08:24:20+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5iguj$uo5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3e7c2b28.237800105@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> A timing test of Fujitsu COBOL running on a Pentium shows decimal
> arithmetic slow on both DISPLAY and PACKED-DECIMAL formats, medium speed
> on BINARY with or without digits right of decimal point,  high speed on
> native COMP-5 integer. 

>      05  display-number                    pic s9(10)v9(03).
>      05  packed-number      packed-decimal pic s9(10)v9(03).
>      05  binary-number      binary         pic s9(10)v9(03).
>      05  integer-number     binary         pic s9(09).
>      05  comp-5-number      comp-5         pic s9(09).

Any fool can make a PIC S9(9) perform better than a PIC S9(10)V9(3), 

Just doing a single literal ADD is hardless a representitive test of speed, 
as I stated, mine also dod MOVE, MULTIPLY and DIVIDE, you know, the sort of 
stuff that _real_ programs do on _real_ values.

> The difference between DISPLAY and COMP-5 is 30:1.

No. The difference between PIC S9(10)V999 DISPLAY and 4 byte integer for 
ADD may well be 30:1.

Adding useful _real_world_ calculations brings them somewhat closer.

> This supports my recommendation to use comp-5 for 'bread and butter'
> variables.

It isn't about 'bread and butter' variables, you have once again completely 
missed the point.  No one is trying to tell you to use S9(10)V999 DISPLAY 
as 'bread and butter'.  BINARY is portable, COMP-5 is needed sometime for 
OS interfacing (such as Windows).

Once the PICs are corrected to match then the figures are not so wildly 
different.

I had found a system where PACKED was _FASTER_ for 'real world' variables 
such as S9(10)V999.  I had also stated that Fujitsu had a poor 
implementation of PACKED and DISPLAY.

It is useful to know the characteristics of all your systems and to _not_ 
make some blanket 'recommendation' just because one system has particular 
characteristics.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Hardware support for decimal (was something else)

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T10:31:51+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5iodm$2d1$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3e7c2b28.237800105@news.optonline.net> <b5iguj$uo5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:

Further to my:

> I had found a system where PACKED was _FASTER_ for 'real world' variables
> such as S9(10)V999.  I had also stated that Fujitsu had a poor
> implementation of PACKED and DISPLAY.

MF on Unix/Linux on Intel has somewhat different characteristics with all 
the performances much the same.  For a test on a really slow P133 with 
SCOusing _all_ S9(10)V999 and more than just an ADD, the range was 10.97 
(COMP-5) to 12.41 (DISPLAY) with PACKED (11.22) slightly outperforming 
BINARY (11.54).
 
> It is useful to know the characteristics of all your systems and to _not_
> make some blanket 'recommendation' just because one system has particular
> characteristics.

MF _may_ convert DISPLAY to PACKED or BINARY for the calculations and/or 
may use the BCD support in Intel CPUs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Hardware support for decimal (was something else)

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T18:40:33+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5jl23$eel$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3e7c2b28.237800105@news.optonline.net> <b5iguj$uo5$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e7d3db6.308096931@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> In that case, any fool can write a fast running program.

If they are prepared to, say, ignore the cents in money calculations, yes.
 
> I don't think so. Most routine activity is addition. Multiply and divide
> are used in the analysis phase.

I don't know what sort of applications that you write but mine seem to be 
full of:

        MULTIPLY product-price BY order-quantity GIVING extended-value
        MULTIPLY extended-value BY tax-rate GIVING tax-value
        MULTIPLY product-weight BY shipped-quantity GIVING shipped-weight
        DIVIDE shipped-weight BY carton-count GIVING average weight
    
But then mine are _business_ applications.

>>I had found a system where PACKED was _FASTER_ for 'real world' variables
>>such as S9(10)V999.
> 
> It was probably a calculator. 

It was MicroFocus Cobol.

> Did you know all calculators internally use
> decimal arithmetic? It makes no sense because response time is not an
> issue. It's just a cultural thing.

No. It is not 'culture' it is CPU cost.  The display has to be decimal, the 
input has to be decimal, to make a simple circuit it is better to build a 
completly decimal machine than one that does conversion to and from binary.
```

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-23T18:02:30+00:00

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:

>> Did you know all calculators internally use
>> decimal arithmetic? It makes no sense because response time is not an
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>completly decimal machine than one that does conversion to and from binary.

Actually, the cost of producing a chip is a function of accounting rather than
design. The least expensive chips use .75 and .50 micron technology, made by
equipment which was fully depreciated ten years ago. That's ok for calculators
because speed doesn't matter. They will go to deep sub-micron only when the
equipment becomes too expensive to maintain.
```

#### ↳ Re: Hardware support for decimal (was something else)

- **From:** "Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-25T07:29:54-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E803DB2.62348577@istar.ca>`
- **References:** `<3e7c2b28.237800105@news.optonline.net>`

```
If timing is your only concern, then use of binary may be faster.  It
definitely should be used for subscripts and reference modification.  On
virtually all currently produced platforms binary arithmetic is 30 -
1000 times faster (see  http://www2.hursley.ibm.com/decimal/decifaq.html
for details), in part due to decimal being done by software.  However,
if you want to have decimal rules and decimal rounding (see the faq for
why this is important) so that results match what people are expecting,
then binary and floating point binary won't do.  The code to maintain
decimal results while doing arithmetic in binary must be interesting to
say the least.  Remember that 1/5 is a repeating value in binary instead
of just .2. 

Robert Wager wrote:
> 
> A timing test of Fujitsu COBOL running on a Pentium shows decimal arithmetic
…[112 more quoted lines elided]…
> .
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Hardware support for decimal (was something else)

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-25T12:43:45+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e804b2e.1603364@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3e7c2b28.237800105@news.optonline.net> <3E803DB2.62348577@istar.ca>`

```
"Clark F. Morris, Jr." <cfmtech@istar.ca> wrote:

>If timing is your only concern, then use of binary may be faster.  It
>definitely should be used for subscripts and reference modification.  On
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>of just .2. 

We're not discussing floating point, so let's set that aside. Binary integers
store exact values. For example, suppose COBOL fields were defined 'binary pic
s999v99. The 1 and 5 would be stored as the binary value of 100 and 500. After
division, the result would be 20. 

Rounding is just as precise. Before dividing,  you shift the numerator left one
bit giving 200, do the division giving 40, add 1 to the quotient giving 41, then
shift right one bit giving 20. If was  2/3, 400 divided by 300 is 133, plus 1 is
134, shifting right (dividing by 2) gives 67. There is no rounding error.
```

```
DeLaPena wrote:

> Hi Mate,
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well Matey, I ain't your mate, but still..........<G>

Richard beat me to the punch and pointed out the difference between GUI
(Windows stuff) and DOS Text mode which is what you want to do. Before you
go any further, check out his comments about will the M/S compiler work with
Windows XP.

First - as I suspected you are using Screen Section - big, big challenge to
emulate a Listbox with the Screen Section.. So that you understand the
problem, lets take the 'Windows' approach using GUIs. You use a visual
design tool to set up the Listbox, then your support GUI tools gives you
code to put a table of entries (elements) into a Listbox.  Once displayed,
by moving the Mouse (a mouse event) over the scroll bar  the list
automatically moves up and down without any coding in COBOL - this is done
by Windows API support. When you want a particular record in the Listbox you
click on it and your application can pick out the record by its index
position.
(Assume your Listbox shows 25 records at a time, even though there might be
300 records/elements, (250 being 'hidden')).

Because MicroSOFT COBOL V 5.0 and Micro FOCUS COBOL V3 are virtually the
same animal the following should apply to use of the Screen Section. You
have got to emulate both the moving up and down (scrolling) and selecting a
particular record that you want. The actual Lisbox is no big deal in Screen
Section - it's that 'visually' moving the contents and selecting a record
that's your problem. You will have to confirm for me - does MS COBOL V 5
contain M/F's CBL_SCR routines :-

CBL_SCR_ALLOCATE_COLOR Allocate color for RGB value
CBL_SCR_ALLOCATE_VC_COLOR Allocate color in virtual colormap
CBL_SCR_CREATE_VC Create virtual colormap
CBL_SCR_DESTROY_VC Destroy virtual colormap
CBL_SCR_GET_ATTR_INFO Get attribute information
CBL_SCR_GET_ATTRIBUTES
etc....

and CBL_MOUSE routines :-

CBL_GET_MOUSE_MASK Get mouse event mask
CBL_GET_MOUSE_POSITION Get mouse screen coordinates
CBL_GET_MOUSE_STATUS Get number of events in queue
CBL_HIDE_MOUSE Hide mouse pointer
CBL_INIT_MOUSE Initialize mouse support
CBL_READ_MOUSE_EVENT Read mouse event queue
etc......

If you have the above then you have part of the solution - if not then you
are kinda stuck. (Check out the Microsoft COBOL on-line help file using the
index to see if it has the above).

Sure you want to pursue this ? <G>. E-mail me privately because my last
reply to you  bounced. I can send you a couple of Micro Focus programs on
using Screen Section, plus I can try and dig out some code where I generated
a Listbox before I switched to Windows.

Purely as an aside - get real. Tell your teacher/instructor to get Net
Express University Edition for about 100 bucks, (www.micofocus.com) - and
you can do the whole damn thing in Windows <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB  (Your best mate - but only if you get it to work !)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Screens + scrollbox

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-24T22:47:39+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5ldlo$6rm$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<2673654.1048359010@dbforums.com> <2675661.1048454998@dbforums.com>`

```
DeLaPena wrote:

> 
> Well the meaning is that we create a complete program with gui, creating
> the gui in SCREENS. 

The SCREENS program is _not_ a GUI creator.  GUI = Graphics User Interface.

SCREENS generates _Text_ mode (not graphics) and only sets the appearance 
of the resulting screen and not much of its behaviour and so is not a 'user 
interface', only a screen display.

> Than we need to generate cobol and do copy
> "screenname.ss" inside screen section. I hope this is easy to understand

> ? It should be "DOS" screens if you know what i mean.
> I"m working in Windows XP Professional (dunno why this matters) and I
> work with compiler of Microfocus. version 5.0 or something like that ?

That is probably Miscrosoft version 5.0 which is a reboxed (and somewhat 
modified) version of Microfocus 3.0 or so.

Yes it does matter that it is Windows XP.  There have been reports that the 
indexed files of older MF and MS (including 5.0) become corrupted under XP.
```

#### ↳ Re: Screens + scrollbox

- **From:** "Merlin Moncure" <wizard_32141@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-24T18:02:28-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v7v3k794j28hde@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<2673654.1048359010@dbforums.com>`

```
Using character mode screens it is possible to emulate some of the more
'modern' GUI controls such as grids, etc. but as other posters have
mentioned this is a tremendous programming challenge.  I rate it impossible
without some previous experience writing user controls.   Some oo
exeperience will help as well.

Most of the trick lies in hacking the screen section to display/edit data
from an externally managed data source, such as a work file (or, if you are
lucky) a dynamically allocated memory buffer.  This is highly vendor
specific and may rely on special extentions to the language to help you out,
for example mouse handling.  All things considered, though, Unless you have
built in GUI support from the vendor (AcuCorp AcuCobol), it may not be
worthwhile).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Screens + scrollbox

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-26T22:36:17+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5qlmi$ep4$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<2673654.1048359010@dbforums.com> <v7v3k794j28hde@corp.supernews.com>`

```
> "DeLaPena" <member25583@dbforums.com> wrote 

>> part is to create a scrollbox, listboxes and so on. Any of you who can
>> help me or show me some examples plz ?
>> It really needs to be done with screens and cbl file for code !

Let us assume that you are doing a list box from an indexed file.  eg 
courses for a given semester where either the main key or an alternate key 
is giving the records in sequence.

The 'listbox' is an 10 line area of the screen.  10 records at a time will 
be shown and there may be more than 10 records.  Each'page' needs a start 
key so that any particular page of 10 records can be shown.  However we 
accept that the list needs to be shown forwards from the start (ie paged 
through) and not jumped to any point.

         01  Page-Index             PIC S9(4) BINARY.
         01  Page-Array.
             03  Page-Key           PIC X(24) OCCURS 100.
         01  Line-Index             PIC S9(4) BINARY.
         01  Line-Array.
             03  Line-Key           PIC X(24) OCCURS 10.

       

To start put the first key in Page-Key(1):

          MOVE 1      TO Page-Index
          MOVE Semester-code  TO Page-Key(Page-Index)
          .

To display a page start the file at that page's Page-Key and then read the 
next 10 records storing each key in a line table.

     Display-Page.

          MOVE Page-Key(Page-Index)  TO Course-Key
          START .. Course-Key
              INVALID
                  MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO Course-Key
          END-START
          MOVE 1   TO Line-Index
          PERFORM
              UNTIL Line-Index > 10
                 OR Course-Semester NOT = Semester-Key
              READ ...
                  AT END MOVE HIGH-VALUES TO Course-Key
              END-READ
              IF ( Course-Semester = Semester-Key )
                  MOVE Course-Key  TO Line-Key(Line-Index)
                  MOVE data items to screen line
                  ADD 1       TO Line-Index
              END-IF
          END-PERFORM
          PERFORM
              UNTIL Line-Index > 10

              MOVE HIGH-VALUES    TO Line-Key(Line-Index)
              *> this is only done if there were less than 10 in this 'page'
              blank out remaining screen-line data
              ADD 1    TO Line-Index
          END-PERFORM 
          *> save key for start of next page
          ADD 1            TO Page-Index
          MOVE Course-Key  TO Page-Key(Page-Index)
          .


If a line is selected then get the number of it and use this to fetch the 
key from the line-table. 

If 'next page' is selected (eg Page-Down key) then just perform the 
display-page above.

If 'previous page' is selected then subtract 2 from page-index, check that 
it is > zero, and perform display-page.

If 'home' is selected move 1 to page-index and perform display-page.

The last key on a page will also be the first key on the next page to give 
continuity.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Screens + scrollbox

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-26T02:01:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e80ff20.15706967@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2673654.1048359010@dbforums.com> <v7v3k794j28hde@corp.supernews.com> <b5qlmi$ep4$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>If 'next page' is selected (eg Page-Down key) then just perform the 
>display-page above.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>continuity.

What if the user presses End to get the last page, so (s)he can scroll
backwards? What if (s)he wants to know how big the search selection is? You
cannot do either.  For example, I'm looking for 'az' but I type only 'a'. If it
showed 'page 1 of 2', I'd hit PgDn. If it showed 'page 1 of 100', I'd hit End
and search backwards. 

I do this by reading ALL records into a list. You can do it with a table. The
speed ratio of READ NEXT vs. START is roughly 20:1. It takes longer to do a
START than to read 2 pages. 

Then I present the user with 'page 1 of n', with an option to type over the 1 to
jump to any page.  Word processors and Acrobat do the same. 

Suppose the user holds PgDn and lets it repeat at 20 times per second. Can you
keep up? I thought not.
```

- **References:** `<2673654.1048359010@dbforums.com> <v7v3k794j28hde@corp.supernews.com> <b5qlmi$ep4$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e80ff20.15706967@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> What if the user presses End to get the last page, so (s)he can scroll
> backwards? What if (s)he wants to know how big the search selection is?
> You cannot do either.  

Read my specification for the initial student implementation:

  """However we accept that the list needs to be shown forwards from the 
start (ie paged through) and not jumped to any point."""

Going to the 'end' is easy enough, just perform display-page until the end 
of the key, paging backwards from there.

> For example, I'm looking for 'az' but I type only 'a'.
> If it showed 'page 1 of 2', I'd hit PgDn. If it showed 'page 1 of 100',
> I'd hit End and search backwards.

Fine, write your own if you don't like the code, it wasn't intended to be 
for you to use.

It was intended as a starting point for development, not a complete 
solution for all needs.  I wasn't going to write the whole student system 
for them, only showing one technique.

Please do post complete code that does as you specify.

> I do this by reading ALL records into a list. You can do it with a table.
> The speed ratio of READ NEXT vs. START is roughly 20:1. It takes longer to
> do a START than to read 2 pages.

Given this is an interactive system can you please post a detailed timing  
comparison between reading two pages and user deciding which key to press 
next.
 
Regardless of your list/table, my mechanism will get the _first_ page up 
faster.  Given that only a subset of the file is to be in the list (in my 
case there are several semesters in the file, one is to be shown) then you 
will still need the start.

The issue here relates back to an earlier thread about response times, Mine 
are consistent for every page, yours will be very variable - first page 
slow, others fast.

> Then I present the user with 'page 1 of n', with an option to type over
> the 1 to
> jump to any page.  Word processors and Acrobat do the same.

Please feel free to post code that will do this.
 
> Suppose the user holds PgDn and lets it repeat at 20 times per second. Can
> you keep up? I thought not.

Is 'keeping up' a requirement of the student system ? Can the user read 
fast enough ?

Will the students _learn_ anything if presented with a complete solution 
with all issues resolved ?  Or will they learn from an outline (as mine) 
upon which they can build ?
```

```
> Now that we have GUI, the subject is moot. 

You might have and use 'list boxes', the students do not, it is not
'moot' for them.  But then you often have difficulty understanding
that not everything is about you.

> List boxes have moved from the COBOL
> domain to the third-party GUI tools provider. The good ones let us populate a
> list box with more than 10 records and let the user scroll between pages.

I could use list boxes if I wanted to write medioce software, but I
prefer to have better control and more even response times so, where
appropriate, and for reasons outlined, I do my own lists
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Screens + scrollbox

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-27T04:19:12+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5r9o5$n62$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<2673654.1048359010@dbforums.com> <v7v3k794j28hde@corp.supernews.com> <b5qlmi$ep4$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e80ff20.15706967@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> I do this by reading ALL records into a list. You can do it with a table.

I work in multi-user environments where data may change from time to time. 
Reading all records into a table or list means that it will become stale, a 
page at a time minimizes this, page forward and get _current_ data.

It is also important to rebuild the page-key table as the user moves 
forward (as my code does) as there may be more or fewer records between 
keys at some later time.

I also allow records to be maintained from the listbox (but I don't use 
screen section, this is also for SP/2) - press the key and the maintenance 
screen pops up (if the user has adequate security level) and can change, 
add, delete.

It is then only necessary to redisplay the current page to show the new 
current details when the maintenance is complete.

Reading into a list/table, or directly into a dialog box list may be 
copying what Windows does, but that does not mean it is the best user 
interface.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Screens + scrollbox

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-03-27T02:17:48-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0303270217.2d3922cd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2673654.1048359010@dbforums.com> <v7v3k794j28hde@corp.supernews.com> <b5qlmi$ep4$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e80ff20.15706967@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> The speed ratio of READ NEXT vs. START is roughly 20:1. 

Doing a test with Fujitsu 7 for Linux I wrote an ISAM file of 9000
records (key 000001 to 009000).  I then read through it using READ
NEXT 5 times, I then did a start on all 9000 keys 5 times.

      READ NEXT x 5     2.94 sec   (total 45,000 READ NEXT)
      START on all x 5  7.19 sec   (total 45,000 START)

That looks more like 2:1 than it does 20:1.  It always pays to check
out any of the mis-information you try to pass off as facts.

> It takes longer to do a START than to read 2 pages. 

Is this another thing that you 'discovered' 30 years ago and never
checked again ?

I doubt that 0.0016 sec is noticable in an interactive system.
```

## Thread: Virus alert — 8 msgs

### Virus alert

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-22T20:12:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e7cc383.276808639@news.optonline.net>`

```
New virus warning

It is called C-Nile.
Even the most advanced programs from Norton and
McAfee cannot take care of it.

1.  Causes you to send same e-mail twice
2.  Causes you to send blank e-mail
3.  Causes you to send to wrong person
4.  Causes you to forward to person who sent it to you
5.  Causes you to forget to attach the attachment
6.  Causes you to hit "SEND" before you've finished writing

It mostly affects those of us born before 1950
```

## Thread: COBOL DB2 sub-program — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### COBOL DB2 sub-program

- **From:** "Klaus Sch���fer" <KSchaefer@nature-store.de>
- **Date:** 2003-03-22T21:22:46+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5igmp$54u$03$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Hello,

I need to write a COBOL SUB-Programm for OS/390 environment that does EXEC
SQL to DB2 to do commit processing. This program must be CALLed from several
other COBOl-programs, so will be linked (static) to these programs and must
handle several call parameters.

Can someone give me a sample of how this SUB-program must look like? Do I
have to bind the SUB-program or does it take the DB2-environment from the
main program?

All help is appreciated very much!

Klaus
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL DB2 sub-program

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-03-22T16:26:24-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5ike0$7j8$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<b5igmp$54u$03$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
In article <b5igmp$54u$03$1@news.t-online.com>,
Klaus Sch�fer <KSchaefer@nature-store.de> wrote:
>Hello,
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Can someone give me a sample of how this SUB-program must look like?

Yes, that 'someone' is a programmer with, oh, four years' worth of 
experience in COBOL and DB2.  Find such a person, supply a halfway 
competent set of specs, pay a decent rate and you'll be all set.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL DB2 sub-program

- **From:** B Berman <bberman@netbox.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-22T22:13:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E7CE1BE.B0D3AC2C@netbox.com>`
- **References:** `<b5igmp$54u$03$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
"Klaus Schï¿½fer" wrote:

> I need to write a COBOL SUB-Programm for OS/390 environment that does EXEC
> SQL to DB2 to do commit processing. This program must be CALLed from several
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> main program?

Why would you want to use a static call and link the sub-program into each load
module that calls it ? If it were ever to need maintenance, every program that
referenced it would need to be relinked and re-bound. It seems to me that a
dynamic call would be the way to go, especially in an OS/390 environment.  In
that context (dynamic), yes, the sub-program would need a bind.

I won't even question why a separate module just for Commit logic...

Bob
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL DB2 sub-program

- **Date:** 2003-03-23T00:50:26+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<43qp7vo0b8n70qh2k8fgfd89se1j7275qa@4ax.com>`

```
wrote:

:>I need to write a COBOL SUB-Programm for OS/390 environment that does EXEC

:>Can someone give me a sample of how this SUB-program must look like? Do I
:>have to bind the SUB-program or does it take the DB2-environment from the
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL DB2 sub-program

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-22T23:46:10
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e7cf5c5$1_2@text-west.newsgroups.com>`

```
"Klaus Sch�fer" <KSchaefer@nature-store.de> wrote in message
news:b5igmp$54u$03$1@news.t-online.com...
> Hello,
>
> I need to write a COBOL SUB-Programm for OS/390 environment that does EXEC
> SQL to DB2 to do commit processing. This program must be CALLed from
several
> other COBOl-programs, so will be linked (static) to these programs and
must
> handle several call parameters.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Klaus,

review the design here.

Static linkage for a utility module is stupid.

It should be dynamic and it will need to be bound. (But really, you
shouldn't even do it...)

It looks to me as though you intend to call this module when you are
satisfied that the transaction is complete. A review of the overall
transaction design may cause you to change your mind about this.

(I hope so...<G>).

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL DB2 sub-program

```
"Klaus Schï¿½fer" <KSchaefer@nature-store.de> wrote in message news:<b5k0pi$dog$02$1@news.t-online.com>...
> Hello Guys,
> 
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
The DB2 equivalent of dynamic linking is package binding.  If your
shop still does plan binding even if you don't have to relink for
every change to this subroutine, you will have to bind all the plans
using this.
```

```

> The DB2 equivalent of dynamic linking is package binding.  If your
> shop still does plan binding even if you don't have to relink for
> every change to this subroutine, you will have to bind all the plans
> using this.
```
