###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Possible Date Functions (was: Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-30T19:24:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e87444f.426696356@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b5qalm$dp$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3e81067a.17589963@news.optonline.net> <v8cn88tiqp1o30@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:

>This got me to thinking about how I have sometimes applied
>mathematical functions to dates and that it might be useful to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
><g>]

>Are there other date functions that may make sense?

I propose INTEGER-TO-HISTORICAL-DATE and HISTORICAL-DATE-TO-INTEGER. This would
be useful to historians and genealogists. Pope Gregory standardized the calendar
in 1582, but the Gregorian calendar was not adopted by some countries until many
years later:

Italy, Spain		1582
Catholic Germany	1582
Hungary		1587
Scotland		1600/1752 (see below)
Protestant Germany	1700
England and US		1752
France 			1806 (after 13 years of a 'sans-culottes' calendar)
Russia			1918
Yugoslavia		1919
Greece			1923
Ethiopia			still on Julian calendar

As a result, we would see Napoleon retreating from Borodino Field on August 26,
1812 (according to a Russian text), 12 days before the battle took place
(according to a French text).

Sweden is an interesting case. In 1700 it decided to convert from Julian to
Gregorian gradually, by dropping Feb. 29 for some 40 years. They did so in 1704
and 1708, then changed their mind. The year 1712 had *two* leap days in
February. In 1753, they converted to Gregorian all at once. 

A related issue is when a year begins. We now take it for granted a year begins
on 01/01. In historical times, the year generally began on 03/25, which was
called Feast of Annunciation or, in England, Ladies Day. It was thought to be
the day of Christ's conception. Thus, the day after 1600-03-24 was 1601-03-25.
Scotland adopted the 01/01 convention in 1600 but not the rest of the Gregorian
standard until 1752. Other places adoped 01/01 long before they adopted the
Gregorian calendar:

Venice			1522
Spain			1556
France			1579
Russia			1725

Washington's actual birthday was Feb. 11, 1731. It is represented as Feb. 22,
1732 under the Gregorian calendar. Needless to say, dates written before the new
calendar are not designated O.S. (old style).

A third issue is leap year on the century mark. The year 1700 was a leap year in
England and others on the Julian calendar; it was a common (non-leap) year
elsewhere. In Russia and Greece, 1800 and 1900 were leap years.

The sans-culottes calender had a 13th month containing 5 days (6 in leap years).

Thus, the HISTORICAL-DATE functions would need inputs of country and, in some
cases, city.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Possible Date Functions (was: Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-31T17:24:48+00:00

```

On 29-Mar-2003, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:

> FLOOR-OF-YEAR (argument-1) returns the value for the
> first day of the year.

It is pretty easy to get these values with a couple of calls.  Would making
these more efficient be worth the bloat of having them in our language?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Possible Date Functions (was: Re: Convert MainFrame Data [ EBCDIC ] To Ascii)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-31T18:01:30-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v8hhfn67f0vpaa@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <b5qalm$dp$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3e81067a.17589963@news.optonline.net> <v8cn88tiqp1o30@corp.supernews.com> <b69tl0$lgv$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:b69tl0$lgv$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 29-Mar-2003, "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> It is pretty easy to get these values with a couple of calls.  Would
making
> these more efficient be worth the bloat of having them in our language?

The issue is not efficiency. The issue is communication.
Replacing a common, oft used, expression by a function
simplifies programming and improves communication.

Most (maybe all) of the functions I listed shouldn't make it
into the standard; but there are useful functions for
manipulating dates that should. I 'kind-a' know what they
are; but would need to take the time to review source
code so I don't miss any that would have helped me in the
past and, maybe, help others in the future.

Of course, anyone can make their own list at any time
and submit that list to J4!
```

```
Capellone
```

```
01  CTRL-FILE-STATUS        PIC X(02).

    88 CTL-AT-END                       VALUE '10'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus mfrun.gnt

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-27T04:01:45+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5r8ne$mpu$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3de78cdc$0$231$4d4ebb8e@news.nl.uu.net> <2681967.1048610744@dbforums.com> <3e80e39d.8662726@news.optonline.net> <b5qr42$gus$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e81184c.22152018@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>No.  -O is something else.  it is -u, or OMF"GNT"
> 
> I work with MF on Unix every day. It is definately -O.

Sigh, maybe you do work with it, but you never seem to read the manuals:

Object COBOL User Guide
Chapter 9: Descriptions of cob Flags

""" -----------------------------------------------------------------
Compile to .gnt Code for Unlinked Environment (-u)

Compiles the COBOL source code files (.cbl, .CBL or .cob) into intermediate 
code (.int) and then code generates them to dynamically loadable native 
code (.gnt) files.

You can supply intermediate code files to the cob command instead of COBOL 
source code files; these are just code generated. You can also input any 
object module files (.o) to the cob command; these are statically linked to 
the dynamic loader to produce the static run-time library for dynamically 
loadable files. Dynamically loaded programs can call any of the modules in 
the statically linked run-time library as well as any other valid 
dynamically loadable program.

Examples

The following examples illustrate the types of processing available using 
the -u flag:

cob Command line Output Files
cob -u  tmp/a.cbl a.int, a.gnt
cob -u b.cbl b.int, b.gnt
cob -u a.int a.gnt
cob -u tmp/b.int b.gnt
----------------------------------------------------------------- """

""" -------------------------------------------------------------
Enable Optimization (-O)

Enables maximum performance at run time by carrying out the minimum 
run-time checks. This option is for use after all debugging has taken place 
and maximum performance with minimum run-time checks is required. The exact 
operation of this flag is environment dependent, but at a minimum it passes 
the NOCHECK directive to the Compiler. If any .c files are being processed, 
this flag also is passed through to cc.

---------------------------------------------------------------- """

The -O option can be added to either .int or to .gnt, but does not cause 
production of .gnt.  

The question was "the command to get the gnt files.". 
The answer is -u or OMF"GNT".
```

- **From:** "Simon Tobias" <Simon.Tobias@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-27T09:54:48
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5uhhu$ocq$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<3de78cdc$0$231$4d4ebb8e@news.nl.uu.net> <2681967.1048610744@dbforums.com> <3e80e39d.8662726@news.optonline.net> <b5qr42$gus$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e81184c.22152018@news.optonline.net> <b5r8ne$mpu$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3e81ddcb.72718978@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
> FWIW, to make an option the default, put it in $COBLIB/etc/cobopt (not to
be
> confused with $COBOPT).

I think you mean $COBDIR/etc/cobopt. Per the Server Express User's Guide :

At start-up, the cob command searches for options in the following sequence
of locations:
  1.. System default options in the file $COBDIR/etc/cobopt
  2.. User default options in the environment variable COBOPT
  3.. Execution-time options on the cob command line
SimonT.
```

```
MFDEVP wrote:

> I am trying to produce .gnt files for MF-COBOL programs in unix
> environment.Could anybody please help me the command to get the
> gnt files.

Add OMF"GNT" to the compiler directives. eg:

   cob -u prog1.cbl -C OMF"GNT"
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus mfrun.gnt

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-27T04:57:11+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5rbvc$o61$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3de78cdc$0$231$4d4ebb8e@news.nl.uu.net> <2683537.1048632363@dbforums.com>`

```
MFDEVP wrote:

> 
> Thank you so much.I was able to generate by using
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> code this is happening with the PC.

It shouldn't be too hard to determine what the problem is: either a program 
is CALLing itself, or it is CALLing a program that has CALLed it.

It the programs are created as .int and a REF and COPYLIST are directives 
then you should get a listing that directly shows the PC numbers.  However, 
with a .gnt you also need to do something like ASMLIST"name.asm" and 
SOURCEASM to be able to track the PC addresses.

It would be best to delete the .gnts and recompile to .int for testing and 
then only go to .gnt for distribution (if ever).

Alternately add ANIM directive on the compile (to .int not .gnt) and 
animate the programs to see what is going on.

Don't forget to rm *.gnt.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus mfrun.gnt

- **From:** "Simon Tobias" <Simon.Tobias@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-27T16:40:50
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5v9bg$qlu$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<3de78cdc$0$231$4d4ebb8e@news.nl.uu.net> <2698167.1048780835@dbforums.com>`

```
> I am very much curious to know how to find the PC number by Micro Focus
> Cobol program compiling into assembly listing.I am keep on trying
> different options to generate code in assembling listing but I am
> end up with just .lst files.Could anybody please help me to identify
> this problem.

If you are using Net Express or Server Express, then you can animate
generated code.

For Server Express, if you compile your program thus :

cob -ug myapp.cbl

and then debug with :

anim myapp.gnt

You can either step through your program, or hit Zoom. Either way, execution
should stop on the offending source line. This will probably be much quicker
than trying to create assembly listings.

If you really want to create an assembly listing on UNIX, you can pass -S to
cob, i.e.

cob -uS myapp.cbl

though that directive is provided as-is (undocumented), and is not a
recommended method to resolve your problem.

Simon.
```

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help with COBOL and CLISTS

- **From:** "Giulio Belrango" <giuliobelrango@rodgers.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-25T21:53:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xd4ga.10939$s421.7921@news02.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
I am working in an IBM Mainframe OS/390 environment. I am writing TSO CLISTS
to handle JCL job submission prompting users for various input. TSO CLISTS
tend to scroll or roll from the top of the screen to the bottom of the
screen. I was looking for a way to clear the screen prompt the user and
clear the screen again, thus giving a more panel looking dialog. I used to
have an old Assembler program that could do this. Can this be done in COBOL.
If it can can someone forward the code.

Any help appreciated

Thanks...
```

## Thread: about the word Returning — 57 this month / 60 total

### about the word Returning

- **From:** Pil <member27114@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-25T22:04:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2683387.1048629847@dbforums.com>`

```

This is with the  fujitsu v30L10 compiler.
I'm trying to call the msgbox function in USER32.Dll
when i write this:
                    CALL "MessageBoxA" WITH STDCALL LINKAGE
                                  USING BY VALUE 0
                                  BY REFERENCE msgnaam
                                  BY REFERENCE msgnaam
                                  BY VALUE 1

it works
But when i write
 CALL "MessageBoxA" WITH STDCALL LINKAGE
                                  USING BY VALUE 0
                                  BY REFERENCE msgnaam
                                  BY REFERENCE msgnaam
                                  BY VALUE 1
                                  RETURNING msg-flag
i get the following error:

JMN2503I-S  USER WORD 'RETURNING' IS UNDEFINED.

My question is what am i doing wrong? It's probably a compiler option i
have to set?

Thanks in advance for the answers :)
```

#### ↳ Re: about the word Returning

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-27T05:02:40+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5rc9l$ocj$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<2683387.1048629847@dbforums.com>`

```
Pil wrote:

> This is with the  fujitsu v30L10 compiler.
> 
> JMN2503I-S  USER WORD 'RETURNING' IS UNDEFINED.

Have you looked in the manual (or equivalent) for the version 3 compiler ?

CALL has no RETURNING option in version 3, thus it thinks this is a user 
word.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Freeloaders... was: about the word Returning

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-27T14:00:09
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e8303fe_1@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<2683387.1048629847@dbforums.com> <2686830.1048699933@dbforums.com> <ZXCga.1229$jl4.848427@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Ther's an easy lesson to be learned here, Michael.

Some time ago I stopped to consider what the overall effect of providing
support here is.

Being of a generous disposition (like many here) and feeling that it doesn't
do any harm to help, I spent time (just as you outlined) researching
problems and providing answers. I found that in many cases the people
concerned never even say "thanks".

We have people posting here for support who are actually taking jobs from
programmers in Europe and America, then requiring assistance from those same
programmers. And when they get it, they accept that it is their right, and
CLC is here to provide free COBOL support.

Nowadays, I am very selective and careful about who I respond to. The last
time I actually spent more than an hour of my time on something for CLC was
last week when Carlos was having problems with COBOL pictures on forms in
PowerCOBOL. Because he is a regular poster here and often provides help to
others (in other words, his heart is in the right place <G>) I saw no
problem in setting up a project and experimenting on his difficulty.

On the other hand, today I see a post in broken English (I know it isn't
everybody's first language, but it is necessary to at least get a clear
statement of the problem) for some help with Dreamweaver and a tenuous
connection to COBOL. My first impulse (when I remembered the strife I had
when I first moved to Dreamweaver after using Notepad and hand crafted HTML
<G>) was to try and help. (I have extensive experience now with Dreamweaver
and regularly embed components with it). But there are several problems
here... I don't believe the poster would understand the response (this is
due to language difficulty; I'm not suggesting for a moment he is not
capable of understanding <G>..) and I believe it would require several hours
to establish exactly what his problem is and solve it. Why would I invest
that amount of time, for free, and in the likelihood that once he has the
solution, he will not visit CLC either as a poster or a lurker unless he has
another problem he needs solved urgently for free...?

Helping youngsters do their homework (with broad suggestions), debating
matters COBOL, and the occasional laugh are all good reasons to frequent
CLC.

Providing professional programmers with information they are too lazy to
find out empirically for themselves (as most of us had to), or providing
free consultancy to Companies who SHOULD be buying the expertise is another
thing entirely.

Like you, I was disappointed the poster neglected to share the solution
(mainly so I could correlate it against my own thoughts on the subject and
maybe learn something) but it just demonstrates the attitude that is
becoming far too prevalent here.

It is not for me to say what others here should do. But I would urge all of
you to think about providing free advice just because you happen to know the
answer.

My own criteria  before responding are as follows:

1. Is this poster likely to contribute in the future?

2. Does this poster have a right to expect free advice? (like, would he do
the same for me, is he gainfully employed and doing this on behalf of a
corporation that could/should be buying it.)

3. Has the problem been clearly stated? (If I get into this, am I going to
spend time finding out what his REAL problem is?)

We all need advice occasionally (self included), but we should EARN the
right to ask for it, if it is going to be provided free.

Pete.



"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:ZXCga.1229$jl4.848427@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com...
> "Pil" <member27114@dbforums.com> wrote in message
> news:2686830.1048699933@dbforums.com...
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
I haven't the faintest idea who Pat Buchannon is. And I am quite capable of
making my OWN statements without assistance from anyone else... <G>

What is there in my e-mail that indicates I am located outside Europe or
America and what bearing does my location have on my opinion? (I would
accept that geographical background, throughout a lifetime, has an influence
on forming attitudes and opinions... but, how do you think my current
location influenced what I wrote?)

> Not that I disagree with you that people should be appreciative and also
> provide what help they might in other ways.  Bread cast upon the
> newsgroups and all that.
>
> But why does it matter where they are from?

It doesn't matter where they are from (or what Race, Religion, Colour,
Language, Accent, or Hat Size they have, either...)

My protest is against people who feel they have a moral right to free advice
when they have done nothing whatsoever to earn such a right, are unlikely to
do anything in the future to justify an investment of my time in them (I do
not speak for anyone else here other than myself), and, in some cases, are
actually depriving the people they are asking for help, of making a living.

In other words...freeloaders.

Pete.
```

```
on, and others have different country designations. (I travel a lot...<G>) I
maintain different addresses for different purposes and as an anti-spam
measure. Nowadays, with easy Web mail interfacing, I can access and post
mail on a server in New Zealand just as easily as I can on a server located
in the UK or the US, irrespective of my actual location. Now that we've
sorted that out, were you expecting that as a NZer I'd probably be a fascist
<G>?
```

```
>
> dashwood@enternet.co._nz_

Fair enough. This is one of half a dozen e-mail addresses I can be reached
on, and others have different country designations. (I travel a lot...<G>) I
maintain different addresses for different purposes and as an anti-spam
measure. Nowadays, with easy Web mail interfacing, I can access and post
mail on a server in New Zealand just as easily as I can on a server located
in the UK or the US, irrespective of my actual location. Now that we've
sorted that out, were you expecting that as a NZer I'd probably be a fascist
<G>?

For the last year I have been in the Midlands of England and that is where I
was when I wrote the above post. I will be leaving the Midlands in
approximately two weeks time for California. (It is very possible I'll meet
some Buchannon types there <G>...or not...<G>

> > It doesn't matter where they are from (or what Race, Religion, Colour,
> > Language, Accent, or Hat Size they have, either...)
> >
> > My protest is against people who feel they have a moral right to free
advice
> > when they have done nothing whatsoever to earn such a right, are
unlikely to
> > do anything in the future to justify an investment of my time in them (I
do
> > not speak for anyone else here other than myself), and, in some cases,
are
> > actually depriving the people they are asking for help, of making a
living.
> >
> > In other words...freeloaders.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> non-E/A types.

Well, I guess if they are hard-working they would be doing the experiments,
hitting the books, and not simply looking  for free advice from the people
whose job they may have taken...<G>

Seriously, I have no problem really with the Market place deciding what it
will pay for certain skills, and I certainly have no bias against any
particular Ethnic group. But I do see, and have personally experienced,
posters here who simply use, take, contribute nothing, and don't even have
the common decency to acknowledge the help they received. That was what
prompted my post.

Pete..
```

```
> Fair enough. This is one of half a dozen e-mail addresses I can be reached

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…

If I were a NZer I would segment my time between being a 
slacker-beach-bum and a slacker-mountain-climbing-bum.  I cannot imagine 
why anyoneliving in such a beautiful place would ever look to fascism 
for entertainment.

> For the last year I have been in the Midlands of England and that is where I
> was when I wrote the above post. I will be leaving the Midlands in
> approximately two weeks time for California. (It is very possible I'll meet
> some Buchannon types there <G>...or not...<G>

My condolences, you will very likely meet many, but there will be many 
more 'colorful' types out there.  Watch out for the excessively crunchy 
ones -- lately they are prone to agitation and can be quite dangerous.

> Well, I guess if they are hard-working they would be doing the experiments,
> hitting the books, and not simply looking  for free advice from the people
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> prompted my post.

You are right, there are many who abuse they system.  But we are free to 
ignore them or not.  My personal line-in-the-electrons comes if they 
just post a general "how do I do this" question.  But if something in 
the post shows they have made an effort to understand I like to give the 
benefit of doubt.

I remember my first hard disk driver.  I implemented most of what I 
needed in 100 instructions, although it looped back upon itself at odd 
bytes to add another 50 or 60 instructions and data.  Sometime after I 
finished it a well experienced guru complemented me on a very compact 
driver -- and explained to me that the vector table was for me to put a 
branch to my real code...not the code itself.  I wish usenet had been 
around then, would have saved me a world of trouble.  However, I would 
not have been able to offer much at the time.  I hope I've made up for 
it since.

Please be sure to stock up on plenty of your favorite adult beverage and 
tobacco product before you get to Calafornia, I understand there are 
lynchings and worse for those who indulge in such politically incorrect 
things.

Regards,

Joe Zitzelberger
```

```
Not that you really care about WHAT the answer is - but someone else
might:

In V3 there is a "PROGRAM-STATUS" implicit data item that is Pic S9(9)
COMP-5 that contains the returning value.
```

#### ↳ Re: about the word Returning

- **From:** Pil <member27114@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-27T19:16:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2699066.1048792607@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<2683387.1048629847@dbforums.com>`

```

Noboddy made posts so i thought nobody cared.
Here the answer:
When control returns to calling programs from subprograms,
return code values can be passed with special register
PROGRAM-STATUS (or RETURN-CODE). Special register
PROGRAM-STATUS is implicitly declared as PIC S9(9) COMP-5,
and you do not have to define it in the program.
When a subprogram writes a value to special register
PROGRAM-STATUS, the value is written to special register
PROGRAM-STATUS of the calling program.

Will i be on here more? probabbly not because i'm a student who has
to make a cobol project, but i don't like to program in cobol, i
prefer c :P
(And don't get upset i didn't say cobol isn't a good programming
language, i just said i didn't like it)
```

```
COBOL skills are nice to have. With "C" you'll make bags of money and have a
lot of fun. Then be unemployed for a while. It's during that time you can
use your COBOL skills to work on prosaic projects like payroll, inventory,
etc. until another high-paying, "C" shop opens.

It's kinda like teaching for nine months, then clerking at 7-11 during the
summer.

But, for those of us who like running a stop-and-rob, we have fun all year
long (and the fringe benefits - all the beef jerky you can eat, yum - can't
be beat).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: about the word Returning

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-29T12:49:47-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7C6858B240C1B144.C976313D7CAEC56B.78367BB0C0E3BE42@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<2683387.1048629847@dbforums.com> <3e83cd7d.199636072@news.optonline.net> <b619vf$l61$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e84f638.275609954@news.optonline.net> <b62v14$lcm$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e850c07.281194608@news.optonline.net>`

```
On Sat, 29 Mar 2003 03:07:48 GMT, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
enlightened us:

>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>on  emotion rather than reason. 

Again your facts are wrong.  You didn't know about dates and you
certainly don't know anything about physiology.  The brain needs
glucose and oxygen to operate.  Glucose is the form of sugar that
travels in your bloodstream to fuel the mitochondrial furnaces
responsible for your brain power. Glucose is the only fuel normally
used by brain cells. Because neurons cannot store glucose, they depend
on the bloodstream to deliver a constant supply of this precious fuel.
This blood sugar is obtained from carbohydrates: the starches and
sugars you eat in the form of grains and legumes, fruits and
vegetables. (The only animal foods containing a significant amount of
carbohydrates are dairy products.) 

So your statement that the brain runs on sugar is false just like your
statement that "you cannot add a packed 1 to a date field".

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Micro$oft Haiku Error Message #111

The Tao that is seen
Is not the true Tao - until
You bring fresh toner.
 
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~

Remove nospam to email me.

Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: about the word Returning

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-30T00:25:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e8623b3.352800932@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2683387.1048629847@dbforums.com> <3e83cd7d.199636072@news.optonline.net> <b619vf$l61$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e84f638.275609954@news.optonline.net> <b62v14$lcm$1@panix1.panix.com> <3e850c07.281194608@news.optonline.net> <7C6858B240C1B144.C976313D7CAEC56B.78367BB0C0E3BE42@lp.airnews.net>`

```
SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote:

>On Sat, 29 Mar 2003 03:07:48 GMT, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
>enlightened us:
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>>Your argumentation, Mr. Dwarf, corresponds to tabloid newspapers. It's all
based
>>on  emotion rather than reason. 
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>statement that "you cannot add a packed 1 to a date field".

You just confirmed what I said -- that the brain runs on glucose (sugar) -- then
present it as proof that I was incorrect. 

Every cell in the body (except adipose tissue) contains mitrochondria like those
found in the brain. Other cells can burn sugar (glucose and fructose are the
building blocks of all larger sugars and carbohydrates), fatty acids and, in a
pinch, amino acids (protein). Why can't the brain? 

The answer it it could (see qualifier below), if they could get through the
blood-brain barrier, which is like a sieve that allows only small molecules
through. Fatty acids do not float around loose in the blood, they are
transported by protein escorts. The packages are called lipoproteins. The
packages are way too big to fit through the BBB. 

Mictochrondia do not do the whole process. They receive as input ADP and produce
as output ATP, which the rest of the cell can use as energy. In order to burn
sugar, it is first converted to pyruvate, then to AcetylCoA, finally to ADP.
This is done by enzymes. If the brain did happen to get a fatty acid, it
couldn't handle it because it doesn't have enzymes for anything but sugar. It
doesn't need them. If it got AcetylCoA which happened to come from fat (or
protein), it could handle that. Such low level intermediate molecules don't
float around in blood; they have a life span of microseconds.

It doesn't matter whether your diet contains enough carbohydrtes, hydrocarbons
or protein. Your body can take molecules apart and _synthesize_ what it needs.
In a pinch (starvation, childbirth) it can even tear protein molecules down and
use the atoms to build sugar. ALL molecules larger than monosaccharides
(glucose) and fatty acids are synthesized. The cholesterol in your blood didn't
come directly from your GI tract, it was synthesized in your liver -- probably
from lipids, but could have been from sugar. For confirmation, plug 'de novo
synthesis' into your search engine.

If you'd like, I could describe the citric acid cycle, glycolysis, the electron
transport chain, etc. in more technical detail using my own words. I used to
debate these subjects in sci.med.nutrition, where PhD's (and the infamous rabble
rouser Jai Maharaj) flamed me ... at first. They calmed down when they realized
I wasn't blowing smoke. This article was original; yours was cut-and-pasted from
a Web site. What intellectual bankruptcy. I won't embarrass you by citing the
URL. 

My apologies to the group for posting off-topic.
```

```
 What is sugar made of?

 Sugar is a carbohydrate, so it�s made from carbon, hydrogen and oxygen. The
main three natural sugars are glucose and fructose which are isomers and have
the formula C6H12O6 and sucrose (the sugar you might put in your tea or coffee)
which has the formula C12H22O11. Glucose and fructose are monosaccharides
(�single sugars�) whereas sucrose is a disaccharide (�double sugar�).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: about the word Returning

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-03-29T13:00:52-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<b64n0k$978$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<2683387.1048629847@dbforums.com> <3e850354.278967326@news.optonline.net> <b633d1$p0i$1@panix1.panix.com> <3E854DBF.965A766F@worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <3E854DBF.965A766F@worldnet.att.net>,
Arnold Trembley  <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>canned broiled eel.  "Make mine Hanamako!" 

Hmmmmm... my memory is, admittedly, porous, Mr Trembley... but do you 
recall why I stated a preference towards such things?

*I* didn't... so I did a bit of research and came up with (from 1 Apr 
1998)  (also notice the perpetuation of an error of mine; I shifted the 
consonants of 'Hamanako' to 'Hanamako')... a joke which seems to have run, 
gaggingly, for nigh a half-decade:

<http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=7kepWhCNP4qd-pn2-q0NyTidbhtnC%40localhost&output=gplain>

--begin quoted text:

Anyway, I was talking to DD at a DCY2K meeting and one strategy might be
to cultivate an "unusual" store of delicacies.  I can see the jackbooted
food police kicking in his door and confiscating his hoard of English
Stilton cheese (a blue with the look and smell of toe), canned sardines,
raw onions, salt dried octopus legs, tins of u-na-gi (japanese sea eel in
soy), frozen chicken livers, jars of pickled pigs feet.  
Urp-urp-whoop-ooop.  Don't you have any Kraft macarroni and cheese?  
Well... no.

--end quoted text

... and was engendered by, I believe, a completely off-the-cuff remark I 
made which struck Mr Hamasaki so forcefully that later on that:

<http://groups.google.com/groups?q=g:thl3954594422d&dq=&hl=en&lr=&ie=UTF-8&selm=36189428.72FA%40erols.com>

--begin quoted text:

Mr May, my apologies for not having been more explicit; it appears that
Things Which Have Gone On Before have dropped beneath your Topic-Horizon.

You see, a while back at a Washington, DC, Year 2000 User's Group meeting
(Y2K relation) Mr Hamasaki and I were discussing tactics for surviving the
Gatherers and Sharers whom many posit will be using their jackboots to
kick down doors for food redistribution, either Official or Amatuer
(Survival relation).  I suggested the tactic of laying in stocks of food
which most folks would find to be unpalatable, such as wheels of Stilton
cheese and canned eels; upon seeing such 'provender' the Latter-Day
Socialists would then wander off, disappointed, muttering 'But somebody
said they had Food... you call *that* food?'

To be truthful I had forgotten I had said this... Mr Hamasaki, knowing a
Good Idea when he hears one (hey, that's why *he* makes the Big Bucks,
neh?), retained it... and at the September WDCY2K meeting
e'er-so-generously supplied me with a can of Hamanako brand Broiled Eels.
suitable for stashing, placing in a bug-out bag or laying aside in
anticipation of its becoming a Valued Heirloom.  Now I'll grant you, Mr
May, that concern over the appropriate sauce for said eels might seem a
bit... namby-pamby for a Real Survivalist but I'll admit to being rather
new at this game... and besides, what's broiled eel without the sauce?  
See?  I *knew* you'd agree!

--end quoted text

>
>Have you exhausted your stockpile of Y2k Cheetos?  :-)

I never had such a thing, Mr Trembley... but I just wandered over to the 
kitchen and there in a cabinet, having survived a change-of-residence in 
November, 1999...

... is a can of Hamanako Broiled Eels.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: about the word Returning

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-30T00:25:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,comp.software.year-2000
- **Message-ID:** `<3e862615.353411301@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2683387.1048629847@dbforums.com> <3e850354.278967326@news.optonline.net> <b633d1$p0i$1@panix1.panix.com> <3E854DBF.965A766F@worldnet.att.net> <b64n0k$978$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <3E854DBF.965A766F@worldnet.att.net>,
>Arnold Trembley  <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>>> >restaurants selling my kind of food charge $20-50 per meal whereas truck
stops
>>> >selling your kind charge $5?
>>> 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>recall why I stated a preference towards such things?

<droll quote about food police deleted for brevity>

When Americans are polled for food preferences, the 'least favorite' is often
tofu. How can that be? Tofu has no flavor of its own, it absorbs the flavor of
the sauce. I could understand (without agreeing) if they designated boiled eel,
Stilton cheese or Limburger cheese. I'm not a big fan of tofu. I seldom use it
in my own cuisine, but wonder why they single it out as Most Hated. 

Then it came to me. It threatens their daily meat diet by delivering as much or
more protein. Their argument for eating meat has always rested on a false
perception of protein requirement. The 'official' recommendation is 60g/day
(after a lot of politicing by vested interests). The average American consumes
120g/day. The average Scotsman probably consumes >200g/day. Scotland's record on
heart disease is abyssmal .. about three times worse than the rest of Europe. 

If tofu can provide as much protein, their argument for eating meat goes down
the drain. It's like taking periods away from a COBOL programmer. They cry like
babies and look for any support, no matter how tenuous, to let them keep doing
it the old way.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: about the word Returning

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: about the word Returning

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-28T07:35:52-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<E8ScnRC2Ze0r0hmjXTWcoQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<2683387.1048629847@dbforums.com> <2699066.1048792607@dbforums.com> <h7KcneX09ZhLPh6jXTWcqg@giganews.com> <3e83cd7d.199636072@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net>
>
> Today's lunch consisted of granitas (shaved ice) made from cucumber-mint
and
> tomato-basil. The main course was pizza topped by sliced tomatoes,
kalimari
> olives, artichoke hearts, lots of black (european) oregano, and a little
feta
> cheese.

Things heretofore bizarre now make sense.

You recall that Adam & Eve were vegetarians; it was only Noah and his
decedents that received permission to eat meat.

Of course Adam & Eve started out in trouble. By the time of Noah, the world
was so hopelessly depraved, corrupt, and just all-round wicked, that God had
to destroy almost everything and start over.

I'm not sure there's a connection between vegetarianism and debauchery, but,
frankly, I'm not willing to take the chance.

I heard one woman say she's a vegetarian but does eat eggs, "Because I
believe in a woman's right to choose," she said.
```

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Hey, you're the one who brought up the topic of what food eats.

No, you introduced the food topic by praising convenience store jerky. 

Speaking of convenience stores .. a high-school counseler tried to dissuade me
from dropping out by saying I'd be pumping gas for the rest of my life. Today's
archetypical low grade job is 'flipping burgers'; back then it was pumping gas.
<fade out, fade in> I spent five years developing point-of-sale systems used in
convenience stores and truck stops. The most important feature of these systems
was integrated control of fuel 'pumps' (properly called dispensers, the pump is
underground), credit card readers, fuel tank monitors, etc. When my software
turned a dispenser on, I was pumping gas. 

The counseler's prediction turned out to be right. :>
```

```
Vegetarians do not subsist on green leafy 'vegetables' nor salads. You's starve
```

- **References:** `<2683387.1048629847@dbforums.com> <2699066.1048792607@dbforums.com> <h7KcneX09ZhLPh6jXTWcqg@giganews.com> <3e83cd7d.199636072@news.optonline.net>`

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3e83cd7d.199636072@news.optonline.net...
>
> Today's lunch consisted of granitas (shaved ice) made from cucumber-mint
and
> tomato-basil.

Silly me.  I thought shaved ice was made from frozen water.

And I don't know what either cucumber-mint or tomato-basil are, though I
have had dishes that included both cucumber and mint, and both tomato and
basil (particularly nice with buffalo mozzarella and a bit of extra virgin
olive oil).

And while I can imagine that a cucumber-and-mint-flavored "sno-cone" might
be tasty, I can't say the same for a tomato-and-basil-flavored one.

> The main course was pizza topped by sliced tomatoes, kalimari
> olives, artichoke hearts, lots of black (european) oregano, and a little
feta
> cheese.

I recognize all of these save the kalimari olives.  I've heard of calimari
being used on pizza, and I've heard of kalamata olives being used on pizza,
but I've never heard of kalimari olives.   What distinguishes them from
other varieties and/or  processing methods?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

<a id="t-e2250193d4"></a>
## Thread: Micro Focus - Compiler (COB) "options" for creating GNT output — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Micro Focus - Compiler (COB) "options" for creating GNT output

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-25T22:16:37-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5r9kl$smr$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
From the ServerExpress (Unix "type") Micro Focus User Guide

(when talking about "switches" for the COB command in:

 http://supportline.microfocus.com/documentation/books/sx2011sp1/cypubb.htm

"Enable Optimization (-O)
Enables maximum performance at run time by carrying out the minimum run-time
checks. This option is for use after all debugging has taken place and
maximum performance with minimum run-time checks is required. The exact
operation of this flag is environment dependent, but at a minimum it passes
the NOCHECK directive to the Compiler. If any .c or .C files are being
processed, this flag also is passed through to cc or the C++ compiler.

Specify Output File-name (-o filename)
Changes the default name of the linked executable module created using
the -x, -z or -Z option to the filename specified. The default filename for
system executable files is the basename of the first file entered to the cob
command. The default filename for callable shared objects is the basename of
the first file entered to the cob command with .so appended. The default
filename for shared library files is the basename of the first file entered
to the cob command with a prefix lib and a suffix .so appended."

and also

"Compile to Generated Code for Unlinked Environment (-u)
Compiles the COBOL source code files (.cbl, .CBL or .cob) into intermediate
code and then generates them to dynamically loadable native code (generated
code) files. These files have the file extension .gnt.
If the -g flag is also specified, the resulting generated code contains
debugging information so that it can be debugged with Animator.

You can supply intermediate code files to the cob command instead of COBOL
source code files; these are just code generated. You can also input any
object module files (.o) to the cob command; these are linked to create an
executable RTS. Dynamically loaded programs can call any of the modules in
the linked executable RTS as well as any other valid dynamically loadable
program.

Examples:
The following examples illustrate the types of processing available using
the -u flag:
cob Command line     Output Files

cob -u  tmp/a.cbl     a.int, a.idy, a.gnt
cob -u b.cbl             b.int, b.idy, b.gnt
cob -u a.int             a.gnt
cob -u tmp/b.int      b.gnt
cob -ug a.cbl a.int, a.idy, a.gnt
"
From the NetExpress documentation (see:

http://supportline.microfocus.com/documentation/books/nx31sp1/mcpubb.htm

"The directive GNT is used to create .gnt code. The 16-bit directive
OMF"GNT" is also supported and has the same effect."

and from the Object COBOL documentation at:

 http://supportline.microfocus.com/documentation/books/ocds4140/oc41indx.htm

"OMF
Specifies which format of object code to produce.
Syntax:
 >>-.---.--OMF--"code"----------------------><
    .-/-+
Parameters:
code Either OBJ or GNT.

Properties:
Default:OMF"OBJ"
Phase:Generate
Environment:16-bit and 32-bit on OS/2 and Windows NT
$SET:No

Dependencies:
On the 16-bit COBOL system:
Set to OMF"OBJ" at end by DEFFILE, EANIM, FASTLINK or LITLINK.
OMF"GNT" sets NOOBJLITE and NOMASM at end.
If OPT"0", OMF"GNT" sets OPT"1" at end.

Comments:
The possible values of code are:
OBJProduce object code.
GNTProduce generated code.

Generated code is the Micro Focus standard format for native code. It is
relocatable, dynamically linked and managed by an advanced version of the
shared run-time system. It is not suitable for input to the Linker.
32-bit
On 32-bit COBOL systems, use the directives OBJ or GNT in preference to this
directive."
```

---

<a id="t-dc150fad76"></a>
## Thread: Kobol and Linux — 5 msgs

### Kobol and Linux

- **From:** Scott Brady <bradys_cc@yahoo.com.au>
- **Date:** 2003-03-26T17:36:09+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QAcga.89$1s1.531@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
Hello all,

I am a student in an Australian University.

I have only just started studying COBOL.

As Linux is my operating system of choice I have recently
purchased a Kobol.

I am used to using Microfocus Personal Compiler under windows.

I am having problems getting Kobol to open a file on the disk.

Please note the following code.

ENVIRONMENT DIVISION
...
...
FILE CONTROL
SELECT Input-File ASSIGN TO DISK "C:\report.dat"
     ORGANISATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
....
...

My Question is how do I reference a file through Linux such that it
recognises the file I want to input.

Any help would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks in Advance.

Scott Brady.
```

#### ↳ Re: Kobol and Linux

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-27T07:47:29+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5rluo$sh0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<QAcga.89$1s1.531@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
Scott Brady wrote:

> FILE CONTROL
> SELECT Input-File ASSIGN TO DISK "C:\report.dat"
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> recognises the file I want to input.

You must use a Unix/Linux name.

         C:  -> this is CP/M, DOS, Windows drive specifier
                Linux does not use these.
         \   -> this is DOS, Windows separator
                Linux uses /
         name   DOS, Windows is case insentive.
                report.dat, RePorT.dAt are the same.
                Unix/Linux is case sensitive you must
                specify _exactly_ whet the name is.

Typically, the file might be:  "/home/scott/data/report.dat"

You also need to worry about 'permissions' where a file or directory may or 
may not be accessible by a program.  If you own the file then there should 
be no problems.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Kobol and Linux

- **From:** Scott Brady <bradys_cc@yahoo.com.au>
- **Date:** 2003-03-26T18:21:27+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ifdga.108$1s1.549@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<b5rluo$sh0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<QAcga.89$1s1.531@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <b5rluo$sh0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
I am aware of the linux naming conventions and have already tried this.

The program runs perfectly under in Windows (Microfocus Personal Compiler)

In the linux Kobol compiler. The program runs but it enters a never 
ending loop.

It is supposed to display the contents of the file to the screen in the 
form of a table. The table headers are displayed but the detail lines 
contain nothing. The report.dat file was written on windows. Would this 
have anything to do with it? Would it not be correctly recognizing the EOF.

It just seems as though the file is not being read properly

Thanks in Advance

Scott Brady
bradys_cc@yahoo.com.au

Richard Plinston wrote:
> Scott Brady wrote:
> 
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Kobol and Linux

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-26T02:49:50-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5rpjh$gc7$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<QAcga.89$1s1.531@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <b5rluo$sh0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <ifdga.108$1s1.549@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`

```
What is the file status code after the OPEN?  (I don't think your
Select/Assign shows you checking file status codes)

Also, my guess is that you do NOT need the "disk" word, i.e you may have
better luck with:

FILE CONTROL
SELECT Input-File ASSIGN TO "C:/report.dat"
     ORGANISATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL
        File Status working-storage-fs.

Finally, it is possible that your line sequential file DOES have different
"record delimiters" on Linux and Windows.  How did you get it from one to
the other?

P.S. Did you get a clean compile with "ORGANISATION"?  I didn't know that
Kobol supported this Micro Focus extension (rather than "ORGANIZATION")
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Kobol and Linux

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Scott Brady <bradys_cc@yahoo.com.au>
- **Date:** 2003-03-26T21:00:19+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dAfga.219$1s1.1598@newsfeeds.bigpond.com>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<b5rpjh$gc7$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<QAcga.89$1s1.531@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <b5rluo$sh0$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <ifdga.108$1s1.549@newsfeeds.bigpond.com> <b5rpjh$gc7$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Many Thanks William.

All it took was to remove the DISK and it compiled perfectly.

ORGANISATION was supposed to be ORGANIZATION. That was a typo error on 
my part.

The Kobol compiler as far as I can ascertain has very little 
Documentation. For a comercial product I find this strange.

Anyway problem solved.

Thanks again.

William M. Klein wrote:
> What is the file status code after the OPEN?  (I don't think your
> Select/Assign shows you checking file status codes)
…[83 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: AIX IBM Cobol Set and VSAM Files — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### AIX IBM Cobol Set and VSAM Files

- **From:** michael.bierenfeld@web.de (michael)
- **Date:** 2003-03-26T04:01:54-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f11c8440.0303260401.75748f27@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi there,

I am using IBM Cobol for AIX and I am stuck while accessing VSAM
Files.

The following Code

       FILE-CONTROL. 
           SELECT RK-EIN       ASSIGN USING RK-EIN-NAME 
                               FILE STATUS IS F-STATUS.

...

       FD  RK-EIN 
           DATA RECORD IS BLESAVE-DATEN. 
           COPY 'BLESAVE.cpy'. 

..

       01  RK-EIN-NAME0            PIC X(12) VALUE 'RK.dat'. 

RK-EIN-NAME is concatenated with the path to eg.

'/home/lvmbi/rk/quellen/RK.dat'

When I try to open the file with OPEN INPUT RK-EIN the system issues
F-STATUS 35 which means 'File not found'. The File is there for sure.
The Dokumentation speaks something about using some strange ("as for a
C/Java/C++ Programmer") namingconventions like 'VSAM-RK.dat' or
'STL-RK.dat'. Strangly enough using
'STL-/home/lvmbi/rk/quellen/RK.dat' results in finding the file but as
it is a VSAM File copied from another station I receive some data
mismatch errors like corrupted File Header etc. Using
'VSAM-/home/lvmbi/quellen/rk/RK.dat' results in Filenot found.

I have no clue and if somebody knows the secret pls let me know.

Regards

Michael
```

## Thread: Fujitsu - asking for business case for ANSI/ISO conformance — 21 this month / 27 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu - asking for business case for ANSI/ISO conformance

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-26T08:02:32-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b5sbts$p06$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
To comp.lang.cobol with various CC's

In response to a couple of ServiceWise incidents reporting ERRORS in Fujitsu
V7 COBOL for Windows ANSI/ISO conformance to the '85 (plus amendment)
Standard, the following reply was sent to me:

"Error noted. Due to current architecture of the compiler, a major rewrite
would be necessary to address this and related issues. Unless a strong
business need is identified, a fix will be low priority."

Note:
  This has *NOTHING* to do with their current OO and "miscellaneous" issues
where neither their Windows V7 nor .NET compilers do not meet the 2002
Standard definition (e.g. Local-Storage, Validate, etc).  This is simply an
acceptance that their CURRENT product does not (and architecturally CANNOT)
meet the ANSI/ISO '85 Standard.

The specific problems that these relate to are erroneous WARNING and
ANSI/ISO conformance messages.  However, (to me) this is also a statement of
how much (or little) business need they are CURRENTLY aware of in meeting
ANSI/ISO conformance requirements.

For future reference, I will stop suggesting that comp.lang.cobol
participants use Fujitsu's FLAGSW feature for determining ANSI/ISO
conformance.

If any readers of this note who do support the COBOL Standardization process
and use (or are considering using) the Fujitsu product line *are* interested
in current or future conformance, I suggest you communicate YOUR business
needs to them.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu - asking for business case for ANSI/ISO conformance

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-27T01:44:50
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e82579b_2@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<b5sbts$p06$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I don't blame them. They have a superb product that certainly meets MY
business needs and they are probably coming to the conclusion that the
Standard has lost so much credibility it would be foolish to spend a lot of
money pinning themselves to the letter of it. This looks to me like a
sensible and pragmatic approach. I commend them for it.

Pete.
```

```
No, I am NOT employed - but I am doing things with COBOL *other* than just
"reading books and articles"

My problem (referenced in this thread) with Fujitsu was their inability to
meet the ANSI/ISO requirement for flagging extensions in their compiler.

Have you found a way to determine which syntax in your Fujitsu COBOL source
code is and is not ANSI/ISO conforming?  Do you CARE about ANSI/ISO
conformance?

As Pete indicated, it is entirely possible that Fujitsu is "accurate" in
their observation that an insufficient number of their customers "care
about" ANSI/ISO conformance to make this a valid business need.

I find this "interesting" - in that I believe it was partially due to
responses from Fujitsu customers that ANSI did reaffirm the '85 Standard
last year.
```

```
William,

You kind of missed my point...

Theorethical requirements are (often) of no importance to practical
useability....And to be frankly honest, i dont care about ANSII/ISO (yes,
ANSII has 2 I's) compliance (as long as it's (not being) adds value).

Bare in mind. I just did a huge migration job from RM to Fujitsu with
practically no severe problems. I just wanted to point out that <drifting>
on standards might not be the way to go.

Your articles/readings usually make sense (I read them usually), but this
time I just felt I needed to blow the horn (which is'nt my instrument).
Others did too BTW....

Regards and respect, Danny.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu - asking for business case for ANSI/ISO conformance

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-28T19:23:02-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b62shq$t5h$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b5sbts$p06$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b62pcd$33s$1@reader08.wxs.nl> <b62q39$uav$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b62rsb$46a$1@reader08.wxs.nl>`

```
No, ANSI does *not* include a 2nd "I" - and I tried to reply that I fully
understand that "caring about" ANSI/ISO conformance probably is NOT
worthwhile.  In fact, I tried to say that during the reaffirmation process
of the '85 Standard.  However, there were "lots" of people who claimed that
"conformance" WAS an issue and that it was important BECAUSE of the
portability that it "guaranteed".

P.S.  For conformation on the spelling "ANSI" - see the cover of any ANSI
COBOL (or other) document - or view (online)

     http://www.ansi.org/

Besides my PERSONAL annoyance that I can't use the (paid for) Fujitsu
compiler with any positive verification of when I have ANSI/ISO conforming
source code (which is important for MY uses of COBOL), my original note was
intended to alert others who MIGHT still care about this - that they need to
communicate their business cases to Fujitsu.

If, as I suspect, (and both your note and Fujitsu's reply to me SEEMS to
confirm), there really are NOT many (if any) programmers who actually DO
care about ANSI/ISO conformance, then I certainly don't see Fujitsu doing
much to implement it (either for the soon to be "dropped" '85 Standard or
the recently approved 2002 Standard).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu - asking for business case for ANSI/ISO conformance

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-29T02:29:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Dy7ha.4185$cO3.166573@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<b5sbts$p06$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b62pcd$33s$1@reader08.wxs.nl> <b62q39$uav$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b62rsb$46a$1@reader08.wxs.nl> <b62shq$t5h$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

William M. Klein <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message news:b62shq$t5h$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net...
> 
> If, as I suspect, (and both your note and Fujitsu's reply to me SEEMS to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> the recently approved 2002 Standard).

Bill, 

Your suspicions are well-founded.  In many, many years, in many organizations
in both an employment and consultative capacity, when engaged in new development.
I have NEVER ONCE had cause to care about standards or conformance with them, 
or about portability.

I have never had ANY colleague or employer express the opposing view either.

Conversions are a different story, but conversions were always due to acquisitions.
I was never, ever, directed to develop a project which would facilitate any takeover-induced
transition to another hardware &/or software platform.  Cost and common sense made it so.  

It was simply thus.  A corporation would conduct a feasibility study,
and select a hardware supplier for the long haul, and the COBOL standard,
quite frankly, was whatever the hardware supplier - usually IBM - said it was.
If IBM provided useful extensions - and they did - there was no need
not to utilize them, and inconvenient not to in many cases.

As a corporation was pretty much locked in to their hardware choice,
there was no reason to consider a change, so portability was not an issue.
It would have been a cost with no benefit.

I am well aware of the existence of standards, but always considered them
to be something for the hardware and software companies to bat around on.

I still do, like it or not.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu - asking for business case for ANSI/ISO conformance

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-29T17:02:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e85c052.327356709@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<b5sbts$p06$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b62pcd$33s$1@reader08.wxs.nl> <b62q39$uav$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b62rsb$46a$1@reader08.wxs.nl> <b62shq$t5h$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <Dy7ha.4185$cO3.166573@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Hugh Candlin" <no@spam.com> wrote:

>Conversions are a different story, but conversions were always due to
>acquisitions.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>made it so.  

Conversions are also done to save hardware cost and, less often, because the old
machine is no longer supported. I've converted many, many programs from
mainframe to Unix and PC. Most COBOL programs get a clean compile the first or
second time. It's very easy

When Realia was first published, it didn't come with Realia manuals, it came
with IBM mainframe manuals. It was >99% compatible. Micro Focus was also heavily
influenced by IBM de-facto 'standards'. 

>It was simply thus.  A corporation would conduct a feasibility study,
>and select a hardware supplier for the long haul, and the COBOL standard,
>quite frankly, was whatever the hardware supplier - usually IBM - said it was.
>If IBM provided useful extensions - and they did - there was no need
>not to utilize them, and inconvenient not to in many cases.

Outside the IBM camp, conversions to another mainframe brand were more frequent.
The platform provided a convenient scapegoat for system design failures. IBM
avoided that through Account Management; the seven dwarfs didn't. I did half a
dozen conversions to Burroughs, Honeywell and RCA. They were not as smooth as
the above, but still not bad. Usually the only work was rewriting SELECT
statements. Early on, I wrote a tool to do that, as well as flagging other
incompatibilies. 

During these conversions, I never once heard anyone mention ANSI standards. In
their mind, they were converting from X COBOL to Y COBOL, as though hardware
companies had invented the language. Still, standards were the reason
conversions were so easy. 

One of the most frustrating things, when seeking contracting work now, is that
mindset. They'll ask, 'Do you know Tandem COBOL?" I answer, "COBOL is an ANSI
standard language; it's the same on all machines." Their next question repeats
the first, "Yes, that's fine, but how many years' experience do you have on
(louder this time) TANDEM COBOL?"

SQL is also an ANSI standard language, but nobody seems to know or care. If you
haven't worked on their brand of database, you're not qualified. Having done
extensive SQL on four other brands means nothing. There used to be *some*
rationale to that thinking in the days of SQL 2 (which Oracle still uses),
because administrative tools were different on each brand. SQL 3 (1998)
incorporated administration into the language standard. But trying to convince a
know-nothing headhunter is as maddening as  .... posting here. 

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu - asking for business case for ANSI/ISO conformance

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-29T11:18:06
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e8580f9_1@127.0.0.1>`
- **References:** `<b5sbts$p06$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <b62pcd$33s$1@reader08.wxs.nl> <b62q39$uav$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <b62rsb$46a$1@reader08.wxs.nl> <b62shq$t5h$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:b62shq$t5h$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net...

>
> If, as I suspect, (and both your note and Fujitsu's reply to me SEEMS to
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Bill, that may not be a fair conclusion.

Any compiler vendor is between a rock and a hard place...the Standard and
the Marketplace.

Fujitsu have done a superb job of negotiating this narrow pathway. Their
products implement the standard (certainly for the most part) and at the
same time respond to the needs of the Marketplace. In many cases (especially
with OO) they were years ahead of the standard, responding quickly to the
perceived needs of the Marketplace, and providing a superb OO
implementation.

(Personally, I'd back their product against the standard all day long, but I
don't have to...they generally comply with the standard.)

I don't intend to rake over all my personal bitterness about the standard
(it still hurts), but I think it is fair to say the standard has lost much
credibility with many in the COBOL Community. I don't personally know of
ANYONE working on the "shop floor" (with the possible exception of yourself
<G>) who would consult the ANSI standard in preference to the vendor's COBOL
manual.

I believe your conclusion is unfair because, whatever the rights and wrongs
of the standard, Fujitsu in the past have endeavoured in general to comply.
There is no reason to suggest that they will NOT do so in the future.

The fact that fewer and fewer people CARE about it, does not mean the
vendors (including Fujitsu) will automatically ignore it. A lot of good work
has gone into the preparation of the standard and it makes sense for
vendors, who are implementing the new functionality anyway, to do so in
conformance with it.

You wouldn't seriously suggest that, in 2003, any vendor would waste time
and resource attempting to comply with the '85 standard, would you?
Compliance must be focussed on the 2002 standard, and my personal belief is
that vendors should not be too concerned about how well they comply... The
reality is that very few people (outside ANSI and J4, and a few major
Corporations, all of whom have a vested interest in promoting the importance
of the ANSI/ISO standard...) give a rhetorical rodent's hindquarters about
compliance with the COBOL standard.

In the days when COBOL was the only game in town and applications were
likely to be ported to different platforms, it made sense to strive for a
common standard. Experience has shown that the only time compliance has any
significance is when porting or migrating. Those of us who've done a lot of
this, know very well that even with compilers that DO purport to be ANSI
compliant, there is still a lot of code that needs to be "massaged"  and
reviewed in order to implement the migration properly. So what importance
does it have if one or other compiler is not 100% compliant, or, as in
Fujitsu's case, is unable to tell you exactly how compliant they are? I
guess my argument can be summarised as follows:

1. ANSI compliance is only important when porting or migrating COBOL.
2. If you are porting or migrating COBOL, there are so many factors to be
considered, that ANSI compliance is at the bottom of a heap of priorities.
3. The current governing body for the standard has little credibility
anyway. (Rightly or wrongly...it may be unpalatable, but I contend that it
is true. Anyone who doubts this should check with their colleagues as to who
has, and actually uses, the ANSI standard document when they need to check
the syntax of a COBOL construct...)
4. Vendors developing COBOL compilers should try and achieve compliance with
the standard, but there is no point in breaking their hearts over it if they
are not 100% successful.

It really doesn't matter.

Pete.
```

```
Danny Maijer <info@liveartists.com> wrote in message news:b62rsb$46a$1@reader08.wxs.nl...
> 
> Theorethical requirements are (often) of no importance to practical
> useability....And to be frankly honest, i dont care about ANSII/ISO (yes,
> ANSII has 2 I's) 

No, it doesn't.  ANSI stands for American National Standards Institute.

ASCII - the American Standard Code for Information Interchange (ISO-646-US-ASCII) -
has 2 I's.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Nigerian Email Scam - redux

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-03-27T01:38:59

```

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:b5skul$huh$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 26-Mar-2003, "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
>
> > I am Mrs. Roseline Coleman wife to the late Chife Paul Coleman from
Sierra
> > Leon I am writing you in absolute confidence primarily to seek your
> > assistance to transfer our cash ..[some references to diamonds but
otherwise
> > familiar]..."
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Huh?  Would someone confuse the Sierra Nevada mountain range with the
country of
> Sierra Leon?   I know they are targeting dumb people, but not many people
are
> that ignorant.

You mean to say you never heard the legend of Sierra Leon, Howard?

Sierra Leon was, just as Michael said, an old guy who lived in the
Californian mountains. (Michael never mentioned the Sierra Nevada, you did
that, and it is incorrect because it was the San Bernardinos...)

Neither is there any connection with an African country called Sierra Leone,
famous for being the home of green monkeys. (Actually Sigourney Weaver was
planning a trip out there to study them and would have done so if she hadn't
been killed in "Gorillas in the Mist"; but that's what you get when you mess
with monkeys...)

There are many campfire stories about Sierra Leon. He eked a living by
panning for gold in the hinterland of the San Bernardino and was seldom seen
by humans. (I saw him once through the heat haze, at a drugstore in Duarte
where he came in to buy sunglasses. All the kids in the Mall stopped looting
and pillaging for a few minutes and in the awestruck silence you could hear
the hushed whisper: "That's Sierra Leon...he eats mountain lions and farts
thunderbolts...".)

He had a one-eyed mule he called Midnight ... (Ooops, that's another story,
"Old Rivers"<G>).

There are those who say that on a calm night, in the ranges to the North of
The Valley, you can hear a coyote howl at the moon and stop suddenly. Sierra
Leon likes them curried...

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: Nigerian Email Scam - redux

- **References:** `<Ralga.992$jl4.631076@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
here is another take on those whacky scammers in Africa
guy puts car for sale in the paper (or Ebay possibly)
person calls and says he wants the car shipped to him overseas
says he will send a cashiers check for the car and 1200$ for shipping
also - must use his friend to do shipping
days later a cashiers check is delivered
friend in the shipping business is called
he says he needs 1,200 asap so the guy drops the cashiers check at his bank
who put a 14 days hold
on the out of country check but our seller wires 1,200 bucks to the
forwarder

bottom line - forwarder and buyer split $1,200
bank informs seller after about ten days that the check was fake
seller out money and no one can be touched

this is apparently happening a lot lately so all beware please

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

otherwise
> familiar]..."
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
and call b.cbl using COPY1

alright?)

    Click on CANCEL to debug the application

000010       05  WIDTH PIC X(15) .

000030       05  APPITITE PIC X(15) .

002000                                       'INIT-COMPOSE      '.
002100              88 CD-COMPOSE          VALUE

Paul
```

#### ↳ Re: copybook reference

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-27T19:14:47-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OBudnfX8H7ybPx6jXTWcow@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<53Gga.4185$lf3.508423@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

"Paul" <paulhbliu@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:53Gga.4185$lf3.508423@news20.bellglobal.com...
> Hi
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> and call b.cbl using COPY1

Huh?

First, "COPY1" is meaningless at run time. "COPY1" is used only by the
compiler. COPY1 is the name of something used at compile time (a copy book)
and the name COPY1 does not survive the compile process.

Linkage Sections are used to map data from the calling program. Don't use a
Linkage Section in a calling program - only use Linkage Section in a called
program.

You see, a Linkage Section does not allocate memory, it only passes the
location of memory already defined by the calling program. A Linkage Section
uses no memory itself, nor are values stored there. The Linkage Section only
defines the layout of the data in the calling program.

Memory is assigned by Working Storage in program "A".

The calling program, "A" calls program "B" via CALL 'B' USING MYPARAM. But
what is actually passed is the address of MYPARM. Now when "B" gets control,
it uses its Linkage Section to define the layout of stuff beginning at the
passed address (the address of MYPARAM). Specifically,

"A" (calling program)
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

COPY COPY1.
   (01  MYPARAM.
       02  MYPARAM-1   PIC X(50).
        02  MYPARAM-2  PIC 9(5).     )

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
...
    CALL 'B' USING MYPARM.    *>not COPY1


"B" (called program)

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  FROG   PIC XXX.
01  PEANUT  PIC 9(3).
(other items as needed)

LINKAGE SECTION.
COPY COPY1

PROCEDURE DIVISION USING MYPARM. *> not COPY1
...
MOVE 'HELLO WORLD' TO MYPARM-1
...
GOBACK.




>
> In b.cbl, to refer the variable, I executed
> set CD-INIT-COMPOSE IN CD to true  (Is this way to refer the variable
> alright?)

No. You've defined CD-INIT-COMPOSE as alphanumeric. Alphanumeric variables
cannot take on the values "TRUE" or "FALSE," nor can they be SET (as in SET
MY-INDX UP BY 1). You should
    MOVE 'INIT-COMPOSE' TO CD-INIT-COMPOSE.

>
> The result is various in two situations, can't understand why
> 11.when COPY1 have defined "01" and "02" level, there is the run time
error
>     The memory could not be "read" (or "written")
>     Click on OK to terminate the application
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: copybook reference

- **From:** "Paul" <paulhbliu@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-28T15:04:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YU1ha.7049$062.1021779@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<53Gga.4185$lf3.508423@news20.bellglobal.com> <OBudnfX8H7ybPx6jXTWcow@giganews.com>`

```

```

## Thread: OT (?) - Calendar FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates) · [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam) · [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### OT (?) - Calendar FAQ

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-27T21:11:28-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b60eh3$2e9$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I think that this reference comes up every few months in comp.lang.cobol -
but in case you are interested, you might want to look at:

 http://www.landfield.com/faqs/calendars/faq/part2/

which (among other things) says,

"2.15. What is the Julian Period?
--------------------------------

The Julian period (and the Julian day number) must not be confused
with the Julian calendar.

The French scholar Joseph Justus Scaliger (1540-1609) was interested
in assigning a positive number to every year without having to worry
about BC/AD. He invented what is today known as the "Julian Period".

The Julian Period probably takes its name from the Julian calendar,
although it has been claimed that it is named after Scaliger's father,
the Italian scholar Julius Caesar Scaliger (1484-1558).

Scaliger's Julian period starts on 1 January 4713 BC (Julian calendar)
and lasts for 7980 years. AD 2001 is thus year 6714 in the Julian
period. After 7980 years the number starts from 1 again.

Why 4713 BC and why 7980 years? Well, in 4713 BC the Indiction (see
section 2.14), the Golden Number (see section 2.12.3) and the Solar
Number (see section 2.4) were all 1. The next times this happens is
15*19*28=7980 years later, in AD 3268.

Astronomers have used the Julian period to assign a unique number to
every day since 1 January 4713 BC. This is the so-called Julian Day
(JD). JD 0 designates the 24 hours from noon UTC on 1 January 4713 BC
to noon UTC on 2 January 4713 BC.

This means that at noon UTC on 1 January AD 2000, JD 2,451,545
started.

   >>>

Note that some people use the term "Julian day number" to refer to any
numbering of days. NASA, for example, uses the term to denote the
number of days since 1 January of the current year."

 ...

and

"2.15.3. What is the Lilian day number?
--------------------------------------

The Lilian day number is similar to the Julian day number, except that
Lilian day number 1 started at midnight on the first day of the
Gregorian calendar, that is, 15 October 1582.

The Lilian day number is named after Aloysius Lilius mentioned in
section 2.2."
```

<a id="t-d6c2397778"></a>
## Thread: Case Statement in DB2 — 6 this month / 12 total

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Case Statement in DB2

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2003-03-28T03:38:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030327223829.08824.00000043@mb-fc.aol.com>`

```
This isn't exactly COBOL but the people on this newsgroup have such a variety
of experience and such I figured I'd ask here first :).

First a side bar:  I was in STROBE training(?) today.  I say training(?)
because I'm still not sure I have a clue how to use the product (spent the
morning just trying to get into it).  Anyway as part of the discussion we had
submitted to the presenters a list of program we felt were 'nasty' and had them
run the STROBE on them.  Then they selected a few others at random.  All passed
with flying colors, that is no unreasonable resource hogging was found.  Some
were programs not touched since Y2K, some were new.  Some were 'structured',
some were 'spaghetti' code, some where who knows what.  Anyway it was one of
these programs that triggered the following question as the presenter suggested
using a CASE in a DB2 SELECT instead of the way the program is currently coded
to improve it even more (even though it wasn't bad in the first place) so
......

None of us have ever used the CASE statement in a DB2 SELECT so we decided to
(gasp) RTFM.   There were 2 examples given and neither appeared to do anything
that would replace the following SELECTS 

EXEC SQL
     SELECT PART_PREFIX,
                   PART_BODY,
                   PART_SUFFIX,
                   PART_COLOR,
                   ORDER_QTY 
         FROM BM_ORDER_V1 
                   WHERE VENDOR_PART_PREFIX = :BMCPP0XX.VENDOR_PART_PREFIX  and
more like this
                    AND ORDER_MONTH = 2
END-EXEC

Do something with data from SELECT

Repeat SELECT with ORDER_MONTH = 3

Do something etc 
Now - this 5 more times with the only thing changing is the value of
ORDER_MONTH

Using multiple values of ORDER_MONTH would require a cursor and I'm not sure
they want to do that so....

The examples of the CASE in the SELECT appear to 1) replace ORDER_MONTH with
something else - like ORDER_MONTH = 2 replace with 'LAST MONTH' etc or to check
to see if there is a zero in a field to prevent zero divide.  What kind of CASE
statement would allow different processing depending on the value of
ORDER_MONTH?

For the newbies - this is a OS/390 LE environment and we're V6 of DB2, and not
the most current version of COBOL (we're beyond COBOL II and not up to
Enterprise).
```
