#### ↳ Re: Case Statement in DB2

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-31T10:48:03-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E888D63.9C7857E2@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<20030327223829.08824.00000043@mb-fc.aol.com>`

```
YukonMama,
Our shop has had STROBE for over two years.  Actually, we had it some years before
as well, but dropped the license.  We have documented improvements in applications
that have resulted in machine cost reductions of over $600,000.  We continue to
study programs, and find candidates for improvements.  Most of the biggest savings
have come from either improving table handling* or introducing "smart" alternatives
to mindless* data base access.

I have found that the most important factor in using a performance analysis tool
like STROBE is knowing where to look.  That is, you can't save much money unless
you are looking at the jobs that cost the most.  Oftentimes, programmers may think
they know where to look, but programs may slip thru the cracks just because they
have been running for years (maybe decades) without any attention from anyone.

If you really want to do your shop a favor, create a report of the most costly
batch job steps.  (I'm assuming you'd know if an on line transaction was seriously
out of whack, because response time would be so bad that users would complain.)
Choosing the "top jobs" from that report, run STROBE against those big resource
users.

If you are lacking such a report, and don't know how to get one, I suggest
selecting System Management Facility (SMF) Type 30 records from your production
system (maybe one or more LPARs).  Here are SORT statements I use to select what I
want:
  SORT     FIELDS=(11,4,PD,D,7,4,BI,D)
  INCLUDE  COND=(15,4,CH,NE,C'SA90',AND,       SYSTEM ID  =PROD LPARS
                 15,4,CH,NE,C'SD90',AND,
                (06,1,CH,EQ,X'1E',AND,         RECORD TYPE=30
                 245,1,CH,EQ,C'2',AND,         JOB CLASS  =2
                (23,2,BI,EQ,X'0004',OR,        SUBTYPE    =4 OR 1
                 23,2,BI,EQ,X'0001')))
I eliminated two LPARS, SA90 and SD90, chose only a specific Job Class, and certain
"subtypes".  I would have to go look again to see what is significant about the
subtypes.  In a reporting program, I drop records with less than a threshold CPU
time.  That selection could be added to the INCLUDE above.  We have a relatively
fast CPU, and I drop steps of less than 30 CPU seconds.  We have another shop that
runs a CPU of about 1/3 the speed, and I also look at their "big hitters".

You could get a decent report using SORT facilities.  I wrote COBOL programs to do
the job.  If your shop uses the infamous MICS tool to do billing, be aware that it
is SAS based, and is itself likely to be one of the costliest things you run.  I
prefer to read the SMF data directly, and then prove to the billing people how much
money they're wasting with MICS.

You'll need to read SMF documentation (for type 30 record contents), which can be
found in BookManager on your system, in the OS/390 or z/OS Bookshelf, or on the
Web.

*Improving table handling often entails changing sequential lookups to binary
searches.  Sometimes, table contents are in the same sequence as the records they
are being matched against, so saving the last occurrence number can provide the
starting point for the next search.  Several different "solutions" are possible;
this takes analysis and thought.

*Mindless means doing a data base access to validate some data element in millions
of records, when there are only a few valid values for the data element.  After the
data base information is returned, and the validity is established, it can be
tabled so that subsequent validations are a table lookup.  In one case 10 millions
DB2 calls were reduced to 95.  You can imagine how much CPU time was reduced, as
well as elapsed time.  Once, we found that an IMS data base was dumped before the
program that was doing "lookups" against it -- we changed the program to a two file
match and saved over 95% of CPU time.

=====
YukonMama wrote:

> This isn't exactly COBOL but the people on this newsgroup have such a variety
> of experience and such I figured I'd ask here first :).
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
> Enterprise).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Case Statement in DB2

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-31T21:24:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-0A8197.21240931032003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<20030327223829.08824.00000043@mb-fc.aol.com> <3E888D63.9C7857E2@attglobal.net>`

```
In article <3E888D63.9C7857E2@attglobal.net>,
 Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net> wrote:
<snip>
> *Mindless means doing a data base access to validate some data element in 
> millions
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


My favorite Strobe story is one where a co-worker ID'ed an:

      Initialize Big-assed-area
      If some-condition
         use big assed area
      End-If

The initialize was happening for each account every night, but only half 
fell into the IF.  Moving the initialize into the scope of the IF 
resulted in almost halfing nightly production.  Quite amazing -- and no 
one had ever thought to look at it -- they were too hung up on reducing 
I/Os.

One place where I would disagree with you is that you should try to get 
a good picture of all your CICS trans, even when they are not 
troublesome or causing response issues.  If you can level out any trans 
that cause peaks, even if those peaks do not cause response time 
failures, then the entire region is the better for it.  

Usually the low-hanging fruit is taken care of by non-stupid programmers 
writting the things, but some of the most unusual things can cause 
performance problems...
```

<a id="t-a16724e3c1"></a>
## Thread: how to move a raw address to a pointer variable — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### how to move a raw address to a pointer variable

- **From:** rama_sathish@indiatimes.com (sathish rama)
- **Date:** 2003-03-28T00:32:06-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fa4b4402.0303280032.5990a07f@posting.google.com>`

```
can any one help me in moveing a  raw address like x'09870102' to a
pointer variable and is it possible to address one's(program) owm
working storage.
```

#### ↳ Re: how to move a raw address to a pointer variable

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-28T06:27:09-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b61b3d$e8l$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<fa4b4402.0303280032.5990a07f@posting.google.com>`

```
Define a field as PIC  X(04) then, redefine this field as POINTER.

03  WS-POINTER-X PIC  X(04).
03  WS-POINTER REDEFINES WS-POINTER-X POINTER.
*
MOVE X'09870102' TO WS-POINTER-X.
*
If you're COBOL Version/Release is OS/390 2.2 or greater, you can SET the
ADDRESS OF Working-Storage items via a POINTER.

For any Version/Release less than OS390/390 2.2, you'd have to write a
sub-program that gets Called, passing the name of the WS item and a WS
work-pointer. The sub-program's job would be to set the passed WS
work-pointer to the address of the passed WS item and return to the Caller.
The coding required for such an endeavour is minimal at best.

As far as support for other compilers, perhaps others might have this
information.

Bill

"sathish rama" <rama_sathish@indiatimes.com> wrote in message
news:fa4b4402.0303280032.5990a07f@posting.google.com...
> can any one help me in moveing a  raw address like x'09870102' to a
> pointer variable and is it possible to address one's(program) owm
> working storage.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: how to move a raw address to a pointer variable

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-28T18:36:29+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E84968E.B699DA9C@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<fa4b4402.0303280032.5990a07f@posting.google.com> <b61b3d$e8l$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
WOB wrote:
> 
> Define a field as PIC  X(04) then, redefine this field as POINTER.
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> > working storage.

Bill's example should be correct for IBM mainframe COBOL environments,
but I am curious why you would want to address your own working
storage with a pointer.  You already have addressability to it by
name, which is how you would also "pass" working storage items to
called programs.

If you want to address your entire working storage section with one
pointer, there may be problems with uninitialized working storage
being allocated at run time, and not located within the
working-storage layout as it exists in the COBOL source code file. 
Your working-storage section is not necessarily one contiguous block
of memory in the load module.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: how to move a raw address to a pointer variable

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-30T15:40:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-7387D5.15400230032003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<fa4b4402.0303280032.5990a07f@posting.google.com> <b61b3d$e8l$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3E84968E.B699DA9C@worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <3E84968E.B699DA9C@worldnet.att.net>,
 Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
>
> Bill's example should be correct for IBM mainframe COBOL environments,
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> of memory in the load module.

The problem with addressibility by name is that it is bound at compile 
time.  To get any form of late binding you need to use pointers.  A 
common example might be processing a blob of CR/LF delimited data of 
unknown size.

Outside of IBM environments, this can be fixed with a nested program 
that accepts two parameters and sets one to the address of the other.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: how to move a raw address to a pointer variable

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-31T00:11:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e876912.3586448@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<fa4b4402.0303280032.5990a07f@posting.google.com> <b61b3d$e8l$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3E84968E.B699DA9C@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:


>Bill's example should be correct for IBM mainframe COBOL environments,
>but I am curious why you would want to address your own working
>storage with a pointer.  You already have addressability to it by
>name, which is how you would also "pass" working storage items to
>called programs.

Here is an example of why you might want to address working-storage with a
pointer. Suppose you call the OS to get an environment variable. If it doesn't
exist you want to use a default in working-storage.

01  mypath-pointer  pointer.
01  default-mypath  value './*.txt' & x'00'  pic x(8).

call 'cbl-getparm' using 'mypath' & x'00', mypath-pointer
if  mypath-pointer equal to null
    set mypath-pointer to address of default-mypath
end-if

When would you want to set a pointer to an absolute address? When you have a
memory-mapped device, such as the DOS-mode display adaptor at B800:0.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: how to move a raw address to a pointer variable

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-28T12:49:39-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b625g6$lj7$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<fa4b4402.0303280032.5990a07f@posting.google.com> <b61b3d$e8l$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I do NOT think that it is correct that current releases of COBOL allow you
to set the address of a WS item to a pointer.  It is true that COBOL for
OS/390 & VM V2R2 *added* support for setting a pointer to the address of a
WS item - but I don't think that it works the other direction.  I think that
you may ONLY set the address of a Linkage Section item to a pointer.

I have *not* checked the manual on this, so I could be mistaken.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: how to move a raw address to a pointer variable

- **From:** wobconsult@sprynet.com (WOB Consulting)
- **Date:** 2003-03-28T19:30:15-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<173f80ae.0303281930.46f2d2d5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<fa4b4402.0303280032.5990a07f@posting.google.com> <b61b3d$e8l$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <b625g6$lj7$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill,

I believe that you're right about setting a WS item to a POINTER. I
think I got it "Bass Akwards" ;o)
```

#### ↳ Re: MF - Cobol to Oracle HOW, what ways? (migration)

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-29T17:02:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e85a899.321282629@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<d576fe29.0303281955.4411f47d@posting.google.com>`

```
habibcs@hotmail.com (Habib Ahmed) wrote:

>thanx
>> thnx for ur contribution to the COBOL programming community.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>> AIX OS.

Do the conversion on a PC, using Microsoft's ODBC driver for flat files. 

..Install an Oracle ODBC driver on the PC
..Export the MF-COBOL "database" (indexed file) to a flat file
..FTP the file to a PC (not essential, you could read it from the Unix box)
..Describe the flat file in a 'schema', following driver instructions
..Write SQL to load the Oracle database from the flat file

You can do it in one day, with no programming required.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF - Cobol to Oracle HOW, what ways? (migration)

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-03-29T17:35:33-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0303291735.7ecde91c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<d576fe29.0303281955.4411f47d@posting.google.com> <3e85a899.321282629@news.optonline.net>`

```
A straight conversion of a "Flat" COBOL accessed file to a relational
database such as Oracle buys little - except the added expense of an
Oracle database license and user licenses for each connection.

Often such a conversion is undertaken under circumstances where the
actual business problem being solved is one of the following:

access to the data from systems written in other languages
Open access to data via the internet or another network
Necessity for multiple entities to update the data
Corporate edict

All but the last are fairly easy to do with COBOL itself.  There are
pitfalls with the conversion, not the least of which is the fact that
as soon as the data is stored in an open system such as an RDB,
garbage can appear more easily in the data unless strict controls are
placed within the table definitions and in the programs and systems
that will now update the data.

If the determination is made that such a conversion is necessary then
what is needed are two things.  First - someone that understands the
COBOL based system.  Quite frequently COBOL systems have business
rules coded in the programs.  For example - a field labeled Whazzis -
might have a value of 0, 1, 2, 3, or 99.  These are the only valid
values and the field's values only have meaning in the context of how
the program makes use of the field.  Additionally, data might be
organized such that a single header record is referenced by multiple
detail records, or perhaps records of different types, or perhaps a
record that is a varying length table of values.  Great understanding
of the COBOL program is necessary.

Second - a qualified, and talented DBA to normalize the COBOL data
into the relational database to help make the data held there
meaningful.  I can't overstate this necessity.  You can't approach an
RDB - especially Oracle - as a neophyte and expect good results.  For
each RDB in use - you should have a full time DBA to monitor and tune
the database on a continuing basis -- something you may not have been
used to with your COBOL flat file based system.

I don't want to marginalize or detract from the awesome power of a
correctly implemented RDB.  Nor should a conversion to one be
trivialized.


robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message news:<3e85a899.321282629@news.optonline.net>...

…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
```

## Thread: How to compute Easter — 20 this month / 27 total

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to compute Easter

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-03-29T17:02:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3e85d13c.331687202@news.optonline.net>`

```
This code snippet computes Easter in any year between 1900 and 2099. The
algorithm was developed by Blaise Pascal. 


     05  gregorian-date.
         10  absolute                        pic  9(02).
         10  yymmdd.
             15  yy                         pic  9(02).
             15  month-da                   pic  x(04).
     05  filler redefines gregorian-date.
         10  year                           pic  9(04).
         10  month                          pic  9(02).
         10  da                             pic  9(02).
     05  absolute-date                      pic  9(06).
     05  work-year                          pic  9(03).
     05  day-of-week-work                   pic  9(01).
     05  quotient                           pic  9(06).
     05  temp-date                          pic  9(04)v9(02).
     05  filler redefines temp-date.
         10   filler                             pic  9(04).
         10   leap-year-value             pic  9(02).
     05  pascal-year                        pic  9(04).
     05  pascal-sub                         pic  9(02).

     05  days-to-month-values value
          '000031059090120151181212243273304334365'.
         10   days-to-month occurs 13 indexed x-month pic  9(03).


     05  pascal-values value
              '04140403032304110331041804080328041604050325'.
         10   pascal-date   occurs 11       pic  x(04).

 compute-easter.
     compute work-year = year - 1900
     divide 19 into work-year giving quotient
         remainder pascal-year
     compute pascal-sub = pascal-year + 1
     if  pascal-year greater than 10
         subtract 11 from pascal-sub
     end-if
     move pascal-date (pascal-sub) to month-da
     if  pascal-year greater than 10
         subtract 1 from da
     end-if
     perform compute-absolute-date
     compute easter = absolute-date - day-of-week-work + 7.

 compute-absolute-date.
     compute work-year = year - 1900
     compute absolute-date =
         (work-year * 365.25) + days-to-month (month) + da
     divide 4 into work-year giving temp-date
     if  leap-year-value equal to zero and month less than 3
         subtract 1 from absolute-date
     end-if
     divide 7 into absolute-date
         giving quotient remainder day-of-week-work.
```

```
download-library area, Date-Time section. It will compile under Micro-Focus
COBOL, but more than likely, is valid for most COBOL environments.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to compute Easter

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-29T17:32:32-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b65aem$cd3$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3e85d13c.331687202@news.optonline.net> <b64qpb$ehe$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <bfdfc3e8.0303291457.15a41f96@posting.google.com>`

```
As long as CLC has gotten into this discussion, I searched the web before
posting a comment on the name of some of the variables in the original
source code.  I am now a bit more confused than when I first thought about
this.  The original code has:

  05  pascal-year                        pic  9(04).
  05  pascal-sub                         pic  9(02).
         > > >
   05  pascal-values  ...

where I would have used

  05  paschal-year                        pic  9(04).
  05  paschal-sub                         pic  9(02).
         > > >
   05  paschal-values  ...


I have checked a couple of the online dictionaries and was wondering if this
is a "Catholic" versus "Protestant" spelling difference - or something else.
(I *assume* that no one thinks that these numbers have much to do with
Blaise Pascal)

Anyone able to give me a "definitive" reference on the "uses" of the two
spellings?

P.S. NEEDLESS-TO-SAY, I am fully aware that the spelling of data names has
NO impact on the source code - as long as the same spelling is used
throughout.  However, I am interested in which might be the "better"
spelling for these specific variables, i.e. "more self-documenting" <G>.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How to compute Easter

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-29T19:57:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<v8cfgtjuc6lsd5@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3e85d13c.331687202@news.optonline.net> <b64qpb$ehe$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <bfdfc3e8.0303291457.15a41f96@posting.google.com> <b65aem$cd3$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Quoting from Knuth, "The Art of Computer Programming,"
Volume 1,"Fundemental Algoritms," Addison-Wesley, 1973,
page 155, "The following algorithm, due to the Neopolitan
astronomer Aloysius Lilius and the German Jesuit mathemetician
Christopher Clavius in the late 16th century, is used by most
Western churches to determine the date of Easter Sunday for
any year after 1582." and "The first systematic algorithm for
calculating the date of Easter was the 'canon pashalis' due
to Victorius of Aquitana (457 A.D.)." An algorithm similar
to that posted, was presented in Knuth.

Blaise Pascal lived from 1632 to 1662. It is possible, though
I think unlikely, that Pascal may have modified the algorithm
of Lilius and Clavius. I think it unlikely, because Knuth tends
to comment on such things; and Pascal's interest in
mathematics was the more mundane theory of probability.

Given the above, I might prefer 'lilius-' or 'clavius-' as
prefixes, to give proper attribution for the algorithm.
```

```
And you're the SOB who doesn't like going off topic ! Start counting - just see
how many messages this is going to generate <G>

DD  is already ZOOMING through the Web searching out authorities. Seeing as you
mentioned 'Rat Catchers',  from a Family Bible bought in UK many moons ago but
authorized by the US Catholic bishops.  With your 'mix' of religion you should
know the first quote already :-

Pasch - the most important feast of the Jewish calendar, celebrated from the
evening of 14 Nisan until 21st Nisan  (March/April). {Gee whiz - hadn't realized
they were making cars that long ago !}. Every male Israelite was obliged to make
a  pilgrimage to Jerusalem for the celebration. As the name indicates, the
Pasch, (a Hebrew word meaning passage) was instituted to commemorate the
deliverance of the Israelites from Egypt when the angel of death  passed over
their houses, sparing the first-born. On the evening of the 14th Nisan, every
Israelite family gathered together for the ritual eating of the Paschal lamb, a
ceremony especially commemorative of the departure from Egypt. During all eight
days of the feast no leavened bread could be eaten; hence the Pasch is often
referred to as the Feast of Unleavened Bread.  The first and last days of the
octave were observed with Sabbath  rests and assemblies. The rules for the
celebration of the Pasch are found in Ex. 12 and Lev. 23. Christ's death and
resurrection took place during the celebration of the Pasch; and Christians
commonly use the name to refer to the feast of the Resurrection or Easter, which
is known as the Paschal feast.

Paschal Candle - "The large candle lighted during the ceremonies on Holy
Saturday morning..... Made from pure beeswax and symbolizes Christ the Light of
the World. The five grains of incense pressed in the form of a cross represent
Christ's five wounds etc....".

- Not clear in the quote but the midnight mass on the Saturday leading up to
Easter Sunday. Crucifixes, pictures, statues etc., are swathed in purple cloths
as from Good Friday. As the priest reaches the point in the midnight mass,
'Gloria in Excelsis Deo', altar boys continuously ring bells, candles are lit,
the church is bathed in light and the choir chants the Gregorian Gloria.

Paschal Lamb - ( Summary - reference to the Paschal Lamb  eaten to celebrate
Passover and a common name for referring to Christ - having sacrificed Himself.)

Paschal Tide, Paschal Time, Paschal Season - The liturgical season of joy which
begins with the Alleluia at the Mass of Holy Saturday (midnight mass - just like
Christmas(s) ), and ends on the Saturday after Pentecost, thus lasting eight
weeks or 56 days.

What the above has to do with computing Easter - I have absolutely no idea - and
at best you might turn a Paschal Lamb into a Pascal Goat <G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> Pasch - the most important feast of the Jewish calendar...

It's commonly transliterated in English as "Pesach," which
is as close to the correct pronunciation as it could be (the
"e" and "a" are pronounced as in "que tal," and the "ch" as
in the Scottish "loch").

To drag this kicking and screaming back to something vaguely
related to this group, the first edition of Calendrical
Calculations (1997, Dershowitz and Reingold) refers to COBOL
only in connection with Y2K.  I wonder if the Millenium
Edition mentions it at all...

Louis
```

```
I believe that the variable "easter" is still a relative day value, and has to
be converted back to a Gregorian date.  Robert's code got as far as making it a
Sunday date, but didn't quite finish the job.  I simplified and recoded (see
below), taking advantage of Instinsic Functions, and eliminating the need to
consider Leap Year.  I haven't tried to validate all my results yet, but I
posted about 20 years worth.

By the way, are you in Deutschland?  One of my wife's relatives, Franz Hauschka,
works for Fujitsu-Siemens in Augsburg (and Ingeborg Rosa is my wife's name).
Colin
```

#### ↳ Re: How to compute Easter

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-03-31T15:21:16-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0303311521.2e46ced@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3e85d13c.331687202@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> This code snippet computes Easter in any year between 1900 and 2099. The
> algorithm was developed by Blaise Pascal. 

Can you provide a reference to where Blaise Pascal is attributed with
this algorithm (as distinct from, say, it having been written in
Pascal).
```

#### ↳ Re: How to compute the date on which Easter falls

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-31T15:24:41-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E88CE39.41B849EB@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<3e85d13c.331687202@news.optonline.net>`

```
I took a pass at this process:
 identification division.
 program-id. eastertm.
 environment division.
 data division.
 working-storage section.
 01  data-structure.
     05  easter                             pic  9(08).
     05  gregorian-date                     pic  9(08).
     05  gregorian-date-x                   redefines
         gregorian-date.
         10  absolute                       pic  9(02).
         10  yymmdd.
             15  yy                         pic  9(02).
             15  month-da                   pic  x(04).
     05  filler redefines gregorian-date.
         10  year                           pic  9(04).
         10  month                          pic  9(02).
         10  da                             pic  9(02).
     05  relative-day                       pic  9(08).
     05  paschal-year                       pic  9(04).
     05  paschal-sub                        pic  9(02).
     05  paschal-values value
              '04140403032304110331041804080328041604050325'.
         10   paschal-date  occurs 11       pic  x(04).
 procedure division.
 1.
     move 01 to month
     move 01 to da
     perform
     varying year from 1900 by 1
     until   year   >  2099
     perform compute-easter
     display 'Easter falls on ' easter '.'
     end-perform
     goback
     .
 compute-easter.
     compute paschal-year = function mod((year - 1900) 19)
     compute paschal-sub = paschal-year + 1
     if  paschal-year greater than 10
         subtract 11 from paschal-sub
     end-if
     move paschal-date (paschal-sub) to month-da
     if  paschal-year greater than 10
         subtract 1 from da
     end-if
     compute relative-day =
             function integer-of-date(gregorian-date)
     compute easter = function date-of-integer
             (relative-day -
             function mod(relative-day 7) + 7)
     .

Since I compiled with the option INTDATE(ANSI), I believe the program would
work from 1601 forward to year 9999.  However, I haven't yet proven that the
program works, so I stayed with Robert's range of 1900 - 2099.  Note that
using the Intrinsic Functions Integer-of-Date and Date-of-Integer, one
reason for the limit on his algorithm, Leap Year calculations, is avoided.

Here are some of the answers I got.  If someone has a way to verify them,
please let me know where to look.
Easter falls on 19900415.
Easter falls on 19910331.
Easter falls on 19920419.
Easter falls on 19930411.
Easter falls on 19940403.
Easter falls on 19950416.
Easter falls on 19960407.
Easter falls on 19970330.
Easter falls on 19980412.
Easter falls on 19990404.
Easter falls on 20000423.
Easter falls on 20010415.
Easter falls on 20020331.
Easter falls on 20030420.
Easter falls on 20040411.
Easter falls on 20050327.
Easter falls on 20060416.
Easter falls on 20070408.
Easter falls on 20080323.
Easter falls on 20090412.
Easter falls on 20100404.

Robert Wagner wrote:

> This code snippet computes Easter in any year between 1900 and 2099. The
> algorithm was developed by Blaise Pascal.
```

## Thread: ASCII File (written with Microfocus) content and IBM Cobol Set for AIX — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### ASCII File (written with Microfocus) content and IBM Cobol Set for AIX

- **From:** michael.bierenfeld@web.de (michael)
- **Date:** 2003-03-31T01:17:35-08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f11c8440.0303310117.24090f07@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,

I have plain ascii files written with JAVA and or with a
MicrofocusCobol PGM. I have to read and write these files under AIX
with IBM Cobol set for AIX.

When I read the content of the file with ORGANIZATION IS LINE
SEQUENTIAL the content of the cobol variable is somewhat crappy.

Here is the file content :

00020040001102Beitrags- und Leistungsï¿½bersicht

And here is the copy that is used to store the values

01 BLESAVE-DATEN. 
         03  M0-SATZ. 
           05 BL-T0-KEY        PIC 999. 
           05 M0-S-TSHV        PIC 9(11).
           05 M0-VAR. 
              07  M0-S-PROGR       PIC X(32). 

M0-S-PROGR contains cryptic characters. Do I have to use a compiler
switch or do I have to convert the data ? What is the default encoding
for files ?

Kind regards

Michael
```

#### ↳ Re: ASCII File (written with Microfocus) content and IBM Cobol Set for AIX

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-31T11:04:37+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b693ll$svp$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<f11c8440.0303310117.24090f07@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Michael,

Line sequential in COBOL Set for AIX and Micro Focus should be pretty much
the same. My suspicion is that your Java program might not be terminating
the record with the correct CR and/or LF character/s so the COBOL will then
fill the buffer with whatever comes after the end of your data and the size
of the buffer itself. The file will also need terminating with the correct
end-of-file marker also.

If you use Micro Focus Server Express 2.0 or later as your Micro Focus COBOL
then you could call a COBOL program from Java to write out the record for
you and then you would have no problem.

Regards,
Paul

www.microfocus.com

"michael" <michael.bierenfeld@web.de> wrote in message
news:f11c8440.0303310117.24090f07@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> Michael
```

```
Hi,

thx for your reply. It was a compiler switch using "cob2 -host
foo.cbl" results in having EBCDIC Chars in LINE SEQUENTIAL Files. My
fault ..

Regards

Michael
```

## Thread: Power Cobol Question — 4 this month / 7 total

### Power Cobol Question

- **From:** "Mudd Bug" <muddbug@cox.net>
- **Date:** 2003-03-31T14:01:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KSXha.55626$4v3.39517@news1.central.cox.net>`

```
I am working on a Power Cobol application for a client that converts files
from one vendor format to another. There are four different file types and
the
application has a single form into which the client specifies directories,
default
values and translation tables among other options. These options are saved
to
a file and is loaded upon execution. This program is currently in production
and
everything if working as specified by the client on a MS Windows machine.

I now have a request to allow the program to run in batch mode via a .bat
file with
the file to be process to be passes via a command line argument. (example:
'process INCLEARINGS'
would process the inclearing file using the saved options).

I am new to Power Cobol and have not been able to retrieve the command line
arguments. I have
specified the ARGUMENT-NUMBER and ARGUMENT-VALUE in the SPECIAL-NAMES
SECTION
of the main form. The code to retrieve the arguments has been attach to the
'OPEN' event of the form
but this does not work because, I guess, it is in a sub program. I don't see
how to get this code to
be in the main program and be executed prior to the form being displayed.
I'm sure there is a simple
solution but it seems to be eluding me.

Thanks for any help.
```

#### ↳ Re: Power Cobol Question

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-03-31T17:06:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3E88767E.1830940@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<KSXha.55626$4v3.39517@news1.central.cox.net>`

```
I don't know POWERCOBOL but trying to indicate 'conditions' via SPECIAL NAMES
seems a somewhat odd approach. As a suggestion, as you specifically mention
batch processing, (and, presumably from a batch file ?) consider using a Command
Line with Runtime Switch settings.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Power Cobol Question

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-31T18:57:30-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qqydnXGvUKhnfhWjXTWcpQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<KSXha.55626$4v3.39517@news1.central.cox.net> <VmGdnYqlKPrFNxWjXTWc3Q@giganews.com> <8d5ia.59462$4v3.44034@news1.central.cox.net>`

```

"Mudd Bug" <muddbug@cox.net> wrote in message
news:8d5ia.59462$4v3.44034@news1.central.cox.net...
>
> "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > of the main form. The code to retrieve the arguments has been attach
to
> > the
> > > 'OPEN' event of the form
> > > but this does not work because, I guess, it is in a sub program. I
don't
> > see
> > > how to get this code to
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> What does this mean, Right-clicking on POW-SELF ?

Type the word POW-SELF in your program. Highlight the word by
double-clicking. Right-click on the highlighted word. There are two lines in
the info box of interest: Methods and Properties. Clicking on each provides
a list of things that are doable.

Right-clicking on the name of any form element does the same thing. Suppose
you have a Text Box named MY-TEXT-BOX. Type MY-TEXT-BOX in your program.
Double-click. Right-click. Whoopee!

But that's not all, folks!

Click on the Method or Property of your choice in the pop-up menu.
PowerCOBOL lays in the skeleton of the command. In the case of the property
of interest above, clicking on "CommandLine" as a property changes

POW-SELF

to

 "CommandLine" of POW-SELF

You now put MOVE in the front and "TO MY-COMMAND-LINE" at the end. This is
not the best example. Some Methods and Properties can get verbose, so you'll
appreciate not being allowed to forget an essential parameter.
```

<a id="t-56923c18f7"></a>
## Thread: Yes COBOL and C routines — 1 msgs

### Yes COBOL and C routines

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2003-03-31T19:50:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030331145053.20407.00000188@mb-fu.aol.com>`

```
In one of my papers I mention that with the advent of LE and USS,
you can call virtually any C or UNIX service directly from COBOL
(as well as from LE-enabled Assembler, PL/I, etc.).

One member of the list asked if he could call regcomp and regexec
from a COBOL program. So I took up the challenge.

It was a challenge, since the parameters and processing are not
clearly documented, but I was able to accomplish the task in at
least a minimal way.

Thought some of you might want the challenge of fixing this up,
to make it more general or proper. It's, perhaps, a good example
of my point, though. So enjoy or ignore, as you choose.


       process test(sym,none) numproc(pfd) opt rent
       process flag(w,w) map xref(short)
      *  Copyright (C) 2003 by Steven H. Comstock          Ver4
       Identification division.
       program-id.  'COBREG'.

       environment division.
       data division.
       working-storage section.
       01  res         pic s9(9)   binary  value 0.
       01  dest        pic s9(9)   binary  value 2.
       01  work-bin    pic s9(9)   binary  value 0.
       01  n           pic s9(9)   binary  value 0.
       01  fc          pic x(12)   value   low-values.
       01  dtitle      pic x(80)   value   'Snap shot'.
       01  dopts       pic x(255)
            value 'regst(0) nocond notrace page(0)'.
       01              pic x(4)    value   'Ver4'.
      *
       01  Pattern            pic x(20) value z'in*'.
      *
       01  In-string          pic x(80)
                value z'In the beginning...'.
      *
       01  Match-msg.
           02                 pic  s9(4) binary value +81.
           02                 pic  x(09) value 'Match at '.
           02  match-start    pic  zz9.
           02                 pic  x(09)  value ', found: '.
           02  match-val      pic  x(60)  value spaces.
      *
       01  Inmess.
           02                 pic  s9(4) binary value +10.
           02  inmess-string  pic  x(10) value
                  'In COBREG'.
       01  Outmess.
           02                 pic  s9(4) binary value +15.
           02  outmess-string pic  x(15) value
                  'Leaving COBREG'.
      *
       01  regex-t.
           02  re-nsub        pic  s9(9) binary.
           02  re-comp        pointer.
           02  re-cflags      pic  s9(9) binary.
           02  re-erroff      pic  s9(9) binary.
           02  re-len         pic  s9(9) binary.
           02  re-min-col-val pic  s9(4) binary.
           02  re-max-col-val pic  s9(4) binary.
           02  re-cols occurs 10 times.
               03  re-lsub    pointer.
               03  re-esub    pointer.
           02  re-map         pic  x(256).
           02  re-shift       pic  s9(4) binary.
           02  re-dbcs        pic  s9(4) binary.
      *
       01  reslts.
           02   starts occurs 20 times.
                03  start-ptr pointer.
                03            pic s9(9) binary.
      *
       01  binary-reslts redefines reslts.
           02   startx occurs 20 times.
                03  start-off pic s9(9) binary.
                03            pic s9(9) binary.
      *
       procedure division.
       thecode.
             call 'CEEMOUT' using Inmess, dest, fc

             call 'regcomp' using regex-t,
                                  pattern,
                                by value 0
                   returning res

             if res not = 0
             then display 'regcomp failed'

             else

             call 'regexec' using regex-t,
                                  In-string,
                                by value 9,
                            by reference reslts,
                                by value 0
                   returning res

                   if res = 0
                   then
                     display 'match found'

                     perform varying n from 1 by 1
                        until start-off(n) = -1

                        move start-off(n) to work-bin
                        add 1 to work-bin
                        move work-bin to match-start
                        unstring In-string(work-bin:)
                             delimited by x'00'
                             into match-val
                        call 'CEEMOUT' using Match-msg, dest, fc

                     end-perform

                     call 'CEE3DMP' using dtitle, dopts, fc

                   else
                     display 'no match found'
                   end-if

             end-if

             call 'CEEMOUT' using Outmess, dest, fc

           goback.

Kind regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

<a id="t-c1eb868d9b"></a>
## Thread: IBM and "Next Sentence" extension — 1 this month / 7 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### IBM and "Next Sentence" extension

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-03-31T20:05:33-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b6as5f$nv0$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I was sent a private email indicating that you-know-what (oops - I mean
you-know-whom <G>) has claimed that IBM had an extension allowing NEXT
SENTENCE in source code other than IF and SEARCH.

I believe that they did allow it in their (long "dropped" ON statement), but
they certainly did NOT allow it either as a "phrase" in general conditional
statements, e.g.

   READ ABC
       AT END
              NEXT SENTENCE

nor did they allow it (as Micro Focus does - and documents - as an
extension) as a "general" imperative statement.

One *could* argue that when the IBM "PC" COBOL compiler (COBOL/2) was
actually a "re-badged" Micro Focus compiler, that this meant that "IBM
supported it" - but no "built-by-IBM" compiler has EVER allowed "NEXT
SENTENCE" to be used with many (non-Standard) statements.

Anyone claiming that this was a "general" IBM extension, should post a
"clean compile" listing from such an IBM compiler.
```
